Or are they a member of a registered gun club and target shooting is their hobby, etc.?

## Investigators with Magic

If you took the Magical advantage (page 51) in Step Three, you should choose the Magic skill (page 93) as one of your six starting skills to boost. Like other chosen skills, its starting value is 60%. If you didn't take the Magical advantage, then your character cannot choose the skill of Magic. Investigators with the Magic skill begin the game with:

- Three spells (one mastered, two unmastered)-see Investigators and Spells , page 170.
- A Signare (page 164).
- Magic points equal to one-fifth of their POW, plus 1 magic point for their mastered spell.

## Investigators and the Sense Vestigia Skill

Sense Vestigia is a common skill, so characters who do not take the Magic skill have it at its base value of 30%. Everyone can  sense  the  residual  magical  energy  that's  built  up  all around them, even if they don't realise that is what they're feeling-you know, that prickly sensation on the back of your neck or the cold shiver that runs down your spine whenever you enter a certain room...

- Those without the Magic skill can choose to take Sense Vestigia as one of their six starting skills, boosting it to 60%.
- However, selecting Magic as one of your six starting skills automatically boosts your Sense Vestigia skill to 60% for free. This is why the Magical advantage counts as two choices, as it gains you an additional starting skill at 60%, along with the aptitude to cast spells.

## By My Troth

In  a  London-based  campaign,  practitioners  who  join  the Folly are expected to swear an oath, like the one taken by Peter  Grant 2 .  The  exact  form  of  the  attestation  depends on  whether  the  investigator  is  a  police  officer  or  a  civilian consultant ( The Practitioner's Oath , page 171).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000108_5c5cc5239abdba557e4f3723e41a00fe0dae31ef5022f74ed8a87b002fe27207.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000109_f3856adc4f7e8f6ae3c405071f5da0e7a4058f05f64786b5d17a780e21a1a8c0.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000110_93d62fe9d72a94257e83e539e43cc81bae0f8bef25ecadf6c99a8fe5bd54ba37.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000111_0014527abd0b3ea49f8ab45cf536869a1f174f7f5503ecfa605b9811e140d015.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000112_a74bb533f97b2de66f85b5ee0440f3bd357064052b1b5deb6417f55cd75ea269.png)

## Nafeesa Jones (continued)

As  we  established,  Nafeesa  Jones  is  an  officer in the London Metropolitan Police Service. The Police  Officer  occupation  requires  the  expert skill, Law. Nafeesa's player writes 'Law' in the space  provided  on  her  character  sheet  and writes  down  '60/30'  next  to  it,  as  all  chosen starting skills begin the game with a value of 60% and a half value of 30%.

Next, Nafeesa's player looks at the recommended skills for Police Officers: Athletics,  Drive,  Fighting,  Firearms,  Navigate, Observation,  Read  Person,  Research,  Social, and  Stealth.  As  Nafeesa  hasn't  been  in  the service  for  long,  her  player  decides  she's not  been  on  any  firearms  or  driving  training courses,  so  they  take  Fighting,  Observation, Read  Person,  and  Social  as  Nafeesa's  next four  chosen  skills,  and  boosts  their  starting values up to 60%.

In  Step  Three,  Nafeesa's  player  chose  the Magical advantage, allowing her to take Magic as her sixth starting skill, also at 60%; this has the added effect of boosting her Sense Vestigia skill  to  60%  for  free.  Nafeesa's  player  writes all of her six chosen skills, plus the bonus skill Sense Vestigia, and their values down on her character sheet in the relevant sections, along with their half values.

With these skills, Nafesesa has the makings of a good officer-she has a competent knowledge  of  the  law,  can  diffuse  difficult situations  with  her  Read  Person  and  Social skills, and if anyone resists arrest, her Fighting skill should stand her in good stead. Her other common  skills  remain  at  their  base  value, so Nafeesa has:

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000113_aacbd848693d91690db1fde9d227c1388f6a2646ab6c4730f9cf86e6d8d38004.png)

- Law (60%): she has a good understanding of British law.
- Magic (60%): she is a Newtonian Apprentice (page 170).
- Fighting (60%): she can restrain a belligerent criminal.
- Observation (60%): she has her wits about her.
- Read Person (60%): she's a reasonable judge of character.
- Sense Vestigia (60%): she's pretty good at identifying vestigia.
- Social (60%): she knows what to say to diffuse a difficult situation.

Alongside  these,  Nafeesa  has  30%  in  each of  the  remaining  common  and  combat  skills (i.e., any common or combat skills she did not select in her starting six).

- Athletics (30%): she can maybe catch an escaping shoplifter.
- Drive (30%): she can drive, but hasn't been on any advanced driving courses.
- Navigate (30%): she has a reasonable knowledge of London's backstreets.
- Research (30%): she can carry out background checks.
- Stealth (30%): she can maybe creep up on a burglar.
- Firearms (30%): she can fire a stun gun if needs be.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000114_d8c5814b295230243d9deb600732b3a88f58e35e4f4e45efb0fdf165265f5757.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000115_69e077a1de2ae11f8552492c3d293f96f0208883d1f7cee6d06688c122183fba.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000116_4522ef8e31f21b8095c3ea32ec56931032bcf7fd3c223ffedda7896ffcc09a3a.png)

1

## Step Five: Create a Backstory

The next step is to flesh out your character's backstory. The term  'backstory'  is  used  to  encompass  everything  in  the investigator's  background,  both  past  and  present:  where they live, their family and friends, relationships, their social life,  hobbies  and  interests,  bad  habits  and  complications, childhood,  schooling,  career  history,  and  so  on.  You  don't have to detail all this right from the off, though! Think of the novels-when we first meet Peter Grant, we know nothing about him. But as we read, we begin to learn more: who he is, where he lives, his upbringing, his parents, and so on. We don't get these at the start of the first novel-rather, these details slowly unfold either as they become relevant or when they serve to give Peter more depth and interest as a character.

You  may  wish  to  come  up  with  a  backstory  for  your investigator before you begin play. Some  people find this  helpful  when  it  comes  to  getting  a  handle  on  who their  investigator  is.  Other  players  prefer  to  create  their investigator's  backstory  as  they  go-this  can  be  considered as 'discovering' or 'revealing' their backstory through play. In  practice,  most  players  do  a  bit  of  both.  Think  of  it  like an artist creating a picture: they start with a rough sketch, and then alter or elaborate upon it. That way, we get to know the  character  better  through  the  story  that  unfolds  as  we play the game.

When creating a backstory, you can start with the present and  work  backwards,  start  with  early  childhood  and  work forwards,  or  you  can  hop  back  and  forth  as  much  as  you like.  The  questions  presented  below  are  meant  to  act  as a  springboard  for  your  imagination,  and  are  split  into  two main  sections:  childhood  and  present.  There  is  also  an optional step where you can describe your investigator's first interaction with magic and the demi-monde ( Brush with the Supernatural , page 57).

Note: if you want to skip creating a backstory altogether and go straight to playing the game, that's absolutely fine. Come back here later if you wish.

## Childhood

Our  childhood  plays  a  strong  role  in  making  each  of  us the  person  we  are  today.  Think  about  your  investigator's childhood and consider these questions.

## What did 'family' mean to your investigator as a child?

- Nuclear family: one or two parents, and one or two siblings. This may appear unremarkable, but most families have their secrets-what's yours?
- Not much: you're estranged from your family or perhaps you never knew them. Did something terrible happen to them? Did you fall out with your family and leave, or did they leave you? Perhaps you're still in contact with one family member, most likely a sibling or a parent. Or were you brought up in care, or in a string of foster homes?

## What did 'home' mean to your investigator as a child?

- Comfort: a place where there was always a hot meal, affection, and peace. Fond memories of celebrations, family holidays, and shared dinners.
- Bad memories: was it a cold, loveless place? A place of raised voices and closed doors? A place not of trust, but of betrayal and cold stares? You don't have to talk about it if you don't want to.
- Uncertainty: why was it good sometimes and bad at others? Was there a bad event that changed everythingsuch as a death, a fire, or an illness? Was one of your parents in and out of prison, or did they sometimes come home drunk?

## Where did your investigator live as a child?

- City: crowds, traffic, noise, street lights, strangers, anonymity. Did you live in a rundown tower block, a luxury townhouse, or a leafy street in the suburbs?
- Rural: isolation, school bus, country lanes, mud, old barns, dark nights. Did you live in an old farmhouse, a cramped cottage, or on a country estate?
- Small town: local cinema and swimming pool, hanging out with friends in the town centre. A place where everyone knows everyone. Did you live in a modern housing estate, a Victorian terrace, or an upmarket detached house with its own pool?
- It's complicated: there was no one stable place. Perhaps your family moved around; maybe because of their work, perhaps even overseas. Or you were sent off to boarding school-how was that? Or did you and your family flee your home country to escape war or repression?

## What culture was your investigator brought up in?

- Large family: you have an extended family made up of siblings, cousins, uncles, aunts, and so on. You attend regular family gatherings. Does this make your life better or more complicated?

The Rivers  of  London novels  present  us  with  a  diversity  of characters, from a variety of different cultural backgrounds. We learn very quickly that Peter Grant is of mixed heritage and  that  his  mother  is  from  Sierra  Leone.  We  are  shown how this shapes Peter's character, not least in his preference for spicy food!

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000117_8c180b752c9e2e18f0a50a229186afb6c79a1c70c38a90826cf674d31b519c6f.png)

The obvious thing for most of us is to play someone from our own culture. If you wish to play someone of a different gender, sexuality, ethnicity, or culture, it should go without saying  that  you  should  aim  to  do  so  sensitively  and  with respect, avoiding simple or offensive stereotypes.

## The Present

This is obviously the recent part of your backstory: who you are now. Consider the same questions listed for childhood, but reframe them for today.

- What does family mean to your investigator now?
- Where does your investigator live? We don't need an exact address, but decide on an area, at least. What kind of place is it? Who else lives there?
- What does home mean to your investigator now?
- How does your investigator's cultural background-or their gender or sexuality-impact their present situation?

## Brush with the Supernatural (Optional Step)

The  players  and  GM  should  decide  whether  each  starting character has experienced a brush with the supernatural or not. If the GM is running a case file designed to provide a first experience of the demi-monde in all its weird glory, they might decide that the investigators have not yet had a brush with the supernatural-as that's going to happen during this first game. However, this might have a knock-on effect as to the  skills  an  investigator  has,  as  well  as  their  background. For instance, if your investigator begins the game working at  the  Folly  or  has  the  Magic  skill,  then  a  brush  with  the supernatural is pretty much a given.

Consider the following questions:

- What was your investigator's first brush with the supernatural? A ghost or fae, or some other member of the demi-monde? Did your investigator spontaneously perform some magic or witness someone casting a spell?
- Did that experience change the path of your character's life? In what way?
- How did it make your character feel? Excited, frightened, intrigued?
- Did your character seek out people who knew about the supernatural? Did they join any groups, such as an occult organisation, a church, or even just a social media group?

## GM ADVICE

An alternative way of finding out how the investigators first came into contact with the supernatural is to run short flashback scenes for each investigator as part of Session Zero ( Creating Your Character , page 44). Another suggestion for how to use flashbacks can be found in Chapter 5 ( Using Flashbacks , page 224).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000118_a1ea48b21fefe5295d0a9f17c3d68ae10e20f22eeb93abab554474d7a2f87a8c.png)

## Affluence Rating

Another part of your investigator's backstory is their affluence rating, i.e.,  what  is  their  financial  situation? RoL:RPG abstracts personal finances into four brackets: poor, average, wealthy,  and  rich  ( Table  2: Living  Standards ,  page  58). Most  professions-such  as  police  officer,  lecturer,  doctor, and  tradesperson-require  people  to  work  for  a  living. There is a wide range of incomes across these jobs, but, for game purposes, they broadly fall into the affluence rating of 'average,' meaning that person lives from payday to payday. Some professions, such as artist, could fall into any bracketthey could be very poor or exceedingly rich, depending on whether or not they've been 'discovered,' for example.

For  many  people,  ill  health  or  continued  absence  from work  could  lead  to  a  loss  of  employment  and  a  fall  in income, potentially causing them to drop down an affluence rating  bracket,  e.g.,  from  average  to  poor.  However,  some professions-such as Dilettante-don't require that person to  be  actively  employed  to  derive  an  income,  and  so  they aren't prone to loss of employment. Of course, an economic recession could have a major effect on such an individual's finances, perhaps even involving a Luck roll to see if their income  stream  has  weathered  the  storm  ( Luck  Rolls , page 113). Conversely, if an investigator acquires a higherpaying job or receives an inheritance, their affluence rating might be raised.

The default affluence rating for investigators is average. If you wish to choose anything other than average, this might have an impact on the game, so you should discuss and agree upon it with your GM. You can choose the rating of poor if you wish, though this may have some drawbacks in play.

- If you wish to choose the wealthy rating, you must choose the Wealthy advantage (page 52).
- If you wish to choose the rich rating, you must choose the Rich advantage (page 51).

These  may  be  difficult  questions  for  anyone  unfamiliar with the Rivers of London novels to answer; in which case, the GM and the other players can be invited to offer suggestions.

The  only  profession  which  demands  a  certain  level  of wealth is Dilettante, which always begins the game as Rich. If  you wish to choose the Dilettante occupation, you must select the Rich advantage during Step Three (page 50).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000119_435b4ae473b60419cd8d9195bf03021f45e33257ab681f7653aba0ce1c6c16b5.png)

1

## Living Standards and Day-to-Day Expenses

There's  no  need  to  keep  a  careful  track  of  an  investigator's expenditure  on  food,  accommodation,  or  incidental  travel expenses  during  a  game,  so  long  as  what  they're  spending falls  within  the  bounds  of  their  living  standard  ( Table  2: Living  Standards ).  This  system  is  intended  to  minimise bookkeeping for both the GM and the players, and helps to keep the game flowing smoothly.

a different feel to one where the group is staying in a luxury hotel suite. Of course, starting investigators may live at the Folly, especially if they are working or training there.

For instance, in play, accommodation and travel are likely to  feature  primarily  as  background  details  to  the  main story.  The  characters  may  live  in  a  rundown  tower  block or a plush Knightsbridge apartment; they may have an old banger (beater/junker) or a brand-new Italian sports car-as determined by their affluence rating and living standard. In game terms, these things often just add flavour and colour. On the other hand, flavour  and  colour  are  important,  and the story of a group staying in a seedy motel is going to have

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000120_f84a2afc705672499df3bd09b24e8774388c878aef7ed87cd6a5d2ffcc0fd696.png)

Example: Nafeesa begins the game with an affluence rating of average, affording her an average living standard. She may stay in moderately priced hotels, eat out (economically), and take the occasional  taxi.  None  of  this  requires  any  bookkeeping  on  the player's part.

Where living standards are likely to have an impact is in major purchases. For us, as real people, a major purchase might be a holiday/vacation, a large TV, or a car; the kind of things for which most of us have to save money. For investigators in the game, these spends might be a large bundle of cash with which to bribe someone, or to buy their way into an illegal gambling game, or to purchase a rare occult book or arcane artefact from a London dealer.

## Table 2: Living Standards

## Affluence Rating: Poor

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000121_5372ab8d437234d83ce747d8445f631e65a4062969e1e163cde21d8d8715b527.png)

## Affluence Rating: Wealthy

Able to afford the bare minimum of a roof over their head and at least one meagre meal each day.

- Accommodation: restricted to the cheapest rental housing or fleabag hotel.
- Travel: public transport of the cheapest sort; any vehicle owned will be cheap and unreliable.
- Major purchases: no major purchases are possible.

## Affluence Rating: Average

A reasonable level of comfort, three meals a day, and occasional treats.

- Accommodation: an average home or apartment, either rented or privately owned; able to stay in moderately priced hotels.
- Travel: standard forms of travel can be used, but not first class; likely to own a reliable car.
- Major purchases: one major purchase is possible each year.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000122_1aee2445deb7eda2eb3f9a96cff5f9f9b309dc4aa20b00ef5d934b2935ba7f61.png)

This level of wealth affords luxury and comfort.

- Accommodation: a substantial residence, perhaps with some domestic help (cleaner, gardener, etc.); possibly a second home in the country or abroad; can afford to stay in expensive hotels.
- Travel: first class; this person would own an expensive car or its equivalent.
- Major purchases: one major purchase can be made every month.

## Affluence Rating: Rich

This level of wealth affords great luxury and comfort.

- Accommodation: a plush residence or estate with abundant domestic help (personal assistant, financial manager, personal chef, etc.); second homes in the country and abroad; able to stay in the world's top hotels.
- Travel: first class or private jet; can own numerous luxury cars, probably chauffeur-driven.
- Major purchases: there are few limits to what this character can afford.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000123_84a4422a1277bcb265c25a6cc52e1227b68361319d2caddb30a71a444a8dde03.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000124_076799b5f69d1c6ffa9ff2630d2cf2328a9cab6d027f9995188fed86e3d60438.png)

As  we've  already  mentioned,  careful  tracking  of  money isn't  a  part  of  this  game,  so  the  GM  should  use Table 2: Living Standards as a guide to handwaving most spending. If  it  seems  that  an  investigator  is  overreaching  themselves financially, the GM may decide they've run out of credit. This can  also  be  used  to  build  story-perhaps  the  investigators have to beg a loan from a shady contact, pawn an item of value, or lean on a wealthy relative to get the funds they need. Any of these options can be used to introduce an NPC, with their  own  potential  complications  and  demands,  thereby enriching the story.

## GM ADVICE

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000125_fd939451477dba304968ad0be1960ab74f297d5e7f9d1c60b87acc9d133b3eb9.png)

You are  the  arbiter  of  whether  something  is  within the bounds of an investigator's living standard or if they've  exceeded  their  spending  capability/credit level.  Having  said  that,  one  thing  to  note  is  that membership of the Folly can go a long way towards mitigating  the  problems  of  a  Poor  affluence  rating. The  Folly  can  provide  food  and  rent-free  lodging at  the  drop  of  a  hat.  It  also  has  immense  financial resources based on its vast property portfolio and 80 years of accumulated investments, and these are outside  of  official  Met  budgetary  control.  So,  even those  characters  who  are  not  being  paid  a  salary by  the  Folly  will  be  able  to  bill  it  for  essential purchases-such as successive phones, when their old ones inevitably succumb to magical destruction.

## Friends and Family

The  final  part  of  the  backstory  step  is  for  each  player  to identify  one  or  more  individuals  who  hold  a  special  place in  their  investigator's  life.  Each  person  should be someone the  investigator  knows  personally.  Someone  they  can  call up whenever they want and who always has time for them. Chances  are,  the  investigator  has  known  these  people  for many years, through thick and thin. They could be a relative, a partner, a child, a friend, or a neighbour.

Each occupation has a short list of suggested contacts-if you are struggling to come up with people, we suggest you take  a  look  at  these  for  inspiration.  Whoever  these  people are,  you  trust  them  and  they  trust  you.  Your  friends  and family are not the same people covered by the Connected advantage  (page  50),  whose  assistance  always  comes  with strings attached.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000126_bfe797418c0c232f9d9e09047adfc98323765a1e8a2103d1e7a2d3593cff2aaf.png)

## Step Six: Equip the Character

The final step-other than, potentially, coming up with your character's name-is to figure out what tools and equipment your  investigator  possesses.  As  a  baseline,  an  investigator automatically  begins  the  game  with  whatever  equipment would be reasonable for their occupation.

Example: a  uniformed  British  police  officer  would  start  off with their warrant card (police ID) and, while on duty, would wear  a  stab  vest  and  a  utility  belt  holding  their  handcuffs, Airwave radio, PAVA spray, baton, and a PPE pouch (gloves, CPR mask). Other professions, such as a nurse or doctor, may have professional equipment on them (or in their vehicle) while on duty, but wouldn't necessarily have access to it when off duty.

Some  suggestions are provided in each occupation's description as to what equipment someone with that occupation  might  possess;  these  are  not  exhaustive  by  any means,  and  we  encourage  players  to  talk  with  their  GM about what starting equipment they think is appropriate for that occupation, subject to the GM's approval.

An investigator may buy additional items during character creation, if they are available and if their cost falls within the bounds of an investigator's living standards ( Table 2: Living Standards , page 58). The GM has final say as to whether or not specific tools and equipment are available to purchase in this  way.  They  may  also  ask  the  player  to  explain  why  the investigator needs that specific item and ask them to include it as part of their investigator's backstory, particularly if the item, or its ownership, is unusual in any way.

1

## The Folly Consultant's Card

Police  officers  seconded  to  the  Folly  already  have  their Warrant Card (page 211) as part of their equipment, which grants them their Police Powers (page 211). In order to gain access to crime scenes and sit in on interrogations, etc., the Folly's  civilian  consultants  are  expected  to  swear  an  oath similar to the constable's attestation ( Constable , page 197), which  grants  them  official  Folly  consultant's  status,  along with  their  consultant's  card.  While  the  card  proves  their official credentials, it does not grant them full police powers, nor does it gain them a bonus die on Social rolls with lawabiding  citizens  ( Police  Officer/Detective  (Rivers) ,  page 71).  Civilian  consultants  must  also  sign  a  lengthy-and comprehensive-non-disclosure  agreement  regarding the Folly's activities.

## Equipment and Possessions

Once you've worked out your investigator's starting equipment, it's time to add it to your character sheet. There's no  need  to  write  a  detailed  list  of  absolutely  everything your investigator owns-just list the most notable or useful items.  In  many  cases,  starting  investigators  don't  really have  anything  exceptional  or  worth  writing  down  beyond the  equipment  that  comes  with  their  occupation-that's fine, as you'll soon be uncovering all manner of strange and remarkable objects during the game.

Remember: items  that  fit  the  profile  of  your  investigator's living  standard  do  not  need  to  be  purchased-they  simply own those, just as they automatically own-or have access to-the equipment that comes with their profession.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000127_38680fb50156ca385f2f285c1f577ac07836dfca554c5f87ae5a8aba1427b952.png)

## Weapons

Weapons are a specialised type of item that some investigators may own, either as part of their occupation or because they chose to purchase it as an additional item. Players should refer to Table 3: Weapons (page 61) for details on the main types of  weapons  available  to  their  investigators.  If  a  particular weapon type isn't there, use the following general rules as a guideline for what skill it uses and how much 'base damage' it can inflict.

- Melee weapons use the Fighting skill.
- Ranged weapons (e.g., bows, crossbows, etc.) and firearms use the Firearms skill.
- Most melee weapons have a base weapon damage of 1.
- Projectiles fired from ranged weapons other than firearms have a base weapon damage of 1.
- Heavier handguns (e.g., .45, machine pistols, etc.), rifles (including assault rifles), shotguns, submachine guns, and machine guns have a base weapon damage of 2.
- Regular handguns (e.g., .38, 9 mm, etc.) have a base weapon damage of 1.

If an investigator has either the Signature Firearm (page 51) or Signature Weapon advantage (page 52), now is the time  to  specify  what  that  weapon  is,  if  they  haven't  done so  already.  Remember,  the  bonus  die  gained  from  these advantages only applies to one particular weapon, not every example of its type.

The Combat section  (page  123)  explains  how  weapons are used in combat and how damage is calculated following a  successful  attack.  In  brief:  if  your  attack  against  your opponent is a success, you make a Damage Roll (page 132) to see how much additional damage you add to your weapon's base damage.

For  fire,  explosions,  or  other  types  of  attacks  that  are not  covered  by Table 3 ,  refer  to Table 7: Other Forms of Damage (page 149).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000128_ca39f7ca36b3e6b64b022ae1da74f3a72876e982cab121a56e97c5a6c1d77338.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000129_64d494bf64cdb1a627aeceaf7dec35911352235bb2bc0148153caaf8e2b95aa9.png)

Table 3: Weapons

| Melee Weapons                     | Melee Weapons         | Melee Weapons      | Melee Weapons             |
|-----------------------------------|-----------------------|--------------------|---------------------------|
| Type                              | Skill                 | Base Weapon Damage | Base Range (metres/yards) |
| Axe                               | Fighting or Athletics | 1                  | Touch or STR/5*           |
| Brass Knuckles                    | Fighting              | 1                  | Touch                     |
| Chainsaw                          | Fighting              | 2                  | Touch                     |
| Cricket/Baseball Bat              | Fighting              | 1                  | Touch                     |
| Improvised (rock, scissors, etc.) | Fighting or Athletics | 1                  | Touch or STR/5*           |
| Knife                             | Fighting or Athletics | 1                  | Touch or STR/5*           |
| PAVA Spray/Mace                   | Fighting              | Special**          | Up to 3                   |
| Police Baton                      | Fighting              | 1                  | Touch                     |
| Spear                             | Fighting or Athletics | 1                  | Touch or STR/5*           |
| Unarmed (fists, kicks, etc.)      | Fighting              | 0                  | Touch                     |

| Firearms                 | Firearms   | Firearms           | Firearms                  |
|--------------------------|------------|--------------------|---------------------------|
| Type                     | Skill      | Base Weapon Damage | Base Range (metres/yards) |
| Bow                      | Firearms   | 1                  | 30                        |
| Crossbow                 | Firearms   | 1                  | 50                        |
| Heavy Handgun (.45)      | Firearms   | 2                  | 15                        |
| Machine Gun‡             | Firearms   | 2                  | 100                       |
| Regular Handgun(.38,9mm) | Firearms   | 1                  | 15                        |
| Rifle (.30-06, AK-47‡)   | Firearms   | 2                  | 100                       |
| Shotgun (12-gauge)       | Firearms   | 2                  | 50                        |
| Stun Gun                 | Firearms   | Special†           | Up to 3                   |
| Submachine Gun‡          | Firearms   | 2                  | 15                        |

## Key

* See the Athletics skill description for further details (page 82).

‡ Capable of Automatic Fire (page 329).

**PAVA Spray/Mace: on a successful attack, the target must make a Hard CON roll; if failed, they are blinded and in pain for 1D6 rounds and, while blinded, all actions they attempt are made with a penalty die. PAVA Spray/Mace attacks do not get a bonus die for Point-Blank Range (page 138).

† Stun Gun: on a successful attack, the target must make a Hard CON roll; if failed, they are stunned for 1D6 rounds and all actions are made with a penalty die. Stun gun attacks do not get a bonus die for Point-Blank Range (page 138).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000130_c61cd9f6ff8b93c8f79879067c35aeb7e62e2344b016fa209551afdd4b9e0da9.png)

## Occupations

This  section  looks  at Investigator Occupations in  greater detail.  Each  entry  contains  a  brief  description  about  the occupation,  along  with  suggested  contacts,  required  and recommended  skills,  and  suggested  equipment.  Individual skill definitions can be found in Chapter  2:  Skills . Occupations held by characters in the Rivers of London novels and graphic novels are identified with '(Rivers)' after the occupation's name.

## ADDITIONAL RULES

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000131_d9d8d96737ca080d25f4940e16c353eae672b2cc81e6a66d786ae4096c25dadf.png)

If  a  player  has  their  heart  set  on  an  occupation for  their  investigator  that  isn't  listed  here,  rules for creating new occupations can be found in the Advanced Character Creation section, page 309.

## Architect (Rivers)

Peter Grant always wanted to be an architect but didn't get the right exam results-that's one of the reasons he ended up joining the Metropolitan Police Service. The rest, as they say, is history. Architects are trained to design and plan buildings, from a small conversion to a private house or a multimillionpound construction project, such as Skygarden 3 or One Hyde Park 4 . The architect works closely with the project manager and  oversees  the  construction.  Architects  must  be  aware of  local  planning  laws,  health  and  safety  regulations,  and general public safety. Some may be part of a large firm, or they may work freelance, depending on their reputation to get them commissions.

Architect  is  a  broad  category  and  may  encompass  more specialised occupations, such as naval architect or landscape architect, as may the associated Art/Craft skill.

Required skills: Art/Craft (Architecture). Recommended skills: Observation, Research.

Suggested  contacts: local  planning  and  city  engineering departments, construction firms.

Equipment: laptop, sketchbook, drawing pens and pencils, and possibly drawing board, tracing paper, scale ruler.

## Artist (Rivers)

While the Folly now has its own artist in residence 5 , someone who takes the Artist occupation could be a painter, a sculptor, a  textile  artist,  and  so  on.  Some  may  be  self-absorbed  and driven with a particular vision, while others are blessed with a great talent that is able to inspire passion and understanding. Talented or not, the artist's ego must be strong enough to withstand  critical  appraisal  and  whatever  other  obstacles are thrown in their way, and to keep them working if/when success arrives. Some artists care not for material enrichment, while others have a keen entrepreneurial streak.

Required skills: Art/Craft (choose a discipline).

Recommended  skills: History,  Languages,  Observation, Read Person, Research.

Suggested contacts: art  galleries,  critics,  wealthy  patrons, the advertising industry.

Equipment: depends on the discipline, but usually a sketchbook  and  pencils,  along  with  the  basic  tools  and equipment they need to create a finished piece. For example, a textile artist may have a sewing machine and a studio full of threads and fabrics, while a potter would probably have a wheel and a kiln, as well as clay and glazes.

## Athlete

An athlete probably plays in a professional football, cricket,  or  rugby  team  (baseball,  basketball,  or  American football  team  in  the  US),  or  may  compete  at  a  national  or international  level  in  an  individual  sport.  Those  who  are employed by well-financed, high-profile teams are likely to live very comfortably, whereas those playing for lesser teams or relying on personal sponsorship may not be quite so well off. Successful professional athletes enjoy a certain amount of celebrity within the arena of their expertise.

Required skills: Athletics. Recommended skills: Fighting, Ride, Stealth. sports  writers,

Suggested  contacts: sports  personalities, trainers, other media stars.

Equipment: depends on the discipline, but includes appropriate sports clothing, footwear, and equipment, along with access to training facilities if they cannot afford a gym/ court/pool of their own. For example, a professional tennis player  may  have  several  tennis  rackets,  tennis  shoes,  and tennis balls, along with access to a private practice court and a personal trainer.

## Author

Without  an  author,  there  would  be  no Rivers  of  London novels,  nor  would  there  be  this  book!  Someone  who  takes the Author occupation uses words to define and explore the human condition, especially the range of human emotions, through a range of mediums, including short stories, novels, poetry,  plays,  screenplays,  and  comics.  Their  labours  can often be solitary and the rewards solipsistic: only a relative handful make much money. The work habits of authors vary widely.  Typically,  an  author  might  spend  months  or  years researching in preparation for a book and then withdraw for periods of intense creation. And, as for the hours they keep…

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000132_c595766872fb79358d69e5dcc8548af8467e8f7dc510191ae93cdd0ec5d1c8ea.png)

Required skills: Art/Craft (Literature), Research.

Recommended  skills: History,  Languages,  Observation, Occult, Read Person, Social.

Suggested contacts: agents, publishers, editors, bookshops, fans, specialist contacts (a crime author might seek to make police contacts, for example).

Equipment: notebook,  pens,  pencils, laptop,  books  on whatever topic it is they're currently researching.

## Chancer (Rivers)

Zachary Palmer 6 ,  one  of  the  Folly's  occasional  consultants, is definitely something  of a chancer (opportunist).  As opposed  to  someone  who  is  poverty-stricken  through  ill health or unemployment, the chancer's life is chosen, perhaps compensating for a social, philosophical, or economic need, or  perhaps  taken  due  to  a  desire  to  break  free  of  societal constraints.  The  chancer  takes  jobs,  sometimes  for  days  or months, but a job for life is anathema to them, as is a fixed place of abode. As a result, someone who takes the Chancer occupation  most  likely  has  an  affluence  rating  of  poor. Chancers have their  own  code  of  ethics,  which  may  allow for minor transgressions of the law. They may sometimes be pressured into outright criminality but usually avoid doing so wherever possible.

Required skills: Observation, Stealth.

Recommended skills: Athletics,  Fighting,  Navigate,  Read Person, Sleight of Hand.

Suggested contacts: businesses who employ staff on a casual basis (manual labour, delivery drivers, etc.), criminals, police, homeless, gang members.

Equipment: potentially  nothing  more  than  the  clothes  on their back, though they may have the tools associated with their current job.

## Clergy, Member of the

Members of the clergy are the priests or ministers of their faith, responsible for officiating rites and religious ceremonies, as well as seeing to the pastoral care of their congregation. In  some  faiths,  they  are  ordained  following  a  period  of church-prescribed training (e.g., the Catholic and Anglican churches). Their consecration as clergy sets them above the members of their congregation. In other faiths, such as Islam, the individual may see to their own training and there is no official ordination-the member of the clergy is not seen as separate from or above the congregation they serve.

Where a member of the clergy serves is different between faiths. For example, the hierarchy of the Christian Church usually  assigns  clergy  to  their  respective  parishes  or  sends them on evangelical missions, most often to a foreign country as  a  missionary.  Within  Islam,  the  community  seeks  out  a new imam if a vacancy arises, either from within their own congregation or by invitation.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000133_2bfe81d360b11d1e36d7d357baf6365e1b976256f9e53e90f4a22ce87341325c.png)

Footnote: 6 Whispers Under Ground

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000134_062b07ce95a8b24c54983b5680ff08c00ebfcad81328111ab272e2e11fbd6474.png)

1

Different faiths have different hierarchical structures with some having clearly  defined  management  levels  and  routes of progression through the ranks, while others have a more informal  internal  structure.  For  example,  in  the  Catholic Church  a  priest  may  rise  through  the  ranks  of  bishop, archbishop, and cardinal, while a Methodist pastor may in turn rise to district superintendent and bishop. Many clergy (not  just  Catholic  priests)  bear  witness  to  confessions  and, though they are not at liberty to divulge such secrets, they are free to act upon them.

Some who work in the church are trained in professional skills, acting as doctors, lawyers, and scholars-use the  occupation  which  best  describes  the  nature  of  the investigator's work, as appropriate.

Required skills:

Read Person, Research, Social.

Recommended  skills: Accounting, History, Languages, Observation, Occult.

Suggested contacts: church hierarchy, local congregations, community leaders.

Equipment: clerical  vestments,  holy  book,  other  religious paraphernalia (such as prayer beads, small prayer carpet, and so on).

## Computer Specialist (Rivers)

Someone with the Computer Specialist occupation may focus on  one  of  three  main  aspects  of  computing-programmer, technician, or hacker-or combine a little of all three!

The computer programmer's responsibilities usually include designing, writing, debugging, and/or maintaining the source code of computer programs and application platforms. They may work for a software or tech development house, like the Serious Cybernetics Corporation 7 or work freelance. May also be referred to as a software engineer.

The computer technician is responsible for the development and maintenance of computer systems and networks. They often work alongside other office staff to ensure the systems under their care maintain their functionality, security, and integrity. May also be referred to as a database administrator, IT systems manager, or network administrator, etc.

And then we have the computer hacker, who uses computers and computer networks either for criminal gain or to promote their political ends (sometimes referred to as 'hacktivism'). Hacking requires illegally breaking into computers and user accounts, and may result in anything from defaced web pages, doxing,  and  swatting  to  denial-of-service  attacks  through email bombing and ransomware-based extortion.

Required skills: Computer Use, Tech. Recommended skills: Research.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000135_98508c2a55894a51633d9a884199068225d1a33005ea1760f1f86f53f9258cd7.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000136_63331387c51032e08fc1db7efdb784757d1f446eb8c57a006b8efc155e66e1bc.png)

Suggested  contacts: other  IT  workers,  corporate  workers and managers, specialised internet web communities.

Equipment: high-spec  computer,  probably  with  multiple large monitors, high-spec laptop or tablet for portable use.

## Criminal (Rivers)

Criminals  come  in  all  shapes,  sizes,  and  shades  of  grey. Some are  merely  opportunistic  jacks  of  all  trades,  such  as pickpockets and joyriders, while others are highly specialised, spending their time researching and planning specific types of  crimes,  such  as  bank  robbers  and  cat  burglars.  As  you might expect, there are many different specialisms on which someone with the Criminal occupation could focus.

A street-level criminal is pretty much at the bottom of the ladder, so to speak. They may work solo, perhaps as a mugger, pickpocket, shoplifter, or petty burglar-perhaps occasionally teaming up with others when the reward is worth the effort and risk. Alternatively, they may work for someone, such as an organised gang or crime family, and be involved in more serious crimes like drug dealing or armed robbery. If they do work for someone, their loyalty and devotion is expected, if not demanded, up to and including taking the fall for those above them if a crime goes wrong.

A  confidence  trickster  is  someone  who  specialises  in defrauding  people  having  first  gained  their  trust.  Often a smooth talker, they may work alone or as part of a team. Some schemes are  elaborate,  involving  teams  of  scammers and rented buildings (e.g., telemarketing scams, which tend to target the vulnerable and less well off). Others are simple affairs,  transactions  requiring  only  one  con  artist  and  no more than a few minutes. The wealthier the mark, the bigger the potential score.

Forgers are the artists of the criminal world. They specialise in faking all kinds of official documents, such as passports and birth certificates, as well as falsifying signatures. Beginners may be employed to produce fake IDs for petty criminals, while the best work on counterfeiting currency.

Smugglers  specialise in illegally transporting people, goods,  or  items-even  information-to  wherever  they're wanted.  It's  a  lucrative  but  high-risk  business,  and  the smuggler usually has a public profession, such as boat captain, pilot, or businessman, to act as a cover for their illicit activities.

A fence is someone who trades in stolen property. They take in stolen goods and then sell them on to other criminals or unsuspecting legitimate customers. Principally a middleman between thieves and buyers, the fence either takes a cut of the profit or, more usually, buys the stolen goods at a very low price then pockets the mark up.

Some  follow  strict  codes  of  behaviour,  and  there's  usually nothing personal in what they do-it's purely business.

## Required skills: none.

Recommended skills: Athletics, Drive, Fighting, Firearms, Locksmith, Mechanical  Repair, Navigate, Observation, Read Person, Social, Stealth.

Suggested contacts: fences, gang members, drug dealers and users, counterfeiters, money launderers, police, ex-cons.

Equipment: varies  depending  on  the  type  of  criminal. A burglar may carry a set of lockpicks, muggers are never without  some  sort  of  concealed  weapon,  and  drug  dealers usually have some of their product carefully secreted somewhere about their person.

## GM ADVICE

Carefully consider whether or not to allow Criminal as an occupation in a game. If the other investigators are  law-abiding,  or,  indeed,  police  officers,  a  clash is  likely  to  occur  at  some  point.  However,  some players-and  thereby,  their  investigators-may  be prepared to turn a blind eye to a degree of low-level criminality. For this reason, it's recommended that if anyone is thinking of playing a criminal, you discuss this  during  Session  Zero  before  character  creation begins.  If  people  aren't  happy  with  it,  or  there's  a feeling that it may lead to an unwelcome clash, then you have two options:

- Don't include Criminal as a potential occupation.
- Allow players to choose Criminal as a former occupation. They get all the skills and contacts, but now they've gone straight. Perhaps their encounter with the supernatural convinced them that a life of crime really wasn't for them anymore!

## Dilettante (Rivers)

Dilettantes  are  self-supporting,  living  off  an  inheritance, trust  fund,  or  some  other  source  of  income  that  does  not require  them  to  work.  Usually,  the  dilettante  has  enough money that specialist financial advisers are needed to help manage it.  They  are  potentially  well  educated,  though  not necessarily  accomplished  in  anything,  depending  on  the source their wealth.

Enforcers are the muscle of the underworld. Hired to be guards or otherwise the strong arm of a criminal enterprise.

Money frees the dilettante to be eccentric and outspoken, occasionally in ways that could be detrimental to other people's well-being 8 . The dilettante has had plenty of time to learn how to be charming and sophisticated; what else has been done with that free time is likely to betray the dilettante's true character and  interests.  Covers  trust  fund  babies,  wealthy  individuals (possibly retired), and dot com millionaires.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000137_ebfe56624f2ae399ba3755a0f808f1581a60f159a9b29b6a9433d389eae97c11.png)

1

Note: in order to take the Dilettante occupation, you must take the Rich advantage (page 51).

Required skills: none.

Recommended  skills: Art/Craft  (any),  Drive,  Firearms, Languages, Ride, Social.

Suggested contacts: variable, but usually people of a similar background  and  tastes,  fraternal  organisations,  bohemian circles, high society at large.

Equipment: let's face it, pretty much anything they wantafter all, money's no object to a dilettante; most likely linked in  some  way  to  how  they  made  their  money  or,  if  they inherited their wealth, their personal passion.

## Doctor of Medicine (Rivers)

Doctors  study  the  workings  of  the  human  body,  with  a view  to  treating  its  various  ailments  and  diseases.  Many continue  to  work  with  living  people  after  they  qualify, like  general  practitioners,  surgeons,  or  other  specialists, such  as  psychiatrists,  while  others  concentrate  on  medical research or dealing with the dead as a pathologist or medical examiner.  Most  doctors  share  the  core  aim  of  wanting  to help  patients;  many  also  work  to  promote  scientific  and medical  understanding,  though  some  are  more  focused on  the  opportunity  to  gain  money  and  prestige  through their position.

If a general practitioner, the doctor will be based in a rural or  neighbourhood practice in or near a small town or city. Many  physicians  are  employed  by  large  urban  hospitals, allowing them to specialise in areas like pathology, toxicology, orthopaedics, and gastroenterology-even cryptopathology, if you know the right people. Doctors may also serve as partor  full-time  medical  examiners,  conducting  autopsies  and signing death certificates for the city, county, or state.

Required skills: Medicine.

Recommended skills: Observation, Read Person, Research, Science (Biology), Social.

Suggested  contacts: other physicians,  medical  workers, patients and ex-patients.

Equipment: if a general practitioner, a high visibility jacket or vest plus a doctor's bag of some sort, containing a photo ID, a small number of forms (including a prescription pad), safety alarm, alcohol wipes, disposable gloves, alcohol gel, sharps bin,  small  torch  (flashlight),  specimen  bottles,  syringes, dressings and surgical tape, and diagnostic  equipment (stethoscope,  blood  pressure  monitor,  thermometer,  pulse oximeter,  etc.),  along  with  a  small  range  of  drugs  (e.g., painkillers, antibiotics, and drugs to treat a variety of illnesses such as diabetes, heart problems, asthma, and anaphylaxis). Specialists will have access to the equipment appropriate to their specialism.

## Driver

There are many types of professional driver: cabbie, delivery person, lorry driver, ambulance driver, bus driver, chauffeur, and  so  on.  Some  may  work  for  a  company  or  a  private individual who provides the vehicle they drive as part of their job, while others may own their own work vehicle (most often a cab, van, or rig). With the rise of the gig-economy, many drivers acquire work through an app. Some drivers may even be professionals competing in the world of motor sports!

One  of  the  most  common  types  of  professional  driver encountered,  particularly  in  London,  is  the  taxi  driver  or cabbie. If they work for a company (large or small), the driver probably  rents  their  cab  from  the  company,  giving  them access  to  their  employer's  dispatcher  service.  Alternatively, they may have their own cab and licence. Those who drive the  famous  London  black  cabs  are  all  members  of  the Worshipful Company of Hackney Carriage Drivers, having passed  'The  Knowledge.'  As  with  any  other  form  of  taxi service,  some  work  for  a  company,  others  are  fully  selfemployed. Regardless of who they work for, taxi drivers are usually  required  to  obtain  a  special  licence  that  includes  a background check by the police. In London, this is handled by the Public Carriage Office.

Another  type  of  professional  driver  is  the  chauffeur;  as with taxi drivers, they may either be directly employed by an individual or firm, or work for an agency that hires both car and chauffeur out for single engagements or on a retainer basis.

## Required skills: Drive.

Recommended skills: Navigate, Observation, Read Person, Social.

Suggested contacts: customers, businesses, law enforcement, and general street-level life.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000138_ddf3e11eff776a01e219c68e91c4c0ea57a725a0e30e4534502dad0225133bfe.png)

Equipment: vehicle and appropriate licence, mobile phone, satellite navigation device, relevant A-Z Guide (if in the UK) or street maps (physical or virtual) as back up; potentially a credit card reader and/or cash box if receiving money directly from customers.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000139_0548699205a6a8c769794712314c8430200381af46f56d4be7998174c4822fc6.png)

## Entertainer (Rivers)

This  occupation  is  a  catch-all  for  those  involved  in  the entertainment industry: actors, singers, dancers, clowns, comedians, DJs, musicians, radio or television personalities, or anyone else who earns a living in front of an audience. These people love to be seen, love to show what they do best, and love the consequent applause. They may perform solo or in a group.

Many entertainers struggle to get by, eking out a living by playing small venues as often as they can. A fortunate few get regular work, such as performing in a bar or hotel. For the minority, great success and wealth can be found by being in the right place at the right time-having a modicum of talent might also help, but in a world obsessed with celebrity, it's not always essential. A staggeringly small few make it, becoming mass-media celebrities and household names.

Note: if  the  desire  is  to  be  a  famous  film  actor  or  media celebrity, you must take the Rich advantage (page 51).

Required skills: Art/Craft (Acting or instrument). Recommended skills: Disguise, Observation, Read Person. Suggested contacts: theatre, film industry, television industry,  club  owners,  unions,  entertainment  critics,  and maybe even organised crime.

Equipment: varies depending on the type of entertainer; for example, a British actor may have an Equity union card (and a box of stage makeup), while their US counterpart may have a SAG card; a musician will have their instrument, while a DJ will have their decks and records, or laptop.

## Farmer (Rivers)

An  agricultural  worker  and/or  manager  who  might  own the  land  on  which  they  raise  crops  or  livestock,  or  who  is employed to do the same for a third party. The life of a farmer is hard work, suited to those who enjoy manual labour and outdoor activities, and always subject to the vagaries of the weather and consumer demand. Many farmers carry on the family tradition while others come into the profession after attending agricultural college.

Required skills: Art/Craft (Farming), Drive.

Recommended skills: Accounting,  Athletics,  Mechanical Repair, Observation, Read Person.

Suggested  contacts: local bank,  local politicians, local rural businesses.

Equipment: farm vehicle, shotgun (licenced), tools (hammer, axe, etc.); other equipment varies depending on whether the farm is arable, pastoral, or mixed-or, indeed, more highlyspecialised, such as a fish farm.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000140_bcc365f5e38488d184a4b662c3a928446491319d4124c261881bd2daa7656802.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000141_6834eff7bcbf6fc3d5fc661e090d37f7a07ebc8b41c6d13120aa01f572e3915a.png)

1

## Firefighter (Rivers)

Firefighters  are  trained  to  extinguish  fires  that  threaten  lives and  property,  or  the  environment  (in  the  case  of  wildfires). Firefighters work closely with the police and medical services and are frequently called upon as a rescue service for such things as road traffic accidents and collapsed buildings. Firefighters are skilled in using a wide range of personal protective equipment and tools. They are trained to work quickly and safely, and to enter burning buildings to save the likes of you and me. They may  be  involved  in  fire  safety  education  programmes  and community outreach programmes aimed at fire prevention.

The  fire  service  uses  ranks,  beginning  in  the  UK  with firefighter,  rising  through  leading  firefighter,  to  sub-officer and station officer. The service is overseen by a commissioner. In Britain, fire services are organised along regional lines, with London having its own dedicated service; in the US, fire services are organised at a municipal level. While most large fire services in the UK and the US employ full-time staff, there are still parttime  volunteer  firefighters  in  both  countries.  An  investigator who is a volunteer firefighter should use the occupation which best describes their main form of employment.

Required skills: Athletics.

Recommended skills: Drive, Navigate, Observation.

Suggested contacts: police, ambulance drivers, and paramedics.

Equipment: high-visibility  protective  clothing,  helmet,  air supply, gloves, axe, first aid kit, rope.

## Influencer

An  influencer  works  through  social  media  to  affect  the decisions  made  by  their  audience,  usually  in  respect  to purchasing. They use blogs, videos, and other social media tools  to  show  off  their  knowledge  or  expertise  and  build a  following,  or  otherwise  use  their  celebrity  to  endorse and  promote  products.  Some  influencers  have  millions  of followers, while others may be more niche (but may have a more powerful effect over a smaller number of people).

The  original  influencers  were  celebrities,  such  as  those from the world of music, film, sport, or fashion. Companies have  always  sought  out  famous  people  for  advertising  and endorsements, and influencers are simply an extension of that desire in social media form.

Businesses (especially advertising agencies) seek out influencers in order to gain access to their followers, thereby creating and manipulating trends, and thus building markets. A successful influencer can become very rich-and work with an  agent  and  a  team-but  lower-level  influencers  usually work solo and simply make a living wage.

Required skills: Social.

Recommended  skills: Computer Use (though many influencers may have very limited skill with computers). Suggested contacts: myriad followers, businesspeople, other influencers.

Equipment: tablet, mobile phone, webcam, mic, ring light, selfie stick.

## Journalist

The journalist uses words to report and comment upon topics and newsworthy incidents, writing as many words in a day as  an  author  may  in  a  week.  Journalists  usually  work  for newspapers, magazines, radio, television, and internet news services, although many are freelance.

Investigative journalists research deep into and report on their  specialist  topic  area,  e.g.,  political  affairs,  corporate accountability, and serious crime. The best attempt to remain independent  of  the  corruption  and  self-serving  they  all too frequently witness, but the grubby reality of the world sometimes overwhelms the worst, who eventually forfeit any sensibility except the power of their words.

Reporters work in the news and media industries, either freelance  or  for  a  newspaper,  magazine,  website,  or  news agency. Most work outside the office, interviewing witnesses, checking records, and gathering stories. Some are assigned to specific beats like crime, sports, or business, while others cover social events, celebrity gossip, and so on.

Reporters carry press passes, but these are of little value other  than  to  identify  an  individual  as  employed  by  their respective employer (usually a newspaper). The real work is similar to that of a private detective, and some reporters may resort to subterfuge to gain the information they want.

Required skills: Research, Social.

Recommended  skills: Art/Craft  (Photography),  History, Observation, Read Person.

Suggested contacts: news  industry,  politicians,  street-level criminals, or law enforcement.

Equipment: press pass, notebook, mobile phone, potentially portable  audio/video  recording  equipment  (although  mobile devices now have access to sufficiently high-quality recording apps that separate devices may be the preserve of the old guard).

## Lawyer (Rivers)

Learned in the law of the country in which they serve, the lawyer acts as an attorney, counsel, or solicitor, able to relate abstract  legal  theories  and  knowledge  to  present  solutions for  their  clients.  May  be  hired  or  appointed  by  a  court  on an individual basis or may be privately retained by a wealthy client or business.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000142_29c58fbfcad3a4f26be719c86cb02fe53418b8ea45c625130027886f87db9283.png)

The term 'lawyer' tends to mean an attorney in the US, while in England it loosely refers to a variety of professions including barristers, solicitors, and legal executives. In North America, the state or provincial bar of a particular state or province must sanction one's practise of law. In England and Wales,  the  lawyer  must  be  sanctioned  by  the  appropriate approved regulator (the Solicitors Regulation Authority for solicitors, the Bar Standards Board for barristers, or CILEx Regulation for chartered legal executives).

With the right client, a lawyer could become a celebrity in their own right, and a small number do enjoy the media attention for political or financial gain. Some acquire a degree of celebrity by championing a particular cause, such as racial equality, women's rights, or labour relations.

Required skills: Law, Research, Social.

Recommended skills: Accounting, Computer Use, History, Observation, Read Person.

Suggested contacts: depending on the area of law in which they choose to work, they may have contact with criminal organisations,  businesspeople,  campaigning  organisations, government officials, etc.

Equipment: mobile phone, smart suit, legal manuals, legal pads, briefcase.

## Lecturer

In RoL:RPG ,  the term 'lecturer' encompasses educators of all sorts, including school teachers, tutors, college/university lecturers and professors.

School teachers must generally have a degree and either work as general teachers with lower ages or are subject-specific teachers with older children and teenagers. Those who teach in further and higher education frequently hold higher-level qualifications, such as master's degrees and PhDs.

In  the  UK,  professors  are  academics  who  hold  subjectspecific  PhDs,  employed  by  universities  to  teach  students alongside their ongoing academic research. As a result, they may have a discernible academic reputation in their area of expertise. In the US, there is no requirement to have a PhD before  calling  oneself  a  professor.  They  may  be  employed by both universities and colleges in either a purely teaching capacity or along similar lines to their UK colleagues.

Required  skills: Research,  plus  a  skill  relating  to  their subject specialism.

Recommended skills: Languages, Read Person, Social. Suggested contacts: fellow academics and teachers, universities, libraries.

Equipment: course syllabus, lecture notes and presentations, mark  schemes,  coloured  pens,  and  access  to  the  stationery cupboard; other tools and equipment will depend on the age range they teach and/or their subject specialism, e.g., a nursery school teacher is likely to have access to glue and crayons.

## Librarian (Rivers)

Librarians  are  usually  employed  by  public  institutions  or universities,  responsible  for  cataloguing  and  maintaining stock, as well as dealing with lender enquiries. The librarian is also a keeper of electronic media and databases. Some large businesses employ librarians to manage a large collection or data. A few librarians are employed by wealthy collectors to provide custodial care of private libraries.

It's  never wise to mess with librarians-something Peter Grant learns the hard way on more than one occasion 9 . They have a world of information at their fingertips and can prove to be invaluable allies when it comes to hunting down weird and obscure information.

Required skills: Research.

Recommended skills: Accounting, Computer Use.

Suggested contacts: booksellers, community groups, specialist researchers.

Equipment: library  card  (ID),  stationery,  and  books-lots and lots of books.

## Nurse

Nurses are trained healthcare professionals, usually working in hospitals, nursing homes, or alongside general practitioners. Generally,  a  nurse  assists  an  individual,  sick  or  well,  with minor treatment and activities contributing to their health and/or  recovery-or  to  a  peaceful  death-including  those that the person might otherwise have performed unaided if they had the necessary strength, will, or knowledge.

Some nurses have undergone additional training to become nurse  practitioners,  able  to  assess  patient  needs,  order  and interpret  diagnostic  tests,  diagnose  disease,  and  formulate treatment plans-the exact parameters of the  role  vary  by  country.

Required skills: Medicine (starting at 60%, or Medicine at 40% along with an additional expert skill of the player's choice at 40%).

Recommended skills: Observation, Read Person, Social.

Suggested contacts: hospital workers, physicians, community workers.

Equipment: a nurse's bag containing syringes, dressings and surgical  tape,  saline,  alcohol  gel,  alcohol  wipes,  disposable gloves,  specimen  bottles,  bandage  scissors,  clinical  waste bags, sharps bin, diagnostic equipment (stethoscope, blood pressure monitor, thermometer, etc.), and a small selection of drugs (mostly over-the-counter ones, such as painkillers and cold medication).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000143_34349318ee4c3e8bed5d00212932f92d2283b01263e099ddee017bb3f998354e.png)

1

## Paramedic (Rivers)

Trained in emergency health care, paramedics-along with emergency medical technicians (EMTs)-are usually on the front line of dealing with a medical emergency outside of a hospital situation. They may be part of an ambulance crew on the ground, up in a helicopter as part of an air ambulance service, or acting alone as a first responder.

Due to the  nature  of  their  work,  they  must  be  prepared  to administer emergency medical care to patients in a wide variety of settings, often in difficult and/or hazardous environments. In the UK, specialist paramedics, known as HART (Hazardous Area Response Teams), are the only medical personnel allowed into a major incident's 'hot zone' to treat victims on the spot-ordinary paramedics must wait for members of the other services, such as fire and rescue, to bring victims to them under such circumstances.

While there is a distinction between a paramedic and an EMT in terms of qualifications and professional registration, in  the  UK,  the  term  is  usually  used  by  the  general  public to refer to both occupations. In the US, EMT is the more commonly encountered term.

Required skills: Medicine, Observation.

Recommended skills: Read  Person,  Science  (Biology),  Social. Suggested  contacts: other paramedics, hospital  staffparticularly those in Accident and Emergency (the emergency room), police, firefighters.

Equipment: a paramedic's bag containing alcohol gel, disposable gloves, clinical waste bags, sharps bin, airways tubes (nasal and oral) and lubricating gel, tourniquets, dressings and surgical tape, bag valve mask, foil blanket, head torch, shears, diagnostic  equipment  (stethoscope,  blood  pressure  monitor, pulse oximeter, thermometer, etc.), and syringes, along with emergency response drugs (painkillers, adrenaline, etc.).

## Parapsychologist

Parapsychologists  spend  their  time  attempting  to  observe, record,  and  study  the  supernatural.  Sometimes  nicknamed 'ghost hunters,' they generally use technology to try to capture evidence  of  paranormal  activity,  be  it  centred  on  a  person  or a  particular  location.  A  major  portion  of  their  time  is  spent debunking mistaken phenomena or fake mediums rather than recording  actual  evidence.  Having  said  that,  in  the Rivers  of London world there is always the chance they will come across some genuinely 'weird bollocks,' as DCI Alexander Seawoll has been known to call anything associated with the demi-monde 10 .

Some  parapsychologists specialise in the study of a particular  phenomenon,  such  as  extra  sensory  perception, telekinesis,  hauntings,  etc.  Prestigious  universities  offering courses on parapsychology are few and far between, and even where  they  do,  such  courses  are  often  unaccredited.  There are  many  online  degrees  available  in  the  subject,  though. Several  universities  do  have  parapsychology  units,  such  as Edinburgh  University,  and  there  are  independent  research facilities,  such  as  the  Rhine  Research  Centre  in  Durham, North  Carolina.  Standards  in  the  field  are  often  based entirely upon personal reputation, and so the most acceptable representatives  tend  to  hold  degrees  in  related  areas-e.g., physics, psychology, or medicine.

Those who choose this path are unusually sympathetic to the notion of invisible mystical powers, while also seeking to validate that belief to the satisfaction of physical scientists. This  situation  represents  an  unusual  cohabitation  of  faith and  doubt,  and  the  parapsychologist  may  have  difficulty separating  their  conflicting  desires.  A  person  uninterested in observation, experimentation, and proof is not a parapsychologist, though they may be an occultist.

Required skills: Occult, Read Person.

Recommended  skills: Art/Craft  (Photography),  History, Languages, Science (Anthropology), Sense Vestigia.

Suggested contacts: universities, parapsychological publications.

Equipment: notebook, pens, flashlight, EMF magnetic field detector, dowsing rods, infrared thermal imager, hand-held recording equipment.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000144_8c9b13529a1763860ae1948252e059b9af3b651de5d0b1de48899b05e5e2dcc2.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000145_db33fddfb290340c5af8a8185777d2ea00d28d6b3025fb3b34c94ed66d35c89a.png)

## Police Officer/Detective (Rivers)

This occupation covers all walks of policing, from uniformed officers-like Detective Constables Judith Hua and Francis Austin 11 -to  plainclothes  detectives,  like  Peter  Grant  and DCI Thomas Nightingale.

Uniformed police  officers  are  employed  to  maintain  law and order-usually at street level if a member of the lower ranks-wherever there is human habitation, e.g., towns and cities,  although rural officers do exist. The job may require them to be on foot, behind the wheel of a patrol car, or sitting at a desk (e.g., the Case Progression Unit 12 ).

The  plainclothes  branches  of  police  forces  examine  crime scenes, gather evidence, conduct interviews, and try to solve homicides,  major  burglaries,  and  other  crimes.  If  a  junior detective,  they  work  the  streets  in  search  of  information, often in cooperation with a uniformed patrol officer. The more senior the detective, the more likely they are to be a manager, coordinating  a  number  of  staff  on  important  investigations, but rarely enjoying the luxury of concentrating on a single case.

Detectives  sort  truth  from  lies  through  evidence  and reconstruction,  and  their  crucial  function  is  to  marshal enough evidence to facilitate an arrest, in turn leading to a successful criminal prosecution and conviction.

The qualifications required to become a police officer, and subsequently a detective, vary by country. More information can  be  found  in Chapter  5 in  the Training section  for

## The Four Es

British  police  officers  are  trained  to de-escalate situations before resorting to physical intervention, using what is known as the 'Four Es' model:

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000146_682788314ad5513da8bfbfc142efaa922e32b6aa7e97cc70afd6427b510453df.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000147_e95dd70652c99414cce9738376cc7773869755336b065049d70d34f0d397d751.png)

- Engage: the officer verbally establishes their awareness and understanding of the situation with the other person or persons.
- Explain: the officer explains the situation to the person or persons involved with dignity and respect.
- Encourage: the officer encourages reasonable behaviour/compliance, reinforced with further explanation, if required.
- Enforce: if the previous three steps fail to resolve the situation, the officer must then escalate to physical intervention, which may involve restraint and/or arrest.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000148_e88617bbbeeb3ead4f8258ff3f0f28a504d991735eb94fc2b894fec156b557fe.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000149_7a6c5be2752d596ef5de5f410df08342edcc7b07d66a395c92b58c3e8688922e.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000150_43a9591aa599ab317500b03f38b60df54db1715a90bc1fa614ee013a48a2883a.png)

Footnotes: 11 Detective Stories , 12 Rivers of London

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000151_f45146f017e99118f7b85b81ef2ed322311b0d6a3bf48add85cb4afd86ea877b.png)

1

London-based police officers  (page  199).  Once  on  the  job, further  training  and  qualifications  lead  to  promotion  in either the uniformed or detective branches.

UK  police  officers  are  trained  to  de-escalate  situations verbally and use physical restraint as a last resort ( The Four Es , nearby). To reinforce this, police officers in uniform, or any off-duty or plainclothes officer who shows their warrant card or police badge, should be granted a bonus die on Social skill  rolls  when  dealing  with  law-abiding  members  of  the public. Note: uniformed police officers who join the ranks of the Folly, even if only on a temporary basis, are expected to swap their uniforms for plainclothes.

## Required skills: Law.

Recommended skills: Athletics,  Drive,  Fighting,  Firearms, Navigate, Observation, Read Person, Research, Social, Stealth. Suggested contacts: law enforcement, firefighters, community  leaders, informers, reporters, the homeless, criminals (especially repeat offenders).

Equipment: off  duty,  warrant  card  (UK)  or  police  badge (US);  if  in  uniform  on  duty  in  the  UK,  stab  vest  and  a utility  belt  holding  handcuffs,  Airwave  radio,  PAVA  spray (incapacitant), baton, and a PPE pouch (gloves, CPR mask). Non-uniformed officers may forego the belt, but will usually have a hidden stab vest, radio, incapacitant, and PPE pouch somewhere on their person.

## Private Investigator

The  private  investigator,  often  referred  to  in  fiction  as  a 'private  eye,'  is  a  freelance  detective  who  usually  acts  in non-police  situations  to  gather  information  and  evidence on  behalf  of  private  clients.  Their  work  may  be  connected to  impending civil cases,  tracking  down fleeing  spouses  or business partners, or acting as an agent for private defence attorneys in criminal cases. Like any professional, the private investigator separates their personal feelings from the job at hand and cheerfully works for the guilty and innocent alike, as long as their fee is paid.

Many  private  investigators  were  police  officers  earlier in  their  career  and  aren't  afraid  to  make  good  use  of  old connections  now  they're  no  longer  on  the  force;  however, this is not always the case, and some private detectives have no  previous  police  experience.  In  most  locales,  the  private investigator must be licensed and, if later proven to be guilty of  illegal  activity,  the  licence  may  be  revoked-effectively ending the private eye's career.

Required skills: Law, Research.

Recommended skills: Art/Craft (Photography), Computer Use, Disguise, Drive, Locksmith, Observation, Read Person, Social, Stealth.

Suggested contacts: law enforcement, clients.

Equipment: notebook,  pens,  camera,  covert  surveillance equipment, PI licence, laptop, mobile phone.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000152_b4ae50811f14e5d83dc324f874e7afcd35806d94f7fd552debe4b71b1746953c.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000153_b4a4cb0d9ea670346d6ff7ee416a5c75f19fd466c7004eb93bd11fe1a485a573.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000154_509a1e1f2fffac273ca9a24570146693c668fbcd742c58eb679020895170462f.png)

## The National Crime Agency

Founded  in  2013,  the  NCA  heads  the UK's  fight  against  serious  and  organised crime. It is separate to the police but does employ attested constables, among  a  range  of  other  personnel from a wide variety of specialisms and backgrounds.  Not  only  does  it  investigate  serious  crime  and  offer  victim support, but it also provides specialist advice,  support  services,  and  personnel  to  individual  police  forces-such as forensic specialist advisers, as well as hosting several important databases, including the National Injuries Database (page 210). Its personnel are referred to as 'Officers.'

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000155_7ae6e663977a48e664afc9638c354743f24df7184755caf7dd25ccf52f89ef45.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000156_46db49be849ff25892951fae6cf7bda84b9fceb82b2d1acc26c77f77e268165d.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000157_dc627fe1127191c3f27c6b993dea1f308ca5ef2388a8a01411ae2683b2afeb7b.png)

## Service Member (Rivers)

The term 'service member' is used in RoL:RPG as a general term  for  anyone  in  the  military,  including  the  army,  navy, and air force.

All  members  of  the  military  undergo  a  similar  basic training-'boot camp'-where  new  recruits are taught how to march, shoot, salute, and the beginnings of how to navigate  military  bureaucracy.  On  graduation  from  basic training  they  move  on  to  trades  training,  which,  in  the case of the army, can be anything from the combat skills of the  infantry  to  the  explosive  talents  of  engineers  to  highly technical skills as found in the signals. Some units or corps even have specific names for their members such as sapper (Royal  Engineers),  gunner  (Royal  Artillery),  or  trooper (Armoured Corps regiments).
