The most recognised units are of course the frontline ones, and specifically the infantry, but they only make up about one in ten service members. Service members are a diverse and uniquely skilled group that is hard to pin down to a single set of commonalities, save that they all serve in the military.

It is assumed that investigators who take this occupation have  recently  left  active  military  service,  either  because they have retired or their term of enlistment was complete; however, full active service personnel may be permitted, with the GM's approval. Alternatively, an investigator may be a reservist,  in  which  case  they  should  refer  to  the  skills  and equipment associated with their main occupation first.

Required skills: Athletics, Fighting, Firearms.

Recommended  skills: Languages, Mechanical Repair, Navigate,  Observation,  Stealth,  Survival,  or  any  skill(s) appropriate to their previous military career.

Suggested  contacts: ex-service  members,  such  as  those who  have  joined  the  police;  members  of  the  intelligence community; ' My mate who's in… '  ex-service  members who are now involved in strange or unusual occupations and may be international; hometown friend(s).

Equipment: varies depending  on the service and the investigator's role within that service, and what they brought away  with  them  when  they  left  it;  for  example,  a  serving military officer may have been able to carry a firearm while on active duty, but it is unlikely they'd have been allowed to take that weapon with them when they returned to civilian life (particularly in the UK).

## Social Worker (Rivers)

Social workers assist individuals and families to ensure their personal and social welfare. Their goal is to improve people's lives and to safeguard the vulnerable. Their work may take them into schools, hospitals, and private homes or institutions. The occupation divides into a number of areas, working with either vulnerable adults or children and families.

Being a social  worker  can  be  a  demanding  and  stressful occupation;  they  are  often  called  upon  to  make  tough decisions about people's lives. Their work deals directly with young  offenders,  drug  abusers,  the  homeless,  those  with mental  health  issues,  people  with  physical  disabilities,  the elderly, and so on. A day in the life of a social worker may include conducting interviews, writing assessments, liaising with community and government organisations, and giving evidence in court. No two days are likely to be the same.

Service members are commonly deployed for six months to a year to a variety of locations around the world, and thus may have experience and understanding of other cultures and languages.  Depending  on  their  specific  role,  an  individual service member's expertise may focus upon combat, engineering, human resources, IT, logistics, or medicine.

Required skills:

Social.

Recommended skills: Observation, Research.

Suggested contacts: families and individuals who have been involved with social services, police, doctors.

Equipment: photo  ID  card,  mobile  phone,  case  notes, notebook, pen.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000158_a82f9380ca0e7f2207a2977ea0fbf34ea6ce23a4593f3623c5ab9852698886bd.png)

1

## Special Agent (Rivers)

Special  agents  are  trained  and  employed  by  a  government to investigate major crimes. They may work at a regional or national level. The complexities of their remit and authority are specific to each country and organisation, of which there are many. For example, in the US, a special agent may work for the FBI, the CIA, the FDA, the DHS, and so on. In the UK, they are most likely to work for the National Crime Agency (NCA),  the  National  Counter  Terrorism  Security  Office (NaCTSO), MI5, or MI6 (the Secret Intelligence Service).

Special  agents  have  a  good  understanding  of  law  and procedure,  as  well  as  being  trained  in  combat  and  the  use of  technical  equipment  (surveillance  equipment,  etc.)  if they're a 'field agent' as opposed to an analyst or surveillance specialist. Agents are sometimes trained at special academies, such as Quantico for the FBI in the USA.

## Required skills: Law.

Recommended skills: Athletics, Drive, Fighting, Firearms,  Navigate,  Observation,  Read  Person,  Research, Social, Stealth.

Suggested contacts: law enforcement, government organisations.

Equipment: photo  ID  card;  anything  else  depends  on  the agency they work for, but usually includes a registered firearm of some sort, along with access to some form of body armour.

## Tradesperson (Rivers)

A tradesperson (craftsperson) is someone whose occupation requires particular training and experience. An apprenticeship is  a  common  route  into  becoming  a  tradesperson.  Some individuals may work for themselves, such as a plumber who undertakes household maintenance, while others work as part of a team or are employed by a company. The range of jobs is too many to list, but includes builder, plumber, electrician, metal worker, carpenter, decorator, and so on.

Required skills: Art/Craft  specialism  (or  an  alternative  if one from the list of expert skills seems more appropriate).

Recommended skills: Observation.

Suggested  contacts: other  tradespeople  in  the  same  or related area of work, trade suppliers, customers.

Equipment: varies  depending on the trade; for example, a carpenter  would  have  access  to  saws,  planes,  screwdrivers, drills, etc., while a plumber would have wrenches, drain rods, chemicals, sealant, etc.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000159_9b38c2247f51ad506884ebc945c2325edcb89be3c42d331eef2fb4735b8e50e0.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000160_916c65de9ed992abc41aa4b8e563d9ce0f66c17966a9e20d3755bbc63b052bd8.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000161_8decaae66ea0f91af1f185121880b9081a5c1ed5fcba58b9efdd81aeab0429a4.png)

## About the Character Sheet

The RoL:RPG  character sheet has various boxes  for  the  player  to  fill  in  during  character creation, e.g., Portrait ( A ), Investigator Info ( B ),  Characteristics  ( C ),  Advantages  ( D ),  Skills ( E ), and so on. Some are straightforward, while others require a little more explanation.

All  the  information  the  player  needs  to access during the course of a game is located on  the  character  sheet's  front  page  ( A-H ). Characteristics and skills have a space to fill in their full and half values. If you are using a downloaded 'autocalc' sheet from www.chaosium.com, it will automatically calculate half values for you once you type in the full value in the space provided.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000162_4577a04ec915a8f611513ae8e7c20c4523e154a2c138983d29ae0bfd7c58436f.png)

The Damage box ( F ) contains check boxes for the  different  damage  conditions: Hurt (page 144), Bloodied (page 144), and Down (page 144). It also contains an Impaired check box ( Impairment , page 118). The GM will let a player know when they need to check these boxes during a game as a result of any damage their investigator suffers during combat.

The spaces for Luck and magic points in the Supplemental Info box ( C )  are  a  little  different-they  are  labelled  'Starting'  and  'Current,' rather than 'Full' and 'Half.' The 'Starting'  space  records  the  original  value,  while the 'Current' space records how that changes during  a  game.  When  it  comes  to  Luck,  the 'Starting'  value  should  be  updated  after every session of play ( Recovering Luck Points , page 115). The starting value for magic points should be updated every time an investigator masters a new spell ( Magic Points and Spells, page 172).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000163_c1b2ace90c10ef78a5bba7e4521968d354edadcecbc765236dee838395b8f862.png)

The Weapons box ( G ) has space to write in the weapon's name and its associated full and half skill values, the damage it inflicts, and its range (if applicable). The Magic Spells box ( H ) has a space to write down a spell's order and its name. The check box next to a spell should be ticked if it has been mastered. There is also additional space for spells on the back of the character sheet.

The  character  sheet's  back  page  contains boxes  to  record  the  investigator's  name  and occupation  ( I ),  contacts  ( J ), signare  ( K ),  backstory ( L ), equipment ( M ), and case file notes ( N ). The Contacts box is the only one that needs a little more explanation. If the investigator's relationship with a particular contact is good, the player should tick the 'Secure' check box. If, on the other hand, the investigator has done something  to  hurt  their  relationship  ( Contacts, Connections, and the Social Skill , page 86), they should tick the 'Sour' check box.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000164_6e8c837ad34e8d4b1f400debc49d892d656937c3f9e13abeff2ef24546fd7e26.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000165_5ba5b1994ae7878eb1998ca5aff6ef3263496dd263d48550ca1bed59641b6044.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000166_97e62f4837cc1893f0c13a70dd3a7d201bb6fcae24666335c6df3f51dbdbf3f7.png)

1

## THE CHARACTER SHEET: SUMMARY OF CHARACTER CREATION

## DETERMINE OCCUPATION :1

Pick an occupation from the list on page 45 and write it in the space provided. Further details can be found in the Occupations section (page 62).

## ASSIGN CHARACTERISTICS 2

Distribute 280 characteristic points among: STR, CON, DEX, INT, and POW.

- Assign each characteristic a value from 30 to 80. The higher the value, the stronger the characteristic.
- Allocate characteristic values in blocks of 10 (30, 40, 50, 60, 70, or 80) and write them on the character sheet. An average human characteristic value is 50.
- Calculate the half values for each characteristic and write these down (e.g., the half value of STR 40 is 20). The 'autocalc' character sheet calculates these half values for you.

Roll 2D10, add the results together, then add this total to 50 to generate your Luck value. Write this in the space for 'Starting Luck.'

Your default MOV rating is 8 (unless you choose the Speedy advantage in Step Three ).

Finally: choose your investigator's age (page 49) and write it on the character sheet.

## DETERMINE SKILLS

All common and combat skills begin at 30%. All expert skills begin at 00% (zero).

Choose a total of six skills; these should include:

- All the skills required by your occupation.
-  Any skills you wish from your occupation's recommended skills.
- Any other skill you wish, whether common, expert, or combat (as long as you have no more than six skills).

Boost any chosen common or combat skills  from  their starting value of 30% to 60%.

Boost any chosen expert skills from their starting value of 00% to 60%.

## ADVANTAGES

3

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000167_b790e0c19f89e7829207038400f86471a957d5123025ee9983082cb528f5fd17.png)

Choose two minor or one major advantage for your investigator (page 50).

- Make sure that your investigator has the necessary prerequisites to take that advantage.
- If you choose the Speedy advantage, remember to adjust your MOV rating to 9.

Note: investigators who do not choose the Magical advantage cannot choose Magic as a skill in Step Four .

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000168_a1b023a55ff1e2703530702d6a8902d9e0834b5e6db4ce713c2ef0855e81caf8.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000169_8a5fcc96ca470cb1f356874f3d76c2c5f31f137452dcdbdf8ea5e7c0eb229f90.png)

4

Write  the  skill  values  for  chosen  common  and  combat skills (60%) in the space provided, then write down their half values (30%). The 'autocalc' character sheet calculates these half values for you.

Write the chosen expert skills in the space provided, along with their full value (60%) and their half value (30%).

An investigator's own language is a free skill  (the same value as their INT or 60%, whichever is higher). If from a bilingual family, they also receive their second language at the  same  value  (page  52).  Write  language  skills  in  the space provided for expert skills, and add their full and half values.

If  you  chose  the  Magical  advantage,  boost  your  Sense Vestigia skill to 60%.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000170_e27c0c6042c0a8bf936ef60bb2b06115594f085b05f6fd9e0e6a3708bd3d744d.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000171_7f2191942e4aa0dab84efb2e8c9f4e55c4edf6c740dd8ab9f80da342a389c151.png)

## MAGIC

If  you  chose  the  Magic  skill  during Step  Four: Determine Skills (page 52) and your investigator is a Newtonian  apprentice, you start the game  with Werelight as one of your first order spells.

- Pick one other first order spell and one second order spell from the Spell List (page 179).
- Mark Werelight as your mastered spell by ticking the box next to the spell's name ( Mastering a Spell , page 171).

If you chose the Magic skill and your investigator is a

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000172_1854b6615670b484601cc3296afdfc35a513431616d5aa39908ee710b8704472.png)

## MORE MAGIC

Look at Table 10: Signare (page 165) and choose one element from each column. Alternatively, roll 1D100 three times (one for each column).

-  Choose two signare to keep and discard the third. Or, just create your own signare .
- If your investigator is being trained by a character with  a  known signare ,  you  should  incorporate  one aspect from that into your own as a third element.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000173_fc3e9a595f94116b1c666216b61ba91d98cc9833f6a2acb412681b5591462b07.png)

hedge  wizard, c hoose  two  first  order  spells  and  one second order spell from the Spell List (page 179).

- Mark  one  of y our  first  order  spells  as  mastered b y ticking  the  box  next  to  the  spell's  name  ( Mastering a Spell , page 171).

Divide  your  POW  characteristic  by 5  t o  determine number of magic points, then add 1 for your mastered spell. Write  this  value  in  the  space  for 'Starting  Magic Points.'

Now go to the More Magic box.

## BACKSTORY

5

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000174_b0b2e189e9ac7e615cc69a758047b1b4f78f97e1cd7ad722ab820d61c9926114.png)

Answer the questions about your Childhood (page 56) and The Present (page 57), then write these answers in the space provided.

Determine if your investigator has had a supernatural encounter ( Brush with the Supernatural (Optional) , page 57). If so, write down the details.

Next, determine their Affluence Rating (page 57) and write it in the space provided in the Investigator Info box.

Finally, write down the names of one or more friends or family members your investigator can rely on. This is the final step of creating your backstory.

- If you took the Connected advantage (page 50) during Step Three: Advantages , you can add names here, too-although you can wait until later if you haven't worked out who they are yet.

## EQUIP THE CHARACTER

6

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000175_4ae05fe96d1bb7c6659ff644f56186fa45ed6bd9fec51595124401a73171eaf8.png)

Determine the equipment your investigator has as part of  their  occupation  (page  59)  and  write  it  on  the character  sheet.  Any w eapons  the  investigator  has should be written down in the Weapons box.

The investigator may purchase other equipment with the  GM's  permission,  as  long  as  it  fits  within  their Living Standard ( Table 2: Living Standards , page 58).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000176_9f1dde6ffabd5fbd0d79fa373750e8dfa0858904527046c32428684bef4aa088.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000177_4508ab96d7c5261f1ac5fb6765bce70515eb0829319f27e1ac88bb45efd7a94c.png)

THIS CHAPTER LOOKS at skills in Rivers of London: the Roleplaying Game and provides definitions of what each skill is and how they're used within the game.

Skills  in  the  game  are  divided  into  three  core  types: common,  expert,  and  combat.  The  skill  descriptions  in this  chapter  have  been  organised  the  same  way,  with  all the common skills grouped together, as are the expert and combat  skills.  For  an  overview  of  the  three  groups  and their respective skills, see the Skill List (page 81). In terms of  describing  skills  mechanically,  the  entries  detail  what constitutes a 'Regular' or 'Hard' difficulty level for a skill, whether another skill can oppose its use, and what 'pushing' that skill roll might look like-along with some suggested consequences for failing a pushed roll. See also A Few Notes on Skills (following) for further details.

The entries aren't meant to be an in-depth discussion of every possible thing someone with that skill could get up to, but  rather  act  as  guidelines  for  both  the  Game  Moderator (GM) and players.  There  will  be  times  in  the  game  when specific-quite probably unusual-situations arise, requiring the GM to make a judgement call about how a certain skill can be used. Some suggestions on using each skill under such special circumstances are included in the skill descriptions to help the GM make a judgement call. As always, GMs and players  should  listen  to  each  other's  suggestions  regarding anything not covered by a particular skill description, with the GM having final say on what a skill does and doesn't do in a particular situation.

## ADDITIONAL RULES

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000178_b23f77cde5e9ff7902d6cc782358d5c913c0d5a26b8a000193036ea5453f7dd9.png)

## A Few Notes on Skills

As noted in character creation, all skills are given a numeric percentile value-the higher, the better. For example, a skill value of 50% means that the investigator has a 50/50 chance of  succeeding  with  that  skill  every  time  they  try  to  use  it, while someone who has a 75% value in the same skill should succeed three times out of four, dice willing! Simply put, the higher a character's skill  value,  the  more  likely  they  are  to achieve their goal when it comes to rolling the dice.

## Skill Values

As well as determining your chance of success, what else does a  skill's  percentile  value  say  about  how  well  your  character can perform a skill? Here's a rough guide to the general level of ability you get with certain skill values.

- Below 50% -Amateur: possesses some talent or rudimentary training (e.g., hobby level).
- 70%-89% -Expert: advanced expertise (corresponds with a master's degree or PhD).
- 50%-69% -Professional: enough knowledge and ability to eke out a living from the skill (equivalent to a bachelor's degree in a specific subject).
- 90%+ -Master: among the world's best in that particular skill.

As  with  character  occupations,  the  list  of  skills  for RoL:RPG  isn't  exhaustive.  We  think  we've  covered the bases pretty well, but if a player really wants a skill  for  their  investigator that doesn't exist in this chapter,  guidelines  for  how  to  create  it  can  be found on page 321.

Most  skills  are  linked  to  a  character's  occupation  in  some way-they are either required or recommended skills ( Step Four: Determine Skills ,  page  52).  If  an  investigator  increases  their value in a skill unrelated to their occupation over time, their player and GM may wish to discuss switching the investigator to a new occupation that better reflects their areas of new expertise, but it's not a requirement and should really follow from the player wishing to develop their character as the game progresses. Developing a rationale for why the investigator has achieved a high percentile in an unrelated skill helps build more depth to the character and can make an interesting addition to their backstory.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000179_58e0e643affcf1638873883b462d743e80cf25554524efc5b8a1eec9e0b23d8d.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000180_4a87c7d7b833a243a1de899820f2c7cae60e58bf6bc40ca47cbbb8de51852fb9.png)

## Skill Specialisations

Certain skills-Art/Craft, Languages, and Scienceencompass a number of different areas of knowledge, so these broad skills are divided into specialisations. This means that while  you  cannot  select  a  generic  'Science'  skill,  you  can select Science (Astronomy) or Science (Chemistry), and so on.

In play, the player states their goal and may suggest the skill specialisation their character could use to achieve that goal,  with  the  GM  deciding  how  applicable  a  particular specialisation  is  to  the  situation  in  hand.  Usually,  this  is straightforward; i.e., the player wants to analyse some powder found at a crime scene and suggests using their investigator's Science (Chemistry) skill, to which the GM  agrees. But,  sometimes,  an  investigator  may  not  have  the  exact specialisation required, so the GM can allow them to use an alternate specialisation to achieve their goal. In this case, the alternative skill specialisation is rolled at an increased level of difficulty or a penalty die is imposed on the roll ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice ,  page  111)-but  only  if  the  GM  agrees there is sufficient overlap with that specialisation.

Example: the  characters  are  trying  to  crack  a  numerical  code. This calls for a Science (Cryptography) skill roll. An investigator lacking Science (Cryptography) asks if they can use their Science (Mathematics)  skill  instead.  The  GM  allows  the  roll  at  an increased level of difficulty, as there is a sufficient overlap between the two skills. A Regular success was required when using Science (Cryptography), i.e., a roll equal to or below the full skill value. So,  a  Hard  success-a  roll  equal  to  or  below  half  of  the  skill's value-is required when using Science (Mathematics) in its place.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000181_600532a6ffec3e587cd82025485f52543083f985d4f85f655027784ac784d46a.png)

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

Each  skill  entry  tells  you  which  skill  (if  any)  works  in opposition  to  it,  as  well  as  giving  suggestions  as  to  what constitutes a Regular and Hard difficulty level. The Regular difficulty level (requiring a roll of equal to or below the skill's value) is the default roll. Where things are significantly more difficult, a Hard difficulty level (requiring a roll of equal to or below half of the skill's value) may be required. The GM should rarely call for a Hard difficulty roll-i.e., only in the most challenging situations. For other factors that may affect a skill roll, see Determining the Difficulty Level (page 105) and Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice (page 111).

## Some examples of where a Hard difficulty might be called:

- Demolitions: attempting to defuse an explosive device of a radically different design to any seen before, perhaps of futuristic origin.
- Observation: eavesdropping on a whispered conversation.
- Tech: working on weird or arcane technology that uses an unfamiliar form of electronics.
- Locksmith: opening a security lock with only a piece of wire (i.e., no proper tools); cracking open a highsecurity lock.
- Read Person: reading someone's intent or determining that a person is lying if their relevant opposing Social skill is 70% or above (or, you could use an opposed roll for this instead; Opposed Skill Rolls , page 116).
- Medicine: diagnosis and treatment of strange, new medical ailments.

## Pushing Skills

Examples are provided for each skill regarding what might constitute  a  pushed  roll  ( Pushing  the  Roll ,  page  108), along with some of the possible consequences for failing a pushed roll. As with their respective skill descriptions, these suggestions are guidelines for the GM and do not cover every possible occasion where a roll could be pushed. The latitude for  what  could  justify  a  pushed  roll  is  wide  and  should  be determined by the investigator's actions and motivations, as well as what's going on in the game at the time.

Likewise,  the  consequences  of  a  failed  pushed  roll  are best decided by taking inspiration  from  current  game events, the environment, and non-player characters (NPCs). The  examples  merely  provide  ideas  and  it  is  intended  that both players and GMs fashion their own justifications and consequences as appropriate to their games and playing styles.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000182_39eca9b17edd834c5b39b6a9d8338f12498ffeaba0bd7b783c2ac5af8532499b.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000183_4f4af6c3df1b3ecedd5ed3f7462f201785944e1aac608fbb7d2e8a5e763e6535.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000184_8baf02eb3b27f250818a7a18c6cbe24a8877090fb4a74d77bd52237af9b02bdb.png)

## Skill List

## Common Skills

Athletics

Drive

Navigate

Observation

Read Person

Research

Sense Vestigia

Social

Stealth

## Expert Skills

Accounting

Acting-see Art/Craft

Animal Handling

## Combat Skills

Fighting

Firearms

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000185_4e502084c8b2332f2f44164a7abc459f46591dffa342b69376c9b99fb0e7519e.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000188_49f983dc96c316a71b88a1724ecfba078111d0dadfe6e422d6bfe3c182563118.png)

2

## Common Skills

This section describes each of the nine common skills that all investigators can use. Where a common skill has been chosen as one of an investigator's six starting skills during character creation ( Step Four: Determine Skills , page 52), its starting value  is  boosted  to  60%.  Otherwise,  non-chosen  common skills start at 30%.

## Athletics

A general athletic ability encompassing climbing, jumping, swimming, and throwing, along with other field event and gymnastic skills. Simple feats of strength can be performed using the STR characteristic or the Athletics skill, whichever is  higher. Simple sprints can be performed using the DEX characteristic or the Athletics skill, whichever is higher.

- Using Athletics to climb: allows a character to climb trees, walls, and other vertical surfaces with or without ropes and climbing gear, and also encompasses rappelling and abseiling. Conditions such as firmness of surface, available handholds, wind, visibility, rain, etc., may all affect the difficulty level. Failing this skill on the first roll indicates that the climb is perhaps beyond the character's capability. Failing a pushed roll is likely to indicate a fall with resultant damage or becoming stuck and unable to go up or down. One successful Athletics roll allows the character to complete the climb in almost all cases (rather than requiring repeated rolls), with a challenging or longer climb having an increased difficulty level (again, rather than needing repeated rolls).
- Using Athletics to jump: a character may leap up or down vertically, or jump horizontally from a standing or running start. It's up to the GM to decide what constitutes a Regular difficulty jump or a Hard one. As a guide, a Regular success is required for a character to safely leap down vertically a distance equal to their own height, jump horizontally from a standing start across a gap as wide as the jumper's own height, or run and then jump horizontally a distance of twice the jumper's own height. A character might achieve double these distances with a Hard success, although bear in mind the world record for the long jump is around 9 metres/yards.
- Using Athletics to throw: use Athletics to hit a target with an object. A palm-sized object of reasonable balance can be hurled a distance of up to STR divided by 5 in metres/yards. The effective range can be extended up to STR divided by 2 in metres/yards, but only if a penalty die is applied to the Athletics roll ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111). If the object being thrown is heavy-over 2 kg (5 lbs)-the range given above should be halved. If the Athletics roll fails, the object lands at some random distance from the target. The GM should compare the result of the dice roll to the highest number which would have indicated success, then choose a distance between the target and the thrown object that feels comparable. The Athletics skill is used in combat when making thrown attacks, such as with knives, spears, grenades-or even a tin of baked beans!

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty: a task that would challenge the average person, such as: climbing a drainpipe; running and then jumping horizontally to a distance of twice the jumper's own height; swimming in a fast-moving current; throwing a basketball through a hoop.
- Hard difficulty: a task that would challenge a professional sportsperson, such as: climbing surfaces slick with rain; attempting to jump from a rooftop and through the window of a neighbouring building; swimming against a rapid current while fully clothed; throwing a basketball through a hoop from centre court.

## Pushing Examples

Reassessing  the  task;  taking  a  longer  route;  putting  every ounce of strength into the task and risking injury. In some instances, a pushed roll is not possible:

- Using Athletics to break a fall: when falling, Athletics may be used to lessen the potential fall damage. If falling from a height, a successful roll prepares for the fall, reducing any damage incurred by half (round down).
- Once an item has been thrown, it is quite literally out of the character's hands and therefore there's nothing the player can do to justify pushing the roll. If there are multiple missiles available and it's only a matter of time until the character succeeds, then there's no risk connected with failure, so there's no need to roll dice. If multiple missiles are available and there is a distinct risk connected with failure, then the GM might ask for a pushed roll if the character fails their first roll.
- If a character tries to leap an abyss and their first roll fails, they cannot have a second attempt-for obvious reasons! Although they do have the option of spending Luck to succeed instead ( Luck , page 113). However, if a character is trapped in a pit and jumping or climbing up to escape, they could have any number of attempts, summarised with one pushed skill roll.
- Using Athletics to swim: only roll Athletics in times of crisis or danger, or when the GM judges it appropriate. Failing a pushed Athletics roll when swimming may lead to the person being washed away downstream, or even partially or completely drowned ( Table 7: Other Forms of Damage , page 149).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000189_6cd103b1e65891c8aed2cc62cd69aa2c15ba4ac70b459113bfdec35e57348df6.png)

Sample  consequences  of  failing  a  pushed  roll: physical harm, or you drop or break something of value; if swimming, the  current  pulls  you  under  and  you  lose  consciousness, awaking at an unknown location.

## Drive

Anyone with this skill can drive a car or light truck, make ordinary manoeuvres, and cope with ordinary vehicle problems.  If  the  character  wants  to  lose  a  pursuer  or  tail someone, a Drive roll would be appropriate. This skill can also be used to operate other more unusual vehicles or machinery, such  as  tractors,  18-wheelers,  and  construction  machinery (such as a crane or forklift). Note that such unusual vehicles are very different to cars and unless the use of the specific machine fits with the character's occupation, any attempts to operate such things is made with a penalty die ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111).

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty: to successfully weave through light traffic at speed to avoid pursuit.

## Pushing Examples

Driving the vehicle to its limit; not slowing down, regardless of the risk.

Sample  consequences  of  failing  a  pushed  roll: crashing; skidding to a halt, unable to proceed; being pursued by the police.

## Navigate

Allows the user to find their way in clear weather or storms, at  night  or  during  the  day.  Those  with  a  higher  skill  value are  familiar  with  astronomical  tables,  charts,  instruments, and satellite  location  gear.  One  could  also  use  this  skill  to measure and map an area (cartography) or use the skill  to read  detailed  topographical  maps.  If  a  character  has The Knowledge advantage (page 52), a bonus die ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111) should be granted to the roll if they are in their specified area.

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty: keep on the right route and find your destination in your own city.
- Hard difficulty: to successfully weave through heavy traffic at speed to avoid pursuit.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000190_a1921a39e6e802267b41f4deb9265b4da5de3ab500355cec35ca92d8ccaca974.png)

83

2

- Hard difficulty: head in the right direction when there is no clear route or landmarks; find your way in a totally unfamiliar city.

## Pushing Examples

Getting a map out and delaying while you attempt to work out where you are; going back to where you started and trying again; asking a stranger for directions.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: you get lost and find yourself being watched or ambushed by a fox who wants your cheese puffs; you go around in circles and your companions stop following you-you're on your own now.

## Observation

Covers what might be detected by a person's mundane senses: sight,  smell,  sound,  taste,  and  touch.  For  example,  finding a  secret  door  or  compartment,  noticing  a  hidden  intruder, spotting  an  inconspicuous  clue,  or  becoming  aware  of ambushers; equally, they might taste that a drink has been spiked, eavesdrop on a whispered conversation, or smell the faint scent of yesterday's perfume upon someone.

If a character has only a fleeting chance  to notice something,  perhaps  while  running  past  it  or  otherwise distracted, the GM may raise the difficulty level. Where the character  is  conducting  a  thorough  search,  the  GM  could award an automatic success. The difficulty level might also be adapted to reflect the environment, it being harder to spot something small in a cluttered room.

A high Observation skill value can indicate a good level of general awareness. This skill can be rolled with a penalty die to read lips, if the character lacks the expert skill of Read Lips (page 96; Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111).

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

Observation  can  be  opposed  by  Stealth  when  someone  is creeping  up  on  you.  Observation  can  also  be  used  to  see through  a  deception  if  an  opponent  is  using  the  Disguise skill,  e.g.,  to  notice  some  element  of  the  disguise  that isn't quite right.

- Regular difficulty: searching a cluttered room for clues; hearing something approaching you (when its Stealth skill is below 70%); eavesdropping on a nearby conversation.
- Hard difficulty: searching for something that has been purposefully well hidden; hearing something creep up on you (when its Stealth skill is 70% or over); eavesdropping on a whispered conversation.

## Pushing examples

Taking  more  time  to  observe  the  area;  pulling  the  place apart; dismantling the furniture and smashing the porcelain statuettes  to  find  the  hidden  object;  telling  everyone  to  be quiet-or making a noise yourself-and then listening.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: inadvertently drop something of your own while searching, which makes it clear that you have been there (the character will not realise this at the time); the enemy has caught you unawares (you are surprised); you misheard the conversation and get the facts wrong; you are caught trying to eavesdrop and find yourself in a very embarrassing predicament.

## Read Person

Read Person is a perception-based skill that allows the user to study an individual and so form an idea of their motives and character, e.g., is that person bluffing or are they really going to do what they've just threatened?

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

Read Person can be used to oppose the Social skill, especially when Social  is  being  used  for  deception.  Read  Person  can also  be  used  to  penetrate  someone's  disguise,  i.e.,  when  an opponent is using the Disguise skill.

- Regular difficulty: read someone's intent or determine if the person is lying if their Social skill is below 70%.
- Hard difficulty: read someone's intent or determine if the person is lying if their Social skill is 70% or above.

## Pushing Examples

Being very unsubtle and asking highly personal, intimate, or downright rude questions of the target; immersing yourself in data regarding a specific person (the target of your study) in order to think like them and understand their motivations.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: you somehow reveal  your  own  motivations  to  the  target;  the  target  is offended by your prying questions and refuses to speak with you further, or takes action against you-perhaps violence or a call to the police; unknown to you, the target is connected with your adversaries and you unwittingly reveal your agenda to them; spending all week locked in your room in order to build the profile has left you a physical and mental wreck, inflicting a penalty die to all rolls until you rest and recover ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111).

## Research

Covers locating information using the internet, a library, or some other resource. The GM should consider the information sought  and  the  resource  used,  and  specify  a  reasonable

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000191_378c89cc3903eb5b204d56ce320e2f79b70f8ebc37de6700d496b8d32a74805f.png)

amount of time that the task will take. If the dice roll is equal to or below half the character's Research skill (i.e., a Hard success when only a Regular success was needed), they have found the information in half the time. This skill can locate locked case records or a rare-book special collection, but a Social  skill  roll  or  special  credentials  may  have  to  be  used to get access to the books or information in question. Note that to obtain information from other people uses the Social (page 86) skill, as the Research skill is concerned with nonverbal information gathering.

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty: locating a difficult to find book or piece of information in a library or on the internet.
- Hard difficulty: identifying a piece of information in a disorganised library, or if you are extremely pressed for time.

## Pushing Examples

Pressing someone, like a librarian or IT contact, for assistance; taking longer to systematically work through the stacks or data.

Sample  consequences  of  failing  a  pushed  roll: you  fall asleep over your research; you waste a great deal of time; you find misleading or wrong information, which leads to danger.

## Sense Vestigia

While the Observation skill deals with things that can be detected by a person's mundane senses, this skill detects the imprint  magic  leaves  behind  on  the  physical  environment, what practitioners call Vestigia (page 161).

Most  members  of  the  demi-monde-well,  the  nonhuman ones, at least-have an increased innate sensitivity to vestigia ;  they're  born  with  it  and  it's  why  they  can  'smell' practitioners, as signare and vestigia are closely related ( Signare , page 164). As a result, all fae characters begin with this skill at 60%. Practitioners can also train their senses to detect and understand the vestigia around them. For human investigators,  those  who  take  the Magic skill  (page  93)  as one of their six starting skills automatically boost their Sense Vestigia skill to 60% (for free).

Vestigia are usually made up of several distinct elements, and detecting these may require a Sense Vestigia roll. Note that  most vestigia encountered  is  assumed  to  be  'strong,' requiring  a  Regular  success  to  detect  all  of  the vestigia's elements.  In  some  cases,  the vestigia may  be  considered 'weak,' requiring a Hard success to detect all of the vestigia's elements. When 'extremely strong vestigia ' is encountered, usually no skill roll is necessary to detect it.

85

The very act of  asking  for  a  Sense  Vestigia  roll  tells  the players that their investigators are sensing something strange.  Whether  they  are  able  to  accurately  identify  the vestigia depends on their dice roll. At the GM's discretion, in  the  case  of  strong vestigia ,  they  may  allow  investigators to automatically identify one element present (e.g., either the smell, sound, or sensation that forms the strongest element of the vestigia ), whether they pass or fail their Sense Vestigia roll.  See Vestigia  Strength (page  161)  for  further  details about weak and strong vestigia .

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty: an awareness of the presence of strong vestigia ; a Regular success detects all elements present.
- Hard difficulty: an awareness of the presence of weak vestigia ; a Hard success detects all elements present.

## Pushing Examples

Close your eyes and spend more time attempting to feel the surroundings,  really  concentrating  on  anything  that  feels unusual or out of place; clear everything and everyone out of the location so you are not distracted and can focus solely on the attempt.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: things might happen while you've got your eyes closed! The character might fail to see someone pulling a weapon on them, for example. You misunderstand the element of the vestigia detected.

## Social

Covers  all  forms  of  social  interaction.  The  GM  should encourage  players  to  describe  which  aspect  of  the  Social skill they're using to achieve their goal-logical persuasion, intimidation, seduction, flattery, verbal trickery, or deception. The skill may be used to compel an NPC to act in a certain way, but not in a manner completely contrary to that person's normal behaviour.

Backing up the use of the Social skill with a bribe, gift, or promise (if you are attempting to ingratiate yourself), or with a weapon or some other powerful threat (in the case of intimidation), may reduce the difficulty level or gain a bonus die  on  the  skill  roll  ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice ,  page 111), at the GM's discretion.

If a character has the Silver-Tongued advantage (page 52), they gain a bonus die on Social rolls when using eloquence and persuasion.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000192_65ea8a91eeea33518345a6d0634ef57b89fd6b5731c8c7929c4d780cafbc8b36.png)

## Contacts, Connections, and the Social Skill

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000193_72a134c649b3db5c64aeb91c38594ec8dcbb3f89f97b80fca8676663c051f97c.png)

Each  occupation  has  a  list  of  contacts.  These  are  people  with  whom the investigator may have established a relationship. Contacts can be really useful in game; for example, when the investigators  need  advice  on  where to  go  next  or  if  they're  lacking  a  particular  area  of  expertise.  Not  every interaction with an investigator's contacts requires a Social roll, but, when it does, that roll is made with a bonus die. Note that if a deal leads to a negative  outcome  for  the  contact,  the GM can decide that the relationship is soured and that individual  cannot  be called upon again.

If the investigator has the advantage Connected (page  50),  they  can  gain favours  without  the  need  for  a  Social skill roll with regard to whomever they are  connected.  Again,  if  the  relationship turns sour for whatever reason, a Hard Social skill roll might be required to repair things, otherwise the connection becomes strained and is no longer of value to the investigator.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000194_6446f1265dd75ef2437a04650de66a6aa0783f7f51809b3736a2be55af30831f.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000195_26412dab7efddf80eacd3cf64304cd2673160fb347e7b4b04d0b93781c074fe8.png)

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

When used to deceive someone, Social can be opposed by the  Read  Person  skill.  Where  it  is  used  to  intimidate  or coerce someone, it can be opposed with POW. The opposing skill or characteristic can be used to determine the difficulty level  ( Determining  the  Difficulty  Level ,  page  105),  or the GM may instead call for an Opposed Skill Roll (page 116), depending on the situation. Where the skill is used to persuade someone to act (to do something), the player should state their goal. Then the GM must decide if it is reasonable and if it counts as a Regular or Hard difficulty.

- Regular difficulty: getting someone to do something if their opposing skill is less than 70%, or if it will inconvenience or cost them in some way.
- Hard difficulty: getting someone to do something if their opposing skill is 70% or more, or if it will put something on the line for them, e.g., doing what you ask would risk their job, their safety, their reputation, and so on.

## Pushing Examples

Overtly  flattering  the  target  with  affection;  building  trust by  imparting  a  secret;  using  a  weapon  or  tool  to  enhance intimidation.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: the  target takes offence and will have nothing further to do with you; the target is associated in some way with your enemies and, while they may play along with you, they also inform on you; if  the  target  was  being  threatened  with  a  weapon,  perhaps actual physical harm is done to the target-e.g., accidentally pulling  the  trigger  of  the  gun  you're  waving  at  the  target; the  target  simply  laughs  in  your  face  and  won't  break  no matter what you do; the target somehow turns the tables and intimidates the intimidator; you inadvertently reveal a secret to the target.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000196_1c1d17def5b06bc74c0d970d3aa18d7551d324e664d9810f0f5ada3d6b943dc9.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000197_31456de1d5fa766e5ba6954e74c41fec64486516597aa47cdd91fe84733321a3.png)

## Stealth

The art of moving quietly and hiding without alerting those who might see or hear you. A high skill value suggests either that the character is adept at moving quietly (light-footed) and/ or is skilled in camouflage or covert techniques. The skill also can be a measure of whether a character can maintain a level of patience and cool-headedness to remain still and unseen for long periods. Thus, when attempting to avoid detection, the player should attempt a Stealth skill roll for their investigator.

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

When attempting to sneak or hide, the opponent's Observation skill is used to set the difficulty level for the roll. Situational modifiers may also apply (e.g., darkness or loud noises).  Alternatively, depending on the situation, the GM may call for an Opposed Skill Roll (page 116).

- Regular difficulty: moving quietly around the house to avoid detection by average people, i.e., those with an Observation skill of less than 70%.
- Hard difficulty: sneaking past an attentive guard dog; having only seconds to find a hiding place; trying to sneak past someone with an Observation skill of 70% or above.

## Pushing examples

Taking  more  time  to  study  the  area;  removing  your  shoes or  changing  your  clothing  to  something  more  appropriate; causing a distraction; sitting tight until the coast has cleared.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: unbeknownst to  you,  you  have  been  spotted,  and  your  target(s)  decide to  snoop  on  you,  trap  you,  or  instigate  a  surprise  attack on  you!  The  alarm  is  sounded  and  suddenly  everyone  is looking for you.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000198_5cd4835dd055a3e3447a2ef5e15658d4c3b6e98927a9669b092285b1cd2ce826.png)

87

## Expert Skills

If a character lacks an expert skill, they have nothing beyond a layperson's very basic grasp of the topic; meaning that, under most circumstances, they couldn't attempt the necessary skill roll. If none of the investigators possess a particular expert skill needed when working a case file, they may consult an NPC (for instance, one of their contacts), seek some other source of help, or they may try their Luck ( Trying Your Luck , page 114).

## ADDITIONAL RULES

There  are  a  huge  number  of  expert  skills  in  the real world, and only a small selection of these are described  in  the  following  section.  If  you  wish  to include an expert skill that is not listed here, guidelines for how to create it can be found on page 321.

## Accounting

Grants  an  understanding  of  accountancy  procedures  and can  reveal  the  financial  situation  of  a  business  or  person. Inspecting  ledgers  or  electronic  spreadsheets,  one  might detect  cheated  employees,  siphoned-off  funds,  payment  of bribes  or  blackmail,  and  other  notable  things,  along  with whether the subject's financial condition is better or worse than  publicly  claimed.  Looking  through  old  accounts,  a character could see how money was gained or lost in the past, to whom, and what purchases were made.

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

If someone were trying to conceal something, the difficulty level would be based upon the Accounting skill of the person doing  the  concealing,  i.e.,  if  their  Accounting  skill  was 70% or higher, the roll would be Hard ( Determining the Difficulty Level , page 105).

- Regular difficulty: comprehensive and well-documented set of accounts.
- Hard difficulty: disorganised and partial accounts.

## Pushing Examples

Taking  more  time  to  review  documents;  visiting  banks, businesses, or people to validate findings; double-checking the maths and data.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: discussions between  characters  and  third  parties  alert  some  enemy faction  to  the  characters'  intentions;  a  vital  part  of  the accounts is destroyed or lost, e.g., perhaps in their tired state, the  character  spills  coffee  over  them  or  accidentally  hits the delete key.

## Animal Handling

The ability to command and train animals to perform simple tasks.  The  skill  is  most  commonly  applied  to  domesticated animals like dogs but may include birds, cats, monkeys, and so on-at the GM's discretion. For riding animals, such as horses or camels, the Ride skill is used for breaking in and controlling the mount in question.

Example: while an officer in the Met's Dog Support Unit would have the Animal Handling skill as part of their training and could use it to deal with any troublesome animals they encounter in the course  of  their  duties,  Nafeesa  Jones,  as  a  general  police  officer, doesn't have this expert skill. When she encounters a snarling stray dog in a junk yard she's investigating, she'll have to try her luck ( Trying Your Luck ,  page  114)  to  see  if  she  can  get  the  animal under control and potentially learn a new skill in the process!

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty: getting a trained dog to sit, fetch, or attack on command.
- Hard difficulty: getting an untrained or unfamiliar dog to perform a task on command.

## Pushing Examples

Taking greater personal risk in terms of getting closer to, or directly handling, the animal.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: the  animal attacks  the  trainer  or  someone  else  nearby,  most  likely causing damage; the animal escapes.

## Appraise

Used to  estimate  the  value  of  a  particular  item,  as  well  as to  assess  the  type  and  quality  of  the  materials  used  in  its creation, and the level of workmanship involved. In addition, where relevant, it could pinpoint the age of the item, assess its historical relevance, and detect forgeries.

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

If attempting  to  use Appraise  to  detect forgeries, the difficulty  level  is  set  by  the  creator's  Art/Craft  (Forgery) skill, i.e., if their skill was 70% or higher, the roll would be Hard ( Determining the Difficulty Level , page 105).

- Regular difficulty: an item of common availability at its time of manufacture, made of a common material, e.g., a 50-year-old gold watch.

## Pushing Examples

Checking an item's validity with another expert; conducting testing; researching an item.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: accidentally ruining or damaging the item in question; bringing the item to the attention of other people, leading to its theft; inadvertently activating whatever function the item might serve.

## Art/Craft

The  Art/Craft  skill  covers  a  host  of  specialisations.  What counts as an 'art' or a 'craft' can be something of a fraught question, and may be tied to whether or not the specialisation is  used  to  earn  a  living  versus  being  undertaken  purely  for enjoyment's  sake.  In RoL:RPG ,  we  make  no  distinction between the two, as there is often a substantial overlap.

When taking Art/Craft as a skill during character creation, the player should choose one specialisation to begin at 60% and two other specialisations to each begin at 30%. A generic 'Art/Craft' skill cannot be chosen.

## Examples of Art/Craft Specialisations:

| Acting      | Forgery                 |
|-------------|-------------------------|
| Barbering   | Morris Dancing          |
| Calligraphy | Opera Singing           |
| Carpentry   | Painting and Decorating |
| Cooking     | Photography             |
| Dancing     | Pottery                 |
| Embroidery  | Sculpting               |
| Fine Art    | Writing                 |

The  holder  of  a  specialisation  has  a  breadth  of  knowledge within that particular field, i.e., a knowledge of the subject, its  history,  and  its  contemporary  practitioners,  as  well  as the ability to perform it. Thus, a successful skill roll might provide information about an item, such as where or when it was made, some point of history concerning it, who might have made it, or some insight into the technique used in its construction or performance.

An Art/Craft specialisation enables the making or repair of an item (which includes such things as a musical composition, a stand-up routine, and so on), typically requiring time and the  necessary  equipment,  as  determined  by  the  GM.  In  a situation where gradations of success might be appropriate, a higher level of success indicates the item/performance is of higher quality and/or precision.

- Hard difficulty: an unusual or rare item, perhaps made of uncommon materials, e.g., a foreign-made object several hundred years old, such as an ancient book binding.

An Art/Craft specialisation might also be used to make a  duplicate  or  fake  item.  In  such  cases,  the  difficulty  level would  depend  on  the  intricacy  and  distinctiveness  of  the original piece. In the case of faking documentation, a specific specialisation-Forgery-is used.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000199_450075d2aa937945b632ce3a482eb3a9d89555ef33da6382df92b9634de05dbd.png)

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty: making a standard, saleable piece (item, composition, performance, and so on).
- Hard difficulty: making a higher quality or particularly intricate piece.

## Pushing Examples

Reworking the  object,  piece,  or  composition  from  scratch; conducting further research; checking with another expert.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: a vast amount of time and/or money is wasted in creating a failed attempt; the  audience  or  customer  is  highly  offended  or  physically injured  by  some  aspect  of  the  work;  the  critics  slam  your work, and no one desires your services any longer.

## Examples of Art/Craft Specialisations

Acting: the performer is trained in theatrical and/or film/television acting, and  is able to adopt  a persona, memorise scripts, and utilise stage/movie make-up to alter their appearance.

Fine Art: the  artist  is  accomplished  in  the  art  of  painting (oils,  acrylic,  watercolour),  as  well  as  sketching  in  pencil, crayon,  or  pastels.  While  serious  works  of  art  might  take many  days  or  months  to  complete,  the  artist  may  sketch accurate  impressions,  objects,  and  people  in  next  to  no time. The skill also denotes a familiarity with the art world and  art  history,  and  the  artist  may  be  able  to  determine  a particular artist's work, their school, and known history with a successful skill roll.

Forgery: adept  at  fine  detail,  the  user  can  produce  highquality  fake  documents,  covering  everything  from  a  person's handwriting  to  a  bureaucratic  form  or  permit,  or  a  duplicate of a book. The forger requires suitable materials (inks, grades of  paper,  etc.),  as  well  as  an  original  from  which  to  copy.  A successful  roll  indicates  the  forgery  passes  a  normal,  cursory inspection. Someone spending time and thoroughly examining the forgery would use the Appraise skill (opposed by the original forger's skill) when determining if the fake can be spotted. If forging  something  ancient,  the  Science  (Archaeology)  skill might be employed in place of Appraise to detect the fake.

89

Photography: covers  both  film  and  digital  photography. Regular snapshots do not require a skill roll. Allows the user to take clear pictures, process and develop them (if film), and enhance half-hidden details. The skill extends to cover video cameras, video playback equipment, digital photography, and digital editing, where the user is adept at the manipulation of digital images. In this case, radically different versions can be created from an original source, such as changing the location of a person in an image, who they are with, and what they are doing.  Rolls  would  be  required  to  achieve  effective  candid photographs  or  shots  that  capture  fine  detail-especially at  long  range,  at  speed,  or  in  low  light.  This  skill  can  also allow the user to determine if a photograph or video has been tampered with or fabricated, as well as the angle and position from which a photograph/video was originally taken.

## Computer Use

Covers  programming  in  various  computer  languages,  the retrieval and analysis of obscure data, breaking into a secured system,  exploring  a  complicated  network,  and  detecting or  exploiting  intrusions,  back  doors,  and  viruses.  Special manipulation of a computer system may require this roll, at the GM's discretion. General, everyday use of a computer, such  as  surfing  the  internet,  collecting  email,  or  running commercially available software does not warrant a skill roll.

The internet places a wealth of information at a character's fingertips. The GM may decide that the use of the internet to find highly specific and/or obscure information requires a combined Computer Use and Research dice roll ( Combined Skill Rolls , page 322).

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty: write, analyse, and debug a computer program.
- Hard difficulty: decipher and hack into a secure network without being identified or traced.

## Pushing Examples

Taking  longer  to  develop  a  program;  using  someone  else's code as a shortcut; using untested software, e.g., a virus, to exploit a system.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: accidentally erasing  sought-after  files;  corrupting  the  whole  system; leaving  evidence  or  alerting  others  through  your  actions; infesting your own computer/network with a virus.

## Demolitions

The user is familiar with the safe use of demolition procedures, including setting and defusing explosive charges. Mines and similar devices are designed to be easy to set (no roll required) and more difficult to remove or defuse (usually requiring a roll).  Also  encompasses  military-grade  demolitions,  e.g., anti-personnel mines and plastique.

Given  enough  time  and  resources,  those  proficient  with the Demolitions skill may rig charges to flatten a building, clear  a  blocked  tunnel,  or  repurpose  explosive  devices  in order to construct low-yield charges, booby-traps, and so on.

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty: defuse a common or known type of explosive device; know where to place charges for greatest effect when destroying a bridge or building.
- Hard difficulty: defuse an unfamiliar explosive device.

## Pushing Examples

Taking until the very last second to defuse the bomb; doublechecking  all  the  circuits/connections  by  hand;  conducting research into the construction and design of a device.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: if  defusing or removing an explosive device, the consequence of failing a pushed roll is clear-it explodes! If using the Demolitions skill to place charges, the consequence of failing a pushed roll may be a failure to detonate at the right time-or at all-or that the detonation fails to have the desired effect-either too great or too little.

## Disguise

Used  whenever  someone  wishes  to  present  themselves  as another  person.  The  user  changes  posture,  costume,  and/or voice to enact their disguise, posing as either a specific individual or type of person. Theatrical makeup and/or a fake ID may help; a  successful  Art/Craft  (Acting)  or  Art/Craft  (Forgery) roll may grant a bonus die towards the Disguise roll at the GM's discretion ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111).

## Opposing skill/Difficulty level:

There  are  two  distinct  aspects  to  this  skill:  either  you  are trying to conceal your true identity (such as when the police are  looking  for  you)  or  you  are  impersonating  someone. Disguise can be opposed by Observation and Read Person, either to detect a flaw in the disguise or notice that the person is attempting to pass themselves off as someone they are not.

- Regular difficulty: convince strangers that you are who you say you are; for example, to return to the scene of a crime that you perpetrated yesterday and escape notice.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000200_650fe8dd4def55637cf43299142166986587d621860d4a0b772a68facc738c75.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000201_b51ff19ddaeaf09448be4bafcce5ab2494c7a2681aa5b5353c2e801c087f22fd.png)

- Hard difficulty: convince professionals in a face-to-face meeting that you are who you say you are; for example, to pass as a bank employee to gain access to a bank vault.

Note: for a face-to-face meeting with someone who actually knows  the  person  being  imitated,  this  skill  alone  will  not suffice,  instead  requiring  a  combined  Art/Craft  (Acting) and Disguise skill roll at Hard difficulty ( Combined Skill Rolls , page 322).

## Pushing Examples

Undergoing a fully immersive and intensive preparation, i.e., losing oneself in the role; stealing personal items and utilising them in the disguise; overt exaggeration to confound the target; feigning a sudden attack of illness to disorient the observer.

Sample  consequences  of  failing  a  pushed  roll: being arrested;  causing  offence,  leading  to  violence;  finding  that the person being imitated is wanted by a criminal gang who comes looking for vengeance.

## Diving

To dive safely,  a  scuba  diver  needs  to  be  familiar  with  the physics of diving, air pressure, and the physiological processes that  occur  when  breathing  with  pressurised  air.  Someone with the Diving skill is trained in the use and maintenance of  diving  equipment  for  swimming  underwater,  including emergency  procedures and proper weighting. The skill includes the ability to navigate underwater. Usually, diving in normal conditions requires no skill roll.

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty: diving in challenging conditions; using poorly-maintained equipment.
- Hard difficulty: diving in dangerous conditions; exceeding the depth or time limits.

## Pushing Examples

Pushing the limits of the equipment; methodically doublechecking equipment; gaining professional assistance.

Sample  consequences  of  failing  a  pushed  roll: become trapped  underwater;  attacked  by  sea  creatures;  suffer  the bends (decompression sickness); get lost underwater.

## History

Enables a character to recall significant facts or details about a country, city, region, or person at any point in the subject's history.  A  successful  roll  might  be  used  to  help  identify tools, techniques, or ideas familiar to our ancestors, but little known today.

91

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty: recall some pertinent and useful piece of information.
- Hard difficulty: recall some obscure, highly detailed, and useful piece of information.

## Pushing Examples

Conducting  further  research;  consulting  another  expert; hiring assistants to conduct detailed research.

Sample  consequences  of failing a pushed  roll: your consultations  alert  your  adversaries,  who  become  aware  of your intent; your facts are erroneous and lead you into danger; much time and money is wasted on fruitless research.

## Languages

Like Art/Craft and Science, Languages covers a wide range of  specialisations.  Each  specialisation  represents  the  user's chance to understand, speak, read, and write in a particular language.  A  generic  'Languages'  skill  cannot  be  taken, so  when  choosing  this  skill,  the  exact  language(s)  must be specified.

Everyone  knows  their  mother  tongue  and  automatically receives it at the same value as their INT or 60% (whichever is  higher, Step Four: Determine Skills ,  page  52),  and  if  a character  was  raised  in  a  bilingual  household,  they  receive both relevant languages at the same value (assuming they are equally fluent in them); if they aren't as fluent in the other language,  they  receive  it  at  half  their  INT  value  or  30% (whichever is higher). If you wish for your character to know more languages beyond these starting ones (if your character was raised in a multilingual home, for example), you must select the Language skill when determining your character's skill choices. For clarity, in Chapter 6: A Rogues' Gallery (page 231), an NPC's mother tongue is marked as 'own.'

When taking Languages as a skill (i.e., beyond knowing their mother tongue), the player should choose one specialisation to begin at 60% and two other specialisations to begin at 30% each. An individual can take this skill more than once in order to know more languages. Sign language is a Language skill specialisation.

A successful Languages roll normally allows comprehension of an entire book, presentation, or discussion. No roll is required for reading everyday texts or understanding presentations in your mother tongue. However, if a document is  extremely difficult  to  read  or  is  in  an  archaic  or  unusual dialect,  the  GM  may require a successful roll against your mother  tongue  to  decipher  its  meaning;  the  same  goes for  highly  technical  documents  or  presentations  full  of uncommon subject-specific terminology or jargon.

When a character does not have the required Language specialty, they may roll against their lowest specialism with a penalty die if the languages belong to the same language group,  e.g.,  Romance  languages  ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111).

Note: there  is  no  requirement for your investigator to take Language  (Latin)  if  they  take  Magic  as  a  starting  skill, particularly  if  they're  a  newly-emerged  hedge  wizard  as opposed to a Newtonian apprentice-they can always take it later by spending a development point during an Investigator Development Phase (page 120). In the novels, Peter Grant may be learning Latin (and, later, Greek), but he doesn't need to  know  it  fluently  to  cast  spells,  as  the formae are  taught individually  on  a  need-to-know  basis.  DCI  Nightingale is  merely  teaching  Peter  the  same  way  he  was  taught,  and because he believes it helps his apprentice to understand the structure and process of Newtonian magic.

## Language Proficiency

As we mentioned ( Skill Values , page 79), a skill's percentile value tells you something besides your chances of succeeding at a dice roll. The following applies for Languages specialisations:

- At 30%, transactional requests can be understood, e.g., ' How much is this? ', ' Where is this? '
- At 70%, a character can pass for a native speaker of a foreign language.
- At 50%, a person is fluent in that language.

## Identifying Unknown Languages

- To identify a present-day human language that's unknown to the character, use an INT roll.
- To identify an extinct human language, use a Science (Archaeology) or History roll.

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

Everyday conversation, perhaps with pauses for clarification (when speaking to a less than fluent foreign speaker), does not  require  a  roll  for  those  who  are  fluent.  If  both  parties are sympathetic to each other and there is sufficient time for conversation, most situations should be covered without the need for a dice roll.

Dice  rolls  are  most  likely  to  be  required  where  there  is information or clues that might otherwise be missed. In this respect, the use of the Language skill can be compared to the use of the Observation skill (page 84), in that they can both be used to acquire clues.

- Regular difficulty: conversation (perhaps at speed); written material containing a few technical or uncommon terms.
- Hard difficulty: high content of unusual technical terms; historically archaic phrases; speaker has unusual dialect.

## Pushing Examples

Taking longer to think through the words you want to say; taking  long  pauses  to  answer  what  you  have  been  asked; referencing other books/apps to make a translation.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: exaggerated or  loud  discussions  between  a  character  and  third  parties alerts  an  enemy  faction  to  that  character's  intentions;  a word or phrase is  misunderstood-perhaps the meaning is reversed; the listener takes offence at an unintentional slur and they either respond with their fists or turn everyone in the neighbourhood against you.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000202_509af47f1455f587c401cea6389a8b8c4e53c5164541cc466f30c013280aced6.png)

## Language Groups

There  are  over  7,000  living  languages, spread across nearly 150 language groups.  Each  group  has  comparable linguistics  and  generally  a  common root language.  Some  common  language  groups  are  as  follows-these are  not  exhaustive  lists  and  are  provided as general examples:

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000203_d5901114ef256e7f8da04d306911b7cfbec9c4444b5b0006f6652bf7bfb8f71e.png)

- Romance languages: Spanish, Italian, Portuguese, French.
- Germanic languages: German, English, Dutch, Swedish.
- Afro-Asiatic languages: Egyptian, Hebrew, Arabic, Berber.
- Sino-Tibetan languages: Mandarin Chinese, Burmese, Xiang.
- Slavic languages: Russian, Polish, Czech, Croatian.
- Celtic languages: Irish, Welsh, Cornish, Gaelic.
- Niger-Congo languages: Swahili, Shona, Zulu.
- Indo-Iranian languages: Bengali, Hindi/Urdu, Pashto, Persian, Punjabi.
- Algonquian languages: Cheyenne, Cree, Ojibwe, Mohican.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000204_556cda17d4b7b1a9f2cc800f9aa099abb7278e1b6743a79f2a8ec18d2164c28d.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000205_060eb14d108dc5d52d539932d7086a7c33e39311009aa1ade913a3b0319f77be.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000206_6b723d73f98e6c2b2757eca9b23e60d2d486d4794ba62849b4aa9a989ccbb45a.png)

## Law

Represents  a  character's  understanding  of  their  country's laws,  precedents,  legal  manoeuvres,  and  court  procedures. The practise of law as a profession requires intense application and study over many years. As a skill, Law may only rarely get tested with dice rolls, and those players who choose the Police  Officer  occupation  for  their  investigators  may  feel they are being penalised by being compelled to take it as a required skill. While it's true the skill may not be rolled very often, the Law skill-and the knowledge associated with itis in effect purchasing the authority the investigator gains by being a police officer.

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty level: understanding and utilising the details of a relevant law or legal procedure.
- Hard difficulty: remembering or understanding an obscure legal precedent; cross-examining a highly competent hostile witness.

Using  the  Law  skill  when  in  a  foreign  country  may increase the difficulty level of any associated skill roll, unless the character has spent many months studying that nation's legal system.

## Pushing Examples

Delaying  to  consider  your  argument;  explaining  in  great detail  the  nuances  of  the  case/situation;  taking  significant time  to  conduct  research;  bending  the  letter  of  the  law  to drive your argument forward.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: misinterpreting  a  law  or  stepping  outside  the  accepted legal procedure leads you to break the law and draw police attention; wasting valuable time and money on research and legal fees; you are held in contempt of court and thrown in the cells for at least 24 hours.

## Locksmith

A locksmith can open car doors, hotwire cars, jimmy library windows,  figure  out  Chinese  puzzle  boxes,  and  penetrate ordinary  commercial  alarm  systems.  Someone  with  the Locksmith  skill  may  repair  locks,  make  keys,  or  open locks with the aid of skeleton keys, picks, and other tools. Especially difficult locks may require a higher difficulty level.

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty: opening or repairing a standard lock.
- Hard difficulty: opening a high-security lock.

93

## Pushing Examples

Completely dismantling the lock; taking longer; using force to pry the mechanism.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: the  lock  is damaged beyond repair-perhaps your lock pick has snapped off inside the lock; you break the puzzle box-perhaps only smashing  it  will  open  it  now;  you  trip  an  alarm  or  make enough noise for someone or something to come looking…

## Magic

Covers the ability to cast spells and to detect spellcasting. Any non-fae  investigator  wishing  to  have  the  Magic  skill  must first take the Magical advantage (page 51) during character creation. The starting Magic skill value is 60%. Those taking the skill should always discuss how they acquired it with their GM, i.e.,  they  should  determine  a  rationale  for  how  they gained it, such as being apprenticed to a Master-as Peter Grant is to Nightingale-or having learned it elsewhere. A few things to note about the use of magic:

- Each practitioner has their own magical signature ( Signare , page 164).
- One needs a clear head to practise magic, and it is very hard to perform while drunk or using recreational drugs ( Casting in Exceptional Circumstances , page 175).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000207_64c1f9374fc4b3b02fb6dc6161f675f138d94c855d8ce389049042a2fc28ecdb.png)

- The use of magical spells destroys silicon chips ( Spells and Technology , page 176).
- Human beings are very difficult to control with magic. The central nervous system generates its own autonomic magical resistance, making it really tricky to inflict gross changes.
- The overuse of magic can lead to brain damage ( Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation , page 177).

Note: the  magic  of  the  demi-monde  is  quite  different  and does not use this skill (see The Magic of the Demi-Monde , page 243, for further details).

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

All spells require a Magic roll to cast; some may be opposed by either POW or CON-see individual spell descriptions for further details.

- Regular difficulty: casting a spell; sensing a spell being cast (but does not determine which spell is being cast).
- Hard difficulty: sensing a spell is being cast and knowing which spell it is (having some idea of its potential effects).

## Pushing Examples

Yelling the forma ; exaggerating your movements; taking more time.

Sample  consequences  of  failing  a  pushed  roll: suffering actual  damage ( Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation ,  page 177) is a hazard for magical practitioners, causing their brains to take on the appearance of a diseased cauliflower.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000208_4268fd66ba6b1104b7e61ceaa2883cfbabe3a53e47db67b93c974f22d8df8f64.png)

## Mechanical Repair

Allows  a  character  to  repair  a  broken  machine  or  create  a new one. They may also use basic carpentry and plumbing techniques to construct or repair items, e.g., a pulley system or a steam pump. Special tools or parts may be required, at the GM's discretion. Can be used to open simple household locks, but nothing more advanced-see the Locksmith skill (page  93)  for  more  complex  locks.  Mechanical  Repair  is  a companion skill to Tech, and both may be necessary to fix complex devices, such as a car or an aircraft (see Combined Skills Rolls , page 322).

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty: repair or create a simple device; open a basic household lock.
- Hard difficulty: repair a very badly damaged device; create a complex device with limited resources.

## Pushing Examples

Completely dismantling the device; taking longer; risk using excessive  force  to  'encourage'  the  device  into  action,  e.g., hitting the thing until it works.

Sample  consequences  of  failing  a  pushed  roll: you  have broken  the  device  beyond  repair;  you  hurt  yourself  while working  on  the  device-perhaps  cutting  your  hand;  you become obsessed with the device and find you have spent the whole day and night working on it.

## Medicine

Permits the user  to  diagnose  and  treat  illnesses,  accidents, injuries,  diseases,  poisonings,  etc.,  and  make  public  health recommendations. It grants knowledge of a wide variety of drugs and potions-natural and man-made-as well as an understanding of the side effects and contraindications.
