Note: everyone in the game is able to perform basic first aid on an injured or ill person, no roll required; a successful INT roll  is  only  required  if  treating  someone  suffering  from  a mortal wound (see First Aid , page 146, for further details).

The Medicine skill can be used to save the life of a character who has suffered a mortal wound ( Medical Aid , page 147). Treatment  using  the  Medicine  skill  must  be  carried  out within one hour of the mortal wound being sustained or the character dies, unless they receive first aid in the interim. The GM may grant an automatic success for medical treatment in a contemporary, well-equipped hospital.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000209_20d5cbf720b9c32b48096fbf209331c7d3e3e67d5b302d5a802b4ae2995d0bc5.png)

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty: diagnosis and treatment of standard medical ailments, with access to equipment-at least a doctor's bag containing drugs and instruments-and a suitable environment.
- Hard difficulty: diagnosis and treatment in a dirty and unsafe environment with the minimum of equipment.

## Pushing Examples

Consulting  with  colleagues;  conducting  further  research; trying  something  experimental  or  risky;  performing  some form of clinical experiment.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: you misdiagnose and worsen the patient's condition; your good standing  comes  into  question  and  you  are  investigated  for malpractice;  in  the  case  of  a  dying  character,  if  a  pushed Medicine roll is failed, the patient dies.

## Occult

Covers  familiarity  with  the  tenets  of  secret  knowledge passed down from Africa and Asia, from the Medieval and Renaissance  West,  and  from  the  Americas  and  Oceania as well. Someone  with  the  Occult  skill therefore has understanding  of  a  subject  that  falls  outside  the  normal bounds  of  human  knowledge.  They  can  recognise  occult paraphernalia, words, and concepts, as well as folk traditions, and can identify grimoires of magic and occult codes.

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty: recall occult theories or information (from folklore or occult movements); understand the meaning of tarot cards or other divination techniques.
- Hard difficulty: identify rare or unique occult tomes; recall the finer details of secret occult practices and rituals.

## Pushing Examples

Taking more time to study a site or item; conducting further research;  consulting  another  expert;  integrating  with  other occultists  by  purging  oneself  and  undertaking  extreme personal sacrifice and risk.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: information is misremembered; great offence is caused; signs or omens are read in a way entirely contrary to their meaning.

95

## Pilot Aircraft

The aircraft equivalent of the Drive skill (page 83). The pilot understands the behaviour of differing forms of aircraft, from gliders to cargo planes and helicopters. No skill roll is required for  anyone with a modest value in Pilot Aircraft to fly on a calm day with good visibility. A skill roll is required, though, for piloting in a storm, navigation by instrument, low visibility, and other difficult situations. Bad weather, poor visibility, and damage may raise the difficulty level of a skill roll.

A Pilot Aircraft skill roll is always required for any attempt to land an aircraft, even under the best conditions. The consequence of failure is situation-dependent. Failing a skill roll to land on a flat,  grassy  field  on  a  calm  summer's day may simply indicate a  bumpy landing, perhaps deterring more delicate passengers from flying again. At the other extreme, failing a skill roll to land on icy tundra during a storm may result in serious damage to the aeroplane. Failure commonly represents damage to the craft, which must be repaired before the next take-off.

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty: land a light aircraft in a farmer's field during daylight hours.
- Hard difficulty: attempt an emergency landing in a farmer's field; attempt to maintain control of a faulty aircraft while in the air.

## Pushing Examples

Pulling the aircraft up and making a second and final attempt at  landing; pushing the vehicle to its limit; making a risky manoeuvre to lose a pursuer.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000210_a6c1a2ca073ddeb49b7e3e191e5877baeb536fbc50f548cbb453fc471e1ad895.png)

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: the aircraft is somehow damaged and repairs have to be made before it can fly  again,  which  may prove impossible in remote locations; passengers are injured-in some cases, severely-during the manoeuvre  or  accident;  you  emergency-landed  the  plane in the jungle and awake to find you are being held captive by guerrillas. Reserve burning wrecks for exceptional circumstances, such as when taking a crazy risk at high speed.

## Pilot Boat

The water equivalent of the Drive skill (page 83). The pilot understands the behaviour of all forms of motor and sailing craft, from a kayak to an ocean liner, in various conditions (wind, storms, and tides), and can read wave and wind action to suggest hidden obstacles and approaching storms. No roll is required for anyone with a modest value in Pilot Boat to sail on a calm day with good visibility. A skill roll is required, though, for storms, navigation by instrument, low visibility, and  other  difficult  situations.  Bad  weather,  poor  visibility, and damage may raise the difficulty level of a skill roll.

Note: there  is  a  massive  difference  between  a  kayak  and  a liner,  but  the  Pilot  Boat  skill,  like  the  Pilot  Aircraft  skill, is  considered  to  be  very  broad.  If  the  character  only  really knows about simple craft (canoes, rowboats, and kayaks), the GM may wish to use the Athletics skill instead.

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty: pilot across a strong tide or in poor conditions.
- Hard difficulty: pilot a craft with faulty equipment or in severely bad conditions.

## Pushing Examples

Manoeuvring the craft to make a second and final attempt at  docking;  pushing  the  vehicle  to  its  limit;  making  a risky manoeuvre.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: the  craft  is damaged and repairs have to be made before it can be used again,  which  may  prove  impossible  in  remote  locations; passengers are injured-possibly severely-during the manoeuvre or accident; you are shipwrecked and wash up on an unknown deserted shore.

## Read Lips

reader-then only half of the conversation can be deciphered. Read Lips  may  also  be  used  to  silently  communicate  with another person if both are proficient.

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

Depending on the situation, Read Lips is usually unopposed. If  attempting  to  read  the  lips  of  a  target  who  wishes  to remain hidden or unobserved, the skill may be opposed by the target's Stealth.

- Regular difficulty: understand the spoken communication of a person who is in clear sight and in relatively close proximity.
- Hard difficulty: determine the spoken communication of a person whose mouth is occasionally obscured and/or is some distance away, e.g., in a large crowd of people.

## Pushing Examples

Putting yourself in an obvious position and staring unsubtly at  the  target(s);  filming  the  target-and  thus  likely  to  be observed doing so.

Sample consequences of  failing  a  pushed  roll: the  target becomes  aware  that  you  are  watching  them  intently  (or filming them), takes offence, and confronts you; the drunken man across the bar is angered, mistakenly thinking that you are looking at him, and punches you; you are so intent on the target that you miss what is happening in your close proximity, e.g., someone steals your case or you walk into a lamp post.

## Ride

Applies  to  saddle  horses,  donkeys,  and  mules,  as  well  as granting  knowledge  of  a  riding  animal's  basic  care,  their riding gear, and how to handle the steed at a gallop or on difficult terrain. Should a steed unexpectedly rear or stumble, the  rider's  chance  of  remaining  mounted  is  equal  to  their Ride skill. An unfamiliar mount-such as a camel-might be ridden successfully, but the level of difficulty would be raised.

If a character falls from a mount, either because the animal has  collapsed,  fallen,  or  died-or  because  a  pushed  Ride roll failed-the GM should consult Table 7: Other Forms of  Damage (page  149),  most  likely  using  the  moderate  or severe category, depending on how hard the ground is. Basic animal handling and riding a mount at walking pace do not require a Ride roll.

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

Besides  being  of  importance  to  deaf  characters,  the  Read Lips skill allows an inquisitive character to 'listen in' on a conversation  without  requiring  them  to  hear  the  speakers. Line of sight is necessary, and if only one speaker's lips can be  seen-the  other  presumably  with  their  back  to  the  lip

- Regular difficulty: riding at speed.
- Hard difficulty: riding a mount at speed in rough, unfamiliar terrain; holding onto a scared mount.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000211_b5f1f7b1c8d24d10838b974d89bff39928f86f51fe602725f46b219dc421cb42.png)

## Pushing Examples

Whipping  or  pushing  the  mount  aggressively-perhaps forcing a horse to jump a ravine or high embankment; taking the  risk  of  keeping  your  seat  rather  than  jumping  clear  as trouble approaches.

Sample  consequences  of  failing  a  pushed  roll: you  are thrown  from  the  mount  and  possibly  suffer  damage;  the mount is injured; your foot is entangled in the mount's bridle and you end up being dragged for some distance.

## Science

Like  the Art/Craft  and  Languages  skills,  the Science skill  covers  a  range  of  subject  specialisations.  As  a  result, a  generic  'Science'  skill  cannot  be  taken,  though  the  skill can  be  chosen  more  than  once,  each  time  with  different specialisations. Practical and theoretical ability with a science specialisation suggests some degree of formalised education and training, although a well-read amateur scientist may also be a possibility.

When taking Science as a skill, the player should choose one specialisation to begin at 60% and two other specialisations to  begin  at  30%  each.  While  each  speciality  covers  quite different fields of study and research, they all share certain underlying  principles,  methodologies,  and  practices;  e.g., someone specialising in Biology understands Chemistry and Physics as it applies to their subject, and so on.

Each  specialty  covers  a  particular  discipline  and  the following list is not exhaustive. As we've already mentioned, many specialties cross and overlap in knowledge, for example, Mathematics and Cryptography, Botany and Biology, Chemistry and Pharmacy. When a character does not have the obvious discipline specialty, they may roll against their lowest  specialism  with  a  penalty  die  ( Bonus  Dice  and Penalty Dice , page 111).

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty: conducting an experiment; analysing an unusual substance; being aware of current scientific theory within your field of specialty.
- Hard difficulty: conducting a particularly demanding experiment; deciphering disorganised/partial scientific records; working with improvised equipment or limited resources.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000212_0ab9a94af7be07fc54bbd3d568ce41ce00c4d89399d5c94e46a9bfa381037ef9.png)

97

## Pushing Examples

Taking  more  time  for  study;  conducting  further  research; moving to a better-equipped laboratory; consulting another expert; taking risks.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: the experiment goes completely awry, causing unforeseen damage,  e.g.,  explosion,  fire,  electrical  shock,  expulsion to  the  fae  world;  you  incorrectly  prepare  the  formula  for  a particular pharmaceutical with disastrous results; your tool slips, destroying a vital piece of equipment or rare and delicate artefact; some higher authority seizes your results/findings.

## Examples of Science Specialisations

Archaeology: allows  dating  and  identification  of  artefacts from  past  cultures  and  the  detection  of  fakes.  Ensures expertise in setting up and excavating a dig site. On inspecting a site, the user might deduce the site's purpose as well as the way of life of those who left the remains. Archaeology also helps identify written forms of extinct human languages.

Astronomy: the  user  knows  the  names  of  important  stars, when eclipses  and  meteor  showers  are  likely  to  occur,  and how to find  out  which  stars  and  planets  are  overhead  at  a particular hour of the day or night or on a particular day. The skill also provides knowledge of current perceptions about life on other worlds, the existence of or the formation of galaxies, and so on. An academic might also be able to calculate orbits, discuss  stellar  life  cycles,  and  have  knowledge  of  infrared astronomy or long-baseline interferometry. Navigation by the stars is also a possibility.

Biology: the  study  of  life  and  living  organisms,  including cytology, ecology, genetics, histology, microbiology, physiology,  and  so  on.  With  this  skill,  the  user  might develop a vaccine against some hideous bacterium, isolate the hallucinogenic properties of some jungle frog, or perform an analysis of blood and/or organic matter.

Botany: the  study  of  plant  life,  including  classification  of species, structure, growth, reproduction, chemical properties, evolutionary principles, diseases, and microscopy.  Subdisciplines of botany include agronomy, forestry, horticulture, and palaeobotany. With this skill, the user might identify the properties of a particular plant-whether poisonous, edible, or psychotropic, for example-and its particular uses.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000213_c115ff7d2798b07521301e8ba3e30e6ecd6bf561372468fcd10506354c727629.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000214_30c182ee5f80df720f7a00414a0e862d093920d9713bb1c0e1910133ccd5d8fe.png)

Chemistry: a study of the composition of substances and the effects  of  temperature,  energy,  and  pressure  upon  them,  as well as how they interact with one another. With chemistry, the user might create or extract complex chemical compounds, including  simple  explosives,  poisons,  gases,  and  acidsrequiring at least a day or so with the proper equipment and reagents. The user could also potentially analyse an unknown substance to determine its components or purpose.

Cryptography: the  study  of  secret  codes  and  languages developed  by  one  or  more  persons  to  conceal  the  contents of  a  conversation  or  message.  A  specialised  branch  of mathematics,  this  skill  enables  the  identification,  creation, and/or  deciphering  of  a  code.  Codes  are  usually  written but may take other forms, such as a message hidden within a  musical  composition,  graphic  art,  or  computer  program. Cracking a  code  can  be  painstaking  work,  often  requiring lengthy research and number crunching.

Engineering: while technically not a science, engineering is grouped  here  for  convenience.  Science  is  about  identifying certain phenomena through observing and recording, whereas engineering  takes  such  findings  and  uses  them  to  produce practical applications, like machines, structures, and materials.

Forensics: the analysis and identification of evidence. Normally  associated  with  crime  scene  investigation-the collection of fingerprints, DNA, hair, and body fluids, etc.and laboratory examination in order to determine facts and provide expert evidence for legal disputes.

Geology: used  to  determine  the  approximate  age  of  rock strata, recognise fossil types, distinguish minerals and crystals, locate promising  sites for drilling or  mining, evaluate  soil  samples,  and  anticipate  volcanism,  seismic events, avalanches, and other phenomena.

Mathematics: the  study  of  numbers  and  logic,  including mathematical theories. Also applied and theoretical solution design and development. The skill might allow the identification of bizarre geometries, solve puzzling formulae, and decrypt complex patterns or codes (see Cryptography for the specialised study of codes).

Meteorology: the scientific study of the atmosphere, including  weather  systems  and  patterns,  and  atmospheric phenomena. Use this skill to determine long-range weather patterns and to forecast effects like rain, snow, and fog.

Pharmacy: the study of chemical compounds and their  effect  on  living  organisms.  Traditionally,  this  has involved the formulation, creation, and dispensing of

99

medications-whether a shaman using traditional herbs or a pharmacist creating synthetic compounds in a laboratory. The application of this skill ensures the safe and effective use of pharmaceutical drugs, including synthesising ingredients, identification of toxins, and knowledge of possible side effects.

Physics: grants theoretical understanding of pressure, materials, motion, magnetism, electricity, optics, radioactivity, and  related  phenomena,  as  well  as  some ability  to  construct  experimental  devices  to  test  theories. Practical devices, such as automobiles, are not the province of physicists; however, experimental devices may be, perhaps in conjunction with Tech or Mechanical Repair ( Combined Skill Rolls , page 322).

Zoology: the  study  of  biology  that  relates  specifically  to the  animal  kingdom,  including  the  structure,  evolution, classification, behavioural habits, and distribution of animals, both  living  and  extinct.  Use  this  skill  to  identify  animal species from their interactions with the environment-tracks, droppings, marks, etc.-as well as their likely behaviours and territorial traits.

## Sleight of Hand

Enables  the  visual  covering-up,  secreting,  or  masking  of an  object  or  objects,  perhaps  with  debris,  cloth,  or  other intervening or illusion-promoting materials, such as a secret panel or false compartment. Larger objects of any sort should be increasingly hard to conceal. Sleight of Hand includes fine manipulation,  such  as  pickpocketing,  palming  a  card,  and clandestine  use  of  a  mobile  phone.  Characters  lacking  the Sleight of Hand skill can attempt a Hard DEX roll to achieve simple feats of sleight of hand.

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

Commonly  opposed  by  Observation.  Adjust  the  difficulty level according to the size of the item being concealed.

- Regular difficulty: picking the pocket of someone with an Observation skill below 70%; concealing a small item, e.g., something that would fit in a pocket or up a sleeve.
- Hard difficulty: picking the pocket of someone with an Observation skill of 70% or higher; removing someone's watch or belt without them noticing; concealing a larger item.

## Pushing Examples

Taking more time to study the target and their behaviours; physically touching or bumping into the target; setting up an elaborate rig inside one's jacket to conceal a card or loaded dice; having someone cause a distraction.

Sample  consequences  of  failing  a  pushed  roll: you  feel a  police  officer's  hand  on  your  shoulder;  while  no  one  can prove you pickpocketed the priceless diamond, someone has their suspicions, and you notice that for the last week you've been tailed by a mysterious dark figure; you're caught redhanded and the victim, a local hard man, doesn't take kindly to thieves-you're taken out the back and shown the business end of a cricket bat!

## Survival

Provides  the  expertise  required  to  survive  in  challenging environments, such as in desert or arctic conditions, as well as upon the sea or in wilderness terrain. It provides knowledge of hunting, building shelters, finding water, and hazards (such as the avoidance of poisonous plants), etc. Being able to find your way in any terrain requires the Navigate skill (page 83), while the Survival skill may help you to survive long enough to be rescued if you get lost or separated from your companions.

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

- Regular difficulty: find/make shelter; locate water and sources of food; notice an environmental hazard.
- Hard difficulty: as above but when you are badly injured or if the weather is severe.

## Pushing Examples

Trying something risky, e.g., drinking water without purifying it first or eating unidentified berries; using all of one's clothing to improvise a shelter; burning every possession to make a signal fire.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: the cave you found is inhabited by a very angry bear; having burned all of your clothes to make a signal fire, you have now given away your position to your pursuers-will your rescuers reach you before the pursuers do?

## Tech

Enables the repair or reconfiguration of technological devices, such as mobile phones, computers, electric motors, fuse  boxes,  and  burglar  alarms.  To  fix  an  electrical  device may require  special  parts  or  tools,  as  the  parts  needed  for electronics  often  cannot  be  jury-rigged-they  are  designed for precise jobs. Often without the right microchip or circuit board, the skill-user is out of luck unless they can contrive some form of ingenious workaround.

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

Time is a factor  in  fabrication  or  repair,  and  the  difficulty level  should  be  lowered  or  an  automatic  success  granted  if time  is  unlimited,  especially  if  the  character  has  access  to manuals and parts. Where a character has the correct parts and  instructions,  putting  together  a  standard  computer  or something similar does not require a skill roll.

- Regular difficulty: repairing minor damage to a computer or electronic device-the kind of service one might expect under warranty.
- Hard difficulty: repairing something that is badly damaged; working without the proper tools; working with very limited time (rushing).

## Pushing Examples

Taking longer to repair or reconfigure the equipment; taking a  risky  short  cut;  buying  new  and/or  expensive  equipment and components.

Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll: you blow the fuses and plunge the building into darkness; you wreck the thing beyond repair; you create a device that does something other than what was intended.

## Track

Enables a character to follow a person, vehicle, or animal over earth and through vegetation. Factors-such as time passed since  the  tracks  were  made,  rain,  and  the  type  of  ground covered-may affect the difficulty level.

## Opposing Skill/Difficulty Level

If tracking someone who is trying to conceal their tracks, the opponent's Stealth skill should be used to set the difficulty level;  modifiers  might  also  be  applied  for  the  prevailing terrain ( Determining the Difficulty Level , page 105).

- Regular difficulty: tracking an animal or human in the forest under normal (temperate) conditions.
- Hard difficulty: following a trail that is several (or more) days old.

## Pushing Examples

Backtracking  and  taking  more  time  to  study  the  area; walking  in  increasing  spirals  to  locate  the  tracks;  taking longer,  possibly  making  more  noise  and  making  yourself more visible.

Sample consequences of failing a  pushed  roll: the  tracks you're  following  lead  directly  into  the  path  of  danger;  you find yourself going round and round in circles, and night is falling as you realise you are now totally lost; you follow the tracks, only to be ambushed or caught in a trap laid by the person/thing you are following.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000215_0ffcd3ed72dcd1d86530f2330b333a4cdcc40bdcfbc579652dacc873ac6e0c0f.png)

## Combat Skills

The final skill group concerns combat skills, of which there are  two:  Fighting  and  Firearms.  For  more  on  their  use  in combat,  see  the Combat section  of  Chapter  3  (page  123). Combat  skills,  like  common  skills,  automatically  begin  at 30%. An investigator can boost them to 60% during character creation by selecting them as part of their six starting skills ( Step Four: Determine Skills , page 52).

## Fighting

A  character's ability in melee (hand-to-hand) combat. Fighting also allows a character to instinctively evade blows ( Step 2: Target Responds , page 127).

Those starting with a Fighting skill of 60% may wish to choose a  formal  training  style  as  part  of  their  background (to account for their skill level), such as a martial art, boxing, or  advanced  personal  security.  How  did  your  investigator learn to fight? Was it a part of their occupation (e.g., formal military training), for pleasure as a hobby (e.g., martial arts classes), or an element of their backstory (e.g., learning the hard way as a result of street-fighting?).

The skill also includes the use of melee weapons, such as knives, clubs (including a police baton, cricket bat, etc.), and improvised weapons. Humans have developed a vast range of  weapons  throughout  history,  many  requiring  a  great deal of specialist skill  to  use;  however,  in  this  game,  these are all covered with the single skill of Fighting. While the main  focus  of RoL:RPG is  solving  mysteries,  combat  can sometimes occur,  but  for  ease  of  play,  no  differentiation  is made between fighting styles or melee weapons.

If a character has the Signature Weapon advantage (page 52), they gain a bonus die on damage rolls ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111; The Damage Roll , page 132) when using that particular melee weapon (but no other).

Note: as a combat skill, Fighting cannot be pushed.

101

## Firearms

Firearms is a catch-all skill covering the use of all types of firearms,  including  handguns,  rifles,  shotguns,  and  their derivatives. It is also the default skill for those using a bow or crossbow of any kind. Those starting with a Firearms skill of 60% may wish to specify how they became proficient to account for their skill level. How did your investigator learn to  shoot?  Was  it  a  part  of  their  occupation  (e.g.,  formal military training), for pleasure as a hobby (e.g., sport shooting or archery), or an element of their backstory (e.g., growing up on a farm)?

For  ease  of  play,  no  differentiation  is  made  between  the varying types of firearm. In this game, they are all covered with the single skill of Firearms.

If a character has the Signature Firearm advantage (page 51), they gain a bonus die on damage rolls ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111; The Damage Roll , page 132) when using that particular gun or bow (but no other).

Note: as a combat skill, Firearms cannot be pushed.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000216_b73ae80feafe047adf4e673a0907d5837bdc0aa52ca9ead539d32818b6df62a6.png)

## The Basic Rules

THIS CHAPTER COVERS the standard game rules for Rivers  of  London:  the  Roleplaying  Game ( RoL:RPG )-the system  used  to  ensure  fairness  and  balance,  as  well  as  to resolve conflicts and challenges as they arise during play.

The chapter is divided into key rules sections: dice rolling and skill rolls; the investigator development phase; combat; wounds  and  healing;  other  forms  of  damage;  and  chases. While it's useful to read through all of this, when starting the game, the best advice is to concentrate on the dice rolling and skill  rolls,  combat,  and  healing  sections  first.  You  can keep coming back to this chapter to build your knowledge of the rules as you go, until you are a rules wizard!

## When to Roll Dice

There's a huge amount of fun to be had playing a roleplaying game,  and  a  lot  of  that  fun  comes  from  discussing  your investigator's actions with your fellow players-including the Game Moderator (GM)-without the need to roll any dice whatsoever.  Players  say  what  their  investigators  are  doing, sometimes  even  speaking  'in  character'  to  better  express themselves. The GM states what the non-player characters (NPCs) are doing, as well as describing the world in which the game takes place and how that world is affected by the investigators' actions.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000217_c2b2a3db53af32569f5e28d7abc9f91d8a273034fd2cf9c6edf1acd95f205361.png)

Likewise,  you  could  simply  say  that  your  investigator manages to climb to the top of St Paul's Cathedral in the pouring  rain  at  midnight-unlikely,  but  possible.  But  if there's no dice roll to test your investigator's skill, there's no tension  or  drama  either-and  both  drama  and  tension  are important in a story.

Equally,  there's  no  need  to  roll  dice  for  everything.  For example, each investigator has a Drive skill, but dice don't need to be rolled every time an investigator gets in a car to go somewhere-that would be pretty dull! If you say your investigator  is  driving  to  the  shops,  unless  someone  takes issue with you, then it's done-simply move on with the story so you can get to the exciting bits. Normal day-to-day stuff that everyone 'just does' should simply happen-no dice roll needed. However, if the GM describes how another car is attempting to push your investigator's car off the road, you probably are going to object to this. You could say, ' Hold on, I'm putting my foot down and getting away, ' but still, the GM may insist the car is barging your car off the road. There's a disagreement, a conflict-a reason to roll dice to see whether your car is indeed pushed off the road. The story has reached a moment of tension that requires a definite outcome. Time to reach for the dice!

So far, so good. Everyone accepts what everybody else says and together you create an interesting story. Until, of course, someone doesn't accept what somebody else says. When that happens in a game, you can either disagree with that person or you can ask for clarification. Ultimately, if you don't agree that  what  they've  said  is  happening  in  the  story,  you  can resolve the situation with dice. For example, an investigator doesn't believe what Jimmy Four-Fingers (played by the GM) is telling them about the whereabouts of a missing person, so the GM calls for a dice roll to resolve the situation-in this case, probably a Read Person roll, which enables the players to judge whether Jimmy's lying or not.

- The GM decides if a dice roll is required and what skill is being used. Deciding when a dice roll is needed and when it is not is up to the GM. Some groups may use dice rolls more than others. Published case files (adventures) for RoL:RPG advise the GM on when to call for dice rolls, but the author can't foresee all the tricky situations that the players and their investigators may get themselves into, so the GM may need to call for extra ones.
- Roll dice when a character is challenged. Don't roll for something that you'd expect an investigator to be able to do with ease. Consider the investigator's skills: a trained demolitions specialist with all the time and necessary equipment is going to be able to set an explosive charge

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000218_e1c25d48ff0852cb70c7edab5d3a486f652a8eebf60e48b02281cb2f6eeb6033.png)

3

without needing to roll dice but disarming an IED is definitely going to require a dice roll because there's risk of harm and it's dramatic!

- Learn from experience when to roll dice. If you, as GM, call for a dice roll and the players lean forward to observe the result and then cheer if it goes well-or groan and laugh when it doesn't-you're probably doing it right! As we said above, don't call for rolls when characters are doing normal and easy things. Judging when to call for a dice roll is something you'll develop a knack for the more you play the game. If you are ever in doubt, don't be afraid to turn to your players to get their advice on whether they think a dice roll is needed.

When  considering  if  a  dice  roll  is  warranted,  consider the  downside.  What  happens  if  the  roll  is  failed?  If  the consequence  is  irrelevant  or  downright  dull,  then  a  roll  is not needed. Only call for a dice roll when the result (good or  bad)  pushes  the  story  forward  or,  at  least,  results  in something dramatic.

Example: Nafeesa is investigating a house and decides to climb over the garden fence to access the back door. Does she need to make a dice roll (Athletics skill) to climb the fence? Ask yourself if the focus of the story is on that fence-is it important to what happens next? If Nafeesa fails the skill roll, what would that mean? In all likelihood, she'd have to look for another way in or find some other way over the fence, which just delays her getting to the important part  of  the  story:  what's  inside  that  house.  So,  in  this  case,  you could say she climbs over easily.

Alternatively, Nafeesa is being chased by a dog and decides to climb over that same fence to escape. Now, if she fails, she must face the dog and there's a risk of it attacking her. The situation feels more dramatic and could have a major impact on what happens next, so a dice roll would seem appropriate.

When attempting a skill roll, the player concerned should agree  a  goal  with  the  GM,  i.e.,  what  the  investigator  is hoping to achieve. If the skill roll is successful, they achieve their  goal.  If  the  task  is  something  that  anyone  could attempt, use the appropriate common skill for the dice roll. But  when  attempting  something  for  which  an  investigator has no appropriate skill listed on their sheet, the GM must make a call:

- Is the knowledge or task clearly something covered by an expert skill? If so, the player can choose to spend Luck points and attempt the skill roll ( Trying Your Luck , page 114).
- Is the knowledge something that anyone with some general knowledge might know? For example: a roll to decide whether the investigator remembers who had the Christmas number one last year. In this case, there's not really a common or expert skill that covers this, so a characteristic roll is made instead; in this case, an INT roll.
- Like the last point, sometimes there is not an appropriate skill for the situation or goal, so a characteristic roll is used instead. For example: grabbing the keys off a table before someone else could be a DEX roll; staying awake through the night while on your own would be a CON roll, and so on.

Note: Luck can be used to adjust the outcome of some dice rolls. We cover this later, in Spending Luck (page 113).

## Skill Rolls

The word 'roll' is used in conjunction with the appropriate skill or characteristic; so, when it's time to get the dice out, the GM will, for example, call for a 'Research roll' or a 'STR (Strength) roll.' The GM has the final say about which skill or characteristic is appropriate to roll, depending on the situation and the nature of the opposition. We use the term 'skill roll' regardless of whether a skill or a characteristic is used.

As we've already mentioned, a player should be clear about what they want their investigator to achieve by stating a goal before they roll any dice. If the goal isn't acceptable, the GM should  help  to  rephrase  it.  Well-worded  goals  define  the objective  clearly  and  concisely,  so  everyone  knows  what  is being attempted. Sometimes, a player may ask to make a roll, but the GM says it isn't necessary and that the task is simply accomplished without needing to roll the dice.

Example: Nafeesa Jones wants to force a door open. Unless told otherwise, the GM might automatically assume that she is simply bashing into the door and creating a loud noise in the process. If Nafeesa wants to force open the door quietly, she needs to state this as her goal. 'Break the door down' and 'lever the door open quietly' are two different goals with potentially different consequences for Nafeesa and the story, and ones the GM needs to consider when setting a difficulty level for the roll.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000219_5c17bf7fc1ea7e5eeb01ec1d7609e191fc8a14efcbc252f14237d1ac327c6ff3.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000220_5269b8a38a6021513e562eecdf2f873948a96a49de6acea41d7cf3cc293e1748.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000221_2ce019045c8ff246c1ac51aa9826f4bbb4b5f6b14ca5884061319e1d02375cfa.png)

## The GM's Decision is Final

While the rules are designed to be as clear  and  flexible  as  possible,  from time to time you may encounter a situation where they do not provide a clear solution.  In  such  circumstances,  the GM's decision is final. While players are encouraged  to  offer  suggestions  and ideas,  they  should  respect  the  GM's decision  and  move  on  with  the  game once the GM's decision has been made.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000222_70aa58716605d0b9386a09503929dc1b0ff67444117d5f9c04251f58178745f4.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000223_3d5e90497a8f0629d3393e01f7ec610d9630b35bd0932fa5efc8d2509d8f876f.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000224_d405405183b79ff914e91ccadcebfdfd669007605a2a667c7b4210db69f539b2.png)

## Skill Rolls: Determining the Difficulty Level

The GM determines the difficulty level for all skill rolls. There are two levels of difficulty in the game: Regular and Hard.

- Regular difficulty: a task that would challenge a competent person. The player needs to roll equal to or below their investigator's full skill or characteristic value in order to succeed. This level of difficulty should be used in the vast majority of cases.
- Hard difficulty: a task that would challenge a professional. The player needs to roll equal to or below one half of their investigator's skill or characteristic value in order to succeed. This difficulty level should be used occasionally and only in the most challenging of circumstances.

If you, as GM, feel that the task is easy, then don't call for a dice roll. Likewise, if you want a clue to be obvious, don't ask for dice rolls before giving the clue to the players. Save dice rolls for challenging and dramatic situations.

If  an  investigator  is  facing  a  living  being,  the  GM  has a  choice:  they  can  either  use  an  opposed  roll  ( Opposed Skill  Rolls ,  page  116)  or  set  the  difficulty  level  using  the opponent's skill level. In this case, to set the difficulty level, the GM should decide which skill or characteristic is being used by the opponent (human or otherwise) to counter the investigator's action-examples can be found with each skill entry ( Chapter 2: Skills ).

Example: Nafeesa  is  trying  to  get  information  out  of  a  street criminal  named  Jimmy  Four-Fingers.  Nafeesa  is  putting  the pressure on Jimmy. The player's goal is to find out an address from the reluctant Mr Four-Fingers, so the GM calls for a Social skill roll. POW can be used to oppose Social when it is used to intimidate ( Social , page 86), and Jimmy's POW is 80, so the difficulty level of Nafeesa's Social roll is Hard. The player rolls 17, which is less than half of Nafeesa's Social skill of 60%-a success!

## GM ADVICE

Using an NPC opponent's skill level to set a difficulty level prioritises speed of play over statistical balance. Using an opposed roll is statistically more balanced, but takes slightly longer.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000225_4cef5d8978f5488d43bc66279c7697e8ce13054a36edc57da75a3ed1a281a111.png)

In the case of non-living opponents, such as a locked door, the GM must weigh up all the pertinent factors and make a  judgement.  A  standard  challenge  would  be  of  Regular difficulty.  Where  the  GM  judges  the  challenge  is  truly exceptional,  they  may  use  the  Hard  difficulty  level.  This method is also used for Firearms (page 101), where hitting a  target  within  standard  range  is  Regular  difficulty,  and hitting a target at longer range is Hard difficulty.

Wherever possible, the situation should be framed so that the dice are being rolled by the players because, let's face it, they won't get much fun out of watching the GM roll the dice every time. For example, if a character is attempting to use their Stealth skill to furtively follow an NPC, the GM would use the NPC's Observation skill to set the difficulty level,  with  the  player  rolling  the  dice  to  see  if  they  can achieve their goal. If the situation was reversed and the NPC was following the character, this time, the GM would use the NPC's Stealth skill to set the difficulty level and the player would roll the dice against their Observation skill to see if they manage to spot the tail.

Always remember: the  players  should  be  rolling  the  dice more often than the GM.

## Rolling the Dice: Levels of Success and Failure

There are five different outcomes when a dice roll is madefurther details on each follow below:

- Critical success: a dice roll of 01 (the best possible result).
- If the opponent's skill or characteristic is below 70, the difficulty level is Regular.
- Regular success: the dice roll is equal to or below the character's full skill or characteristic value.
- Hard success: the dice roll is equal to or below half of the character's skill or characteristic value.
- If the opponent's skill or characteristic is equal to or above 70, the difficulty level is Hard.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000226_e6da4610776e851d16f9efed78d4ca5c57990213d87b66d2f68e8d979d222280.png)

3

- Failure: (called a Weak success in opposed rolls) The dice roll is above the character's skill or characteristic value.
- Fumble: a dice roll of 100 (the worst possible result).

When making a skill or characteristic roll, the player states a goal, the GM confirms which skill or characteristic is being used, and the player then rolls the dice. The level of successor failure-may have an effect on how the outcome of the roll is  resolved  or  on  what  the  investigator  achieves  ( Opposed Skill Rolls , page 116).

If a dice roll is successful, i.e., is a Critical, Hard, or Regular success, the player should be encouraged to help describe the outcome. This can include aspects of the story beyond their investigator, such as the NPCs' actions and the surrounding environment; however, such things may be moderated by the GM. Thus, both player and GM are involved in describing the outcome of a successful roll.

Example: Nafeesa  achieved  her  goal  of  intimidating  Jimmy Four-Fingers by rolling under the difficulty level set by the GM. The player now describes the scene.

'I push Jimmy up against the wall and threaten him, ' Tell me the address or life is going to get a lot harder for you.' Yeah, Jimmy is terrified and he agrees to take me to the address, and I follow him.'

There's  no  need  to  take  issue  with  the  player's  description  of Nafeesa's interrogation technique-it's just description-but does Jimmy escort Nafeesa to the address? The GM considers this. The player's goal was to find out the address. The GM jumps in, slightly altering what the player has said, ' Great, Jimmy leads you part of the way, then points down the street at a shabby, derelict house. He refuses to go any further.'

## Critical Success

A roll of 01 indicates a Critical success. This means that the investigator has performed outstandingly well, and has either successfully achieved their goal to the highest possible degree or-optionally-something beneficial has occurred. Think of it as a ' Yes, and… ' situation. The nature of the good fortune is at the GM's discretion, although the players are welcome to make suggestions. The chance of achieving a Critical success with the dice is 1 in 100, but the player can also buy a Critical success by spending Luck points ( Spending Luck , page 113).

- In Opposed Skill Rolls (page 116), a Critical success beats every other outcome.

Example: Nafeesa  looks around  the  outside  of the derelict house. Her goal is to find evidence of who might have been there recently. The GM asks for an Observation roll while considering what evidence there might feasibly be-perhaps a footprint or a discarded scrap of paper with a note on it. Nafeesa's player rolls a 01-a Critical success. The GM describes the footprint and, as a result of the Critical success, has a passer-by say, ' Hello .'  The passer-by can give a description of the people who left the house earlier that day. That Critical success really paid off, as Nafeesa now knows exactly who she's looking for.

## Hard Success

A  roll  equal  to  or  below  one  half  of  the  specified  skill  or characteristic  value  indicates  a  Hard  success.  This  means that the character has performed exceptionally well, and has successfully achieved their goal, whether the difficulty level was  Hard  or  Regular.  If  there's  something  extra  that  the character can achieve beyond what would be expected with a Regular success, now is the time for the GM to add that in, but if you're at a loss to think of something extra, just narrate what happens and move on with the story. In Opposed Skill Rolls (page 116), a Hard success beats a Regular success.

## Regular Success

A roll equal to or below the specified skill's or characteristic's full  value  indicates  a  Regular  success.  This  means  that  the character  has  performed  proficiently  and  has  successfully achieved their goal if the difficulty level was Regular. If the difficulty  level  of  the  dice  roll  was  set  at  Hard,  a  Regular success indicates a good attempt but a failure nonetheless. In Opposed Skill Rolls (page 116), a Regular success beats a Weak success (failure).

## Failure (or Weak Success)

A roll that is above the specified skill or characteristic value usually indicates a failure. This means that the investigator has performed poorly and failed to achieve their goal. Exactly how the investigator has failed is entirely up to the GMthough,  of  course,  the  players  may  throw  in  suggestions. Think about the circumstances surrounding the failed roll.  Could it represent a failure to act rather than a failed attempt? Did the investigator miss the opportunity to step in  and  do  something  because  they  were  too  slow  to  react? Or  is  this  a  ' Yes,  but… '  or  ' No,  but… '  situation?  If  it's  a ' Yes, but…, ' the investigator does achieve their goal to some extent, but at some cost-for example, perhaps they did pick the lock, but took so long that the security guard spots them opening the door. If it's a ' No, but…, ' then, while they didn't pick the lock, at least no one knows they tried. Remember, complications  are  good  for  drama  and  can  help  take  your story in new and interesting directions. Sometimes, though, a fail is just a fail. The key thing is to make sure that the story

- In combat, a Critical success on an attack roll gains the attacker a bonus die on their damage roll ( The Damage Roll , page 132).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000227_0bd1754db60bb97f13c0800044f00e5b4963723d70c29e1af905f3bba6faf6be.png)

doesn't stall because of it-keep it going, just with some juicy complications!

When a player fails  a  roll,  in  most  cases,  they  have  the option to spend Luck to change that failure into a success. For more on this, see the Spending Luck section on page 113.

Initial  failure  at  a  skill  roll  should  not  usually  cause  the investigator to take damage, depending on the circumstances. Worse things may happen if the player chooses to push the roll  and  fails  a  second  time  ( Pushing the Roll ,  page  108). As  we've  already  mentioned,  a  normal  failed  roll  tends  to mean that some obstacle has prevented success or the person didn't make a good attempt. So, save ' What's the worst that could happen? ' style consequences for failed pushed rolls and Fumbles (following).

Example: Nafeesa makes her way to the derelict house. She finds the  ground  floor  windows  and  doors  are  securely  sealed  with metal covers-it looks like the place has been repossessed. Looking round the back, she spots an open upper floor window. There's a drainpipe that she could climb up, and the GM sets the difficulty level of the climb as Regular. Nafeesa's player gets a result of 63 for  her  Athletics  skill  roll-a  failure.  The  GM describes the lack of  handholds  and  that  the  drainpipe  feels  loose.  Nafeesa's  goal was to get to the window, but she's still stuck on the ground. Note how  the  GM  did  not  take  the  situation  to  its  extreme,  that  of Nafeesa climbing partway then falling and suffering harm-this is  something  that  could  happen  if  the  roll  is  pushed  and  failed ( Pushing the Roll , page 108).

In Opposed Skill Rolls (page  116),  both  sides  roll  and compare  their  results,  and  there  is  always  a  winner  and  a loser, even if both roll poorly. So, in an opposed roll, a failure is instead known as a Weak success because, even though it's not a great result, it's still a better (higher) level of success than  a  fumble.  This  means  that,  although  both  opponents performed inadequately, one still ends up beating the other. In a match between two complete amateurs, there is still a winner and a loser.

## Fumble

If the dice read 100-remember, that's 00 on your 'tens' die and 0 on your 'units' die-a fumble has occurred, meaning the investigator has performed abysmally. Not only did they fail to achieve their goal, but something really bad happenssomething worse than a straight failure. The nature of that misfortune  or  bad  luck  is  at  the  GM's  discretion,  and  the options include:

- An Involuntary Action (page 108).
- When fumbling a Drive roll, this may mean an engine breakdown, a brakes failure, or a blowout.
- If using a firearm, a fumble may indicate a malfunction, causing the gun to jam or misfire.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000228_2f384f9d8e2726fa501ff3df2b37becc253b77c0cca2720d22a7571fdba90c31.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000229_9cb3d6eab350a32e4b2b774084b0ea94c8d1c8d0ac95371826119b37819c6945.png)

3

Example: consider what would happen if, instead of rolling a 01 when investigating the derelict house, Nafeesa's player rolled a 100 for her Observation roll. The GM still describes the footprint and how Nafeesa is crouched down looking, but, this time, suddenly there's a foot on the small of her back pushing her to the ground. The people she was after have returned, and the GM took inspiration from the fumbled roll to have them sneak up on Nafeesa without any need for a dice roll on their part. Things suddenly got a lot more complicated for Nafeesa…

The chance of achieving a fumble with the dice is 1 in 100, but this is increased to any roll of 90 or above if the character is impaired ( Impairment, page 118).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000230_649fdc0b53e7e7cde123a0479ddfd83ef22d7b475364bcd2748dd8badefeeb11.png)

## Involuntary Actions

When a player rolls a fumble (or fails a  POW roll to see if they're impaired; Impairment ,  page  118),  the  GM  may compel  their  investigator  to  commit an involuntary action. The GM should choose  whichever  result  they  feel  is most appropriate for the situation from  the  following  list.  Alternatively, they could roll 1D10 for a random involuntary reaction.

## The investigator:

1. Drops whatever they are holding.
2.  Cries out in terror or frustration.
3.  Says something inappropriate, unwise, or incriminating.
4.  Freezes on the spot.
5.  Sobs uncontrollably or giggles hysterically with fright.
6.  Lashes out with a fist, throws a held weapon, or pulls a trigger.
7. Backs away or hides.
8.  Says or does something to get a friend in trouble, putting them in danger somehow.
9.  Confesses something, or begs for mercy and forgiveness.
10.  Becomes enraged, lashes out, and breaks something.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000231_c61007de22275c94950393c1d244b35f66a33bbfbbd338bf0e8a53e3fa13b606.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000232_ce907e46a92943c5d4e7e42ffd5e8e81e1beebebaf0a1b9ec7068317b012a9b7.png)

## Pushing the Roll

If  a  player  fails  their  initial  skill  or  characteristic  roll,  they have the choice to either spend Luck to change their failure into a success ( Spending Luck , page 113), or they can choose to push the roll. Pushing a skill roll provides the player with a second and final attempt to achieve their goal. A pushed roll is only allowed if it can be justified, and it is up to the player to  do  this.  Whenever  a  player  asks,  ' Can  I  push  the  roll? ' the GM should not simply say yes or no, but instead should always ask, ' What action are you taking to push the situation? ' Then the player should describe the extra effort or time taken to justify a pushed roll. If the player is unsure, other players and the GM might offer suggestions.

By  making  a  pushed  roll,  the  player  is  upping  the  ante and giving the GM permission to inflict dire consequences should the roll be failed a second time. Pushing a roll means pushing the situation to the limit.

- If you are breaking down a door, pushing the roll may mean throwing yourself at it with no heed for your own safety; failing the pushed roll might mean taking 1 damage or attracting unwanted attention because of all the noise you're making.
- If you are searching a room, pushing the roll could mean turning it over quickly; failing the pushed roll means you've left a real mess, making it obvious someone's been there-you've probably broken one or two things as well. Alternatively, pushing the roll could mean spending too long searching when the bad guys might return at any moment; failing the pushed roll might mean you are discovered, which could lead to a confrontation.
- Pushing a roll may mean resorting to less than legal means to achieve your goal, like stealing a vital document because the owner won't let you see it; failing the pushed roll may lead to you being caught. If you are a Police Officer, this might lead to being disciplined, suspended, or even dismissed. For anyone else, failing the pushed roll might mean arrest-or it might mean that the document's owner now has a powerful hold over you, regardless of your occupation.
- If you are trying to understand someone with Read Person, pushing the roll can mean studying that person too obviously; failing the pushed roll means you've probably caused offence by staring at them so hard, and could mean they start shouting at you and causing a scene.
- If you are picking a lock, pushing the roll may mean using a little too much force; failing the pushed roll might mean you break your lock picks-or even the lock. How are you going to get that door open now?
- If casting a spell, pushing the roll may mean channelling every ounce of your willpower into the spell, forcing

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000233_70e9e80da927e69799876ac2202d17a733aff989761476f00aaa5fb16e931d8c.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000234_8ba1720333dcf6f403d4a6e5e86ada62cded595f03dc96dd076b243c9302f607.png)

the formae to line up properly; failing the pushed roll risks severely overreaching your abilities and causing yourself lasting harm ( Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation , page 177).

- If you are climbing a wall, pushing the roll could mean continuing even though it feels completely unsafe, or making a jump upward in the hope of reaching a better perch; failing the pushed roll might mean falling and taking damage, or becoming stuck halfway up.

When making a pushed roll, the goal must be achievable. The skill and difficulty level normally remain unchanged but may be modified if the situation changes.

## GM ADVICE

A  pushed  roll  is  not  simply  a  re-roll;  time  always passes between the attempts (rolls). How much time depends on what the investigator is doing to justify the pushed roll-it may be seconds or even hours. Only skill  and  characteristic  rolls  can  be  pushed; Luck, opposed, and combat rolls cannot be pushed.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000235_ec1e9aec2ec0160f49a8ca82531610aa608ab2e27690d123c434997975370489.png)

## Foreshadowing Failure

Before rolling the dice for a pushed roll, the consequences of failure may be foreshadowed by the GM. If so, the GM says something like, ' If you fail... ' followed by as much detail as they desire. The GM should highlight things that would be apparent  to  the  investigator,  even  if  they're  something  the player hadn't considered. Things the investigator would not be aware of might only be hinted at, at the GM's discretion. Players  are  welcome  to  offer  their  own  suggestions  for consequences-especially as they often come up with more exciting ones!

## There  are  two  reasons  why  the  GM  should  foreshadow the consequence:

- The player requests foreshadowing to clarify the dangers-as the investigator would be aware of them-so that they can gauge the risk and decide whether to make the pushed roll.
- The GM chooses to foreshadow to highlight the danger and increase the drama of the situation.

As  we  mentioned  in Failure (page  106),  it's  always  far more  entertaining  if  the  GM  focuses  on  an  interesting consequence rather than a complete and utter lack of success, so be creative when it comes to foreshadowing what might go wrong if the pushed roll fails.

109

## Pushed Roll: Success

The player's goal is achieved (as it would have been for the original roll) and none of the consequences of failure happen.

Example: Nafeesa manages to escape from the goons at the derelict building, but they come looking for her. Nafeesa is trying to hide behind a market stall in the main street. The GM determines this requires a Stealth roll at Regular difficulty. Nafeesa's Stealth skill is 30%, and her player rolls an 84-a failure. The GM describes the stall as being little more than a few boxes on the pavementnot  enough  to  hide  behind.  Believing  it's  only  a  matter  of  time before she's caught, Nafeesa decides instead to try to hide in plain sight among the passing crowds, hoping that the goons won't see her in the throng of people. The GM approves this as justification for a pushed roll and foreshadows what might happen if the roll is failed, 'Nafeesa is trying to mingle with the crowd of people in the street-if the pushed roll is failed, she could well find herself bumping into one of the pursuers and being caught.'

Nafeesa's player thinks the risk is worth it and makes a second Stealth roll, this time succeeding! Nafeesa's quick thinking means that she melts away into the crowd and escapes.

## Pushed Roll: Failure

Failing  a  pushed  roll  grants  the  GM  free  rein  over  the outcome, which could be damage (1 or 2 depending on the situation; rarely more), loss of equipment, isolation from the rest of the investigators, capture, and so on. The GM can even incorporate the player's goal being achieved, even though the player failed the pushed roll-but at some form of cost. It's that ' Yes, but… ' again…

When  a  player  fails  a  pushed  roll,  they  are  giving  the GM licence to make the investigators' lives more difficult. Remember, the consequence should be interesting and steer the  game  towards  the  dramatic,  taking  the  characters  one step deeper into trouble and/or danger.

Example: in  the  previous  example,  instead  of  succeeding  in the pushed Stealth roll, let's say Nafeesa failed it. The GM now has licence to make matters far worse for Nafeesa and says, 'As Nafeesa tries to blend into the crowd, she finds herself jostled as she goes against the flow of people. She is pushed against a  market  stall  and  stumbles  over  it.  As  Nafeesa  hits  the pavement, fruit falling on her head, her sudden cry makes the crowd part around her. She looks up to see everyone staring at her, including the goons who were in pursuit. They quickly shout out, in fake friendly voices, 'There you are! Sorry we got separated. Let's get you up, eh?' as they come forward and grab Nafeesa. The crowd seems satisfied, and no one notices the knife that one of the goons is now holding to Nafeesa's ribs as they march her into an alley.'

3

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000236_56e6183120106f8ac819dfe646374ec15fbb297023def5c335c77c85ece3d4bf.png)

## Can Multiple Players Roll Dice for a Skill Roll?

Sometimes,  a  scene  comes  to  a  point  when two or more characters want to work together towards the same goal. This is most likely to occur  when  investigators  are  cooperating  to achieve  a  task  that  one  investigator  would otherwise be unable to complete on their own ( Human Limits ,  page  112),  but  there  are  other occasions where this might also be the case. How  many  characters  may  be  involved  in  a particular task is up to the GM.

## Some potential situations include:

- Reading a book: only one person at a time may read the book if there's only a single physical copy of it available, unless their associates can somehow get hold of another physical copy or a digital version to read as well.
- Picking a lock: only one person may attempt to pick the lock, either by rolling the Locksmith skill or by casting Clausurafrange (page 190).
- Carrying a body: up to four people, each taking one limb, could assist in carrying a body. If the body were exceptionally heavy, each character involved would be asked to make a STR roll; as long as the person with the highest STR value succeeds at their roll and no one else fumbles, they complete the task.
- Research: all the investigators can consult a resource like the internet, so they may all make a Research roll. Only one successful roll is required to find the soughtfor information.
- Spotting a clue: everyone who's in the right location may roll Observation. Only one successful roll is required.
- Determining if someone is withholding information: everyone who can observe the subject may roll Read Person. Only one successful roll is required to figure out what the subject's intentions are, or, for example, realise whether or not they are lying.
- Sneaking past a guard: the player with the lowest Stealth skill should be asked to roll; on a failure, the guard is alerted to their presence-the GM should decide if everyone has been detected or whether it's just the person who failed the roll.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000237_4f4d21c9072d14a1a065e6e4de696d31d9f4654f62660dcb2641c7a1f168d3af.png)

The examples above are intended as guidelines. For tasks in which the number of participants is restricted, like reading a single copy of a book or attempting to pick a lock, further attempts  may  be  made  by  other  characters, but  such  attempts  always  require  additional time.  It  may  not  be  apparent  in  some  situations  whether  another  skill  roll  should  be allowed or if a player is required to push a skill roll-that's for the GM to decide. In general, when an investigator  is  attempting  the  same goal a second time, ask for a pushed roll.

Example: two  players  wish  to  interrogate  a suspect  and  decide  to  use  the  classic  good cop,  bad  cop  routine.  Both  use  their  Social skill, with one aiming to intimidate, the other to befriend. They take it in turns to play their roles, each rolling against their Social skill to see if the suspect cracks. Once the characters have both failed one dice roll, they must either give up, or one of them must push the roll.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000238_5e5e89842b03825c28fea46814ed632b15406cc38cec8252260d8473a8f37442.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000239_0b3b7927f89d399ec46c41d9ef0f1c37b595d2c0f2ddfba7844f785e6e93caa4.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000240_593c042955dd46604caeacc560a2f201f5e7985b4d89b680d2ffd51a8d556995.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000241_c06e09751ded74c4a1607c60784741cb91513bf7a42450b4261ca9b4f322e5b7.png)

## Failed Dice Rolls and Sudden Endings

Wherever  possible,  the  GM  should  avoid  describing  an outcome that brings the game to an abrupt halt or end. Be creative and describe an outcome that allows play to proceed, just  not  necessarily  in  the  way  the  players  intended.  In situations that would suggest sudden death as an outcome, use some of the options we've already mentioned, such as:

- A setback: a loss of equipment or the death or injury of an NPC ally.
- A deal: the enemy offers a pact or deal.
- The investigator is taken captive or knocked unconscious, rather than killed.
- Fainting: the character passes out, only to awaken later to find themselves unharmed and the situation changed.

Example: Nafeesa has fallen into the River Thames and has failed her Athletics roll to swim to the bank. The GM describes how she's floundering and offers her a pushed roll, which she also fails. Now the GM considers the options. The obvious outcome is that Nafeesa drowns, but that takes her out of the game. While investigators aren't invulnerable, it's good to find ways to keep the story going, if at all possible. So, the GM has the scene fade to black. After dealing with what's happening to the other investigators, the GM returns to Nafeesa a little while later and describes how she has washed up on the banks of the Thames, battered and bruised. The downside is anything she had of value on her person is now lost or ruined, but, on the other hand, she's alive! And possibly owes Mama Thames and her girls a favour…

## Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice

We've  talked  about  the  difficulty  level  of  a  roll,  which is  determined  by  how  challenging  someone  would  find that  task  to  perform.  Sometimes,  though,  there  are  other factors  that  can  affect  the  dice  roll,  such  as  the  prevailing conditions, the environment, and the time available. Under these circumstances, the GM may allocate a 'bonus' die or a 'penalty' die to a roll rather than adjusting the difficulty level.  A  bonus  die  represents  a  positive  influence  of  some sort,  whereas  a  penalty  die  represents  a  negative  influence. Some Advantages and Disadvantages (pages  50  and  311, respectively)  may  also  apply  a  bonus  or  penalty  die  under certain situations; see each advantage and disadvantage for further details.

If an influence is so small that it would only modify the situation  in  a  minor  way,  don't  bother  allocating  a  bonus or  penalty  die-keep  these  for  significant  things  that  will influence the attempt. Driving normally in light rain should not  incur  a  penalty  die,  but  racing  through  torrential  rain probably calls for a penalty die!

applied to a skill or characteristic roll. For more details, see Multiple Bonus or Penalty Dice on page 143. In addition, one bonus die and one penalty die cancel each other out when applied to the same skill roll.

## ADDITIONAL RULES

The alternative character creation rules permit your players  to  start  with  more  experienced  investigators;  however,  an  experienced  investigator  must start play with a Disadvantage (page 311).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000242_20e1b719bab277f11b36086825856d4d96a5320f403daedbe5c153f1067f40aa.png)

## Rolling a Bonus/Penalty Die

When a bonus or penalty die is allocated, roll an additional tens  percentile  die  alongside  the  usual  pair  of  percentile dice when making the skill roll. You are now rolling three separate dice: one units die and two tens dice.

- If you gain a bonus die: use the tens die that yields the better (lowest) result.

Example: Nafeesa  is  in  Jimmy  Four-Fingers' apartment and wants to search the place. Jimmy is in a police cell, so there's no rush. Since she can take her time, the GM grants her a bonus die on her Observation roll. Nafeesa's player rolls a 20 and 60 on the two tens dice and a 1 on the units die, which are combined to read as either 21 or 61. As the extra die  was  a  bonus  die,  the  player  takes the lower roll of 21, resulting in a Hard success against Nafeesa's Observation skill of 60%. The GM describes how Nafeesa finds a SIM card taped to the underside of a drawer. 60

20

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000243_99289511fafdf74e35a0316af144ec95774b96f35afe18e4660b713d88c35e5b.png)

- If you incur a penalty die: use the tens die that yields the worse (highest) result.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000244_6617606fcecf3c1efe0a07dfcb733feec509349ad2a8be25c3a5c16a68b964e8.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000245_93250f4429d97fb99093a7445c656c09d525004e283601f875a009d8bc25f1ae.png)

Example: Nafeesa pays Jimmy Four-Fingers a visit  at  his  apartment.  While  she's  there,  he gets a phone call and steps into another room to speak privately. Nafeesa uses the time to do a hasty search of the room. The GM calls for an Observation roll, but says it incurs a penalty die because time is short and Nafeesa  is  rushing.  The  player  rolls  a 20 and 60 on the two tens dice and a 4 on the units die, which can be combined to  read  24  or  64.  As  the  extra  die  was  a 20

Note that bonus dice and penalty dice do not stack. You can only have a maximum of one bonus die or one penalty die penalty  die,  the  player  must  take  the  higher  result-a  failure! Nafeesa hears Jimmy ending his call and nonchalantly returns to her seat, having found nothing.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000246_c28dd459632808f1338728a74f7181f615ccb2941730fd431084b8ae2ae9ee12.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000247_492440d3463b1096daf9bdcd3e290942a33a8e414d598a5acc42c25d4d682e83.png)

Where the result of a penalty die means the roll is failed, the player could spend Luck points to turn that  failure  into  a  success,  but  we'll  talk  more about that in the Spending Luck section (page 113).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000248_121b29bf102844ab8815aae317f7ec24b6bf395f9450cb5c3da38705dfe934e1.png)

## GM ADVICE

## Difficulty Levels vs Bonus &amp; Penalty Dice

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000249_dce0fc2bce1bbf701505cf76ef72ea08501959c7fe9bf6c8f6678ec32ba81ef3.png)

If  you're  unsure  whether  to  change the  difficulty  level  of  a  roll  or  apply a  bonus  or  penalty  die,  think  about what's happening in the scene. Is it the circumstances  around  the  task  that are  affecting  the  investigator's  ability to  perform  it,  or  is  it  the  task  itself that's posing the challenge? If it's the task, change the difficulty level. If it's the circumstances, allocate a bonus or penalty die. Sometimes there may not be a clear answer, so go with what feels right for that situation.

You  can,  if  you  want,  alter  the  difficulty  level  and  allocate  a  bonus  or penalty die at the same time, but this should  be  the  exception  rather  than the rule. Don't forget that some investigator  advantages  and  disadvantages also allocate a bonus or penalty die to certain skill rolls ( Advantages and Disadvantages ,  pages  50  and  311,  respectively).

In  the  case  of Opposed  Skill  Rolls (page  116),  you  don't  set  difficulty  levels. So, if there are factors that provide a distinct edge or drawback for one of the characters involved, the GM should allocate a bonus die or a penalty die to only that player.

## Human Limits

There are limits to what humans can achieve. As we mentioned in Skill  Rolls: Determining  the  Difficulty Level (page 105),  an  unopposed  skill  or  characteristic  roll  moves  from Regular to Hard if the skill or characteristic being used to set the difficulty level has a value of 70 or above.

- The upper limit of what can be overcome with a Hard success is 100 + the character's skill or characteristic.
- Achieving any task where the opponent's skill or characteristic is beyond 100 + the character's skill or characteristic is impossible for that character, and no dice roll is allowed.

Example: Nafeesa has STR 70. When attempting to make a STR roll to shove an NPC out of her way, the GM checks that NPC's STR to determine the difficulty level. Thus, if the NPC's STR is between 70 and 170, the difficulty level of Nafeesa's STR roll is Hard. Given the maximum STR for a human is 100, pushing a human out of her way is never going to be beyond Nafeesa's limit. However, certain creatures are stronger than humans, so if it was a  creature  and  its  STR  exceeded  170-like  a  police  horse  or  a unicorn-Nafeesa would automatically be unable to move them (no need for a roll).

A  character  can  seek  help  in  achieving  tasks  that  are beyond  their  human  limit.  This  usually  concerns  tests  of strength,  like  the  one  in  our  example  above  (see  also Can Multiple Players Roll Dice for a Skill Roll? ,  page 110). If an  investigator  seeks  assistance  from  their  colleagues,  the GM should think about how many people would need to be involved to make the task achievable, and how that affects the  difficulty  level  of  the  skill  roll.  Everyone  involved  gets to roll at the new difficulty level determined by the GM. As long as the investigator with the highest value in the relevant skill or characteristic succeeds and no one else fumbles, the investigators  jointly  achieve  their  goal.  Of  course,  if  the attempt is failed it can be pushed ( Pushing the Roll ,  page 108), or Luck could be used to alter the outcome ( Spending Luck ,  page 113)-although remember that fumbles cannot be avoided by spending Luck.

When it's a heavy inanimate object, like a car, rather than a person or creature, the GM should judge how many people they think could feasibly attempt to move the object and how hard they think it would be to do so.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000250_d74da78ceadb275961064858e31e089457983b41e01630a7dbc5f864c2e89677.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000251_e29a7cc33686df5d2987929cb3a143fa1180f7a150f3133f177482557b5d43ba.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000252_246ed31aa1610cd4ddb406b41ecf9087fd57f1fdc2a2a97dea5893cb5e457503.png)

Example: Nafeesa calls on her colleague Eli Venturini (STR 50) to help move an unconscious person to safety. The GM judges that, even with two reasonably strong people like Nafeesa and Eli, this is still going to be a tricky thing to do because the unconscious person is very large, and so decides to set the difficulty level at Hard. Both Nafeesa and Eli's players must now make a Hard STR roll to see if they can move the person. Nafeesa has the higher STR of 70, and

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000253_468b4ee08e0479200ea09b0c3cf93c1ccfd7c7ffc0a275b99de089d40a362a79.png)

so she must roll equal to or below 35. As long as Eli doesn't fumble, they succeed in moving the person to safety.

However, if Jordan Schneider (STR 70) and Mina Patel (STR 40) also lend a hand, things would be different. The GM judges that, with more people involved, the difficulty lowers to a Regular STR roll-there is strength in numbers! As long as Nafeesa rolls a success and the others don't fumble, the person is moved to safety.

## GM ADVICE

While the temptation may be to let as many people help  as  possible,  there  may  be  a  limit  on  how many  people  could  realistically  do  so-in  terms of  accessibility,  if  nothing  else-if  space  is  limited or the target object is reasonably small. And don't forget:  the  more  people  who  are  involved,  the higher the chances are (statistically) that someone is going to fumble!

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000254_4f1345835a20983aa7a77a40db720fbcecbbb5face5e9a5f1b6210ec4449a5c2.png)

## Intelligence Rolls

Everyone knows bits of information about a range of different topics. An Intelligence (INT) roll can represent what's stored in  the  brain's  attic-perhaps  something  the  investigator learned in school, from reading, or from watching documentaries  and  the  like.  For  example,  the  investigator might know it's dangerous to pour water into sulphuric acid without  possessing  the  Science  (Chemistry)  skill  or  know how many legs spiders have without the Science (Biology) skill. Identification of present-day languages is an excellent use for the Intelligence roll. Remember, though, if a specific skill is more applicable to the situation, then it should be used rather than making an INT roll.

While some players like solving in-game puzzles, others don't, so the GM can call for an INT roll when an investigator is attempting to solve an intellectual conundrum. By  successfully  rolling  equal  to  or  below  the  investigator's INT value, the player receives a solution from the GM, e.g., solving a puzzle or remembering the answer to a riddle.

## Luck

All investigators have a Luck value. Some NPCs-such as really important human antagonists or members of the demimonde-also have a Luck value, but most regular humans do not. Luck has many uses in the game.

## Luck Rolls

Luck  rolls  may  be  called  for  by  the  GM  when  external circumstances  are  in  question,  and  when  determining  the fickle hand of fate. But if a skill or characteristic is applicable to a situation, then it should be used rather than Luck. To make a Luck roll, the player attempts to roll under or equal to their current Luck value.

113

For example, finding one of London's iconic black taxicabs doesn't normally require a dice roll, but getting one quickly in  the  middle  of  chase  might-' Follow  that  car! '  At  most times of day, Affluence Rating (page 57) could be a factor in attracting the attention of a taxi driver on the lookout for a well-dressed fare who may tip generously. No skill is going to make a taxi appear, though-it's a matter of chance whether one may be driving down the road, so the GM must decide or call for a Luck roll instead.

Luck rolls are handy to determine if an investigator has remembered to pack a vital piece of equipment before they left the house this morning or remembered to fill the car up before going on a long journey. If a player asks, ' Do I have a light with me? '  ask  them to make a Luck roll.
