If the roll is successful, then yes, they have a torch; if it's failed, then no-or maybe they do, but the batteries are dead, which is something they won't discover until they try to use it later. A successful Luck roll may permit the investigator to find the item they need at their current location, such as ball of string or an improvised weapon.

- If the Luck roll concerns more than one investigator, it becomes a 'group Luck roll,' and the player whose character has the lowest Luck score among those present should make the roll on behalf of the group.
- If something bad is supposed to happen to one random member of the group, the GM can ask who has the lowest Luck score at that moment and have that individual suffer the unfortunate event.

As  well  as  Luck  rolls,  investigators  can  use  their  Luck value in two ways to achieve better results, called Spending Luck (following) and Trying Your Luck (page 114).

## GM ADVICE

Luck rolls are great for determining if the investigators  have  enough  signal  to  make  that  allimportant phone call or access the internet on their mobile devices. But only use this where there could reasonably  be  a  patchy  service-like  the  middle of  the  countryside-otherwise  it  will  look  as  if you're just being mean.

## Spending Luck

An investigator's Luck value is also a pool of Luck points. After the player has made a skill or characteristic roll, they can spend their Luck points to alter the result, if desired.

- A player can use Luck points to alter a roll on a onefor-one basis, i.e., for every point of Luck spent, the dice result is reduced by one. For example, aiming for

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000255_a68aa6a467e39049a6e83788c386403ba239de3c0180a012b7b2e39a94fe72c6.png)

3

- a Regular success against a Social skill of 60%, you roll 66-a failure; you can let the failure stand or spend 6 Luck points to reduce the roll's outcome to 60, making it a success.
- A player may spend any number of Luck points, up to their current Luck value, on a given roll.
- The points spent are deducted from the investigator's Luck value, which reduces the chance of passing future Luck rolls.

The use of Luck points can be narrated if it feels appropriate, but there is no requirement to do this if you can't think of anything  that  fits  the  situation.  So,  if  a  player  decides  to spend Luck points to convert their investigator's Stealth roll into a success, they might suggest, for example, that just as the guard was about to spot them, they were distracted by their phone ringing or that the investigator was about to kick a discarded drink can but stepped over it at the last minute, and so didn't alert the guard to their presence.

## What Can I Spend Luck On?

Luck can be spent on most skill and characteristic rolls made by investigators, including opposed and unopposed rolls, e.g., Fighting and Firearms skill rolls during combat, and Magic skill rolls when casting spells (in or out of combat).

- Spend Luck points to alter the dice result to 01 to achieve a Critical success, or to change a Regular success to a Hard one, and so on.
- Spend Luck points to prevent an investigator from dying if they suffer a fatal blow in combat ( Damage Conditions , page 133).
- Spend Luck points on combat damage rolls to increase the amount of damage inflicted on a target. This means that Luck points can be used to improve your level of success on the dice roll-it does not mean that you can spend 1 Luck point to add 1 damage ( The Damage Roll , page 132).
- Spend Luck points to reduce the effect of wounds after a fight ( Wounds and Healing , page 144).

Example: Nafeesa attempts to sneak past Jimmy Four-Fingers. The GM uses Jimmy's Observation skill to set the difficulty level. Jimmy has Observation 60%, which is below 70, so Nafeesa needs a Regular Success. Nafeesa has Stealth 30% and rolls 34-at this stage, a failure. Nafeesa's player chooses to spend 4 Luck to lower her result from 34 to 30, and so achieve a Regular Success. Thus, she tip-toes silently past Jimmy, who doesn't notice a thing.

## GM ADVICE

Remember,  when  an  unopposed  skill  roll  is  failed, the  player  usually  has  the  option  to  push  the  roll or spend Luck points; the choice is theirs. However, when an opposed skill roll is failed, the player only has the option to spend Luck to change the result, as you can't push opposed rolls-the same goes for combat rolls ( Pushing the Roll , page 108).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000256_0edb1a4bab3a5b63a283e5afdd32609f0d1408c1ab10dc10212fd6d64d1e7e45.png)

## What Can't I Spend Luck On?

There are a few things that Luck points cannot be spent on.

- Luck points cannot be spent on Luck rolls.
- Fumbles always apply and cannot be bought off with Luck points.
- Luck points cannot be spent to alter the result of a pushed roll.
- A player may only spend Luck to alter one of their own dice rolls, not someone else's.

In play, a character's Luck decreases through spending and rises through recovery ( Recovering Luck Points , page 115).

## Trying Your Luck

Another useful trick is 'Trying Your Luck.' If a particular expert skill is required and none of the investigators present in the scene currently possess it, any investigator can spend 10  Luck  points  to  attempt  the  roll,  allowing  the  player  to use  the  half  value  of  one  of  their  characteristics  ( Table 4: Which Characteristic  Should  I  Use? ,  page  117)  in  place of the expert skillfor this one skill roll only. If they fail the skill  roll,  they  may  spend  additional  Luck  points  to  make it a success (as per Spending Luck , page 113), or they may choose to push the roll (no additional Luck points need to be spent in order to be allowed to push the roll).

If an investigator makes a successful use of an expert skill through the Trying Your Luck method, and no one else in the group has that expert skill, they can now choose to spend an additional 10 Luck points to permanently add that skill to their character sheet at a rating equal to half their appropriate characteristic value. This means spending a total of 20 Luck to retain that expert skill (possibly more, if they used Luck points to make the roll a success).

Rather than thinking of this as the investigator spontaneously  acquiring  the  skill,  think  of  it  as  revealing (through play) something they already knew. This also means that the investigator is the only one in the group who's got that expert skill, so whenever it's required, people are going to look to them. There is no limit on how often a character can  use  Trying  Your  Luck  to  make  skill  rolls,  but  each investigator may not acquire more than one new expert skill per game session through this method. Note that the only

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000257_69419e71c6b51db904edc4ffc1b41ce5a7328d7dee2e8837826f73736523a4de.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000258_590b2f9327d380c7b412228d4c41d9bb133df12206f99aaf587914dbb91155e5.png)

## To Spend or Not to Spend?

Whether it's worth spending Luck points on a roll or not can be something of a vexed question, and for players, it can be a tricky balancing act between using Luck to ensure they succeed and keeping enough in reserve in case the GM calls for a Luck roll at a crucial moment. So, here's our advice on the matter.

## GMs

Encourage your players to spend Luck points when  they  really  want  to  achieve  success. Sometimes, all a player needs is a Regular success-if  spending  Luck  points  to  raise  a  Regular  success to a Hard success isn't going to benefit the player, then tell them so. If they are determined to defeat some opponents in combat, the players probably want to spend Luck  points  to  win  the  opposed  rolls  rather than just rely on the fate of their dice rolls.

## Players

Never  be  afraid  to  check  with  the  GM  to see  if  a  roll  is  worth  spending  Luck  points

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000259_c36812b348e6caecbd6a11c24aee1a0b9033357da35701b3af205bc0b5310f4f.png)

skill this doesn't apply to is Magic, as an investigator must first have the Magical advantage to acquire this skill.

## Recovering Luck Points

After every session of play, each player makes an improvement check for their investigator's  Luck.  The  player  rolls  1D100 and  if  the  roll  is above their  present  Luck  value,  they  add 1D10 points to their Luck. If the roll is equal to or below the investigator's present Luck value, no points are recovered.

Once during each case file, each investigator may choose to take some rest and relaxation (R&amp;R) and gain an automatic increase  of  1D10  Luck  points.  The  player  should  describe what  their  character  is  doing  for  their  R&amp;R,  and  they're encouraged to tie this into the elements of their backstory. We're not talking about a five-minute breather here, though-it  should  be  a  morning,  afternoon,  or  evening's worth of restful  activity,  like  settling  down  on  the  sofa  to watch the rugby, reading a book, or going for a nice long walk to clear your head.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000260_9ec6783e625be8c1e3211c62e806fb6efb8240b49e5855050438017e4e98edb7.png)

on. A player can always ask the GM if spending  Luck  points  is  worth  it  in  the  situation at  hand.  The  GM  should  be  honest  and  say whether it is or not.

Timing  is  important.  If  it's  early  in  a  game session, players may not want to spend large amounts  of  Luck  in  case  a  more  serious  or urgent  situation  later  arises.  If  it's  late  in  a session or case file, players can afford to be a little bolder with their spending, as they will be able to improve their Luck values sooner rather than later (Recovering Luck Points , page 115).

Remember,  an  investigator's  Luck  points are the player's resource, so they get to use them in a way that best supports their enjoyment of the game. Some players like to make their investigators' lives difficult-sorry, more interesting-so they always have the option of not spending Luck points. If a player feels that it's more dramatic for their investigator not to succeed at everything they do, then that's fine. But, hey, there's also a good case for if you've got it, flaunt it…

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000261_6d6351bd2f3f831af504f29a5dbe04fe0c21cbf383d992b07adf9348d42f7a6d.png)

- If the result is less than or equal to their investigator's current Luck value, their Luck remains unchanged. If the result exceeds their investigator's current Luck value, their Luck is raised to be equal to the higher value.

If the player doesn't spend any Luck  points, their investigator's Luck value is likely to rise towards a score of 70 over multiple cases or adventures; chances are, however, it's going to get spent! An investigator's Luck value usually varies throughout play. The starting Luck value is not used again in the game, except for optional fae characters ( Playing Fae Investigators , page 318). Thus, the starting Luck value can be exceeded in play.

At the start of every new case file, each player rolls 2D10+50 and compares it to their investigator's current Luck value:

Example: Nafeesa is pursuing a suspect into  the  London Underground.  They  both  skip  the  barriers  and  race  down  the escalator. A tube train is just leaving. The GM asks for a DEX roll  to  see  if  Nafeesa  can  board  the  train  before  the  doors  close. Nafeesa has DEX 40 and rolls 46-she isn't going to make it! Nafeesa's  Luck  is  at  57,  and  the  player  chooses  to  spend  6  Luck

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000262_431e16d2eb0a068c986111dddfb19c606bb542928c788629a1e974e79cf727d6.png)

3

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000263_897124b99e0fa35b37c60f63bd2cf771ba6abaf9479e657d87a418e57fccf47e.png)

points, reducing her result by 6 down to 40, just making it aboard the train as the doors close behind her.

At the end of the session, Nafeesa's player makes a check for Luck recovery. Nafeesa's Luck is currently 51 points. The player rolls 73 and so adds 1D10 to Nafeesa's Luck; they roll an 8, increasing her current Luck to 59.

## Opposed Skill Rolls

There may be times when two characters are opposing one another,  when  both  sides  need  to  roll  dice  to  determine  a victor. Opposed skill rolls, such as these, are the standard for melee combat ( Combat , page 123). Opposed rolls will most often be made against the GM (who is rolling for an NPC)such as during combat-but they may also occasionally occur between investigators.

To make an opposed roll, both sides declare a goal, which ultimately  boils  down  to  one  will  win  and  the  other  will lose.  The  goal  should  specify  a  definite  action  or  outcome the player wishes to achieve. Be very clear about this. Saying an investigator is shoving someone describes what they are doing, but what is the goal? Is the goal to make them fall down the stairs, fall to the floor, drop what they are holding, or break their concentration so they can't cast a spell? What outcome is the investigator trying to achieve?

Having established the opposing character's goals (probably just trying to stop whatever the investigator is attempting), each side selects a skill or a characteristic to use-not necessarily the same one-both of which must be approved by the GM. Once that's done, the opposing players both roll percentile dice. The side with the higher level of success is the winner.

In an opposed roll, there should always be a winner and a loser.  Story-wise, both sides failing wouldn't be much fun. If both sides roll over their skill value and one gets a failure while  the  other  gets  a  fumble,  then  that  'failure'  instead becomes a 'Weak success,' as a failure always beats a fumble in an opposed roll ( Failure (or Weak Success) , page 106).

If the results are tied, i.e., both sides get the same level of success (or failure), then a tiebreaker is used. It's possible for

Critical Success &gt; Hard Success &gt; Regular Success &gt; Failure (or Weak Success) &gt; Fumble

Best result                                                                                                      Worst result

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000264_885efe174c57c0ae677b6de44e15549e11b37837c15e91427a52d2efed9b3d38.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000265_958b237d2c3105cd2778252ac3bfa951c4b24522cae85a709943cfee1dd4e2ca.png)

## Table 4: Which Characteristic Should I Use?

When Trying Your Luck, refer to the chosen expert skill below to find the related characteristic. When more than one characteristic is listed, use the one with the higher rating.

| Expert Skill    | Attribute        | Expert Skill      | Attribute   |
|-----------------|------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| Accounting      | INT              | Mechanical Repair | DEX or INT  |
| Animal Handling | INT or POW       | Medicine          | INT         |
| Appraise        | INT              | Occult            | INT         |
| Art/Craft       | DEX, INT, or POW | Pilot Aircraft    | INT         |
| Computer Use    | INT              | Pilot Boat        | DEX or INT  |
| Demolitions     | DEX or INT       | Read Lips         | INT         |
| Disguise        | DEX or POW       | Ride              | DEX or STR  |
| Diving          | CON or DEX       | Science           | INT         |
| History         | INT              | Sleight of Hand   | DEX         |
| Languages       | INT              | Survival          | CON or INT  |
| Law             | INT              | Tech              | DEX or INT  |
| Locksmith       | DEX              | Track             | INT         |

a failure to become a Weak success during the Tiebreakers process (following).

Note: opposed skill rolls cannot be pushed.

## Tiebreakers

Sometimes, both sides in an opposed roll achieve the same level of success (or failure): a tie! Now, while an impasse might occasionally  make  sense  depending  on  the  circumstances, it  can  cause  the  story  to  slow  down.  To  prevent  that  from happening, there are a couple of ways to break a tie and keep the action moving.

## Breaking the Tie by Spending Luck Points

If the player has enough Luck points, they can spend these to improve their level of success: for example, from a failure to a Regular success, from a Regular success to a Hard success, or from a Hard to a Critical success. As long as the level of success  they've  adjusted  their  roll  to  is  higher  than  that  of their opponent, they win. Knowing when to do this can be tricky, and there may be times where it's actually better not to spend Luck to break a tie and instead save it for another occasion ( To Spend or Not to Spend? , page 115).

## Highest Skill Wins

If the two sides remain tied on the same level of success, the skills  that  were  used  are  compared,  and  the  side  with  the higher skill value wins. Combat is an exception to this, where the character whose turn it is wins the tie ( Opposed Combat Rolls , page 131). If the two sides have the same skill value, the GM must decide to either let the tie stand or to use a random tiebreaker to determine a winner, e.g., simply asking one side to pick odd or even, and then rolling a D6. You could even just toss a coin!

Example: Nafeesa is arm-wrestling her colleague Jordan Schneider; the loser must pay for lunch. The GM calls for an opposed STR roll as it isn't really a combat situation. Both have STR 70. Nafeesa's player rolls 37; a success. Jordan's player rolls 59, also a success-a tie! Nafeesa's a little low on cash today and really wants to win. As this is an opposed roll, it can't be pushed, so Nafeesa's player decides to spend 2 points of Luck to turn her result into a Hard success. As it's the level of success that matters in an opposed roll ( Opposed Skill Rolls , page 116), Nafeesa wins, and Jordan's left with the bill.

## Impairment

There are situations where the investigators may be exposed to physical or mental stresses or trauma. For example, physical trauma can occur as an outcome of combat or through other physical harm, such as a car crash or falling from a window, while witnessing violence or other shocking events may lead to mental trauma. Both could lead to an investigator suffering from what we call 'impairment.'

- In game terms, suffering from impairment increases an investigator's fumble range.
- While an investigator is impaired, a dice roll of 90 or above for any skill roll counts as a fumble-rather than the usual result of 100 ( Fumble , page 107).

Note: players  cannot  spend  Luck  points  to  alter  a  fumble ( What Can't I Spend Luck On? , page 114).

## Suffering Impairment

Impairment caused by physical trauma is dealt with separately under Wounds  and  Healing (page 144). This section discusses impairment caused by mental trauma.

If  an  investigator  is  in  a  stressful  situation,  the  GM considers whether what they are seeing or experiencing could cause impairment. The GM should take their lead from how the investigator is reacting; for example, are they panicking or displaying fear, shock, or horror at what's going on around them? If they are, the GM should call for a Regular POW roll. It may be the case that a player suggests their investigator is in a stressful situation and should make a POW roll.

If the player passes the POW roll, the investigator is fine. If, however, the roll is failed, the investigator is impaired, and the player should mark the Impaired box on their character sheet  to  remind  them.  The  investigator  also  performs  an Involuntary Action (page 108).

In  story  terms,  an  impaired  character  finds  it  hard  to focus or concentrate, or they may zone out, lose motivation, or experience an emotional outburst because of the trauma (mental or physical) they've experienced.

Example: Nafeesa comes under gunfire for the first time, and the GM asks for a POW roll. Nafeesa's POW is 60, and her player rolls an 87: a fail; she decides not to spend 27 Luck points to turn that into a success, so the result stands. Nafeesa is now impaired and the player marks the Impaired box on her character sheet. The GM thinks a suitable involuntary action would be to freeze. Nafeesa's player agrees and narrates how the young police officer stops dead in her tracks, trying to work out where the shot came from.

## GM ADVICE

An investigator with the Steadfast advantage ( Advantages ,  page  50)  doesn't have to make a POW roll  for  impairment under certain conditions. They may still suffer impairment if they are taken down or  receive  a  mortal  wound,  though  ( Wounds and Healing , page 144).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000270_360483642f87928f4b0434b6c953c1b10b898148ec4dd299f4a92fb436ec3518.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000271_dd1e81796034ca3461ab6e15350f6bdaffc2fe082edd467c03d3e9d2cb67f573.png)

## Some  situations  where  the  GM  may  wish  to  check  for impairment are:

- When an investigator is the target of gunfire.
- When an investigator sees a close friend or ally get seriously injured (i.e., suffers a Mortal Wound , page 133).
- When an investigator stumbles across or sees a grisly corpse.
- When an investigator sees a close friend or ally die.
- When an investigator witnesses a shocking or unexpected manifestation of the supernatural.

Some of these situations may always warrant an impairment check, such as witnessing a close friend or ally being seriously hurt, but the GM can decide that a particular situation only affects an investigator the first time they experience it, e.g., the first time an investigator is shot at with a gun, as in our previous example. Equally, the GM may wish to call for a POW roll if, for example, an investigator is later fired on by a more powerful type of gun, or a significant amount of time has passed since the original shooting happened, or if the investigator suffered a mortal wound as a result of being shot on a previous occasion.

The GM may also decide that if someone has been warned they  are  about  to  see  something  horrific,  such  as  a  grisly corpse, they can mentally prepare themselves and thus avoid the POW roll; alternatively, the GM could grant the player a  bonus  die  on  the  roll  to  represent  their  attempt  to  steel themselves ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111).

Example: having  recovered  from  her  experience  of  being  shot at  ( Impairment and Recovery ,  nearby),  Nafeesa  is  called  to  a murder scene. She hasn't seen a dead body before, but her player states that, when Nafeesa first arrives on-site, she asks the officer in charge for a full briefing-including details of what she's going to see when she's taken through the cordon-so she can ready herself for what's ahead. As a result, the GM decides to grant a bonus die

## GM ADVICE

There is no hard and fast rule as to whether something causes an impairment check only once or every time an  investigator  encounters  it-it  should  always  be judged by the GM based on the current situation and the nature of the traumatic event, as well as taking into  account  the  events  of  the  story  so  far  and  an investigator's  personal  backstory.  As  always,  use impairment as a tool to help make the story dramatic and  engaging  for  the  players.  If  it  doesn't  make sense  to  have  an  investigator  make  that  POW  roll, don't ask for one.

And remember, an investigator is either impaired or they are not. Once they are impaired, an investigator can't suffer from further impairment until they've recovered ( Impairment and Recovery , nearby).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000272_2c0574e461b249cbe27e8eeafb5ae87d8c0fd7ead787561211cd4a2a712238d3.png)

119

on her POW roll. Nafeesa's POW is 60. Her player rolls 74 and 24 (thanks to the bonus die), and so takes the best result: a Hard success! Nafeesa avoids becoming impaired this time.

A year later,  Nafeesa  is  called  to  another  murder  scene.  She's seen  quite  a  few  dead  bodies  since  that  first  one,  and  the  GM decides Nafeesa is used to dealing with corpses now, so no POW roll is required.

## Other Effects of Impairment

As well as increasing an investigator's fumble range, when the  GM  considers  an  impaired  character  is  in  a  stressful situation,  they  can  ask  the  player  for  a  Regular  POW  roll to test the character's composure. On a roll of 90 or above, the  character  performs  an Involuntary Action (page  108), perhaps flying into a rage or sobbing uncontrollably. Lasting for  just  a  second  or  two,  the  investigator  acts  normally following an involuntary action, but their reaction can have an impact on following events.

Example: not long after the shooting incident, Nafeesa attempts to  persuade Jimmy Four-Fingers to give her the name of one of his  contacts.  The  GM asks for a Social roll and Nafeesa's player rolls 94. This counts as a fumble, as Nafeesa is impaired. Nafeesa's previous experience has obviously left her more shaken than she realised.  The  GM  narrates  an  involuntary  action  for  Nafeesa, telling how she flies into a rage and slams Jimmy against the wall, screaming threats at him. The involuntary action is brief, but as Nafeesa  regains  her  self-control,  she  realises  that  a  passer-by  is filming the incident on their phone...

## Impairment and Recovery

Impairment isn't a permanent condition. When an investigator is impaired, they can recover in one of the following ways:

- When an investigator ends a case file and begins a new one, they erase the check mark in the Impaired box on their character sheet, indicating they are no longer impaired.
- At any time, a player may choose to spend 10 Luck points to remove the impairment check mark from their character sheet. They should be invited to explain how they somehow managed to bury or recover from their trauma.
- If an investigator takes a break from the case file of three or more days, maybe to spend time relaxing with friends and family, they may make a Regular POW roll (the GM may wish to play this out as a short scene within the scenario). With a success, the investigator recovers from their impairment and removes the check mark from their character sheet. They gain a bonus die to the POW roll if they attend therapy or counselling ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111).

3

## Investigator Development Phase

People's experiences shape them, and investigators in RoL:RPG are  no  different:  they  grow  and  evolve  as  they interact with the world around them. We represent this growth in the game through the 'investigator development phase.'

Typically, this phase occurs at the end of a case file or, if playing through a campaign, at the conclusion of a chapter. As every group has their own pace at which they play the game, an investigator development phase may happen after one  session  or  it  may  be  several  sessions  before  you  reach one.  If  a  case  file  or  campaign  chapter  continues  beyond, say, three or four sessions, the GM should look for a pause in the narrative to allow an investigator development phase to take place.

## There are four main stages to the investigator development phase:

- Spending Development Points (following) to improve skills, to buy a new skill, to buy a new spell, to master a spell, or to buy an advantage ( Spending Development Points , nearby).
- Reviewing Employment and Affluence Rating (page 122).
- Altering an Investigator's Backstory (page 122).
- Training (page 122).
- Recovering from their impairment if an investigator is impaired ( Impairment and Recovery , page 119).

## Development Points

To  grow  and  evolve,  an  investigator  needs  development points, which they gain through the course of play. Players then  spend  development  points  during  the  investigator development phase to improve and advance their investigators

( Spending Development Points , nearby).

## Gaining Development Points

At  the  end  of  every  case  file  or  campaign  chapter,  each investigator gains a number of development points, determined by the GM. The standard rule is 1 development point per session of play. How long a session takes varies from group to group, but it's likely to be in the range of two to three hours, depending on how twisty-turny the case file is or how many people there are playing through it. Development point  awards  for  pre-written  case  files  are  detailed  in  the rewards section of each scenario.

## GM ADVICE

A GM may wish to alter the number of development points awarded. For example, awarding an additional 1  or  2  development  points  at  the  end  of  a  case  file may suit a group that plays less frequently (perhaps only  once  every  month).  Increasing  the  number  of points  awarded  means  the  investigators  advance in  skills  and  abilities  more  quickly.  Conversely,  if the  group  prefers  to  see  their  characters  develop more gradually, it may be preferable to award fewer development  points,  perhaps  only  1  per  case  file, rather than 1 per session. This may suit a group which plays very regularly (perhaps every week). It's up to you to judge which system works best for your group, so that you can adjust the investigators' development to a comfortable speed for everyone.

For  what  this  means  in  terms  of  the  speed  of investigator advancement, see Slow vs Rapid Advancement on page 121.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000273_ab6e1719e955019fd3979a60701f79c5255fed5623f5136e8c7016b57bd777d3.png)

## Spending Development Points

During an investigator development phase, there are various  ways  a  player  can  spend  the  development  points they've earned:

- Increasing Skills.
- Buying Advantages.
- Learning and Mastering Spells.

## Increasing Skills

During an investigator development phase, each investigator may  spend  development  points  to  increase  one  or  more skill values.

- Spending 1 development point raises one skill by 10% or two skills by 5% each, up to a maximum value of 80%.
- If a skill has a rating of 90% or higher, spending 1 development point raises the skill by 1%, up to a maximum value of 99%.
- If a skill has a rating of 80% or higher, spending 1 development point raises the skill by 5%, up to a maximum value of 90%.
- Note that any single skill can only be raised by one step (by either 10%, 5%, or 1% per their current rating) per development phase.

Note: the more frequently a group plays, the more development points they are going to get.

An  investigator  can  also  spend  1  development  point  to acquire a new skill. The chosen skill starts at a value equal to half of the associated characteristic value-refer to Table 4: Which Characteristic Should I Use? , page 117, for the list of skills and their associated characteristics.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000274_6e3df3e8be0e1f54a0c08ea0e9332a375ef14730da111d8bbf73f18a973ef1a7.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000275_0370d3a50670b105e9d09786446ab876e9d25d133dd6a2a11f3a6166c32b7f82.png)

## Learning and Mastering Spells

Skill  progression  is  limited  during  a  development phase. Thus, if a character has 2 development points to  spend,  they  may  not  increase  a  single  skill  by more than 10% (or 5% if rated at 80% of higher, and so on). This restriction is designed to stop characters advancing too rapidly. The GM could ignore this rule and allow the same skill(s) to be increased multiple times  in  a  single  development  phase  (e.g.,  spend  2 development points to increase a single skill by 20%, and  so  on),  but  this  does  mean  investigators  will quickly  become  highly  proficient  in  certain  skills, and does not mirror the progression of characters as seen in the Rivers of London novels.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000276_d476ab19b23691a4f10f97df2d25f488aad43736319e375d3eb1cefd9496fc14.png)

During an investigator development phase, any practitioner investigators can spend 1 development point to either learn a new spell ( Learning More Spells , page 172) or mark one spell as mastered ( Mastering a Spell , page 171). Thus, during a single investigator development phase, if a character has 2 points  to  spend,  they  may  learn  a  new  spell  (1  point)  and master a spell (1 point). If they had 3 development points, they could not learn two or three spells, nor could they master more than one spell, as characters are limited to learning one new and/or mastering one spell per development phase.

## Slow vs Rapid Advancement

The  number  of  development  points  awarded over the course of a game has an impact on how quickly the investigators advance. For example, let's say a group takes two sessions to complete their first case file. The GM awards each player 2 development points to spend during the ensuing development phase, meaning they could:

- Increase two skills by up to 10% each (or four skills by up to 5% each, or some combination thereof) depending on their current skill values.
- Increase one skill by up to 10% (or two skills by up to 5% each) depending on their current skill values, and learn a new expert skill.
- Increase one skill by up to 10% (or two skills by up to 5% each) depending on their current skill values, and-if they're a practitioner-learn a new spell or master an existing one.
- If they are a practitioner, learn one new spell and master an existing one.
- Save their development points until they have enough to buy either a regular or major advantage.
- Increase one skill by up to 10% (or two skills by up to 5% each) depending on their current skill values.
- Learn a new expert skill.
- If they're a practitioner, learn a new spell or master an existing one.
- Save their development point until they have enough to buy either a regular or major advantage-they'll just get there a bit more slowly.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000277_b135ceb3f57caa2a2ed2e53bdf9d009da8e2f85b77f28ccb81da4cd84ef2e8da.png)

As you can see from this example, the more development  points  a  GM  awards,  the  more rapidly the investigators advance. If the GM is generous  in  awarding  development  points  at the end of every case file or campaign chapter, the  players  can  soon  end  up  with  powerful, highly skilled investigators. If that's what the group  wants,  that's  great!  But  if  it  isn't,  the GM should stick with the slow burn option of awarding  a  smaller  number  of  development points at the end of a scenario. Thus, gaining lots  of  skills,  spells,  and  advantages  would take longer.

But what if the GM had instead decided to award just 1 development point per case file? The  players  would  have  just  1  development point to spend, meaning they could:

Remember: although development points may be awarded at the end of every session, they can only be spent during an investigator development phase at the end of every case file or campaign chapter.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000278_b8857d999292bec1570b512947f22f076a3a8c88519078f7ef55810c0db1d813.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000279_106fae5ada206ee9dab86a39feaeb1895b1057fef3635d71b47236228db75e53.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000280_2e1f65a64724171f72b25a2fabe87a0c32eac8bfcad96afd6b8b2f1a710c86fa.png)

## GM ADVICE

3

Example: a case file has finished after a couple of sessions of play and the GM awards Nafeesa 2 development points. She spends 1 development point to boost her Research skill by 10% (from 30% to 40%). She spends the second development point to learn a new spell.

## Buying Advantages

During an investigator  development  phase,  an  investigator can purchase a new advantage (see Step Three: Advantages for the list of advantages, page 50).

- An advantage costs 5 development points.
- A major advantage costs 10 development points.

Depending  on  how  many  development  points  the  GM awards per session (and at the end of a case file), it is unlikely that an investigator can purchase an advantage during every investigator development phase, particularly not a major one. Advantages  provide  significant  benefits,  so  accessing  them takes longer than increasing skills or gaining spells.

## Altering an Investigator's Backstory

It's not just the investigators who are likely to be affected by their exposure to magic and the world of the demi-mondetheir  entire  lives  could  be  turned  upside  down  because  of their  experiences.  So,  during  an  investigator  development phase, each player may alter or add backstory entries on their character  sheet  whenever  they  feel  it  is  appropriate  to  do so.  They  should  always  discuss  any  changes  with  the  GM, who  has  the  right  to  veto  anything  they  feel  could  derail the rest of the game. A GM may also suggest changes to an investigator's  backstory  based  on  what's  happened  to  them and/or their friends and family during a case file.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000281_78a15e8895aeef31b65fedfa5670d51e929061858d6bc07719d0c5610d724d85.png)

## Reviewing Employment and Affluence Rating

Another  factor  that  could  be  affected  by  an  investigator's contact with the demi-monde is their job-less so if they're seconded to the Folly or work in a field that can accommodate their investigations-as most employers won't take kindly to their  staff  disappearing  for  prolonged  periods  of  time  and neglecting  their  duties.  And,  if  a  character  loses  their  job, they lose their income.

Where  a  character's  financial  situation  has  undergone  a significant change for better or worse, the GM should review their Affluence  Rating (page  57).  The  affluence  bands  in the  game are intentionally broad, so moving up and down the scale is unusual. However, if events in the story indicate that a character should change their location on the affluence scale, they may do so-with the GM's approval.

Example: Nafeesa acts against a powerful politician but fails to gather sufficient evidence to make her case stick. The GM warns that Nafeesa risks losing her job if she continues with her investigations. If Nafeesa were to lose her job, after a time of unemployment, her affluence rating would fall from average to poor.

## Training

Given  enough  money,  someone  will  teach  pretty  much anything to pretty much anyone. It's no different in RoL:RPG . Providing there is a sufficient period between case files (several weeks at a minimum), investigators may receive some form of training. For example, the Folly has a Vestigia Sensitivity Training programme that it encourages new recruits to take; it lasts for three days and teaches non-practitioners to hone their  senses,  thereby  improving  their  ability  to  detect  the residue left behind in the environment by magic.

To provide a sense of game balance and gradual improvement,  GMs  are  advised  to  award  no  more  than  1 development  point  to  each  investigator  for  their  training between  case  files,  regardless  of  whether  the  downtime between cases is a few weeks or a few years.

Example: Nafeesa  finishes  a  case,  so  her  player  works  through an investigator development phase. There is at least three months of game time before the investigators pick up their next case, and the player suggests Nafeesa might get some driving experience in that time, perhaps the equivalent of a one-week intensive training course. The GM agrees, and awards Nafeesa 1 development point to spend on increasing Nafeesa's Drive skill from 30% to 40%.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000282_63b6360463c99303227273fb0ffcb86b1297a75b866de550c26241d6fe7eba9a.png)

## Combat

Despite their best efforts to avoid confrontation and bloodshed, investigators are sometimes faced with situations where the outcome is going to be decided with their fistsor  anything else that's potentially going to inflict harm on their opponent.

In RoL:RPG ,  combat  encompasses  a  range  of  skills  and goals. Sometimes, an investigator may have no option but to hit someone, but, then again, they may instead wish to merely restrain an opponent without causing undue harm, while at other times, the goal may be simply to escape unharmed.

Because of its nature, combat has its own rules, which focus on  the  action.  They  help  to  determine  who  is  doing  what, when  it  is  done,  and  the  degree  of  success  achieved.  And rather than having a single roll decide the outcome of a fight, a series of opposed rolls are made until there is a clear victor.

## Combat Overview

Combat is divided into rounds ( The Combat Round ,  page 124). The GM should follow the same basic process during each round combat takes place:

1. Use each investigator's actions in the story to suggest which rolls are required ( Declaration of Intent , page 124; Set Your Goal , page 127; Target Responds , page 127).
2. Resolve the dice rolls ( Dice Rolls , page 130).
3. Incorporate the outcome of the rolls back into the story ( Narration , page 133).
4. Move onto the next character ( Next Player's Turn , page 133).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000283_f4737cab620dfb8b54d61230db77c33961eed034db18ea915e7fbf03f695f857.png)

## Important: T one

Some players love to wallow in grisly and gory fight descriptions, while others prefer to gloss over the unpleasant details. Is  everyone happy with 18-certificate (R-rated) level violence or would they prefer it to be more family friendly (PG-13)? It's important to talk as a group and agree on a tone that is acceptable to everyone, then stick with it. This is a topic you might discuss and agree upon upfront when creating characters  for  a  new  game  ( Considerate Gaming , page 44).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000284_244ed02f17b8edf8784c87dfd4f881c353bbb45f802ac139216e61133e1f5ca3.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000285_5bd508fb662836a21a87be9687059dde819a7775f56772a7e008c31c0e9d3901.png)

## Setting Combat Expectations in Rivers of London

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000286_952fd9ee483d28df4f4ce0732683cb71b7d01f5461c659e00ef8ea0e80591efd.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000287_589e64ced48a9fa5b36c3c611fbce3e50222527c1a36efd45afb11606652cb86.png)

Combat is only one potential element in the story of an RoL:RPG game, not its focus. As a result, combat scenes play out  quicker  than  in  some  other  roleplaying  games,  and  player  character death is quite rare.

- Combat is fast: fights should play out in just a few combat rounds. It takes 3 damage to take a character out of a fight, and most attacks inflict 1 or 2 damage, so two successful hits can usually knock a character down. While a single punch could put someone out of action, the average character would need to get a Critical success (one in a hundred chance) to achieve that outcome ( The Damage Roll , page 132).
- Investigators have an edge: investigators are not necessarily any tougher than anyone else, but they (unlike some other characters) have Luck points to spend. This doesn't mean they'll win every fight, but it does give them an edge, and players should be encouraged to make use of the Luck spending rules ( Spending Luck , page 113).
- Defeat rarely means death: it's almost impossible to be killed in a melee fight. An accumulation of minor blows won't kill someone-it takes a very powerful attack to inflict a fatal blow, such as a Critical success on a damage roll with a shotgun ( The Damage Roll , page 132). Losing a fight usually means a character has been knocked down and temporarily incapacitated, and investigators recover quickly from such things ( Wounds and Healing , page 144).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000288_8d8cd16c671a968b126af10cac9d6f2c0efc131634215f535cc88d1a0d7a03a1.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000289_ff09c1ba70a14d8a8f86ed31f83b4323c21d5c5860f637147e8e7252343bae84.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000290_e5fcef8a38da1b7748031cda1d9a139926f11cf3a2d5d828fe3a4d1081236507.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000291_0fb5cb2987713647b7c358447539b16c2caf06c958db7ab1f381731945333e82.png)

3

Each  combat  sequence  is  its  own  discrete  entity,  and  no two are ever going to be exactly the same. For example, in one combat, a Fighting roll may represent a few good punches, while in another, it's a knee to the groin, a series of kicks, or even a headbutt. The GM's description of the scene and its outcome should elaborate upon the investigators' stated intentions and be relative to the success they achieve on their dice rolls.

Most importantly: don't just repeatedly roll dice without any story. It's really boring. Think of the fight scenes in the novels: they're cinematic and entertaining to read, which is what you're aiming for with combat in this game.

## Declaration of Intent

Once it becomes obvious that combat is about to occur, the GM should ask each player involved 'What is your investigator doing?' The GM should always encourage players to describe their investigator's actions, rather than use game terminology. ' I'm rolling to hit, ' is not something an investigator would ever say and it's not very interesting, story-wise. As a GM, you should endeavour to paint a picture with your descriptions of combat and encourage your players to do likewise.

When the player has described their investigator's action, the GM should then confirm with the player which skill is being used in the action.

Player: 'I'm going to punch  the guard  and  then  run  for the open door.'

GM: 'The guard is going to try to grab you. This is going to be an opposed roll, using your Fighting skill.'

GM: 'The guy is running straight at you-what are you doing?'

Player: 'I raise my stun gun and shoot him.'

GM: 'OK, give me a Firearms roll.'

GM: 'Hannah's character is acting crazy-he's heading back into the burning building. He'll probably die in there!'

Player: 'I'm going to grab him and wrestle him to the ground, so

I can stop him from entering.'

GM: 'Let's have an opposed Fighting roll, then.'

Notice how the request for a dice roll develops out of what the player says. In the first example, the goal is to get past the guard, and the punch is simply a method of achieving that goal; damage may be inflicted as a consequence, but the GM uses the dice to judge whether or not the manoeuvre is accomplished.

In the second example, the player's goal is to incapacitate an attacker, so the dice are used to determine if the stun gun hits, and to gauge its effect.

Once the action and type of roll have been determined, the dice are rolled, often by the GM and the player simultaneously (an Opposed Combat Roll ; page 131), and the results used to determine if a manoeuvre or attack is successful.

## The Combat Round

Combat  is  structured  into  rounds.  During  each  combat round, each player takes one action (a turn).

A combat round doesn't represent a specific, fixed amount of time. Instead, it's deliberately elastic, meaning that everyone who  wants  to  get  involved  has  a  chance  to  complete  one action-referred to as 'taking a turn.' When every investigator and GM-controlled character (NPC) have had an opportunity to act, that combat round is over, and if the encounter has not fully resolved, another round can begin. So, in every combat round, everyone involved gets to take one turn-meaning that everyone gets to 'initiate' an action once per round.

Before a new combat round starts, the GM checks to see if the scene has been resolved. If the fight is over, then no more combat rounds are required, and the story moves on.

## Who Goes First?

Combat can begin in a variety of ways. Generally, one person says their character is making an attack and initiates combat. Then, one or more characters may wish to react (respond) to that  initial  attack.  Of  course,  only  those  characters  in  the same scene can join in.

What happens in hand-to-hand (melee) combat is decided with opposed rolls. If there's any disagreement about which character is initiating combat, the character with the highest DEX among those vying for first place is the one who acts first. In the case of a draw (i.e., both have the same DEX), use the standard Tiebreakers rules (page 118).

Example: Nafeesa is in Jimmy Four-Fingers' apartment, pressuring  him  for  information.  When  she  threatens  him  with arrest, the GM states that Jimmy stands up and pushes Nafeesa towards the wall to get her out of his way, so he can leave. The player isn't having that and says Nafeesa is going to grab Jimmy's arm as soon as he moves. The GM realises this is the start of a scuffle  and  considers  who's  making  the  first  attack.  Did  Jimmy attack  first  with  the  push  or  did  that  just  provoke  Nafeesa  into making the 'real' first attack? They're not sure, so they go with highest DEX. Jimmy has DEX 60 and Nafeesa has DEX 40, so the GM decides Jimmy acts first in the combat round.

In  the  third  example,  the  player  is  trying  to  restrain  a fellow character without inflicting harm. Again, the dice are used to gauge the manoeuvre's success.

Alternatively,  if  Jimmy  had  not  made  any  move  to  raise  his arm, and Nafeesa just decided to restrain him, the player would clearly  be  initiating  combat  and  Nafeesa  would  act  first  in  the combat round.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000292_8410efe6dea4a84eb782eb71856438fbc40e5b5346105cdbc66aabcdf5546818.png)

## GM ADVICE

Because  melee  combat  uses  opposed  rolls,  the character acting first doesn't necessarily get to  inflict  damage  first.  Quite  the  opposite  may happen-the  person initiating combat may be the first one to suffer damage if the opposed roll doesn't go their way!

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000293_23993933b24b9c60ff23a2ffb9c6f36a571b6e5a8f0fa12e6728f806724d5de0.png)

## Who Goes Next?

Once  the  first  attack  has  been  resolved,  the  player-or GM-who initiated  that  attack  rolls  a  die:  if  they  roll  an even number, play passes to that person's left; if they roll an odd number, play passes to their right. This roll is made only once in each combat scene-i.e., once the direction of play is established, continue using that same direction for the entire combat. Doing it this way means the players won't know in advance which direction combat is going to proceed, so won't be able to 'game' the situation by choosing where to sit.

Once every player has taken their turn, and play has come full circle back round to the first player or the GM (whoever initiated the first roll), that's the end of the first combat round. If  the  combat  has  not  been  resolved  (there  are  still  people fighting), that first player now starts a second combat round, with everyone getting another turn in the same order. Play as many combat rounds as are required to resolve the situation.

When playing through a combat scene, the order of play is intended to mirror the order of play used in most board games, simply  proceeding  clockwise  (or  anticlockwise)  around  the table, which is something most people are familiar with.

## Playing Online

Of course, you may not be playing around a table-you may be playing the game online, in which case the GM should use  their  on-screen  view  of  the  players  at  the  start  of  the combat  to  place  everyone  in  a  set  order.  This  could  be  as simple as writing down people's names in a list on a piece of paper, starting with the person in the top left of the screen and  working  down  to  whomever  is  in  the  bottom  right. The important thing is that the GM manages the order of play and ensures that each player is aware of their place in the turn order.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000294_3c2c2276c8f9b03b0a30faaf0126f759aa81f5d36122f03edd9df098f8aeb477.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000295_cf8120c560f6afcb6fbbdaed64d5bf6fd4c18145c280273a532ebec2eb4b6513.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000296_35dba6e758da8a8d7fe574231309e615427d3fa00ff538aefc6e6d75a92dfdc6.png)

## GM ADVICE

People can drop in  and  out  for  a  variety  of  reasons  during an online game session. Some video conferencing and online gaming platforms reorder how people appear on your screen when this happens, so be sure to make a note of the original order you set up and stick with that, rather than relying on what you can see on your screen at any given time. As long as everybody gets to have a turn each round, it's all good.

## Handling NPCs

There may be more than one NPC in the scene, and the GM plays all of them. If one of the NPCs acted first in the combat round, once that character's turn is resolved, the GM rolls the die and play passes either to their left or right, just the same as it does for any other player. Then, when play circles back round to the GM, they play the rest of their NPCs in whatever order they wish. Round two starts with the NPC who initiated combat taking their next turn.

Diagram A: with four NPCs-Annie, Celia, Ella, and Frank-let's say that Annie is the first to act in combat. After Annie's turn, play passes to Player 1, then Player 2, then Player 3, and then back to the GM. The GM then plays Frank, then Celia, then Ella, and that's the end of round one. The GM starts round two with Annie taking her next turn.

## Diagram A

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000297_6c5e8ecc367fd42325a0009ef2dc62b3e26a3e2fbeb238609e0267bd90bb9230.png)

Diagram  B: the  investigators  start  a  fight.  Jordan  initiates the  combat,  so  his  player  becomes  Player  1.  Play  then  passes  to Player 2, and then to the GM, who decides to play their NPCs in a slightly different order this time-Ella, Annie, Celia, then Frank. Finally, it's Player 3's turn, meaning Nafeesa gets to go last this round.

## Diagram B

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000298_ba68c19358eca027c9ca9347f52d96eb2b75bed426bf60c51fc013e55c4557e7.png)

## Actions in a Combat Round

On their turn in a combat round, the player decides on one action  they  want  their  investigator  to  perform  during  that turn. They may use their action to:

- Initiate a melee attack using the Fighting skill.
- Initiate a spell attack using the Magic skill, if the investigator possesses the skill.
- Initiate a firearms attack using the Firearms skill.
- Flee-this may require an opposed roll to break free of the fight, if already engaged in combat.
- Perform some other action requiring time and perhaps a dice roll, such as picking a lock while others fight. Note that simple actions-like speaking-don't 'use up' a character's turn, but moving anything more than a short distance may ( Distances in Combat , page 127).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000299_40e7e724ab5ba6d6e1c598c2eede720937751e54e1654496c5c7e0ef73b9720a.png)

A character may wish to draw a weapon on their turn. For ease of play, under most circumstances, drawing a weapon counts as a 'free' action. The only exception to this is where it's  clear  that  the  weapon  is  somehow  difficult  to  accesssuch as a handgun carried in a briefcase, for example-which takes one action (one turn) to extract the weapon, ready for use in the following round.

Important: note that initiating an action on your turn in a combat round is different to 'reacting' to someone else's attack on you. Reactions, which we call 'responses' in combat, are covered in Step 2: Target Responds , on page 127.

## Distances in Combat

In play, distances are judged upon verbal descriptions of the environment  rather  than  quantifiable  measures.  The  game uses a 'theatre of the mind' approach, rather than a tactical battle map showing exact locations and distances. That said, during combat, some actual measurements, such as ranges for different types of firearms, are used. The GM is the final arbitrator on whether a target is in range or not.

When playing through a combat scene it's important that everyone keeps track of where each character is in relation to everyone else. Because this is all done with words, and people may miscommunicate or misunderstand each other, the GM makes  the  final  determination,  but  they  should  always  be ready to listen and adjust to ensure fair play. If it helps, draw a sketch map and ask people to indicate their positions, or use a token or miniature to represent each character. If you're gaming online, some platforms allow you to upload maps and create character tokens for use in your game. See also Who's Where? (page 221).

## GM ADVICE

Some players may struggle to visualise what's going on in combat without the use of maps, tokens, and/ or miniatures. Always use what makes the game as accessible as possible for your group.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000300_6b3851870128f491fd5321fbbd2b629c91d31294fa4c6bf93c207417f5cbf4bc.png)

In combat, you can use a character's MOV to determine whether  movement  takes  up  their  entire  turn  or  not.  A character  can  move  a  number  of  metres/yards  up  to  their MOV rating and take another action on their turn. If they wish, they can move further than this, moving up to five times their MOV (in metres/yards), but this counts as their entire action (turn) for that combat round. For example, a character with MOV 8 can use their turn in a combat round to:

- Move up to 8 metres/yards and act normally (such as attack, use a skill, cast a spell).

127

- Move up to 40 metres/yards, but have to wait until the next round to take another action.

Also,  where  this  chapter  uses  the  term  'arm's  length,' this  indicates  approximately  1  metre/yard.  Point-blank  for firearms is 3 metres/yards.

## Running Combat Encounters

All  combats,  whether  involving  fists,  swords,  firearms,  or spells, are resolved using the same six steps.

- Step 1: Set Your Goal (following) : describe what your character is doing (what action are you initiating?).
- Step 3: Dice Rolls (page 130) : usually, both you and the target roll percentile dice (an opposed roll) to determine who wins. Sometimes, especially for ranged attacks, only the attacker rolls dice (an unopposed roll).
- Step 2: Target Responds (below) : the target describes how they intend to respond (how is the target reacting?).
- Step 4: Damage Roll (page 132) : figure out any damage or other effects.
- Step 6: Next Player's Turn: play moves on to the next character (an investigator or NPC) who now starts their turn with Step 1.
- Step 5: Narration (page 133) : the winner describes what happens.

Let's look at each of those steps in a little more detail.

## Step 1: Set Your Goal

This  is  the  essence  of  roleplaying-put  yourself  in  your character's shoes and describe what they do. If this were a TV show, your character would be on screen right now. Describe what's happening so everyone can see it in high definition!

'I stand up, kick the chair out of the way, and punch Louis hard in the nose.'

'I pull out my pistol, level it with both hands, and shoot Rhonda.'

'I shape the forma in my head, throw my hand forward, and shoot a fireball at the vampire.'

Remember, you're describing what you want to do-what action you are initiating. Whether it plays out as you intended will depend on how the dice fall.

## Step 2: Target Responds

Whoever is playing the target now says what that character is  doing  in  response  (their  reaction  to  the  attack).  If  that's you, just put yourself in the shoes of your character and think about  what  you  would  like  to  see  them  do.  That  might  be to dodge the blow, attack back, or run away. Don't concern yourself with the rules at this point, just describe what we see the character doing.

3

'Louis is not having that! He throws out a sharp right hook to knock you flat before you even touch him.'

'Rhonda's not having that! She knocks the gun to one side and punches you in the face.'

'The vampire makes to dodge behind the van.'

The  GM  should  consider  how  the  target  is  responding and fit it into one of the following four options (each of the responses is detailed below):

- Response 1: Do Nothing (unlimited reaction).
- Response 3: Fight Back (limited to once per combat round).
- Response 2: Dodge (unlimited reaction).
- Response 4: Flee.

## GM ADVICE

While a character can (usually) only 'fight back'  in response once per combat round, they may respond with dodges multiple times in the same round and can keep running away or doing nothing if they like, too (see also Outnumbered , page 135).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000301_ddbd2adcf0d9835d78363dbec48875e34857e9f5bccdac7b48df0409ef526e02.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000302_58afcd3391dada1f195f2821a38f83134f22c1a90998f1a854b89bcd3b31c6b7.png)

## Important: Attack vs Response

Don't  get  'responses'  confused  with 'initiating' an action/attack.  You  can only  initiate  an  action/attack  once  in a  combat round, but you can respond multiple times to attacks targeting you in  that  same  round,  even  though  you may  be Outnumbered (page  135).  The only  limited  response  is  fighting  back, which  can  only  be  chosen  once  per combat  round,  unless  you  have Fast Reactions (page 51).

## Response 1: Do Nothing

Whether  the  attacker  needs  to  roll  dice  depends  on  the nature of the attack:

- Melee: if you're just going to stand there and let them hit you, then there's no need for any dice to be rolled; the attacker just makes a damage roll to see how much damage they inflict on you. Doesn't sound like a great option, does it?
- Magic: the attacker must make an unopposed Magic roll to hit you ( Step 3 , page 130).
- Firearms: the attacker must make an unopposed Firearms roll to hit you ( Step 3 , page 130).

## ADDITIONAL RULES

For those wanting more complexity, rules for surprise attacks can be found on page 327.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000303_48ab05bd25d9a8b8b7c5906ddb46db76c8dc50606b174f4c90c1f1f42c590dcd.png)

## Response 2: Dodge

There  are  two  different  types  of  dodging  a  character  can do-depending  on  how  they  are  being  attacked.  First, there  is  'dodging'  a  blow  in  melee  combat;  and  second, there is 'diving for cover'  to avoid being shot by firearms, bows, or magic.

- Dodging in Melee: if you choose to dodge a melee attack, you roll your Fighting skill in an opposed roll against your attacker as part of Step 3 (page 130). This includes dodging items that are thrown at you with the Athletics skill.
- Dive for Cover: you can't dodge bullets or fireballs! But you do have the option to dive for cover, which is the next best thing to dodging, under the circumstances. Diving for cover may mean jumping behind a physical barrier, throwing yourself to the floor, or ducking and diving to present a more challenging target.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000304_4f5b6487014dcf89bef9a8d90b24b7b0c4a59a9381ca2a067dad4da282f29eae.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000305_91dc5585a0c6db3b179ddbd1aa969ffdf334c2afe6bb61a1d852ee3fb7e04b90.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000306_6632140ba64f023ee9dd9022ff3bf2a9d38be26bfb452e86606acd4e3e1ffff6.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000307_8e2bd937295d843f35581a3c907fc7e9e12b268240f832a861447bfa80f756ae.png)

When diving for cover,  the  target  has  made  themselves harder to hit, and in Step 3 (page 130), the attacker incurs a  penalty  die  on  their  unopposed  Firearms  or  Magic  roll ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111). Diving for cover is a desperate response to a desperate situation. A character who  dives  for  cover  must  forgo  their  next  action  when  it comes to their turn in the combat round. If they have already had their turn this round, they must forgo their turn in the following round.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000308_ae06519d330751d549e4715656594e8548eb96843a132457b5ca6faf4f7896c4.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000309_14ffd1d2798e29aecb590b6aec38da3660ac0a01019fe71f3d90ee77925445b4.png)

## Diving for Cover Example

Nafeesa: DEX 40, Fighting 60/30 . Izzy: DEX 70, Fighting 60/30, Firearms 30/15.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000310_b1abd7ef364e1bdd7eb88ee0bb690dfb3550527ff70a31f9c0afd6f931dfccd8.png)

Step 1: Set Your Goal: Izzy is shooting Nafeesa with a handgun.

Step  2:  Target  Responds: Nafeesa  sees  Izzy preparing  to  shoot  her,  so  she  opts  to Dive  for Cover (nearby), meaning Izzy incurs a penalty die on her Firearms roll.

Step 3: Dice Rolls: the  two  combatants  are  10 metres/yards  apart,  which  is  within  standard range  ( Table  5:  Firearms ,  page  132),  so  the difficulty  level  of  the  shot  is  Regular.  The  GM rolls  with  a  penalty  die,  getting  27  and  77  for Izzy, and takes the higher of the two rolls. 77 is a failure; the shot misses.

Step 4: Damage: the  missed  shot  inflicts  no  damage.

Step  5:  Narration: the  player  describes  how Nafeesa leaps behind a car and avoids being shot.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000311_41d53317e098aef057dcbdb0ac20b1872e8ed4068473d429e35312eec39830c1.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000312_1bd81319e218032ad9d2c56a083bffb46960cf58d0112d7be5c73bfb12d55eb8.png)

## ADDITIONAL RULES

Rules for investigators who wish to take a bullet for their  companions  can  be  found  in Holding  Your Action on page 328.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000313_d132fac77437a39f41001b7326c276fb4ca97ba548d512e40c7e2d8df0d9a2b6.png)

## Response 3: Fight Back

You  can  'fight  back'  using  melee,  firearms,  or  magic  in combat,  but  you  can  only  choose  the  fight  back  response once  per  combat  round.  An  exception  to  this  is  when  an investigator  has  the Fast  Reactions advantage  (page  51), allowing them to make a second fight back action if attacked a second time in the same combat round.

options open-so this time, they decide to fight back. Should the investigator be attacked a third time in this same combat round, they could now only choose to dodge, flee, or do nothing, as they've already used their one fight back response.

For  further  details  on  what  happens  if  a  character  is attacked  more  than  once  in  a  single  combat  round,  see Outnumbered (page 135).

## Fight Back Using Melee

If your attacker is within arm's reach, you can fight back with melee, regardless of how they're attacking you. Picture what the attacker is doing and think how you could beat them. You might say that you are quicker and get your blow in first, or how you try to knock their gun away, or you could describe how you swirl to avoid their attack and then hit them.

'I  weave  around  the  blow  and  strike  back  with  an  upper  cut to the chin.'

'I grab the gun and twist it out of their hand.'

To fight back using melee, you make an opposed roll using your Fighting skill during Step 3 (page 130). If you win the opposed roll, your attack both hits your attacker and negates their attack on you. If you want to achieve something other than simply inflicting harm, such as tripping your attacker up or disarming them, see Combat Manoeuvres (page 134).

## Fight Back Using Firearms

If you are holding a loaded firearm in your hand, you can fight back by letting off a single shot at your attacker, regardless of how they are attacking you. To do this, roll your Firearms skill  in  the  opposed  roll  during Step 3 (page  130).  If  you win the opposed roll, your attack both hits your attacker and negates their attack on you.

## Fight Back Using Magic

Note: For more details on magic and spells, see Chapter 4: Newtonian Magic .

If you're able to use magic, you can fight back by casting a spell  at  your  attacker,  regardless  of  how  they  are  attacking you. To do this, you roll your Magic skill in the opposed roll during Step 3 (page 130).

Example: the first time an investigator is attacked in a combat round, their response is to dodge-perhaps they're trying to assess their opponent to find some weakness they can exploit when it gets to their turn in the round. If the investigator is attacked again this round, they can respond again-they still have all of their response

To  cast  your  spell,  make  a  Magic  roll,  including  any relevant  modifiers  ( Ranged  Attack  Modifiers ,  page  138; Mastering a Spell ,  page 171). If you win the opposed roll, you've managed to cast your spell before your opponent gets their attack in. If the spell inflicts damage, it negates your opponent's attack, whether they are casting a spell, firing a gun, or throwing a punch.

Not all spells are designed to inflict harm, though. If the aim of the spell is to distract your attacker, the target is required to make a POW roll to test whether they can maintain their concentration.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000314_bbcfe26a5d64823b46d4148dfa2de9a0a7da47b3e0429219d32d90d0547b3fd2.png)

3

If they fail the POW roll, their attack (whatever it might be) fails. To see this in action, let's look at this scene from Moon Over Soho , where Peter Grant is confronting the Faceless Man.

' If you're going to shoot ,' he said, ' then shoot .'

So I shot him. It was worth it just for the obvious outrage it caused  him,  and  I  enjoyed  it  right  up  until  the  point  where  he caught the bloody fireball. Just snatched it out of the air and held it, Yorick-like, in front of his face.

I'd released it as soon as it got near him but it hadn't exploded. He  twisted  it  this  way  and  that,  as  if  examining  it  like  a connoisseur, which perhaps he was. I figured he wanted me to lob another one at him so he could catch it, or deflect it, or do something else with annoying insouciance. So I didn't. Besides, the more time he spent taunting me, the further away Simone could get.

' You know ,'  he  said,  ' when I first  saw  you  I  thought  you were with the Thames girls, or a new sort of fae, or something really outlandish like a witch doctor or an American. ' The man popped the fireball like a soap bubble, and rubbed his thumb and finger under his nose.

Let's  pick  up  the  action  at  the  start  of  Peter's  turn  in  the combat round:

Step 1: Set Your Goal: Peter is aiming a fireball at the Faceless Man, hoping to cause him damage.

Step 2:  Target  Responds: the  GM  makes  a  Magic  roll  for  the Faceless  Man  and  gets  a  Hard  success,  indicating  he's  able  to detect which spell Peter is casting ( Sensing a Spell Being Cast , page 176). The Faceless Man chooses to fight back with magic and picks  the  second  order  spell Invisible  Catch (page  184).  Being unsure of Peter's abilities, the Faceless Man boosts the spell with 3  magic  points,  making  it  capable  of  catching  a  fireball  worth up to 5 damage.

Step  3:  Dice  Rolls: both  are  within  standard  range  and  with no other modifiers. Peter makes a Magic roll and gets a Regular success ( Levels of Success and Failure , page 105). The GM then rolls  Magic  for  Peter's  opponent  and  gets  a  Hard  success.  Peter elects not to spend Luck to alter the result ( Spending Luck , page 113), so the Faceless Man wins the opposed roll.

Step 4: Damage Roll: the Invisible Catch spell inflicts no damage, but it does catch Peter's fireball.

Step  5:  Narration: the  Faceless  Man  catches  the  fireball  and studies it, mocking Peter, as he tries to divine Peter's Master.

Step 6: Next Player's Turn.

## Response 4: Flee

When someone attacks you, you can try to avoid getting hit and run away as long as there is an escape route available.

- Fleeing a Melee Attack: if you choose to flee, you still make an opposed roll against your attacker using your Fighting skill during Step 3 (page 130). If you win, you evade their blow and run away. If they win, you suffer damage before you flee-as long as you're still able to do so after suffering the effects of their attack.
- Fleeing a Ranged Attack: running away is always an option when bullets or spells start flying. You can run away at full speed (moving five times your MOV rating in metres/yards) with much the same effect as diving for cover, except that you put greater distance between you and your attacker. Your attacker makes an unopposed Firearms or Magic roll ( Step 3 , page 130).

If you run away at full speed and your MOV is 8 or higher ( Fast-Moving Targets, page 138), your attacker makes their Firearms or Magic roll with a penalty die. If your MOV is 7 or lower, your attacker does not receive a penalty die as you are not a Fast-Moving Target (but you may still be able to reach cover or get out of range next round; Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111).

A character opting to flee in combat must forgo their next action when it comes to their turn in the combat round (in the same manner as when diving for cover). If they have already taken their turn this round, they must forgo their next turn in the following round. A character could flee for a round or two and then stop if they so wish, but, by that time, they would have  moved  a  considerable  distance  away  from  the  action. Usually, people don't return to combat once they've fled from it-unless they've gone to fetch help!

## Step 3: Dice Rolls

In most  cases, both opponents  roll percentile dice to determine what happens in a fight-an opposed combat roll. In  the  case  of  ranged  attacks  (both  Firearms  and  Magic), only  the  attacker  rolls  dice-an  unopposed  combat  rollunless the target has chosen to fight back ( Response 3: Fight Back , page 129).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000315_1318fd07214029d8bb1d9e2ebd6727208a0793453501782ef83c4ebc7d35ffae.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000316_4d6d018811afd20255685c250c4e78319a8b170eba5f773d25ed41f595e17cc2.png)

## Opposed Combat Rolls

Both  sides  roll  percentile  dice  and  compare  their  results with  the  appropriate  skill  to  determine  their  individual level of success:

best  result worst result

- Hard success : a dice roll equal to or below half of the character's skill value.
- Critical success : a dice roll of 01.
- Regular success : a dice roll equal to or below the character's full skill value.
- Fumble : a dice roll of 100 is a fumble (or 90 or above if they are impaired).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000317_69b1e99361e7fd01759967fab2da7b2691bc8f05d0c8e287461a54e2a9fbd929.png)

In opposed rolls, the side with the higher level of success is  the  winner.  A  Critical  success  beats  a  Hard  success,  a Hard  success  beats  a  Regular  success,  and  so  on.  If  both sides  achieve  the  same  level  of  success,  they  each  have  the option of spending Luck points to raise their level of success ( Spending Luck , page 113), whether they initiated the attack or are responding to it; but ultimately, if they are still tied, the character whose turn it is in the round wins the tie.

*Weak  Success: normally,  rolling  over  your  skill  value  is considered  a  failure,  but,  in  combat-as  in  other  opposed rolls-we call it a Weak Success (page 106). Consider two rank  amateurs  playing  chess:  someone's  still  going  to  win, and  it's  the  same  with  a  fight-even  when  both  sides  are inept, one is still going to beat the other.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000318_0f92a4c34bf1900379467e25907ab8aa2c658b68ea545f2f0f8e2b928775fb6e.png)

## Which Skill to Use in Combat

-  If a hand-to-hand melee attack, use the Fighting skill.
-  If throwing something, use the Athletics skill.
-  If shooting a firearm or a bow, use the Firearms skill.
-  If casting a spell, use the Magic skill.
- Weak success* : a dice roll above the character's full skill value.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000319_62131b4c7658cfe614da02d9f8b4e9e2ec96cbb48e1908dacc7bcbccac53a57d.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000320_3d0b036ebae6fa44af40a883760b948b3132ec034d709a6ae02c2bfe18dc5c27.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000321_007feb8d0583a5c53aeff8befbdb46653f71e79caa8c31a2425f209a4dde7426.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000322_df76c95019da37a22ac37d49b7cc226fd9ec630d48ff989c9ae768641d5cdf33.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000323_43f711c33f3e40b1b3b4b54278f16b12162609dbc8a71d99b479b129943ceff6.png)

3

## Unopposed Combat Rolls

If you're making a ranged attack and the target has chosen to respond by doing nothing ( Do Nothing , page 128), Dive for Cover (page 128), or Flee (page 130), the attacker now rolls  percentile  dice.  The  difficulty  level  of  a  ranged  attack roll is set according to distance. For firearms, refer to Table 5: Firearms (nearby) for weapon ranges. All ranged spells have the same range as a standard handgun.

- A target within standard range: Regular difficulty level.
- A target beyond standard range (up to maximum range): Hard difficulty level.

## Step 4: Damage Roll

Following a successful attack, if the goal was to inflict damage (rather  than  to  restrain  or  some  other  combat  manoeuvre), a  damage roll should be made to determine the amount of damage inflicted.

- For melee attacks, the damage roll is a STR roll. This includes all unarmed attacks (kicks, bites, and punches) and all melee weapon attacks (police baton, clubs, knives, etc.).
- For firearms and other ranged attacks, the damage roll is a DEX roll.

## Table 5: Firearms
