To determine the amount of damage inflicted, make a 'damage roll' and add the weapon's base damage ( The Damage Roll , nearby). Missed shots do not cause damage.

| Firearm         |   Base Weapon Damage | Standard Range   | Maximum Range     |
|-----------------|----------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Bow             |                    1 | 30 metres/yards  | 100 metres/yards  |
| Crossbow        |                    1 | 50 metres/yards  | 150 metres/yards  |
| Heavy Handgun   |                    2 | 15 metres/yards  | 50 metres/yards   |
| Machine Gun     |                    2 | 100 metres/yards | 300 metres/yards  |
| Regular Handgun |                    1 | 15 metres/yards  | 50 metres/yards   |
| Rifle           |                    2 | 100 metres/yards | *300 metres/yards |
| Shotgun         |                    2 | 50 metres/yards  | 150 metres/yards  |
| Submachine Gun  |                    2 | 15 metres/yards  | 50 metres/yards   |

*Rifle: the maximum effective range can be doubled to 600 metres/yards with effective bracing and use of a telescopic sight.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000324_0d8d03ae0cef3d6e65fb8038525d1a9ca81dea937266e4cc1160575808896ec2.png)

## The Damage Roll

Roll  percentile  dice  and  compare  it  with  the  attacking character's STR (melee) or DEX (firearms/ranged):

- Failed or fumbled: 1 damage (no weapon damage is added)
- Hard success: 2 damage + base weapon damage
- Regular success: 1 damage + base weapon damage
- Critical success: 3 damage + base weapon damage

If a player rolled a Critical success on their attack roll (or bought one with Luck points), they gain a bonus die to their damage roll  ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice ,  page  111).  If a  character  has  the  advantage Damage Bonus (page  51), they add +1 damage to unarmed and melee attack damage, but not to ranged, firearm, or spell attack damage. The final amount of damage inflicted may also be affected by Cover and Concealment (page 138) and Armour (page 135).

## Spell Damage Rolls

Spell  attacks  in  combat  are  treated  the  same  as  firearm attacks, but replace the Firearms roll with a Magic roll. With a success, the caster makes a damage (DEX) roll and applies the  base  spell  damage  to  the  result,  if  applicable.  For  base spell damage values, refer to individual spell descriptions in Chapter 4: Newtonian Magic .

## Damage Conditions

Damage is cumulative, i.e.,  every  time  a  character  is  dealt damage  in  combat,  it  is  added  to  any  damage  they  have already suffered in the fight so far. The amount of damage suffered affects the character in different ways.

- A character suffering a total of 1 damage is Hurt (page 144).
- A character suffering a total of 3 or more damage is Down (page 144).
- A character suffering a total of 2 damage is Bloodied (page 144).

Whenever  an  investigator  suffers  damage,  their  player should tick the relevant box or boxes on their character sheet. Thus,  an  investigator  suffering  1  damage  would  tick  the Hurt box; if they suffer another 1 damage during the same combat, they tick the Bloodied box, and so on. Note that big damage is serious:

- A character suffering 4 damage in a single blow (be that from a spell, a weapon, or a firearm) has taken a Mortal Wound (page 146).

Example: Nafeesa  is  hit  by  her  opponent.  The  GM  makes  a damage roll using Nafeesa's opponent's STR and gets a Critical success! That's 3 damage, plus 1 base weapon damage for the brass knuckles they were using, for a total of 4 damage. As that 4 damage comes from the same blow, Nafeesa has suffered a mortal wound. She urgently needs medical attention if she's to survive.

- An investigator suffering 5 or more damage in a single attack has taken a fatal blow and must spend 30 Luck points, otherwise they are killed outright. If they can spend 30 Luck points, the damage is reduced to 4 (a mortal wound)-perhaps that lucky silver cigarette case in their breast pocket saved the day!

## Outcomes Other Than Damage

If the attacker chooses to use a combat manoeuvre instead of inflicting damage, they may restrain, disarm, knock prone, or otherwise move their opponent. For more information, refer to Combat Manoeuvres (page 134). Spells can also have an effect other than causing damage, so refer to individual spell descriptions for details in Chapter 4: Newtonian Magic .

## Step 5: Narration

The winner now takes the lead on describing what happens, which should incorporate what was said in the earlier steps. So, if you, the person throwing the initial punch, win, you could say, ' Yeah, so like I said, I kick the chair out of the way and move in on Louis. I see he's going to try to hit me, so I block with my left and smack him in the mouth with my right.'

Or, if your opponent won, the GM might say, 'So, you go to kick that chair out of the way and almost fall over it-you're an easy target! Louis just smacks you with a right hook to the side of your head before you even know what's happening.'

The surroundings should be brought into the story whenever  possible.  For  example,  fighting  in  the  London sewers is very different to fighting in an office. The sewers are dark, foul, and slippery-you could drown your opponent or  get  washed  away  by  a  sudden  torrent  of  water.  On  the other hand, an office is well-lit and there are lots of things around-you could strangle your opponent with a cable or find yourselves confronted by security staff. You get the idea.

## Step 6: Next Player's Turn

When  a  character's  turn  is  over,  play  passes  to  the  next character  in  the  round,  as  described  in Who Goes Next? (page 125). If every character has already acted this round, including the NPCs, then, if the fight is not over, it's time to start the next combat round.

Example: let's  change  the  previous  example  and  say  Nafeesa's attacker wasn't using brass knuckles but a shotgun instead. In this case, the damage roll is a DEX roll, but the GM still achieves a Critical success. Nafeesa isn't wearing ballistic armour ( Armour , page 135), so that's 3 damage plus 2 base weapon damage for the shotgun, for a total of 5 damage: a fatal blow. Nafeesa's player opts to spend the 30 points of Luck to reduce the fatal blow to a mortal wound-she's still very badly injured, but at least she has a chance of surviving now.

## Turn Structure at a Glance

Step 1: Set Your Goal.

Step 2:

Target Responds.

Step 3:

Dice Rolls.

Step 4: Damage Roll.

Step 5: Narration.

Step 6: Next Player's Turn.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000325_5e0b9af6fb45896c59059906043901b489d348c99f59fb497bf88d6774e61fa2.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000326_739a532a918f218a77dedb9bdbe0588686e80360da34ae89e907a7fdf59bc9fc.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000327_bab0c1b7957169bfb402a2a1e9933a64c397721df29c9aa430895587138dea40.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000328_e2a2fed09133d612228e895e341a805a289a8496bb7a22b7b9233e1cf62b62e5.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000329_488dcaa732a7f03e180de19a0aec2d631ebf31194563263fdaba7c788328ccb8.png)

## Other Factors in Combat

The  previous  section  covers  the  format  for  all  combats but  makes  reference  to  some  of  the  elements  that  are listed below.

## ADDITIONAL RULES

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000330_623914d316c8bc20d874e31a5abf1ba4aa09a7838dc98e71274d18543b7902ef.png)

Optional combat rules are presented in the Additional Rules chapter, starting on page 327.

## Combat Manoeuvres

If someone describes making an attack with a goal other than inflicting physical harm, we call this a 'combat manoeuvre.' The GM should consider the main focus of each goal ( Step 1: Set Your Goal ,  page  127).  If  the  key  goal  is  to  achieve something  other  than  inflicting  harm,  such  as  pushing someone out of the way, throwing an opponent to the floor, restraining someone in a headlock, disarming an opponent, and  so  on,  then  treat  it  as  a  combat  manoeuvre.  Either side-or, indeed, both sides-could be attempting a combat manoeuvre at the same time.

Any character performing a combat manoeuvre rolls their Fighting skill in the opposed roll during Step 3 (page 130). If  a  character  performing  a  combat  manoeuvre  wins  the opposed roll, they can:

- Restrain their target, imposing a penalty die on actions made by that target-until the target escapes from restraint or is released. Note that the person being restrained (or a third party) can attempt to attack the character doing the restraining, and if they succeed on their turn in the round or a following one, they are released.
- Knock the target prone. Subsequent Fighting attacks made against a prone character gain one bonus die. A prone character may stand up on their turn in the round as a free action.
- Apply handcuffs to an already restrained character.
- Disarm the target or knock whatever the target was holding to the ground. If the character initiating this combat manoeuvre wins by two levels of success or more (for example, they get a Hard success against an opponent who rolls a Weak success), they may wrest whatever was being held away from the target and take it for themselves.
- Move the target up to 5 metres/yards, perhaps pushing their opponent through a doorway or down a stairwell.

Although a combat manoeuvre is different to most other forms  of  attack-in  that  the  primary  goal  is  to  restrain, disarm, or move the target in some way, rather than inflict damage-it may still  lead  to  some  damage,  depending  on what the manoeuvre is; e.g., if one character successfully uses a combat manoeuvre to push their opponent off a rooftop. If the  combat  manoeuvre causes something to happen in the story  that  would  obviously  cause  damage,  the  GM  should refer to Table 7: Other Forms of Damage (page 149).

There  is  a  limitation  on  combat  manoeuvres.  If  the opponent's STR exceeds that of the character attempting the manoeuvre by 50 or more points, the combat manoeuvre is ineffective and automatically fails. A character might be able to grab hold of their opponent, but if that opponent is much stronger than them, there's little they can realistically achieve.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000331_d61f8e3901b65de4c1af48ee0682deab46172706f03a6024097ec8310b7117b0.png)

## Police Training

In the UK, the police receive extensive training  on  how  to  de-escalate  confrontations so they can avoid having to engage in melee combat. As a result, a police  officer  character's  first  line  of action in combat should be to try and talk  their  opponent  down,  using  an opposed Social skill roll. Depending on what they say to their target, the target might oppose with Read Person to see if they believe what the officer is saying, or POW, if the target is all riled up and spoiling for a fight. Don't forget that, if the officer is in uniform or shows their warrant card, they get a bonus die on the  Social  skill  roll  ( Police  Officer/ Detective , page 71).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000332_2f1b0dea2c0264c0bc512a213204e792edffbdafc5f60b88d7765561982f3a10.png)

Even if the officer character fails to dissuade their target from getting physical, their training dictates that they'll first  attempt  to  restrain  an  opponent rather  than  try  to  cause  them  harm. (See also The Four Es , page 71).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000333_19d00cc8561f317cef60a13254ff48636887c419bd9e6e527c84af98d9a75826.png)

## Improvised Weapons

In  games,  everyone  is  encouraged  to  make  use  of  the environment  in  their  fight  narrations.  Doing  so  adds  a unique  feel  to  each  combat  and  helps  to  bring  the  setting to  life.  Picture  the  scene  and  grab  what  comes to mind. If the fight takes place in a bar, you should be swinging pool cues, throwing bottles, and hitting each other with barstools. Fire  pokers,  hat  stands,  and  ornamental  swords  may  be  at hand inside  a  mansion.  Computer  monitors,  power  cables,

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000334_29ab26e4c6bccc33edfba73a0752aa2fab56b63080672383b4afc754bba37e87.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000335_54a3528ce93ccc155b2e950bbf949cba3ab8ba447e366a81a1f416d683ac6e2a.png)

and scissors could all be deadly in an office. The GM should encourage the players to improvise and try not to block their ideas  without  good  reason.  If  you  want  to  introduce  the fickle hand of fate to determine whether a handy improvised weapon is present, call for a Luck Roll (page 113). If the roll is successful, there it is!

All  sorts  of  items  can  be  used  to  inflict  physical  harm: staplers,  cricket  bats,  rocks,  and  so  on.  Even  an  electric kettle or toaster could be smashed over the back of someone's head for a nasty injury. Improvised weapons are used with the  Fighting  skill  if  they  are  used  in  melee  combat  or  the Athletics skill if they are thrown at the target. If the item is particularly cumbersome or unwieldy, the GM may allow it to be used as a weapon with a penalty die ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111).

All  improvised  weapons  have  a  base  weapon  damage  1 ( Table 3: Weapons , page 61).

## Outnumbered

If  in Step 1: Set Your Goal (page 127), you attack (target) an  individual  who  has  already  been  targeted  by  someone else  this  round,  you  make  your  Fighting  roll  with  a  bonus die ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice ,  page 111). As, when a character is 'outnumbered' by the opposition, that character is at a disadvantage. This only applies to melee attacks.

Remember,  each  character  may  initiate  one  action  on their turn in a combat round. In addition, they get to react (respond) to each attack made against them ( Step 2: Target Responds , page  127),  which  creates  the  opposed  roll  in melee combat. A character's first response is free but if they are targeted again in the same combat round, while they still get to respond, their opponent now gains an edge-the bonus die on their Fighting roll. This doesn't apply to ranged attacks like firearms and spells.

Example: let's  go  back  to  our example from Response 3: Fight Back (page  129).  Our  investigator  is  engaged  in  melee  combat with three opponents. The first time the investigator is attacked in the combat round, their response is to dodge-as this is their first response (reaction), it's free. Then the second opponent attacks them; the  investigator  can  still  respond,  but  the  second  attacker is now awarded a bonus die on their Fighting roll-because the investigator is now outnumbered. As the investigator still has all of their response options open, this time they decide to fight back. When the third opponent attacks, they are also awarded a bonus die on their Fighting roll-as the investigator is still outnumbered. The  investigator,  having  used  their  one  fight  back  response,  can now only choose to dodge, flee, or do nothing. Perhaps it's time for them to run away…

135

The exception to the outnumbered rule is when a character has the Fast Reactions advantage (page 51), as those with this advantage can never be outnumbered; thus, their opponents do not gain the bonus die. Remember, someone with Fast Reactions can also use the fight back response twice in the same combat round -rather than once like everyone else.

## Armour

Characters  can  wear  armour  to  lessen  the  effects  of  being damaged.  In  some  cases,  the  environment  may  provide 'armour' in terms of some general protection, such as when hiding  behind  a  tree  or  wall  ( Cover  and  Concealment , page 138). There are three types of 'worn' armour: Natural Toughness, regular armour, and ballistic armour.

- Natural Toughness: can be taken by an investigator as an advantage (page 51). Unarmed and blunt melee weapon attacks-batons, cricket bats, etc.-against a person with Natural Toughness inflict 1 less damage. The damage from knives, bullets, or damage-causing spells, e.g., Fireball (page 183) is not affected. Some beings possess natural toughness innately; an elephant does because of its tough skin, and the fae do, too, because, well… just because.
- Ballistic armour: is heavy, and generally restricted to military or specialised police issue, e.g., Armed Response/SWAT teams or other officers attending a firearms incident. Damage inflicted for melee and firearms attacks is reduced by 2 damage. Note that this armour type does not protect against certain spell attacks (see individual spell descriptions).
- Regular armour: includes the standard issue Met stab vest and is uncommon outside of the police. Such armour reduces the damage from melee attacks by 1 damage. Note that this armour type does not protect against firearms and certain spell attacks (see individual spell descriptions).

## GM ADVICE

Armour cannot help against poison damage, drowning, or certain magical attacks (see individual spell descriptions). A character with Natural Toughness  who  also wears regular or ballistic armour  gains the benefit for both types, but remember  that  Natural  Toughness only  applies to  unarmed  and  blunt  weapon  attacks,  not  knives, firearms, and spells.

The  damage  reduction  provided  by  all  three types of armour stacks with that from the Shield spell (page 185).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000336_6a1fc01a624c9a7a407f62fea8554d9ef7a69d642085e169bf42e41c9a3a4fa3.png)

## An Extended Combat Example

This extended example takes you through and builds on many of the concepts we've discussed in the preceding sections. Nafeesa is in Jimmy's flat again, trying to get some more vital information out of him, but will she succeed? There are three characters in the scene: Jimmy, Nafeesa, and Izzy.

## Combat Round 1

## Jimmy's turn:

Jimmy: STR 70, DEX 60, Fighting 40/20.

Nafeesa: STR 70, DEX 40, Fighting 60/30.

Step 1: Set Your Goal: Jimmy is attacking Nafeesa, pushing her hard against the wall.

Step 2: Target Responds: Nafeesa decides to dodge Jimmy's attack. Step 3: Dice Rolls: the GM rolls 13 for Jimmy's Fighting roll-below half of Jimmy's skill value of 40%, so a Hard success. The player rolls 73 for Nafeesa's Fighting roll, above Nafeesa's skill value of 60%, so a Weak success. The GM's Hard success beats the player's Weak success. Step 4: Damage Roll: as Jimmy has beaten Nafeesa, he makes a damage roll. As it's melee combat, that's a STR roll. The GM rolls a 52; as Jimmy's STR is 70, that's a Regular success, which inflicts 1 damage, plus the base weapon damage. Jimmy is unarmed, so, in this case, he inflicts a total of 1 damage (as he isn't using a weapon of any sort; Table 3: Weapons , page 61). Nafeesa is not wearing her body armour, so the player ticks the Hurt box on the character sheet ( Damage Conditions , page 133).

Step 5: Narration: the GM describes how Jimmy pushes Nafeesa hard. Her head smacks against the wall, making her see stars. Step 6: Next Player's Turn.

## Nafeesa's turn:

Jimmy: STR 70, DEX 60, Fighting 40/20.

Nafeesa: STR 70, DEX 40, Fighting 60/30 (Hurt).

Step 1: Set Your Goal: Nafeesa is going to try to grapple Jimmy to the ground.

Step  2:  Target  Responds: Jimmy  is  dodging  ( Response  2: Dodge , page 128)

Step 3: Dice Rolls: the  GM  rolls  15  for  Jimmy,  another  Hard success! The player rolls 43 for Nafeesa, a Regular success.

Jimmy has the better level of success and wins the opposed roll.

Step  4:  Damage  Roll: Jimmy  won,  but  he  was  dodging  so  no damage is inflicted.

Step 5: Narration: the GM describes how Jimmy slips like a fish out  of  Nafeesa's  hands.  Nafeesa's  attack  has  failed,  but  Jimmy hasn't achieved anything, either.

Step 6: Next Player's Turn.

## Izzy's turn:

Jimmy: STR 70, DEX 60, Fighting 40/20. Nafeesa: STR 70, DEX 40, Fighting 60/30 (Hurt). Izzy: STR 40, DEX 70, Stealth 30/15, Fighting 60/30.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000337_dd7d4ef9b11f29a96c3847eb873de70a2cab6372898b5ae2797a0ccd60622b55.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000338_b36dc07a8fb8ee712590b75e22b7e85173cb6272d070d49c778bc8e4449a5994.png)

Up to this point, it was just Jimmy and Nafeesa in the fight, but unbeknownst to Nafeesa, Jimmy has a friend-Izzy-hiding in the cupboard. It's the GM's turn again as Nafeesa has taken her turn, and the GM decides to have Izzy act rather than stay in hiding. While Nafeesa and Jimmy were fighting, Izzy exited the cupboard and now makes her attack.

Step 1: Set Your Goal: Izzy rushes at Nafeesa, intending to shove her to the floor.

Step 2: Target Responds: Nafeesa tries to fight back, lashing out with an elbow.

Step 3: Dice Rolls: the GM rolls with a bonus die because Nafeesa is Outnumbered (page  135),  rolling  95  and  45  for  Izzy,  and taking the better result because of the bonus die: a Regular success. The player rolls 13 for Nafeesa, a Hard success. The player has a better level of success and wins the roll.

Step 4: Damage Roll: the  player  rolls  03  for  Nafeesa's  damage (STR)  roll  and  decides  to  spend  2  Luck  points  to  adjust  the result to a Critical success ( Spending Luck , page 113), inflicting 3  damage  on  Izzy.  The  GM  marks  Izzy  as  Down  ( Damage Conditions , page 133).

Step 5: Narration: Nafeesa's  player  describes  how  she  pulls  her hand  back  sharply  to  grab  her  handcuffs  from  her  belt  (with  a view to restraining Jimmy next round), accidentally smacking the unseen Izzy square in the face with her elbow. The GM adds that Izzy hits her head on the floor as she goes down and is now out cold, with blood pouring from a broken nose.

Step 6: Next Player's Turn.

## Combat Round 2

## Jimmy's turn:

Jimmy: STR 70, DEX 60, Fighting 40/20. Nafeesa: STR 70, DEX 40, Fighting 60/30 (Hurt). Izzy: STR  40,  DEX  70,  Stealth  30/15,  Fighting  60/30 (Down-cannot act this round).

Step 1: Set Your Goal: Jimmy is pulling out a knife and trying to stab Nafeesa.

Step 2: Target Responds: now faced with a more serious threat, Nafeesa drops her handcuffs and tries to disarm Jimmy. She has chosen to fight back with a Combat Manoeuvre (page 134).

Step 3: Dice Rolls: the GM rolls 55 for Jimmy, a Weak success. The player rolls 43 for Nafeesa, a Regular success. The player has a better (higher) level of success and wins the roll.

Step 4: Damage Roll: Nafeesa used a combat manoeuvre, so no damage is inflicted.

Step 5: Narration: the player describes how Nafeesa grabs Jimmy's arm and beats it against the wall, forcing him to drop the blade on the floor. Jimmy is now disarmed.

Had  the  player  chosen  restraint  instead  of  disarming,  Jimmy would now have a penalty die on his actions, but he would still be holding his knife-meaning Nafeesa would still be at risk of being stabbed on Jimmy's next turn.

Step 6: Next Player's Turn.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000339_9c78455b2de03025f73bfd8d185695d2895404842174f8636a09d257ccfcaa9a.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000340_2f2cd7b99c321250520e260a7d45a857e3aa13e91592b0ef77bf64a63d625fb2.png)

## Nafeesa's turn:

Jimmy: STR 70, DEX 60, Fighting 40/20. Nafeesa: STR 70, DEX 40, Fighting 60/30 (Hurt). Izzy: STR  40,  DEX  70,  Stealth  30/15,  Fighting  60/30 (Down-cannot act this round).

Step  1:  Set  Your  Goal: the  GM  describes  how  one  of  Jimmy's buddies  is  knocking  on  the  front  door  and  shouting.  Nafeesa, realising that the odds are not in her favour, is making for the back door (fleeing).

Step 2: Target Responds: Jimmy decides to try to take Nafeesa down with a swift headbutt to the face before she can leave.

Step 3: Dice Rolls: the GM rolls 21 for Jimmy, a Regular success. Nafeesa's player rolls 69, a Weak success. Nafeesa is already hurt, so her player decides to spend 9 Luck points to lower the roll by 9 to 60, adjusting it to a Regular success. That means a tie, which Nafeesa wins because it's her turn in the combat round.

Step 4: Damage Roll: Nafeesa  is  simply  fleeing,  so  no  damage is inflicted.

Step 5: Narration: the player describes how Nafeesa manages to sidestep Jimmy as his forehead rushes towards her face. Nafeesa is now out of the back door and running.

Step  6:  Next  Player's  Turn: the  GM  decides  that  Jimmy  isn't giving pursuit, so the combat ends here.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000341_c83c7b9321ca03fc4c70d14e63962c775df746cd0d5587b6ce84a5147c575a9c.png)

## An Extended Combat Example (Continued)

## The Next Day

The next day, Nafeesa spots Izzy leaving a corner shop (convenience store)  and  shouts  for  her  to  stop.  Izzy,  still  smarting  from  that broken nose, reacts with an attack, triggering combat.

## Combat Round 1

## Izzy's turn:

Izzy: STR 40, DEX 70, Athletics 30/15, Fighting 60/30. Nafeesa : STR 70, DEX 40, Fighting 60/30.

Step 1: Set Your Goal: Izzy drops her shopping bag, grabs a tin of baked beans, and hurls it at Nafeesa. This is a thrown attack, using the Athletics skill.

Step 2: Target Responds: Nafeesa is attempting to dodge (thrown attacks can be dodged).

Step 3: Dice Rolls: the GM decides that Izzy is about 20 metres/ yards from Nafeesa, which is far enough away to qualify as beyond standard range; thus, Izzy must roll Athletics with a penalty die. The GM rolls 18 and 28 for Izzy and takes the higher result because of the penalty die: a Regular success. Nafeesa's player rolls a 25, a Hard success. Nafeesa wins the opposed roll, and the attack misses. Step 4: Damage Roll: it's a miss, so there's no damage.

Step 5: Narration: the tin of baked beans whistles past Nafeesa's head as she ducks out of the way.

Step 6: Next Player's Turn: perhaps  Nafeesa  decides  to  pursue Izzy,  initiating  a  chase.  So,  for  the  moment,  we'll  leave  this example here…

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000342_1a4a990332013b122e993615cace57f60234e3c1e5055f5c53679df9844616b7.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000343_306ee0309050d59dee27626217695c9e4535bf4eece98db52f6efa8b06de5be0.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000344_784a6232456ebec6d0c868df7a242a659cf49a503cbb4b9c55a547591a85d623.png)

3

## Ranged Attack Modifiers

There are a variety of factors that can give an attacker a distinct advantage  or  disadvantage  when  making  a  ranged  attack, granting them either a bonus die or a penalty die on their skill roll-such  as Dive  for  Cover (page  128; Bonus  Dice  and Penalty Dice ,  page 111). The most common options besides diving for cover are detailed here, but others may occur during play-if  they  do,  the  GM  should  use  their  discretion  as  to whether a penalty or bonus die is justified.

Each relevant penalty or bonus die for a given firearms, spell,  or  melee  attack  is  added  up,  then  applied  to  the appropriate skill roll to determine the outcome. One penalty die cancels out one bonus die, and vice versa. If you end up with more than one bonus die or more than one penalty die on a roll, refer to Multiple Bonus or Penalty Dice (page 143).

## Cover and Concealment (Penalty Die)

When  a  character  isn't  wearing  armour,  one  way  they can  attempt  to  protect  themselves  is  by  using  cover  and concealment. The degree of protection offered by cover and concealment  is  variable-a  wooden  fence  may  not  stop  a bullet, but it does obscure the target, making the shot more difficult.  Concealment  of  at  least  half  of  the  target's  body adds one penalty die to a firearms or spell attack roll.

Common sense dictates  that,  if  the  shooter  can't  see  their target, it's going to be harder to hit them, but if they're hiding behind something that's barely larger than they are, then it may still be worth a shot or two through that cover, particularly if it's something relatively fragile, like a wooden crate. If shooting at a target through cover, the GM should use their judgement in selecting a comparable material from Table 6: Cover Protection (nearby). The points of protection are deducted directly from any damage before it is applied to the target.

Example: Nafeesa is using the cover of a plywood crate, but her opponent's rifle shot still hits the mark (even with a penalty die), passing  through  the  wood  and  into  Nafeesa.  The  shot  inflicts  2 damage but this is decreased by the 'armour' provided by the crate. In this case, 2 damage is reduced to 1 damage.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000345_add265f232d657d838359155a9f9ed1ea1117e7bfe319e7965acb84b85b011d8.png)

## Table 6: Cover Protection

| Material                          | Blocks   |
|-----------------------------------|----------|
| 2.5 cm (1 inch) plywood           | 1 damage |
| 2.5 cm (1 inch) brick or concrete | 2 damage |
| 4 cm (1.5 inch) bulletproof glass | 2 damage |
| 5 mm (3/16 inch) steel plate      | 2 damage |
| Large sandbag                     | 3 damage |

## Point-Blank Range (Bonus Die)

If the target is within point-blank range, meaning within 3 metres/yards or less, the attacker gains a bonus die on their relevant skill roll. This counts for spells, firearms, and other ranged weapons.

## Aiming (Bonus Die)

When a character wants to increase their chance of hitting with a firearm or spell, they can take a pause to aim their attack.  The  intention  to  take  careful  aim  must  be  declared on  the  character's  turn  in  the  order  of  combat,  with  the character  taking  an  aiming  action  for  their  turn  (they  can do  nothing  else).  The  attack  is  made  on  their  turn  in  the following  round.  If  no  other  actions  are  taken  before  the attack is made, i.e., they take a turn aiming and nothing else, the  attacker  gains  one  bonus  die  on  the  relevant  skill  roll. But if the aiming character suffers damage or moves while aiming, the aiming advantage is lost. Only one bonus die may be gained by aiming.

Spending a combat round aiming improves the attacker's chance  of  hitting  their  target.  The  rules  don't  cover  called shots;  if  a  player  describes  aiming  at  a  specific  area  of  an NPC-for example, the target's head-the player and GM should  interpret  the  dice  rolls  and  consequent  damage  to determine if the specified area is hit. So, a firearms attack that  only  inflicts  1  damage  could  be  interpreted  as  hitting the target's ear or grazing the side of their face. Instead, if it inflicts 5 damage (a fatal blow), the shot has hit the target smack dab between the eyes ( Damage Conditions ,  page 133).

## Fast-Moving Targets (Penalty Die)

A target moving at full speed (MOV 8 or more) is harder to hit.  Apply one penalty die to the skill roll. This counts for both spells and firearms attacks alike.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000346_000fc200fda35f76d475f815af281ab2222a430817fb4eeca4d8ae8ca2927e54.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000347_1f5cffe6a2e408180f06494123aa8b4e681bfa256c383a226dcac53a8cfce072.png)

## Point - Blank Range Example

Nafeesa : Magic 60/30. Jimmy: DEX 60, Fighting 40/20.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000348_38e7dffa48c75f840e255141dbc4d1b589ccf808491a1dd02f4409aaf13bd960.png)

Step 1: Set Your Goal: Nafeesa is casting Fireball at Jimmy Four-Fingers.

Step  2:  Target  Responds: Jimmy  is  charging towards her, wielding a knife.

Step 3: Dice Rolls: Nafeesa's player rolls Magic with a  bonus  die  because  Jimmy  is  in  point-blank  range, rolling  18  and  88,  and  taking  the  lower  result-a Hard success. The GM doesn't make a roll-Jimmy isn't  diving  for  cover,  he's  just  charging  in,  so  the player's roll is unopposed.

Step 4: Damage Roll: Nafeesa's player makes a damage roll; as she is casting a spell, that's a DEX roll ( Damage Roll , page 132). They get a success, so that's 1 damage plus base weapon damage; in this case, the weapon is a fireball, which inflicts 2 base damage, so that's 3 damage overall.

Step  5:  Narration: the  player  describes  how the  fireball  hits  Jimmy  in  the  chest  and  he  just staggers  and  falls  (the  GM  marks  Jimmy  as Down). Nafeesa whips out her Airwave and calls for  an  ambulance,  and  then  rushes  to  see  how badly hurt Jimmy is.

Step 6: Next Player's Turn.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000349_5c55d8fa356e20f067de25681742b54ac5fc32f6f2aa0556d836b1cb438545fd.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000350_f74585c45bfa777624994db1ec9cd0fc251cf8bb0aceac297557da5e5f5e9adb.png)

## Target Size (Bonus or Penalty Die)

Smaller  targets  are  harder  to  hit.  If  the  target  is  the  size of  Toby  (a  small  dog)  or  below,  apply  one  penalty  die  to the skill roll.

## Reloading Firearms

Normally, it takes one combat round to reload any firearm, and a character can do this as their action on their turn in the combat round. Note that it is possible for a character to reload a firearm on their turn in the combat round, then fire a shot from that same firearm as a fight back action later in the same combat round ( Fight Back Using Firearms , page 129).

## Multiple Shots (Bonus Die/Increased Damage)

Many firearms-such as machine pistols, submachine guns, and assault rifles-can fire multiple shots per round. In RoL:RPG , the term 'multiple shots' always means three shots fired in rapid succession. Some automatic weapons can be set to fire a short burst of three shots with one pull of the trigger, which these rules also treat as multiple shots. Firing multiple shots places speed above accuracy. This counts for firearms and certain spells (see individual spell descriptions for further details).

When firing multiple shots, only one dice roll is made to hit the target. Before rolling to hit, the attacker must choose either to increase their chance to hit or increase their damage:

- Increased chance to hit: roll to hit with a bonus die, but roll only once for damage (as for a single shot)-basically, firing multiple shots with this option increases the chance to hit but does the same damage as a single shot.
- Increased damage: do not take the bonus die, gambling that you'll do more damage if you hit. Make the damage roll as normal, but double the damage indicated by the level of success and then add base weapon damage, e.g., a failure inflicts 2 damage, a Regular success with an automatic handgun inflicts 3 damage (Regular damage of 1 doubled to 2 + 1 base weapon damage), and so on.

If  the  attacker  chooses the increased damage option but fails the roll, they cannot then decide to add the bonus die retroactively in hopes of changing their failure into a success.

## GM ADVICE

In RoL:RPG, 'multiple shots' like the ones described in this section are always directed at a single target.

On the other hand, larger targets are easier to hit. If the target is the size of a Ford Asbo (standard car or larger), apply one bonus die to the skill roll.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000351_9a82edd35759bcff29a90e0d6d4f6f6c9170096aeb1ed5be7515c9efab3ccfce.png)

## ADDITIONAL RULES

For rules covering shooting at multiple targets with semi- and fully-automatic weapons, see the Automatic Fire section, page 329.

3

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000352_b8e4239e2c6b7d2036299ad550f101ca9d2b40054986264f6d9488cb9576e075.png)

## Multiple Shots Examples

First, let's play through an NPC choosing the bonus to hit option. Izzy pulls a pistol and lets off three shots at Nafeesa at point-blank range (3 metres/yards).

Izzy: DEX 70, Firearms 30/15.

Nafeesa:

DEX 40.

Step  1: Set  Your  Goal:  Izzy  is  shooting  at  Nafeesabam, bam, BAM!

Step  2:  Target  Responds: Nafeesa  acts  on  instinct  and dives for cover.

Step  3:  Dice  Rolls: Izzy  incurs  a  penalty  die  because Nafeesa is diving for cover.  Izzy also gains a bonus die for Point-Blank Range (page 138), as well as another bonus die for firing multiple shots. That's two bonus dice and one penalty die-one bonus die cancels out one penalty die,  so Izzy ends up with one bonus die on her Firearms roll. The GM rolls 61 and 11, taking the lower result, a Hard success. That's a hit.

Step  4:  Damage  Roll: the  GM  rolls  89,  which  is  over Izzy's  DEX.  That  means  the  shot  does  1  damage.  The player marks the Hurt box on Nafeesa's character sheet. She was lucky, this time.

Step 5: Narration: the GM describes how Izzy fires off 3 rapid shots, one of which grazes Nafeesa's thigh.

Step 6: Next Player's Turn.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000353_822619ef1d4390c7233edf72d9f159e7bed0409891e8455038b235604257f777.png)

## Shooting into Melee Combat

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000354_ad7d5b12be13102b67435ea2c80a70cf63167cbb227319e79a17fb2877b12959.png)

## Ranged and Thrown Weapons

Combatants in melee combat are constantly on the move. If the shooter misses because of a fumble, the GM can declare that they've hit a random ally instead. This counts for spells and  firearms  alike.  If  it's  an  investigator  who  fumbled  the shot, the GM could use the other investigators' Luck values to see who gets hit, if appropriate ( Luck Rolls , page 113).

Ranged missile attacks (such as crossbows) are treated like firearms  attacks.  So,  if  you're  using  a  bow,  for  example, anywhere  that  says  'this  applies  to  firearms  attacks'-or words to that effect-also applies to you.

Thrown weapons attacks are resolved in the same way as melee  Fighting  attacks  but  use  the  Athletics  skill  instead of the Fighting skill. If the attack is made beyond standard range, the attacker must roll with a penalty die.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000355_38d407e4aeff2e4392faa6bd9871798a64665fb7193f2797207bec59906a35f0.png)

Now let's replay that with the increased damage option.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000356_488ada8cd2d4ee3dc9375ecd93560b02182a44f1ab50bb42d30b41e3e31d006b.png)

Izzy:

DEX 70. Firearms 30/15

Nafeesa:

DEX 40

Step  1: Set  Your  Goal:  Izzy  is  shooting  at  Nafeesabam, bam, BAM!

Step  2:  Target  Responds: Nafeesa  acts  on  instinct  and dives for cover.

Step  3:  Dice  Rolls: Izzy  incurs  a  penalty  die  because Nafeesa is diving for cover. Izzy also gains a bonus die for Point-Blank Range (page  138).  The  bonus  and  penalty die cancel each other out.  As Izzy decided not to gain a bonus die for multiple shots, she makes a straight Firearms roll with no extra dice. The GM rolls 11; a Hard success. That's a hit.

Step  4:  Damage  Roll: the  GM  rolls  89,  which  is  over Izzy's  DEX. That means the shot inflicts 1 damage, but this  is  doubled  to  2  because  of  the  Multiple  Shots  rule, which means Nafeesa is bloodied.

Step 5: Narration: the GM describes how Izzy fires off 3 rapid shots, one of which hits Nafeesa squarely in the thigh.

Step 6: Next Player's Turn.

## Combat FAQ

Combat can be literally life and death for the characters, so players may have all sorts of questions about what's allowed, what they can do, and so on. Let's try to answer a few of those in the following sections.

## Can I Push My Combat Roll?

No.  There  is  no  option  to  push  combat  rolls  (whether Fighting, Firearms, or Magic). Outside of combat, skill rolls are resolved with one or two rolls, but combat is resolved with a sequence of rolls. Pushing a skill roll indicates a subsequent attempt-a  concerted  effort  to  succeed.  In  combat,  this subsequent attempt would simply be your next punch, your next shot, i.e., your next attack in the following round.

## What Does it Mean if I Roll a Critical Success in Combat?

Critical Success (page 106) rules apply as normal, including the award of a bonus die on the successful attacker's damage roll  ( The Damage Roll ,  page  132).  A  Critical  success  can also be used to incorporate aspects of the surroundings into the  fight,  and  the  GM  should  encourage  the  attacker  to elaborate when describing their superb attack. For example, did some element of the surroundings help them to achieve that  Critical  success?  Did  a  passer-by  distract  the  target momentarily, allowing the attacker to land the perfect blow, or did the weather help in some way to give the attacker an opening they weren't expecting?

## What Does it Mean if I Roll a Fumble in Combat?

Fumble (page  107)  rules  apply  as  normal,  and,  like  a Critical  success,  can  also  be  used  to  incorporate  aspects  of the  surroundings  into Step  5:  Narration (page  133).  A fumble  means  an  exceptionally  poor  outcome.  This  could be  a  particularly  ineffective  attack,  or  it  could  indicate  the character is having a bad interaction with their surroundings, or  that  more  enemies  arrive.  A  fumble  grants  the  GM permission to add something interesting to the story in the opponent's  favour.  For  example,  if  using  a  gun,  a  fumble could indicate a Firearms Malfunction (page 143). If using a  melee  weapon,  it  could  mean  dropping  the  weapon  or  it breaking. In the case of unarmed attacks, it could mean that the  character  who  fumbles  harms  themselves  in  some  way (suffering 1 damage)-after all, it's pretty common for people to injure their own hands in a fist fight.

## What Are My Options When Someone Tries to Hit Me?

If someone tries to hit you with a punch or a weapon, such as a knife, you can:

- Take no direct action and hope they don't inflict too much damage when they hit you ( Do Nothing , page 128).
- Attempt to avoid their blow and hit them back ( Fight Back , page 129). You can only fight back once per combat round unless you have the Fast Reactions advantage (page 51).
- Attempt to avoid their blow ( Dodge , page 128).
- Attempt to hit them with a spell before they hit you ( Fight Back , page 129). You can only fight back once per combat round unless you have the Fast Reactions advantage (page 51).
- Attempt to avoid their blow and run away ( Flee , page 130).
- If you have a firearm in your hand, you can attempt to shoot them before they hit you ( Fight Back , page 129). You can only fight back once per combat round unless you have the Fast Reactions advantage (page 51).
- If the attacker is using a weapon, you could attempt to disarm them using a Combat Manoeuvre (page 134). This could allow you to knock the weapon to the floor or perhaps wrest possession of it from your opponent.
- Attempt to avoid their blow and move your attacker using a Combat Manoeuvre (page 134). This could be to knock them to the floor or push them away, or, if you're on top of a building, it might be to push them off!
- Attempt to avoid their blow and restrain your attacker using a Combat Manoeuvre (page 134).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000357_1697ce39fc2f96de06051445e765cf9f6ffcad2b30d3507fa10a317ba6458823.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000358_9316b1872e20b66fd5eff3d1cd5a5a0c1745421ef4c5746a5a68bd4573b8aa61.png)

3

## What Are My Options When Someone Shoots at Me?

If being shot at from beyond arm's reach, you can:

- Take no direct action and hope that the shot misses ( Do Nothing , page 128).
- Dive for Cover (page 128).
- Turn and flee, in which case the shooter incurs a penalty die if you have MOV 8 or higher ( Fast-Moving Targets , page 138).
- Attempt to hit them with a spell before they hit you ( Fight Back , page 129). You can only fight back once per combat round unless you have the Fast Reactions advantage (page 51).
- If you have a firearm in your hand, you can attempt to shoot them before they hit you ( Fight Back , page 129). You can only fight back once per combat round unless you have the Fast Reactions advantage (page 51).

In addition, if being shot at from within arm's reach, you can fight  back  using  your  Fighting  skill  to  perform  one  of the  following  options  (remember,  though-you  can  only fight back once per combat round unless you have the Fast Reactions advantage; page 51):

- Attempt to inflict harm on the attacker. Use your Fighting skill to oppose your attacker's Firearms roll. If the shooter loses the opposed roll, they take damage and have fired and missed.
- Attempt to restrain the attacker. Use your Fighting skill to oppose your attacker's Firearms roll ( Combat Manoeuvre , page 134). This could be to restrain the hand and arm holding the gun (pointing it away from you as it fires) or restrain/knock the shooter and somehow foil their shot.
- Attempt to disarm the attacker. Use your Fighting skill to oppose your attacker's Firearms roll ( Combat Manoeuvre , page 134). This could allow you to knock the firearm to the floor or perhaps wrest possession of it from your opponent.
- Attempt to avoid their shot and move your attacker using a Combat Manoeuvre (page 134). This could be to knock them to the floor, or push them away, or, if you're on top of a building, it might be to push them off!

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000359_096887fccbb0610309570c58438b0efa7a5a59c462b216c09e7336f936a24e9e.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000360_6d26fdca84fd4c2d8a5502aafd662fe117bb583a91302e77ba180e71b0fe63d0.png)

## What Are My Options When Someone Casts a Spell at Me?

If being targeted with a spell from beyond arm's reach, you can:

- Take no direct action and hope that the spell fails ( Do Nothing , page 128).
- Dive for Cover (page 128).
- Turn and flee, in which case the caster incurs a penalty die if you have MOV 8 or higher ( Fast-Moving Targets , page 138).
- Attempt to hit them with a spell before they hit you ( Fight Back , page 129). You can only fight back once per combat round unless you have the Fast Reactions advantage (page 51).
- If you have a firearm in your hand, you can attempt to shoot them before they hit you ( Fight Back , page 129). You can only fight back once per combat round unless you have the Fast Reactions advantage (page 51).

In addition, if being targeted with a spell from within arm's reach, you can fight back using your Fighting skill to perform one  of  the  following  options  (remember,  though-you  can only fight back once per combat round unless you have the Fast Reactions advantage; page 51):

- Attempt to inflict harm on your attacker. Use your Fighting skill to oppose your attacker's Magic roll. If the attacker loses the opposed roll, they take damage and their spell fails.
- Attempt to restrain the attacker. Use your Fighting skill to oppose your attacker's Magic roll ( Combat Manoeuvre , page 134). This could allow you to restrain the attacker and cause their spell to fail.

## Firearms Malfunctions

Firearms can be prone to jamming or misfiring. With any fumbled  attack  roll,  the  GM  may  decide  that  the  shooter does not merely miss, their weapon doesn't even fire. If the weapon is a revolver, break-open gun, or bolt-action rifle, the problem is merely a dud round. If the weapon is lever-action or automatic, the malfunction is a jam. Fixing a jam requires a successful Mechanical Repair or Firearms skill roll. The user can keep trying to fix the problem once per combat round (on their turn) until they succeed. Later, outside of combat, the effort of fixing a jam might be easy, so no skill roll is needed.

Alternatively, the GM  may  decide  that a Firearms fumble leads to some other outcome, such as hitting an ally ( Shooting  into  Melee  Combat ,  page  140)  or  the  wielder injuring themselves in some way.

143

## Multiple Bonus or Penalty Dice

Sometimes, after you've considered all the factors affecting a combat roll, you may find that you've incurred more than one penalty die-and/or gained more than one bonus die-on a single roll. However, when it comes time to roll the actual dice, you cannot have more than one penalty die or one bonus die applied to that roll at a time.

Remember that one penalty die and one bonus die cancel each other out, so add up all the bonus and penalty dice that apply to a roll first, and remove any that cancel one another out. Then see if you have more than one of either sort still applied to the roll. If you do, then any excess dice are taken care of as follows:

- If, overall, an attack gains two or more bonus dice, make the attack roll with one bonus die. If the attack is successful, you gain a bonus die to the damage roll.
- If, overall, an attack incurs two or more penalty dice, make the attack roll with one penalty die. If the attack is successful, you incur a penalty die to the damage roll.

Example: targeting  a  speeding  rat  would  incur  two  penalty dice-one  for Fast-Moving  Targets (page  138),  the  other  for Target Size (page 139)-but only one penalty die is applied to the combat roll. If the Firearms roll is a success, the rat is hit. The damage roll is now made with a penalty die because of that second applicable penalty die.

## What Factors Affect the Damage Roll?

The damage roll can be affected by the following factors:

- If a player rolled a Critical success on their attack roll (or bought one with Luck points), they gain a bonus die to their damage roll.
- The damage roll may incur a penalty die if the successful attack roll originally incurred two or more penalty dice ( Multiple Bonus or Penalty Dice , page 143).
- The damage roll may gain a bonus die if the successful attack roll originally gained two or more bonus dice ( Multiple Bonus or Penalty Dice , page 143).
- Successful attacks against certain fae characters with an iron weapon gain a bonus die to the damage roll ( The Effects of Cold Iron , page 245).
- If a character has the advantage Natural Toughness (page 51), they suffer 1 less damage from unarmed and blunt melee weapon attacks. The damage from knives, bullets, or damage-causing spells, e.g., Fireball (page 183) is not affected.
- If a character has the advantage Damage Bonus (page 51), they add +1 damage to unarmed and melee attack damage, but not to ranged, firearm, or spell attack damage.

3

- A character wearing regular armour, e.g., a Met stab vest, suffers 1 less damage from melee attacks ( Armour , page 135). Damage from firearms and some spells is unaffected.
- The final amount of damage inflicted may also be affected by Cover and Concealment (page 138).
- A character wearing ballistic armour suffers 2 less damage from melee and firearms attacks ( Armour , page 135). Damage from certain types of spell attack is unaffected.

## Wounds and Healing

So, a character got injured during combat? How does that affect them, both narratively and in game terms? This section looks at what happens when someone gets injured and how quickly they can heal.

Always  remember,  damage  in RoL:RPG is  cumulative ( Damage Conditions , nearby), and:

- If a character suffers a total of 1 damage, they are Hurt (nearby).
- If a character suffers a total of 3 damage, they are Down (nearby).
- If a character suffers a total of 2 damage, they are Bloodied (nearby).
- If a character suffers 4 damage in a single blow (be that from a spell, a weapon, or a firearm), they have a Mortal Wound (page 146).
- If an investigator suffers 5 or more damage in a single attack , they have suffered a fatal blow and must spend 30 Luck points, otherwise they are killed outright. If they can spend 30 Luck points, the damage is reduced to 4 (a mortal wound).

Note: in the list above, 'character' means both investigators and GM-controlled characters or creatures. However, only investigators can spend Luck to avoid certain death.

## Describing Injuries

When  someone  is  injured,  descriptions  of  their  wounds should be appropriate to the type and degree of damage they received. A character is not merely shot but shot in one arm

## GM ADVICE

or the other; likewise, a fall might mean the character has sprained an ankle, or now has a lump on their head.

## Damage Conditions in Depth

Here,  we  look  closely  at  what  each  damage  condition means.  Again,  'character'  means  both  investigators  and GM-controlled  characters  (NPCs)  and  creatures.  When  a damage  condition  is  applied  to  an  investigator,  the  player should check the appropriate box on their character sheet.

## Hurt: 'Ow, That Smarts! '

The character has taken a painful blow or a flesh wound. They are in pain but are otherwise able to carry on as normal.

## Bloodied: In Serious Trouble!

The character is still standing but is the worse for wear. They may have blood running down their face or from somewhere else, but they're not out of the fight yet!

While bloodied, the character is impaired ( Impairment , page  118).  Any  rolls  of  90  or  over  count  as  a  fumble  and trigger an Involuntary Action (page 108). In the case of an investigator,  the  player  should  check  the  Impaired  box  on their character sheet to remind them of their investigator's impairment status. A character is either impaired or not, so if they were already impaired there is no additional effect to being bloodied.

## Down: Out of Action!

The character is floored and out of the fight. They are on the ground, groaning and bleeding. They are not dead. They are not unconscious, but neither are they really aware of what's going  on  around  them.  In  effect,  they  are  incapacitated. If  other  characters  are  still  fighting,  leave  the  downed character on the floor until the fight is done and dusted or someone  tends  to  them.  If  an  already  downed  character takes further damage it is ignored, but a determined effort to kill them would normally be successful ( Death-Bloody Murder! following).

## Death: Bloody Murder!

During  combat,  avoid  saying,  'You  took  1  damage,' when  someone  is  hit.  Instead,  describe  the  effects of damage based on the level of detail you and your players are comfortable with ( Important: Tone , page 123).  If  the  victim  is  an  investigator,  first  describe the  scene  and  injury  and  then  let  them  know  how much damage they've suffered in numerical terms, so  they  can  check  off  the  relevant  boxes  on  their character sheet.

When a character suffers 5 damage from a single attack it is a  fatal  blow  and  causes  instant  death.  An  investigator  can choose to spend 30 Luck points to turn that fatal blow into a Mortal Wound (page 146). Otherwise, the only way for an investigator to be killed in combat is for another character to

## ADDITIONAL RULES

If a downed character wants to stay in the fight, see Spending  Luck to  Resist  Incapacitation (page  333) for  an  advanced  optional  method  to  keep  them in the action.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000361_f3392107a78f313ace75ba25d6d54987f8886eed8eb35fa870ac366db070d44c.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000362_d1c01308ddffd578f5c8f367f69361f99352d6b8f726dcb69ceb174f72425a97.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000363_9916242239da6e1d5f5da1c171065a2d80ca1d345678d7a30a45388298eb36de.png)

purposefully declare they are murdering them. For example, if the character is down already and someone states they are strangling  them  or  putting  a  bullet  in  their  head  at  close range-clearly with the goal of murdering them-then the target is well and truly dead.

NPCs, by default, leave downed investigators on the floor and  run  off,  or  take  them  captive,  rather  than  murdering them. We see Peter Grant get beaten sometimes and, rather embarrassingly,  kidnapped 1 ,  but  do  you  imagine  he  would ever be murdered in the novels? At the same time, however, we want the threat of death to create tension. In the novels, Peter fears he might die, and we, as readers, share his fear even though we feel sure he will survive… this time .

## After the Fight

Once a fight is finished, stop using combat rounds and check to  see  how  badly  the  characters  have  been  injured.  This section,  except  for Down (page  144)  and Mortal Wound (page 146), applies to both investigators and NPCs. The GM can use both the down and mortal wound rules for characters besides the investigators if they wish, but equally, they can just decide how badly injured an NPC is-or whether they live or die-as required by the plot.

## After the Fight: Hurt

Anyone  who  was hurt at  the  end  of  the  fight  is  soon fully  recovered.  In  the  case  of  an  investigator,  the  player should  erase  the  check  mark  from  the  Hurt  box  on  their character sheet.

## After the Fight: Bloodied

Anyone  who  was bloodied at  the  end  of  the  fight  soon recovers, though it should be noted that they may now look terrible: ruffled, torn, dirty, or blood-stained clothes; grazed knuckles, a split lip, or a black eye; and so on. In the case of an investigator, the player should erase the check marks in both the Hurt and Bloodied boxes on their character sheet.

If the character was impaired because of being bloodied, they lose the impairment once the fight is over. In the case of an investigator, the player should remove the check mark from the Impaired box on their character sheet. If they were impaired for any other reason, they recover in the usual way ( Impairment and Recovery , page 119).

## After the Fight: Down

Anyone who was down at  the  end of the fight soon makes a partial recovery. As with bloodied, it should be noted that they  may  now  look  terrible:  ruffled,  torn,  dirty,  or  bloodstained clothes; grazed knuckles, a split lip, or a black eye;

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000364_8db03238d5c03a08049615e51983e85b403ddaf84f930c94ba36ce86fd079d9e.png)

Footnote:

1 Lies Sleeping

145

3

and  so  on.  If  an  investigator,  the  player  should  erase  the marks in the Bloodied and Down boxes on their character sheet, but apply the following two conditions:

- The investigator remains hurt until the end of the following day, or they can spend 10 Luck points to remove the check mark from the Hurt box on their character sheet.
- The investigator remains impaired until the end of the following day ( Impairment , page 118), or they can spend 10 Luck points to remove the check mark from the Impaired box on their character sheet.

Thus, with a spend of 20 Luck points, the investigator has been very lucky indeed-though they were briefly knocked down  and  out  of  the  fight,  they  have  escaped  any  lasting harm and have managed to walk away with barely a scratch! In the case of an NPC, the GM can either apply these rules or decide if it suits the story for the character to recover from their injuries, and how long that recovery takes.

## After the Fight: Mortal Wound

A character takes a mortal wound when they suffer 4 damage in  a  single  blow .  A  mortal  wound  means  the  character's injuries are severe. The character is automatically down and can take no actions, and they require either urgent treatment from  a  trained  professional,  or  hospitalisation  ( Medical Aid ,  page 147). If they do not get such help, they are dead within  an  hour,  although First  Aid (following)  can  buy them some time.

In the case of an NPC, the GM can either apply these rules or decide if it suits the story for the character to recover from their injuries, how long that takes, and what the long-term effects might be.

## First Aid

It  is  assumed  that  all  characters  can  provide  some  level  of first aid. First aid is only useful when dealing with a mortal wound, when it can help the dying person survive until they receive medical assistance ( Medical Aid , following). Anyone attempting to administer first aid should make an INT roll:

- With a success, the time it takes a mortally wounded character to die extends from 'within an hour' to 'within

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000365_b935cb6e8b999eaba46e644c87648749d1825db6cb56b64d6c6a003f73402fe0.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000366_2d3eccee5dce080ec2c4135156e524b34858010e5ec1f79a3a8a1c0968f8e0f9.png)

two hours,' granting more time to benefit from proper medical treatment and/or for an ambulance to arrive.

- If the first aid roll is failed or fumbled, the first aid is superficial and grants no benefit.
- If first aid is successfully applied before a Luck roll is made to call an ambulance ( Medical Aid ), then one bonus die is applied to the roll ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111).

## GM ADVICE

Luck points can be spent on the INT roll for administering  first  aid  ( Spending  Luck , page  113). Alternatively, a player has the option of pushing the roll,  but  failure  means  they  haven't  extended  the injured party's time to receive medical treatment before they die.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000367_5047918462d0202b317161509c446ac43856fec50feeb2980afd40f2d1df1d65.png)

## Medical Aid

Doctors and nurses in this game do not possess the healing magic  that  some  players  may  be  accustomed  to  in  other fantasy RPGs, and cannot miraculously heal people in the middle of a fight. Healing magic is not a part of Newtonian practice,  but  it  is  said  to  feature  in  some  other  magical traditions. As a result, medical aid works in the game much as it does in the real world.

Only the Medicine skill can save the life of a character who has sustained a mortal wound, and anyone with the Medicine skill can try to save a dying person. To save someone with a  mortal  wound, a character must have the Medicine skill and  suitable  equipment;  the  minimum  equipment  being what you'd expect to find in a doctor, paramedic, or nurse's bag (see  the  relevant  occupations  in Chapter 1 for  further details).  How  and  where  the  treatment  is  administered affects the Medicine roll:

- If administered single-handedly in a street, with just the bare minimum of equipment, the Medicine roll incurs one penalty die.
- If administered with an assistant and quality facilities and equipment, grant one bonus die to the Medicine roll.
- If administered in a safe, clean place with reasonable equipment and supplies, the Medicine roll is made without bonus or penalty dice.
- If administered in a well-equipped and modern hospital, the GM should forgo any dice rolls and declare the dying character is automatically saved.

If the Medicine roll is failed, the character dies-although Luck points can be spent to turn it into a success ( Spending Luck , page 113). It's hard to see what would justify pushing the roll in this case, but if the players come up with something that works for you, then the GM should allow it.

147

The  alternative  to  making  a  Medicine  roll  is  to  rush the  patient  to  hospital.  An  ambulance  with  paramedics is  hopefully never too far away in a modern city, but if the wounded character is in  a  remote  location  or  other  factors would make access difficult, the GM can call for a Luck roll on behalf of the dying character to determine whether help reaches them in time. If the Luck roll is failed, the character dies on their way to hospital or before the ambulance arrives. The GM must use their discretion to judge whether a Luck roll is required and which investigator should make the roll.

If getting an ambulance or a medical professional might be difficult or take too long, another option is First Aid (page 146).

## Recovering from a Mortal Wound

If medical aid is successfully administered for a mortal wound, the  injured  investigator's  player  must  choose  two  of  the following-each of these is explained in the following sections.

- Injured: the investigator remains bloodied for three days, then hurt for the rest of the week (four more days), plus the investigator is impaired for the whole duration.
- Development Loss: the investigator loses 1 development point.
- Luck Loss: the investigator loses 20 points of Luck.
- Disfigurement: the investigator takes a significant physical disfigurement. An investigator can take this option only once ( Disfigurement , page 148).

## Injured

In narrative terms, if a player decides their investigator remains bloodied for three days, and then hurt for the rest of the week, think  of  it  as  showing  that  the  investigator  is  bruised  and aching after their ordeal, and unable to act at full capacity.

## Luck Loss

When  a  player  decides  to  spend  20  Luck  points,  so  their investigator recovers from a mortal wound, consider this as like a cat using up one of its proverbial nine lives. The investigator's lowered  Luck  means  that  they  may  not  be  so  fortunate  the next time around. Of course, following such an incident, the character's Luck value may continue to rise and fall according to the rules ( Recovering Luck Points , page 115).

Example: during a fight scene, Nafeesa is hit by a shotgun blast, suffering  4  damage.  Her  Met  vest  offers  no  protection  against firearms, so she takes a mortal wound. The player marks the boxes for Hurt, Bloodied, and Down. Nafeesa is on the ground, bleeding. Fortunately, Morgan Omans is also on the scene and calls for an ambulance, which arrives promptly. Nafeesa is taken to hospital and receives treatment. The player must choose two options from the  list.  Firstly,  the  Injured  option  is  taken-bloodied  for  three

days and then hurt for the rest of the week, plus the Impaired box is checked on the character sheet. Secondly, they choose Luck Losslosing 20 points of Luck. The player describes how Nafeesa stays one night in hospital but then checks herself out, and re-joins the investigation, much to Morgan's concern.

## Development Loss

The investigator has suffered a significant setback and does not  develop  their  skills  as  swiftly  as  they  might  otherwise have done. Perhaps, rather than devoting time to practising magic or some other skill, they must use the time undergoing physiotherapy or rest to recover fully from their injuries. This is represented by the loss of a development point.

If  using  the  standard  development  point  award  system ( Gaining Development Points ,  page  120),  an  investigator may not have any development points to lose in this way, e.g., if they haven't saved any from a previous case file. It is up to the GM whether they allow a player to choose this optiondeferring the loss of a development point until the end of the case file. The GM should make a note of who should have a development point deducted when it comes to awarding them at  the  end  of  the  case  file.  The  investigator  may  only  defer once per case file, and the deferment could mean they receive no development points at all for the scenario.

Of  course,  if  the  investigator  has  saved  development points, the loss is immediate.

## Disfigurement

As we witness at the end of the first novel, characters can suffer significant  physical  changes  because  of  injury,  and  these  can have a major effect on the story. The nature of the disfigurement should be appropriate to the injury and have a lasting effect in the investigator's life, such the loss of (or loss of the use of) a limb, hand, or foot, or a significant facial disfigurement of some kind. Note that going forward, this should be considered a feature of the investigator and not a handicap. Such characters, as we see in the novels, are not penalised in their ability to achieve their goals, though their priorities may change.

A  player  can  choose  this  option  only  once  for  their investigator. The disfigurement must be significant: a scar on the upper leg is rarely going to be seen, so does not count, no matter how nasty it is.

Example: Nafeesa is in another fight and is downed by opponents wielding knives, suffering 4 damage. The player has marked the boxes for Hurt, Bloodied (plus ticked the Impaired box), and Down. After the fight, Nafeesa gets medical aid, and so the player decides to take Luck Loss-losing 20 points of Luck-and Disfigurement-writing on her character sheet that Nafeesa now has a large scar running down the side of her face. The disfigurement is a lasting reminder of the fight and something that other people are going to notice.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000368_b678fd30ba1a498a7cdc4facc909db2143bca7bbc12d5066671690f025f35d8a.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000369_0f29a77c2fe3083beca29aee46b4946a31004daa20b0672947b02a476be72281.png)

## Other Forms of Damage

Sometimes, the GM is forced to make a judgement call about the amount of damage inflicted by a random event, such as dropping a TV set on someone's foot, running into a doorpost, or being accidentally Impello -ed over a balcony. Whatever the cause, consider the likely injury and rate its level on Table 7: Other Forms of Damage , and then deal the suggested damage to  the  victim.  Don't  feel  compelled  to  limit  yourself  to  the example amounts of damage listed-you may wish to increase or decrease these based on the circumstances surrounding the particular incident. The information provided here is a guide.

Each injury  type  is  for  one  incident  or  one  round-one round  of  drowning,  one  round  of  being  burned,  etc.  The character suffers damage on each successive round they are still exposed to the source of the harm.

The GM should consider each situation individually, using their discretion in selecting the appropriate level of damage. Anything  less  than  Moderate  damage  can  be  described without recourse to rules or ongoing damage effects.

Example: Nafeesa  fails  a  pushed  Stealth  roll  while  attempting to follow someone through a fairground, and the GM rules she is struck  by  one  of  the  fairground  rides.  There  is  no  damage  listed for  fairground rides so the GM refers to Table 7: Other Forms of  Damage .  The  GM  feels  it  would  be  likely  to  knock  someone unconscious and chooses Severe damage, which inflicts 2 damage, but Nafeesa's MET vest reduces this to 1 damage. Thus, the GM narrates  the  scene,  saying  the  ride  hits  Nafeesa's  body  rather than her head.

## Table 7: Other Forms of Damage

| Level                                                                                   | Suggested Damage                                                                                                                                                                              | Examples                                                                                                                                                                                         |
|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Minor: a person could survive numerous occurrences of this level.                       | Make a CON roll: if failed, take 1 damage; otherwise, no damage is suffered.                                                                                                                  | Mild acid, breathing smoky atmosphere*, a thrown fist-size rock, falling (per 3 metres/ yards) onto soft swamp.                                                                                  |
| Moderate: it would take a number of occurrences at this level to kill a person.         | 1 damage                                                                                                                                                                                      | Falling (per 3 metres/yards) onto grass, strong acid, breathing water*, exposure to vacuum*, fire (e.g., a burning torch).                                                                       |
| Severe: one or two occurrences at this level would render a person unconscious or dead. | 2 damage                                                                                                                                                                                      | Falling (per 3 metres/yards) onto concrete, fire (flamethrower, running through a burning room), being 6 to 9 metres/yards from an exploding hand grenade or stick of dynamite, a mild poison**. |
| Deadly: the average person has a 50% chance of dying at this level.                     | 3 damage                                                                                                                                                                                      | Hit by a car at 48 kph (30mph), being 3 to 6 metres/yards from an exploding hand grenade or stick of dynamite, a strong poison**.                                                                |
| Terminal: outright death is likely at this level.                                       | 4 damage (probably a Mortal Wound , page 146).                                                                                                                                                | Hit by a speeding car (65+ kph/40+ mph), being within 3 metres/yards of an exploding hand grenade or stick of dynamite, a lethal poison**.                                                       |
| Splat: outright death almost certain at this level.                                     | Make a CON roll: if a success, it's a Mortal Wound (page 146); if a failure, immediate death (but can reduce to a mortal wound if 30 Luck points are spent-see Damage Conditions , page 133). | Being involved in a high-speed head-on collision, being hit by a train.                                                                                                                          |

## Notes

* Asphyxiation  and  Drowning: make  a  CON roll  for  the  character  at  the  start  of  their turn in each round (or each minute if not in combat).  Each  time  the  CON  roll  is  failed, the character suffers 1 damage as long as the threat remains. For Moderate damage (water and vacuum), apply a penalty die to the CON roll after the third round (or after 3 minutes; Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111).

** Poison: always call for a CON roll. If failed, apply the damage as stated. With a successful CON roll, move up one level on Table 7: Other

Forms  of  Damage .  For  example:  with  a  successful CON  roll,  a  lethal  poison  is  treated  as  a  strong poison  (Deadly  rather  than  Terminal);  with  a  Hard success,  move  up  two  levels  on Table  7: Other Forms  of  Damage .  Poisons  may  cause  a  variety  of additional symptoms including: stomach pains, vomiting, diarrhoea, chills, sweats, cramps, jaundice, altered  heart  rate,  impaired  vision,  convulsions, unconsciousness,  and  paralysis-it's  up  to  the  GM whether the symptoms permit the victim to act or not, act with a penalty die, or with an increased level of difficulty. At the GM's discretion, they may allow a poison's effects to be shaken off completely if the CON roll is a Critical success.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000370_f8316f81d8c9b2f9fbdfa107403f9cc3907f870e62a0c6136f10526725efc91f.png)

3

## Chases

Action  scenes  in  films  and  books  mostly  fall  into  one  of two  categories:  fights  or  chases-both  of  which  can  be used  to  increase  the  drama  and  excitement  in  your  game. An entertaining chase is more than a simple test of speed, though, and should be made up of a string of locations that challenge the characters as they race through them.

A chase is played out in rounds, much like combat rounds. At  the  start  of  each  chase  round,  the  GM  describes  the situation  and  then  asks  what  action  each  investigator  is taking  when  it  comes  to  their  turn.  The  GM  determines what dice  rolls  are  required,  the  outcome  of  which  should be  incorporated  into  the  story  of  the  chase,  perhaps  even changing the situation faced by the next player.

While some measure of gritty realism might be desirable, avoid  becoming  overly  preoccupied  with  trying  to  recreate reality.  Always  bear  in  mind  that  the  aim  is  to  create  an exciting  story,  not  to  mirror  real-world  physics.  Accurate distances and speeds are not tracked but are instead abstracted and described in terms that support the scene's narrative.

These rules assume the fleeing character has an escape route. If a character is trapped, they must escape before a chase can ensue. Likewise, if a character is already engaged in combat, they  may  need  to  make  an  opposed  Fighting  roll  to  escape combat before they can flee ( Response 4: Flee , page 130).

## The Chase Round

A chase can begin in a variety of ways. As a general rule, one person says they're making the first move and then another character  responds  (reacts),  and voila !  They've  initiated  a chase. If there's any disagreement about who acts first, the character with the highest DEX goes first; in the case of a draw, use the standard Tiebreakers rules (page 118).

On their turn in a chase round, each player may be asked to  make either a DEX or Athletics roll to determine their investigator's  progress  in  the  chase  ( You  Want  to  Run Away? , page 151).

Once the first player's turn has been resolved, that player rolls a die: if an even number is rolled, play passes to the player's left; if an odd number is rolled, play passes to the right, and continues in the chosen direction for the entire chase-just like in combat ( Who Goes Next? , page 125). The GM oversees the order of play and ensures everyone gets to act on their turn, in order, in the chase round. If you're playing online, refer to Playing Online (page 125). If you were using combat rounds prior to the start of the chase, simply continue with the same order of play that you were using during the combat.

Unlike combat, though, where the GM may have NPCs acting at the start and end of each combat round ( Handling NPCs , page 126), in a chase round, the GM gets only one turn in which all of their NPCs act. Also, the GM does not make chase rolls. They only need to roll dice if an NPC makes an attack; it's important
