![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000371_c5f8657cc10a93808986ba0dadfb07e6254856829cf610d9c8e88a757157b806.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000372_6c2c744ee3e45755352b203169a571f604c7bb0e91d4febb4eb28d070bdbb03f.png)

## How Big is Each Location?

The distance covered in one chase round is not defined and is intentionally elastic. Think of it like a chase scene in a movie, where the camera often cuts from one location to the next-our hero is in their hotel room, someone is banging on the door-cut-our hero is exiting the window  and  climbing  down  the  drainpipe to the street below-cut-the pursuer is looking out the same window-cutour  hero  is  running  down  the  streetcut-the  pursuer  is  exiting  the  hotel entrance and giving pursuit, and so on. One location can encompass the hotel room to the street below, while another location can be the street alongside an entire  city  block,  or  just  a  section  of busy road the investigators are crossing.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000373_027f9ffcbe4009a0b82fd3366c4724a2b8da7003867f48e82b0467106372c251.png)

Consider  each  location  as  presenting one type of challenge for those involved in  the  chase.  Thus,  each  location is  tied to a predominant style of movement: for example, getting from the hotel room to the street requires climbing down a drainpipe; the city block is an all-out run; and crossing the busy road requires the person to dodge through traffic, and so on.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000374_8643a77633079b297b6e96b4c2fca25b9d306576877eb192fa26f0eb3dc63f9d.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000375_12725ebc203cc8cf5154cb9b5bcbaa26a5b26abe664fcfbcc79cba7fc9732550.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000376_bf31c97b00a82c853acb34104d424f8dec637d7379841f1742881625de98b410.png)

for players to keep track of when the GM's turn occurs in the chase round so they know when an attack might be imminent.

The  following  rules  are  presented  in  two  parts. Part  1 presents the rules for when the investigators are fleeing from one or more NPCs. Part 2 turns that around and deals with when the investigators are the ones chasing the NPCs.

## Part 1: You Want to Run Away?

The players use the following four steps when fleeing (each is explained in the following sections):

- Step 1: Establish the Location: describe where your investigator is headed based on the GM's description of the location.
- Step 3: Track Successes: each player keeps a running tally of how many chase successes and failures they have accumulated so far.
- Step 2: Chase Roll: the investigator's actions determine whether they're using a burst of speed (DEX) or negotiating obstacles (Athletics) to avoid their pursuers.
- Step 4: Combat or Next Player's Turn: if the investigator decides not to make a ranged attack against their pursuer, play moves on to the next character's turn.

## Step 1: Establish the Location

The current chase location-where the chase starts or where it's  moved  on  to-should  be  described  by  the  GM.  The player  then  states  where  their  investigator  is  heading.  For example:  if  the  chase  starts  in  a  London  street,  the  player says whether they are running away along the road, running down a side street, or into a shop or somewhere else, such as a Tube station.

The player concerned may ask questions about where they are. The GM may have to think on their feet a little here, and make snap decisions about locations-for example, is there a back door to the shop, and is it locked? Is there a Tube train boarding as they enter the platform? In either of these possible situations, it is an opportunity for the GM to call for a Luck roll to determine if something 'useful' is in the player's favour.

- Luck roll is successful: that element of the location is skewed in the investigator's favour-so yes, there is an open door at the back of the shop to escape through, or yes, the doors of the Tube train are still open.
- Luck roll is failed: that element of the location is skewed against the investigator-there is no back door, or there is one, but it's locked; the Tube train has just pulled out or won't arrive for another three minutes.

151

One of the great things about playing a game set in the modern-day is that we have access to online maps displaying views of the streets where our chases may take place. These allow  you  and  your  players  to  see  photos  of  the  streets, vehicles, shops, houses, building sites, Tube stations, and so on,  and  to  navigate  your  way  through  them  together-see Use the London Setting (page 340) for further details.

Rather than simply running away, once a chase is under way, the investigator may take other actions, including:

- Stop moving, wait for their pursuer to catch up, and engage the pursuer in combat (including casting combative spells)-in which case, simply shift to combat rounds with that NPC.
- Perform some other action requiring time and perhaps a dice roll, such as barricading a door or casting a spell that isn't a ranged one.
- Stop moving, turn, and make a ranged attack of some kind ( Ranged Attacks Mid-Chase , page 156).

## Step 2: Chase Roll

The  next  step  in  the  chase  is  to  establish  whether  moving through the location counts as a burst of speed or negotiating obstacles .  There  may  be  a  brief  discussion  about  this,  but ultimately the GM has the final say.

## Burst of Speed

If  the  location  is  relatively  free  of  obstacles  and  hazardssuch as when sprinting down an open road-the challenge is  simply  one  of  speed.  Compare  the  MOV  speed  of  the investigator with that of their opposition:

- If the investigator's MOV is higher, they gain an automatic chase success. The player doesn't need to make a chase roll this round.
- If the investigator's MOV is equal to that of their opponent, the player must make a DEX roll.
- If the investigator's MOV is lower, they incur an automatic chase failure. The player doesn't get to make a chase roll this round.

If  the  player  has  to  make  a  chase  roll  using  DEX,  the GM should  refer  to  the  pursuing  NPC's  DEX  to  set  the difficulty level.

- If that NPC's DEX is 69 or below, the difficulty level of the DEX roll is Regular.
- If the NPC's DEX is 70 or above, the difficulty level of the DEX roll is Hard.

If the investigator succeeds with their DEX roll, they gain a chase success; if they don't succeed, they incur a chase failure.

3

## Negotiating Obstacles

If  the  location  features  an  obstacle  or  hazard-such  as  a  wall  to  climb over, a stream to swim across, or a gap to leap over-an Athletics roll is used to gauge the investigator's progress. If the player has to make a chase roll to negotiate hazards, the Keeper should refer to the chasing NPC's Athletics skill to set the difficulty level.

- If the NPC's Athletics skill is 69 or below, the difficulty level of the Athletics roll is Regular.
- If the NPC's Athletics skill is 70 or above, the difficulty level of the Athletics roll is Hard.

If the investigator succeeds with their Athletics roll, they gain a chase success; if they don't succeed, they incur a chase failure.

## Pushing the Roll

If the player fails the chase roll (either DEX or Athletics), they might decide to accept a chase failure this round. Alternatively, they could spend Luck points to turn the result into a success

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000377_2e8bb1ac87afc2bc86699347397a280bed638e2f6d10fcf6ec9edd4dec7a8be9.png)

( Spending Luck , page 113), or attempt to push the roll ( Pushing the Roll ,  page 108). Failing a pushed roll always means something bad happens and, in a chase, the default outcome includes losing the chase, thus being caught by their pursuer.

## Step 3: Track Successes

Each round, an investigator either gains a chase success or incurs a chase failure. The player should keep track of how many successes and failures their investigator accumulates for the  chase.  You  could  just  keep  a  tally  of  the  successes  and failures on some paper or use coloured beads or counters (two colours: one for successes and one for failures).

## Winning the Chase

If  a  player  gains  two  chase  successes  before  incurring  two chase failures, they win the chase, and have outpaced and lost their pursuer. The chase is over, and they got away.

## Handling Chases with Multiple NPCs

While each player runs one investigator, the GM may have several NPCs to manage in the scene. These NPCs all act on the GM's single turn  in  the  chase  round.  As  we've  already mentioned,  the  NPCs  move  forward  in  the chase  automatically  without  the  need  for dice  rolls.  Ideally,  treat  the  NPCs  as  one group  who  all  act  together.  But  what  if  the NPCs have different MOV ratings and/or different Athletics values?

- If the investigators are being pursued by a group of NPCs, then focus on the fastest NPC.
- If the fastest NPC catches an investigator, then it's up to you to use your discretion as to whether the other NPCs soon catch up-if you're not sure, it's another good opportunity to ask the player for a Luck roll!

Alternatively, if the investigators are pursuing a group of NPCs ( You Want to Catch Someone? , page 154), ask the players which specific NPC or NPCs they're prioritising.

- If they choose one specific target, the rest can be ignored, and everyone can focus on that one NPC; assume the others get away.
- On the other hand, if the investigators would be happy to catch any one of a group of NPCs, then just deal with the slowest NPC in the group. Again, the other NPCs can get away. Either way, you're then managing only one NPC.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000378_bd26945c9af115415c8ff9b50e47cb6be09fd871804d87b3c6793e49ba89aa0a.png)

If  the  investigators  decide  to  split  up  and each  follow  a  different  NPC,  then  you  have multiple chases to track. This may happen, but it's  likely  to  be  very  rare.  Depending on your confidence, you can either run out each of the chases  to  their  conclusion  in  turn  or  switch backwards and forwards between them. The former option is probably the best if you're all still learning the rules, but it does mean some players will have to wait a while for their turn (perhaps it's a good time for them to go and take  a  break).  The  latter  option  means  more excitement for everyone and ensures no one is sitting around for too long waiting for their investigator to do something.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000379_a92ba98c672d4add7c05dd3b0bd4436a722a9cdc4d9cba1c49b7f5946b98d2c9.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000380_8a96df97caee2cd44d068c5c9e9360ea8a39d894e5777dfcde86a9659cd60efe.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000381_22beee2ac2bbb7c069f0ebf78f363e3229fd487a50486a0f14aec8ee108771f9.png)

## Losing the Chase

If a player incurs two chase failures before gaining two chase successes,  their  investigator  loses  the  chase.  Their  pursuer catches up and can now, on the GM's next turn, try to grab or  attack  them.  However, if the NPC fails to incapacitate, trip,  or  restrain  them, the investigator can carry on fleeing on following rounds. But from now on (starting next round):

- Each time the investigator incurs a chase failure, the NPC stays in range and can attack them again on the GM's turn.
- If the investigator now gains a chase success on two consecutive rounds, they outpace and lose their pursuer. The chase is over, and they got away.
- Each time the investigator gains a chase success, they move ahead, out of melee range of their pursuer.

## Step 4: Combat or Next Player's Turn

Fleeing characters can potentially make a ranged attack; see Ranged Attacks Mid-Chase (page 156) for further details on how this works.

## The GM's Turn

Use  the  following  two  steps  for  the  GM  when  they  are controlling NPCs in pursuit of the investigators:

- GM's Turn Part 1: Move Forward : automatic.
- GM's Turn Part 2: Combat or Next Player's Turn .

## GM's Turn Part 1: Move Forward

On  the  GM's  turn,  the  GM  simply  describes  the  NPCs moving through the location however they wish. The GM does not roll dice for NPC movement in a chase. The NPCs move forward, and the players'  dice  rolls  decide  who  wins the chase. Be creative and use the players' results to describe the pursuers catching up (investigator chase failure), falling behind (investigator chase success), or stumbling over obstacles the investigators negotiated with ease (investigator chase success) or vice versa.

## GM's Turn Part 2: Combat or Next Player's Turn

If the investigator incurs two chase failures before they gain two chase successes, the pursuing NPC catches up to them. The GM can now have the NPC try to tackle them, hit them, trip them, or whatever else they wish to do.

If  the  GM  decides  that  the  NPC  is  going  to  attack  the investigator,  then  play  through  the  six  steps  of  combat ( Running  Combat  Encounters ,  page  127).  Remember, though,  that  the  fleeing  investigator  has  their  back  to their  attacker,  and  both  are  moving  at  speed.  The  fleeing investigator  can't  'fight  back'  without  stopping.  For  the purposes of combat within a chase, because both the attacker (the  NPC)  and  their  fleeing  target  (the  investigator)  are

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000382_cdf6250a02ad57acab27b63fd531a39cf1133cc403bf0df66d16cc88f88f907b.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000383_a2c699ed299685f796a01be60f3b14ba85806092e34d027ba56803878839bf4d.png)

3

moving at speed, the fleeing target is automatically treated as dodging ( Response 2: Dodge, page 128).

The chase ends here if the attack inflicts enough damage to incapacitate the investigator (marking them 'Down'-or worse; Wounds and Healing ,  page 144). Alternatively, the NPC may use a Combat Manoeuvre (page  134)  to  try  to grab the investigator or knock them prone.

Following  the  attack,  if  the  investigator  is  not  down, restrained,  or  prone,  they  can  continue  to  run  away  using the guidelines in the Losing the Chase section (page 153). Or, on their turn in the round, the investigator can, of course, decide to stop fleeing and engage their pursuer in combat-or whatever else they wish to do.

## Part 2: You Want to Catch Someone?

Investigators use the following four steps for chases when they are pursuing someone (each is explained in the following sections):

- Step 1: Establish the Location: describe what your investigator is doing based on the GM's description of the location.
- Step 3: Track Successes: the player keeps a running tally of how many chase successes and failures they have accumulated so far.
- Step 2: Chase Roll: the investigator's actions determine whether they're using a burst of speed (DEX) or negotiating obstacles (Athletics) to catch up with their target(s).
- Step 4: Combat or Next Player's Turn: if the investigator decides not to make a ranged attack, play moves on to the next character.

## Step 1: Establish the Location

The current location should be described by the GM, who also  says  where  the  NPC is heading, much the same as in Step 1 of You Want to Run Away? (page 151). NPCs face the same potential location obstacles as do the investigators, meaning  this  is  an  opportunity  for  the  GM  to  call  for  a 'group Luck roll' ( Luck Rolls ,  page 113) if more than one investigator is involved in the chase, or a normal Luck roll if it's just a single investigator.

- Group Luck roll is successful: that element of the location is skewed against the NPC-there is no back door, or there is but it's locked; the Tube train has just pulled out or won't arrive for another three minutes.

Once the chase is underway, an investigator may wish to take other actions besides pursuing their target, including:

- Making a ranged attack of some kind, including casting a spell ( Ranged Attacks Mid-Chase , page 156).
- Performing some other action requiring time and perhaps a dice roll (including casting spells that aren't ranged ones).

## Step 2: Chase Roll

Resolve  this  step  exactly  as  before-see Burst  of  Speed (page 151)  and Negotiating  Obstacles (page 152) for further details.

## Step 3: Track Successes

Each round, an investigator either gains a chase success or incurs a chase failure. Each player should keep track of how many  successes  and  failures  their  investigator  accumulates for the chase.

## Losing the Chase

If  an  investigator  incurs  two  chase  failures  before  gaining two chase successes, they lose the chase and are left out of breath, while the NPC runs off out of sight.

## Catching Up with the Quarry

If an investigator gains two chase successes before incurring two chase failures, they catch up with the NPC and can now try  to  grab  or  hit  them  ( Combat or  Next  Player's  Turn , following). However, if the investigator fails to incapacitate, trip, or restrain them, the NPC can carry on fleeing in the following rounds. If combat doesn't ensue and the target isn't restrained-i.e., the chase continues-from now on (starting next round):

- Each time the investigator incurs a chase failure, the NPC moves out of range.
- If the investigator incurs a chase failure on two consecutive rounds, the investigator loses their quarry and is left out of breath, while the NPC runs off out of sight.
- Each time the investigator gains a chase success, they catch up and can make another attack on the NPC on their next turn.

## Step 4: Combat or Next Player's Turn

- Group Luck roll is failed: that element of the location is skewed in the NPC's favour-so there is an open door at the back of the shop to escape through, or yes, the doors of the Tube train are still open.

As  mentioned  in  the  previous  section,  if  the  investigator gains two chase successes before incurring two chase failures, they catch up with the fleeing NPC and can now try to tackle them, hit them, trip them up, or whatever else they wish to do.

Depending on what the investigator does, it may be time to switch from the chase rules to play through the six steps of  combat ( Running Combat Encounters ,  page  127).  The

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000384_c63a3f613cb1524fecfa74679f13de6c7c199953964fb0618fc7e7361d0f0db4.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000385_e75a240df496001d19f324d1ad123d5310c478071983ec84408cb2beb15628e8.png)

## Chase Example

Nafeesa is fleeing from Greg on a London street.

Nafeesa: MOV 8, DEX 40, Athletics 30/15, Fighting 60/30. Greg: MOV 9, DEX 50, Athletics 50/25, Fighting 30/15.

## Chase Round 1

## Nafeesa's turn:

Step  1:  Establish  the  Location: Nafeesa  is  running  down  the street at a sprint.

Step 2: Chase Roll: clearly, this is a Burst of Speed (page 151), but Nafeesa's MOV is lower than Greg's, so she automatically gets a chase failure this round. Greg is closing in!

Step 3: Track Successes: Nafeesa has 1 chase failure.

Step 4: Next Player's Turn.

## Greg's turn (GM):

GM's Turn Part 1: Move Forward: Greg automatically moves forward; no need for any rolls.

GM's Turn Part 2: Combat or Next Player's Turn: Greg's not caught up with Nafeesa yet and doesn't have any ranged weapons, so the chase continues. It's now Nafeesa's turn again.

## Chase Round 2

## Nafeesa's turn:

The GM says there's busy traffic on the street.

Step 1: Establish the Location: Nafeesa opts for dodging her way through the traffic.

Step 2: Chase Roll: the GM agrees that this counts as Negotiating Obstacles (page  152).  Greg's  Athletics  skill  is  below  70,  so  the difficulty  level  for  Nafeesa  is  Regular.  The  player  gets  24  on Nafeesa's Athletics roll: a chase success.

Step 3: Track Successes: Nafeesa  now  has  1  chase  failure  and  1 chase success.

Step 4: Next Player's Turn.

## Greg's turn (GM):

GM's  Turn  Part  1:  Move  Forward: Nafeesa  has  made  her way through the traffic and gets ahead, but Greg is still coming, weaving through the traffic behind her.

GM's Turn Part 2: Combat or Next Player's Turn: Greg's still not caught up with Nafeesa and doesn't have any ranged weapons, so the chase continues and it's back to Nafeesa.

## Chase Round 3

## Nafeesa's turn:

Her player asks what she can see. The GM replies that there's lots of people and cars driving down the street, and that there's a Tube station nearby.

Step 1: Establish the Location: the  player  asks  if  there's  a  bus that Nafeesa might leap onto. The GM asks for a Luck roll, which the player fails. There's no option but to keep running, so Nafeesa heads into the Tube station.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000386_84f2814f79f7c3a9611c102feef7a2ca56fb6a3ebc3422fb1e565e846022c35f.png)

Step  2:  Chase  Roll: this is  another  burst  of  speed,  which means  another  automatic  chase  failure  (as  Greg's  MOV  is  9  to Nafeesa's MOV 8).

Step 3: Track Successes: Nafeesa now has 2 chase failures and 1 chase success. Those 2 chase failures mean that she's lost the chase and Greg has caught up with her.

Step 4: Next Player's Turn.

## Greg's turn (GM):

GM's  Turn  Part  1:  Move  Forward: Greg  catches  up  with Nafeesa as she gets to the escalator leading into the Tube station.

GM's Turn Part 2: Combat: Greg makes an attack:

Combat Step 1: Set Your Goal: Greg tries to grab hold of Nafeesa as she's running.

Combat Step 2: Target Responds: Nafeesa just keeps running; under the circumstances, this counts as dodging ( GM's Turn Part 2 , page 153).

Combat Step 3: Dice Rolls: both sides roll percentile dice for an opposed Fighting roll. The GM rolls 83 for Greg: a Weak success ( Opposed  Combat  Rolls ,  page  131).  The  player  rolls  38  for Nafeesa: a Regular success. Nafeesa wins the roll because she has the higher (better) level of success.

Combat Step 4: Damage Roll: no damage is inflicted.

Combat  Step  5:  Narration: the  player  describes  how  Nafeesa screams ' Stand on the right! ' as she charges down the escalator. Greg failed to stop Nafeesa, so the chase continues.

## Chase Round 4

## Nafeesa's turn:

Step 1: Establish the Location: the player asks if there's a Tube train on the platform. The GM asks for another Luck roll, which the  player  passes  this  time.  The  player  asks  if  Nafeesa  can  get aboard this round and the GM agrees.

Step 2: Chase Roll: this is another burst of speed, which means another automatic chase failure.

Step 3: Track Successes: Nafeesa now just tracks her chase successes and chase failures round by round. This round was a chase failure, so Greg has kept up with her.

Step 4: Next Player's Turn.

## Greg's turn (GM):

GM's Turn Part 1: Move Forward: Greg gets on board the Tube train just as the doors close. The GM and player agree the chase is over, with them shut in a train carriage to who knows where…

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000387_4e6a42f58263ddf313c519a6dd3cdec7d440efdc6d443e721b076c524b5c878b.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000388_246d2f8d7c8b10c90b94d4b2d811db62930f01589092a882c938e657a0e687c0.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000389_f0658c15c3d9bf232108100506ae4800c566d06781de147396ab38779dc2dff5.png)

fleeing  NPC  can't  'fight  back'  without  stopping.  For  the purposes of combat within a chase, because both the attacker (the  investigator)  and  their  fleeing  target  (the  NPC)  are moving at speed, the fleeing target is automatically treated as dodging ( Response 2: Dodge, page 128).

The  chase  ends  here  if  the  attack  inflicts  enough  harm to incapacitate the  NPC  (marking  them  'Down'-or worse; Wounds and Healing ,  page 144). Alternatively, the investigator may use a Combat Manoeuvre (page 134) to try and grab the NPC or knock them prone.

Following the attack, if the NPC is not restrained, prone, or down, they can continue to run away using the guidelines in  the Catching Up with the Quarry section  (page  154). Or, on their turn in the round, the NPC can decide to stop fleeing and engage the investigator in combat-or whatever else they wish to do.

## The GM's Turn

The  GM  uses  the  following  two  steps  when  the  NPC  is fleeing from the investigators:

- GM's Turn Part 1: Move Forward : automatic.

## GM's Turn Part 1: Move Forward

On  the  GM's  turn,  the  GM  simply  describes  the  NPCs moving through the location however they wish. The GM does not roll dice for NPC movement in a chase. The NPCs move forward, and the players'  dice  rolls  decide  who  wins the chase. As when the investigators are being pursued, the GM should use the dice results to help narrate how the chase is going, working in elements of the surroundings to give it lots of flavour.

## GM's Turn Part 2: Combat or Next Player's Turn

Fleeing  NPCs  can  potentially  make  a  ranged  attack;  see Ranged Attacks Mid-Chase (following) for further details on how this works.

## Ranged Attacks Mid-Chase

Each  character,  following  their  chase  roll  (if  they  have  to make one), can make a firearms or spell attack on their turn in the chase round.

- If the character moved forward in the chase this round, their Firearms or Magic roll incurs a penalty die because they are attacking on the move ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111).
- GM's Turn Part 2: Combat or Next Player's Turn .

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000390_e9c9db884f680150634cb2fcd534d13a3bc07acca60be90af4b568db5c3a6233.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000391_21cc5ddc611710a5e3774ae272afa89d2fc5ffa05ee3f69f3266a1770574dba6.png)

- If an investigator stops moving to make a ranged attack, they can do so without incurring the penalty die, but their progress in the chase for that round is treated as an automatic chase failure.
- If an NPC stops moving to make a ranged attack, they can do so without incurring the penalty die. If this is the only NPC in the chase, immediately following the GM's turn, all players gain one additional chase success. If there are multiple NPCs and some stop and some continue to move, refer to Handling Chases with Multiple NPCs (page 152).

## Vehicle Chases

The rules for vehicle chases mirror those for chases on foot. If the players and NPCs find themselves in a car chase, for example, use the same structure and steps already outlined in the previous sections, with a few minor tweaks.

## Burst of Speed (Vehicles)

Whenever  the  driver  can  put  their  pedal  to  the  metal  on sections  of  open  road,  treat  it  as  a  burst  of  speed.  Use  the same rules as for Burst of Speed (page 151), but substitute the Drive skill for DEX.

## Negotiating Obstacles (Vehicles)

Whenever the location features an obstacle or hazard-such as  sharp  turns  in  narrow  London  back  streets,  slaloming through  rush-hour  traffic,  or  debris  on  the  road-use  the same  rules  as  for Negotiating  Obstacles (page  152)  but substitute the Drive skill for the Athletics skill.

## Vehicular Combat

Drivers  may  use  their  vehicles  to  'combat'  other  vehicles, mirroring the combat rules but substituting the Drive skill for the  Fighting  skill.  These  rules  for  vehicular  combat  assume that the vehicles involved are travelling at speed. The default goal in vehicular combat is to collide with the other vehicle and put it out of action, either through direct damage or by causing their opponent to lose control of their vehicle, but most likely a combination of both. Think The Fey and the Furious . Vehicular combat uses the same six steps as regular combat:

- Step 1: Set Your Goal: describe what your character is doing.
- Step 3: Dice Rolls: roll percentile dice.

## Firearms vs Vehicles

All  the  best  action  movies  have  their goodies  and  baddies  shooting  at  each others' vehicles with a wide variety of firearms.  However,  vehicle  damage  is on a different scale to character damage. To take out a vehicle, a character with a firearm could either target the driver or the tyres.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000392_e2a0b2b799f6ed26f55ef654c6b13ef5942d341c0e624052af638411f552e367.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000393_211198fa6edc85025c7c2c26e555874d11dfd357f45a699d2d8ea61d3b7af592.png)

Tyres are small targets and so incur one  penalty  die  to  the  Firearms  roll. Plus,  a  penalty  die  may  be  imposed if  the  vehicle  is  moving  at  high  speed ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111; Target  Size ,  page  139; Fast-Moving  Targets ,  page  138; Multiple Bonus or Penalty  Dice ,  page  143).  Damage  rolls  are made as for regular combat; 1 damage to a tyre is enough to puncture it. Drive rolls  for  a  vehicle  with  one  or  more burst tyres are made with a penalty die.

The  same  conditions  apply  if  the attacker is trying to shoot the driverordinarily, a person wouldn't count as a small target, but they most definitely are if you consider how little of them can be seen over a steering wheel.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000394_4bfb4d95e86cc4ca731a29a120b0b1027c950f328e995c97881fdece59bec5d2.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000395_4b43878beafc9b2f69d32f3dcac05ff30e819601f05fbd4274da498f09e42a41.png)

Steps 1 and 2 remain much the same as in the Running Combat  Encounters section  (page  127).  In Step  3:  Dice Rolls ,  the  two  drivers  make  an  opposed  combat roll using their Drive skills. Determine the winner by the usual method ( Opposed Combat Rolls , page 131).

Next comes Step 4: Damage , in which you figure out any damage or other effects. There is no need to make a damage roll;  use  the  following  conditions  to  work  out  how  much damage each vehicle suffers (if any):

- If the vehicle's Build is greater than double the other's, the one with the higher Build is dealt no damage, while the one with the lower Build suffers 3 damage.
- Step 2: Target Responds: the target describes how they intend to respond.
- Step 4: Damage: fi gure out any damage or other effects.
- Step 6: Next Player's Turn.
- Step 5: Narration: the winner describes what happens.
- If the vehicle's Build is greater than the other's-up to double its Build-the one with the higher Build suffers 1 damage, and the one with the lower Build suffers 2 damage.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000396_421c8055e1ec8ac3a4e453ed1df444908d1681b306e66bfee6ae86ceeed92d57.png)

3

- If the vehicles have equal Build values, they both suffer 2 damage.

Damage dealt to a vehicle is recorded in three levels-the same as damage done to a person, just with different names. Also  like  the  damage  done  to  people,  vehicular  damage is cumulative.

- When a vehicle takes 1 damage, it is Pranged (still drivable).
- If a vehicle takes 3 damage, it is Wrecked (no longer drivable).
- If a vehicle takes 2 damage, it is Damaged (still drivable-just!).

## Wrecked Vehicles

A vehicle is wrecked once it's received a total of 3 damage. Wrecked vehicles are out of the chase, and when it comes to Step 5: Narration (page 133), the GM, with suggestions from  the  players,  should  describe  the  event  in  a  way  that seems appropriate to the location. For example: it may be that the vehicle has rolled spectacularly into a ditch, burst through a shop window in a shower of glass, or been forced to execute a soggy dive into a river, and so on. The driver and passengers may be injured, and the GM should refer to Control Roll (following) to see how badly they've been harmed.

## Control Roll

Whenever a vehicle is damaged in a collision, or the driver suffers damage, the driver must make a Drive roll to maintain control of the vehicle.

- If a vehicle suffers 2 damage in one collision, the Drive roll incurs a penalty die ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111).

If the Drive roll to keep control is failed, the vehicle crashes and the driver and any passengers suffer damage. The degree of  damage  is  primarily  determined  by  the  vehicle's  speed (which is judged by the GM according to the situation):

- At low speed (such as when chasing through dense urban traffic, under 40 kph/25 mph): 2 damage to vehicle and passengers.
- At high speed (such as when chasing on the motorway (freeway), over 120 kph/75 mph): 4 damage to vehicle and passengers.
- At medium speed (such as when chasing through empty streets, up to 120 kph/75 mph): 3 damage to vehicle and passengers.

## Narrating the Outcome of Vehicular Combat

After  the  vehicular  combat  rolls  and  damage  have  been taken care of, next comes Step 5: Narration (page 133), in which the outcome is described by the winner of the opposed Drive roll, unless the vehicle has been wrecked in the process ( Wrecked Vehicles ,  nearby).  This  is  followed  with Step 6: Next Player's Turn (page 133).

## Repairs

After a vehicular combat, if enough time and money is spent on the vehicle, pretty much any damage can be fixed, though wrecks may be beyond repair. The cost and time needed for such repairs should be determined narratively between the GM and players-that is to say, the rules do not attempt to address this level of detail. The GM can, at their discretion, ask for a Luck roll to see whether the vehicle can be repaired or not. A success indicates that it can be fixed and put back on the road, while a failure means the vehicle is a total write-off. Remember, you cannot spend Luck points on a Luck roll, nor can you push Luck rolls.

- Bicycles and motorcycles involved in a collision always incur a penalty die on any Drive roll made to maintain control.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000397_3d570eb0d27a60f4d83b7a57d3bc6bd542a00684f38ee7be75e8e223c71ca678.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000398_e65004043d5e67296af96e9932567673a8554fe97ef24a1742ac389c2442c74e.png)

## Table 8: Vehicles

| Vehicle            |   MOV |   Build | Passengers   |
|--------------------|-------|---------|--------------|
| Bicycle            |    10 |       1 | 1            |
| Horse (with rider) |    11 |       5 | 1            |
| Motorcycle, Light  |    13 |       2 | 1            |
| Motorcycle, Heavy  |    16 |       4 | 1            |
| Car, Economy       |    13 |       5 | 4            |
| Car, Standard      |    14 |       6 | 5            |
| Car, Deluxe or SUV |    15 |       7 | 5            |
| Sports Car         |    16 |       6 | 2            |
| Pickup Truck       |    14 |       7 | 2+           |
| 5.5-tonne Truck    |    13 |       8 | 2+           |
| 18-Wheeler         |    13 |      10 | 2+           |

## Notes

MOV:

rating of the vehicle's speed and manoeuvrability in chases.

Build:

rating of the strength and size of the vehicle.

Passengers: the number of people that can be accommodated.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000399_16551b8aebe683f986ca34ed97caa121e3514c2e51055e6d2865f249d7c5672b.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000400_3443b85be93a63c967491e09d2eba9fb7cd050729db8ac235a8bcae9f7009cb8.png)

AV

U

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000401_1dbfe58700e3529b60a42f90c0e5cde06e62b5ff29f5e0eabb022ea6895f9e62.png)

MAGIC IS FUNDAMENTAL in the world of Rivers of London -without it, there would be no Folly, no Nightingale, and no Peter Grant. At least, not in the way that we have come to know them over the course of the novels. As you might expect, magic is also important in Rivers of London: the Roleplaying Game , so this chapter discusses the nature of spells, how spells are learned, how they are cast, and what they actually do. But first, we're going to look at two other important side effects of magic: vestigia and signare .

## Vestigia

Magic leaves an imprint on the physical environment-what Newtonian practitioners call ' vestigia .'  Everyone can sense this imprint without necessarily being aware of the fact; thus, Sense  Vestigia  is  a  common  skill  with  a  starting  value  of 30%. Some people, though, are much more attuned to the vestigia around them. Investigators who take the Magic skill during  character  creation  automatically  boost  their  Sense Vestigia  skill  to  60%,  as  do  any  fae  investigators  ( Sense Vestigia , page 85).

It's  also  possible  for  people  to  hone  their  senses  when it comes  to detecting and understanding vestigia .  An investigator can improve their ability to perceive vestigia with a few hours of training, even though they lack the Magic skill. The Folly has a formal three-day programme for this, called Vestigia  Sensitivity  Training.  An  investigator  without  the Magic skill may wish to take this course during downtime between case files, as doing so grants a development point to spend on their Sense Vestigia skill ( Training , page 122). Alternatively, they can spend general Development Points (page  120)  to  increase  the  skill  during  an Investigator Development Phase (page 120).

## Vestigia Strength

It seems that vestigia can manifest in a huge variety of ways and tends to be registered by humans as a sensation of some sort-a sound, a smell, a physical sensation, or a combination of  all  three. However, the amount of vestigia left  hanging around can vary widely, which affects how easy they are to detect, and any vestigia the investigators encounter are likely to be one of three strengths: weak, strong, or extremely strong.

## Weak Vestigia

Generally, weak vestigia have been around for a very long time and  includes  the  underlying  levels  of vestigia that  surround us  in  our  general  environment-sort  of  like  background radiation, just for magic-it's always there, but you only find it  if  you  deliberately  look  for  it.  Also,  some  materials  don't retain vestigia very well, so even recent magic use on or around such materials may not leave a strong imprint ( Vestigia in the Environment , page 163).

## Strong Vestigia

Generally, the kind of vestigia left behind by recent magic use or other supernatural occurrences. Vestigia are also generated by human activity, especially that involving strong emotions, such as festivals and football matches, but even some low-key events like a village fete can leave traces. Such vestigia can build up over time and become strong if the emotions creating it become trapped within certain materials that are good at retaining vestigia ( Vestigia in the Environment , page 163).

## Extremely Strong Vestigia

As well as being able to sense vestigia , some creatures and beings,  such  as  revenants,  ghosts,  and  vampires,  feed  on vestigia ( Werelight , page 182, and Vampires , page 255).

Really  strong  negative  emotions  can  leave  a  stain  on  the environment.  Places  such  as  massacre  sites  can  be  saturated with powerful and unpleasant extremely strong vestigia , which can develop into a hostile force (known as either a despair or a  malignancy,  depending on its behaviour) 1 .  Even  those  who haven't honed their senses can be well aware that something bad happened in places  like  that.  Very  powerful  magic  used  against  (or

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000402_13bc7e14b2bc433f38f36bd422d61fdce0836cba8da08efbb4d642541c11224c.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000403_e88f12ee95e1d684b62955f9b0852d72eadbe2e15f454be4bfb332fe7ba92a41.png)

4

in the presence of) certain materials also generates an extremely strong vestigium ( Vestigia in the Environment , page 163).

## Detecting Vestigia

When in the presence of vestigia , the GM may ask a player for a Sense Vestigia roll. The GM decides whether the vestigia are weak or strong. In some cases, the vestigia may be extremely strong, so the GM may decide to forgo the skill roll altogether and just tell the investigators what they can sense-'obvious' clues like this are indicated in pre-written case files (scenarios).

## For extremely strong vestigia :

- Usually, no skill roll is necessary to sense extremely strong vestigia .

## For strong vestigia :

- A Regular success permits someone to detect all a particular vestigium's elements.

| Vestigia                         | Possible Clue                                                                                                                                                                     |
|----------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Cigarette smoke                  | The location was once somewhere where smoking regularly took place, like a pub or club.                                                                                           |
| Expensive cologne/perfume        | The perpetrator's father or mother always wore this scent, and they associate it with a strong emotion, which is why it's hanging around at the scene of the crime.               |
| The Beatles singing 'In My Life' | Asong the victim associates with the perpetrator, or the perpetrator associates with themselves, which is why it's left traces either on the victim or at the scene of the crime. |
| A surge of passion               | The perpetrator or victim associates the location with sexual activity, even if the crime itself wasn't sexual in nature.                                                         |
| A vivid flash of sunlight        | The crime took place either early or late in the day, whenthe sun was low in the sky.                                                                                             |
| The smell of new leather seats   | The victim was in a new car-either with their attacker or on their way to meet them-or the perpetrator has connections to the motor trade.                                        |
| The smell and feel of coal dust  | Theperpetratororvictimassociatesthelocationwithcoaldust,perhapsacellaroroutbuilding.                                                                                              |
| The stink of formaldehyde        | The perpetrator or victim works in a mortuary or some other industry that uses formaldehyde, such as plastics production.                                                         |
| Thescent of hyacinths            | The perpetrator or victim is a gardener or allotment holder-or it could even be as simple as Hyacinth being the perpetrator's or victim's name.                                   |
| The stench of vomit              | The perpetrator was revolted by the crime, so much so that it made them feel sick inside, a feeling that repeats every time they think of it.                                     |

Table 9: Example Clues from Vestigia

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000404_9749537e786edad6c8c33df1cd88ecd716bc750bfbb8d25e7f9a6bf0f07ec8c2.png)

## For weak vestigia :

- A Regular success permits someone to detect the main element of that vestigium ; its core element.
- A Hard success permits someone to detect all of that particular vestigium 's elements.

## Using Vestigia in Case Files

The vestigia sensed  in  a  particular  location  or  attached to  a  particular  object  or  person  might  only  provide  some background colour and depth, but it could equally supply a clue of some kind-either to a person or a place's history, or to the nature of a crime or who committed it. For example, if a series of crimes were being committed by the same genius loci ,  then  their vestigia would  leave  a  trace  at  each  of  the scenes of infraction, and this would be detectable when the investigators finally meet up with the perpetrator. The same goes for a practitioner's Signare (page 164), which is detected in the same way as vestigia .

## GM ADVICE

Don't  forget,  members  of  the  Folly  are  trained to  carry  out  an  Initial  Vestigium  Assessment  (IVA) the  moment  they  arrive  at  the  scene  of  a  crime to  determine  if  there's  any  magical  involvement ( Types of Evidence , page 204).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000405_e86c66ed6bc5f17e367da677e3854bbaf2e916c19a8a579a63d753ae8c7af56f.png)

Sometimes,  the  sensations  derived  from vestigia only make sense in hindsight-they are often subtle clues, quite tangential or obscure, that give an inkling of the truth rather than concrete evidence you could produce in court. It could be just enough to let the investigators know that something is  'off'  about a crime scene, or it might point them in the right  direction  to  look  for  physical  clues  or  further  leads. For example, the song Peter Grant hears when investigating the death of Cyrus Wilkinson 2 leads him to the dead man's connection to jazz, and so on.

- If the investigators are struggling to figure out what a particular vestigium means, i.e., how it relates to the case, permit them an INT roll. If they are successful, they understand what the vestigium is trying to tell them.
- If the clue is essential to moving the plot forward (an 'obvious' clue), let the investigators have it with no need for a roll. Remember, keep dice rolls for dramatic or tense moments where success or failure adds interest to the plot.

## Vestigia in the Environment

Some  materials  hold vestigia  longer than others.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000406_5a300c7566567f1d833fcc5bb6d3fcf62361288b6e709ffc16cf2c8324bbec74.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000407_784121888e0be38bc24b4dc4ede7c55faebbec18081ea0ad81f2895013f4078b.png)

- Stone: soaks up everything around it, meaning some old houses are positively dripping with vestigia.
- Concrete: almost as good at storing vestigia as stone.
- Metals: not as good at storing vestigia as stone.
- Wood: not as good at storing vestigia as metals. Green wood dampens magic and can act as a magical insulator.
- Plastics: some types of plastic, e.g., PVC, retain vestigia very well, some better than metals.
- Corpses: the human body is terrible at retaining vestigia. Magic strong enough to kill a person results in some vestigia, but even then, it might only last a few days.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000408_8ddef25178f5a69d7cce8b975e37c13d8c03f62915118142a815adfe5ae43044.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000409_0e7a46bd844cf852e6bd4b90e2339ef60ae46d8159648433b17d706a2a4dc68b.png)

2 Moon Over Soho

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000411_ff28ba4fc802801570b1a097b847b0fb6d31bc74a5e2e00cfd2c5dc941d315ab.png)

## Signare

The  man popped the fireball  like  a  soap  bubble,  and  rubbed  his thumb and finger  under  his  nose.  'Who  trained  you?'  he  asked. 'Not Jeffers, that's for certain. Not that he was without skill, but you've got spirit. Was it Gripper? He's just the kind to bleat about what he's doing. Have you noticed that about journalists? All they really want to talk about is themselves.'

-Moon Over Soho

Every practitioner has their own unique signarea magical signature that can be perceived by other practitioners and  members  of  the  demi-monde  in  much  the  same  way that  one  detects vestigia .  Just  as  an  expert  can  determine whether  a  painting  is  a  genuine  'old  master'  by  studying the brushstrokes and technique, so can a practitioner detect subtleties  in  the  casting  of  a  spell  that  distinguishes  who performed it. Each practitioner has their own unique signare , but  an  element  of  a  master's signare is  passed  on  to  any apprentices they may teach.

In the novels, Peter Grant senses Nightingale's signare as being like the sound of cymbals, as heavy as a mallet, and as sharp and controlled as the point of a needle; there is also a tick-tock precision and the smell of willow. This clockwork precision is passed on to Lesley May, whose signare combines the tick-tock element with the razor strop and a seagull's cry.

## Detecting Signare

As signare and vestigia are  related, signare are  detected in  exactly  the  same  way -using  the  Sense  Vestigia  skill ( Vestigia , page 161).

## GM ADVICE

Fae investigators ( Playing Fae Investigators , page 318) don't  generally  have  signare-anyone  successfully using  Sense  Vestigia  on  a  fae  investigator  simply becomes aware of something strange and otherworldly about them (the same as they would for any other fae character).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000412_191d91d4c168df740f3258d7d9ff424ff9cdd460d35cd21a33d40bf97038d6f2.png)

## Creating an Investigator's Signare

An  investigator  choosing  the  Magical  advantage  during character  creation  and  who  subsequently  takes  the  Magic skill as one of their six starting skills needs to create their own signareso  other  practitioners  and  members  of  the demi-monde can recognise them for who and what they are.

To create a signare for your investigator, either choose one sound, one smell, and one other sensation from Table 10: Signare or,  if  you  can't  (or  would  rather  not)  decide,  roll 1D100 on each of the three columns instead. Choose to keep any two of them and disregard the third. If you don't like what you've rolled, roll again (or choose this time). Feel free to adjust the descriptions as you wish-the listed entries are there for inspiration. Alternatively, create your own signare using these as a basis for your own ideas. If your investigator is  being  trained  by  a  character  with  a  known signare ,  you should  incorporate  one  aspect  of  the  teacher's signare into your own as a third element.

For example: Nafeesa's player isn't sure which signare to choose, so  they  decide  to  roll  randomly.  They  roll  1D100  for  a  sound, getting 01-the song of a blackbird. This is followed by a 77 for smells-menthol rub. Finally, a 07 for other sensations-the taste of fresh apple. Nafeesa's player decides to keep the sound and the taste  of  apple  because  they  suggest  a  garden  theme,  which  quite appeals to the player. If Nafeesa is being trained by Nightingale, it would make sense for her to have his tick-tock element as well. So, Nafeesa's final signare is: the song of a blackbird, the taste of fresh apple, and a tick-tock sound.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000413_c71f58b2e9b944447ec9a374712f34c89988ace4b2078dba6302acc08b742388.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000414_937c7f1c3768b7de30ee41ad0bce908cb5b73c16f519f702edb4b29df90bf667.png)

## Newtonian Spells

' A  spell  is  a  series  of forma strung  together  to  achieve  an  effect, while a ritual is what it sounds like: a sequence of forma arranged as  a  ritual  with  certain  paraphernalia  to  help  move  the  process along,' said Nightingale. 'They tend to be older spells from the early part of the eighteenth century.'

-Rivers of London

Spells  from  the  school  of  Newtonian  magic  are  cast  by forming  shapes  in  the  mind,  to  which  a  Latin  word  is attached, known as a forma . Different formae have different effects;  for  example, lux is  the forma for  light-as  a  single forma , it can be used to cast the spell that many wizards learn first: Werelight (page 182). Spells that use a single forma are referred to as 'first order' spells and are relatively simple to cast. Higher-order spells use multiple formae strung together to create more complex spells. Roughly speaking, the number of formae used to create the spell determines the order of the spell. A selection of spells, based on those found in the novels, is presented later in this chapter ( Spells , page 178).

## BEN SAYS

Newtonian magic in the world of Rivers of London is complex and involved. A system that reflected all its nuance and idiosyncrasies would be longer than this book and probably not that much fun. By necessity, the system presented here abstracts much of that complexity to smooth the playing experience.

········

## Investigators and Spells

To be able to cast spells, an investigator must first choose the Magical advantage during character creation ( Advantages , page 51). Once done, they can choose Magic as one of their six starting skills ( Investigators with Magic , page 54). Starting investigators with the Magic skill begin with three spells:

- Two 'first order' spells (one mastered, one unmastered).

## GM ADVICE

Why so few spells? Well, the investigators in a standard Rivers  of  London  game  have  limited  experience,  so they  haven't  really  had  the  opportunity  to  learn much  in  the  way  of  magic  just  yet.  Remember,  in Rivers  of  London,  Peter  Grant  spends  almost  the entire  first  half  of  the  book  only  knowing  how  to cast a werelight, and he doesn't learn how to cast a fireball until Moon Over Soho.

## ADDITIONAL RULES

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000420_8647f534421d9bb923b17b035f2aabd92d97d318f2b0f1725485d438ab71040e.png)

If you want your investigators to begin the game with more spells, allow your players to make use of the optional Spellcaster Experience  Package during character  creation-see Investigator  Experience Packages , page 309, for more details.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000421_02a97e539d5b650c7196e35819a309dc1569586cb16f0a7a1c43f86b764eab94.png)

## Newtonian Apprentices

The safest and most efficient way to learn Newtonian magic is to be taught by an experienced practitioner.

- All investigators beginning the game as a Newtonian practitioner's apprentice (like Peter Grant is to Nightingale) start with Werelight (page 182) as their mastered spell, after which the choice of spells is up to the player.
- A common second spell to learn is Impello (page 178). These are both first order spells.

In  a  UK-based  game,  the  only  way  for  an  investigator to  become  a  full  Newtonian  apprentice  (like  Peter  Grant) is  to  be  inducted  into  the Society of the Wise (page  260) before  they  learn  any  spells.  DCI  Thomas  Nightingale is  their  Master,  assisted  by  Varvara  Sidorovna  Tamonina (see  their  write  ups  in A Rogues' Gallery ,  pages  233  and 240 respectively). The GM should encourage the player to explain how their investigator came to the Folly's attention and was thereby taken on as an apprentice during Step Five of character creation ( Create a Backstory , page 56).

- One 'second order' spell (unmastered).

Some spells, particularly those from higher orders, have prerequisites  for  learning  them  ( Spell  Prerequisites ,  page 178). An investigator must always have the correct prerequisite spell(s)  for  any  spell  they  wish  to  acquire  during  character creation  or  learn  during  an Investigator  Development Phase (page  120).  See  the  individual  descriptions  in  the Spells section (page 178) for further details.

If  an  investigator  already  knows  how  to  cast  spells before  they  join  the  Folly,  they  are  classed  as  a Hedge Wizard (following), though Nightingale and Varvara will  undoubtedly  be  responsible  for  any  further  training they receive.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000422_ab0fd2319f6f1ab28a0884a625a9d2934c84c1b3efbecb84a864d2cb38827a98.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000423_510989aa83e358ef9811e6e2e90a8da7d73cb94fa24a191546c94e41cd636632.png)

## The Practitioner's Oath

Investigators who are also practitioners are  expected  to  swear  an  oath  upon joining  the  Folly  ( By  My  Troth ,  page 54),  whether  they  are  police  officers or civilians. Police officer practitioners do  so  in  front  of  the  Commissioner and DCI Nightingale, promising to keep secret what they're taught,  among other things (for the oath's exact wording, see Game Moderator Advice , page 217).  The  civilian  oath  is  nearer  to  the one taken by Patrick Gale and his associates  ( The  City  of  London ,  page  275), and is  likewise  accompanied by a suitably binding non-disclosure agreement, separate  to  the  one  required  for  the Folly consultant's card.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000424_061a43d4861cdb16ca8769be5dee51711b56e850f9b2624a2a72323da4a594ab.png)

## Hedge Wizards

The term 'hedge wizard'-or, more rarely, 'hedge witch'-is an umbrella term used for the many alternatives to being a formally-trained Newtonian practitioner. Some investigators may  have  spontaneously  discovered  their ability, while others may have been taught by a 'rogue' practitioner-or a practitioner from another school of magic entirely.

- The choice of spells for a hedge wizard is entirely open, but again, they should start play with two first order spells (one of which is mastered) and one second order spell (unmastered).

## Mastering a Spell

As  we  learn  in  the  novels,  casting  a  spell  carries  inherent risks-including Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation (HTD, known in some US circles as Rebenstock's Syndrome; page 177)-but, through practise, individual spells can be mastered. A mastered spell is safer and more reliable to use than an unmastered one.

- Apprentice practitioners schooled by a master of Newtonian magic begin play with the spell Werelight already mastered.
- Once a spell is mastered, the player should check the Mastered box next to the spell's name on the character sheet.
- Hedge wizards begin play with one mastered first order spell of their choosing.
- A mastered spell gains a bonus die to the Magic skill roll made to cast it.

Example: Nafeesa begins play with Werelight already mastered; her player marks the check box next to the spell's name on Nafeesa's character sheet. Morgan, as a hedge wizard, decides which spell they  have  mastered.  Morgan's  player  chooses  Flash  Bulb  as Morgan's mastered spell. Like Nafeesa's player, Morgan's player now marks the Mastered check box on their character sheet so they can see at a glance which of their spells are mastered and which are unmastered.

During  each Investigator  Development  Phase (page 120),  a  player  has  the  option  of  spending  1  development point to select one of their investigator's unmastered spells and mark it as mastered.

- An investigator must have two spells of a given order mastered before they can master spells of the order above.

Example: Nafeesa is an apprentice Newtonian practitioner and so automatically starts with Werelight (mastered) plus two other spells of her choosing. Her player picks the first order spell Impello and the second order spell Fireball. Morgan is a hedge wizard, and chooses the first order spells Flash Bulb (mastered) and Aqua , and the second order spell Shield.

- To begin with, an investigator can only master first order spells. Once they have mastered two first order spells, they have the option of mastering a second order spell, and so on.
- Once an investigator masters a spell, it is mastered permanently.

## GM ADVICE

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000425_888a36d6ba5f38fc660db819f5a1706914d1fb3d4209a01acf506d6477963cfd.png)

A  player  can  spend  a  maximum  of  1  development point to master an existing spell or learn a new  spell;  they  cannot  do  both  in  the  same development phase if they have only 1 development point  available  (as  learning  a  spell  and  mastering one requires 2 points), nor can they learn or master multiple spells at a time. This restriction reflects that learning and mastering spells requires considerable  effort  to achieve  ( Learning and Mastering Spells , page 121).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000426_03013463404a27f7f0755476274281d3920de808bab0cc52520d77cf7a16cc13.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000427_a952407b24d3a53831ab11ee2ac62aff6d489bcf4f043d1831af96078caafaaa.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000428_1f499c5ea179835135afa479512be9f5bc0a95241fc1fc13d5f6eadacff9f8e5.png)

## Learning More Spells

During each Investigator Development Phase (page 120), a player has the option of spending 1 development point to learn a new spell.

4

- An investigator must know two spells of a given order before they can learn spells belonging to the order above.
- Thus, an investigator with two first order spells and two second order spells could learn either an additional first order spell or a third order spell. They couldn't, however, learn any more second order spells for the moment, as that would lead to them having more second order spells than first order ones: a definite imbalance.
- In addition, the balance of the orders of spells known by an investigator cannot be top heavy, i.e., an investigator can never know more spells in any given order than they know in any of the orders below it.

## GM ADVICE

An investigator can spend only 1 development point to learn one new spell during an investigator development phase ( Learning and Mastering Spells , page 121).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000429_eccdcb3abce16f60c4f9bea0c1771c75a92fbf886b4243bbccb3b6198f4bf22d.png)

Example: Nafeesa  knows  two  first  order  spells,  Werelight  and Impello ,  and  one  second  order  spell,  Fireball.  During  her  first investigator development phase, she has 1 development point to spend.  She  could  mark Impello as  mastered,  learn  a  new  first order spell, or learn a new second order spell. She opts to master Impello .  If  she  had  2  development  points  to  spend,  she  could invest that second point to also learn a new spell or use it to also increase her Magic skill, but if she had 3 or more points to spend, she  couldn't  spend  any  further  points  to  buy  more  new  spells  or master other ones until her next investigator development phase.

## Casting Spells

This  section  takes  you  through  how  a  character,  be  they investigator or non-player character (NPC), uses their Magic skill and magic points to cast spells.

- A Magic skill roll must be made when attempting to cast a spell, unless the caster is not pressed for time ( Spellcasting Rolls , page 173).
- Various factors affect whether or not the spellcasting roll gains a bonus die or incurs a penalty die ( Table 11: Mastered vs Unmastered Spells , page 174; Ranged Attack Modifiers , page 138).
- The standard rules for Multiple Bonus or Penalty Dice apply to spellcasting rolls (page 143).

## Magic Points and Spells

Magic points must be expended to cast spells. Remember, an investigator begins the game with magic points equal to onefifth  of  their  POW,  plus  one  point  for  their  mastered  first order spell. Every time an investigator masters a spell, they gain another magic point.

Example: Nafeesa has POW 60, which divided by five, results in a magic point value of 12 points. She begins with Werelight as  a  mastered  spell,  increasing  her  magic  point  score  by  one  to 13. During her first investigator development phase, where she masters  a  second  spell,  her  magic  point  score  increases  by  one, giving her a new total of 14 magic points.

Spent magic points regenerate naturally, returning to their normal maximum value for The Next Scene (nearby).

- The base cost of attempting to cast a spell is a number of magic points equal to the spell's order.
- The cost of a mastered spell can be boosted (raised) if the caster wishes to increase or alter its effects ( Boosting a Spell , page 176). Not all spells can be boosted.

Example: Nafeesa  has  mastered  Werelight.  When  casting  this spell, she can either spend the base cost of 1 magic point (because it is a first order spell) to get one golf ball-sized sphere of light, or she can spend 2 magic points (the base cost plus a boost of 1 magic point) to get an intensely bright ball of light.

- Once a practitioner runs out of magic points, they may continue to cast spells, but such attempts may cause them harm ( Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation , page 177).
- There is no need to track 'negative' magic points, but a practitioner must roll for HTD every time they attempt to cast a spell when they have zero magic points available.
- It takes one combat round (or the narrative equivalent) to cast a spell-the same amount of time it takes to throw a punch or fire a gun in combat, i.e., it takes up the caster's turn in a combat round ( The Combat Round , page 124).
- Each spell requires a certain number of magic points to be spent before it can be cast ( Magic Points and Spells , following).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000430_110cbeecb1cb5510f087ff51554869f74640b234ac108bc57ec6f96e09fa1564.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000431_db0e9b7e6d272fb9cd3ecd9ce222f541076ae3115e59f31103650bc3ef949b98.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000432_33982617f4b4e0251ca4cf8efac7682badd36221d2c46b36eb31a7812e3c8741.png)

## The Next Scene

The phrase 'the next scene' is used to indicate  the  passing  of  time  between one scene and the next, and its nature is  elastic.  It  is  necessary  that  a  short period  of  time  (approximately  30  minutes  or  so)  passes  between  scenes: moving from one room of a house to the next is not sufficient time, whereas driving  across  London  from  one  location to another is. If in doubt, the GM decides whether enough time has passed for a character to refresh their magic points.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000433_f09e11dba884ce67ceeac47cfdf7274e7b2572d54886c134d50da0ace2bba5d7.png)

Example: while exploring an abandoned underground  station,  Nafeesa  casts  Werelight, costing her 1 magic  point.  While  there,  she defends herself using Fireball, costing her 2 magic  points.  She  has  spent  a  total  of  3  magic points,  reducing  her  available  magic  points  to 10. By the time she arrives back at the Folly (at least  30  minutes  later),  she  has  recovered  her lost  magic points and her total is back up to 13.

## Spellcasting Rolls

Casting  a  spell  requires  skill  and  concentration.  Casting an  unmastered  spell  can  be  challenging  and  the  outcome unpredictable,  whereas  casting  a  mastered  spell  is  more straightforward due to the caster's familiarity with the spell through practise and experience. As a result, mastered spells gain a bonus die on their spellcasting roll.

To make a spellcasting roll, the player should roll percentile dice and compare the result with their investigator's Magic skill.  If  rolling  with  a  bonus  die,  the  caster  takes  the  best result ( Rolling a Bonus/Penalty Die , page 111).

- Regular success or better: the spell works just as it should.
- A fumble: the caster is drained of all magic points and must roll for Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation (page 177).
- A failure: the result is in the hands of the GM; something happens, just not what the caster originally intended ( Failing Spellcasting Rolls , page 174).

Example: Nafeesa's attempt to cast Werelight in the underground station requires a spellcasting roll. As she has mastered Werelight, the Magic roll gains a bonus die. Nafeesa's player rolls 22 and 62, and takes the best result: 22-a Hard success. Nafeesa's werelight pops into being, illuminating her surroundings. Nafeesa decides to cast another spell-Fireball. She must make a new Magic roll to see if she successfully casts it, but, as she hasn't mastered that spell, she doesn't gain a bonus die on the roll this time. Nafeesa's player rolls 08, another Hard success! A ball of fire flies at her target…

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000434_971f704e30ab45f1ab6fb026cc68898a8717a6578946e20300dc5c183271c1ec.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000435_33eb9342fa800b4f503200d459f3d9cc66809c9e04ba8b04ef3c320cb692e6d9.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000436_824ff812b58fd74016a0f44192c3cab446c033dbac6aa8e73d191d8c3436cd85.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000437_2d83c93153930b188702d3a9c6cc44996b8c1921bfa455e93b13063a2d62aeb2.png)

4

If  the  spellcasting  is  made  under  unusual circumstances, such as within an area imbued with magic, refer to Casting in  Exceptional  Circumstances (page  175).  When  casting spells  in  combat,  where  speed  is  of  the  essence,  refer  to Spellcasting in Combat (following), Which Skill to Use in Combat (page 131), and Fight Back Using Magic (page 129).

If  the  caster  has  unlimited  time  and  no  other  pressing concerns,  the  GM  should  just  let  the  spell  work  with  no Magic skill roll required. It can be assumed that, sooner or later, the caster will roll a success and that they are resting long  enough  to  recoup  lost  magic  points  between  such attempts.  In  this  situation,  roll  1D100  to  see  how  many minutes it takes them to safely cast the spell. However, dice rolls  should  always  be  made  when  casting  in  exceptional circumstances and when using magic in combat.

Example: Nafeesa  is  practising  casting  Fireball  in  one  of  the teaching labs upstairs in the Folly. It's not a spell she's mastered yet, but because there's no time pressure in this situation, the GM doesn't call for a Magic skill roll to see if she manages to cast it

## GM ADVICE

Luck  points  can  be  spent  to  turn  a  casting  failure into  a  success,  but  as  usual  they  cannot  be  used to  buy  off  a  fumble  ( Spending  Luck ,  page  113).  If the  casting  roll  is  made  outside  of  combat,  there is  also  the  option  to  push  the  Magic  roll  ( Pushing a  Spellcasting Roll ,  page 175). Remember: combat rolls  cannot  be  pushed  ( Can  I  Push  My  Combat Roll? , page 141).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000438_0f4fa2f0c55881f2af3df1c23600580a4e0d82bdb8b206778524affb57ad4c7e.png)

## Table 11: Mastered vs Unmastered Spells

The following table summarises the differences between a mastered and an unmastered spell.

| Spell Type   | Can I Boost the Spell?   | Normal Conditions              | Exceptional Circumstances                                                               | In Combat                                                                                                                                      |
|--------------|--------------------------|--------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Mastered     | Yes                      | Bonus die to spellcasting roll | Bonus die to spellcasting roll cancels out penalty die due to exceptional circumstances | Bonus die to spellcasting roll cancels out any one penalty die incurred as a result of combat situation (e.g., if target is diving for cover)* |
| Unmastered   | No                       | -                              | Penalty die to spellcasting roll                                                        | Bonus or penalty die to spellcasting roll based on combat situation (e.g., if target is diving for cover)*                                     |

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000439_9ba4d9d2060e20fbc6e1950b5ccbbe1ed1af46c6f5755ae2255bf93b2a1f7c43.png)

or  not-it's  clear  that  at  some  point,  she  will  succeed.  The  GM instead asks Nafeesa's player to roll 1D100 to see how long it takes her. The result is 52, so 52 minutes later Nafeesa finally manages to throw out a perfect fireball.

## Spellcasting in Combat

In combat, spells work in the same manner as firearms, but the caster makes a Magic roll instead of a Firearms roll. For example,  if  the  caster  is  attacking  with  the  Fireball  spell, they  make  a  single  Magic  roll  to  determine  if  the  spell  is successfully cast and whether it has hit the target.

- The damage roll for spells usually uses the caster's DEX to reflect that they are attempting to hit a target. Thus, use DEX rather than POW or STR when consulting the Damage Roll section (page 132).
- The target(s) of a spell during combat have the same response options as if they are being shot at (e.g., Dive for Cover , page 128, or Fight Back , page 129).
- As with a firearms attack, spellcasting rolls in combat are subject to the same bonuses and penalties as firearms ( Ranged Attack Modifiers , page 138).

## Failing Spellcasting Rolls

I can run you up a werelight in torrential rain or while reading the  newspaper  and  the  size  and  intensity  of  the  light  will  be consistent every time. So imagine my surprise when I flicked open my palm and got a werelight the size of a football and the colour of a yellow party balloon.

- -Broken Homes

If  an  investigator  fails  their  Magic  roll  to  cast  a  spell,  a player has the option to spend Luck points to adjust the roll ( Spending Luck , page 113) or they can push the roll instead ( Pushing a Spellcasting Roll , following). But in combat, a player only has the option to spend Luck points to alter their Magic roll result ( Can I Push My Combat Roll? , page 141).

If a player chooses not to spend Luck or push the roll (if permitted), failing the initial Magic skill roll to cast a spell does  not  mean  that  the  spell  completely  fails-something always happens, even if it's just a disappointing fizzle or brief flash  of  light.  To  keep  things  interesting,  we  suggest  that failing  an  initial  spellcasting  roll  results  in  an  unintended effect-perhaps weaker, stronger, or weirder than the practitioner  was  aiming  for.  Alternatively,  in  the  case  of a  spell  directed  against  someone  or  something  (such  as  in combat), a failed spellcasting roll could indicate that the spell simply  misses  its  target,  e.g.,  the  fireball  shoots  off  in  the opposite direction to its intended mark.

The effect of a failed spell is in the GM's hands, who should be creative with strange and unexpected results, although the effect  should  not  cause  significant  harm  to  the  caster.  The GM should  also  be  willing  to  take  suggestions  from  the player whose investigator was casting the spell, but, as always, the GM's word on what actually happens is final. Failing a pushed spell roll has its own consequences-see Pushing a Spellcasting Roll (following).

## Pushing a Spellcasting Roll

If  the  initial  Magic  roll  to  cast  a  spell  is  failed,  the  magic points are still spent, and as long as their investigator is not using magic in combat, the player immediately has the option to push the roll-with a bonus die if the spell is mastered. Pushing a spellcasting roll can be done by screwing up your eyes and focusing really hard on the shapes in your mind, or yelling the formae at the top of your lungs, or by some other means that makes sense for that particular spell. As always, the  GM  should  encourage  the  player  to  say  what  they're doing to push the roll. There is no immediate magic point cost for pushing a spellcasting roll.

- Pushed roll is successful: the spell works normally; no additional magic points are spent above the original cost of the spell.
- Pushed roll is fumbled: the spell fails, and the caster is drained of all magic points and must roll for Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation (page 177) with a penalty die.

Example: Nafeesa  attempts  to  cast Impello .  As  it's  a  first order spell, she spends 1 magic point (the base cost) and rolls her Magic skill, getting an 84: a failure. Nafeesa's player decides to push the casting roll, and this time rolls a 06: a success. The book Nafeesa was attempting to levitate lifts off the desk. The spell has cost  Nafeesa  a  total  of  1  magic  point.  For  what  would  happen if  Nafeesa  had  failed  the  pushed  roll,  see  the  example  under Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation (page 177).

## GM ADVICE

Pushing a spellcasting roll forces the spell to work, but at the risk of self-inflicted harm. Thus, pushing a  spellcasting  roll  is  a  gamble  for  the  player-not on if  the  spell  will  work  (it  will,  unless  the  pushed roll  is  fumbled),  but  whether  their  investigator  is badly  injured  in  the  process.  And,  like  all  pushed rolls,  pushing  a  spellcasting  roll  takes  additional time, which is why they cannot be pushed in combat. Alternatively, an investigator could choose not to  push  the  spellcasting  roll  and  instead  take  a breath  and  then  attempt  a  'fresh'  casting  of  the same spell, which means paying the spell's magic point cost again.

## Casting in Exceptional Circumstances

The  environment  in  which  one  performs  a  spell  can  have an  effect  upon  the  casting.  Areas  imbued  with  magic  can intensify, dampen, or alter the effects of a spell.

- Casting a spell in 'exceptional circumstances' inflicts a penalty die on the character's Magic skill roll.
- The bonus die gained from mastering a spell cancels out the penalty die for casting in exceptional circumstances.

If  the  roll  is  successful,  the  spell  works  normally.  If the  roll  is  failed,  the  GM  decides  the  outcome  ( Failing Spellcasting  Rolls ,  page  174).  If  the  roll  is  fumbled,  the investigator loses all remaining magic points and must roll for Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation (page 177).

It's  also  worth  pointing  out  that  magic  and  alcohol/ recreational drugs don't mix well-casting spells requires a focused mind. If the caster is judged to be drunk or high, any spellcasting should be treated in the same manner as casting in exceptional circumstances.

## GM ADVICE

- Pushed roll is failed: the spell works normally, but the caster is drained of all magic points and must roll for Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation (page 177).

Most  people  would  struggle  to  even  think  about casting  a  spell  when  the  pressure's  on,  but  magicwielding  investigators  in RoL:RPG  are  exceptional. They can broadly do what Peter Grant does, which includes having the wherewithal to attempt spellcasting in exceptional circumstances and during combat.
