![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000440_a16f71bb5dd45856e6d31dd556564a5841da206d451c64c44214c08882f33763.png)

4

## Boosting a Spell

A caster may choose to spend additional magic points when casting certain spells to boost their effects. To take advantage of  this,  the  practitioner  must  have  mastered  the  spell  in question.  The  effects  of  boosting  a  spell  with  additional magic  points  are  noted  in  each  spell  entry.  Unmastered spells  cannot  be  boosted  in  this  fashion.  In  story  terms,  a spell boost can be rationalised as the use of magical finesse, through such techniques as:

- Adjectivia: (sing. adjectivum ) a forma that has the sole
- purpose of changing other formae .
- Inflectentes: a method for 'bending' formae .

The number of magic points a practitioner can spend to boost  any  single  spell  is  limited.  The  limit  is  equal  to  the highest order of spell they have mastered. So, an investigator who has only first order spells mastered can spend a maximum of  1  additional magic point to boost any of their mastered spells. If they had a second order spell mastered, they could spend up to 2 magic points boosting each of their mastered spells. Any increase in the magic point cost of a spell above its base cost is considered a boost. The base cost to cast a spell is equal to the spell's order.

Example: Nafeesa casts Werelight, one of her mastered spells. The spell is first order, so costs 1 magic point to cast. Nafeesa elects to spend a second magic point to boost the spell. Her Magic skill roll is successful, creating a ball of intensely bright light, for a total cost of 2 magic points.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000441_4edb4b79d8b709d7b6ddedf14455cf2491c161a57426be502c0c12d8833a834c.png)

## Sensing a Spell Being Cast

For once Nightingale didn't object, partly because sensing formae is the key to winning a magical duel.

-The Hanging Tree

In a scene, when a character declares they are casting a spell, any nearby practitioners should be given the opportunity to make a detection (Magic) roll before the caster makes their spellcasting roll:

- Regular success: the practitioner senses a spell is being prepared, but has no idea which spell is being cast. They may attempt to rapidly cast a spell of their own in response ( Fight Back Using Magic , page 129).
- prepared, and, if they are familiar with the spell, the GM response ( Fight Back Using Magic , page 129).
- Hard success: the practitioner senses a spell is being should tell them its name. If they are not familiar with the spell, some hint of the spell's effects should be given. They may attempt to rapidly cast a spell of their own in

If  the  caster  is  an  NPC,  the  GM  should  offer  any practitioner investigators present the opportunity to make a detection (Magic) roll-on a success, they detect that a spell is being cast as previously described.

Example: Nafeesa is facing off against a hedge wizard who plans to cast Ice Blast (page 187) against her. The GM asks Nafeesa's player for a Magic roll to see if she can detect the upcoming spell. Her player rolls a 42-a Regular success. This means Nafeesa knows a spell is about to be cast, but she doesn't know what it is. If she'd rolled a Hard success (30 or lower), she would get a sense of icy coldness in her face but not the spell's name, as Varvara hasn't taught Nafeesa Ice Blast yet.

## Spells and Technology

I  pulled  my  phone  and  gave  it  a  shake-it  made  the  sandy the rainmaker  sound that indicated it had been fried by magical discharge.

-False Value

As a rule, magic and microprocessors do not get on. Casting a spell around any form of electronic device while it's connected to a battery or other power supply can be disastrous for the item involved, so much so that the Folly equips its recruits with mobile phones that have jury-rigged physical switches to disconnect the battery-far less laborious than having to constantly pop the battery in and out every time you might be in the vicinity of a magical discharge. Recruits are also encouraged to carry a spare phone (battery separate), just in case they need to cast a spell quickly, thereby accidentally disabling their main phone.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000442_6b9b1434872ea464b7af59cb73b5ef9dc4d1cc09204b6ea700f868ff5d9e209f.png)

As long  as  the  microprocessor  isn't  attached  to  a  power source,  it's  safe.  If  it  is  attached  to  a  power  source,  the silicon in its chip is highly likely to be turned into fine sand, rendering the device useless (known as 'sanding'). Whether an electronic device survives or not is related to how far it is from the source of the magical discharge. In game terms, use the following guidelines:

- Any powered electronic device within arm's length of the magical discharge (i.e., within 1 metre/yard) has its chip immediately destroyed.
- If a spell has been boosted, then any powered electronic device beyond 3 metres/yards also has its chip destroyed if the owner fails a Regular Luck roll. The maximum range for this effect is 15 metres/yards.
- Any powered electronic device farther away than arm's length, but still within point-blank range (i.e., between 1-3 metres/yards) has its chip destroyed if the owner fails a Hard Luck roll.

Of  course,  magic's  effects  on  electronics  can  be  very handy if you need to disable a car, camera, or computer, for instance-and it forms the basis of Peter Grant's Car Killer spell (page 183). Peter even used the effect to create a form of 'magic detector' once 3 .

Anything without a microprocessor is, as far as we can tell, immune to the damaging effect and leads to  a  potentially useful way to spot practitioners who are attempting to hide their abilities-they all wear mechanical watches and tend to drive cars that predate electronic engine management systems.

## GM ADVICE

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000443_0599580171954498c4159a7992286ed5de2e454ca966818d2f79bac770a1cc4d.png)

## Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation (HTD)

Investigators may be tempted to overuse magic. But, in doing so,  they  put  themselves  in  real  danger.  When  a  character overuses magic, they risk suffering from the effects of HTD, which  can  cause  them  physical  damage.  In  the Rivers  of London books, the effects accumulate over time, leading to the tell-tale sign of a brain that looks like a diseased cauliflower.

In  the  game,  HTD  is  simplified  into  a  dice  roll  with immediate effects. If a character fumbles a Magic roll when casting a spell, fails a pushed spellcasting roll, or continues to cast spells when they've run out of magic points, they must make a POW roll. If they fumble a pushed spellcasting roll, the POW roll incurs a penalty die ( Pushing a Spellcasting Roll , page 175). The result of the POW roll determines how badly injured a character is by their overuse of magic.

- Fumble: the character's magic points are reduced to zero, and they suffer severe physical trauma (such as a stroke, heart attack, or aneurysm). They immediately suffer 5 damage-a fatal blow. If they do not (or cannot) spend 30 points of Luck straight away to reduce the damage to a mortal wound, they die ( Damage Conditions , page 133).
- Regular success: the character's magic points are reduced to zero, and they suffer 2 damage.
- Fail: the character's magic points are reduced to zero, and they immediately suffer 4 damage-a Mortal Wound (page 146).
- Hard success: the character's magic points are reduced to zero, and they suffer 1 damage.
- Critical success: the character's magic points are reduced to zero, but they suffer no damage.

If you're feeling mean, you may wish to ask players to make an INT roll every time their investigators cast a  spell  to  see  if  they've  remembered to disconnect the  battery  in  their  mobile  phone  or  Airwave  radio beforehand.  Investigators forgetting to do so is likely  to  happen  when  they  are  suddenly  plunged into  combat,  have  to  react  quickly  to  a  developing situation, or have a spell cast at them they weren't expecting. Outside of the aforementioned situations, though,  we  suggest  you  take  it  as  read  that  the investigators have remembered  to  take all the necessary  precautions,  unless  it  would  make  it interesting for the story if they suddenly had to find a spare phone. Thus, avoid using this rule too often, as it can get in the way of the excitement, and only use it when it makes narrative sense to do so.

Footnote:

3 Foxglove Summer

Example: Nafeesa's player rolls 86 on a pushed Magic roll: a failure. Nafeesa  reduces  her  magic  points  to  zero  and  her  player  makes  a POW roll for HTD, achieving a Regular success. This means Nafeesa suffers 2 damage. As she's currently uninjured, her player marks off the Hurt and Bloodied boxes on Nafeesa's character sheet, along with the Impaired box ( Bloodied: In Serious Trouble , page 144). Despite the damage, the pushed spell works ( Pushing a Spellcasting Roll ,  page 175), although the cost to Nafeesa was significant.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000444_1488c25b75298bf3d49c9129fba7a42f0e1c031c9c404a01b1edcf9dd140b30c.png)

## Spells

This  section  contains  the  spells  available  to  investigators. Classical spells-those that have existed for centuries-each have a Latin name and may also have a colloquial 'shorthand' name as well. Modern spells, such as those devised by Peter Grant, may have somewhat informal English names and completely lack a Latin name.

## GM ADVICE

Remember:  the  base  cost  for  a  spell  is  equal to its order.

## Spell Prerequisites

As Newtonian spells consist of a sequence of formae strung together,  certain  spells  have  prerequisites,  i.e.,  you  cannot learn a particular spell unless you have first learned the spells containing the base formae that  combine to form the spell. A  spell's  prerequisites  are  listed  in  the  spell's  description. Where a spell has no prerequisites, the entry reads 'none.' An investigator cannot take a spell during character creation nor  learn  it  during  an Investigator  Development  Phase (page 120) unless they satisfy all of that spell's prerequisites.

## First Order Spells

## Aqua

Prerequisite: none.

A simple forma used to generate a sphere of water the size of a ping-pong ball just above the caster's palm. Useful if you need a source of water in a hurry.

Boost: spending more magic points increases the amount of water generated. Each additional point forms an additional ball of water or makes the existing ball of water larger.

It's a lot like lux in that you shape the forma in your mind, open your palm and, hopefully, find yourself with a globe of water the size of a ping-pong ball.

-Broken Homes

## Flash Bulb

## Prerequisite: none.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000445_e40536b5ae7a9e9e7239edee97a9515217dd547ebc1339b3c44887434bd0287b.png)

Formerly known as fulge ,  this spell creates a bright flash of light.  Can  be  used  as  a  distraction  or  to  temporarily  blind someone. When cast in front of someone, the target must make a POW roll: if failed, they are stunned and partially blinded for 1D10 rounds. Targets blinded in this way lose their next action, and any actions that require sight are made with a penalty die while stunned.

The spell knocked her on her back, but she rolled, did something that I didn't recognise, and a viciously bright flash in front of my face blinded me. I went crashing down to the cobbles. All I could see was a bruise-coloured blotch in front of my eyes. -Lies Sleeping

## Impello

## Prerequisite: none.

Impello is a forma used to push, pull, or levitate objects. This is  usually  the  second  spell  that  a  Newtonian  apprentice  is taught. One limitation of Impello is that the caster cannot lift themselves from the ground, not even by lifting something to which they are attached. The spell is useful for pushing an opponent back a metre/yard, thus potentially enabling a character to flee. If the target object is small, i.e., something you  could  conceal  in  one  hand,  the  GM  may  ask  for  a successful POW roll to accurately target the object.

The  effect  of Impello is  comparable  to  an  invisible  hand that can be used to push, pull, or lift a target. In a situation where the GM would call for a STR roll to test whether the character is strong enough to move an object, the GM should instead call for a POW roll. Impello could easily levitate an apple with no POW roll required, but if lifting or pushing something  heavier,  the  GM  should  call  for  a  POW  roll. The  difficulty  level  of  the  POW  roll  should  be  set  by  the

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000446_356201de498462d70bc871d12e533d893edda79b7bb892b11c96cc0f5c37ce1a.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000447_a8de538cfefb7487f14dab61e34e751ff572180fcc0ebe5eb9ab25d8aaaaa01a.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000448_137d87c3d2fbc8f4e6d4bbd206d4c8557f86a2c047b6afe54af8bafbaffcd6cc.png)

179

4

target's STR ( Determining the Difficulty Level , page 105). Alternatively,  if  used  against  a  character-rather  than  an inanimate object-the GM could call for an opposed POW vs STR roll ( Opposed Skill Rolls , page 116).

Although  the  basic Impello spell  isn't  intended  to  inflict harm, it can cause damage, e.g., if the spellcaster successfully uses it to push an opponent down some stairs. If the use of Impello causes something to happen in the story that would obviously  cause  damage,  the  GM  should  refer  to Table 7: Other Forms of Damage (page 149).

We sat down on opposite sides of the lab bench and Nightingale placed an object between us. It was a small apple. ' Impello ,'  he said, and the apple rose into the air. It hung there, rotating slowly, while I checked for wires, rods and anything else I could think of. -Rivers of London

## Noisemaker

Prerequisite: Werelight.

Creates a small cloud of gas that expands with a humorous farting noise, as well as making things shake (if the cloud is  large  enough).  Useful  for  creating  distractions  if  cast at a distance.

Now, I have the advantage over my nineteenth century peers of knowing what infrared is and how it relates to imparting heat energy to a small cloud of gas so that it expands with a humorous farting sound-oh, how they must have laughed. -The Hanging Tree

## Rock Drill

Prerequisite: Impello .

Creates  a  force  powerful  enough  to  break  through  rock  or concrete.  The  power  of  the  spell  comes  through  bashing Impello repeatedly against the surface you want broken. The spell can break up 7.5 cm (3 inches) of concrete per round or 30 cm (1 foot) of compacted earth per round.

Boost: on  each  round  following,  1  additional  magic  point can be spent to sustain the spell.

You don't make an invisible pneumatic drill by picturing it in your mind. You do it by shaping the correct variation of impello , lining it up in the right direction and then essentially turning it on and off as fast as you can.

-Whispers Under Ground

## Scindere

Prerequisite: none.

The effect of Scindere is comparable to an invisible hand used to hold an object stationary. Scindere can hold something up to  the  size  of  a  large  watermelon  (approx.  10kg/22lbs).  An opposed STR vs POW (of the caster) roll would be required if someone was attempting to move something held in place with Scindere .  If  someone  wishes  to  use  magic  to  move  an object held by Scindere (such as using Impello vs Scindere ), the GM should call for an opposed POW roll between the two spellcasters to determine who triumphs ( Impello , page 178).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000449_e306ecf5dc32a7e6f8d7035c8bfa31c68dac672bbb452973ac72472547d55bcb.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000450_8f2fc14a70eef394aa75236510070fbd3161ae68d9e410b2bf048d023dde9399.png)

## ImpelloSpell Tree e

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000451_dc5fe06edf23486aa681d5bd2bd644fd33642191ffbc6b6fe4c2dccda399476d.png)

4

Boost: the  base  cost  permits  the  caster  to  hold  objects  up to  the  size  of  a  large  watermelon.  Boosting  the  spell  with additional points increases the weight of what can be lifted or held in place. Instead of dealing with precise weights, the game uses objects to provide a sense of what can be achieved:

- +1 magic point (total cost of 2 magic points) : increases the amount that can be lifted/held to that of any human being.
- +3 magic points (total cost of 4 magic points) : increases the amount that can be lifted/held to that of an average car, and so on.
- +2 magic points (total cost of 3 magic points) : increases the amount that can be lifted/held to that of any horse or cow.
- Prior to the end of the spell's duration, 1 additional magic point can be spent to sustain the spell for another 5 minutes.

The  first  time  Nightingale  demonstrated  the forma ,  I' d  asked how long the apples would stay fixed in place. He'd said that it depended on how much magic the apple had been imbued with. -Moon Over Soho

## Snapdragon

## Prerequisite: none.

Produces a flash and a loud crack that can be used to cause a  distraction  or  scare.  Not  originally  Newtonian,  but  now adopted into the roster.

'She'd already taught me the snapdragon by the time I was seven,' said  Lady  Helena.  Nightingale  asked  for  a  demonstration;  she made a flicking gesture with her hand and there was a flash and a loud crack that echoed off the walls and caused Molly to suddenly appear behind Nightingale's chair.

-The Hanging Tree

## Terra

## Prerequisite: none.

Generates a small golf-ball-sized rock or handful of earth. One of the basic  forms,  producing  similar  effects  to  those achieved by the Quiet People (page 253). Useful if you need rocks to throw or if you need earth to smother a fire.

Boost: spending  more  magic  points  increases  the  amount of earth or rock generated. Each additional point forms an additional ball or makes the existing ball/handful larger.

It [aqua] used to form the empedoclean along with lux , aer and terra -two of which went out of fashion when the four-element theory of matter failed to survive the age of Enlightenment. -Broken Homes

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000452_cc14cc97b76c18a0f370454e3a1ccfd6a072dc8729fcf4dc91afdf8bf1b1d0be.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000453_26ea0b1533931533fa9c3cc6893b8b31bd6c06318a6d3ff806baa92897bea3ec.png)

## Werelight

## Prerequisite: none.

Generates a small ball of bright light about the size of a golf ball with the brightness of a domestic lightbulb. The standard werelight gives off no heat. Werelights do not usually move of their own volition, but while on the caster's hand, they move with it, or the caster can position them in a static location. See Light Bulb (page 184) and Bumblebee (page 186) for mobile variations  of  the  Werelight  spell.  A  werelight  lasts for 5 minutes.

Boost: spending  1  additional  magic  point  increases  the brightness of the light created or generates an additional ball. Boosted with one magic point, the light is so intense that it is painful to look at, and could illuminate a large hall. Prior to the end of the spell's duration, 1 additional magic point can be spent to sustain the spell for another 5 minutes.

Ghosts  are  drawn  to  the  magical  energy  of  a  werelight, and  they  enjoy  feeding  from  it.  While  a  ghost  feeds,  the werelight's  colour  slowly  changes  from  a  soft  white/milky blue to yellow to red/dark crimson; the ghost becomes visible and is likely to converse with the caster.

## Werdlight Spell Tree

Mastered: change  the  brightness  of  the  light  (sort  of  like a  domestic  lightbulb  on  a  dimmer  switch).  The  caster  can also  change the werelight's colour, even shifting down the spectrum  to  something  barely  visible.  Cast  at  a  very  low power, the werelight produces a soft glow without blowing nearby microprocessors. It can also be modified to go beyond the range of visible light and into infrared, thus creating heat.

I  have  a  nice  low  powered  werelight  that  I  can  conjure  in  an interview room without blowing all the surveillance-you'd be amazed how often I have to use it. My latest refinement was to add a scindere forma so that I can park it over my shoulder and use it as a reading light.

-The Hanging Tree

## Second Order Spells

## Candano

## Prerequisite: none.

Also known as Tanglefoot, from the Greek ( khandánō ), this spell  creates  a  magical  bolo  that  wraps  around  a  person's lower legs and ankles. An effective way to prevent someone from running away and/or to trip them up.

In combat, the spell's attack is resolved in the same manner as a firearms attack ( Combat , page 123), with the attack roll (Firearms) replaced by the spellcasting roll (Magic). If the spell  is  cast  successfully,  its  target  is  permitted  a  DEX  or STR roll  (whichever  is  higher).  With  a  Hard  success,  the target avoids becoming entangled by the bolo or breaks free from it. Otherwise, the target's legs become entangled for 2 rounds; if they attempt to move, they fall over and become prone ( Combat Manoeuvres , page 134). However, the target cannot stand up again on their turn as a free action until the spell ends. Attacks against a prone character gain a bonus die.

## Car Killer

Prerequisites: Werelight + Impello .

Magic has the side effect of frying microprocessors ( Spells and Technology ,  page  176)  and  thus  modern  vehicles  can be disabled with a single well-placed spell. The spell's title is 'Car Killer,' but the spell can be used to disable any piece of modern technology containing a microprocessor.

And once you've quantified an effect, it becomes that much easier to weaponize it. All you need to do is modify your werelight a bit with a couple of formae inflectentes and, after about three weeks of trial and error, you have a projectable spell that will burn out every microprocessor within a conveniently small radius.

-Foxglove Summer

## Fireball

Prerequisites: Impello + Werelight or Flash Bulb.

Uses the forma lux (light) coupled with iactus (an adjectivum ) and impello (to  move  it  about),  to  create  the  classic  fireball. The spell can vary widely in magnitude. This is typically a loud  and  flashy  spell,  causing  a  sudden  flash  of  light  and sounding like a bedsheet being ripped in half. The resulting fireball  is,  however,  a  relatively  slow  projectile  and  can  be stopped by modern ballistic armour.

In combat, the spell's attack is resolved in the same manner as  a  firearms  attack  ( Combat ,  page  123),  with  the  attack roll  (Firearms) replaced by the spellcasting roll (Magic). A damage  roll  should  be  made  using  the  caster's  DEX.  The basic  spell  inflicts  1  weapon  damage,  which  is  added  to the  result  of  the  damage  roll,  if  applicable  ( The Damage Roll , page 132).

Boost: each additional magic point can be used to increase the spell's damage by +1. The attacker can choose to describe this  as  a  single  larger  fireball  or  an  additional  fireball;  the result is the same.

Mastered: the  size  of  the  fireballs  can  be  altered.  Narrowgauge fireballs can be used to disguise the method of attack, creating wounds that look more like those of a handgun.

' You didn't say the Latin,' I pointed out.

' You practise this in silence,' he said, ' from the outset. This spell is a weapon. It has a single purpose and that is to kill. Once you've mastered it you are under the same obligations as any other armed constable,  so  I  suggest  you  familiarise  yourself  with  the  current guidelines on firearms use.'

-Rivers of London

## Impello Palma

## Prerequisite: Impello .

Creates an invisible hand-like force that can be used to swat at objects and people, such as knocking a book out of someone's hands or slapping a person across the face. Often deployed as a non-lethal means of delaying or distracting someone.

If  used  in  combat,  the  spell  is  an  attack  and  is  resolved exactly  the  same  as  a  firearms  attack  ( Combat ,  page  123), with the attack roll (Firearms) replaced by the spellcasting roll (Magic). If successful, it inflicts 1 damage total.

Boost: additional magic points can turn the invisible force into  a  temporary  barrier  that  lasts  as  long  as  the  caster maintains their concentration or 5 minutes, whichever occurs first. There are two ways another person may attempt to push through the invisible barrier:

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000454_e5019d91f35f9ad1b86658604ea7e7a5a3211455e371e56af7ff21fa2b3bc296.png)

4

- If trying to run and force their way through the barrier: ineffective-it's like bouncing off a plate-glass window; the person suffers 1 damage.
- If trying to slowly and deliberately push their way through the barrier: it's like walking through treacle/ molasses, but if the person succeeds with an opposed POW roll versus the caster, they pass through unharmed; if failed, they cannot pass through.
- +1 magic point (total cost of 3 magic points) : creates a barrier the size of typical single door.
- +2 magic points (total cost of 4 magic points) : doubles the size of the barrier (e.g., a double door).

Note: this barrier does not protect against damage; for that, see Shield (Basic) (page 185).

I  didn't give him a chance to finish the movement-I made the formae in my head, flung out my left hand, still holding my phone, and shouted, much louder than I'm supposed to, ' Impello palma .' -Whispers Under Ground

## Impello Vibrato

## Prerequisite: Rock Drill.

A subtler  and  more  advanced  version  of Rock Drill (page 180). Can be used to cause matter to vibrate, making it useful for making out of reach objects fall over or fall off a surface on which they're resting, all the way up to causing a structure to collapse or crumble. It can only be used to vibrate inert matter such as earth, brick, or concrete. This spell is unusual as it requires the spellcaster to concentrate for three or five rounds; this means they can undertake no other actions while the spell is performed. If the caster is injured or otherwise caused to be distracted, the caster should make a POW roll to determine if the Impello vibrato spell is disrupted (fails).

The effect is like someone hitting the targeted area with an invisible, silent hammer . The GM must figure out what effect  this  might  have:  a  hammer  could  break  a  ceramic or  glass  object  or  knock  out  the  load-bearing  bricks  of  a chimney, while a sledgehammer (see Boost ) could destroy a wall or cause a larger structure to fail. If aiming to collapse a structure, e.g., a chimney, the entire structure need not be vibrated,  just  the  critical  load-bearing  parts -a  successful INT roll  prior  to  use  allows  the  caster  to  target  the  spell efficiently to minimise their magic expenditure. The spell has no visible effect until the caster ends the spell.

Boost: spending  2  additional  magic  points  upgrades  the invisible, silent hammer to a sledgehammer.

I'd been working on loosening up the chimney stack with what I call Impello  vibrato but  which  Nightingale  calls  will  you  stop messing about and pay attention, while Faceless had been chatting. -Moon Over Soho

## Invisible Catch

## Prerequisite: Impello .

Combining Impello with iactus (an adjectivum )  allows  the caster to catch objects in mid-air. It can be used upon living or non-living objects. If the target object is moving quickly (such  as  if  thrown),  the  casting  roll  must  be  made  with  a penalty die (as per Fast-Moving Targets , page 138).

The effect of Invisible Catch is comparable to an invisible hand.  In  a  situation  where  the  GM  would  call  for  a  STR roll to test whether the character is strong enough to catch an object, the GM should instead call for a POW roll. The spell could easily catch an apple with no POW roll required, but  if  catching  something  heavier,  such  as  a  child  falling from an upstairs window, the GM should call for a POW roll.  The  spell  can  be  used  to  catch  other  magical  effects, such as fireballs (see Boost ). It cannot catch bullets-they're way too fast.

Boost: for  the  spell  to  be  powerful  enough  to  hold  an incoming projectile, such as a thrown weapon, heavy object, or  fireball-but  not  a  bullet-the  total  number  of  magic points spent must equal or exceed the damage the projectile would otherwise inflict.

So I shot him. It was worth it just for the obvious outrage it caused him, and I enjoyed it right up until the point where he caught the bloody fireball. Just snatched it out of the air and held it, Yoricklike, in front of his face.

-Moon Over Soho

## Light Bulb

Prerequisites: Scindere + Werelight.

Uses lux iactus scindere to fix a werelight in position to provide light. Those who don't know any better would mistake this for  a  standard  light  fitting.  This  spell  combines  features  of the Werelight spell (page 182) and Scindere spell (page 180), and is fixed in a location chosen by the caster. The light lasts for 5 minutes.

To collapse a structure where the spell has been targeted with  a  successful  INT  roll  requires  three  rounds.  The structure  collapses  immediately  after  the  caster's  turn  in the third round is over. When the spell hasn't been targeted with a successful INT roll, it takes five rounds to push the weakened structure over.

Boost: each additional magic point spent allows the caster to increase either the size or the brightness of the light created. Prior to the end of the spell's duration, 1 additional magic point can be spent to sustain the spell for another 5 minutes.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000455_d8aa745739807bc712bd9c434ef20fa6cf99dfe599d77d48505a764a6ef3319e.png)

Note: if the caster wishes to fix the werelight in a position they cannot  physically  reach,  they  must  combine  this  spell  with Impello (page 178) to manoeuvre the werelight into position. This combined version of the spell is third order and has a base cost of 3 magic points; it can be boosted as described above.

I fixed the werelight to the ceiling with scindare in the hope that Reynolds would mistake it for a light fixture...

-Whispers Under Ground

## Serra Obscura

## Prerequisite: Shield.

Creates a small force blade up to 6 cm (2.4 inches) in length, which extends from the caster's palm, thumb, or finger (caster's choice). The blade can be used to cut through rope or other materials, or can be used as a melee weapon. The blade lasts for 1 minute (or the equivalent of 6 combat rounds). If used as a weapon in combat, attacks are made using the wielder's Fighting skill, as per nonmagical melee weapons. The blade has a base damage of 1.

While  they  were  chatting,  I  created  a  little  shield  and,  very carefully, sliced off the ropes around my wrist. Foxglove Summer

-

## Shield (Basic)

Prerequisite: none.

Uses aer  congolare to  control  the  air  in  front  of  the  caster, allowing  them  to  form  a  shield  that's  capable  of  stopping bullets-or, at least, slowing them down dramatically. It's also rather handy at protecting against melee weapons and fists.

Each firearms attack should be resolved in the usual way ( Combat ,  page  123),  with  the  result  of  the  damage  roll reduced by the shield. The spell reduces the damage inflicted by an attack (or each bullet) by 2, and can withstand a total of ten times that number, i.e., once the shield has absorbed a total of 20 points of damage, it fails. The Shield spell also stacks with Armour (page 135).

Boost: each additional magic point spent allows the caster to choose one of the following:

- Fix the shield in place.
- Increase the defensive value of the shield, reducing the incoming damage by a further 1 (meaning 1 additional point would reduce damage by 3, and withstand a total of 30 damage, and so on).

On each following  round,  1  additional  magic  point  can be spent to sustain the spell, adding another 10 points to the total damage the shield can withstand.

Mastered: can  be  cast  at  an  angle  to  deflect  projectiles hitting  it.  This  increases  the  amount  of  damage  the  shield can withstand from ten times to 20 times, so the basic spell can withstand a total of 40 damage before failing.

Nightingale  took  the  trouble  to  teach  me aer because,  combined with the equally tricky and out-of-fashion congolare , it creates a shield in front of my body.

-Whispers Under Ground

## Wandering Light

Prerequisites: Impello + Werelight.

Uses lux impello to create a werelight that bobs along with the caster in a fixed position. The light could be behind you, to the side, in front, or above. The light lasts 5 minutes.

Boost: each additional magic point spent allows the caster to increase either the size or the brightness of the light created. Prior to the end of the spell's duration, 1 additional magic point can be spent to sustain the spell for another 5 minutes.

I cast a simple lux impello combo which put a yellowish werelight about two metres over my head where, hopefully, it would bob about after me like a balloon, only brightly lit.

-Foxglove Summer

## Shield, Spell ITree

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000456_87460e910b07b57c9394c188befd8530fc83944942460ae04e7353c907f77932.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000457_eaa8756a931a20a9d98a3dee215bbc5dea4cfad8499e85a04c2b3dedaa9f90e7.png)

4

## Waterbomb

Prerequisites: Aqua + Impello .

Causes  a  balloon-sized  ball  of  water  to  appear  where  the caster directs.

Boost: each additional magic point spent allows the caster to choose one of the following:

- Propel the balloon to hit a target.
- Cause the water to be chilled.

While this spell causes no harm, it can cause a distraction. Anyone struck by the spell must make a POW roll, with a penalty die if the water is chilled. Those failing the POW roll lose their next action and must apply a penalty die to any roll made as their next action.

I flicked a water bomb into his face-a nice cold one, thanks to a trick Varvara taught me-and followed up by kicking him in the bollocks. He gave me a puzzled look and then fell flat on his face. -Lies Sleeping

## Third Order Spells

## Ball of Lightning

Prerequisites: Impello + Shield.

Creates  a  fist-sized  ball  of  lightning  that  propels  itself  at a  target.  Like  an  advanced  fireball,  but  based  on  the  use of aer rather  than lux .  The  spell's  attack  is  resolved  in  the same manner as a firearms attack ( Combat , page 123), with the  attack  roll  (Firearms)  replaced  by  the  spellcasting  roll (Magic).  A  damage  roll  should  be  made  using  the  caster's DEX. The basic  spell  inflicts  2  weapon  damage,  which  is added  to  the  result  of  the  damage  roll,  if  applicable  ( The Damage Roll , page 132).

Boost: each additional magic point spent increases the power of the spell by +1 weapon damage.

Just in time, because a fist-sized ball of lightning struck it at chest height and exploded-half blinding me.

- -The October Man

## Bumblebee

Prerequisite: Wandering Light.

The  basis  is  your  bog-standard lux-impello combination-the complications  come  in  the  various  modifiers  you  add  to  the principal formae . I opened my hand and an orangey-red sphere the size of a golf ball immediately shot back down the tunnel the way we'd come.

- ' Ah,' said Nightingale.

'It always does that,' I said. 'Wait a second.'

-Lies Sleeping

## Glitter Bomb

## Prerequisite: Waterbomb.

An icy projectile is generated and thrown at a target causing an  area  to  freeze.  The  name  of  the  spell  evokes  the  light reflecting from the ice crystals that form where the spell hits. A wave of cool air flows outward from the point of impact. Those caught within the blast radius of the spell may suffer damage. The radius of the spell is 2 metres/yards.

The spell's attack is resolved exactly the same as a firearms attack ( Combat ,  page 123), with the attack roll (Firearms) replaced  by  the  spellcasting  roll  (Magic).  A  damage  roll should  be  made  using  the  caster's  DEX.  The  basic  spell inflicts  2  weapon  damage,  which  is  added  to  the  result  of the damage roll, if applicable ( The Damage Roll , page 132).

Boost: each additional magic point increases the radius by 1 metre/yard.

This  was  one  of  Varvara's  wartime  spells  as  translated  by AbigailLedyanaya Bomba in Russian, but we call it a glitter bomb because of the way light sparkles off the ice crystals that form around the epicentre.

- -Lies Sleeping

## Grasping Hand

Prerequisite: Impello (mastered).

A more complex version of the first order Impello ,  allowing the  caster  to  grasp  and  move  structures  with  superhuman strength. This version of the spell is designed to cause damage and is very handy for pulling off car doors!

The effects are hard to quantify precisely but, as a guide, the standard spell allows the caster to rip a locked car door off by its hinges or roll an object like a standard car onto its roof without the need for a STR roll. Enterprising burglars could use it to rip locked doors out of their frames, should the mood take them.

Generates  a  golf-ball-sized  orangey-red  sphere  that  flies about  erratically  for  a  few  rounds,  making  a  distinctive squealing sound as it bounces around. It is apt to shoot off the wrong way first before returning to the caster and then flitting about in the direction it was supposed to go. It doesn't do much, other than create a hard-to-ignore distraction.

While not intended for deployment against the living, if the spell is used against people or creatures, they are picked up  and  flung  with  considerable  force  a  number  of  metres/ yards  equal  to  the  caster's  POW;  refer  to Table 7:  Other Forms of  Damage (page  149).  The  standard  spell  inflicts Severe damage.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000458_78ed770819b5a06dd561d945fc769e77f6e1017a5fb6464f0c6a56609994b686.png)

Boost: each additional magic point spent increases the size of object that can be damaged or rolled by this spell.

- +1 magic point (total cost of 4 magic points) : roll a large vehicle (heavy car or pickup truck); causes Deadly damage if used against living creatures.
- +3 magic points (total cost of 6 magic points) : roll an articulated lorry or rip the door off a bank vault; causes Splat damage if used against a living creature.
- +2 magic points (total cost of 5 magic points) : roll a delivery truck; causes Terminal damage if used against living creatures.

I made it into my own gap between a BMW 5 series and a Jaguar XJ, whose resale value Lesley had recently lowered by ripping open the doors, just before a chunk of the ceiling collapsed onto the spot where I'd been lying.

-The Hanging Tree

## Ice Blast

## Prerequisite: Waterbomb.

An attack spell  creating  a  blast  of  freezing  ice  particles  of varying  sizes.  The  effect  spreads  forward  from  the  caster's hand in a conical shape, extending 30 metres/yards. At the caster's end, the cone is only a hand's width in size, but, at the far end, the effect is 3 metres/yards wide. Anyone in the line of the blast may suffer damage.

The spell's attack is resolved exactly the same as a firearms attack ( Combat ,  page 123), with the attack roll (Firearms) replaced  by  the  spellcasting  roll  (Magic).  A  damage  roll should  be  made  using  the  caster's  DEX.  The  basic  spell inflicts  2  weapon  damage,  which  is  added  to  the  result  of the damage roll, if applicable ( The Damage Roll , page 132). Armour has no effect versus Ice Blast.

Boost: each additional magic point spent increases the power of the spell by +1 weapon damage.

It saved my life, because the next instant I was struck by a freight train full of icicles.

-Broken Homes

## Skinny Grenade

Prerequisites: Fireball + Scindere .

Lux impello scindere creates a projectile that sticks to its target before exploding a few seconds later-although sometimes it can take more than a minute. In effect, a skinny grenade is a delayed-action fireball, with a blast radius of 2 metres/yards.

The spell's attack is resolved exactly the same as a firearms attack ( Combat ,  page 123), with the attack roll (Firearms) replaced  by  the  spellcasting  roll  (Magic).  Roll  1D10  to determine how many rounds it takes for the skinny grenade to explode (there is always a delay between casting and the skinny  grenade  exploding),  and  then  make  a  damage  roll using the caster's DEX. The standard spell inflicts 1 weapon damage, which is added to the result of the damage roll, if applicable ( The Damage Roll , page 132).

Boost: each additional magic point spent increases the power of the spell by +1 weapon damage.

Mastered: the  delay  between  casting  and  exploding  is dictated by the caster (choose from 1 -10 rounds).

'Skinny  grenade,' I said. 'You  use Scindere like  you  do  with Lux  impello  scindere ,  except  instead  of  a  light  in  a  fixed  place you get a bomb.'

'Skinny grenade?'

'From Scindere ,' I said. Nightingale shook his head.

-Moon Over Soho

## Throw Sound

Prerequisites: Shield + Impello .

Allows the caster to throw their own voice, making it seem as  if  it  is  coming  from  elsewhere  in  the  vicinity.  The  spell uses the aer forma and various inflectentes to create a sound. Another useful spell for creating a distraction.

Boost: spending 1 additional magic point allows the caster to create sounds other than their own voice, such as the barking of a dog or the sound of a car.

I noticed then that Nightingale's Jag, which I swore I'd heard pull up in front of the barn, was nowhere to be seen. -Broken Homes

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000459_8ed71ed347ad7a35af02842bf7b2f69c3f82998eb3fdddde93f8f754c2159e8a.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000460_8bc896e963fbb728400c41e30cad2d7a98554ccb355355b0aec58166e85f5145.png)

4

## Fourth Order Spells

## Aqua in Nebula Oborire

Prerequisite:

Aqua (mastered).

Created by a mountebank in the Folly's wild pre-respectable days, this spell raises a mist from a body of water by exciting the water molecules on the surface, but only enough to form a mist in whatever atmospheric conditions prevail-not that the  creator  of  this  spell  knew  that!  A  moderately  difficult spell that some claim only exists to make spectators murmur ' Oooooh! ', it can be used for nefarious purposes with a little planning aforethought.

Once the spell has been cast on a suitably large body of water-at minimum, a large duck pond or a small lake-a mist gradually begins to gather. It takes five minutes for there to  be  enough mist to roll off the water and form an oddly warm wall of water vapour 100 metres/yards in diametersufficient  to  hide  in.  While  in  the  mist,  investigators  gain a  bonus  die  on  Stealth  rolls  and  incur  a  penalty  die  on Observation  rolls,  e.g.,  when  attempting  to  spot  other people moving through the mist. The mist rapidly dissipates after 15 minutes.

## Coffin Bell, The

Prerequisites: Terra (mastered) + Impello (mastered). Moves  2  cubic  metres/yards  of  earth  or  debris.  Especially useful if one finds oneself buried alive-hence the spell's name.

Boost: each  additional  magic  point  spent  increases  the amount of earth moved by +1 cubic metre/yard.

There's a spell that dates back to the Victorian era called the coffin bell ,  designed  to  allow  a  skilled  practitioner  to  dig  himself  out from  his  own  grave.  It's  a  fourth  order  spell,  so  I  reckon  you'd have to be a pretty good practitioner to be able to cast it after being accidentally  buried  alive.  I  also  have  doubts  about  whether  it could shift four cubic metres of loose earth, but it certainly made short work of the pile of office furniture I was under. -False Value

## Flying Ice Saw

Prerequisites: Glitter Bomb or Ice Blast.

Creates a large disk,  3  metres/yards  across,  that  spins  like a circular saw blade or drill head. The blade moves at speed through the air, slicing through whatever it contacts. As it moves,  it  emits  a  loud  humming  sound.  The  disk  inflicts damage on anything it hits. It lasts for one round and can be used to attack 3 separate targets, as long as they are within 10 metres/yards of each other.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000461_5f8e680dd570da922f097ce1ba12c2fe14d24fed420a1d0364c39ec2f2410d20.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000462_dec1dfd03d28b6485504f714e84e61c27f29b8950bbb4dfc33842731cceaf3e4.png)

The spell's attack is resolved exactly the same as a firearms attack ( Combat ,  page 123), with the attack roll (Firearms) replaced  by  the  spellcasting  roll  (Magic).  A  damage  roll should  be  made  using  the  caster's  DEX.  The  basic  spell inflicts  2  weapon  damage,  which  is  added  to  the  result  of the damage roll, if applicable ( The Damage Roll , page 132).

Boost: each additional magic point spent increases the power of the spell by +1 weapon damage.

I  only  saw  it  coming  because  of  the  rain  from  the  sprinklers.  It was like a lens, an optical distortion whirling through the air-a circular saw three metres across, droplets spraying off the top. Even as I was dropping I saw it slice horizontally through the front of the Humvee. And it was fast.

-The Hanging Tree

## Natchaduri

## Prerequisite: Candano .

Also known as Dancing Rope, this spell creates a braid of crimson smoke that can be used to bind, slap, or pummel someone or something up to 15 metres/yards away. The name is an anglicisation of the Bengali নােচর দি ড় ( Nācēra dari ).

Natchaduri acts like a whip, and can be used in one of two ways: to perform a Combat Manoeuvre (page 134), such as entangling a person or knocking something from their hand, or to inflict harm. The natchaduri lasts for 2 rounds, but the caster  is  limited  to  performing  one  action  with  it  (combat manoeuvre or harm).

In combat, the spell's attack is resolved in the same manner as a firearms attack ( Combat , page 123), with the attack roll (Firearms) replaced by the spellcasting roll (Magic). If used to bind, the target is permitted a DEX or STR roll (whichever is higher). With a Hard success the target avoids becoming entangled or breaks free; otherwise, they remain bound for 4 rounds. If used to harm, any damage roll should be made using the caster's DEX. The spell inflicts 1 weapon damage, which is added to the result of the damage roll, if applicable ( The Damage Roll , page 132).

## Telescopium

Prerequisite: Shield (mastered).

Manipulates the air in front of the caster to create two lenses, bending light to create the effect of a telescope. Similar in essence to the shield spell, using two carefully aligned minishields to distort and focus light.

Newton's spell used the aer forma to grab hold of the air in front of your face and then craft it into two lenses that act like a telescope. The great man called it telescopium , which tells you everything you  need  to  know  about  his  approach  to  branding.  Beyond  the usual drawbacks-i.e., the risk of having your brain turn into a diseased cauliflower-if the lenses are the wrong shape you get a face full of rainbows. And if you're stupid enough to look at the sun you can make yourself permanently blind.

-Broken Homes

## Wall Banger, The

Prerequisite: Grasping Hand (mastered).

Rips apart architecture, unzipping brickwork and syphoning it up  into the air only to deposit  it  unceremoniously somewhere  nearby.  The  effect  is  powerful  enough  to  pull apart one face of an average house-bricks, doors, windows, and all-and drop the resulting rubble into two big piles on either side. The spell uses a variant of the impello forma along with temperāre and some other complicated twists.

Boost: for  every  2  additional  magic  points  spent,  an  extra amount of wall of similar size is ripped apart.

Then the front of the barn literally unzipped-bricks fountaining up and away in two diverging streams and the doors abruptly ripped off their hinges and went spinning off through the air like something from a catastrophic decompression.

-Broken Homes

Using the 'rope' for climbing is a challenge as it is shortlived and must be affixed to something. Depending on the situation,  the  GM  may  call  for  a  DEX  roll  to  anchor  the 'rope,' followed by a Hard Athletics roll to scale the 'rope' before it dissipates.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000463_cd42938047d3c9c96091ce508652c4a0289a68ae61375aadd02423bc34faeaf1.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000464_855e48b8cbb82d7e9969d11f66b24e6aeac5bce7b10240663d464ba96c404e76.png)

4

## Fifth Order Spells

## Clausurafrange

Prerequisite: Impello (mastered).

Used to deal with locks with varying degrees of subtlety. The caster must be close to the target lock. A successful casting unlocks any mundane-i.e., non-magical-lock. If the caster has plenty of time-and skill-this spell neatly 'drills' the lock. If you're in a hurry, you can use it to pop the lock right out of its frame.

Boost: spending 1 additional magic point allows the caster to very neatly pick the lock, leaving it undamaged.

I was working to perfect my first fifth order spell, clausurafrange , which was supposed to neatly pop out a lock but was currently just making damp holes in the riverbank…

-False Value

## Seducere

## Prerequisite: none.

Grants  the  ability  to  bend  a  single  target  to  the  caster's will,  with  an  effect  similar  to  the genii  locorum's Glamour (page 243). The single target of the spell may be compelled to do anything from fetching something useful through to performing a dangerous act that could harm themselves or others.  The  spell  works  by  causing  a  biochemical  reaction, releasing hormones-serotonin, etc.-into the target's system. As a fifth order spell, Seducere costs 5 magic points, but its effects are limited to one animal, such as a single dog. It may be boosted to affect a single human.

When Seducere is  used upon a target, they are permitted a POW roll to resist its effects. If targeting animals or nonpractitioners, the outcomes of the POW roll are as follows:

- Fumble or failure: they do as they are bid without question, nor do they ever think to question why they acted as they did.
- Critical success: they are unaffected by the spell and react to the request as though it were asked by another person. They are not aware of anything magical having taken place.
- Regular or Hard success: they do as they are bid without question, but later they perhaps wonder what possessed them to act as they did. Of course, they soon console themselves with any excuse that feels the most rational to them.

## GM ADVICE

Whether the target is aware of the spell or not, if it's an investigator, they can spend Luck to overcome a failed POW roll ( Spending Luck , page 113). The player could also choose to push the roll-represented by them  struggling  to  maintain  their  self-control  and resist the compulsion, just as Peter Grant does when facing Lady Ty's actual glamour 4 -though a second failure would leave the investigator completely at the caster's mercy.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000465_897b1e395c309c0dca502d41c40a50bbc991bb372f733c3969a7a60e2eaaf315.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000466_d68f898e96eedc001e9bb1372ee5d697baf79aaf0cfa2bd1a29c1aec0a523424.png)

## Important: Consent

Certain  spells,  such  as  Seducere  and Vox  imperante  involve  compelling  a character  to  behave  in  a  certain  way if they fail to resist the spell's effects. The  GM  and  players  should  discuss the boundaries of what that entails for their  group  and  set  limits  accordingly to ensure that no one is ever made to feel  uncomfortable  or  unsafe  out  of character through those spells' usage.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000467_283222028dd8137483fb45e365a0a18be7bf2539ac9c44ba48259308931c23e2.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000468_842b9a67979e96b64ed94befe36efb52a52fa3c6dbcf78e2dc9f45fff451f91a.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000469_c17b0ff4e80c09f98a957a375cee1c5d6655bc7ae6d74b4f0d5a33573d1a1373.png)

Whereas, if targeting a practitioner or someone aware of the glamour, the outcomes of the POW roll are as follows:

- Fumble: they behave exactly as bidden. They are unaware of the use of the spell.
- Any level of success: they are aware of the use of Seducere , but are not compelled to act upon it.
- Failure: they begin to act as requested, perhaps taking a few steps or speaking a few words, but then recover their self-control. They are aware of the use of the spell.

Boost: spending 2 additional magic points extends the effect to a single human being.

- '

Seducere ,'  he  said.  'The  Compulsion,  or,  as  the  Scots  say,  'the Glamour'. According to Bartholomew, many supernatural creatures do it as a form of self-defence.'

- -Rivers of London

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000470_53e1999c5ac9e69eac41d1e1f8c5c3f14ff4867abf40d38dd48122cf84097ce0.png)

## Sīphōnem

## Prerequisite: none.

Used  to  disarm  a Demon Trap (page  324).  Each  stage  of the spell has to be carefully worked so as not to trigger the trap, after which the magical essence that powers the trap is released. As this power is released, some clue as to the nature of the trap's construction and the signare of  its  creator may be detected.

As a  fifth  order  spell, Sīphōnem costs  5  magic  points  to cast.  If  cast  successfully,  it  disarms  a  standard  demon  trap. Disarming  a  greater  demon  trap  is  more  challenging,  and requires  the  practitioner  to  achieve  a  Hard  success  on  the casting roll.

Failing the casting roll activates a trigger on the demon trap ( Disarming a Demon Trap , page 326).

To do sīphōnem you have to run through each of the formae in turn, in the right sequence and with the right modifications. And you have to do this faster than the demon trap can release its magical payload.

-False Value

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000471_e0691b994cd56bce3bad962827bb3ee1b2090e583cc13206045db926e3a743f0.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000472_1837215fb13b14e5483dc0b91ed8e0778bba199e8def8a249bb59782fb19bfad.png)

## Vultus Occulto

Prerequisite: Shield (mastered).

An illusion spell that disguises a person, so that their face is hard to see-almost as if the person has no face at all. The spell  lasts  for  10  minutes.  A  determined  magician  can  see through the illusion by spending a whole round staring at the caster and making a successful Magic roll.

Boost: each  additional  magic  point  spent  increases  the duration by 10 minutes.

I  still  couldn't  see  his  face,  but  I'd  done  a  bit  of  reading  since Alexander Smith had given us the description of his face or, more accurately, hadn't. Victor Bartholomew, possibly the most boring magician  that  ever  lived,  named  it  as Vultus  occulto ,  which even I knew was pig Latin, and had devoted an entire chapter on the subject of countermeasures which, typically for Bartholomew, I  could boil down to one sentence: Keep looking really hard and sooner or later you'll see through it. So that's what I did.

-Moon Over Soho

4

## Sixth Order Spells

## Heart Stop

Prerequisite: Impello (mastered).

At  best,  a  temporary  paralysis  spell.  At  worst,  the  caster can  cause  the  target's  heart  to  stop  beating,  which  is,  by all  accounts,  a  very  difficult  thing  to  achieve.  Following  a successful spellcasting roll, the caster must win an opposed roll  with  the  target,  with  the  caster  pitting  their  POW against the target's CON.

The number of magic points spent determines the amount of  damage  inflicted.  If  the  target  fails  the  opposed  roll, the  spell's  effect  lasts  as  long  as  the  caster  maintains  their concentration or 5 minutes, whichever occurs first. Due to the  complexity  and  degree  of  control  needed  to  cast  this spell, it can only ever be directed against a single target. As a sixth order spell, Heart Stop costs 6 magic points and, at this level, inflicts 3 damage if the target loses the opposed roll,  immediately  marking  them  as  Down  ( Wounds  and Healing , page 144). Cast at this level, the damage presents as temporary paralysis.

If  cast  against  an  investigator,  they  have  the  option of  spending  Luck  to  win  the  opposed  CON  vs  POW  roll

( Spending  Luck ,  page  113),  or  to  buy  off  the  effects  of a  mortal  wound  or  survive  a  fatal  blow  ( Wounds  and Healing , page 144).

Boost: by  increasing the magic point spend, the effect can be increased:

- +1 magic point (total cost of 7 magic points) : inflicts 4 damage in a single blow (i.e., a Mortal Wound , page 146) if the target loses the opposed roll. Cast at this level, the damage presents as a severe angina attack or a mild heart attack.
- +2 magic points (total cost of 8 magic points) : inflicts 5 damage in a single attack (i.e., a fatal blow; Damage Conditions , page 133) if the target loses the opposed roll. Cast at this level, the spell presents as a severe heart attack.

Human beings are really difficult  to  directly  control  by  magicDr Walid has postulated that the human central nervous system generates its  own  autonomic magical resistance as a function of evolving in a world where potential predators can do magic. In order to partially paralyse me like that, Mrs Chin had to be a very highly trained practitioner. A true master, in Nightingale's terms. An utter pain in the arse, literally, in mine.

- -False Value

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000473_91df716b3962b7967a8bfe1204b4508961808379c30929f48870d04b2698eba8.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000474_555e35a4a0ff2b474910dae3eb0ad0f920f81691140396c70d41ea93f006be85.png)

## Vox Imperante

Prerequisite: Seducere .

Issues a short, sharp command (no more than three words in length) that causes one or more targets to immediately stop what they are doing and obey the caster's command without thinking. The caster must be able to see the target(s) and the target(s) must be able to hear the caster for this spell to work. With this spell, the caster could command the target(s) to:

- ' Stop! '
- ' Sit! '
- ' Drop it! '
- ' Kill them! '
- ' Open the door! '
- ' Go away! '
- ' Get me… [something/someone]! '

While similar to Seducere , Vox imperante enables the caster to affect groups of animals and people. It is also harder to correctly cast as it is usually only deployed in an emergency and at speed. The spell costs 6 magic points and can affect up to four targets (people and/or animals). When targeted at named NPCs or player characters, those characters make a POW roll to resist its effects, while non-named NPCs and animals are automatically affected.

If targeting a non-practitioner who is a named character or investigator, the outcomes of the POW roll to resist the spell are as follows:

- Fumble or failure: they do as they are bid without question, nor do they ever think to question why they acted as they did.
- Critical success: they are unaffected by the spell and react to the request as though asked by another person. They are not aware of anything magical having taken place.
- Regular or Hard success: they do as they are bid without question, but later they perhaps wonder what possessed them to act the way they did. Of course, they soon console themselves with any excuse that feels rational to them.

Whereas,  if  targeting  a  practitioner  who  is  a  named character or investigator, the outcomes of the POW roll to resist the spell are as follows:

- Fumble: they behave exactly as bidden. They are unaware of the use of the spell.
- Any level of success: they are aware of the use of Vox imperante but are not compelled to act upon it.
- Failure: they begin to act as requested, but then recover their self-control. They are aware of the use of the spell.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000475_1a975176802ee48aa1155cf3fe25bac2a81ffa7ede8b406adf31a9529bdc21e9.png)

Boost: by increasing the magic point spend, the effect can be increased:

- +1 magic point (total cost of 7 magic points) : extends the effect to groups of eight targets.
- +3 magic points (total cost of 9 magic points) : extends the effect to groups of 32 targets.
- +2 magic points (total cost of 8 magic points) : extends the effect to groups of 16 targets.

There's a spell called Vox imperante . To do it you've got to string together six forma and a dozen inflectores with absolute precision. -Cry Fox

## GM ADVICE

The phrase 'named NPC' is used to refer to key nonplayer characters in the game, such as the characters described  in Chapter  6:  A  Rogues'  Gallery ;  this also includes key antagonists you create for your case files.

## GM ADVICE

As with Seducere (page 190), if an investigator is the target of the spell, they can spend Luck to overcome a failed POW roll ( Spending Luck , page 113). The player could  also  choose  to  push  the  roll-represented by  them  struggling  to  maintain  their  self-control and resist the command-though a second failure would  leave  the  investigator  completely  at  the caster's mercy.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000476_82fe7087ab4670c2d67044fbba8e1e7876d2b0f80251816d2ba415b332613ab3.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000477_953ab032f8294005224e22633c60ba540ee5c5c8b59adbae28bb1c9c1b270169.png)

Artemis Dante no

REFUNDS!

## WORKING TOGETHER FOR STRANGER LONDON

KHOCK

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000478_5639ec675d47735a404aee4797761fd8048f20a4aa57a975889f73d8d25c7208.png)

## Bringing the Rivers of London to Life

AT ITS HEART, the Rivers  of  London universe  revolves around the interactions between two very different worlds: one  of  magic  and  fantasy  and  one  of  everyday  humdrum reality. For games based in Britain's capital city, those worlds are represented by London's demi-monde, particularly Mama  Thames  and  her  daughters,  and  its  agents  of  law enforcement, the Metropolitan Police Service (MPS). While their interactions may frequently be fraught with difficulty and  misunderstanding,  they  are  fundamental  to  creating a  rich  and  engaging  atmosphere  within  your  game.  Thus, you, the Game Moderator (GM), must learn to portray the mundane along with the mystical.

With  that  in  mind,  the  first  half  of  this  chapter  aims to  provide  background  details  on  various  aspects  of  law enforcement in Britain, as well as details on how criminal investigations  are  carried  out  in  the  real  world.  While primarily written for the GM, parts of this information may be of use to players who are unfamiliar with British police procedures and fictional crime dramas. We've also provided Table 12:  A  Glossary  of  Common  Police  Acronyms  and Slang (page 214) to help clarify some law enforcement jargon.

The  second  half  of  this  chapter  is  full  of  hints  and  tips for GMs, including helping players immerse themselves in the world so they become a vibrant part of the stories you tell together.

## GM ADVICE

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000479_945dded08ec74682e7e4c461ba28bace2ffb7715599dfe64eacc91147ba1dbe3.png)

## Law Enforcement in London

This  section  is  intended  to  give  GMs  and  their  groups an  understanding  of  what  playing  as  a  member  of  the Metropolitan Police Service (also known as the Met or 'the Old Bill') would be like. It's not exhaustive by any means, and what your in-game version of the Met looks like is up to you. If you would like a deeper understanding of British policing  or  ideas  on  how  to  utilise  the  police  in  a  more realistic  fashion,  see Appendix C for  a  selection  of  books and websites that could help you do just that.

There is no one police force governing all the UK. Each county has its own service with its own uniform and internal structure, so what goes for the Met doesn't necessarily apply elsewhere.  However,  policing  in  Britain,  and  therefore London, is said to be 'by consent'-i.e., with the common consent,  cooperation,  and  approval  of  the  public-based on  Robert  Peel's Nine  Principles  of  Policing (although  they probably weren't actually written by him). All police services have some commonalities, though. Each has teams of officers who  deal  with  non-emergency  and  emergency  responses, as  well  as  general  beat  duties.  They  all  have  a  Criminal Investigation Department (CID) and also specialised units.

Just  as  all  police  services  share  some  similarities,  all  police officers share some common duties. It is their job to maintain the Queen's Peace, prevent crime (or detect it once it has been committed), and to protect life and property. An officer of the law must be prepared both physically and mentally to jump into a case and hit the ground running, no matter what that case may be.

While  the  information  provided  in  the  first  half  of this  chapter  is  meant  to  give  you  an  understanding of how things work in the real world, don't feel that you have to slavishly include every element of it in your  Rivers  of  London  game.  It's  here  to  help  you 'sell' the setting to your players. Remember, even the most accurate TV dramas and crime novels still take some creative licence to make their stories work, so pick and choose what supports your version of the world and ignore the rest.

## GM ADVICE

Something  to  bear  in  mind  is  that RoL:RPG  is  set around  the  time  of  False  Value  (2016).  If  you  want to  run  a  more  contemporary  game,  be  aware  that the Met has undergone massive infrastructure rearrangements  since  2017  and  that  some  of  the departments and organisations mentioned in this book may no longer exist.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000480_0d39041121641e585099533766fc85f292bd8a45a6784087d6080ffb575f9a44.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000481_c8662767223e8ea4db6f8cfce75853e8106e72b8041ea222528a76008ebe6dff.png)

5

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000482_256a222493103edc2282af8f50637ee1c4761014f5278298107c532d1f4d15ef.png)

## Policing Issues

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000483_29bc80f275a8c53406d8a13736869c3d627afd18b25518c46ea4ba48471ac48f.png)

In  an  ideal  world,  all  police  officers would  be  infallible  upholders  of  the law  for  every  section  of  society,  but in the real world this has proven time and  again  not  to  be  the  case.  While there  has  long  been  official  scrutiny regarding the actions of its officers, the state  of  British  law  enforcement  was acknowledged  to  be  deeply  problematical  by  the  1970s  and  1980s.  A  huge push  was  made  to  address  problems and improve  professionalism  within the force as a whole, but there are still ongoing  issues  with  regard  to  institutionalised  racism,  discrimination,  and wrongdoing.

The Rivers of London novels acknowledge that the British police are not always the shining paragons of law enforcement that they could or should be. For example, Peter Grant has been subject to racial abuse from so-called colleagues and profiling by fellow officers while out of uniform. Many police forces  across  the  world  have  been accused,  to  varying  degrees,  of  racial or religious profiling when it comes to identifying  suspects,  with  some  officers  focusing  purely  on  a  particular ethnicity or religion when it comes to the  investigation  of  certain  types  of crime. How much of these issues you wish to portray in your game is up to each individual group and is something the GM should discuss with their players. Some groups may wish to explore police  discrimination  as  part  of  their game, while others may not. See also Considerate Gaming (page 44) and It's Not 'Gritty' (page 218).

## A General Overview of the Metropolitan Police

Up until the early 1800s, most towns and cities were protected by  local  law  enforcement  agents  hired  by  their  individual civic  authorities.  There  was  no  government involvement in policing-well, not directly, at least. These constables were paid by their communities to keep the peace and patrol their neighbourhoods; those who patrolled after dark were known as Night Watchmen.

By the 1800s, London was protected by about 450 constables and  4,500  Night  Watchmen,  each  working  independently within their communities with very little interaction between them.  But,  the  nature  of  law  enforcement  was  changing. Having  created  the  first  centrally-organised  police  force  in the world in Ireland in 1804, Robert Peel brought his ideas to England's capital and, as a result, the Metropolitan Police Service (Met or MPS) came into being in 1829.

Peel's  Met  was  a  full-time,  centrally-organised  police force responsible for the care of the Greater London Area. From its then headquarters in Whitehall, the Met brought a  series  of  professional  standards to the job, as well as the iconic  uniform  and  'custodian'  helmet.  The  nicknames  of 'Peelers' and 'bobbies' were coined in honour of their creator. From London, the idea spread out across England, Wales, and Scotland (though Glasgow had already formed its first professional police force in 1800).

## GM ADVICE

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000484_84f4a80bf47758c4ecc782c9256b55bfcc33add71710a558060aba0ff5c9cf6a.png)

As  odd  as  it  may  sound,  the  City  of  London,  the so-called  'Square  Mile,'  has  its  own  police  force, founded  in  1839.  It  is  separate  from,  but  works alongside, the Met. Then there's the British Transport Police  (BTP),  a  national  force  founded  in  1949-and the law enforcement agency Sgt Jaget Kumar works for.  The  BTP  has  jurisdiction  over  the  national  rail network,  and  within  the  capital  is  responsible  for policing the London Underground and all the city's overground rail services.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000485_9ce0fab9422623e6ad8ffc9368d8435a88e80c4f53b956f98948e0a048ecd3af.png)

In  2016,  there  are  over  40,000  members  of  the  MPS, from constables to support officers and police staff (civilian workers), who work within the boroughs of London to keep the city and its people safe from harm-both mundane and magical, when it come to the Rivers of London .  Its  current headquarters  in  New  Scotland  Yard,  near  the  Victoria Embankment, act as both the bastion of professional British policing and as a museum of crime memorabilia.

The bulk of the Met at the time of Lies Sleeping and False Value is  divided  into  units  called  Borough  Operational Command  Units  (BOCU),  with  one  for  each  of  the  32 London Boroughs. Each unit has its own Met force, working within their respective borough under a Chief Superintendent. Boroughs can request help from one another when it comes

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000486_d7679bc599897518691f464aa2dce0442e32556d37e95d145470a355b4af42f4.png)

to serious crimes such as murder, and the location where a crime takes place determines which BOCU has jurisdiction over the resulting investigation.

The  Met's  specialised  units  include  the  Dog  Support Unit  and  the  Mounted  Branch,  among  others,  as  well as  the  Specialist  Crime  Directorate,  which  contains  the Economic  and  Specialist  Crime  team-the  division  the Special Assessment Unit (SAU; aka Operation Falcon, i.e., the  Folly)  'officially'  belongs  to  (not  that  it's  a  matter  of public record, of course). The Folly also maintains a degree of independence from its parent department, and operates as its own Operational Command Unit (OCU).

## GM ADVICE

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000487_55cb37bc75f8b7320f3cecaa0715c87bc2855c6626769f0cdd43cedadcc0538b.png)

From 2017, the 32 BOCUs are reorganised into 12 BCUs (Basic  Command  Units),  each  containing  from  two to four boroughs each. There's a lot of cooperation between  the  BOCUs/BCUs  and  the  specialist  units when it  comes  to  crime,  and  officers  who  are  part of the Special Assessment Unit can go pretty much anywhere they need to while pursuing a case. Given how small the Folly's police team is, your investigators will  be  cooperating  with  other  units  as  a  matter  of course. This is rather handy, as it means you can leave all the drudgery of police work-the paperwork, the routine inquiries, and so on-offscreen in the hands of  uniforms  or  specialists  so  that  it  doesn't  bog down the action of the game.

## Ranks

There are a number of ranks within the Metropolitan Police Service. Each of them, up to Chief Superintendent, can add the  prefix  'Detective'  to  distinguish  those  officers  from their uniformed counterparts. While uniformed officers are involved in day-to-day (operational) policing, detectives are tasked  with  investigating  serious  crimes,  such  as  murder. For most games set in London, this means officers from the Criminal Investigation Department (CID), Special Branch (who deal with matters of national security and intelligence), or the Folly.

## Constable

Abbreviation: PC  (Police  Constable)  or  DC  (Detective  Constable). Examples: PC Judith Hua, DC Peter Grant.

The lowest rank, representing by far the largest proportion of the Met's workforce. This rank includes new recruits who have just finished their initial month of training. After the trainees have made their attestation in front of a magistrate (i.e.,  they  are  sworn  in)  and  received  their Warrant Card (page 211), they embark on a two-year probationary period. Once their  probation  is  complete,  a  constable  may  choose to stay in that role or apply to join a specialised unit. When additional  staffing  is  needed  at  a  location,  constables  are called in. As first responders, constables are also often the first police officers to arrive at the scene of a crime.

A  constable  answers  to  a  sergeant  or  directly  to  an inspector,  depending  on  the  type  of  duty  they  have  been assigned. Met officers tend to travel in pairs. Patrols can be on foot (often for purposes of visibility), but there is usually a  mobile  unit  (i.e.,  a  police  car,  officially  referred  to  as  an Incident Response Vehicle or IRV) nearby to offer backup.

## GM ADVICE

Slightly  confusingly,  every  attested  police  officer is  classed  as  a  'constable'  in  the  eyes  of  the  law, regardless  of  whatever  other  rank  they  hold.  This is because it's the constable's attestation that gives police  officers  the  powers  of  search  and  arrest mandated  by  the  Police  and  Criminal  Evidence Act  1984  (PACE),  i.e.,  their  ability  to  uphold  the Queen's Peace.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000488_56028b97ebb25c74a5f3b5cc8175f08c830053b69c208007e2145de1b697cbb3.png)

## Sergeant

Abbreviation: Sgt (uniformed) or DS (Detective Sergeant). Examples: Sgt William Daverc, DS Sahra Guleed.

This  is  the  first  of  the  supervisory  ranks,  often  colloquially referred to as a 'skipper.' Sergeants act as team leaders for the constables under their command and as sources of information. It's often a sergeant who delivers the daily briefing and assigns duties to their officers. One sergeant is usually responsible for up to five constables. Most officers become sergeants within the first five to ten years of their career.

## Inspector

Abbreviation: Insp (uniformed) or DI (Detective Inspector). Examples: Insp Francis Neblett, DI Miriam Stephanopoulous.

The  second  supervisory  rank,  inspectors  are  on  call  24/7. Uniformed  inspectors  are,  like  their  sergeants,  involved in  organising  and  overseeing  operational  policing,  while Detective Inspectors are often the ones portrayed as 'police detectives' in crime fiction, e.g., Miriam Stephanopoulos (page  238).  DIs  are  authorised  to  conduct  and  manage investigations into serious criminal activity and, within the Met, are usually tied to a specific department dealing with a particular type of crime. Detective Inspector is the lowest rank that can become a Senior Investigating Officer (SIO), though for crimes such as murder, the SIO is likely to be of a higher rank, such as a Detective Chief Inspector. DIs and above can also use their own cars for work purposes, rather than one from the police pool.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000489_66c560777bdeaeb1eb01db4f90e208174486a96d566283d16d929ed3a36931d6.png)

## Chief Inspector

Abbreviation: Ch  Insp  (uniformed)  or  DCI  (Detective Chief Inspector).

Examples: DCI Alexander Seawoll, DCI Thomas Nightingale.

5

A Chief Inspector is usually the leader of a team of police officers.  In  the  case  of  major  criminal  investigations,  a Detective Chief Inspector assigns work, authorises queries, and speaks to the Superintendent and above for permission to  use  certain  specialised  units,  such  as  forensics  or  the Specialist Firearms Command (SCO19)-or the Folly. They decide the overall focus of the case under investigation and are the person you answer to when things go wrong. They, in  turn,  must  answer  to  their  higher-ups  if  they  decide  to ' go  above  the  law.'  There are many famous fictional DCIs, including the Folly's own Thomas Nightingale (page 233). Rising up to Chief Inspector can take 15-20 years, although it can be fast-tracked.

## GM ADVICE

Keep  in  mind  that,  while  other  Chief  Inspectors exist  within  the  Met  (around  300  of  them),  your investigators will mostly be dealing with DCI Nightingale. Any other DCIs they meet depends on the types of cases on which they consult.
