## Game Moderator Advice

The Commissioner gave a mirthless chuckle. 'Does he know what he's getting himself into?'

'Does any copper?' asked Nightingale.

'Very well,' said the Commissioner. 'On your feet, son.'

We stood. Nightingale told me to raise my hand and read me the oath:  'Do  you,  Peter  Grant  of  Kentish  Town,  swear  to  be  true to our sovereign Queen and her heirs. And well and truly serve your Master for the term of your apprenticehood. And ye shall be in obedience to all the wardens and clothing of that fellowship. In reverence of the secret of the said fellowship ye shall keep and give no information to any man but of the said fellowship. And in all these  things  ye  shall  well  and  truly  behave  yourself  and  secretly keep this oath to your power, so help you God, your Sovereign and the power that set the universe in motion.'

I so swore, although I did almost stumble over the clothing bit.

'So help you God,' said the Commissioner.

-Rivers of London

## The GM's Role: an Overview

Taking on the role of Game Moderator (GM) can be a little daunting,  especially  if  you  are  new  to  roleplaying  games (RPGs).  The  following  sections  exist  to  give  you  a  deeper understanding of what's involved, as well as giving you some tools and ideas to help you along. If you need encouragement, we hope you find it here!

RoL:RPG , like all other RPGs, is a collaborative storytelling  game  that  aims  to  be  enjoyable  for  everyone taking  part.  Although,  as  GM,  you  have  a  very  different role  during  the  game  than  your  players,  you  are  not  their opponent,  as  you  might  be  in  a  traditional  boardgame. Though  your  non-player  characters  (NPCs)  might  oppose the players' investigators at times, it is not your job to outwit them, nor the players' job to 'beat' you-you are, ultimately, all on the same side. You work together as co-creators of each case file (also known as an adventure or scenario), telling a shared story.

The first part of your role involves reading this rulebook so  that  you  understand  how  the  game  works.  You  don't have to memorise everything before you begin playing, but you might want to make a few notes to help you remember the important parts. Appendix B is  a  useful  resource, as it

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000562_19aee62dad90918c30497a6a354c140354b149572dd6d7d6f902c92d8062d626.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000563_d538ec87b10b22d733c39f300ebe3bc22361ed08cfe5a114c2bbee9ae2a20aa6.png)

contains  quick-reference  rules  summaries  to  use  during  a game, or  to  print  out  and  share  with  your  players  to  help them know what to do, too.

The  next  part  of  your  role  is  to  write  a  case  file-or familiarise yourself with a pre-written one. We recommend you  use  the  pre-written  case  file  in  this  book  first,  before designing your own. But, when writing your own adventure, it  should be engaging, a little challenging, immersive, and in keeping with the Rivers of London setting. Your case file needs to be logically coherent. The game rules help you with this, but the rules are not the most important aspect. Nor are rules the final arbiter of what happens in your game: you are . A GM is far closer to an enabler than a tyrant. Your task is to guide the investigators through the plot by giving them the clues they need to solve the case, and so help them to reach a successful outcome.

Your  players'  role,  on  the  other  hand,  is  to  undertake that  investigation  willingly  and  with  focus.  They  need  to keep  notes  of  clues,  ask  questions,  and  proactively  follow up lines of enquiry, all while roleplaying their investigators. Players are not meant to be an awestruck audience to your storytelling prowess, and they are not there to play secondfiddle to much more powerful NPCs, such as Nightingale. The players and their investigators are the stars of the show and should be centre stage in the story.

As the GM, you have a head start, having done all your pregame preparation like reading the case file and preparing some notes to help you remember its key plot points. You are-hopefully-enthused  and  engaged  before  you  even sit  down  with  your  players.  So,  the  next  part  of  your  role is to lift your players up to that level, to get them to invest emotionally in their investigators, in the fictional world, and in solving each case.

## GM ADVICE

every now and again, you might need to arbitrate between players or make a final call on how a rule is being used in a particular situation.

Being a GM might sound like a lot to ask-but there's a tremendous reward and satisfaction in GMing a good game. Remember, be fair. Act from a place of goodwill. Do your best. Finally, don't forget that this is just a game, and all of you are supposed to be having fun.

## Tone of Play

'What  did  you  sense  in  there?'  asked  Nightingale  as  we  walked back to the Jag.

' As in vestigium ?'

' Vestigium is the singular, vestigia is the plural,' said Nightingale. 'Did you sense vestigia ?'

'To be honest,' I said, 'nothing. Not even a vestige.' -Rivers of London

Each RoL:RPG case file is, at heart, a detective investigation. This genre takes many fictional forms, from 'Cosy Crime' to the darkest, most harrowing 'Modern Noir.' So, if you want to stay true to the books, it's important to be aware of tone.

## It's Not 'Gritty'

The Rivers of London books take place in an idealised version of  reality.  There  is  violence-and  murder-but  the  gory details are passed over lightly, and characters tend to shrug off experiences that would, in real life, hurt them emotionally. Nobody  quits  the  job  due  to  grinding  stress,  and  it  takes seven  novels  before  Peter  Grant  even  admits  that  posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) might be an occupational risk 15 . Sexism and racism are certainly flagged up as existing, but are not dwelt upon. The Metropolitan Police Service is a  meritocratic  equal-opportunities  bunch  just  trying  to  do the  right  thing.  Corruption,  brutality,  and  systemic  racial, homophobic, and misogynistic bullying (both internal and external) that might come to light in the real world are, in the fiction, largely under control, so that the police are, above all, professionals.

You may be feeling a little nervous if this is the first time  you've  run  a  game  for  anyone.  Don't  worry. You've  got  this.  No  one  is  expecting  you  to  be perfect-we  all  make  mistakes  when  we're  GMing, but  we  learn  from  them.  The  more  you  play,  the more experienced you and your players will become. Rules  that  at  first  seem  complicated  will  become second  nature.  And,  in  time,  you'll  wonder  what you were so nervous about!

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000564_65f3a52b5088d4f821ae1f4b48e01412380d003bda05aefcf8da38d4a9e6274d.png)

As you can see, to be a GM, you need to be knowledgeable and quick-thinking, as well as flexible and sometimes firm. You need to be sensitive to the players' mood, both as a group and  as  individuals  ( Considerate  Gaming ,  page  44).  You also need to see that everyone gets their say, and that each investigator gets their moments of achievement and triumph, so that everyone in the team feels like they have a place. And,

Police  budgetary  resources  are  something  to  grumble about, not slashed to the point where law and order are in jeopardy.  Most  working-class  criminals  are  just  ordinary folks making a living. And the 'social work' end of police activity-attempting to keep the peace in a populace riddled with pockets of poverty, social marginalisation, alcohol abuse, drug  addiction,  and  mental  health  problems-is  almost entirely missing. So, if you want to stick to the feel of the original  books  when  using  the  London  setting,  remember the following points.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000565_543dc48b4185a1544d901ccb2f9355f744a9f15f28262331666805f9e14ed93a.png)

Footnote: 15

Lies Sleeping

- The Met are the 'Good Guys,' and the stories are told from their point of view. Individual officers may be grumpy, unhelpful, or even crooked, but this is no more than a mild impediment to the investigators. The system itself is largely benign, and the status quo is worthy of being upheld.
- Avoid plots revolving around child abuse, sexual violence, and degradation-or treat these themes with extreme caution. You should be thrilling your players, not disturbing or depressing them. The horrors of the Strip Club of Doctor Moreau are deliberately kept undescribed in the books. See Considerate Gaming (page 44) and Important: Tone (page 123).
- Punch up, not down. Peter Grant is (like most police officers) a working-class lad who really distrusts those he deems 'posh,' and assumes that they probably have something to hide. If you create a genuinely sociopathic or cruel boss villain along the lines of the Faceless Man, they should be entitled and wealthy, or not human at all. Organised Newtonian magic in Britain up until World War II was an exclusive 'gentlemen's club' and this legacy remains. The Little Crocodiles cadre of ethically-challenged magicians originated from the most privileged white, male, Oxford-University-educated social background 16 ( Little Crocodiles , page 263), and Lady Helena Linden-Limmer's matriarchal tradition 17 is upper-class, too ( La Société de la Rose , page 263).
- Your games should maintain a generally optimistic view of humanity, and an inclusive definition of what constitutes a person, with their inherent rights and dignity. The first duty of a Met police officer is to protect people, and Peter Grant, even at his most cynical, does not doubt that the general public deserve protection-even if they are not strictly 'human.' This attitude contrasts with Lesley May's, who comes to see magical beings as literal monsters 18 . Peter even stands up to Nightingale in some cases where his superior officer thinks that keeping the Queen's Peace justifies extrajudicial execution 19 .
- Treat marginalised groups with respect. Our heroes, Peter and Sahra Guleed, both come from immigrant communities. Most of London's downriver gods are Black Anglo-Nigerians. Do your cultural research and avoid stereotypes. And, make sure that NPCs of other ethnicities are as individualised and nuanced as those from your own culture.

## It's Not Horror

Of course, there are supernatural threats and monsters, and even  some  genuinely  horrific  elements-such  as  Larry  the Lark's fate 20 -but Rivers of London is  miles  away  from  the nihilism of true horror. Peter Grant narrates his adventures

Footnotes: 16, 19, 20 Moon Over Soho , 17 The Hanging Tree,

18 Lies Sleeping with a breezy, geeky wit, and although his sense of humour is sometimes dark it is always present. Our heroes are not broken loners squaring off against the uncaring Abyss. Community is a source of strength, both magical and emotional. Family ties matter to everyone. London is a home to which characters feel they belong and a place in which they have a stake. Any supernatural  entities  running  around  causing  trouble  can ultimately  be  contained,  and  many  are  functional  parts  of society-or at least amenable to negotiation.

An RoL:RPG game  should  end  with  the  case  file  being solved  satisfactorily.  Or  if  it  isn't,  this  should  just  be  a stepping stone in a longer and larger plot and, ultimately, a more important victory for the Folly.

If you are familiar with Chaosium's Call of Cthulhu RPG, it's obvious that it would be easy and fun to write crossover scenarios, using the Folly as an investigator organisation in Call of Cthulhu games or introducing Lovecraftian existential horror  into  Peter's  Grant's  London  (Ghouls  on  the  Tube? Cultists in the British Museum?). Just be aware that there will have to be a tonal shift one way or the other. Call of Cthulhu is very much a game about raging against the dying of the light. In Rivers of London , the light is not dying-or, alternatively, Rivers of London is about taking a light into dark places.

If  a  player  fails  a  pushed  roll,  the  results  need  not  be catastrophic or threatening, as they would be in a purely horror game. They may, instead, be humorous or embarrassing-for the investigator, not the player! Peter Grant doesn't stand too much on his dignity, so neither should your investigators.

···································.

## BEN SAYS

Rivers of London: the Roleplaying Game could even be described as 'Call of Cthulhu-now with added hope!'

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000566_dce00436dc890270d8fc90315f85df1cb486c7ae8ef6f03f60d83521a46834fa.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000567_e2bfe5b29ded14ebe1854e65551c3c93f70fa8ef4590a118c44667608199438b.png)

5

## Spirit of Place

Erasmus  Wolfe  wrote  extensively  about genii  locorum in  his ground-breaking and-at two thousand pages-wrist-breaking Exotica .  He theorised that there was an upper limit to the size and  power  of  an  individual genius  loci … None  of  the  really huge rivers of  Europe-the Volga, the Danube, or the Rhineappeared  to  possess  a  single  tutelary  deity.  Instead  there  were Rhine Maidens, plural, a French and a German Mosel, and at least ten recorded gods and goddesses of the Don. -Lies Sleeping

In  this  game,  setting  and  locality  is  fundamental.  The Rivers  of  London universe  is  an  animist  one,  not  a  theistic one: the gods don't create the material world, they emanate from it. Supernatural (i.e., magical) entities abound, but are focused  on  small  areas  of  operation.  The  river  'gods'  like Mama Thames are merely top-end genii locorum -localised magical emanations-and are not personifications of abstract qualities. Note that some of them take serious exception to the very label 'god' and prefer 'spirit' or ' orisa .'

No  deities above this scale exist. This means that your  players  should  not  be  running  into  angels-even  in Islington-devils,  or  gods  like  Mars  or  Thor,  although  it would certainly be possible to encounter a local entity that pretends to be Mars, or even thinks it is him. For example, in one novel we meet the Isis of the Walbrook 21 , but this is not the 'universal' Great Goddess Isis-she is a local river spirit who manifests in Isis-style due to her particular history as a half-Egyptian human.

## Running a Game

The following sections aim to give you the tools you'll need to set up and run a successful Rivers of London game, including information on running published case files and the possible effects of Player Group Size (page 222), as well as discussing Player Agency (page 221).

## Running a Published Case File

By far, the easiest way to start your journey as an RoL:RPG GM is to use a published, ready-to-play case file, such as the one in this book ( The Bookshop , page 285). If this is your first time as a GM, congratulations! As we mentioned earlier, don't worry about getting everything perfect the first time, because there is no such thing as 'perfect.' Every GM has their own individual style, and it might take you a little while to settle into yours. You'll probably be a bit shaky on the rules at  first,  or  even  make  some  decisions  that  you  later  think better of, but none of this should stop you and your gaming group from having fun as you learn the ropes together. Here are some tips that might help.

## ADDITIONAL RULES

Pointers on creating your own adventures can be found in the Writing a Case File section on page 336.

## Be Prepared

If you're nervous about thinking on your feet, or you tend to go blank under pressure, advanced preparedness is what will save you. Make notes and take some time to think ahead as to what might happen in the game. For example, if you think a fight scene is likely, read up on the combat rules to remind yourself  of  the  key  points.  If  you  have  a  meeting  with  an important NPC coming up, jot down a short list of the key information they are going to tell the investigators.

## Use Bookmarks

Until it becomes second nature to find the right rules, tables, etc., bookmarks or sticky notes used as tabs help enormously when  flipping  backwards  and  forwards  through  the  rules. The rule summaries at the back of this book are designed to be copied or printed out as aide-mémoires.

## Read Through the Case File Before Game Day

Read  over  the  adventure  ideally  twice,  if  not  three  times. On the first read-through, you are trying to get an overall picture  of  the  shape  of  the  scenario,  the  personalities  of any major NPCs, the moments of crisis or action when the investigators might be under threat, and the game's pacing. Is it action-oriented or more puzzle-solving in theme? What is  the  atmosphere or tone? Spooky? Crime-caper? Violent? And does  this  change  from  scene  to  scene?  Is  it  likely  to be  one  session  long,  or  more-and  if  so,  where  would  be a  reasonable  break  point?  Will  the  investigators'  actions drive the plot or will you be actively intervening with, say, a villainous counterattack? How does the case file end, and what counts as a 'success' in this particular instance?

The second read-through is when you start taking notes, whether pencilling in the margins or using a notebook. Some GMs  literally  write  out  all  their  notes  about  a  published scenario on prompt cards. Others simply underline the vital details,  maybe using different highlighter pens to pick out skill  rolls,  clues,  important dialogue, etc. It's a question of finding what works for you. You don't need to carry every detail of a case file in your head, but the information needs to be there at your fingertips. Some GMs make a note of page numbers where they'll find key information and use these to navigate when running the game.

While  you're  reading,  ask  yourself:  are  you  happy  with the case file as written or can you think of little tweaks that will suit your purposes better? Check that the way the clues link together makes sense to you and that they'll be clear to your players-remember, they have to be able to solve the

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000568_df7d00e5eaac3c5e82227a54349e2a5e0ebce4f55aaf5819c1894d41ec470f47.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000569_7cc41ed349cc524dbba6e27e1591815ce75d36da94ec6b9af27086738b266abd.png)

case! Clarify clues and add details or incidents if you feel it's needed. If you think your players might miss a clue or if you can't see a logical reason why a clue should be where it is, put it somewhere else (having the same clue appear in different places  is  a  useful  tip  for  these  kind  of  games).  There's  no requirement to stick to what's written if adapting it suits you, your ongoing campaign, or your players, better.

## Go on the Internet

## and Look Up Real Places Used As Settings In the Scenario

Find out what those places look like-it really helps you to describe a location to your players. There are more suggestions about  making  the  most  of  internet  resources  later  in  this chapter, under Props (page 226) and Using Tech (page 227).

## Q&amp;A

Some published scenarios contain a lot of descriptive text that you can either read out verbatim or paraphrase, but players often get a better mental grasp of, for example, a crime scene, if they can ask questions, rather than trying to absorb an infodump all at once. Keep initial descriptions brief and paint your picture in broad strokes, ready for follow-up Q&amp;As. As long as you have a clear image of the scene in your head, you can answer those questions or make incidental details up on the fly, bringing the location to life. Thus, set the scene and then get your players to ask you about the details.

## Who's Where?

Combat  in RoL:RPG is  narrative-driven  rather  than  mapbased; this is not meant to be a wargame. But, in a hand-tohand (melee) combat, things can go more smoothly if both you and the players are clear on the relative locations of any investigators  and  NPCs  present,  as  well  as  environmental hazards like stairwells and lifts (elevators). It can cause upset and  frustration  if  you  and  a  player  have  different  mental pictures and are talking at cross-purposes, so using a sketch map that  everyone  can  see  to  keep  track  of  who  is  where, and  who  they're  fighting,  can  be  very  helpful.  You  don't need painted gaming miniatures (though many people love them)-chess pieces, tiddlywinks, coins, buttons, or similar will do just fine. Virtual gaming platforms can also be really useful  for  displaying  where  everyone  is  during  combat  if playing online. See also Distances in Combat (page 127).

## NPCs

Keep  a  list  of  random  names  that  can  be  used  for  minor NPCs,  should  the  players  ask  (because  they  will).  This  is just  a  list  of  names  you  can  use  as  necessary.  Profiles  for important NPCs will be included in any published case file. If  an  unexpected situation arises and you have to make up NPC stats (characteristics, skills, etc.) on the fly for opposed skill rolls, the main thing to remember is that expert skills start at 60%, while common and combat skills start at 30%. Just set them at a level appropriate to the NPC's job and/or their role in your story. If ever in doubt, go with 50%.

## Backstories

Be ready to make use of investigator backstories. During Step Five: Create a Backstory (page 56), if players invent family members, or particular social circumstances, take note-you can use these later as plot hooks or sources of information. See also Friends and Family (page 59).

## Player Agency

'Does this happen a lot?' asked Caroline.

'Nope,' I said. 'Sometimes Beverley rescues me, sometimes Lady Ty, occasionally Molly-I think there's a rota.'

-The Hanging Tree

Always  remember,  your  players  are  taking  part in an interactive  game,  not  reading  a  novel  or  listening  to  a podcast,  so  their  choices  must  have  an  effect  on  how  the plot flows. This includes actions taken by their investigators via  the  game  rules-e.g.,  what  skill  rolls  they  make-and the players' own  creative  contributions,  e.g.,  deduction, roleplaying, intuitive leaps, crazy ideas that are too good for the GM to resist, and so on.

When  your  players  first  build  their  investigators,  give guidance as to what sort of group will work best with the games you have in mind. Encourage them to specialise their skills  so  they  aren't  all  competing  to  be  the  one  making the  History  roll  or  stun  gunning  the  mad  axeman.  These discussions can, and should, form part of your Session Zero

## ( Creating Your Character , page 44).

Every player should, ideally, contribute to how the story develops in every game session, or they may lose interest. So, when you are running your sessions, make sure to include skill rolls from a variety of disciplines. You might also find that you have one or two players who do all the talking and who tend to make all the decisions. Be alert to quiet players and make sure you engage them. Ask open-ended questions, for example: ' What are you thinking, DC Smith? ' ' What would you like to look into next? ' ' What's your next step, do you think? '

If it all hits the fan and NPC backup has to be called in for the climactic showdown, make sure your players still have something to do. Keep the players front-of-stage with skill rolls and decision-making, while what the NPCs are doing forms  a  dramatic  background  to  the  investigators'  actions. Even  an  elderly  historian  can  play  lookout  and  make  that crucial Observation roll when a villain is trying to sneak out the back, or get into a fist-fight with a minion!

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000570_f839026405fdab3eb9fb546b80bbd269f19868370f9a81dd36f746c5eb107dc5.png)

5

## Potential Problems

RoL:RPG potentially  has  a  twofold  problem  with  player agency.  First,  law  enforcement  investigators  work  within the  police  hierarchy  and  must  adhere  to  the  law  of  the land,  at  least  when  under  public  scrutiny.  Helpfully,  since the investigators are members of a magical taskforce whose existence  must  be  kept  out  of  the  press,  they  have  a  lot more leeway than other officers when it comes to breaking procedure-and even the law-if the end justifies the means. Most of the time, they are expected to deal with demi-monde antagonists in situ , rather than try dragging them into court. Thus, the Powers That Be tend to cover up for investigators, unless they become more of a liability than an asset ( Police Oversight , page 214).

Two major considerations for the investigators are discretion and obeying the chain of command. It's clear that extra-judicial executions by Folly operatives are sanctioned if  the  threat  is  deemed  high  enough  (e.g.,  vampires 22 ),  but they are carried out secretly. Investigators will not get away with initiating gunfire on someone in a crowded street, but persuading  Nightingale  to  call  in  Frank  Caffrey's  snipers? That's a different kettle of fish…

## GM ADVICE

If  some of your investigators are police officers and some are civilians, their working relationship needs to  be  established  right  from  the  start.  It  won't  be much fun for civilian investigators if they're always taking orders or being told to wait safely at the back in dangerous situations-just see how Abigail resents Peter  doing  that!  This  is  a  case  where  gameplay trumps realism. Give your civilian investigators autonomy and treat them as equals on the team if they want to step up and take risks, even in situations where realistically speaking they would be sidelined. If conflict arises between officers and civilians due to legitimately differing goals and worldviews, use it as a roleplaying opportunity.

The whole premise of the game supports the idea that  police  and  civilian  investigators  are  working together as a team to solve cases. No, it's not quite like that in the real world, but the real world doesn't have  water  spirits  either!  So  go  with  the  fiction, embrace the setting inspired by Ben's novels, and don't worry about simulating real life.

But, when it comes to high-level practitioners, the most difficult for the GM to deal with in a London-based game is not any villainous opponent but the investigators' guvnor, Nightingale; and, to a lesser extent, Peter Grant-if you're using him as an NPC. Most senior police officers in crime fiction are grumpy stuffed shirts who exist in a relationship of mutual annoyance with the protagonist, with the put-upon underling  solving  the  mystery  and  earning  their  superior's grudging  acclaim.  Nightingale,  however,  does  not  fit  this mould. He is handsome, clever, and immensely powerful; a literal war hero with a tragic past who gets younger and more athletic  with  time.  His  only  major  flaw  is  that  he's  out  of touch with modern technology. If you are not careful, your players may give up and call Nightingale in every time things get  difficult  for  them,  or  start  to  see  themselves  as  office gophers for the real hero.

The answer to this is make sure you keep Nightingale in the background as much as possible. He might have to be busy on important business as the case progresses, maybe somewhere really far off like Aberdeen or continental Europe. He should be available on the phone to explain pertinent details, dispense advice, or ask critical questions, but even this should not be an automatic fall back, as practitioners often have to turn their phones off. Make the players treat him as a resource, not as the solution. If you have to, critically injuring him is an option; it's exactly what pushes Peter into tackling Mr Punch on his own 24 .  Reducing  his  mobility,  temporarily  or  permanently, goes a long way towards making sure the investigators are the ones in the front line. The same goes for Peter.

## Player Group Size

… writing the ever-expanding Folly Expansion document-now incorporating  the  lessons  learnt  from  Operation  Jennifer.  The principal one being that we needed to maintain a complement of at least six new magical practitioners, and that was only counting the ones that were also police officers.

-Lies Sleeping

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000571_86de85295fa935298cec7f250f62250bccdb7d7e4c0a8f72bb468b4ed85ef42d.png)

Secondly, there's the problem of practitioners who simply outclass the investigators, at least initially. High-level wizards exist,  but  you  don't  have  to  bring  them  in  as  antagonists until the investigators are ready for them. If they are human criminals, they'll certainly be trying to fly under Nightingale's radar  as  they  go  about  their  nefarious  business.  Seducing investigators away from the side of Law, as the Faceless Man does 23 ,  is  a  strategy that could pay them bigger dividends in the long term than any all-out attack on the Folly ever would.

The number of players in your game makes a big difference, and  you  should  only  GM  for  a  group  size  that  you  are comfortable with-and if you're new to GMing, smaller is better, at least until you get the hang of things. RoL:RPG is designed to work best with 2-4 players and a GM. But it can be played with more (we suggest no more than six players plus the GM), or it can be run as a one-to-one investigation, where the GM guides one player through a case (with some extra, helpful NPCs to assist when needed).

Generally speaking, it is much easier to cater for a small group  of  players  than  a  large  one.  You  can  pay  individual attention  to  each  investigator  more  easily,  and  combat,  in particular, flows more swiftly. You'll find the whole gaming experience more relaxed with a smaller group.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000572_5d30ede9080246ff6f9b62f6f7f5d1750e657fb6df42921e068ca1bdc4d082df.png)

Footnote:

24 Rivers of London

The game is written under the assumption that, in a small group, at least one-and possibly all-the investigators are law enforcement officers and, in a London game, it's probable they were seconded to the Folly through normal routes, either after their two-year probationary period in uniform or as a sideways move later  in  their  careers.  They've  been  taught  some  basic Newtonian magic and are ready to tackle cases on their own with minimal supervision. A small, police-focused group like this makes for a tighter, more investigatory style of gameplay.

Not every player wants to take the role of a police officer, so you may have a more diverse collection of investigators. In a larger group (four or more players), in particular, there are definite benefits to having civilian investigators in terms of skill diversity and roleplaying flexibility. Of course, there's nothing stopping you from having all the players take on non-police investigators; perhaps they are loosely attached to the Folly or are acting completely independent of Nightingale and co.

Some NPC police roles might make good investigators, but realistically  speaking,  forensic  medical  examiners  (formerly known as police surgeons) and forensic specialists are likely to be office-bound and their official roles too tightly demarcated to get involved in stakeouts, combat, and general adventuring shenanigans. Civilian consultants, like archivist Harold Postmartin and wildcard investigators like Abigail Kamara, are a better bet for flexibility. They can go places off-limits to the police and get into all sorts of interesting trouble. Be extremely careful allowing investigators who are heavily armed-such as Frank Caffrey's ex-Para firefighters-in a game set anywhere in the UK, as they are liable to upset the balance of the party and cause a lot of legal problems for the Folly.

## ADDITIONAL RULES

The Folly doesn't have to be limited to London, if you don't want it to be. For advice on how to expand the Folly's  operations  to  another  location  in  the  UK, see Your Local Folly? (page 317).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000573_b2cc16d2f82b4030960c49e522e7ee689488f86a82e72100310788a6aae4143f.png)

The following civilian expertise could be brought in to assist with a case:

- Consulting Detectives: (Occupation: Private Investigator) Fictional examples of the consulting detective abound. They tend to be employed when the police are desperate for ideas and are a pretty good fit for the Folly.
- 'Psychics' or Mediums: (Occupation: any, but Entertainer if professional) More common in the USA, but reallife British examples have occurred, with somewhat underwhelming results. In an RoL:RPG setting, they are not so risible, and are a reasonable way for a hedge witch to be co-opted by the Folly.
- that they are far more effective and accurate in crime fiction than in real life!
- Scientific Consultants: (Occupation: Doctor of Medicine, Lecturer) In real life, they are unlikely to step out of their laboratory to chase perps, but fictional settings allow them more freedom.
- Criminal Underworld Contacts: (Occupation: Criminal) May be an informant, also known as a grass, snitch or-more formally-a Covert Human Intelligence Source. A risky role. Note that a contact in the criminal underworld might also be an undercover police officer, but this puts that investigator at double the jeopardy from the people they are trying to blend in with.
- Community Consultants: (Occupation: any, but suits Chancer) Remember, there are many fae of varying levels of magical ability and knowledge in London's demi-monde; they would make excellent Folly irregulars and interesting characters to play-see Playing Fae Investigators (page 318) for more details.
- Digital Forensics Experts: (Occupation: Computer Specialist) Specialists in tracing and retrieving digital files, hacking, photographic enhancement, etc.

In RoL:RPG ,  given  the  Folly's  recent  personnel  expansion, almost  any  occupation  might  be  taken  on  and  trained  in magic on a less 'official' basis, provided their skills, contacts, or backstory are useful and they are brought to Nightingale's attention. Historians in particular are an obvious fit. Remember, anyone could potentially have the ability to use magic. Of course, amateur sleuths who help out the forces of law and order-and always seem to be there at the right moment, somehow-are a favourite of crime writers and TV dramatists, and they include priests, stage magicians, lawyers, and sweet little old ladies.

- Criminal Psychologists/Profilers: (Occupation: Lecturer, with a specialisation in Read Person and Observation) Associated with murder cases in general and serial killer cases in particular, these are experts in the inner, mental life of different types of criminals and reading the psychological implications of physical clues, enabling them to predict their future behaviour. Note

## ADDITIONAL RULES

Further  options  for  bringing  in  larger  numbers  of investigators can be found in the Troupe Play section (page 313).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000574_88250cee2075faf6a889f886cff7279d11f697074c921981336a25cdf2dd1fe3.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000575_7d434e496fec60ea3f6d793404caed8712884ac28134b2da41c2b1ab9a2e75cf.png)

5

## One-to-One

A one player-one GM game is, in some ways, closest to the original books, which are all told from Peter Grant's point of view. The positives are that the player is always centre-stage and engaged in the action. The negatives are that it puts more pressure on them to interpret your clues and come up with clever ideas. There's also less of the back-and-forth bounce of energy that a group of people brings to the table.

In a one-to-one game like this, Nightingale should almost always be on hand with advice as required, though you must make even more sure that he does not take over and steal the show. You need to be careful not to incapacitate the only investigator  through  violence,  so  one-to-one  games  should typically have less physical jeopardy. Being rescued from, say, a kidnapping by the rest of the player group is acceptable to most players. Being rescued by a magical NPC as a literal deus ex machina can feel tedious and belittling if not handled well-although Peter certainly has set a precedent for it, to the point he even jokes about it 25 !

## Using Flashbacks

During  Session  Zero  and  character  creation,  give  some thought to the question: how did the investigators come to be associated with the Folly? Do you want your players to make their own backstory up and introduce themselves in the first gaming session ( Brush with the Supernatural , page 57)? Or do you want to leave this unsaid for the moment?

If you do decide to leave things vague, a nice option two or  three  games  in-after  your  players  have  settled  in  with the rules and you've all come to know each other's characters a little-is for you to run a 'flashback' scenario that covers the investigators' introduction to the Folly and its staff, and the creation of their team. This might mean retconning their skills and attributes, as it would probably be set before they were trained in Newtonian magic, so make sure you keep a copy of their original character sheets (physical or electronic) the first time you play. This is a fun way of adding extra depth to roleplaying and rewarding players for their immersion in the game. Of course, you will have to be extra careful not to kill the investigators during the flashback!

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000576_30bb53485db73cc5ce121c4278674834296641b7cb017439938a863c240fd743.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000577_8394e5e30f54111705bb0059ecd2a5f4d6d39a6c6ef6aa045a8969968374f024.png)

## Don't Sweat the Science…

This game takes place in a fantasy world, not ours, so don't try to explain the physics of magic beyond the rules mechanics-even Nightingale and Peter have only the haziest idea how magic works.

When it comes to mundane science, you are also not expected to be a forensics expert-and don't worry if you have to sacrifice strict factual accuracy for good gameplay. Keep things simple and consistent if you can, but allow yourself some flexibility. For example,  DNA  matching  takes  about  8  hours  from  delivery to the laboratory if it is an urgent priority case-hours or days longer  if  not,  with  an  average  of  24-72  hours.  You  can  cut this  time  down to a couple of hours to keep the momentum up-after all, certain TV forensic shows do this all the time. Conversely it is always easy to cite a weeks-long 'backlog' if you aren't in a hurry to hand out the information, or you want your NPC suspect to be released from custody.

Your  tweaks  to  the  laws  of  reality  should  be  subtle, wherever possible. For example, in RoL:RPG , injuries recover extremely quickly, and rarely remove players from taking part in the action for long ( Wounds and Healing , page 144). Of course, this means avoiding statements like ' You've been shot through the heart… You're spending 30 points of Luck? Up you get, then. ' But, when there's magic and genii locorum on the loose, you also have to accept that sometimes, physics-and subtlety-just have to take a back seat.

## … Or the Police Stuff

The first half of this chapter gives you plenty of information to  help  structure  a  crime  procedural  with  a  fair  degree of  accuracy.  But  sometimes reality can get in the way of a good  game.  Don't  bog  the  action  down  in  paperwork  or bureaucracy. If an investigator group needs resources or access to something or somewhere, this should be granted quickly. Drama trumps realism, every time. Not one of our favourite fictional crime dramas, either in print or on the screen, is an accurate description of day-to-day policing in the real world, and Rivers of London is no exception. Once again, it's more important to be consistent than factually exact.

Example: you  have  made  it  clear  that  in  the  game  universe, fingerprints  of  innocent  interviewees  can  still  be  found  on  file after an old case is closed and the fingerprints have been deemed irrelevant (which, in real-life Britain, is not, in fact, the case). You need this clue to be available for a particular case file. Don't deny the investigators that resource at a later point in the campaign and expect your players not to notice and complain-though you might justify it with, for example, a change in data protection law.

## Pacing the Game

The Metropolitan Police has a very straightforward approach to murder investigations, not for them the detective's gut instinct or the intricate logical deductions of the sleuth savant. No, what the Met likes to do is throw a shitload of manpower at the problem and run down every single possible lead until it is exhausted, the murderer is caught or the senior investigating officer dies of old age. -Whispers Under Ground

The critical  thing  with  pacing  a  gaming  session  is  to  keep your  players  engaged  and,  hopefully,  entertained.  If  they are  messing  around  in  character  (rather  than  moving  your fabulous  plot  along)  and  are  thoroughly  enjoying  being magical practitioners or dogged monster hunters, then this is not actually a problem. Enjoy the ride, and have the plot waiting for them when they're ready to pick it up again.

If a scene is dragging or serving no entertainment purpose, bring  it  to  an  end  as  soon  as  the  important  information has been exchanged. Before any formal 'Next Scene' announcement, though, it's always a good idea to give your players  a  heads-up  such  as,  ' Okay, is there anything else you want to ask Mrs Pepperdine or shall we move on? ' so they can tie up any loose ends.

Keep  your  players  engaged  with  the  story.  Make  sure there is always something for them to do that advances the plot-or, if they hit a temporary dead end, simply skate over any pause: e.g., ' Three days later, you get a phone call from the lab with the fabric analysis results you requested… '

There are several potential causes of slow patches. In real life, the vast majority of investigatory police work is repetitive and dull-it's  following  up  lists  of  'actions,'  taking  statements from witnesses, cross-referencing information, and watching hours of CCTV footage. You should not attempt to replicate that in a game! Don't roleplay every witness interview, just the ones that matter-and just the parts of the interview that are  important (you don't have to roleplay the entire scene). Boil down the results to a summary in which the requisite clues you want the investigators to catch stand out. Suppose, for  example,  the  players  have  to  knock  on  every  door  in  a particular  street  and  take  statements:  it  is not necessary  to name or describe any of these individuals or actually go into conversational detail.

Police  teams  in  real  life  tend  to  conduct  inquiries  and informal interviews in pairs or even solo, rather than moving round in groups, but, if you have more than two players, don't worry about this particular detail. It's much more important to keep all your players involved in the gameplay.

Example: ' You  spend  the  day  going  from  door  to  door,  taking statements  from  the  neighbours.  By  the  end  of  it,  your  feet  are sore,  and  you've  drunk  far  too  much  tea.  Those  few  people  who remember Mr Underhill almost all describe him as a quiet man who kept himself to himself.' [Pause to see if anyone picks up on the 'almost,' which they don't this time.] 'No one reports seeing him since Wednesday night at about 9 pm.'

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000578_fb0b490268cbe26a283fa7b3be2b10fbf17cc3c0db93097ceb10ea80eb3dca86.png)

## Player A: 'Who saw him then?'

'Mr Underhill's house shares an alley with the neighbour at No. 18,  a  Mr  Vardy.  You  interview  that  gentleman  in  his  garage where  he's  fixing  a  lawnmower,  and  note  that  he's  built  like  a brick outhouse. He says that on Wednesday night at around nine he  saw  Mr  Underhill  struggling  to  put  out  his  wheelie  bin  for collection and offered to help. Underhill refused and sounded, and I'm quoting here, 'A bit pissed off, pardon my French. ''

Player  B: 'Wait.  Was  this  normal  for  them?  Was  Underhill usually grumpy?'

Player A: 'And why was he struggling? Was the bin really heavy or something?'

And so on. You can reprise the salient points of the interview, or segue smoothly into a roleplay of the conversation and subsequent actions-checking  the  empty  bin,  contacting  the  local  council refuse department, etc.-as suits your style of play.

## Splitting the Party

At some point, your investigator group is going to split up, and you'll have to converse with only one or two people while the  others  stay  out  of  the  narrative.  Keep  these  interludes fairly  brief.  Most players won't mind just listening in for a little  while,  so  long  as  something  interesting  is  happening and they get their  own  turn  later.  When  you've  reached  a good  moment  to  stop-maybe  the  revelation  of  a  clue,  or some sort of twist (' The door bursts open and a man walks in on you! '), swap back to the other player(s). Dramatic tension keeps the game's energy level high-just think of all those abrupt  chapter  breaks  in  crime  novels  that  push  you  into reading on to find out what happens next!

## Missing Clues

There  may  come  a  point  where  the  players  might  run  out of  actionable  clues,  and  they  don't  know  what  to  do  next. This  may  be  because  they've  missed  some  Observation or  Research  rolls,  or  because  they  haven't  noticed  the  clue sitting right there in their player notes. At this point you have several options:

connections-though try to be subtle with this (see also The Incident Room , page 206).

- If they've failed an Observation roll-well, that's why meticulous photos and samples are taken at crime scenes, and notes/audio/video are made during every interview (see also Failure (or Weak Success) , page 106). So, remind the players that they can review the crime scene data to see if they missed something…
- If investigators check their notes in-character, you can use the opportunity to introduce critical clues, e.g., ' Three of the witnesses remarked upon the assailant's bright blue eyes. Your suspect's eyes are brown .'
- NPCs who have access to crime scene records or other data can bring a clue back to the investigators' attention. This includes scientific specialists but also data entry clerks and the case Exhibits Officer (page 206), who is in charge of bagging, labelling, and curating physical evidence, as well as maintaining the records that ensure the legal chain of evidence.
- At worst, missing a clue means the criminals are still out on the streets. The obvious outcome being they commit another felony, perhaps escalating their crimes, which can kickstart the investigation afresh with new clues to replace those which were previously missed.

## Props

These  are  an  optional  extra.  Like  other  roleplaying  games, RoL:RPG is  a  collaborative  storytelling  game  and  so  can be played simply using the spoken word and maybe the odd sketch map. But many players think more clearly if they have some sort of visual reference point to latch onto. For example, if your players go to the Barbican, don't just say, ' You go to the Barbican Centre. It's a famously ugly concrete complex… '-show them a photo.

- If it's just a failure to remember a certain detail or make use of information they already have, then you can simply remind or nudge them, or give them an INT roll to have their investigators connect the dots-but, remember, they could still fail that INT roll, and if so, be prepared to give them the clue, but with some added complication (like a pushed roll).

Photos can be useful in other ways, too. Your players may want to search out appropriate pictures online to give an idea of what their investigators look like, so you might like to do the same for key NPCs. It will help make all the different ranking police officers the investigators come across during an important case clearer if you can produce a portrait of each of them from your GM file.

Physical props and handouts add atmosphere if you have the time and resources to create them, as do their electronic equivalent. Depending on your skillset and preference, they can vary from the simple to the elaborate.

- It's also helpful for Nightingale to conduct regular debriefing sessions, so the investigators can report on how the case is going. This is also an opportunity for them to look back over their notes, both in- and outof-character, and for Nightingale to ask about lines of enquiry they may have overlooked, or point them at clue

Example: your  players  find  an  incriminating  letter.  You  can simply tell them, 'The letter makes it clear that Lord Wimbledon has business dealings with Doctor Beresford.' Or you could write the letter out (in part or in full) and read it out to them, letting them draw that same conclusion. Or, assuming you are playing in a face-to-face group and not over the internet, you could put the

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000579_6d357a449c99bfeb7c06f549c1944aad39384878a025fc9921f0c68ac8201ca1.png)

letter into a handwriting font, print it, put it in a torn, addressed envelope, and let them decipher it themselves. What about if the investigators  come  into  possession  of  a  mysterious  magical  key? Providing a drawing would certainly be helpful, or you could use that  actual  old  key  you've  had  stashed  in  your  junk  drawer  for years. You could even 3D print one if you have the resources and are really ambitious. None of this is necessary, but it adds to player immersion, the sense of realism, and the gaming fun.

## Using Tech

'He's out doing a recce on his target,' said Guleed. 'He managed to dig out floorplans on Zoopla, of all places, and now is checking to make sure he'll have all the exits covered.'

-The Hanging Tree

As RoL:RPG is  a  tabletop  game,  you  could-technicallyplay it on a desert island with nothing more technologically advanced than some dice. But you can use tech to enhance gameplay, if desired.

If  your  player  group  likes  to  get  really  investigative  and has appropriate resources, such as smartphones, let them use internet search engines to do some of their own investigation during  the  game.  If  your  plot  needs  them  to  know  who took  part  in  the  Battle  of  Blythe  Road  in  1900,  or  where the  London  Stone  is  located,  or  which  Celtic  god  has  his head buried beneath the Tower of London, why not let your players  look  it  up  themselves  instead  of  having  you  hand them the information? After all, their investigators certainly have internet access and would be doing exactly that!

You  might  let  your  players  use  an  online  search  engine freely,  but  be  aware  that  this  can  be  a  time  sink  and  a distraction  unless  you  keep  your  players'  eyes  on  the  ball. One potential way to avoid this is to call for a Research roll and,  upon  success,  simply  send  them  the  useful  URL  via phone or chat room.

This technique works well when you're incorporating realworld historical elements into your plots. Obviously, you have to  be  careful  the  players  don't  come  across  any  spoilers-or anything that clearly contradicts your plot. But you've done the real-life research, so why not let them have some of that fun, too? The sense that the magical Rivers of London world could be real because it fits so seamlessly with mundane reality is a thrill. And, if they do unexpectedly turn up something that clashes with your own fictional world, Nightingale can always remind them that not everything written in the public records is correct; the Folly has a long history of covering things up.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000580_f1d48b9031209a82b3383081a03975630a0d11c89079b4bcc93e33777380fcbf.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000581_f7696aa747e5badbd0426904ca014138d9b13656a81b10a4ccd28114f51efc85.png)

Let's  look  at  another  example  where  tech  can  be  useful. Say you've done your research in locating where in the real world  the  victim's  house  is.  You've  decided  on  an  actual address and you know what it looks like, so why not let your players  also  get  a  look  at  that  address  in  satellite  and  3D street view? It gives them a much clearer idea of what sort of area the victim called home, and probably the victim's class background and lifestyle.  If  the  investigators  can  spot  the building has a side door, they might decide it's better to use that as their entrance. If they swivel around on the 3D map, it might occur to them that the betting shop over the road is an ideal place to go look for witnesses. This heightened 'sense of place' can be the bedrock of an RoL:RPG session.

Really ambitious GMs have been known to create entire in-character web pages for their players to explore, though that is certainly going above and beyond! There are really no limits to the way you can bring imaginative new elements into your gameplay at any level of tech. Some further examples of using tech and props in game:

- Let your players take that 3D virtual tour of the National Gallery on the official website and search around for that one painting with the vital clue.
- Use ambient music or sound effects to
- Got smart lightbulbs? Plunge the game room suddenly into darkness when you trap them underground.
- establish atmosphere.
- Have audio clips ready to play for voicemail messages, phone taps, and interview tapes the investigators find.
- If you are on email terms with your players, send them clues in the form of pre-prepared reports, messages, or (fake) newspaper excerpts with photos, during or even between game sessions.
- Cue up a friend to ring the player group at a certain point and roleplay an NPC passing on information.

These are just a few suggestions, but the key point is: don't exclude  technology  from  your  game  out  of  hand-instead, harness it to enhance your sessions and storytelling ability. If it doesn't work for you, that's fine, but you won't know until you try.

## Dealing with the Unexpected

Occasionally, your players will do something that is completely legitimate, but that you haven't anticipated. And while  you've  put  work  into  being  prepared,  don't  let  that make your plot so stiff  that  your  players  feel  like  they  are being railroaded. You need to be prepared and flexible.

Ask yourself, ' Can I incorporate the investigators' actions into my storyline without breaking it completely? ' If it's at all possible, that's a better option than simply blocking the characters. Roleplaying is a collaborative exercise and the stories you create together are the best ones. Take what your players have thrown at you and run with it-but maybe not in the way they anticipate. Saying, ' Yes, but… ' is a better strategy than a flat-out ' No. '

Example: the players have pulled off an extraordinary series of rolls, meaning their investigators managed to kill an antagonist ahead of your plot schedule. You're the only one who knows his stats as written. Can he be knocked out, but escape later? Can he just be a frontman for a bigger, badder villain? Is magical misdirection or disguise an option? Can somebody have made a 'tragic error' and killed the wrong person by mistake? Can you accept the kill, but turn it into a plot twist and make the unforeseen consequences even more challenging?

Don't be scared to say, ' Okay, I need a moment to think about that, ' and call a comfort break if needed. Or, if the timing is such that you're near the end of a session, leave it on that cliffhanger until next you meet, with a dramatic announcement of ' TO BE CONTINUED… '

## Issues with Players

Ideally, your players are good-hearted  folks who  treat you, your game, and each other with courtesy and respect. However,  this  isn't  always  the  case.  Most  GMs  have encountered obstructive players who, for example, announce that their character is simply going to spend the day drinking in a bar instead of getting on with the investigation. Or those whose investigators turn into gun-crazed psychopaths where their solution to every problem is to shoot it in the head.

Clashes  of  play  style  and  personality  need  handling according to your personal relations with the players and their individual  psychologies.  Your  initial  discussion  about  the type of game you all want to play ( Creating Your Character , page  44)  helps  set  the  tone  and  player  expectations,  and so can also help avoid difficulties based on differing player styles. Beyond that, a combination of in-character and outof-character techniques work best. For example:

'Can I ask why you're doing that?'

'You know this is an investigative game, don't you? Looking for clues is a key part of the game.'

'You know that that is completely illegal, don't you? You're openly breaking the law, and it will get your investigator confined to his desk, sacked, or imprisoned.'

In  the  immortal  words  of  the  Police  Association:  Engage, Explain,  Encourage,  Enforce  ( The  Four  Es ,  page  71).  Try to  find  out  why  the  problem  is  happening.  If  necessary,  take a  break  and  listen  to  the  player,  and  then  explain  why  it's  a problem  for  everyone.  Encourage  them  to  co-operate,  and only bring in negative consequences to their actions as a final resort. Enforcement can be done in-character, e.g., their police superiors come down on them like a ton of bricks for dereliction of  duty  or  they  get  hauled  up  before  the  relevant  Police Standards Department ( Police Oversight , page 214), but, it is far  better  to  get  player  co-operation  by  reminding them how

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000582_f56c8f0015e3e0818336df99feda173fb08158d7059927f60b925b8c9345be1e.png)

the game works. Sometimes, the solution might be changing up that player's investigator; perhaps they designed the character with different expectations in mind, and now, seeing how the game  is  progressing,  wish  they  could  be  playing  a  character more suited to the case file. If so, work with them to bring in their new investigator while the old one takes a leave of absence.

Note  that  sometimes  players,  especially  those  new  to roleplaying,  can  be  passive  or  indecisive  because  they  are nervous. They are worried they will 'get it wrong' and that it will get their investigator into trouble-or even killed. This is  where human-moderated RPGs have the advantage over more  unforgiving  computer  games.  You  should  establish early that there is open dialogue between you and the players. You  don't  expect  them  to  know  everything  about  the  real law or procedure-you certainly don't expect them to know everything their investigator would. They should always be able to drop out of character to ask, ' What are my options? ', ' What would be normal police procedure in this case? ', ' What are my  chances  of  pulling  off  this  action  successfully? ',  etc.  Again, Session Zero is a good time to make this clear, but you can always remind your players whenever you feel the need.

At the other end of the scale is the rarer player who makes other players-or the GM-genuinely uncomfortable through their inappropriate behaviour. This is something you need to address promptly. Players of that sort are probing to see where the social boundaries are, and how much you will allow. If you don't pull them up, they may keep on pushing. This is the time you should remind them that you are running the game, and that if they aren't going to play nice then they are not going to be playing at all. The buck stops with you, and you are in charge (see also Considerate Gaming , page 44).

## Have Fun

You wouldn't be playing this game if wasn't fun! But the thing to  remember  is  that  your  players'  enjoyment  is  important, too-more so than the integrity of the fictional world you have lovingly built. Being a GM can be a lot of work and responsibility, so of course you want to get something out of it for yourself. But you can be sure that, if your players spend a  whole  session  being  bored,  upset,  ignored,  frustrated,  or bullied, it won't matter how much of your heart and soul you poured into creating that case file or campaign, they won't come back for more and your efforts will have all been wasted.

with their phones? End the scene, and bring that vital clue back in at a later point. Have they racked their brains about the crime but got frustrated? If you don't use any of the techniques mentioned in Missing Clues (page 226), introduce an obnoxious journalist for some comic antagonism, or a pickpocket for a chase scene-you can always go back to the main plot later.

Mutual  respect  is  a  prerequisite  for  a  roleplaying  game. The players need to respect your role in guiding them through the plot, trusting that you won't arbitrarily kill them, hinder their progress, or waste their time. You need to respect your players,  their  agency,  their  enjoyment,  and  the  validation they get from achieving something or defeating an opponent or making an intellectual leap of understanding.

## Boundaries

As we discussed in Considerate Gaming (page 44), everyone has  boundaries-such  that,  if  you  cross  them,  things  stop being  fun.  There  are  undoubtedly  topics  you  don't  enjoy discussing and don't want in your games-but they might not be the same things as the ones your players dislike. Excessive gore  and  violence  are  obvious  risky  areas  for  some  players ( Important: Tone ,  page  123),  but  it  can  be  really  difficult to anticipate where these boundaries lie for individuals. For example, you might introduce an NPC who chain-smokesbut one of your players is trying to give up smoking and what you see  as  an  innocent  bit  of  characterisation  makes  them really uncomfortable.

You don't have to anticipate everything or make your game bland. But don't assume that just because you think a detail is perfectly acceptable in a fun crime-based game, that everyone else  will  agree.  Accept  that  people  have  genuine  differences, and that an individual's boundaries can shift depending on their life circumstances and mood. Maybe they've had a terrible day being shouted at by their boss-the last thing they need is to get into a game where their Chief Inspector is being overbearing. If you step over a line inadvertently, even through no fault of your own, apologise and back off from the description.

That doesn't mean you should always let investigators have their way with NPCs, or get off consequence-free for their actions, or work everything out without putting effort into it. Overcoming challenges and using their wits and abilities should be part of the fun for them. But, you should always be aware of your players' mood and tailor what you're doing to take it into account. It's a tightrope walk, and it takes practice to get it right. Is a scene taking so long the players have lost interest and started playing

Just to reiterate: an out-of-character discussion with the group before the game starts might save you a lot of problems ( Creating Your Character , page 44). If you have a player who cannot abide descriptions of animal suffering, for example, you might have to rethink any scenes revolving around certain kinds of demon traps, or make extra sure that the investigator manages to save some victims before they're exploited. However, be aware that asking in advance can put the individual player on the spot. They might not be sure where their boundaries lie, or they might feel too selfconscious to draw a line in the sand before even starting a game. This  is  where  the  safety  tools  we  mentioned  in Considerate Gaming (page 44) really come into their own. They may not be necessary at all for your group, but you still may wish to look at them for some ideas in case the need ever arises.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000583_3288cf4ff80ee5b26950ed260481a21b2fdea379a63fcd729f416a80303ce54d.png)

LANAN

## A Rogues' Gallery

THE RIVERS OF LONDON novels (and graphic novels!) present  a  wide  array  of  memorable  characters.  In  this chapter, we focus on some of the most memorable and useful individuals that you, the GM, might use in your games. As well  as  a  few  familiar  faces,  there  are  details  on  how  fae magic works in Rivers of  London:  the  Roleplaying  Game ,  as well as some weird and wonderful creatures to help populate your case files.

The  various  characters'  pen  portrait  descriptions  in  this chapter are presented from Peter Grant's point of view, while the sections discussing how genii locorum and fae bend reality to their liking are presented in game terms, as are each of the non-player characters' (NPCs') profiles.

## Profiles and Stat Blocks

Each NPC has a section that describes who they are, how they look and  behave,  and  what  their  allegiances are. Each  character  is  also  presented  with  what  we  call  their 'profile'-the characteristics, skills, and spells that describe what they can do in the game, sometimes also known as a 'stat block.'

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000584_23bbe7e95a0b23118851556ab658843433c04941250507f5260ccc8f55f9e729.png)

## Human Characters

This  section  contains  details  on  many  of  the  key  human characters found in the Rivers of London novels. Not all the magic-wielding NPCs in this section have a specific list of spells attached to them-usually because they're from nonNewtonian traditions.  In  some  cases,  there  is  guidance  as to what their specialisms are, and we encourage you to use the  spell  list  in Chapter 4: Newtonian Magic (page  161) as  a  source  of  inspiration  for  what  they  can  do.  In  other cases, we've given them a special ability and/or an item that mimics the effects  described  in  the  novels-these  abilities and advantages are not available to investigators. For the fae and how they do incredible things, see The Magic of the Demi-Monde (page 243).

## ADDITIONAL RULES

Guidance on creating special abilities for non-Newtonian  practitioners  can  be  found  in Creating Non-Newtonian Special Abilities (page 326).

Profiles  for  humans,  creatures,  and  members  of  the demi-monde  are  broadly  similar  in  structure,  with  one  or two variations that account for their special foibles. In each profile,  characteristics  are  presented  first,  followed  by  that character's  skills  and  languages.  For  humans,  spells  and signare come  next,  if  they  have  them,  whereas  the  demimonde  profiles  contain  their  powers,  specific  affinity,  and vestigia (if  appropriate),  as  well  as  any  other  relevant notes. A character's skills are given with their common skills first, followed  by  their  expert  skills,  and,  finally,  their  combat skills. Spells known by a character are grouped by order, and any mastered spells are labelled as such.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000585_0d2880cec1f2f67f0c640ac9b8f8b4e3a2260cc28916cd12f23f23069509beee.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000586_fca561fb68e49473da5df9d841908810f61a0818faf7a2953466ad4d55fc0ba3.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000587_731dfc1012c45bbcd2cb2700b90fa4f32fa29d71a8c71428a8bdabe56ab8f834.png)

## DC Peter Grant

This is a bit weird, innit-talking about myself, but whatever. I'm just your basic North London boy who got bitten by the policing bug and joined the Met. Things started to change when I tried to take a witness statement from a ghost in Covent Garden Piazza 1 . Then I met DCI Thomas Nightingale, and after that, things started to get really strange.

So, TL;DR-I can do magic. You can call me a wizardalthough strictly speaking, I'm still an apprentice. I don't fly around on a broom like certain boy wizards, but, if I'm good, Nightingale lets me drive his vintage Mark 2 Jag. I can shoot fireballs from my fingers-which is occasionally handy-but I'm more likely to knock you down with Impello or break your concentration with a well-placed water bomb.

So, me, right? My ends-or manor, if you're old fashionedis  Kentish  Town  in  general,  and  the  Peckwater  Estate  in particular. My dad, Richard 'Lord' Grant, is from the East End. He's also the most famous jazz trumpeter you've never heard of. He shot his career up his arm twice-heroin-but is now having a bit of a geriatric come back. This pleases my mum,  Rose  Mamusu  Grant,  who's  a  jazz-mad  Fula  from Sierra Leone. She's the one who's kept it together all these years and taught me how to clean to a professional standard, as well as tolerate food so spicy that it's dangerous to serve on a paper plate.

I went to Acland Burghley, my local comprehensive school, and had ambitions to become an architect, which didn't work out. Faffed about for a couple of years, became a Community Safety Officer and got the policing bug. Joined the Met, and the rest, as they say, is career development.

Beverley, the love of life, is giving me 'the look,' which means I should wind things up, and also reminds me that I'm soon to be a Dad. Let's hope a better one than mine.

- Description: I'm a 1.83 metre (6 feet) tall, devilishly handsome man of mixed ethnicity in my late 20s. I inherited my mum's hair, which eats afro picks for breakfast, so I generally keep it shaved short with a fade for preference.
- Allegiances: obviously, there's Nightingale and the Folly, Beverley Brook, Sahra Guleed, and Abigail Kamara (who's actually a half-cousin, if half-cousin is even a thing). Oh, and FBI agent Kimberley Reynolds, of course.
- Traits: Lesley used to say I was easily distracted. Nightingale worries about me not following order and tradition, but I can assure you, I adhere to the scientific method. This has led to me developing the Yap scale ( Toby , page 240) and a few innovative spells of my own design.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000588_44da2e179f12abcce96bce6c9f06ad7183b87a6a7eb1ec2d198b6ee86df49e64.png)

## DC Peter Grant, apprentice practitioner

STR 60

CON 50

DEX 40

INT 70

POW 60

MOV: 8

MP: 20

LUCK: 50

Advantage:

Magical.

## Skills

Athletics 30%, Drive 50%, Navigate 60%, Observation 60%, Read Person 60%, Research 30%, Sense Vestigia 60%, Social 60%, Stealth 30%; Law 60%, Magic 60%, Tech 40%; Fighting 60%, Firearms 30%.

## Languages

English (own) 70%, German 30%, Greek 30%, Krio (own) 35%, Latin 60%.

## Spells

Aqua (mastered), Impello (mastered), Scindere (mastered), Werelight (mastered); Car Killer (mastered), Fireball (mastered), Impello palma (mastered), Shield (mastered); Coffin Bell, Telescopium ; Clausurafrange, Sīphōnem; plus all the others pells listed in the Chapter 4: Newtonian Magic , up to (but not including) fourth order.

## Signare

This is where things get complicated. Nightingale's tick-tock precision is there, but after that, it's all over the place-music, laughter, the smell of chocolate, images of London-and, blimey, is it loud! So loud, in fact, that it hides what spell he's cueing up, meaning an adversary can't make a Magic skill roll to determine what spell he's about to fling at them.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000589_75d90b28ed0a745ab3d48234606c735dfe8c579249aeb5f531eb6f55446b847e.png)

## DCI Thomas Nightingale

Now we come to my governor, the senior magical practitioner and officer in charge of the Folly. I was about to say he's the most remarkable person I've ever met, but over the last few years that's become quite a contested field!

When  I  talk  about  Nightingale's  history,  I'm  talking actual history. Educated at Casterbrook-a boarding school for young gentlemen and future wizards-he quickly proved himself  top  of  the  class  in  practical  magic  and  bottom  in theory.  He  spent  the  interwar  period  trouble-shooting  for the Foreign Office and the Colonial Office where, as far as I can tell, he went everywhere, had adventures, and then filled in forms explaining them. One day, I plan to get my hands on those forms.

During the Second World War, he fought both in occupied Europe and on the front lines, countering German magical forces.  He  led  the  rear-guard  action  during  Operation Spatchcock-the  doomed  British  attempt  to  liberate  the Ettersberg  Camp and capture Nazi magical research. This research, now known as 'The Black Library,' is safely stashed in a secure vault under the Folly. I'm toying with the idea of hanging a sign on the door reading 'Beware of the Leopard,' but I'm not sure Nightingale would find it funny.

For a long time, the Folly was just Nightingale and Molly. Then I was invited in, and, since then, the place has grown busier and busier. It's hard to say, but I think he enjoys the company.  Nowadays,  when  he's  not  teaching  me  and  the other  apprentices,  he  enjoys  watching  rugby  on  the  TV. Oh, and let's not forget the Jag! The man has some taste in motors and drives a classic grey Jaguar Mark 2 with a 3.8 litre XK6 engine.

- Description: for a man who was born around 1900, he looks remarkably well. Fact is, since the 1960s, he's apparently been getting younger. No, I don't know how that works, either. But anyway, to look at him now, I'd say he's 180 cm (5 foot 11 inches) tall, with fine features, grey eyes, and short brown hair. How do I describe his fashion sense politely? He's not what you'd call modernvintage, maybe? He's always well-dressed, usually in a suit, and carrying his silver-topped cane (which, between you and me, is actually his wizard's staff).
- Allegiances: he's known Molly for a long time-and I mean a long time, like since the 1940s. He seems to know everyone, including Father Thames and the other river spirits. In the course of his work, he's made some useful contacts too, including Harold Postmartin and Dr Walid.

Athletics 50%, Drive 80%, Navigate 70%, Observation 80%, Read Person 80%, Research 70%, Sense Vestigia 95%, Social 60%, Stealth 80%; Art/Craft (Blacksmithing) 70%, Law 90%, Magic 95%, Ride 60%; Fighting 60%, Firearms 60%.

All the spells listed in Chapter 4: Newtonian Magic , all mastered, plus a few more we haven't made rules up for yet.

## Signare

A clockwork tick, the smell of willow, and a whispering clash like the sound of cymbals-as heavy as a mallet and as sharp and controlled as the point of a needle.

- Traits: I'd describe him as thoughtful and serious, though his dry sense of humour sometimes breaks through. He approaches problems in a rational and calm manner, but don't let that deceive you-when he needs to, he can throw down against the best of them!

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000590_caff4118c9ac89425fb4e45c7fdc6971e28d68fca4d522d2492266d62c9d4b88.png)

6

## DS Sahra Guleed

Sahra  is  in  her  20s  and  is  a  member  of  the  Murder Investigation Team, led by DCI Alexander Seawoll and DI Miriam Stephanopoulos (page 238). As a kid, she emigrated from Somalia to England with her family. It's not something she talks about much, and I don't like to pry. We've worked a few cases together, and she's had my back a few times. She's trained in a form of oriental sword skill that combines magic and cold steel-I don't claim to understand how it works, but it's damned impressive. She called herself 'the Muslim Ninja' the  first  time  I  met  her 2 ,  and  that  was  before  she  started picking up the sword!

- Description: a slim young woman of medium height, of Somali descent. She has a hijab for every occasion and tends towards bomber or biker jackets when out of uniform.
- Allegiances: she's a dependable officer and an ally to the Folly. She's also engaged to Michael Cheung (page 238).
- Traits: always has a glint in her eye, like she's aware of everything going on around her.
