## The Mystic Art of Fighting

This magical discipline is not part of the Newtonian  tradition.  A  trained  practitioner in the Mystic Art of Fighting can spend 1 magic point to gain a bonus die to  any  Fighting  roll  (but  not  damage rolls),  subject  to  the  usual  rules  on Multiple Bonus or Penalty Dice (page 143).  When expending a magic point in this fashion, the character's  moves should be narrated as looking like the feats  we  might  expect  to  see  in  Hong Kong action cinema.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000595_87d9da063c12daabe66e1f24546dc6350587880982ee2e16e6d7c9857ad0f227.png)

The  woman  arched  in  pain  and  I  watched  as Guleed, still holding her arm, ran up the side of the  Nissan Micra in a way that didn't actually look physically possible and used her whole body weight to bear the nanny face down on to the pavement.

-Lies Sleeping

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000596_ec2fa8aa665b13c4b3e51a85ebb7d09aea2c2a352a4aae51fe5c89c6a13f9b0a.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000597_bef5110efe6eb32b91f9ff910e32db34f96293e3679042def78fd9fb79eb752c.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000598_709f823cfcd8a86a57adb46b550f8cddb29ed93924f0cfd3265d387f7fee1cd1.png)

## The Wizard's Sword

The  wizard's  sword  is  crafted  in  much the  same  way  as  the  core  of The  Wizard's  Staff (page  324),  and  serves  the same purpose as a magic point storage device for anyone who can utilise magic. As a weapon in the hands of a non-practitioner, it is a regular sword, but in the hands  of  someone  trained  in  the  Mystic Art of Fighting (as Sahra Guleed and Michael Cheung are), it is far more effective. A trained practitioner in the Mystic Art of Fighting can spend 1 magic point to gain a bonus die on any damage roll made  using  their  wizard's  sword.  The sword's base weapon damage is 1.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000599_a0951b374c78f93be4aa3f39ea1e9ebc5bf6a8370c4c5dd0c6c31f6ef2ccefd9.png)

## Harold Postmartin

Postmartin  is  a  proper  old-school  academic,  with  a  string of letters after his name (DPhil, FRS, MVP, etc.). Oh, and when I said old, I mean proper old. This guy did National Service  in  Korea  (and  still  has  his  Enfield  No.  2  revolver).

His  job  is  Curator  of  Special  Collections  at  the  Bodleian Library, Oxford. What that means is that he's the archivist of  all  documents  relating  to  the  Folly,  from  Isaac  Newton himself all the way down to yours truly. He can usually be found  in  his  office,  hidden  beneath  the  Radcliffe  Camera in  Oxford. I  don't  like  to  fault  the  man,  but  I  feel  like  he could  maybe  update  his  tech  a  little;  like,  he's  still  using an Amstrad PCW…

- Description: Postmartin is a stooped, white-haired, old white man; frail, but with a glint in his eye and a surprisingly firm handshake. Always dressed in a suit and Oxford University tie.
- Allegiances: integrally aligned with Nightingale and the Folly.
- Traits: views students as an unfortunate but necessary evil and is very protective of his books. He also has something of a reputation as a pirate among the British librarian fraternity 3 .

## Dr Abdul Haqq Walid

Dr Walid works at University College Hospital, London, and is a world-renowned gastroenterologist and cryptopathologist, as well as personal physician to the Folly. He also shares my interest  in  the  science  of  magic,  and  has  conducted  some interesting research of his own. I think he may have coined the term 'Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation'-HTD, for short.

- Description: Abdul is a tall (182 cm/6-foot-ish), livelysome might say 'spry'-white Scotsman in his 50s, with a prominent forehead, a receding hairline, and ginger hair.
- Allegiances: aligned with Nightingale and the Folly.
- Traits: he speaks with a soft Scottish accent and has a dry sense of humour.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000601_6cc5bcb4b2e33519e605fa0cdf9943e701049dd29236784762a68c31e85129a6.png)

## Dr Jennifer Vaughan

A ferociously clever doctor from Newport, Wales, who entered medical school with high hopes of becoming a healer, only to find  that  the  puzzle  was  more  interesting  than  the  person. As  a  result,  she  ended  up  gravitating  downwards  towards the morgue and a career in pathology. She was brought in to provide Dr Walid with much needed pathology expertise.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000605_3f2a3ee4b20b481096c584d36e9e0b361a62d73c5fc6f9b34c341d506123a336.png)

Steadfast.

- Description: a small compact white woman in her early 30s, with clever, brown eyes; her brown hair is cut into an easily manageable and practical style. Rarely seen out of PPE.
- Allegiances: aligned with Nightingale and the Folly.
- Traits: she speaks with the kind of Welsh accent that makes her sound like she's being sarcastic, which most of the time she is.

## Abigail Kamara

When I stupidly made a deal with my teenaged half-cousin Abigail  that  I  would  teach  her  magic  if  she  got  an  A-star in her Latin GCSE 4 ,  I  never dreamt that it would actually happen. And now, a few years later, here she is at the Folly as an apprentice! Abigail is the daughter of Alfred Kamara, who is one of mum's many half-brothers and is not beyond using  the  family  connection  to  get  around  me.  She's  also developed a relationship with the strange talking foxes that popped up recently. I suspect they're up to no good.

- Description: Abigail is a young woman of mixed heritage, her dad being from Sierra Leone and her mum being a white Londoner. She's short and wiry, with a narrow, angular face, sharp features, and a penetrating stare. She used to wear her hair in an enormous puffball afro but now has gone for trendy locs-all the better to keep it out of her eyes when she's blowing things up.
- Allegiances: aligned with the Folly and the Parliament of Foxes; also has a mysterious side gig that she keeps to herself.

## Abigail Kamara, apprentice practitioner

CON 60

DEX 40

INT 70

POW 70

## Spells

Werelight (mastered); other than that, no one's entirely certain which spells Abigail's managed to learn so, for now, the choice is left to the GM-we suggest choosing at least one other first order and one second order spell from the selection in Chapter 4: Newtonian Magic .

## Signare

Hasn't yet solidified, but you definitely get a little bit of Nightingale's clockwork orrery mixed with whatever has caught Abigail's interest in the last few months; plus: fox fur, a blast of cold, and something that might be the opening bars of a popular British talent show's theme tune.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000607_8ca30959aa235261a071f202735baa0781ae5eb7b001b1f7b8c3779ffa959f2b.png)

4 Moon Over Soho

## Special Agent Kimberley Reynolds

I first met Kim when she was sent over by the FBI to observe a  murder  investigation 5 .  Being  on  the  same  case  led  to her witnessing me using magic and the rest, as they say, is history.  She  kept  in  touch  after  she  returned  to  America, and flagged up that certain people in authority were asking questions about me.

- Description: a slim white woman in her 30s, with green eyes and auburn hair, cut in a bob. She dresses very formally in typical FBI fashion (smart trouser suit and a blouse) when she's not wading through London's sewers, often with her gold FBI badge on her belt.
- Allegiances: an ally to me and the Folly, though she does work for a different agency, so there's always some caution in dealing with her.
- Traits: generally, she is quite down to earth, but can sometimes have an air of superiority.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000608_f2c7dd379b4f3dcdfb574055fefe04ecfc759a28d1ecb46e716ff7aa49be52b4.png)

Footnote: 5 Whispers Under Ground

- Traits: if you've not got on the wrong side of her, she has a playful streak, and may well make cheeky comments and retorts. Otherwise, you'll get The Stare.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000609_3762f7134f4dab283166c5263aff9329142651e803b95bc7c5cab129c33a88b3.png)

## DI Miriam Stephanopoulos

Stephanopoulos is, how do I put this politely, an intimidating presence with the Murder Investigation Team. She doesn't so much speak as bark orders and sneer. She also has a wife called  Pam,  who lectures at Middlesex Uni, tucked out in the suburbs where they both raise chickens and grow their own vegetables.

- Description: a short, stocky, middle-aged white woman with a square face and brown hair cut into an unfashionable flat-top. I think my impression on first meeting her was that she looked like she fought Rottweilers for a hobby. Famous (infamous?) for her evil glare.
- Traits: she tends to have an agitated expression on her face, like she's about to start growling.
- Allegiances: a steadfast Metropolitan police officer. She knows about the Folly, but isn't a part of it.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000610_fefd5aa3546ad44f03039cbe12785ef13381d10293c76972aca0ec37b2dc3868.png)

## Michael Cheung

Michael  is  the  Folly's  liaison  in  Chinatown.  His  business card-I kid you not-reads 'Legendary Swordsman.' He's also teaching Sahra Guleed… well, I'm not exactly sure what he's  teaching her, but it involves swords and magic. And I don't mean they sit around rolling dice together-I mean real swords and real magic {lol}.

- Description: an Anglo-Chinese man in his mid-to-late 20s, Michael is short but broad-shouldered, with an

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000611_a47ba9df2fb26d561c73583e511848752480bc7ccd657fd7e6f186c497a8d7d9.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000612_f67185d4ecee29dffddcf8dfa61bda1ac8d898b4f8912c59fe6e3d6439801bc9.png)

athletic build. He keeps his black hair cut short but with a long fringe. Tends to dress casually, although Guleed insists he scrubs up well. Usually has his sword somewhere about his person.

- Allegiances: allied with the Folly as our Chinatown liaison. Sahra Guleed's fiancé.
- Traits: charming (so Guleed says), confident, almost as much of a nerd as me. Almost.

## Frank Caffrey

Frank served in the military (4 th Battalion Parachute Regiment) and remains a reservist. He works for the London Fire Brigade as a Fire Investigator, but he also works with the Folly. When required, he can call upon a group of men: all ex-military-fit, short-haired, disciplined, and reassuringly terrifying-it's like the Computer's gone wrong and all his clones have turned up at once.

- Description: a tough-looking, large, middle-aged white man; stocky, with a broken nose and deceptively mild blue eyes. His hair is cut down to a brown fuzz.
- Allegiances: Caffrey is often called upon to serve the Folly when we need firepower. Or phosphorous grenades.
- Traits: a man of few words. I'm not sure I've ever seen him actually smile.

## Toby

I  know  this  section  is  headed  'humans,'  but  I'm  counting Toby as an honorary human-man's best friend and all that.

I adopted Toby during a previous case 6 , and he's established a new home for himself in the Folly. I soon discovered that Toby is sensitive to magic, and that he communicates this in the only way he can: by barking. It may sound a bit nuts, but I've  codified  Toby's  reactions  into  what  I  know  as  the  Yap scale-the more yaps, the stronger the vestigia .

- Description: a short-haired, brown and white terrier.
- Allegiances: Toby is his own dog, but he is fond of Molly (and me-when I have sausages, at least).

50

## Skills

Observation 60%, Sense Vestigia 60%; Eat Sausages 80%; Bite 60%.

## Varvara Sidorovna Tamonina

Born in 1921,  in  Russia.  Varvara  is  a  night  witch  ( Nochnye Koldunyi ), trained in magical combat by the Red Army during World War II. She was captured in 1943 and transported to Guernsey, where she sat out the rest of the war. After the war, Varvara married and settled down in Wimbledon. In recent decades, she used her magical abilities in the service of dark wizards until Nightingale took her down in an epic boss fight 7 .  Since then, she seems to have seen the error of her ways and now assists Nightingale with tutoring the apprentices-especially Abigail.

- Description: appears to be a middle-aged white Russian woman with a worn face, faded blue eyes, and hair the colour of dirty straw, currently cut into a business-like bob. Just like Nightingale, though, she seems to be aging backwards and is about a hundred years old, give or take. Tends to go for smart skirt suits these days-quite a transformation from the first time I met her 8 .
- Traits: he's a lively little fellow with a nose for trouble and sausages.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000615_57bf3b393f29414db25fb0d4f5a55b8c70414867cc988ea704f6db01c94cd627.png)

Footnote: 6

Rivers of London

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000616_33c5f398d03d54ac5d4235c2beb0ae2c8e2c2db9a50cb549d4c9f188e2b521f3.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000617_7d4a7d33a133b2a8604566e94451f583123d728abfd01bd55629a5646a008db5.png)

Footnotes: 7 Broken Homes , 8 Whispers Under Ground

MOV: 9

MP:

10

LUCK: -

- Traits: she has the steely gaze of someone who has faced death: the kind of gaze that makes you wonder if maybe death backed down first.
- Allegiances: Varvara seems to have made the switch from enemy to ally of the Folly. We know she still has some Russian contacts, though she seems reluctant to discuss them 9 .

## Asterid Bivalacqua

Let's just say my first meeting with Ms Bivalacqua was not the smoothest encounter in the world. You know, on account of her sneaking up on me and holding Nightingale's old service revolver to the back of my head 10 . Some sort of test, apparently, which seemed to amuse her and the governor no end.

She's  definitely  a  hedge  wizard  (not  that  I'd  risk  calling her that to her face), and Nightingale uses her as a consultant for  all  things  Vodun.  He  first  ran  across  her  back  in  the late  1950s 11 ,  but  that's  another  story  altogether.  Currently lives in Richmond-the suburb of London, not the one in Yorkshire-where she has a very impressive greenhouse.

- Description: a slender, old Black woman in her late 70s/early 80s, with white hair in a short, relaxed style. Tends to wear lots of jewellery (chunky necklaces, bangles, and big drop earrings). Her red lips match her elegant nails-quite a feat for such an avid gardener! Oddly, she doesn't appear to be aging backwards like Nightingale and Varvara.
- Traits: evil sense of humour and more than capable of taking care of herself. I think you could safely say she doesn't suffer fools gladly.
- Allegiances: while she has history with Nightingale and the Folly, she's definitely an independent operator.

## Asterid Bivalacqua, green-fingered OAP*

* Old-Age Pensioner.

CON 50

## Languages

English (own) 60%, Jamaican Patois (own) 60%.

## Spells

She's a woman of mysterious power, and her magic is definitely non-Newtonian. If Nightingale knows what she's capable of, he isn't saying. Has an affinity for growing things.

## Signare

The burning taste of Scotch bonnet peppers and the fragrance of expensive rum on a warm summer's evening.

## The Demi-Monde and Other Beings

I stared at him. I didn't believe in ghosts, or fairies or gods, and for the last couple of days I'd been like a man watching a magic show-I'd expected a magician to step out from behind the curtain and ask me to pick a card, any card. I wasn't ready to believe in ghosts,  but  that's  the  thing  about  empirical  experience-it's the real thing.

-Rivers of London.

The terms 'demi-monde' and 'fae' are not strictly defined, and are used to describe those who are somehow touched or changed by magic. This can happen in one of three ways:

- They can be born with it, like Zachary Palmer (page 256), who states that his father was a fairy.
- They can have it thrust upon them, such as those who are snatched and taken to Fairyland, only to return with magical abilities and a changed look in their eyes-or, darkest of all, those who are altered through magic to be part human, part animal: the Chimeras (page 247).
- They can seek it out and acquire it, as with Peter Grant and DCI Nightingale.

'Fae' is a term for people that have been changed by magic in some way, and who are no longer entirely human-or never were to begin with. Typically, the fae are mischievous and capricious,  though  not  necessarily  dangerous.  They  favour rural over urban settings, and some have a vulnerability to cold iron ( The Effects of Cold Iron ,  page  245)-although Peter would, no doubt, point out that everyone is susceptible to cold iron if you poke it hard enough into their vulnerable bits. The High Fae reside in a parallel dimension, known as Fairyland, where they follow their own laws and have their own royal houses. 'Demi-monde,' on the other hand, is more of a blanket term covering all three groups.

## BEN SAYS

Ben's asked us to remind you that there are some normal humans that are also technically part of the demi-monde, but not really. You know, like the ones who just sort of wandered into that cool pub by accident, liked the atmosphere, and stuck around. As long as they don't cause any trouble, the real demi-monde are happy to have them about-after all, they provide a handy bit of cover.

## On the Naming of, Well … Everything

The  Folly,  being  chock  full  of  gentlemen  of  the  Enlightenment,  went forth into the world of magic with the unwarranted confidence of young men on a stag night. They basically stuck a Latin tag on everything from spells to formae  to  fairies-whether  the  fairies  wanted  them  or  not.  It's  not  like they were even consistent. I've found at  least  three  different  classification systems in the Folly's library, all with elaborate Latin tags and, I figure, all the scientific rigour of phrenology.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000620_7e82b9d0d275e1fbd93429f65a3f8398c919285cb82d56509d4260ab8dbe39a1.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000621_f90eb853210823292d2b2e21f8dc8e4000682dbbec1829abc666b5f0ba90e0d5.png)

The  thing  is  that at  no  point  did  it occur  to  them  to  ask  anyone  what  it was  they  called  themselves  or  anything else for that matter. As a result, nobody  can  agree  on  the  names  for anything. To the savants of the Folly, a goblin  is  a  type  of  fae  who  is  of  onehalf human blood, one-eighth unseelie fae  and  three-eighths folloy.  Want  to know what a folloy is? So do I.

Just  like  the  mundane  world,  the demi-monde  is  just  as  full  of  terms that mean different things to different people.  One  person's  unseelie  fae  is another  one's  'Pale  Lady'-although both  could  get  you  killed.  The  Quiet People  are  also  known  as  the  Quiet Ones  except  to  themselves,  because, shockingly,  they  call  themselves  people. When in doubt, keep your mouth shut, your ears open, and your attitude a little bit humble.

'And for god's sake, don't call a goblin a  goblin  unless  you  really,  really,  like hospital food.'

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000622_74a1ce68fa06d92df79e350b67d479922ed80795c0dc9bf042e102c5b729e64f.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000623_98cb6720f966a9b4fab1714998f3e6385f069af2f888e62729fff7d7ce423038.png)

-Peter

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000624_9da20cdef63773d38f8a45aa6ec377b84c7db27aab8ae4d28a496e4896c9e7d2.png)

## The Magic of the Demi-Monde

The members of the demi-monde are varied and strange, and their magic is not the Newtonian magic used by Peter Grant and Nightingale. The more game rules we create for the genii locorum , the fae, and other denizens of what we broadly refer to as the demi-monde, the more we risk defining them too strictly  and  placing  limits  on  what  should  be  unknowable and  magical.  So,  rather  than  creating  a  host  of  new  rules, it's perhaps easier to say that those of the demi-monde who aren't-strictly  speaking-human,  possess  the  power  to break the game's rules instead.

Of course, they don't literally break the rules, but it sounds good, doesn't it? What they possess are three abilities that set them apart from the mundanes. The first of these is The Glamour (following); the second is Luck (nearby); and the third is a Thematic Power (page 244).

## The Glamour

Genii locorum and  many of the fae have the power to sway human minds to their will. They can command a human with a glance or a word-unless the target is prepared for it.

Once  a  character  has  been  versed  in  the  effects  of  the glamour, its power is greatly diminished-sort of like a stage magic  trick.  Let  us  explain.  The  stage  magician  performs a seemingly impossible wonder, and we, the poor audience members, are completely baffled. In the real world, though, we know that magic isn't real-we know we're being tricked and we love it, because good stage magic is wonderful and intriguing.  But,  if  we're  shown  how  the  trick  works,  it suddenly ceases to command the same sense of awe, even if you appreciate the skill involved.

It's  the  same  with  the  glamour.  There's  a  part  of  the primitive  human  brain  that  enjoys  being  subjected  to  the glamour's effect, the same way most of us enjoy watching stage magic-it's as if there's a primal satisfaction in surrendering to  its  power.  Yet,  just  as  with  stage  magic,  if  we're  aware of  what's  happening,  our  mind  registers  its  influence,  and we can attempt to resist it. When the glamour is used on a regular person who knows nothing of its existence, they are permitted a POW roll:

- Fumble or failure: the target does as they are bid without question, and they don't ever think to question why they acted as they did.
- Critical success: the target is unaffected by the glamour and reacts to the request as if asked by anyone else. They're not aware of anything magical having taken place.
- Regular or Hard success: the target does as they are bid without question, but later wonders what possessed them to act that way. Of course, they soon console themselves with whatever excuse feels the most rational to them.

243

When the glamour is used on a person who's been informed about it and how to resist it, they, too, make a POW roll:

- Fumble: the target behaves exactly as bidden, just as someone who is ignorant of the glamour would.
- Regular, Hard, or Critical success: the target is aware of the use of the glamour, but they are not compelled to act upon it.
- Failure: the target begins to act as requested-perhaps taking a few steps or speaking a few words-but then recovers their self-control. They are aware of the use of the glamour.

## GM ADVICE

As  always,  if  the  target  is  an  investigator,  they  can spend Luck points ( Spending Luck ,  page  113)  to  help them  resist  the  glamour's  effects  if  they  fail  their POW roll. They can also push the roll ( Pushing the Roll ,  page  108),  but  a  second  failure  places  them utterly  at  the  wielder's  mercy-potentially  even leading  to  them  becoming  enthralled,  like  Maksim did following his unfortunate first encounter with Beverley Brook 12 .

## The Luck of the Demi-Monde

Non-human  demi-monde  characters have Luck points they can spend to adjust their dice rolls, just like investigators do, only:

- Their Luck is equal to their POW value.
- It refreshes fully every day at a set time.

This means non-human  demi-monde characters can spend Luck points freely to achieve acts that appear to either require great skill or defy probability.

## The Gift of Luck

Some members of the demi-monde have the power to gift their Luck to others. Mama and Father Thames, for example, can give someone their blessing providing they pursue a certain goal .  So long as the character works within the confines of their  agreement,  they  may  draw  upon  that  pool  of  Luck points. For example, if a pact is made with Mama Thames, she might grant an investigator a boon-the GM allocates the player a pool of 50 Luck points to represent that. The player may then choose how and when these Luck points are used in the game, but they should always be spent on actions that are aligned with the agreed pact. If the player abuses the  gift,  Mama  Thames  may  decide  to  seek  some  form  of vengeance or, at least, recompense.

12

Footnote:

Night Witch

6

## Thematic Power

Genii locorum and the fae have a strong connection to some particular sphere-in the case of the rivers, that's an affinity to  water,  while  for  the  Quiet  People,  it's  earth.  Beverley Brook  demonstrates  her  affinity  numerous  times  in  the novels,  which  quite  often  seems  to  result  in  somewhere getting flooded. In game terms, Beverley's affinity to water grants  her  certain  thematic  powers.  The  manifestation  of these powers breaks down into two categories:

- Minor: something which simply illustrates the character's affinity with that element or power. For example, Lady Ty demonstrating her connection with water by perking up some wilted flowers 13 , or when wildflowers sprouted and bloomed on the ground where Father Thames relieved himself 14 . Routine acts don't help to resolve a case file (scenario) or further anyone's agenda; they just add some colour and character to the story.
- Major: an act that somehow furthers the plot, helps to resolve a case file, or provides an in-game benefit. For example, when Molly practises haemomancy or Beverley's over-enthusiastic response to the fire in flooding Covent Garden market 15 .
- Any demi-monde NPC can perform minor manifestations of their power whenever they wish, but it's best not to overdo it or they lose their impact.
- Any demi-monde NPC can perform one major manifestation of their power once per case file. Performing such acts is demanding, emotionally and energetically, and may also cost them socially.

Such powers are defined uniquely for each demimonde character in Other Beings (page  246),  or  within  a particular case file.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000625_6fbefc4d6063732789b0ab900c56bd9bd7f6601bf89c3b4f17a7aba4c3abed70.png)

## ADDITIONAL RULES

If  you want to keep your NPCs on the same footing as your investigators, rules-wise, Luck costs for making minor and major manifestations can be found in Affinities (page 318). These only apply to lower faethe genii locorum are in a different league altogether, and  the  use  of  their  thematic  powers  in-game should remain purely story-based.

## Codes of Conduct

The demi-monde's codes of conduct are mysterious, but it's clear that there's a great deal of obligation and duty among them.  Breaking  agreements  by  manifesting  their  power without  permission  may  lead  to  all  sorts  of  consequences and  complications.  For  example,  Mama  Thames  and  her daughters are under an agreement to keep the Queen's Peace, and they need the permission of an officer of the law if they are  to  use  their  magic  on  London  soil.  Who  knows  what might happen if they act without such permission?

Game-wise, this serves to limit the demi-monde's powers, which, in turn, matches the novels, where they only rarely demonstrate the full extent of their abilities.

There is another form of obligation the demi-monde takes very  seriously:  that  imposed  by  accepting  food  and  drink from another member of the demi-monde. It's always wise when  visiting  a  practitioner, genius  loci ,  or  any  other  type of fae to make sure that all refreshments are offered free of such obligation. According to Nightingale, no one is entirely sure  what  would  happen  if  someone  refused  to  honour  an obligation  gained  in  this  way-but,  equally,  no  one  is prepared to risk finding out one way or the other.

## Genii Locorum

Magic can accumulate at a location, seeping into the stone and other elements of the environment. Usually, this simply manifests  as vestigia ,  but  in  some  cases,  things  go  a  little further.  When  they  do,  that  location  ends  up  having  a spirit attached to it. It may be a minor spirit that inhabits a bookstore 16 or  a  major  spirit,  such  as  those  associated with the River Thames.

Genii  locorum is  a  Latin  phrase  used  by  people  like Nightingale  to  describe  these  spirits;  the  singular  being genius  loci .  When  speaking  of  themselves,  the  actual genii locorum prefer to use such terms as spirit, orisa , or even god or goddess. They have their own traditions and etiquette, and are very fond of ceremonies and festivals.

Just  how  an  individual  becomes  a genius  loci is  unclear. Some are born genii locorum , such as Chester 17 and Beverley Brook, who sprang mysteriously into existence as children. Others lead a normal life prior to their transformation-one notable  example  being  that  of  an  orangutan  who  escaped London Zoo in the 1950s, only to become the genius loci of Regent's Canal 18 . Some locations slowly take on a personality without it ever assuming a corporeal form. Genii locorum are long  lived,  perhaps  even  immortal,  but  they  can  be  killed. The River Lugg, in Herefordshire, was reputedly killed by Methodists long ago 19 .  Alternatively, the destruction of the location itself can lead to the death of the spirit associated with it, such as happened to some of Father Thames' sons 20 .

It appears that there is a limit to the scale of a genius loci's domain. Here in England, the Thames is a major river, but on a global league table, it's  really  not  a  major  player.  The truly  major  rivers,  such  as  the  Volga,  the  Danube,  or  the Rhine, don't have a single spirit but are divided into multiple entities, such as the Rhine Maidens 21 .  Other rivers around the world similarly have multiple spirits, such as the Ganges, the Mississippi, the Limpopo, and the Amazon. One theory is that there's an inbuilt limit on the power of individual genii

## The Effects of Cold Iron

As we've already mentioned, certain members of the demi-monde are vulnerable to cold iron. In narrative terms, this means it acts as a deterrent, and the GM may wish to impose a penalty die on a vulnerable character's actions when they are in the presence of large amounts of cold iron, or in direct contact with it ( Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111).

- Vulnerable to cold iron: the High Fae (including Molly and Foxglove), unicorns, and genii locorum.
- Not vulnerable to cold iron: chimeras, talking foxes, vampires, and members of the lower fae, such as trolls, the Quiet People, and folk like Zachary Palmer.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000628_400fbf2b0031c5138a2608041c3c5878c78581f3807f17b840d544af4022412a.png)

Successful  attacks  made  against  a  vulnerable character with a cold iron weapon gain a bonus die on the damage roll ( The Damage Roll ,  page 132).  Such  a  character,  when  injured  by  a  cold iron  weapon,  cannot  recover  until  the  iron  is removed  from  their  body,  i.e.,  they  remain  on whatever damage condition they ended the combat on. Damage inflicted by a cold iron weapon is untreatable by human medicine, and if the iron remains  in  the  victim's  body,  it  wicks  their  life energy away until they die.  All wounds caused by cold iron should be treated narratively through the power of the High Fae or genii locorum, such as when Peter rushes Father Thames' son, Ash, to Mama Thames to save him after an unfortunate accident with some iron railings 22 .

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000629_009622c82583fce29d4dbc3a364afc9a8f65af80889a7c719956a963720795dc.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000630_4312849a3d6ba3533888a55a504c2c79340f872de80e99b098338a35ad44fc57.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000631_0f057c7d95dd8b549f2cfa6e7556ce4c3f3488d663c8d2ad55b6c767283591ad.png)

locorum , because if the power of such a major river was ever embodied by a single entity, that genius loci would transcend this reality. No one knows if that's true, or if it's just a tale.

Genii  locorum ,  like  other  types  of  demi-monde,  possess magical powers,  but  their  magic  is  quite  different  to  Newtonian magic ( The Magic of the Demi-Monde , page 243).

welcomed,  though  some  may  prefer  other  gifts,  such  as bananas  in  the  case  of  Mrs  Canal.  It's  also  considered very  bad  manners  for  a genius  loci to  turn  up  on  someone else's  'patch'  unannounced-yet  another  element  of  those inscrutable Codes of Conduct (page 244).

Anyone wishing to spend time at the location associated with a genius loci , or who wishes to win their favour, would do  well  to  provide  a  gift  of  some  sort.  Alcohol  is  usually

It  should  be  noted,  that  when  it  comes  to Affluence Rating (page  57), genii  locorum can  choose  to  live  at any  level  they  wish,  from  poor  to  rich.  The  glamour  is  a wonderful thing!

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000632_a4e6f0afd058e783c352bf6a36e9ecc036aa7ae0c3aa84fcb5e126c9e412aa75.png)

## Other Beings

This  section  contains  descriptions  of  several  of  the  key  demimonde characters and creatures found in the Rivers of London novels. Where an entry is for a generic type of being, an example profile is included for an average individual of that type. Specific individuals can vary considerably, just as much as people do, so feel free to tinker with the characteristics and skills if you are creating members of the demi-monde for use in a case file of your own.

## GM ADVICE

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000633_cec1a8fa0c660b438c00909a3053ccd89c9171dbdf74f5060c6eced54a118fda.png)

Members  of  the  higher  demi-monde-like  the  genii locorum-have a vestigia entry as part of their profile, describing  the  sensation  someone  gets  from  them if  they  succeed  with  a  Sense  Vestigia  roll.  For  really powerful demi-monde, i.e., Mama and Father Thames, there's no need for a roll-the power at that level is impossible to miss, even by people who usually don't know what they're sensing. Members of the lower demimonde, like Zachary  Palmer (page  256),  don't  usually have a specific vestigia listed-there's just something about them that those who are sensitive know is 'other.' There are some that do give off their equivalent of a signare when they use their thematic powers, though. These members of the lower demi-monde also have a vestigia listed as part of their profile.

## Beverley Brook

I'm  reliably  informed  that  Mama  Thames  found  Beverley floating in the brook one day, down by Kingston Vale dual carriageway. Now here she is, a genius loci, living in a house on Beverley Avenue, London. She is studying Environmental Science  at  Queen  Mary  University,  and  is  working  on her  dissertation  entitled  'The  Environmental  Benefits  of Waterway Reversion.' The two of us have been together for a while now-did I mention I'm going to be a dad? Twins apparently; double trouble, my Mum says.

When Beverley is around it's safest to assume that the front two rows may get wet. She has a thing with water, and is prone to stripping off and taking a dip at a moment's notice, which can be very distracting. Oh, and one time she flooded Covent Garden.

- Description: Beverley is an attractive Black woman of West African descent. I would say she had a cheeky face, but she'd probably kill me. She has long dreadlocks, and appears to be in her mid-20s. Her skin is smooth, unnaturally flawless, and sufficiently dark that I can't see when she's blushing. Her eyes are upwardly-tilted, like those of a cat; her dark brown irises are flecked with amber and gold around the pupils. Oh, and there's usually a sly smile playing on her luscious lips.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000634_83a13375fae64daa1961479ed72c126604be85b3da94d155029b25118d9466ba.png)

full powers in Greater London. She defers to her siblings (Effra, Fleet, and Tyburn) and Mama and Father Thames.

## Chimera

Chimeras  are  humans  who  have  been  magically  altered. These  strange  creatures  are  a  blend  of  human  and  animal, such  as  monkey,  tiger,  or  leopard-and  they  kind  of  look like anthropomorphised animals. For example, one might be covered in fur and have the claws, teeth, and eyes of a cat; Tiger Boy certainly did 24 .

Dr Walid explained to me that chimeras possess two types of  cells,  some  with  human  DNA,  some  with  animal  DNA. Nightingale made it clear that they are created through warped, dark magic. As altered humans, they speak and understand their mother tongue, and may also learn other languages.

## Dog, Police

Police  forces  across  the  world  use  dogs  for  a  variety  of  tasks including  sniffing  out  drugs  and  explosives,  finding  missing people,  and  catching  fleeing  criminals.  The  most  commonly used breed in the UK is the Belgian Malinois, which is like the German Shepherd only smaller and nimbler. Of course, we at the Folly have our very own 'police dog' named Toby (page 240).

## Police Dog, an example

60

80

## Father Thames

Prior  to  becoming  a genius  loci ,  Old  Man  Thames  was  an important Romano-Brit living in Londinium by the name of Tiberius Claudius Verica. In the mid-1800s, the Thames became so polluted as it passed through London that many of his sons (also genii locorum ) choked and died. In grief and anger,  Father  Thames  forsook  the  city  and  moved  inland and upriver, where he stayed. From 1966 onwards, the tidal section of the Thames gave rise to fresh genii locorum (Mama Thames, Beverley Brook, Lady Ty, and so on), which led to contesting claims over the dominion of the river, with Father Thames claiming ownership and Mama Thames asserting her authority in the light of his absence for over a century.

Today,  Father  Thames  rules  the  Thames  upstream  from Teddington Lock, on the west side of London. Many of the tributaries have their own genius loci :  the Old Man's sonssome old, some new.

His power is great-indeed, he could flood a whole city if he so wished. He has the gift of prophecy, but Oxley warned me off asking him direct questions, as there's often a price attached 25 .

- Description: these days, he's a short, white man with a pinched face and beaky nose. His bleak grey eyes have a dangerous sparkle and stare at you from beneath his prominent brow. Although he's many centuries old-almost a couple of millennia, in fact-he appears to be a man in his 70s, full of a sinewy vigour. His fashion's dated but he's always well-dressed in a smart country suit, with a bright yellow pocket square, a red waistcoat, and a gold-fob watch. Wisps of white hair protrude from beneath the battered homburg crammed on his head.
- Traits: he looks like an old Traveller/Sinti, with smiles and a gift for banter, but one time I witnessed him taking a piss, and the next day that ground was alive with wildflowers.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000637_affe2c43dbe7e721308f09a1e6052802dcaf8dafadd000690b018b683b41be81.png)

## Foxglove

Foxglove was separated from the fae decades ago after being sold by one of the High Fae queens in some mysterious trade deal.  Along  with  others  of  her  kind,  she  was  exploited  in London clubs before being isolated and trapped in a pit for 15 years. Then, one day, yours truly ended up in that same pit-it's a long story 26 .  Anyway, the two of us escaped and ended up back at the Folly, where it quickly became clear that Foxglove and Molly are bosom buddies.

- Description: a tall, slender fae woman, with elongated arms and long-fingered hands. Her slightly inhuman face is long and oval with high, prominent cheekbones. She has a small mouth and chin, and-just like Molly-there are far too many teeth in that mouth. Also, like Molly, she has a cascade of long, gleaming black hair. Foxglove's eyes are large and hazel-coloured, with little flecks of silver and gold in her irises. She tends to wear loose artist's smocks, which are usually spattered with paint from her ongoing artworks.
- Allegiances: very close to Molly.
- Traits: the modern world is something of a mystery to Foxglove. She doesn't speak, but expresses herself through her art and loves to paint and draw.

## Ghost

When a person dies, an imprint of their personality can be left upon their surroundings. Ghosts gain what little power they possess from the magic stored in their location. Stone is especially good at storing magic; old houses are often built

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000641_8e13f42a9a3288f0102ddf40e63c048ac3bfaf185f46dafcc9e19ee172552acd.png)

of stone, hence the reason why ghosts are common in such places. Over time, though, this store of magic fades and any associated ghost tends to degrade. Usually, it takes at least 30 years of magic build-up before you get a ghost 27 .

Ghosts vary in intensity. I grade them on the Annie scale: one  Annie  is  little  more  than  the  feeling  that  someone  is stood behind you; ten Annies and they appear as real as you or I. And if you want to get a ghost's attention, give them a werelight to feed on!

There are three types of ghost:

- Loopers: these are the most common. They're like a short recording being played on a loop. The recording may be seconds or hours in length.
- Simulacra: these are somewhere between loopers and entities, and can be likened to characters in a computer game-they possess a limited range of actions and speech, but these soon become repetitive.
- Entities: these ghosts appear to act like a real person, and may show a level of comprehension in their interactions.

Then  there  are  the  things  that  some  people  mistake  for ghosts,  such  as  revenants  and  'poltergeists.'  Revenants  are spirits out for revenge, and they can feed on ghosts. What people call poltergeists sometimes have other explanationsthey may be a possessed item or even a non-corporeal genius loci 28 . It also takes at least 100 years for something as exotic as a genuine poltergeist to develop, so the age of the building being  haunted  can  be  a  handy  guide  as  to  what  is  or  isn't actually going on.

## Ghost

Ghosts  do  not  have  statistics.  They  are  little  more  than illusory recordings that you can talk to. If it has statistics and claims it's a ghost, it's lying.

## High Fae

The High Fae are flesh and blood, but not like me and you. They  are  tall  and  thin,  with  long  delicate  faces  and  black eyes  (though  they  can  sometimes  be  other  colours,  like Foxglove's). It's all-natural fabrics and natural designs with them-William  Morris  would've  loved  it.  And  they  have an affinity with all of nature-plants and animals, and also some other-worldly animals like unicorns.

The High Fae don't coexist with us on the material plane. They live in a world apart from ours-I suppose you'd call it Fairyland. You have to see it for yourself to understand it, and even then, it's difficult. It's like each world is a layer on the screen, and the opacity setting of the layers can be altered up or down. We normally only see our layer, but there's another one lying just out of sight 29 .

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000642_94d6be6b9f299682958695f2dc84b37c123a08564f9f98d2b1e070d011e6aa78.png)

Footnotes:

6

They're  quite  able  to  defend  themselves-I've  seen  them carrying bows and arrows, and flint-tipped spears. Some wear armour made of overlapping, polished stone scales. However, as Polidori points out, they are vulnerable to cold iron.

## Horse

As a kid, I thought I'd seen horses at the city farms; turns out they were ponies-police horses are like Godzilla compared to them! (Though far less vicious and unstable than Godzilla. And the ponies.) Of course, being a mounted police officer takes years of training-the closest I'll ever get is if I employ some ragamuffin to follow me around clip-clopping a pair of coconut halves together.

## Lady Cecelia Tyburn Thames

Usually known as Ty, or Lady Ty (if you've got any sense), she's  the  eldest  of  Mama  Thames'  daughters,  and  the goddess  of  the  River  Tyburn.  She  makes  no  secret  of  the fact  that  she  read  History  and  Italian  at  Oxford,  though she's  a  little  less  forthcoming  about  crossing  paths  with the  Little  Crocodiles  Dining  Club  (a  private  club  made up of apprentice practitioners back in the 1990s) 30 .  Ty now works  as  a  civil  servant;  she  serves  in  numerous  political organisations, and has contacts in the civil service, business, and  the  government.  She's  pretty  loaded  too,  and  lives  in

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000644_bc9f510e935e5f0fda237eee94036021f6677ee5330bf3d44d9480e62eaec1f6.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000645_57e4497aa98152fdf6b410a7c50952cdf629bd903209b2182c59f8395125c92c.png)

Footnote: 30

Moon Over Soho

Mayfair with her husband, George McAllister-Thames, and two teenage children: Stephen and Olivia.

Her  predecessor,  Sir  William  of  Tyburn,  was  one  of Father Thames' sons. William died in the mid-19 th century during the Great Stink, a period of dreadful water pollution. As genius  loci of  the  same  location,  Ty  shares  some  of  Sir William's  memories.  She  can  also  do  something  like  what Molly does, like that one time she sent me on some sort of vision quest 31 .

- Description: a handsome Black woman with dark eyes and a straight nose (I'd call it 'media-friendly,' just not to her face), Lady Ty appears to be in her late 40s. Although lighter-skinned than her sister, Beverley, she has the same black, slanted cat-shaped eyes as her sister and mother.
- Allegiances: I think of her as an occasional ally, but one that must be handled with extreme care.
- Traits: aloof would be a polite word for it. She's keen to have things run her way, and is somewhat resentful of the Folly's intervention in the dealings of the demi-monde.

## Mama Thames

If  you  ever  find  yourself  knocking  on  the  door  of  an  old warehouse in Wapping, have a care. I know a bloke who did that a few decades back, and he's been there ever since. Mama Thames can have a strong influence over people. I was going

Footnote:

31 Lies Sleeping

to say I was prepared for it, but I'm not sure you can ever be fully prepared for that rush of power when it washes over you.

The woman who would become Mama Thames immigrated to the UK in 1957 from Nigeria to study nursing. Following an unhappy love affair, she found herself on London Bridge, thinking about throwing herself off. The River Thames came to her rescue and offered her the option of becoming a genius loci instead.

Her relationship with Father Thames is one of territorial rivalry over the river whose name they share. She is 'mother' to  a  number  of genii  locorum ,  notably  Beverley  Brook  and Lady Ty, and also Black Ditch, Brent, Crane, Effra, Fleet, Lea, Nicky, Olympia, and Chelsea. She also claims to own all musicians in London. Her power comes from the sea and the ports, and her glamour is about as irresistible as the tide.

- Description: an apparently middle-aged Black Nigerian woman, Mama Thames has a generous figure and perfect skin. Her face is unlined and is as dark as Beverley's, but rounder; her lips are full and dark, and she has the black, upwardly tilting, cat-shaped eyes inherited by her daughters. Her long hair is usually braided, styled by her coterie, and she tends to dress in Austrian lace.
- Allegiances: Mama Thames has an agreement with Nightingale not to interfere with Folly business (this sometimes rankles with Lady Ty).
- Traits: she smiles in a matronly way, and when she speaks, everyone listens.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000646_0073db09c0c229c6e022b1f0f21614ddbdd18862b46ca806ef78634dca4513db.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000647_377ca0080c10ea2d2ae3f2450a9deb89cb78e6383aa75bf26205c6595417c540.png)

## Molly

Molly is something of an enigma. She was rescued from a stage magician in 1911 and brought to the Folly 32 .  She has remained there of her own volition ever since. As a member of the fae, she doesn't show her real age, but she looks maybe mid-20s.  She  can  understand  English,  but  doesn't  speakthough  she  does  hiss  occasionally.  Since  I  moved  in,  she seems to have taken an interest in the world outside the Folly through social media and direct messaging.

Molly doesn't so much walk as glide, and she can move at great speed when she wants to. Also, she can perform haemomancy; that's divination through blood. If she feeds on a person's blood, she can increase their sensitivity to vestigia , extending it outward by several miles. I can tell you from personal experience that this carries dangers for the subject, not least of which is Molly can get a bit carried away while feeding-but there are other dangers of swimming among the city's vestigia -one can meet ghosts there, and some 'ghosts,' let me tell you, can be proper dangerous.

- Description: a small slim woman dressed like an Edwardian maid, in a starched uniform of black skirt, white bib, and white cotton blouse. She has a long, narrow, angular face, very pale skin, and black, almond-shaped eyes. Her long, black hair falls to her waist from beneath her maid's mob cap. She's looked like this for at least 70 years.
- Traits: she never speaks, but sometimes hisses. There's something disturbing about her silent presence, whether it's her strange eyes, the way she glides about, or the number of sharp small teeth that fill her wide mouth.

Footnote:

32 Moon Over Soho

- Allegiances: Molly is an integral part of the Folly, and has a longstanding and strong bond with Nightingale. More recently, she's become very fond of Toby. She's also very close to Foxglove.

## Quiet People

You've probably heard of mole-men and morlocks-well, I'm not sure about them, but the Quiet People are definitely down there, possibly under your feet right now. One of the groups Zachary  Palmer  refers  to  as  having  been  born  'different,' they went underground a long, long time ago, and live in a strange subterranean community below London. That's not the only thing that's different about them, either. Sgt Kumar once jokingly asked me if there were people who could 'bend' earth; I stupidly told him there weren't-how wrong was I?

The Quiet People are sometimes referred to as 'whisperers' on account of them favouring darkness and quiet communication. Recently, Zachary Palmer has been working as their guide to the upper world, taking them to pubs and introducing them to pop music. Quiet pop music, obviously. To be honest, they do look a bit like the kind of people that hang around in late-night jazz clubs, wearing shades and not saying  much.  They're  led  by  a  chap  named  Matthew  TenTons. Oh, and they keep pigs-really fat albino ones 33 .

- Description: long, pale faces, large eyes, and a strange round-shouldered posture. Other than that, they come in all shapes and sizes, and the most common hair colour is light brown. Most of the male ones I've met tend to wear hoodies and shades.
- Traits: pretty secretive, for obvious reasons, and they don't appreciate people poking their noses into their business. Also, pretty friendly and curious, once you get to know them.
- Allegiance: largely keep themselves to themselves, but currently attempting to acclimatise the younger generation to above ground living, one way or another. Close ties with Zach Palmer. On the Folly's radarand Lady Ty's.

## Talking Foxes

Foxes are a common sight in many British cities these days, especially London, and mixed in among them are their larger talking cousins. A quarter bigger and a third heavier than your common or garden Vulpes  vulpes, these  foxes  have  modified throats and can articulate language with the same facility as a human being. They also exhibit human levels of intelligence and  are  self-organising  along  mysterious  lines.  Abigail  says that  they  think  they're  spies  and  are  fighting  a  clandestine battle against a something they only refer to as Uncle Oboe 34 .

They  can  be  playful,  friendly,  and  helpful  when  it  suits them,  or  really  annoying  and  obstructive  when  the  mood takes them-no wonder they get on so well with Abigail.

There have been sightings of talking foxes as far afield as Herefordshire,  Wales,  and  Scotland.  It's  possible  they've

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000651_1d50c0af3721e4d6259e1f47a30b7d8723f47dcac70b02e428fdaed2ba137866.png)

Footnote:

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000652_e27e4943bb2aad159255a7dfea29e553d346c8825609e1e3a0a1586c862d91bc.png)

migrated  to  Europe  and  North  America  as  well,  but  no confirmed instances have been reported yet. London's foxes  certainly  understand  English  and  speak  their  own idiosyncratic version of it, full of spy jargon.

## Troll

Trolls are a type of fae who favour living on riverbanks or under brick or stone bridges. To look at, your average troll is indistinguishable from any other homeless person. Some do interact with humans-as a matter of fact, I know one who  works  as  a  PE  teacher  at  a  comprehensive  school  in Reading. Then there's  Nathaniel,  who  can  be  found  living under  Richmond  Bridge 35 .  Trolls  are  known  to  speak  the local language wherever they're found. It's not known if they have a language of their own.

## Unicorns

These things are large and powerful-the size of a very solid cart horse, except your average cart horse doesn't have a horn in the middle of its forehead. And, when I say a horn, I mean one that looks suspiciously like a narwhal's, only a good bit shorter (half a metre/yard) (yes, I know the narwhal's horn is actually a tooth, but you get what I mean).

Unicorns are usually invisible to the human eye-in our world,  at  least-but  become  visible  when  fed  on  magical energy (such as a werelight) 36 ; they're definitely visible if you happen to end up in Fairyland, though 37 . Their temperament is volatile at best. I'm sure they can be cute and cuddly, but the ones I've witnessed have pretty much all been violent and aggressive. They're closely affiliated with the High Fae, and like them, are vulnerable to cold iron.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000654_e22c0db7d4cda200d173ddc615c1ba6265b3f9c2337568d5c8ff745f3b0c61a3.png)

Unicorns  certainly  understand  spoken  English.  Whether they take any notice of you is a different matter altogether.

## Powers

Invisible to humans when in our world (unless in the presence of magic), Luck of the Demi-monde.

## Other

Can be ridden, but requires a successful Ride roll to convince the creature to let a rider mount it. Unless the rider is used to handling unicorns, it also requires a successful Ride roll to stay in control in situations where a normal horse wouldn't require a skill roll.

## Vampires

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000656_1eb7e8f6ecc8645de5d8f6d9ef0e34d3945efb687f12af07fefcb9128bd024dc.png)

looking at cross sections of their brains. Rather than sucking blood like the vampires you might be familiar with, the real ones feed on magical potential, both from the environment and the living. You can tell they're about by the intense cold they emit.

Vampires  can  look  like  anything-think  of  them  as mindless,  shambling  disease  vectors,  not  smart  enough  to use  tools  or  weapons  (thankfully!).  The  human  ones  have unnaturally smooth skin, pallid lips, and tend to look very ragged.  Even  bits  of  a  vampire  can  cause  you  all  sorts  of problems,  and  there  are  several  historical  examples  where someone decided to try weaponizing them 38 .

On my first  vampire  encounter,  Nightingale  and  I  used phosphorous grenades to dispatch a nest of them in a house in Purley. Frank Caffrey was there with his firefighters; he knows the score. In fact, tracking down a vampire's nest and waiting for them to go to sleep is by far the safest way of dealing with them-otherwise, you're looking at deploying sandbags, flamethrowers, and yet more phosphorous grenades in front of Joe Public, which can be a little tricky to explain.

## Vampire, a human example

CON 80

DEX 50

INT 20

POW 70

DB: +1*

MOV: 8

MP:

-

LUCK: 70

All vampires were living creatures-once. Nightingale refers to them as parasites. Dr Walid has studied them and has found extremely high levels of hyperthaumaturgical degradation when

Advantages: Natural Toughness, Damage Bonus. Disadvantage: vulnerable to intense heat (flamethrower, phosphorous grenades).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000657_df1008345bd69932ab77c954997181edff49232469340ebc50ec85cf58c0edbf.png)

38

Lies Sleeping

6

## Skills

Athletics 30%, Navigate 60%, Observation 60%, Read Person 30%, Sense Vestigia 60%, Social 30%, Stealth 60%; Fighting 60%.

## Languages

Own (e.g., English, German, etc.) 20%.

## Powers

Luck of the Demi-monde.

## Vestigia

Not as such, but tactus disvitae -the smell of antilife, a deep bone-numbing cold.

## Special

Every time a vampire successfully inflicts damage in combat, the target also loses 1D6 magic points. If a vampire manages to inflict either a mortal wound or a fatal blow, the character instantly loses all of their remaining magic points on top of the usual outcomes.

The  base  spell  damage  of  the  Fireball  spell  is  increased to 2 when used against vampires (boosted damage increase remains the same: 1 damage per additional magic point above base  cost).  Treat  flamethrowers  as  inflicting  Deadly  rather than Severe damage when deployed against vampires ( Table 7: Other Forms of Damage , page 149). Likewise, treat the Terminal damage of a phosphorous grenade as Splat damage. All  other  weapons  inflict  zero  damage  against  vampires (no  damage  roll  required).  Only  fire  completely  destroys  a vampire; any other method of disposal risks transmission or bits of vampire falling into the wrong hands.

Anyone  killed  by  a  vampire  risks  becoming  one  as  the infection is passed on. At the point of death, if the character fails  a  CON  roll,  they  join  the  unliving-unless  someone takes great care to burn their body.

## Zachary Palmer

Zachary traces his fae blood to his father, who, he states, was a  'fairy.'  Growing  up,  Zach  spent  some  time  in  St  Mark's Children's  Home.  He's  very  knowledgeable  about  the  demimonde of Britain, and can be called on for such tasks as locating a  goblin market. He is, however, conscious of his loyalty to the demi-monde; as I've found to my cost, his information is sometimes unreliable or comes at a price-usually in the form of food and/or drink. Oh, and he has a way with locks, and can somehow open them with little problem.

- Description: a tall, slim, white man who appears to be in his early 20s. Zachary has a big mouth and a thin, unshaven face, topped with a mop of brown hair. Usually seen wearing a grubby grey hoodie that's going at the elbows.
- Traits: Zach is a bit of a lad; cheeky and evasive-the kind of person that if you lent something to him, you'd never be quite sure if you'd see it again. His relationship with truth is very flexible, and he's keen on slang, such as 'the Isaacs' (Folly practitioners). Oh, and don't get me started on how much food he can put away!
- Allegiances: he might be considered a police informant, though he's not always reliable. He's well known by the Quiet People and is currently acting as their aboveground guide and liaison officer.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000658_66ba413c8e8b5b9b718327ad002f10b9a490e647a037d7d5bdad295baf53d66f.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000659_1dc839af4b5adc63d4fbb9ea9a2786775130e6e2e072cea9429c270688a22b47.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000660_ff932d5cf2e4a9e2a5b286f3dbd6e54c148817cd30c44811cd36778b2f4a04a1.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000661_aed0fcb91991cea91c760b6c008d42d97254e7471b8bff2d64fb50ba6ba527b5.png)

LONDON, CAPITAL CITY of the United Kingdom and home of the Society of the Wise, better known as 'the Folly'the heart of Newtonian magic in the UK, at least as far as policing is concerned! This chapter aims to give the Game Moderator (GM) and players-and their investigators-an overview of the Folly's history and associations, as well as of magical London. It is presented as an induction session given by Sergeant Anthony Finnegan, one of the Folly's recently recruited members of staff 1 , aimed at getting new officers and civilian associates up to speed.

The  GM  can  use  the  following  sections  directly  as  an in-character introduction for their investigators to the workings of the Society of the Wise and its headquarters, or they can paraphrase and adapt the material provided for their own purposes, such as when presenting case files. The GM is free to use the information as they will: it could be shared verbally,  via  player  handouts,  or  even  as  computer-screen presentations during character generation or in the course of starting case files. Scattered throughout the following sections, you'll  also  find  'case  seeds,'  which  suggest  potential  plot ideas and show how a GM can use the information provided in this chapter to design case files of their own.

## ADDITIONAL RULES

For more on creating your own case files, see Writing a Case File (page 336).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000662_08d77045b6d78b685424eb8c7cde158f7fccd3a6f52bb2f35d9e825ea7da47f0.png)

## GM ADVICE

This  chapter  contains  spoilers-potentially  lots  of them. While we've tried to draw a veil over some of the biggest  surprises  to  be  found  in  Ben's  stories,  if  you haven't read all the books and graphic novels, you may wish to check which sections have footnotes to identify the ones you might want to avoid-for now, at least.

Also, there are a few acronyms in the text-if you need  help  understanding  what  they  mean,  don't forget Table  12:  A  Glossary  of  Common  Police Acronyms and Slang (page 214).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000663_c1dea488da94305768479b3c4a09782a17c85b3532bcc9503ec1a3ce320ec9cd.png)

## Induction

Right.  Morning  all.  Welcome  to  the  Folly,  the  official home of English Magic since 1775, and maintainer of the Queen's Peace in the realm of the supernatural since before the Metropolitan Police Service was founded. My name is Sergeant Anthony Finnegan, and DCI Thomas Nightingale (page 233) has asked me to give you this introduction. First, there's  a  lecture  on  the  history  of  magic  in  the  United Kingdom, the types of practitioner you might encounter, and some of the other agencies we routinely deal with. After that, I'll take you for a tour of the building. Then it's back to the briefing  room  for  an  overview  of  London  as  it  pertains  to Falcon business, or 'weird bollocks,' as DCI Seawoll calls it. Even if you know London like the back of your hand, you can still learn something new every day.

## The History of Magic in the UK

Magic  dates  back  to  the  dawn  of  humanity.  Shamans  in ancient  times  led  rituals  to  protect  their  tribes  or  ensure good hunting, while medieval witches might have known a trick or two passed down through their family. There were secret  groups  like  the Sons  of  Weyland (page  262)  with magical  specialties  connected  to  a  trade,  and  alchemists trying  to  turn  lead  into  gold.  In  the  17 th century,  Sir Isaac  Newton  discovered  the  principles  that  let  magic  be performed successfully and safely. He wrote three Principia : the  first, Philosophiæ  Naturalis  Principia  Mathematicaor Mathematical  Principles  of  Natural  Philosophy ,  published  in 1687-was on mechanics. The second: Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia  Artes  Magicis ,  laid  out  the  principles  of  magic  as they are still taught and used by the Society of the Wise, aka the  Folly  ( Chapter  4: Newtonian Magic ,  page  161).  His unpublished third book, Magni Operis Principia Chemica -or Chymical Principles of the Great Work -got stolen in 1719 by Jack Shepherd 2 .

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000664_585dce60fe44900967869ac01c67881282ade74ff79486f92c1bf539bae0ef21.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000665_195a792fb1c645e5238aebcbf94ed001cac585ce74c539a219dfb7156f78a5a9.png)

7

## The Society of the Wise

The Newtonian practitioners who formed the Society of the Wise originally met at a floating coffee house on the River Thames called 'The Folly,' moored outside Somerset House. They were little more than a dodgy secret society, but had the favour of Queen Caroline, who helped form La Société de la Rose (page 263). Originally, both men and women could be  members,  but  when  the  Society  moved  into  an  actual folly (a fake medieval tower) on the grounds of the Earls and Dukes of Bedford's London estate, the membership elected to change, and become male only. A more 'respectable' but less inclusive club. Thus, fellows of the Society may use the initials  'FSW'  (Fellow  of  the  Society  of  the  Wise)  after their name. Sir Victor Casterbrook was the first recognised Society president and is the only person apart from Sir Isaac with a bust in the atrium.

At the end of the 18 th century, Francis Russell, the fifth Duke  of  Bedford,  sold  off  his  estate  and  created  Russell Square,  surrounded  by  posh  Regency  gaffs.  He  let  the Society move to a new club house in the south terrace, but they kept the old name.

Historically,  the  Folly  wasn't  all  that  organised.  It  was part gentlemen's club and part secret and unofficial arm of the British establishment. Fellows served in an ad hoc way: doing  research,  working  for  the  Home  Office,  or  helping the  Metropolitan  Police  via  a  variety  of  arrangements  or agreements. To facilitate this, volunteer wizards were sworn in  as  constables,  with  everything  that  entails  ( Constable , page  197).  They  were  listed  on  Scotland  Yard's  rolls  as inspectors, but with their salaries paid by the Folly, so the Society could keep some degree of control. These agreements are still an aspect of our work-they can make an officer's job difficult, but when you're dealing with deities and the demimonde, they're a necessary evil.

The Society of the Wise was at its height in the late 19 th and  early  20 th centuries  and  insisted  that  no  one  could practise  magic  in  the  UK  without  its  permission.  Many ignored that, and the Folly ignored them if they weren't a problem,  especially  if  they  were  women.  Things  changed with World War II (WWII), when most of Britain's wizards died at Ettersberg (page 262), after which DCI Nightingale became Britain's last official wizard. It was believed magic had declined so much that no more were needed. In 1953, the guvnor formalised the Folly's relationship with the Met, joining as a Detective Inspector-later DCI. The Folly, in its aspect as a policing body, was incorporated into the Met's structure, with the building leased as a police station. DCI Nightingale  is  answerable  only  to  the  Commissioner  and, beyond them, the Home Secretary.

with  is  dealing  with  the  supernatural  within  the  United Kingdom.  Police  officers  are  generally  a  sceptical  bunch, and  many  of  them  are  more  comfortable  using  the  term 'Falcon'-the name applied to operations within the Folly's remit-rather than accepting magic exists. Our call sign is Zulu Foxtrot.

When a series of strange murders were committed in 2012 by London's spirit of riot and rebellion, aka Mr Punch 3 , DCI Nightingale recruited then-PC Peter Grant (page 232) as his apprentice. As the rumours concerning the decline of magic weren't accurate, the Folly is now expanding and recruiting officers  and  consultants  in  accordance  with  the  recently sanctioned Folly Expansion document, which is why you're here listening to me.

We also identify other practitioners, and there's a steadily growing list of nominals, aka suspects, that needs checking. Remember, the existence of magic isn't officially recognised, and  it  isn't  illegal  to  cast  spells.  The  Witchcraft  Act  1735 was replaced with the Fraudulent Mediums Act in 1951, and even that was repealed in 2008. If someone has a complaint against  a  psychic  or  sorcerer  for  being  a  fraud,  it  comes under  general  consumer  protection  these  days.  Genuine practitioners we've identified who aren't otherwise criminals are asked to swear an oath and undertake training for their own safety. Do magic wrong, and it can damage your brain ( Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation ,  page  177).  And  we don't want any of that, do we?

## County Practitioners

Before the war, every county had an official  County Practitioner (CP) who looked out for supernatural problems. Their daily records were sent to the Folly once a year and then filed, often without anyone actioning anything. Let's just say they did a variable job. Some were meticulous, some slapdash, some got carried away with pet projects, while some-like George Buckland (1742-1815)-got up to no good. Buckland was the first Society member to face a disciplinary tribunal, probably for unethical practices involving ghosts-not that it  stopped  him  or  his  descendants.  Then-PC  Grant  found ghosts trapped in Rose Jars (page 326) in the basement of his old house, in Chesham 4 . We took the now-empty jars for safekeeping. The CP files could reveal other old pet projects that've turned feral ( A Pet Project , nearby).

## Hedge Witches and Wizards

Officially, the Folly is the Met's Special Assessment Unit (SAU) , although we were 'Economic and Specialist Crime Unit 9' up until a few years ago. What we're actually charged

The  term  hedge  witch  or  hedge  wizard  generally  means a  magical  practitioner  without  Folly  training,  or  a  Society member  who  has  formally  retired  and  'rusticated'  to  the country. It used to be slang for any country-dwelling witch or  wizard,  but  there  are  plenty  in  London  these  days.  For example,  George  Nolfi  of  Wimbledon-of  the  werelight party  trick  fame 5 -who  learned  the  spell  from  his  mum.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000666_805619ac8ffce8c489713fa435d500d33807b018dfb50068fa60c5eac1e49e5e.png)

Others  develop  magical  abilities  on  their  own,  sometimes due to neurological conditions, like Mrs Edna Phillips, the occasional car-killer of Cable Street 6 . The Folly has files on rusticated Society Fellows, like Hugh Oswald, who suffered from  post-traumatic  stress  disorder  (PTSD)  after  WWII and retired to Herefordshire to keep bees 7 .

When I say files, I mean old manila folders. If you want to know what they say, you have to dig it out the old-fashioned way; there's none of this computer search malarky-yet. You may be assigned the transferral of this information to DC Grant's new standalone database as one of your duties.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000667_1896e314aa51bda5a5a949301bc81f0485d25901625f48dd9298ac3bddb583e6.png)

## Case Seed: A Pet Project

Someone has bought and restored a former County Practitioner's home, which has lain derelict for ages. Since moving in,  every  night  they  have  been  woken by tapping on the window. Opening the curtains, they see a huge rook outside. They've tried ignoring it, but now rooks are gathering in increasing numbers. Is this  an  ordinary  phenomenon,  or  did the long-dead practitioner bind a spirit in  bird  form  as  his  familiar,  in  a  contract to serve the master of the house until dismissed? If so, is it now an angry supernatural threat?

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000668_fc83c0dffee91fa71fed9a7872db248eeb6fa749b2c2084ecddecb5611daf612.png)

## The Special Collection

Oxford, 52 miles-sorry, 84 kilometres-upriver from London, is home to one of England's most famous universities,  with  gleaming  spires,  academia's  top  brains, and the UK's most impressive collection of magical books. Although Newton was a Cambridge man, his magical papers are at Oxford.

Harold  Postmartin (page  235),  DPhil,  FRS,  Curator of  Special Collections at Oxford's Bodleian Library, is the Folly's archivist, curating our rarest books and manuscripts, as  well  as  keeping  track  of  the  unwritten  agreements  that constitute  English  magical  law  enforcement.  The  Special Collection  is  in  the  university's  Radcliffe  Science  Library, stored  underground  in  climate-controlled  rooms,  behind  a metal door with a swipe-card-and-number security system. The index is on an unhackable stand-alone obsolete computer. Every  book  is  uniquely  marked.  Dr  Postmartin  enjoys challenging new recruits to crack his identification code.
