## Casterbrook

The Casterbrook boarding school lies on the Ambrose Estate, near  the  village  of  Stokenchurch,  Buckinghamshire 8 . It taught magic to boys, mainly those from professional family backgrounds, training them as Fellows of the Society. The school  was  requisitioned  during  WWII  but,  afterwards, there weren't enough pupils or teachers for it to reopen. DCI Nightingale has keys to the main gate and building. There's not  much  left  inside,  except  the  names  of  the  honoured dead; wizards who  died serving their  country-many at  Ettersberg-carved  on  the  wall  of  the  school  hall  by Nightingale. The Folly owns the estate, which is part of our property portfolio-that means we have a healthy operational budget, unlike most Met departments. Money from estate farm  rents  also  pays  for  maintenance  of  the  Casterbrook building, Ambrose House.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000672_25cbcda8598553e1f1c4b6252e488120bf0079c28d227e7a1199386714476200.png)

## Case Seed: It's in the T rees

A  tenant  farmer  contacts  the  Folly complaining  that  something  is  killing his  cattle.  It  comes  from  the  woods at  night,  mostly.  He's  tried  hunting  it, but 'it don't seem natural.' Did a Casterbrook pupil or teacher leave something monstrous to grow in the estate grounds, or is it a normal wild beast?

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000673_4a34f9cd2ad6b1fbf9a128ff25144add3a241bd109ef096a38a44bc2f0e9b9b1.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000674_0a8a8d25ddb033d83bbfdebfe8dc162acf889c69aee2c3c56728577c41c7aaaa.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000675_922b2f11f0cf6b074bb5ff92d640546ff784a57071f838eb4e970c564b740ccf.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000676_50da5c793e78f8874d82cfa444f3eac23f6506bc5c60d8faeaae82c18ea0afec.png)

Footnote: 8 Moon Over Soho

7

## Sons of Weyland

From the north of England, the Sons of Weyland claim to be the first true magic practitioners. Their craft is smithing; that is, binding magic into metal. They know horse lore, too. Many travelled around the ancient horse fairs where they had connections with old families and the old ways of the land. Some still possess such connections, although such folk are dwindling in numbers. The Sons of Weyland were willing to train Society Fellows who put in the hard graft necessary to win their approval 9 . DCI Nightingale is one.

## Ettersberg

In  the  latter  days  of  WWII,  British  Military  Intelligence discovered  the  Nazis  were  conducting  horrible  magical experiments  on  live  prisoners:  men,  women,  and  fae.  The research data was stored in 100 filing cabinets-the so-called Black Library 10 -in a bunker near a town called Ettersberg. Folly wizard David Mellenby thought the library could help his research into the science of where magical power comes from, so he devised Operation Spatchcock to acquire the data and stop others getting it.  On  January  19 th 1945,  Britain's wizards secured the cabinets, but at a terrible cost.

Although  Hugh  Oswald  escaped  with  the  cabinetsthanks to then-Captain Nightingale magically blowing up two Tiger tanks while covering the extraction-three out of every five British wizards of military age died as a result of the battle:  2,396  people  in  all.  Mr  Mellenby  survived,  but later shot himself in his lab at the Folly. Most of those still alive were too ill to continue practicing, or broke their staffs and  retired.  Nightingale  remained  active  with  a  couple  of elderly wizards, who are both now dead. He assumed magic was  a  spent  force,  and  so  it  seemed  at  the  time.  However, magic  bubbled  under  the  surface,  and  continued  to  be practised by the demi-monde, other organisations, and the criminal underworld.

## Other Organisations

The Folly isn't the only magic society in existence, though it is the only officially recognised one. There are a few others it would be sensible for you to be aware of.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000677_f5a31eaf7fdc5857d3d398a30c5077603cd7ab7684a84b6f26e5cdff2f8ae6fa.png)

Footnotes: 9 Broken Homes, 10 Foxglove Summer

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000678_ac96eb49813687525c8d76936635c62603338122b9b5f305248595ad88e37317.png)

## Little Crocodiles

Professor Geoffrey Wheatcroft, DD, DPhil, FSW, got his staff in 1939. After the war, he retired to a theology teaching post  at  Magdalen  College,  Oxford,  but  in  the  mid-1950s he  formed  a  male  students'  dining  club  called  the  Little Crocodiles, where he secretly taught magic 11 . Most students didn't take it seriously and learned only the basics, but oneAlbert Woodville-Gentle-achieved what DCI Nightingale calls 'mastership.' In the 1960s and '70s, Woodville-Gentle committed numerous atrocities, including creating cat-girls and  tiger-boys  for  the  Garden  of  Unearthly  Delights  club in  Soho 12 ( Soho ,  page  272).  He  magically  concealed  his features, earning himself the nickname 'the Faceless Man,' and helped train the man who took over his legacy around the turn of the millennium, who we call Faceless Man 2.

## La Société de la Rose

Women trained as magical practitioners in Queen Caroline's court.  They  were  taught  by  Phillip  Boucherett,  a  former protégé  of  Sir  Isaac  Newton,  and  continued  to  learn  the forms and wisdoms in the salons of 18 th century intellectual Elizabeth Montague 13 . They called themselves La Société de la Rose , or the Society of the Rose, which developed separately from the male preserve of the Society of the Wise.

Magic  was  then  taught  mother  to  daughter  by  society members.  Current  members  include  Lady  Helena  Louise Linden-Limmer  and  her  adopted  daughter  Lady  Caroline. Lady Helena is an expert on healing techniques. She taught them to Albert Woodville-Gentle in London in the 1960s, and  was  understandably  horrified  to  discover  how  he'd used  them.  In  2014,  the  Society  of  the  Rose  worked  with the Folly to recover Newton's stolen alchemical manuscript. Lady Helena and Lady Caroline got the book, and our two societies remain on good terms. Lady Caroline prefers to call herself a witch or sorceress rather than a practitioner.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000679_54c1e65befe341dd7786a7aefa0554832fff49bdf0a407d7343217f8e58cd4f1.png)

## Case Seed: The Wise Woman

A  woman has  put  in  a  complaint  that a  sorceress she consulted for magical services to get her errant husband back took her money, but her husband has now gone completely missing. Is this a case of a scam by a fake psychic, or is the sorceress a member of the Society of the Rose, who felt she was doing the woman the greatest favour by ensuring her rotten husband was gone for good?

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000680_c1ee4f104746b6bfd49f0522296b022b791e7c8d9d6871298f3084f8f7cbfacf.png)

Footnotes:

11

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000681_871083ce4d04f0f49baca0f7847067b35480ebdc3b9f552ef5e9ff1f6ac9a59b.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000682_897c4df2d8392cf9977a0c0b719c6c8f91d2429e52acec89716f9a5f5263d707.png)

Whispers  Under  Ground,

13 The Hanging Tree

Moon  Over  Soho,

## The Paternoster Society

The  Paternoster  Society  poses  as  a  historical  society  with a  particular  interest  in  London  and  Arthurian  legends. Publicly, it funds archaeological digs. Privately, however, its members are unofficial magical practitioners. Some trained at  Oxford,  including  the  now-deceased  John  Chapman. Members rent a side annexe at St Paul's Youth Hostel, on Carter Lane, next to St Paul's Cathedral. Its rooms are an imitation of the Folly, with antique furnishings and a portrait of Sir Isaac Newton. A cabinet once held a sword discovered by 18 th century antiquarian William Winston Galt, allegedly in a cellar at Paternoster Row. It was identified by Camilla Turner  of  Museum  of  London  Archaeology  (MOLA)  as being 5 th century Saxon 14 . The society like to think it's King Arthur's Excalibur. Tracing other members of this group is on our action list.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000683_5224cb57f5b9ed14b3e192b39b54e9485028e6e979f67618bee32b3cc0e638b2.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000684_3625af91571e2fba5533d22a16b88cdb550d2eb06f99c748bbfd8a8d1e8afa29.png)

12

7

## The Folly

Now for a tour of our building. It's somewhat grander than the usual section house, I think you'll agree. You might have spotted the words ' Scientia Potestas Est ' and a cartouche of the  Folly's  coat  of  arms  above  the  front  door.  That's  Latin for  'knowledge  is  power;'  good  advice  for  any  thief-taker, wizard or not.

## Ground Floor

Up until now, you've been in what was the visitor's lounge (1), off the entrance lobby on the ground floor. That's the first floor to you Americans. It's now used as the briefing room, and the antique furniture got replaced with metal desks. On the walls you  can  see  portraits  of  Sir  Isaac  Newton,  Queen  Caroline, and the fifth Duke of Bedford, as well as the whiteboard.

There aren't  any  computers  here  or  anywhere  else  inside the  main  building  as  magic  sands  their  chips  ( Spells and Technology ,  page 176). Anything with a microprocessor is at risk of sanding in here, with the exception of the custody suite-most of the time. Valves, though-they're fine. The Folly has wards to protect the building from external magic and  the  like;  they  were  renewed  in  1940  after  the  Post Office  installed  the  phones,  then  again  recently  after  the basement was converted into the custody suite. Opposite is one of the old smoking rooms (5). Remember: no smoking indoors these days.

Beyond the entrance lobby is the atrium (7). It's the poshest room  in  the  building,  with  pillars,  balconies  around  the upper galleries, a domed glass roof, and a big bust of Sir Isaac. It's also the warmest room, apart from the kitchen, in winter.

Doors on the right lead to the dining room (13) and the mundane library (6). The ladder on brass rails isn't just to reach the top stacks of the bookshelves. It leads to a concealed door onto the mezzanine above. Remember what I said about no computers? The library index system is card based, arranged by subject, with a master index arranged by title. This is kept up to date with book details and records of magical things, including ghost sightings. The lecture hall (11) is at the back, with what was once the ladies' gallery. Beyond that's a back door to the mews; go there if you need a ciggy. The WCs are on the atrium's left.

There're two sets of stairs-and secret passages. Three wall panels off the atrium are actually concealed doors to servants' corridors.  The  doors  have  spyholes,  and  the  corridors  are narrow,  bare  brick,  and  just  single  lightbulbs,  which  says everything  about  how  wizards  viewed  the  working  classes back in the day.

## Basement

Below  ground,  there's  the  original  kitchens  and  sculleries, kept as Molly, our cook and housekeeper, likes them. Molly (page 252) has her own space down here, but it's private, so

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000685_6256d3ce06ff1774dcf4bd16fb877f3a15a3bd9ed8f00ab8eac95558ba0a2a60.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000686_27c81bdf246dd6ac2392cd35edb997e4c2f531990bd4723114f38eed1e98beb8.png)

keep your noses out if you don't want them bitten off by Toby (page 240). He has previous 15 . Molly's privacy, among other things, is why we leave the basement off the plans-we don't want outsiders poking around down here.

The bits of the basement I've seen most have recently been modernised to bring them up to current police standards. The vintage gym and shooting range were converted into a brandnew PACE-compliant custody suite, where us custody officers have our desk. A steel door from the rear entrance (14) opens onto a vestibule, then more steel doors throughout. We have six state-of-the-art cells, with toilets, a search room, a medical examination  suite,  an  exercise  room,  and  showers.  Our interview room is deliberately sparse. It has three metal chairs bolted down, facing three more bolted chairs. Being able to watch body language is useful when interviewing practitioners. The middle chair is 2 inches-sorry, 5 cm-forward of those either side to make the subject feel more exposed.

At the north end of the suite, with windows to the front, is Foxglove's art studio, and her private space. If you think that's an odd place for an art studio, it's deliberate. Foxglove (page 248) is a fae who generates a field that negates magic. The Magical Suppression Area, or MSA, stops practitioners in the cells from casting spells, which is nice.

There's a maze of old brick-lined corridors in the basement, leading  to  former  servant  quarters,  store  rooms,  and  other things. They're not much used: dimly lit and full of cobwebs mostly. This one here, to the left, that looks like it ends in a plain wooden door, is out of bounds unless DCI Nightingale says otherwise ( Secure Storage , nearby).

The Folly has an agreement that it can confiscate dangerous magical  items.  Some  of  these  are  kept  down  here.  If  they aren't too dangerous, they're kept in storerooms with some magical  dampening,  from  cork  insulation  to  greenwood panels  to  Faraday  cages.  One  holds  the  Book  of  Cunning Device 16 , a sort of sentient book-cum-musical instrument that

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000687_09db1db597236de1d09f81fba79621271895aac69af8e257aaa515169e6ebd6e.png)

## Secure Storage

Beyond  the  plain  door  is  a  short  corridor and then a grey, steel door without  visible  handles  or  locks,  incised with overlapping circles, which opens by  magic.  The  room  beyond  is  secure storage  containing  the  Black  Library, the Mary Engine, and other dangerous magical  things.  As  Sgt  Finnegan  says, it's out of bounds to everyone without DCI Nightingale's express permission.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000688_71462906ff23d1e6c205284fcb1b3f19ee5a9a4e37e15fb8c1c7ac84f531eeee.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000689_24b7f1645b2516ead98b67342c879d1ca26766d02b4c97f4a41a04ec5e464447.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000690_36c88ac7e7625f2e7e34953583502e8ee1c8e106e823fdecb9bba5ec30a8526d.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000691_a3c5c944c9ceee9679d4e89ae959a7c3ee63d07eda82d54ef3f4cd493d0a3aba.png)

7

DC Grant trapped at the British Library. Given the chance, it likes to go for a wander or fly off using its cover as leathery wings. One action on the to-do list is checking the state of the  magical  dampening  in  minor  storerooms.  Another  is taking the Book for walkies ( Case Seed: Walkies , following).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000692_2d8c66856498bf733b5d3e7dec9a31dbf8c7f11aaeee3042e097a7942324d39f.png)

## Case Seed: Walkies

The  investigators  are  asked  to  take the Book of Cunning Device for a walk around  the  Folly  for  exercise.  It  isn't allowed  outside  the  building.  At  first, the book seems easily controlled, but if they take it into any room containing other books, it gets excited and skitters up the shelves, dumping tomes on the investigators'  heads.  After  they  have spent some time trying to catch it, the Book finds what it was seeking: an old volume  that,  when  opened,  is  obviously  infested  with  bookworms.  The Book has been trying to warn the investigators of a serious threat to its less communicative  siblings.  How  will  the junior  Folly  members  deal  with  that? And are  those  bookworms  more  than mundane larvae?

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000693_453d684454ca4bb957ee8e707dbb44507734d05d3369d97f2abc171eb1adcde3.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000694_f9ba5248e8ac175a6e968cc63c86fdd192350802a5fc7b8bd32e686cfd2e40d3.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000695_4bc404b7256dec1040a690a0c20dd5d2efacb3b5370b224018379f94cb48436c.png)

## Rear Mews and Coach House

You can get out to the courtyard mews at the back via the atrium,  the  basement,  and  the  steel  door  in  the  custody suite.  The  mews  is  the  vehicle  entrance,  with  a  wide  gate onto Bedford Place (17), complete with an intercom system. Next to that is the pedestrian entrance, which leads to the access  ramp  to  the  custody  suite's  door.  On  the  other  side of  the  courtyard  is  the  old  coach  house  (15).  The  bottom floor  is  a  large  garage  where  we  park  the  pool  car.  DCI Nightingale's  Jag  and  the  other  vintage  vehicle-allegedly the  most  haunted  car  in  Britain 17 -in  there  are  off  limits. The wrought-iron spiral staircase leads to the coach house's attic.  It  used  to  be  some  sort  of  artist's  studio  back  in  the day, but now DC Grant calls it 'the tech cave,' because it's outside the magical no-tech zone. You can use laptops there, and watch telly. The ottoman couch and chaise longue are

comfy, and the fridge usually has beer. We keep half a dozen Airwave handsets on a charging rack up there, and there's a HOLMES 2 terminal as well. We put a Portakabin (16) in the courtyard when extra IT staff are needed-it plays havoc with the parking, but what can you do?

## Mezzanine and First Floor

There's  a  mezzanine  level  between  the  ground  and  first floors, but it isn't much used, which is why you'll not find it on any plans of the Folly. Anyone studying at the Folly will be spending a lot of time in the labs (28) and classrooms on the first floor-that's the second floor in American. This first lab looks like an old-fashioned school science room, but the scorch marks in the benches are nothing to do with Bunsen burners.  The  sink  and  fire  extinguisher  are  in  the  corner, there. Beyond the study (29) is the breakfast room (30). The picture on the wall, by our resident artist Foxglove, is called ' American Woman with a Notepad and a Small Annoying Dog.' You might recognise FBI Agent Kimberley Reynolds (page 237), and Toby, the Folly's vestigia hound. There's also a  small  dining  room  (27):  old  wizards  did  a lot of  eating. The first floor has the magical library (18), lined with glassfronted mahogany bookcases, and two reading rooms (19, 22), with  green  leather  armchairs-very  comfy  for  a  quiet  kip after all that eating. Lastly, this floor has the records office (20) and a billiard room (24).

## Second Floor

Back in the day, most Folly wizards had rooms on the second floor  (39).  There's  also  another  smoking room-cum-parlour (41)  and  a  refectory  (40).  Yes,  more  eating.  DC  Grant's room  was  up  here,  and  you'll  have  been  allocated  one  if you're staying on site, but most are unused. Some are locked, including one that belonged to Mr Mellenby. Rare books are in the room over there (35). In this corner is the smithy (32): a metal workshop with a forge and anvil.

## Third Floor

Important  wizards  had  their  suites  on  the  third  floor  (55). The Master's suite (44) is at the front, as well as his office (46). There's a posh lounge for the senior members (56)-not much used now-and a glass-roofed arboretum (47) that extends up into the attic.

## Attic

Tucked up in the roof space is the attic; it includes additional bedrooms for junior practitioners and servants-not slept in since 1945. There's also an observatory (59) with a small glass dome. Foxglove sometimes likes to sleep up there.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000696_0d4bd433789010bcfae9aafd8ff3882901e7fbd9003c23df56d67cabbeaf586e.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000697_9a6f0b4ec97b252e77329b473128e509dfc1f985aa63ea145f448ac5b1493385.png)

267

## Teatime

Molly serves tea and cakes in the atrium at break time, or in the breakfast room if we're busy. Everything stops for tea, just like it must've done for those old wizards. If you don't fancy eating in, Russell Square has an excellent park café.

## Police Stations and Other Facilities

Now we're back in the visitor's  lounge,  let's  continue  with the rest of your induction. The Folly is an operational police station investigating Falcon cases. Here are some of the other stations and departments we deal with.

## GM ADVICE

The numbers in brackets after a location's name refer to the Map of Central London on pages 270-271.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000698_58df7f5b4768936df78581aaaa89049eae40c21002a2be5fc37061d6b074dcee.png)

7

Senior Investigation Officer, and his colleague DI Miriam Stephanopoulos (page 238). DC Grant used to share a desk at AB's Outside Inquiry Office with DS Sahra Guleed (page 234) and DC David Carey when working with AB on Falcon cases. DS Guleed is now a member of the Folly team.

Although  the  Folly  has  since  acquired  our  own  special custody suite for magical practitioners, two cells in Belgravia's custody suite were modified by fitting enchanted strips of iron inlaid with copper filigree on the doors, hidden under blue paint. If you want to chat with officers outside the station, the AB local is a Victorian pub over the road.

## Charing Cross Police Station (4)

Charing Cross Police Station (CX) is on Agar St, Westminster,  and  is  where  officers  Grant  and  May  were probationary constables after leaving Hendon Police College. Young officers from CX are regularly on the beat around  Covent  Garden  (1).  That's  how  then-PCs  Grant and  May  first  encountered  the  deceased  actor  Nicholas Wallpenny 18 ,  and,  ultimately,  why  you're  listening  to  me prattle on now.

## Belgravia Police Station (5)

The Westminster  Murder  Team  is  based  at Belgravia Police  Station  (AB)  on  Buckingham  Palace  Road.  We've often  worked  with  DCI  Alexander  Seawoll,  the  team's

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000699_b376e4ed02a2bead5933b7a17085243d21ab1e76af022a8eddf09ea1861f3477.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000700_cc53b0bfbb7b2df8f4112a98f78e473c678c98fa9e818f6faa1fd8c8b8833788.png)

Footnote:

## Kentish Town Police Station

Situated  on  Holmes  Road,  in  Kentish  Town  (EK),  this station  is  close  to  Hampstead  Heath.  Our  contact  there  is DC Karen Jonquiere, or Kay. She has had special training in  interviewing  children  and  teens,  and  was  involved  in  a Falcon case involving local youths 19 . Abigail Kamara (page 237), DC Grant's half-cousin, has had most contact with her.

## Walworth and Southwark Police Stations

South of the river, Walworth Police Station (MS) at Manor Place runs the East Walworth Safer Neighbourhood Team, which was investigating drug dealing from Skygarden (page 273). Sergeant William Daverc liaised with the Folly to get officers  Grant  and  May  into  the  tower  as  residents  during their investigation 20 . He's still a good contact, although he's now at Southwark (MD), on Borough High Street.

## Bromley and Dulwich

Bromley town centre's police station (PY) is a new red-brick purpose-built building on the High Street. Bromley Murder Investigation  Team  is  headed  by  DCI  Maureen  Duffy, who has worked with the Folly in the past ( Bromley ,  page 282). Also in South East London, in Dulwich, is the Met's Operational Technology Support Unit.

## New Scotland Yard (6)

Several buildings in Westminster have had the name Scotland Yard:  symbolising  British  policing,  and  the  Metropolitan Police  Service's  official  headquarters.  The  original  London police  force  HQ  was  in  Great  Scotland  Yard,  Whitehall, from where it got the name. In 1890, New Scotland Yard was built on the Thames Embankment. It moved to a tower block on  Victoria  Street  in  1967,  then  returned  to  the  Victoria Embankment in 2016.

The  Commissioner  of  the  Metropolitan  Police  Service  is based there. They are fully informed about the Folly's workings and have authority…'  over our police operations. You also need the Commissioner's approval before you can be sworn into the Society of the Wise. The Deputy Assistant Commissioner of Police, Richard Folsom, is an acolyte of Lady Ty (page 250), who has  persistently  wanted  to  get  more  control  over  Folly matters.  Resisting  that,  while  not  antagonising  Lady  Ty  or Folsom, requires diplomacy. You have been warned.

## Empress State Building

The Empress State Building (ESB) in West Brompton, West London, houses Metropolitan Police offices where high-level meetings take place regarding ongoing cases, including those of Falcon relevance. Counter Terrorism Command has space there, as has the Directorate of Professional Standards (DPS; Police  Oversight ,  page  214).  ESB  is  also  an  assessment centre for prospective police officers.

Footnotes:

19

## National Crime Agency

Operation Fretwork 21 was run by Officer Alona Silver of the National  Crime  Agency  (NCA)  through  a  Specialist  Unit based  in  the  Exchange,  Hornchurch,  East  London.  DC Grant worked with her in the past on a street racing-related smuggling ring 22 .

## British Transport Police, London

The  British  Transport  Police's  London  headquarters  are in Camden, and Sgt Jaget Kumar is their guy in charge of keeping track of 'weird shit,' as he calls it. His role in this respect isn't official, obviously, but he's our liaison there. It seems  the  London  Underground  has  more  than  its  fair share of weirdness, particularly ghosts, but, as we now know, the Quiet People (page  253)  as  well.  We  also  work  with patrolman David Lambert, who has the right training to lead people onto the tracks, should the need arise.

## Iain West Forensic Suite

This  forensic  suite  opened  in  2008  and  is  used  almost exclusively for post-mortem examinations in cases of suspicious deaths. If you're on a murder  investigation, you'll likely have to go there. Best not have one of Molly's breakfasts first. It's based at Westminster Public Mortuary (13) on Horseferry Rd, Westminster. Dr Abdul Haqq Walid (page 235) and Dr Jennifer Vaughan (page 236) handle the 'special' cases, including cryptopathology, although they're more usually found at University College Hospital (15).

## A London Orientation

London's more than just a collection of buildings and a bunch of humans living, working, and breaking the law. London's also  what  lies  beneath  the  surface  and  the  history  that made it, as well as the rivers running through it. Plenty of those things are just as alive, active, and lawbreaking as the humans. This part of your orientation deals with the parts of London that've seen the most Falcon activity.

## Bloomsbury

The Folly is in Bloomsbury, which is famous for its literary and  intellectual  types.  Just  along  from  Russell  Square (2),  you'll  find  the  British  Museum  (7)-BM  for  short. When DCI Nightingale was deciding what to do with the 'Drinking  Bell'  ( Covent  Garden ,  page  272),  DC  Grant suggested  offering  it  to  the  BM.  It  wouldn't  be  the  first magical item they'd got their hands on-go and look at the Battersea Shield ( The Thames , page 277) next time you're on a break. However, things sent to the BM don't always stay under lock and key-they're still looking for a mummy that went  missing  in  1933,  for  instance.  It  was  reported  stolen, but DCI Nightingale believes it just wandered off. It might
