have hopped on the last train out of the now disused British Museum Tube Station, which closed that same year.

Opposite  the  BM  is  University  College  London  (UCL). It's  London's  top  university  and  home  to  the  Harry  Price Library of Magical Literature, left to it by the famous ghost hunter  in  1948.  It's  not  the  Bodleian,  and  you'll  need  to wrangle a reader's pass, but it's only two minutes' walk away. The British Library (9) moved out of the British Museum to St Pancras in 1998, roughly a 15-minute walk from here. We have history there, as you already know 23 .

Abigail's brother, Paul, has a serious condition and spends most of his time at Great Ormond Street Hospital, which is  also  in  Bloomsbury.  She  doesn't  talk  about  her  family situation much, but if you see her looking tired or worried, be understanding.

One  important  point  about  the  Folly's  location  is  that Bloomsbury, where the north London borough of Camden meets Central London's West End, is outside the borough of Westminster, where Lady Ty, goddess of the Tyburn, has the most influence. She'd like to extend that influence, but the Folly has so far resisted.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000702_34e919329cfa2697a42cff089932887d92a322932c2855a3c594b7e262780175.png)

## Case Seed: The Lost Mummy

Tourists  reported  seeing  a  mummy walking  around  the  Petrie  Museum  of Egyptian Archaeology in the University College London. Petrie staff say none of their  mummies  are  missing.  Research shows UCL Egyptologist and witch Dr Margaret Murray (1863-1963) warded the  Petrie's  cabinets  to  stop  anything escaping.  Was  this  sighting  a  student prank, or was the British Museum's lost mummy visiting  relatives?  If  so,  what are its plans?

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000703_08683d5db9d1cab6796df8909ef70687989a9b6b9eb8893154f6e47d021cfe89.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000704_669ef311aa734105514b0b3b7c5c994cda09cf95c371d50b361675b834b61db4.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000705_b20ec559e5d109e031711a9d20963d3ba54256a5244a96f19d14c8e3bcf43230.png)

## University College Hospital (15)

University  College  Hospital  (UCH)  is  a  massive  teaching hospital  in  Bloomsbury.  It's  part  of  UCL  and  has  state-ofthe-art  facilities,  with  general  wards  as  well  as  specialist units,  including  that  of  gastroenterologist  Dr  Walid,  who has been the on-call doctor for the Folly for many years. He was recently joined there by Dr Vaughan, who is a specialist pathologist. That's where you'll go if either of them think you need checking for hyperthaumaturgical degradation or any other magical malady.

Footnote: 23 Tales from the Folly

('A Rare Book of Cunning Device')

## Covent Garden (1)

Covent Garden is a Falcon hotspot. Charing Cross polices pickpockets  targeting  distracted  tourists,  but  it  was  the Folly who dealt with Mr Punch, London's spirit of riot and rebellion, when he caused mayhem there a few years ago 24 .

Covent Garden used to be London's main fruit and veg market-now  a  craft  market-but  it's  also  famous  for  its theatres, which weren't always as respectable as they are now. The Royal Opera House, formerly the Theatre Royal, is at the east end of the piazza. We've just about been forgiven for what happened there back in 2012.

Henry  Fielding,  magistrate  and  founding  father  of  the Bow Street  Runners,  lived  next  to  the  theatre  in  the  18 th century. Where his house stood is now a props store, home to the ghost of Colonel Sir Thomas de Veil, Bow Street's first magistrate. You may need to go see him if you want a ghost warrant. Sir Thomas isn't the only Opera House ghost, mind you. Abigail intends cataloguing them.

St Paul's Church, built by Inigo Jones in 1638, is at the west end of the piazza. It's called the Actor's Church because local thespians are buried in the grounds. As well as being instrumental in the recruitment of DC Grant into the Folly 24 , it-or rather,  its  bell-was,  in  2015,  the  focus  of  Faceless Man 2's attempt to rewrite reality 25 . There's also a Punch and Judy Tavern in Covent Garden, where Oxley, one of Father Thames' sons, and his wife, Isis, drink when they're in town.

## GM ADVICE

If  you're  wondering  why  there's  no  mention  of  the genius  loci  who  inhabits  the  basement  of  a  certain Covent Garden business, that's because we've adapted its short story 26  into an introductory case file: The Bookshop (page 285).

## Soho (41)

Albert Woodville-Gentle, the original Faceless Man, operated a gruesome venture called The Garden of Unearthly Delights,  on  Soho's  Brewer  Street-nicknamed  the  Strip Club of Doctor Moreau, as it trafficked in chimeras: humananimal hybrids ( A Rogues' Gallery , page 247). His successor took it over until a Falcon raid in late 2012 shut it down 27 . Faceless  Man  2  also  had  a  secret  base  below  Dean  Street, reached  via  the  back  of  a  shop.  It's  possible  both  Faceless Men had other lairs around Soho, holding any manner of horrible secrets. These need to be traced.

Back  in  the  mid-20 th century,  Soho  was  a  hub  for  vice, organised  crime,  and  police  corruption,  but  that's  mostly been cleaned up. The area's now full of media companies and legit bars and clubs, but still has a few places with ties to their seedy  past.  Some  trade  on  nostalgia:  The  Mysterioso,  on Bateman Street, is a basement jazz club that tries to recreate

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000706_28f63683d922cb40ae25bb639c4a133a07aa4fe6f2c238acae028a8d52e30b4b.png)

Footnotes: 24 Rivers of London, 25 Lies Sleeping, 26 Tales from the Folly ('The Cockpit'), 27 Moon Over Soho

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000707_4898e5fda77d3a268187ba4fc60ba2eef49866460104fc67eb7c447e19ca8a2c.png)

a bygone style. There's usually a long queue to get in, and the management, Stan and Don, employ rough bouncers.

The area attracts a mixed and bohemian crowd. The French House wine bar on Dean Street is aimed at artists, writers, actors, and other entertainers; no phones, music, or TV are allowed. The Groucho Club on Dean Street is private and exclusive-you have to be someone in the publishing, media, entertainment,  and  arts  industries  to  be  a  member,  and there's always paparazzi gathered outside. The Pale Lady, a supernatural colleague of Faceless Man 2, bit off a punter's penis  in  the  gents'  lavatory  back  in  2012,  and  her  teeth weren't in her mouth…

Just  so  you  know,  there  used  to  be  a  very  unusual  type of vampire over in Soho. Standard procedure with vampire nests is to burn them with incendiary grenades and blame a gas explosion. We normally call in Frank Caffrey (page 239), the  Folly's  Fire  Brigade  liaison,  based  at  New  Cross  Fire Station, in south London. In this case, the vampires killed themselves at the Café de Paris once they realised what they were. Seems that was where they got infected, listening to jazz when the place was blitzed by a bomb in the war.

## Leicester Square (39) and Chinatown (40)

Leicester Square is the heart of Westminster's West End, which means cinemas, theatres, restaurants, bars, clubs, and shops. The now-closed Trocadero Centre was off one of its corners.  In  Victorian  times,  the  five-storey  building  was  a  music hall. It was then a restaurant and waxworks before becoming an entertainment complex with arcades and a horror-themed live theatrical experience. DC Grant attempted to arrest the Pale Lady there 28 . The site's now boarded up and long empty, which is deeply suspicious to my mind.

Chinatown borders on Leicester Square. Michael Cheung (page  238),  aka  the  Legendary  Swordsman,  is  the  area's latest  representative  in  a  magical  context.  His  predecessor, Simon Wong, had been around since before the 1970s. Mr Cheung  is  also  DS  Sahra  Guleed's  martial  arts  trainer. The  Folly  has  an  agreement  with  Chinatown,  which  takes responsibility  for  the  peace  within  its  area.  Wong  Kei,  on Wardour Street, is the usual place for meetings between the Folly and Chinatown's representatives. It's a large Chinese restaurant  famous  for  its  good  cheap  food  and  rude  wait staff.  Popular  with  tourists  and  locals,  you  can  get  a  table late at night without a reservation if you don't mind sharing with strangers.

## Charing Cross Road

Charing Cross Road, running north from Leicester Square, was once London's centre for the book trade, and still has bookshops aplenty. Cecil Court, a Dickensian alley just off it, is where burglar George Trenchard tried to flog a stolen magical  book  to  first  editions  specialist  Colin  and  Leech

Footnote:

28 Moon Over Soho in  2013 29 .  Luckily,  they  alerted  us.  Other  shops  in  the area,  like  Watkins  in  Cecil  Court,  Atlantis  by  the  British Museum, and Treadwell's near UCL, specialise in magical books. Their stock's more folkloric stuff, but they'd recognise a Newtonian tome if offered one.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000708_032eada3be3d9df8aef8fcc3ba3708e46d4bdcedf5b0aee94344959862be43a8.png)

## Case Seed: The Key

An  esoteric  bookshop  contacts  the Folly. They bought a box of old books, including  magical  tomes,  via  a  house clearance  after  an  old  man  died.  One book  had  been  hollowed  out  inside and contained a small key. Its vestigia ( Vestigia , page 161) is burnt tallow, dried blood, and a musical box melody. What mystery does the key unlock?

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000709_84445e93952f859a47abdce6f04509c32762928fa9cc934e5871ac4095a94a9b.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000710_904b050cc78c09644f99a64028af8a24ff93725d636880f1e8f337ef29b17434.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000711_508a701b33618869a28afdfd755c9a9687ff070446ab7f96cdfd3648b6f55f04.png)

## Skygarden (18) and Elephant and Castle (42)

One  of  south  London's  most  iconic  buildings,  Skygarden Tower-part  of  the  listed  Skygarden  estate  in  Elephant and  Castle-came  down  in  2013 30 .  Designed  by  German architect  Erik  Stromberg,  you  could  see  its  concrete  spike for  miles.  A  bowl  in  the  centre  of  the  housing  estate  once contained a garden; in it was a huge London plane tree with its own nymph, Sky.

Stromberg intended  the  estate  to  be  an  ideal  community, but, in the 1980s, riots made it a violent no-go area, especially for the Met ( Walworth and Southwark Police Stations , page 269).  Many  believe  County  Gard's  illegal  demolition  was  a land grab, but it was really a bid for magical power. The tower's core  had  been  devised  as  an  industrial  magic-lighthouse powered by the residents' vestigia -a Stadtkrone , or city crown. The Stadtkrone lit up once, just before the tower collapsed.

The Skygarden site is still a crater surrounded by hoardings, due to legal wrangling over who owns the land and arguments between locals and Southwark Council over what's wanted there.  Council security tries to prevent unauthorised entry, but Nicky, goddess of the Neckinger (24), has inspired local druids to set up an eco-protest to stop redevelopment and save the garden as a public park. They have a camp by Elephant and Castle roundabout. DCI Nightingale thinks Sky's lost tree might reseed, with saplings growing on the site, perhaps carrying the nymph's spirit-although such biological effects can take years or even generations to manifest. Initial Folly assessments,  with  Toby's  help,  indicate  lots  of  residual vestigia , making it a hot spot for magic.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000712_ffdb239e0edf5f24a80941bab406c1d917ba76217b7d5ed3aa3172e3b891b87c.png)

Footnotes: 29, 30 Broken Homes

7

Stromberg lived at West Hill House, a fake Le Corbusierstyle mansion in Highgate, north London. He was a member of  the  German  version  of  the  Folly,  but  came  to  London before  the  war.  He  was  fascinated  with  industrial  uses  of magic,  and  kept  an  eye  on  his  experiment  at  Skygarden through  a  telescope  on  his  roof  terrace.  It's  possible  this house still holds a few secrets relating to his theories.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000713_4aa67ffd364b324ae5738cb9053bba630a2981a8b9c8610ecc299c367202ed7d.png)

## Case Seed: Skygarden Souvenirs

A  lead  from  the  demi-monde  says  a small  floating  plastic  object  is  to  be auctioned  at  an  upcoming  nazareth ( Demi-monde Pubs and Nazareths , page 283). There are also reports of kids trespassing on the Skygarden site and claiming to have found things floating in the air. Are these connected, and is there any danger?

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000714_8d7297451e28e92c0b904c878b3e4b6c0b87666463221e72562314455f3f4dcb.png)

## Notting Hill

As  well  as  hosting  the  country's  biggest  Afro-Caribbean carnival  each  year,  Notting  Hill,  in  West  London's  Royal Borough  of  Kensington  and  Chelsea,  is  full  of  expensive houses.  Gentrification  began  in  the  swinging  1960s  when pop stars and movie directors moved there; a far cry from Victorian  times,  when  Notting  Hill  was  a  grim  industrial area bordering on grim farms, known as the Potteries and Piggeries. The Beale family of railway tunnel builders lodged off Pottery Lane (17); they were typical inhabitants until they got the secret recipe for Unbreakable Pottery-like magical Coade stone-from the Quiet People.

Portobello Road, in Notting Hill, has had a famous antique market since the 1940s where you can buy all sorts of goods, from  rip-off  tat  to,  occasionally,  magic  stuff.  Art  student James Gallagher bought an Unbreakable Pottery bowl from a  stall  there.  That  started  his  fascination  with  its  magical properties, which ultimately led to his murder back in 2012 31 . James' housemate was the demi-monde go-between Zachary Palmer (page 256).

## Underground London

## Case Seed: Blue Beads

Abigail  says  a  girl  at  Great  Ormond Street told her she had been hospitalised since her blue bead necklace went missing in Portobello Road Market. One minute she was wearing it, the next it was gone. She claims the beads were a magical charm that helped her breathe, despite a serious chest condition.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000715_c70a0042096ef66492f01b19cb57a1f71c5ee752573297a5c10593f863055f32.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000716_eb40fd59b8bf69b3a4f8215d014d6276fdd5bffac5bb812d3e1d11edea4c4b84.png)

Research shows a common early 20 th century  folkloric  belief  that  ordinary blue glass beads protected against bronchitis.  Very  few  were  genuinely magical. A trip  to  Portobello  Road reveals  ordinary  necklaces  on  stalls, but one trader spotted Zachary Palmer with a set of blue beads on the day of the loss. Zach can be traced to a demimonde  pub,  and  claims  he  found  the magical  beads  on  the  ground.  He  has given them to a Quiet Person child with a serious breathing condition. What do the investigators do?

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000717_cb7febe80a18a8c937bbabc7a7aa5f12ddea269996c8ccc473e6d119f1767dfc.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000718_3e1affa56e19a327b375163062ff5772de5ecd4553f5e6826e6adfabb24edfcc.png)

tunnels and stations, wartime bomb shelters and, of course, underground rivers. There's archaeology under our feet, too. Some  of  underground  London  is  deliberately  hidden,  of course, like the Quiet People's home. Folly personnel have only  seen  a  couple  of  rooms  inside  that.  One  was  a  lowceilinged living room with ceramic walls and empty picture frames-any inquisitive copper would like to know what was in them, and why they're empty. Then, there's a larger room with  a  big  Victorian  table  that  their  leader,  Matthew  TenTons,  and  his  daughter  Elizabeth,  use  for  meeting  others, which occasionally means us.

Above  ground,  not  everything  is  as  it  seems.  The  first Underground  trains  were  steam  powered,  so  tunnel  roof sections were left open for ventilation; some hidden behind fake house facades, including one at 23-24 Leinster Gardens, Bayswater.  Another,  in  nearby  Moscow  Road,  is  officially owned by Beale Property Services, and is used as a goods drop point for the Quiet People.

You'd be surprised  how  much  of  London  is  below  ground. Some is public, including most of the London Underground railway  network;  some  is  private,  like  cellars;  some  you need permits to access, including the sewer system, disused

Inside  Moscow  Road  is  a  trapdoor  down  to  the  railway tracks. Between that and Bayswater Station, there's a secret door leading to the hidden ways network, which the Quiet People use to get around London. This includes secret tunnels

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000719_4380cb4fb5874d8d5a004e969d054c1aec5b6e0c8b5316f68ef6b659d1f68942.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000720_bd2efd00bc23096771df33bbec5f898d0c74fe51b2c3bb02c90342a508bc99fc.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000721_0e88595236bd1c3437f2df1e3fcb455e29aff3115f80e22b2283667fde2be9bf.png)

as well as the Underground, the sewer system, old Post Office (GPO) tunnels, and WWII deep-level air raid shelters. The one at Holland Park has been converted into a demi-monde nightclub-Olympia  and  Chelsea,  goddesses  of  Counter's Creek (26) and the River Westbourne (25), are regulars. Exits to ground level are via spiral stairs and through Holland Park tube station, as well as down to the Notting Hill warren.

As James Gallagher once said, the city goes 'all the way down.' The Folly doesn't yet know how far. We do know the Quiet People raise pigs underground as well as making that Unbreakable Pottery through some sort of magical process. The  Quiet  People  also  frequent  a  secret  demi-monde  pub below Oxford Street, accessible via a Crossrail service tunnel. We know the Folly is only aware of a fraction of the Quiet People's hidden ways-there must be much more we've yet to learn about. Some members of the demi-monde use these passages,  too-including  Zach.  Keeping  track  of  Zach  is an  action  you  might  get  allocated.  He's  a  useful  source  of information, as well as a liability.

Any  exploration  of  the  Underground  has  to  be  done with  British  Transport  Police  permission.  As  I've  already mentioned,  Sgt  Kumar  is  the  Folly  liaison  officer  for  that, while Allison Conte is our Thames Water contact when it comes to sewer access.

## The City of London

Historically, London's two power centres were Westminster (33), where the Tyburn (21) reaches the Thames, and the City of London, aka 'the Square Mile,' where the Fleet (22) and Walbrook (23) run. The area within the old City walls is still somewhat separate from the rest of the capital and has its own police force.

Officers  Grant  and  May  traced  the  elderly-and  now dead-Albert Woodville-Gentle to a flat on the 30 th floor of Shakespeare Tower in the Barbican complex, which is a concrete arts venue and home to the Museum of London (10) as well as apartment blocks.

The Bacchus temple had earlier been dedicated to the solardeity  Mithras. The Mithraeum (12) stood on the banks of the Walbrook ( The Walbrook , page 281), which was Temple Central in Roman days. Isis-not Oxley's missus, the other one-had a sanctuary there, too. The Mithraeum's not some trap-infested maze, though. It's just an ancient cellar with an altar; now open to the public under Bloomberg's offices on Queen Victoria Street.

## Smithfield, Violent Deaths, and Pacificatus

Smithfield  Market  was  London's  main  meat  market  for  a millennium, but closed in the 1960s. It was also London's main  public  execution  site  long  before  the  Tyburn  tree, especially  for  heretics  and  rebels.  Scottish  freedom  fighter William Wallace was executed there in 1305. Lord Mayor of London Sir William Walworth killed Wat Tyler, leader of the Peasants' Revolt, there in 1381. The River Fleet (page 280) runs past it, now underground, but historically that was a place to illegally dispose of corpses, with 13 bodies being pulled out in one year in the early 19 th century.

Violent and sudden death, especially battles, massacres, or executions, can produce dangerous entities, such as revenants ( A  Rogues'  Gallery ,  page  249).  The  Sons  of  Weyland developed  a  way  of  dispersing  negative  energy  from  mass slaughter, called Pacificatus . It was used at Smithfield in the 19 th century when railway sidings were built, capped with a bronze statue of Peace by John Birnie Philip. The sidings are now  an  underground  car  park.  There  are  other Pacificatus installations, but we don't know where they all are. Locating them is on our action list.

The Rising Sun, in Smithfield, is a pub on the fringes of the demi-monde. It has a grisly history, as 19 th century body snatchers John Bishop and Thomas Williams supplemented the corpses they'd dug from graveyards by following drunks out  of  the  pub,  murdering  them  in  dark  alleys,  and  then selling them to anatomists.

Woodville-Gentle and his successor as the Faceless Man weren't the only unregulated practitioners in the City. Patrick Gale, a senior partner at law group Bock, Loupe and Stag, came to the Folly's attention after his junior colleague, Tony Harden, died of brain necrosis 32 . Gale admitted to officiating at  solstice  rituals  at  the  Bloomberg  building, 33 the  site  of a  former  Roman  temple  to  Bacchus,  Roman  god  of  wine. Turns out, he'd also taught magic to some colleagues. After a word from DCI Nightingale, Gale claims to have stopped breaching the peace, and is now working with the Folly on an agreement basis. DCI Nightingale is training Gale and his  former  apprentices-under  oath  and  a  non-disclosure agreement-at  Talacre  Community  Sports  Centre.  Gale's still on our radar as a potential problem, though.

Graveyards are normally peaceful, as their inhabitants died elsewhere, but plague pits can be an exception. New Street, once called Hand Alley, is on the site of a 1666 plague pit where evidence suggests victims were thrown in while still breathing. There are urban legends of things trying to climb out  again.  New  Street  is  opposite  Liverpool  Street  station, just around the corner from the City of London Police station on Bishopsgate. When things are on City of London Police ground, they usually ask us in, but without vestigia training, officers  don't  always  spot  a  Falcon  case  until  it  bites  them. Quite literally, in some instances. SC Geneviève Nguyên is the Folly's City of London Police Liaison Officer; she also deals with fraud cases.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000722_b7f27d88fceed48f4cfffe4e6dbc0a2a2d0f864d169024bb2b015b17caaa34e4.png)

7

## Newgate Prison

Newgate Prison was another City site of historic death and suffering.  The  Central  Criminal  Court,  at  the  Old  Bailey, now  stands  there,  but  a  bit  of  the  old  prison  wall  is  in Warwick Lane, between the court and St Paul's Cathedral (37).  Newgate's  deepest  cells  stretched  under  the  road  and into  the  cellars  of  what  is  now  the  Viaduct  Tavern.  The landlords make a bit of money running public ghost vigils. There's also a legend that, at the time of Henry III, a scholar was thrown into Newgate for sorcery, but was cannibalised by starving prisoners. He cursed them with his dying breath, summoning a black dog that hunted and devoured his killers. Another urban legend claims it's still sometimes seen.

## Old Street: Silicon Roundabout

Old Street Roundabout, just north of the City of London, got  nicknamed  'Silicon  Roundabout'  because  of  the  tech firms that've sprung up there in sparkly new high rises. The roundabout's subway leads to Old Street Tube Station and a  dingy  underground  arcade  that's  a  remnant  of  the  area's run-down past. It has subsequently made an effort to turn its cramped shops, noodle bar, and make-shift nightclub into something DC Grant calls 'cyberpunk.'

DC  Grant  worked  undercover  for  a  spell  at  Serious Cybernetics  Corporation,  the  brainchild  of  former  Silicon Valley  computer  genius  Terrence  Skinner 34 , located  in  the building  on  the  corner  of  Tabernacle  Street  and  Epworth Street.  Skinner  had  managed  to  lay  his  hands  on  a  Mary Engine: a dangerous magical construct capable of who knows quite what; it is now stored at the Folly where we can keep an eye on it. Skinner was never charged, but DC Grant thinks he's  acquired  a  taste  for  the  demi-monde.  There's  more  to investigate from that case, you mark my words.

## Hampstead

There's a derelict house off East Heath Road in Hampstead (45) that we regularly check on, just to make sure it's still a crumbling ruin, and the power of a dangerous genius loci is still safely draining 35 . Hampstead doesn't have many derelict houses. It's one of the wealthiest parts of London and borders on a vast hilltop heathland between two branches of the River Fleet  ( The  Fleet ,  page  280).  The  Folly  has  some  animalrelated  contacts  up  on  the  heath.  A  group  of  intelligent talking foxes, with a den under Gospel Oak Station, watch for  anything odd in the area. They have their own agenda though, which we'd like to discover. There's also a character

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000723_4986468b174933427b0ea1ea2bc9f67cd5b6aa900a8e62157f229d7377a431b7.png)

Footnotes: 34 False Value, 35 What Abigail Did That Summer

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000724_a896c2e7f2a5d23b03c7a4a912432e115fb086111aa8beec4ecbde96334227e5.png)

called the Cat Lady, who can usually be found sitting on a park bench with a large shopping trolley, feeding the strays. She's observant, and seems to have magical awareness, but we need to learn more about her, too.

## The Rivers of London

Most people realise  it  was  the  Thames  that  made  London the capital. For centuries, the location of the Thames enabled trade and travel more easily than by land. But the Thames and her tributaries have an even greater influence over London than most suspect. I'm now going to describe the rivers of London and their areas of magical importance, as you'll no doubt be running up against them at some point. First and foremost: the Thames herself.

## The Thames (27)

London would not exist without the Thames. He's the reason the Romans founded Londinium in 50 CE, although people lived  on  the  banks  and  worshipped  the  river  long  before that.  He  provided  water,  food,  building  materials,  and boat travel. If you're wondering why I'm saying 'he' rather than 'she,'  that's  because  the  river  has  two  powerful genii locorum . Father  Thames (page  247)  was  the  original  one, but he moved upriver in the 19 th century and now only rules above Teddington Lock, where the tidal stretch ends. Mama Thames (page 251) came much, much later and is the acting goddess  of  the  river  in  London.  The  pair  are  currently  on good terms, but that hasn't always been the case. Keeping the peace between them is a Falcon matter.

Other  spots  on  the  river of Falcon  interest include Richmond Bridge, home to the troll Nathaniel. He moved there from Hungerford Bridge, and we like to keep an eye on him. As well as being a collection of plants from all over the world, the Royal Botanic Gardens at Kew (43), are a top botanical research institution. Should you come across weird flora, that's where to go. But note, the scientists there treat Folly  staff  with  some  suspicion  after  an  'incident'  no  one likes to talk about…

The Bull's Head pub, Chiswick, was used in the 18 th and 19 th centuries by traditional barge captains to discuss crews, cargo, and river lore. There are a few old-time barge folk still there.  Just  down  the  river  at  Barnes,  a  New  Orleans'-style pub  also  called  The  Bull's  Head  attracts  the  demi-monde, and hosts jazz evenings. Folk tales relate that both pubs were named after a local legendary beast, possibly of fae ancestry.

## Case Seed: Forging Connections

Reports  of  interference  overnight  at an  archaeological  dig  at  Furnivall  Gardens, Hammersmith, could be a Falcon matter  as  a  witness  reported  seeing a huge, incredibly  muscled  woman walking away from the site late at night with a hammer slung over her shoulder. Investigators  can  find  the  witness  at the  Dove  pub-which  has  the  world's smallest  bar-in  a  tiny  riverside  alley that is the only remainder of the earlier village.  Who  was  the  woman?  Did  she interfere at the dig or protect it?

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000725_3385a5cf0a2e0c4410d365357c745d379f1e6b4b9c83a7fa5fd89a188e7ae412.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000726_67ef49875e30bb6ad2c8c4a6c7f0751679516d6aba9cd730383f5015c4983143.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000727_4989da209f35535828a8bddb0c2dfc90c8fa00b31a47b8f100789d31d43a6655.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000728_e752281dd6f25bd68ee10d1179578f985b44b42cd24083ab12189d64081f92c0.png)

Wandsworth,  South  London,  gets  its  name  from The Wandle (page 282), which reaches the Thames at Battersea. The  Battersea  Shield,  a  stonking  ancient  Celtic  item  with intricate  designs  that  even  the  British  Museum  describes as magical and protective, gets its name from where it was dredged up in 1857. It was thrown into the river as an offering long before the Romans arrived. The original is in the BM ( Bloomsbury ,  page  269),  with  a  copy  in  the  Museum  of London. A few ancient offerings, along with common clay pipes  and  oyster  shells,  still  get  found  by  mudlarks  with permits to search the shoreline at low tide. Others get picked up illegally and not reported. That's an infringement of the law, and sometimes it's a Falcon matter.

The Chelsea Physic Garden was founded by the Worshipful Society of Apothecaries in 1673, where healing herbs such as willow, meadowsweet, and eyebright grew by the Thames. Nowadays, Chelsea is full of expensive houses and boutique shops, watched over by the goddess of Chelsea Creek and her twin sister, Olympia, of Counter's Creek. The garden's still there, though, full of herb lore.

Hammersmith's  now  a  big  commercial  area  in  West London,  but  DCI  Nightingale  has  actioned  checking  for connections  with  the  Sons  of  Weyland.  It  had  the  name Hamersmythe as far back as 1294, and had the most famous forge and smithy on road and river routes out of London, but we now don't know where it stood.

In central London, the river passes Pimlico and Westminster  along  the  Victoria  Embankment  to  the  City on  the  north  bank,  and  Southwark  on  the  south.  London Bridge  (29)  links  the  City  with  Southwark.  The  current bridge is a descendant of the first timber river crossing built by the Romans. One of the first acts on that bridge was an imprisonment with a long sentence, involving our old friend, Mr Punch, and the priest who would become Father Thames. Punch was released in 2015 under an agreement with DC Grant, but he's still probably London's most dangerous spirit.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000729_38bd51595755e099a6896bc695ad2cb8e68e830a83f58dcef44d12a0d3d4fc38.png)

7

In Elizabethan London, Southwark was an outlaw borough outside the City walls, and outside its jurisdiction. Theatres, bear  baiting,  and  brothels  flourished.  In  the  19 th century, it  was  an  area  for  industries  unwanted  north  of  the  river, including power stations. Southwark power station is now the Tate Modern art gallery (16). Sometimes modern art is also magical art, as DC Grant found during his investigation of James Gallagher's murder 36 . The South Bank is now a tourist destination  with  arts  venues,  including  the  Royal  Festival Hall  and  National  Theatre.  Bernie  Spain  Gardens,  a  small park just along the South Bank, has been used by Mama and Father Thames for their Spring Festival, when the Court of the Thames is in session. The court is veiled in a magical mist, so only those invited can find it, but it needs Falcon policing.

Downriver from Tower Bridge (28), Mama Thames lives in luxury converted warehouse apartments (14) between the Tower of London and Shadwell Basin in East London, on the other side of the slipway from the Prospect of Whitby pub. If you visit her, take an offering; the bigger, the better. By the way, Dead Man's Stairs at Wapping is a place where corpses  are  still  often  washed  up.  Wapping  is  also  where the  Metropolitan  Police  Marine  Policing  Unit,  or  Thames River Police, are based. They have legal jurisdiction over the Thames, but consult with the Folly on Falcon matters.

On  the  north  bank,  the  Thames  winds  on  past  the  new financial district of Canary Wharf and the Isle of Dogs. On the south, at Rotherhithe, a point in the river is known as Cuckold's Point.  One  historic  and  annual  custom  involved  horns  being fixed to a pole, with men dressed as women and women as men, all  wearing horns, who would process through Greenwich to Charlton for a festival of a sexual nature. The Horn Fair stopped in the 19 th century, but has recently been renewed-and requires

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000730_695bbafd2447d8d0e15b7bf943d36e5a0bffcef3d214d4d2a31d87ec038aaa23.png)

## Case Seed: Magic Bus

In 1952, a double-decker bus jumped the gap of Tower Bridge (28) as it was opening. Driver Albert Gunter, who had been caught out by a faulty signal, was hailed a hero for saving all his passengers' lives. Now, that vintage bus has been reported stolen. It is deemed a Falcon operation as  the  current  owner,  an  avid  London Transport enthusiast, insists the missing pride of his collection is magical. But is it, really? Or is this a publicity stunt for the owner's new city-tour business?

## Case Seed: The Drowned Sailor

The body of a drowned sailor is found on the Thames foreshore. His eyeballs have been removed and in their place are  pearls.  He  is  wearing  a  backpack containing  a  copy  of  TS  Eliot's  apocalyptic  poem, The  Waste  Land.  The Spring Tide, or King Tide, is in 48 hours. Storms are forecast. Is this part of a ritual to flood London? Who is behind it?

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000731_c9829596c85aa3626b57b20b28d770a95b4dbdecd6b8d6b66ee153fe111d1f2a.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000732_bde33f5e2eeaae8832e9b90aa5714053a7f41b500993f3807a346f6939f09324.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000733_ac16812315b3af26978fa7c50bb5bbf94d52c1b88ecb0ea769a262bc76a9255f.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000734_ac1bfc7f98b850b0919b6178894524b80d7853b654933151448ee79880e0f9a2.png)

policing. The festival site is close to the Thames Barrier, which protects central London from flooding by storm surges.

## The Tyburn (21)

The  Tyburn  begins  high  in  Hampstead  and  reaches  the Thames at Pimlico, running almost entirely underground. Its name comes from the Anglo-Saxon word for boundary; it marks the edges of London's West End, but most think of the Tyburn Tree gallows (11). Lady Ty, goddess of the river, prefers to be associated with power and money. She has a big house on wealthy Fitzjohn's Avenue. Her water's source, the Shepherd's Well, feeds a marble fountain in her garden. All Folly-assigned officers are advised to stay well away from it, if they know what's good for them.

Lady Ty's daughter, Olivia, describes the river's route as a 'posh line.' St John's Wood, where Olivia lives, was once actual woods, owned by the Knights of St John. A hundred years ago, it was nicknamed 'wicked St John's Wood,' and author Dennis Wheatley set his novel, The Devil Rides Out there.  The  book's  fiction,  but  Folly  records  show  we  have dealt with unethical resident practitioners there in the past who may have left unpleasant legacies.

The Tyburn runs beside Primrose Hill-where revivalist druids  have  celebrated  the  solstices  since  1792-then  over the Grand Union Canal ( Grand Union Canal , page 282) in an iron pipe, and into Regent's Park boating lake (30). DC Grant's father, Richard Grant, aka the jazz musician Lord Grant, plays at a festival at The Hub café there each summer.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000735_c743d254f8a532b483520c680b32099f6ac6b6619f56ac0df12ceb78ff8e1dfe.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000736_bb0261190a1bec3de2c2c9215f7d3015ba34a4500154e1bf8b2ef6c09e20f85f.png)

The river has crossed the Circle Line in a Victorian pipe at Baker Street Station since the first Underground station opened in 1863. It then crosses Oxford Street-once Tyburn Road-into  Mayfair,  where  Lady  Ty's  main  address  is  a Regency terraced house on Chesterfield Hill. The 'posh line' continues to Knightsbridge. Note that Harrods department store has requested Folly officials contact the manager before

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000737_da7bd42485f0281c5280e2f8b7a3ec28dab9a468cff6df6b58420814d5682140.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000738_2d6b81a8751d01496172e80b4ca95b190664ee50eafc70f14a77e9e1f81ef53c.png)

going  back  there,  after  an  incident  involving  DC  Grant 37 . Also  posh  is  St  James's,  known  for  prestigious  art  dealers, galleries,  and  auction  houses,  including  Christie's  on  King Street. The Met rents space there for stuff on London's Stolen Art Directory.

The London Library, at 14 St James's Square, also needs a polite approach after DC Grant water-bombed rare books while attempting to apprehend a suspect 38 .  The  river  flows under  Buckingham  Palace  (34)-I'm  relieved  to  say  DC Grant hasn't  yet  managed  to  annoy  the  Queen-and  then divides.  The  northern  branch  flows  around  the  outline  of ancient  Thorney  Island-once  an  eyot  (river  islet)  in  the Thames, and now the heart of Westminster and the centre of government. Below ground, the Tyburn's waters surround the Houses of Parliament (33) at the Palace of Westminster.

The southern branch, once called the Tachbrook, outflows at Pimlico by Vauxhall Bridge. If you go down the manhole on Tachbrook Road into the sewer, you'll find a plaque with a Latin inscription that translates as ' Our mother deity of bounty and  beauty, '  along  with  a  patch  of  wall  that's  moderately clean of excrement. That was put there by DC Grant, along with several other offerings, when requesting a favour from Lady Ty. The rivers always expect something big in return for a favour.

## Hyde Park, Tyburn Gallows, and the Chestnut Tree

About 250 years ago, the Tyburn Gallows (11) stood close to what's now Speaker's Corner, on the north-east edge of Hyde Park (31). The condemned were allowed to proclaim their final words there. It's now somewhere people can give public  speeches,  enshrined  in  law  since  1872.  The  gallows were in the old village of Tyburn, tied to the river by name and association, very close to where Marble Arch (38) now stands. In a nearby alley is The Chestnut Tree (19); a demimonde  pub  with  no  exterior  sign.  The  door  frame  is  said to  be  made  from  the  wood  of  a  tree  that  grew  out  of  the wall behind the bar; its dead branches still spread across the saloon's ceiling. The pub's old name was The Tyburn Tree and was where the condemned stopped for their last drink-and last bequests-before meeting the hangman.

As  you  might  expect,  the  pub  became  a  repository  of things  left  and  unclaimed.  The  pub  was,  up  until  quite recently, owned by one of the second Faceless Man's many front  corporations.  He  used  a  back  room  to  store  magical items and books. That's been emptied; the books are in the Folly library, and you don't need to worry about where the rest are. There are other storage rooms, and possible hidden caches, but the Folly can't search them without permission or  a  warrant.  Neither  of  which  we're  likely  to  get  as  Lady

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000739_a9747ebf318af2b7308403c6a405f8c3eafae2ff73a432108014da6bc6062d63.png)

Footnotes:

37

The Hanging Tree,

38

False Value

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000740_a29cdd2cd923b5313b46d2487192fc0980b52873b1d70be494f0cfe9443df2b1.png)

7

Ty acquired the pub's freehold-although you could always try negotiating with her. The Chestnut's manageress is the part-fae Wanda Pourier; get on her good side, as she can be a useful source of information. The pub offers evening live music  and  real  ale  to  the  demi-monde,  but  the  lunchtime crowd includes business people. There are rumours of a secret exit from the pub to Hyde Park's underground car park.

Hyde  Park  is  the  largest  of  four  Royal  Parks  linking Kensington  Palace  Gardens  to  Buckingham  Palace.  The Serpentine lake there was once fed by the Westbourne river and Tyburn Brook, but since the 2012 Olympics, the water now comes from boreholes. At the south-eastern edge, six busy  roads  converge  at  Hyde  Park  Corner,  overlooked  by London's most expensive block of flats: One Hyde Park. It was the focus of a major Falcon operation into a suspicious death at an illegal rave, as well as a search for Newton's stolen manuscript 39 . Both cases are now resolved.

## The Fleet (22)

The Fleet shares a Hampstead watershed with the Tyburn, but runs through areas both wealthy and poor via Victorian sewer tunnels to the Thames at Blackfriars. The river rises in  a  part  of  Hampstead  Heath  ( Hampstead ,  page  276) that used to be a malarial swamp called Hatchett's Bottom, although some bright spark in the Regency renamed it the Vale of Health (44). It's a strangely isolated suburban village that  keeps  ties  with  old  traditions.  Showmen  park  their fairground rides for the winter at its edge; Henry 'Wicked' Collins is our contact there.

The showmen live on the fringes of the demi-monde and keep ancient wisdom alive, including propitiating the goddess of the Fleet every midsummer to stop the area reverting into a fever-carrying bog. They hold a fair, with music, and offer local ale where the Fleet trickles above ground into Vale of Health Pond. Local folklore has it these midsummer rites once had their counterpart at midwinter, but they involved the Hatchett Man and a blood sacrifice rather than beer. Folly records don't say who or what the Hatchett Man was, or if he exists  outside  stories,  but  children  like  to  scare  themselves over  what  will  happen  if  this  bogey-man  comes  for  them. We've had reports of metal detectorists searching for his axe.

The goddess of the Fleet holds her summer court by the Ladies'  Bathing  Pond  on  Hampstead  Heath  (45).  A  river tributary  is  Highgate  Brook,  which  runs  not  far  from Spaniards  Inn,  a  demi-monde  pub  on  the  Highgate  side. Its  course  goes  through  Tufnell  Park  and  can  be  seen  in a  pipe  next  to  a  footbridge  near  Tufnell  Park  station.  DC Grant,  Abigail,  and  several  of  Fleet's  children  attended

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000741_231ae61582084f6fbf9b322c7f26fc5c0f42af85af9ea3bfdfce42ba0715a650.png)

Footnote:

39 The Hanging Tree

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000742_4bb461a787664d5288bda23deacd78043ad97f0eea490674a12436cbe47bd2f8.png)

Acland Burghley Secondary School, Dartmouth Park Hill, in Tufnell Park, at various times. The comprehensive school, flanked by council estates-including the Peckwater Estate (47)-has  now  received  special  status  as  an  arts  college. Railway cuttings behind the school lead to a graffiti-daubed tunnel haunted by spray can-wielding ghost, Macky 40 .

The  Fleet's  sources  join  at  Kentish  Town.  This  end  of the borough of Camden has council-what you Americans would  call  'social'-housing,  interspersed  with  Victorian terraces. However, Camden Town has been a counterculture magnet since the mid-20 th century,  with  punk,  goth,  rock, and jazz venues including the Electric Ballroom, the Roundhouse, and the Arches jazz club. There's a market and arty shops in former warehouses at Chalk Farm Lock, part of Camden's sprawl, as well as pubs offering entertainment. One of the oldest pubs in Camden is The World's End, with the  Underworld  music  venue  in  its  basement.  It  was  once called Mother Redcap, after a 17 th century witch who lived in a cottage on the site. In the 19 th century, the pub was the last stop for beer and company before leaving London by road or the Regent's Canal.

The  Fleet  briefly  shares  the  same  route  as  the  Regent's Canal,  but  below  it,  before  heading  down  through  St Pancras-home to the British Library (9) and Kings Crosstowards the City. For centuries, industry and poor housing built  up  along  the  Fleet's  banks,  and  the  river  became  a notorious  open  sewer  by  the  18 th century.  Sections  were covered until hardly any of it remained visible.

At  one  point,  the  river  runs  alongside  Mount  Pleasant sorting office, on the site of the infamous Coldbath Fields  Prison,  which  incarcerated  vagrants  and  political conspirators-the poet Coleridge compared it to Hell. The name 'Mount Pleasant' was intended to dispel that image. The Post Office's old railway, which let mail cross the city underground,  is  below  it.  There  were  stories-probably urban  legends-of  letters  arriving  via  the  mail  rail  that bore the names of those doing the sorting. They contained incitements  to  commit  acts  of  sabotage  and  sedition,  long before American slang coined the phrase 'going postal.' The railway closed in 2003, but found new life as a heritage ride and corporate entertaining spot 41 .

Where  the  Fleet  joins  the  Thames  was  once  London's busiest docks, but is  now  where  commuters  alight  at Blackfriars station, on a bridge. You can see the Fleet flowing into the Thames under Blackfriars Bridge. In 1982, the body of Italian banker Roberto Calvi was found hanging below it, right by the Fleet drain. That crime remains unsolved.

## The Walbrook (23)

The Walbrook runs through the eastern side of the City of London, below ground since the 15 th century. The Romans built  loads  of  temples  on  its  banks,  as  I  mentioned  earlier ( The City of London , page 275). It gets its name from the fact it breached the City walls, probably near Moorgate. It goes under Broadgate Circle and Finsbury Circus GardenLondon's oldest public park, dating to 1606-and joins the Thames at Walbrook Wharf.

Its  source is  just  outside  the  City  walls,  near  Shoreditch High Street. Lulu, goddess of the river, runs a flat-roof pub called The Goat and Crocodile opposite the station, with a demi-monde  clientele 42 .  The  building  is  post-Blitz,  but  its sign is old, and possibly magical. DC Grant reports that the vestigia there is burnt earth, incense, and an outdoor market. The front of the pub has recently been repaired after another of DC Grant's little run-ins.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000743_065046502d70f45da679509ab8593949d7eee489f130e506d73baa167c669e5d.png)

## Case Seed: River Head

The  river's  course  runs  through  the  western  side  of  the City  of  London,  past  Smithfield  Market,  where  butchers' waste mixed with its noxious waters in the past ( Smithfield, Violent Deaths, and Pacificatus , page 275). In the City, the river gave its name to Fleet Street, for a long while the home of newspapers, publishing houses, and the print trade. That's the association the river goddess likes to cultivate. She still has  strong  connections  with  the  press  and  other  forms  of media, including broadcasting and the internet.

Footnotes:

40

41

The decapitated body of a metal detectorist  is  found  beside  Vale  of  Health Pond on the morning of the midwinter solstice. Is this the work of the Hatchett  Man,  a  copy-cat,  or  a  coincidental brutal  murder?  Where  is  the  victim's head? After reading the news, Camilla Turner  of  MOLA  contacts  the  Folly to  say  the Paternoster  Society (page 263) had at one time been asking about ancient  Celtic  practices  of  removing enemies'  heads  to  offer  to  their  gods in  exchange  for  magical  power.  Is  the Paternoster Society involved?

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000744_61354f25f983a089800761b575558ce843430fcd5ca1389bdebeace41f0da93f.png)

## The Effra (20)

The Effra runs from Norwood in south London to Vauxhall via Brixton, which is a huge multi-ethnic community including many people of Afro-Caribbean ancestry. It has a warren of independent shops under the railway arches, including one selling herbs to practitioners of magical traditions from the

Whispers Under Ground,

Tales from the Folly

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000745_66b7dfebdffe43488e4099e8a5042f3a2484cf347cee72630553ab6250dc975c.png)

Footnote:

42

Lies Sleeping

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000746_4562ec1b4c684a54c6c47d65fb63581c89a8e0870bf61085aefda5f5a1db1247.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000747_157848d40c37f0090e5567837261d4cb28c9d040ac2eaee9f3b3dc2531633520.png)

7

African diaspora. There's a large street market and even larger nightlife, being full of clubs and music venues. The goddess of the river is a huge fan of dance music. Brixton, and much of  the  route  of  the  Effra,  was  at  one  time  full  of  industry rather than nightclubs. Faceless Man 2 used a former factory off Coldharbour Lane, in Brixton, for part of his operations.

## Beverley Brook

Beverley Brook rises in Worcester Park, South West London, and joins the Thames at Barn Elms. A lot of the river is above ground in parks, recreation grounds, and golf courses. The goddess of the river lives on Beverley Avenue, Wimbledon. Ms Brook is now DC Grant's partner and has sometimes assisted with investigations, but I shouldn't need to warn you about conflicts of interest in any dealings with the rivers and other genii locorum .

## The Wandle

Still  with  many  areas  above  ground,  the  Wandle  rises  in Croydon and joins the Thames at Battersea in Wandsworth. The riverbanks have long been associated with the cloth and clothing  industry,  and  the  current  goddess,  Wanda,  was discovered  in  a  basement  full  of  fabric  in  the  1960s,  after the  river  beside  the  house  burst  its  banks 43 .  The  man  who lived  there  was  in  the  fashion  trade,  although  he  was  also known  to  the  police  as  having  black-market  connections. He adopted baby Wanda and raised her as his daughter. The pair now run a boutique, just called 'The Shop,' which offers cutting-edge fashion. Its customers include stylish members of the demi-monde. The Shop shifts premises more than is natural, but always stays close to the river. You might get sent to check its latest location.

## Other Tributaries

The  Westbourne  (25)  is  another  river  with  a  Hampstead source. She flows through Hyde Park and was the original source  of  the  Serpentine  lake,  created  under  the  orders  of Queen Caroline. She joins the Thames at Chelsea, which is the name the goddess prefers.

Counter's Creek (26) begins just west of Ladbrook Grove and flows to Chelsea Creek through Olympia. The Brent rises in the Borough of Barnet in northwest London, and joins the Thames at Brentford. The Lea flows through the Lea Valley to the Thames and is the least urbanised of London's rivers.

## Grand Union Canal

The Grand Union Canal runs from London to Birmingham. The Regent's Canal is part of that. The spirit of the waterway is an orangutan who escaped from Regent's Park's London Zoo,  and  who  now  lives  in  a  house  on  St  Mark's  Terrace. There's some ongoing dispute between her and Mama Thames, but  the  Folly  doesn't  have  the  full  details.  'Mrs  Canal'

often  knows  more  about  what's  going  on  in  Regent's  Park than Lady Ty, and was helpful when we were investigating Terrence  Skinner,  who  lives  in  a  penthouse  on  Park  Road overlooking the park. In return for information, the goddess appreciates  offerings  of  bananas  and  promises  of  greener living. She's usually accompanied by her follower Melvin, who she took responsibility for after an unfortunate incident at a Mount Pleasant corporate entertainment event. Also under her  protection  is  an  elderly  ex-marine  called  Marcia,  who lives on a canal boat near Camden Lock and grows 'magic weed' underwater. The weed has been deemed not Falconactionable. The Unbreakable Empire Pottery Company has a warehouse with loading gates on the canal's towpath.

## Greater London

London is a huge city, which has sprawled out in rings of residential areas, engulfing smaller towns and villages. Most people live in the outer boroughs, commuting into town for work or pleasure. Falcon investigations can take us out to the suburbs, and you are more likely to find a vampire nest in a 1950s semi-detached house than in a Soho nightclub.

## Bromley

Bromley is on the border between South East London and Kent, and is one of the outer boroughs we've had dealings with. Professional safe-cracker George Trenchard 44 also had a  legitimate  locksmith business in Bromley, before coming to  an  unfortunate  end  in  his  terraced  home.  One  smalltime  drug  dealer's  face  was  found  magically  moulded  into a tree on the edge of the borough after he ran afoul of the wrong people; said drug dealer was also known to frequent the  Glitrrz  nightclub  on  Bromley  High  Street.  DC  Grant sometimes  plays  games  at  Paradice  Board  Games  Café  in Bromley's old mall, but that's not yet a crime.

The Ravensbourne is the local river. Folly records are out of date on whether it has any supernatural inhabitants. The source  is  at  Caesar's  Well  in  Keston.  The  route-mostly below  ground-goes  through  Bromley,  Lewisham,  and Greenwich, joining the Thames at Deptford. Places along it have been associated with rebellions, including Wat Tyler's 1381 Peasants' Revolt. It's on the action list to check.

## Epping Forest

London  isn't  just  a  built-up  city.  Epping  Forest,  on  the border of London and Essex, is ancient woodland. Although it's outside the City of London, it's managed by the City of London Police. Zach mentioned seeing a unicorn in Epping Forest,  which  would  indicate  fae  activity.  Watch  how  you tread-according to DC Grant, those things are not to be messed with ( Unicorns , page 254).

Footnote: 43 Tales from the Folly ('A Dedicated Follower of Fashion')

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000748_7dcdb58bf63d7ae4b4a7ec3817234080f096ee5f14edcdeec9a636f17721b3fc.png)

## Demi-Monde Pubs and Nazareths

Certain pubs, including The Chestnut Tree (19) at Marble Arch, The Goat and Crocodile in Shoreditch, and Spaniards Inn  in  Highgate,  cater  for  the  demi-monde,  who  include practitioners, fae, and other magical beings, as well as people with  an  interest  in  the  weird.  Some,  like  The  Rising  Sun in Smithfield, are on the fringes of the demi-monde, while others,  like  a  shebeen-you  know,  an  unlicenced  pub-on a  Hillingdon tower block's roof or the secret pub-with-noname  under  Oxford  Street  ( Underground  London ,  page 274) are deep inside it.

Even  more  elusive  are  the  nazareths,  formerly  known as  goblin  fairs.  These  are  pop-up  markets  for  London's supernatural  community,  as  well  as  for  socialising  and meetings under truce. The Folly's records show nazareths go back at least to 1534, with one annually at St Bartholomew's Feast on August 24 th . Locations change, but are often empty houses, disused industrial sites, and warehouses. If you don't know already, a nazareths' rules are:

- No weapons.
- No glamours, masks, or helmets.
- Pax Domus , or 'House Peace,' meaning no fights.

It's  a  shadow  market  economy.  The  legality  of  things bought  and  sold  at  a  nazareth  is  often  as  dubious  as  their provenance.  Police  are  not  welcome,  especially  those  from the Folly. They call us the 'Isaacs' after you-know-who. We leave  them  alone  unless  invited  or  there's  a  pressing  need: then  it's  best  to  send  in  someone  unknown.  You  might fit the bill.

## Conclusion

Right,  you  lot-that's  just  a  brief  look  at  London  from  a Falcon  viewpoint.  Our  city  is  huge,  ancient,  and  magical, with far more going on than I can cover in this short briefing. Learning  about  that  is  what  makes  this  job  so  interesting. Good luck in all your investigations. And don't forget, tea in the atrium in five minutes…

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000749_8e6c4fc4380386d3aaf09c970558a237f3717be4334fc2c5e0c12528a96c5168.png)

283

2

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000750_7c5f5793f4c12b71a9301cd6e4d64ea97cba4eec0e57454eaf354851816cf217.png)

THIS INTRODUCTORY CASE fi le  is  for  one  to  four investigators  and  is  designed  to  take  around  two  hours  to play. It is set in a bookshop in Covent Garden, London, and aims to introduce players to the workings of the Folly, as well as helping them to become familiar with the rules of Rivers of London: the Roleplaying Game . Throughout this adventure, you'll find plenty of guidance on how the rules work, along with  additional  support  in  the  form  of  Game  Moderator (GM) notes, like the one following.

## GM ADVICE

This scenario is ideal for one to four investigators, as it is a small and low-key affair. While it is possible to play with up to six investigators, we don't recommend it if this is your first time running RoL:RPG. Advice for  running  games  with  more  than  four  players can be found in Player Group Size (page 222).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000751_22f4575fb2494e484c0b55e48b85ecdc21129f2f475f0dfa27638c02aef8c1b2.png)

If you are going to play through this case file as an investigator, then stop reading now! The information herein is for the GM's eyes only, and the GM is advised to read through the whole adventure at least once before running it for their players. When reading it through, it can be useful to have a notepad to hand so that you make notes of the key and important events, which can then act as an aide-mémoire when running The Bookshop with your players (see also Running a Game , page 220).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000752_90a17debadeadfaeb0e2fec40ea2777bc1b349826db494ac6fe154b0e064873f.png)

'It's not easy being a bookseller,' said Warwick Anderson-bookseller. -'The Cockpit'

to run and play through. It's also a good example of how you can use the Rivers of London books as inspiration for your own case files ( Using the Source Material , page 336).

## GM Background

Stonewaters Bookshop is only a stone's throw from the piazza at  Covent  Garden,  the  scene  of  a  most  unfortunate  series of incidents not that long ago 2 .  Last night, something very strange happened in the bookshop's basement: an apparent poltergeist attack! Both the shop's manager, Saffron Jackson, and DCI Thomas Nightingale would like answers, and it's up to the investigators to find them.

The bookshop is no ordinary bookshop, of course. In the 18 th century, the building's ground floor was home to one of London's most famous cockfighting rings. Potent vestigia -a combination of the power released at the birds' moment of death  and  the  intense  emotions  of  the  spectators-seeped into  the  fabric  of  the  building.  That  latent  magical  energy hung there, undisturbed, for well over 100 years. And then, for some reason, it began to metamorphose.

The Bookshop is a somewhat unusual adventure; like the solo adventure at the beginning of this book, it's based on one of Ben's short stories, 'The Cockpit.' 1 In the Rivers of London timeline, the  story  takes  place  between  the  events  of Whispers  Under Ground and Broken Homes (December 2012 and March 2013), while RoL:RPG is nominally set in 2016, a little after the events of False Value . But we're going to pull it out of the timeline and put the investigators in Peter Grant's and Lesley May's shoes, in the same way your solitary investigator took on Peter's role in 'The Domestic' 1 . Why? Because 'The Cockpit' has a single key location and a single antagonist, making it straightforward

But why? The basement was, until just over a week ago, home to the children's book department. The concentrated reservoir of vestigia contained within the shop interacted with the power of the children's imaginations that had been fired up by all the books  they  either  read  or  that  were  read  to  them,  especially during the shop's weekly Saturday morning children's story time session. As a result, the vestigia evolved into something newnot actually a poltergeist, but rather an infant genius loci ! One that, now the children and their stories have moved elsewhere, is  feeling  rather  petulant  and  abandoned. And, like all small children  who  feel  they  are  being  ignored,  the  newly-formed spirit of books and reading is acting out to get some attention.

## Introducing the Investigators

Thanks to its origins, this is a slightly unusual case file, so the players may wish to create 'practice'  investigators for it, i.e., ones they may not end up using again. This allows the players to get

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000753_715fc2a4da766fb09802fa56e0918954bcc57f13a1d42371968f526bf40b8622.png)

8

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000754_aad13f8421c3ec972cc643148427996061e2af996f4148bed8f3f48b969f8dac.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000755_5e9c7a51556f3660b92cedf167f28e09146532c330c54ccb8009670c975f8505.png)

the hang of building an investigator without being committed to  keeping  them  for  the  long  term-although  it's  perfectly fine if  they  do  want to keep their new investigators once the adventure is over! Alternatively, the players can use the readyto-play (pre-generated) investigators in Appendix A to see how characteristics, skills, advantages, and spells work in play before creating investigators of their own for their next case file.

If the players do decide to create their own investigators, it  is  recommended  that  at  least  one  of  them  chooses  the Police Officer occupation. Other useful occupations include  Architect,  Author,  Chancer,  Lecturer,  Librarian, and Parapsychologist. If they are practice investigators, we suggest  skipping  the  Backstory  step  of  character  creation ( Create  a  Backstory , page 56), unless that helps the players get a handle on who their investigators are. All the investigators should be new recruits to the Folly, and at least one investigator should have the Magical advantage and the Magic skill. Use the following bullet points to help guide the players when they create their investigators:

- The following expert skills should prove very useful: Art/ Craft (Architecture), History, and Occult.
- The most useful spell for an investigator to know for this case file is: Werelight.
- The following expert skills may be useful, but are not essential: Computer Use and Tech.

## GM ADVICE

Even if none of the investigators have the expert  skills  mentioned  here,  one  of  them  can always  try  their  Luck  when  that  particular  skill is  required  ( Trying  Your  Luck ,  page  114).  For  an example of how this works in this case file, see Stonewaters (page 292).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000756_2bfb89c7e1f9a40a1756230767c12660ef29881de2553c7edf9afc848c781aab.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000757_bebfb0e32b5b77c9ce056f137a9295790d4ef23df155ce906ca1368140012694.png)

## Dramatis Personae

This  section  provides  descriptions  for  the  key  non-player characters (NPCs) who are involved in this case file. These include  what  they  look  like,  their  motivations,  and  the role they play in the adventure. These entries don't include their game profiles, which are instead gathered together at the end of the scenario in the Cast section  (page  307)  for quick reference when needed-we suggest the GM copies or prints  out  these  gathered  profiles  for  ease  of  use,  meaning less page flipping when running the case file. Note that DCI Nightingale's game profile can be found on page 233 of A Rogues' Gallery , should you need to refer to that.

## Warwick Anderson, age 29, frightened bookseller

Anderson is the unlucky victim of a most unusual workplace assault. He works in the bookshop as a sales assistant.

- Description: a white man of average build and appearance, with spiky, rather mad-looking, blond hair-'tousled' would be a kind description. Dressed in beige chinos and a red and white checked shirt, with a button-down collar.
- Traits: a firm believer that customers are a necessary evil, especially as they have a habit of getting in the way of restocking the shelves. He's still somewhat shaken by what happened last night and determined not to return to the bookshop until the 'poltergeist' is taken care of.
- Roleplaying hooks: the investigators' principal witness and source of information about exactly what happened in the basement.

## Saffron Jackson, age 36, bookshop manager

Ms  Jackson  runs  this  busy,  central-London  bookshop,  a task that involves organising the shop's displays to maximise sales, staff training and development, and delivering efficient customer service-which usually boils down to deciphering vague  book  descriptions  from  customers  or  giving  them directions to the theatre round the corner.

- Description: a tall, slender Black woman with a long face and relaxed hair cut into a pixie bob. Dressed in a crisp, ivory-coloured blouse, smart suit trousers, and patent leather Oxford shoes.
- Roleplaying hooks: can grant the investigators access to the bookshop after hours.
- Traits: business-like and professional, but rather concerned by what's happening in the basement, not least because of the stock damage it's caused.

## Ernie, ghost-hunting dog-in-training

Another  annoying,  yappy  little  dog,  but  a  Yorkshire  Terrier rather than whatever Toby is. Ernie's owner, Mr Fitzroy, lives a few doors up from the Folly; he's an elderly gentleman and quite happy for 'those nice young people' (i.e., the investigators) to come and take his dog out for him whenever they feel the need.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000758_6b17e51f0eab821008da2ce113478b001dfb245161846bd559d5869a82dc2ae7.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000759_5f1f099e42d28e85c87da1e965f4c64e410645349730160486165e6d40903f61.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000760_e49857d8e502f36005b4cbf2bda6bd29d2f5b96c164eb6587b30f137f5f44d83.png)

8

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000761_9a2931e2b5de2e60a1082ed2e18fa2d7905afcb7c4abe92433024147c90f1155.png)

- Description: a small, short-haired Yorkie with a black and tan coat. Occasionally grubby, if he's been rummaging through the dustbins.
- Roleplaying hooks: while not as adept as Toby in the ghost-hunting department, Ernie makes a half-decent substitute. Besides, taking him for 'walkies' helps an elderly neighbour.
- Traits: fast, cantankerous, and wily, but willing to behave if suitably bribed with sausages (artisan or otherwise).

## The Spirit of Books and Reading, lonely genius loci

This genius loci is the cause of Stonewaters' current 'haunting.'

- Description: a non-corporeal spirit, initially identified through its tendency to throw things about.
- Roleplaying hooks: too newly formed to have developed the skill to use The Glamour (page 243) to make people do its bidding; the investigators need to find a way to placate the infant genius loci before someone gets seriously hurt.
- Traits: lonely and petulant.

## Start: Morning Briefing

Once all your players have their investigators ready, you can begin the game. Make sure everyone is ready to start and has dice and pencils to hand.

Sunday, 8:00 am: DCI Nightingale has called the investigators to meet him in the former visitor's lounge, now the briefing room ( 1 on the map of the Folly, page 289). The GM should read aloud or paraphrase the following to set the scene and establish what the case file is about:

'Good morning, everyone. Thank you for joining me. Earlier, I received a telephone call from our colleagues at the Charing Cross Primary Crime Unit. There  was  an  incident  late  last  night  at Stonewaters Bookshop on New Row that they believe will be of interest  to  us.  One  of  the  booksellers,  a  Mr  Warwick  Anderson, was  assaulted  at  approximately  12:30  am  while  rearranging a  display  in  the  bookshop's  basement.  Mr  Anderson,  though somewhat concussed, managed to telephone the emergency services. The call handler sent a response team to the bookshop, where they had to force an entry in order to render assistance.

'Our colleagues from Charing Cross carried out a search of the premises  while  Mr  Anderson  was  being  seen  by  the  attending paramedics. They found no alarms tripped and no evidence of a break-in,  nor  of  anything  having  been  stolen.  However,  there were obvious signs of a disturbance in the basement matching the description given to them by the victim.

'I  would  like  you  to  interview  Mr  Anderson  to  establish  his recollection  of  events.  He  is  awaiting  you  at  the  Cappuccino Lounge, also on New Row. Then, I would like you to visit the bookshop  and  carry  out  an  inspection,  including  an  Initial Vestigium  Assessment,  to  determine  whether  this  is,  indeed,  a Falcon case. Ms Saffron Jackson, the store's manager, will be on hand to escort you. The bookshop usually opens at 11:00 am on a  Sunday,  and,  by  all  accounts,  Ms  Jackson  is  keen  to  resume business at the earliest possible convenience.

'Report  back  here  to  me  once  you  have  completed  your  initial inquiries. I await your findings with interest.'

And  with  that,  Nightingale  nods  his  farewells  to  the investigators  and  leaves  the  room.  As  this  is  probably  the investigators' first case together, the GM should encourage the  players  to  introduce  their  characters  before  they  head off  to  Covent Garden. If the bookshop is to open on time, they have less than three hours to carry out their preliminary inquiries, and the clock is ticking... They better get a move on!

## GM ADVICE

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000762_0c45ad825441209eea54ca2d703d3ff92d864c312962b0061e076393a6838f31.png)

In most case files, the investigators have free rein over where they go, to whom they talk, and when, without having to report back to Nightingale after each step. But, as this is most likely their first Falcon investigation, we've taken the liberty of being a little more proscriptive. In addition,  having  the  players  report  back  in  this  way tells you exactly what clues they've picked up on and what theories they've developed. It also highlights any misunderstandings that may have arisen, meaning you can  use  Nightingale  to  get  them  back  on  the  right track. See also Case Update (page 297).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000763_f8c75a97b25cf006bfbff39486f1f2f55a064ee13e9dd30dc1707bb3edc60975.png)
