8

## Preliminary Inquiries

Once  the  investigators  have  introduced  themselves  to  the other members of their team, it's time for them to make their way to Covent Garden to interview Warwick Anderson and then visit the site of the alleged Falcon incident.

## The Cappuccino Lounge

The coffee shop lies approximately 20 metres/yards away from Stonewaters Bookshop, on the same side of the narrow street that  forms  New Row. Sitting inside, clutching a glass cup of what looks like the eponymous frothy coffee, is Warwick Anderson. He is accompanied by a uniformed officer out of Charing Cross Police Station-PC Singh, according to his name badge; if asked, his given name is Karnam. Anderson looks exhausted and somewhat rumpled; his blond hair is all mussed up into wayward spiky bits, although that may just be the way he styles it. He also keeps glancing nervously over his  shoulder.  He  relaxes  slightly,  though,  when  PC  Singh acknowledges the investigators' arrival.

## Interviewing Warwick Anderson

After  you  have  set the scene as described  above, the investigators  have  the  opportunity  to  learn  exactly  what happened last night from the sole witness. As this is the first time the investigators have met Anderson, the GM should

Handout: Bookshop 1

encourage them to show him either their police warrant cards ( The Warrant Card , page 211) or their Folly consultant's ID cards  ( The Folly Consultant's Card ,  page  60)  to  establish their credentials.

When the investigators begin questioning Anderson, the GM should give the players Handout: Bookshop 1 ,  which contains  his  witness  statement.  Allow  the  players  time  to read it and absorb what it says, or alternatively, the GM could ask one of the players to read it aloud to everyone.

The investigators, having taken Anderson's statement, may wish to ask some follow-up questions. Use the following to help guide your answers:

- If asked about the cookery books, he explains that due to the public's apparently insatiable appetite for celebrity chefs, his manager, Saffron Jackson, decided to expand that section, and that's why he was rearranging things last night. He adds that a couple of other book sections have moved recently as well.
- If asked which other sections have been relocated, he says that the art and children's sections swapped places last week. 'Saturday Story Time is really popular, and people couldn't get their prams up and down the stairs very easily. We don't have a lift because it's a listed building, so Saffron decided to move it to the ground floor.'

## Warwick Anderson's Statement

Iwas working in the basement.It was around midnight,give or take,moving the celebrity chef books to the other end of thecookery section,whenIwas hit in the back by something.I turned around to find Banksy'sWall andPiece atmyfeetthe paperback, not the hardback, or else that would have really hurt.

Thing is, I was supposed to be the only one in the shop. There was no sign of anyone who could have thrown it, soI'll admit, I was spooked.I went upstairs to take a look around and make sure no one had broken in,but all the doors were locked, and I couldn't see anyone. I was feeling a bit annoyed by this point, so I gave up looking and wentbacktoreshelving thebooks.

Next thingI know,Igethitontheheadbyasofttoy,liketheoneswekeepatthetill points. I was just about to turn around and catch whoever threw it red-handed when a tonofbookssmashedintomefromthe artsectionandflattenedme.Acoupleofthe really big Taschen art books caught me in the back of the head,but I managed to stagger to the phone at the downstairstill and dialled999.

It's all a bit hazy after that,but someone helped me up to the ambulance,where they toldmeI had amild concussion.Iwenthome,thenIgotacall askingmetocomeback in so I could be interviewed by some special crimes unit—you lot,I'm assuming?I told them therewasnowayIwasgoingwithin500metres of that place,but eventually,they convinced me tomeetyou here instead.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000767_29bb9f21b7c4354912a365159262ad2e799f7db255bd70b58685152d804e4c0a.png)

- If asked if there's anything special about the building, besides it being listed, Anderson admits that he doesn't think so. 'It's a bit of an odd shape, but that's because the original owners bought two small shops on Garrick Street and one on New Row, then knocked them together and fitted out the basement. It's a total nightmare when it comes to shoplifters-lots of dead space.'
- If asked how old the building is, Anderson replies, 'I think parts of it date from the 18th century, but I couldn't swear to that.'

## GM ADVICE

Don't  worry  if  the  investigators  don't  pick  up  on the  fact  that  the  children's  section  has  moved recently; there is another opportunity for them to discover this important piece of information when they  speak  to  Stonewaters'  manager  ( Interviewing Saffron Jackson ,  page 294). The same is true about the  bookshop's  age  and  shape-they'll  see  that for themselves in a few minutes.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000768_4cb15814af4db4e6b34dadaa0b6a31c97cdf0b6854448d792aff4d196bf02b45.png)

## A Surprising Development

As  the  investigators  ready  themselves  to  leave,  Anderson blurts out, ' You know what I reckon? I reckon it was a poltergeist! '

If  any  of  the  investigators  has  the Magic skill,  the  GM should ask those players to roll percentile dice and compare the result to their Magic skill value. Investigators with the expert skill, Occult , can also make a skill roll.

## GM ADVICE

Why roll for some things but not others? Because a lot of the information Warwick Anderson has is essential to getting the case moving, so you don't want to keep it from the investigators. However, some does require a little more knowledge and understanding, hence the Magic/Occult skill roll request.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000769_d4b8ff61beb635710efdf1cf08af35ffd1274baa8eeb12d24404c3592bc1f40e.png)

- If the investigator achieves a Regular success (equal to or below their full skill value), they not only know that poltergeists are a type of ghost, but also that ghosts don't normally throw things around or try to hurt people, and if they do, it's pretty serious-in fact, it may not be a ghost at all, but something much more concerning.
- If the investigator achieves a Hard success (equal to or below half of their full skill value), they know that it takes at least 100 years for a genuine poltergeist to develop-so if Anderson is right about the building's age, then a poltergeist isn't beyond the realms of possibility.

291

- If no one succeeds, the players could spend Luck points (on a one-for-one basis) to adjust the roll into a success ( Spending Luck , page 113).
- If no one succeeds and no one chooses to spend Luck or push their skill roll, the investigators can overcome this by doing some research on poltergeists back at the Folly ( Visiting the Libraries , page 298).
- Alternatively, if they really want to, they could choose to push their rolls, i.e., describe what their investigators are doing so they can make the skill roll again; for example, taking a deep breath and mentally running through everything they know about ghosts. There will be consequences if they fail a second time, though-if this is the case, perhaps they convince themselves that it could only be a poltergeist and nothing else ( Pushing the Roll , page 108), or alternatively, they somehow cause offense to Anderson, who refuses to answer more of their questions.

## GM ADVICE

Remember, flat-out failing an investigative roll, like this  one,  is  no  fun.  It's  always  better  if  you  treat a  failed  dice  roll  as  a  'Yes,  but…'  or  a  'No,  but…' situation ( Failure (or Weak Success) ,  page  106);  for example,  while  the  investigators  might  not  get  all the information, they get just enough to keep their inquiries  on  track-or  it  just  takes  them  a  longer time  to  find  all  the  information  they're  after.  In this  case,  although  the  investigators  don't  recall much  about  poltergeists,  the  'No,  but…  you  can do  some  research'  answer  opens  a  potential  line of inquiry, one they can follow up on later ( Case Update , page 297).

## Next Steps

There  is  nothing  more  Warwick  Anderson  can  tell  the investigators-it's  time  for  them  to  visit  the  scene  of  the crime ( Stonewaters , following).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000770_639e5774e9581d3e1f080b55cb22dadec2d936b92d3f8aa986f098162850953d.png)

## Stonewaters

As  the  investigators  approach  the  bookshop's  New  Row entrance, they can see that one of its glass panels has been replaced  by  a  length  of  plywood-evidence  of  how  the response crew got inside last night.

- A successful Art/Craft (Architecture) or History roll identifies that the shop front appears to be Georgianthough not as grand as the Folly, by any means. Given how old it is, the building is probably also listed, meaning that its owners are very limited when it comes to making alterations.
- If the investigators asked Warwick Anderson about the bookshop's history, this confirms his suspicions about parts of the building dating from the 18 th century.

If  none  of  the  investigators  has  the  Art/Craft  (Architecture) or History skill, one of them can try their Luck. To do this, they  spend  10  Luck  points,  roll  their  percentile  dice,  and compare  the  result  to  their INT characteristic  instead  of the asked-for expert skill ( Trying Your Luck , page 114). If they achieve a Hard or a Critical success, they learn that the building is Georgian and, most likely, listed. If they want to, the player can now spend another 10 Luck points to gain either Art/Craft (Architecture) or History (their choice) as a new expert skill for their investigator.

## GM ADVICE

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000771_4e535d4b77c1029ed140c2f14cf822765374678a8c87146b3385e99f9614d3cc.png)

Remember: a Critical success is when the player rolls 01.

If  the  investigators  peer  through  one  of  the  adjoining windows,  towards  the  rear  of  the  building  they  see  a  tall Black woman busying herself by a till. The doors are locked, but tapping on the window gets the woman's attention. She walks calmly to the doors, points at her watch, and mouths: ' Sorry! We don't open 'til 11. '

Flashing their warrant or Folly consultant's cards through  the  window  at  the  woman  prompts  a  mouthed apology. She unlocks the door and ushers the investigators inside, introducing herself as Saffron Jackson, the bookshop's manager.

- A successful Read Person roll notes that Ms Jackson seems relieved to see them, but also slightly irritated.

She asks the investigators if they've spoken to Warwick Anderson, and if he's alright. ' He'd gone by the time I got here, ' she  explains.  As  the  investigators  enter  the  bookshop,  the GM should ask them to make an Observation skill roll.

- A Hard success spots a convex mirror mounted above the left-hand door, presumably to cover one of the dead spots Anderson mentioned.
- A Regular success spots a large, apparently glazed dome set into the ceiling over the till.
- Players can choose to spend Luck or attempt to push the skill roll to turn a failure into a success. Failing the pushed roll might result in the investigator walking into a display and knocking it over, making a fool of themselves in the process.

## GM ADVICE

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000772_36b34ab68ca9edd13abcb7fa7cbddedacbdcb2a9057756ddf75d95df65c7bb79.png)

This Observation  roll is worth  encouraging  the players  to  spend  Luck  on  (or  push)  to  achieve  a Regular  success,  as  the  dome-or,  rather,  what's associated with it-is important to what's happening in  the  bookshop.  They  don't  need  a  Hard  success, although  if  they  do  spot  the  mirror  over  the  door, it  should give them the hint that there are likely to be other security devices elsewhere in the building ( The  Basement ,  page  296).  Remember,  as  GM,  you should always play fair, and shouldn't encourage your players  to  spend  Luck  on  rolls-or  push  them-if they don't help to move the story forward or gain the investigators an advantage of some sort.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000773_428c7437a2249b2ebff2cbc76d612d196fe4e55c45943321f1d778b471d8a4e7.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000774_b36752a0c6423229defdeecf3e12b7d91b841a6780fb9a802ca20a88f152cac8.png)

Chapter 8: The Bookshop

## BOOKSHOP

NEW ROW

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000775_5a5c827191cfeb75e70ec7d75f1048837d11f1f61c2e1c868470be8f0b6892d1.png)

8

## Interviewing Saffron Jackson

If the investigators ask Ms Jackson about what happened the night before, the GM should give them Handout: Bookshop 2 , which contains her witness statement. As with Handout: Bookshop 1, the GM could ask one of the players to read it aloud or give the players time to read it for themselves.

As with Warwick Anderson, the investigators may have some  questions  they  wish  to  ask  Ms  Jackson  as  she  leads them through the shop:

- If asked about which book sections have been relocated, she confirms that the children's and art sections recently swapped places. ' Saturday Story Time's so much easier now that we don't have to worry about getting prams up and down the stairs. '
- If asked about any other odd occurrences, she shakes her head. ' No, nothing. Never heard any ghost stories about the building, and people usually love to gossip about that sort of stuff. '
- If asked about the dome over the till, she says she doesn't know anything about it. A successful Art/ Craft (Architecture) or History roll suggests that the bookshop's central section may have once been part of a hall or ballroom, and the dome is a relic of that.

## Handout: Bookshop 2

## Exploring the Ground Floor

Just  as  Warwick  Anderson  mentioned,  the  bookshop  is  a very strange shape: the entrance from New Row leads to the central section, which lies in the wedge-shaped gap between the buildings fronting onto New Row and Garrick Street. Just beyond the central section's halfway point, a spur containing the travel section leads to one of the Garrick Street entrances and, just beyond that, a set of glass-sided stairs leads down to  the  basement.  Finally,  another  spur  leads  to  the  second Garrick Street entrance; this is where the children's section is currently located.

## Conducting an Initial Vestigium Assessment (IVA)

As the investigators pass the main till and the dome aboveor  if  they  say  they  are  carrying  out  their  Initial  Vestigium Assessment (IVA),  as  Nightingale  requested-the  GM should  ask  everyone  to  make  a Sense  Vestigia skill  roll. Books  absorb vestigia pretty  much  the  same  way  egg-box shaped foam padding absorbs sound, so the vestigia here is dampened, making it weak and harder to detect ( Vestigia Strength , page 161). As always, an investigator can choose to spend Luck or push the roll to achieve a success, or upgrade a

Regular success to a Hard success.

## Saffron Jackson's Statement

Igot aphonecall in the earlyhoursfrom apolice officer askingme to come to thebookshop to take careof thedoor.Theysaid they'd had tobreaktheglass toget inside to seewhat had happened topoorWarwick.Theyleft shortly after thatbecause itwas allkickingoffin the piazza;a fight outside a bar,or something-I don'tknow.

Next,I called themaintenancecompany tosendsomeone over toboard upthe door andclean up the glass. Once the maintenance guy got here, I checked around to see if anything was missing. It didn't look like it, but it's a bit hard to tell in all that mess downstairs.I've not touched anythingdown therebecauseoneoftheofficerstoldmenot tountilyou'dbeen to see it.

So,ifyou'd liketofollowme,I'll takeyou to thebasement.I don'twant to tell you howto do your job, but I'd really appreciate it if you could hurry it upI'd like to get everything all squared away againbefore opening time.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000776_4e7aca385e909a77c621f322d53a036f451b8764bce15a026a3911e76db70ff8.png)

- If an investigator achieves a Regular success, they detect something that smells very much like a slaughterhouse combined with rotting straw.
- If an investigator fails the roll and chooses not to spend Luck or push it, they instead get the standard background vestigium of central London (pain, joy, sweat, tears, and something that sounds very much like a sheep bleating forlornly).
- If an investigator achieves a Hard success, they detect the smells, but also hear shouting and experience a feeling of excitement, desire, and disappointment.
- If an investigator fumbles the roll (i.e., rolls 100 on their percentile dice), no matter how they try, they can't pick up on anything at all-it's almost as if all the magic has been sucked out of the building.
- If the investigators attempt an IVA anywhere in the shop-except when beneath the dome or in the basement-all they get with a successful Sense Vestigia roll is the central London background.

## Handout: Bookshop 3

## COCKFIGHTING

sport in London and elsewhere in the UK. Historically speaking, its roots go back over 6,ooo years, though it first became really popular in England during the Tudor era. By the 18th century, the blood sport was at its height, with all levels of society indulging in the heavy betting that accompanied each match. Upper class venues, like the Royal Cockpit (whose last remaining traces are Cockpit Steps by Birdcage Walk), charged a hefty admission fee to keep the riffraff out.

It took around two years to fully train a fighting bird. Due to the amount of money required for that training, and the size of the wagers on fight days, this blood sport was tightly regulated—more so than any other sport at the time. It was finally banned in England and Wales in 1835 under the Cruelty to Animals Act.

295

## Using the Internet

Either as a result of their Sense Vestigia roll or out of general interest, the investigators may wish to use their mobile smart devices to see if they can find anything on the internet about the dome or the building. If they do, the GM should ask the players to make a Research roll:

- A Regular success brings up little more than the bookshop's web page and the fact that it's a listed building, dating from the Georgian era. They find nothing about the dome or anything else about the location's history.
- A failure only finds the bookshop's website, which conveniently lists its opening and closing times.
- A Hard success reveals that, during the Georgian era, the site was home to a famous cockfighting ring-one of the most famous in London, in fact. The dome may be associated with that, but no one is 100 percent certain (it is, in fact, the only visible internal fitting left from the building's cockfighting days, hence its significance).

If the investigators discover the link between the building and  cockfighting,  a  successful Research or History roll reveals  the  information  contained  in Handout: Bookshop 3 -give the players this handout when appropriate. If they

8

need it,  the  GM may also grant the investigators an INT roll for them to make the link between cockfighting and the bookshop's strange vestigia .

## GM ADVICE

Remember,  the  Research  skill  is  for  hunting  down stored information, physical or virtual-for example, on  the  internet,  in  a  library,  or  in  a  bookshop!  The Observation,  Read  Person,  and  Social  skills  are  for spotting  clues  and  winkling  information  out  of people-whether they realise it or not!

## GM ADVICE

Again,  don't  worry  too  much  if  the  investigators don't  discover  the  link  to  cockfighting  just  yetthey  can  carry  out  further  research  back  at  the Folly if needed.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000777_a4eb48ba25a7eff6c8dd630307ed8713a89eb2113bfec66e14a10e03f1de37aa.png)

## The Basement

Down the  stairs  lies  the  basement.  Its  main  section  runs beneath part of the upstairs' central section. On the far wall, a  security  door  leads  out  to  a  staff  area.  Two  spurs  mirror those on the ground floor; both have fire exit doors leading into a stairwell that emerges on the ground floor opposite the main stairwell.

At the bottom of the stairs, to the right, is a till pointthis  must  be  the  one  Warwick  Anderson  used  to  call  the emergency services. Just beyond the till, between it and the door to the staff area, lies the cookery section. The art section is located directly beneath the travel section.

- As they reach the till, the GM should ask the investigators to make an Observation roll; they gain a bonus die to the roll if they noticed the mirror above the entrance upstairs ( Stonewaters , page 292). If an investigator succeeds at the roll, they spot the CCTV camera, mounted to cover a dead spot behind the till.

## GM ADVICE

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000778_0107f711f4c42b31a944c561a49d5f191f910520554d2d6c2d46f4efe0f4847c.png)

- It takes a successful Social roll to convince Ms Jackson to let the investigators see the CCTV recording-remember, police officer investigators who have shown her their warrant card gain a bonus die to this roll. As always, players can choose to spend Luck or push the roll to achieve a success, although a fumble or a failed pushed roll means Ms Jackson won't let them see the footage without a warrant. If the investigators manage to gain permission to see the CCTV footage, see Caught on Camera (page 297).

There is a large, disorganised pile of books in the middle of the cookery section. The mess appears to have originated from five shelves in the art section. To have thrown them, someone would have had to sneak round behind Anderson without him noticing-extremely tricky, but not completely impossible if he was engrossed in his work. The main problem with  this  theory  is:  where  did  they  hide  when  Anderson searched the shop? And how did they throw so many books at once? The GM should allow the investigators an INT roll to realise these points if their players don't think about it.

- If the investigators have not yet carried out an IVA downstairs, ask them to make another Sense Vestigia roll before they leave the basement. As under the dome, a Regular success detects the smells of a slaughterhouse and rotting straw, while a Hard success reveals all the elements of the building's vestigia -as detailed in Conducting an Initial Vestigium Assessment (IVA) on page 294.

## Cooking Up a Spell

If a player gains a bonus  die to add to their investigator's skill roll, they roll three percentile dice instead  of  the  usual  two:  two  'tens'  dice  and  one 'units' die. They then compare the numbers on the two tens dice and use the one that would give them the best overall  result  (i.e.,  the  lowest  result).  For example, if a player rolled 70 and 20 on their tens dice and a 4 on their units die, the two possible results would  be  74  and  24;  they  would  'keep'  the  24,  as that is the best (lowest) result, and use that to see if they'd succeeded at their skill roll. See Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111.

If the investigators wish to cast a spell while in the basement, they  can-although  they  might  not  want  to  do  so  in  Ms Jackson's presence! It costs a number of magic points equal to the spell's order to cast its basic (unboosted) version. For example, if an investigator wants to cast Impello (page 178), it  costs  1  magic  point  because  it  is  a  first  order  spell.  The GM then asks for a Magic skill roll to see if the spell is cast correctly. Remember, if the spell is mastered, the roll gains a bonus die ( Mastering a Spell , page 171), and investigators can  spend Luck or push the  roll  if  it  fails.  The  following applies to every spell except Werelight -for the effect of that spell, see Casting Werelight , following.

- If the Magic roll is any kind of success, the spell works exactly as expected.
- If the Magic roll fails, the spell does something a little strange: for example, Impello barely lifts even the smallest item; Snapdragon produces an almost silent, dull flash, and so on.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000779_01dac0b032273502052957ae52103ca3da1ee187099817c3d07d5cbfaa34c9c5.png)

- If the Magic roll is fumbled, or an investigator pushes the roll and fails again, there's a popping and fizzling sound as they destroy the microchips in both the store's till and CCTV camera, as well as those in any powered mobile devices in the basement.

## Casting Werelight

Any  investigator  with  the  Werelight  spell  knows  that  all ghosts  find  them  irresistible  (no  roll  required),  so  if  they suspect  there  may  be  a  poltergeist  in  the  basement,  this would be an ideal way to test that hypothesis.

- If an investigator successfully produces a basic (unboosted) werelight, it changes colour from milky white to crimson and slowly fades away; however, no ghostly figure appears. There's definitely something in the basement feeding on the werelight, but it doesn't seem to be a 'normal' ghost.
- If an investigator fumbles or fails a pushed spellcasting roll for a boosted or unboosted Werelight, or if multiple investigators cast Werelight in the basement, refer to Feeding Time (page 302).
- If an investigator has mastered Werelight, they can boost it in one of two ways: they can spend a total of 2 magic points when first casting the spell ( Boosting a Spell , page 176), or they can spend an additional magic point to refresh their fading (unboosted) werelight. If they do, refer to Feeding Time (page 302) for what happens next-the GM should adjust that section as necessary to reflect the difference in the time of day and who is present.

Regardless of which spell someone casts, they feel a faint tickling  sensation,  like  feathers  being  brushed  across  their skin.  That's  most  unusual  and  is  undoubtedly  linked  to whatever's going on down here.

## GM ADVICE

You  may  need  to  remind  your  players  about  the connection between ghosts and werelights if they're not  familiar  with  the  Rivers  of  London  setting.  It's perfectly  fine  for  you  to  do  this,  as  it's  something their investigators would know, having been taught this when learning the spell.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000780_567dd0566bc99d1547980043a36d23aa548664f2633dc9937b6b49c246af2d69.png)

## Caught on Camera

If  the  investigators  convince  Ms  Jackson  to  show  them last  night's  CCTV  footage,  she  leads  them  through  the basement door into a plain, grey corridor, past the storeroom and staffroom, and into her office. After fiddling with her keyboard  for  a  moment  or  two,  she  turns  her  computer

297

monitor  towards  the  investigators  and  shows  them  the recording. The footage's time stamp reads 12:30 am.

- Warwick Anderson appears on the far left of the screen, almost out of shot. Because of the camera's position, the books appear suddenly, entering the frame in mid-flight, shortly before hitting him. The force of their impact pushes him further into the frame as well as knocking him down. Warwick then pulls himself up and staggers drunkenly towards the till, disappearing out of shot again.

Although  the  footage  gives  no  indication  of  who-or what-moved  the  books,  anyone  with  the  Magic  skill realises  that  it  probably  wasn't  as  a  result  of  a  spell  being cast, as the camera is still working-use of that much magic would undoubtedly have fried its microprocessor ( Spells and Technology , page 176).

## Next Steps

Provided the investigators haven't had a spellcasting mishap ( Cooking Up a Spell ,  page  296),  there  is  nothing  else  of interest  in  the  bookshop  for  the  moment.  They  should now  return  to  the  Folly  to  report  their  findings  to  DCI Nightingale and determine what to do next.

If  the  investigators  wish  to  arrange  a  return  visit  to  the shop tonight-so they're present in the bookshop around the same time Anderson was attacked-the GM should ask for a Social skill roll to convince Ms Jackson, with a bonus die for any police officer investigators who have shown her their warrant card. If they are successful, Ms Jackson arranges to meet them after closing time (8:00 pm) so she can show them how to lock up and operate the alarm system.

## GM ADVICE

The  investigators  might  not  think  to  ask  to  come back to the store at night after their initial search of the bookshop. Don't worry, if they later think to do so,  they  can  easily  ring  Ms  Jackson  to  arrange  a return visit.

## Case Update

As promised, DCI Nightingale is waiting for the investigators back at the Folly, only this time he meets them in the atrium (7) for elevenses (morning tea and biscuits). After Molly has delivered  the  refreshments  and  glided  back  to  the  kitchen, Nightingale listens patiently to the investigators as they give him their progress report. Ask the players to recount their experience and findings so far; they can either all do this or nominate someone to speak for the group. Once they have finished  updating  him,  Nightingale  asks  them  what  they think their next steps should be.

- Depending on what the investigators learned at New Row, this could include researching poltergeists, the building's history, or cockfighting ( Visiting the Libraries , following).
- It should also involve the investigators coming up with a plan to work out what's in the basement ( Formulating a Plan , page 299). If the players haven't already begun to discuss what they want to do next, have Nightingale prompt them with, 'I want you to decide what you need to do to resolve the problem in the bookshop. Put your heads together and come up with a plan.'

## GM ADVICE

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000781_b157731ffc0b7091d8a1b22ffad49bea2933dc7e824372e4f471d6eaaa7a755b.png)

Don't be afraid to use Nightingale to clarify anything the  investigators  are  confused  by-although,  he's not  there  to  do  all  the  hard  work  for  them!  The same goes if they're struggling to work out what to do next. In the Rivers of London novels, when Peter Grant  needs  a  nudge,  it's  often  DCI  Nightingale  or someone similarly senior (like Dr Walid) who provides it.  They've  been  in  the  business  longer  than  Peter, and they know what to look for and how to interpret anomalous data. There's no reason for them not to do the same for your investigators as well, when the need arises.

## Visiting the Libraries

Thanks to the Folly's extensive archives, the mundane library on  the  ground  floor  (6)  is  the  ideal  place  to  dig  out  more information on Stonewaters Bookshop, if the investigators haven't already uncovered it ( Exploring the Ground Floor , page 294). The GM should ask investigators searching the mundane library to make a Research roll:

- A Regular success reveals the bookshop's and its dome's links to cockfighting (as per the information in Using the Internet , page 295), along with the information in Handout: Bookshop 3 . It takes two hours to locate this information.
- A failure reveals the same information, but it takes four hours to discover it.
- A Hard success or better uncovers the relevant information after just an hour of searching.
- A fumble suggests the investigator was distracted by something they found while researching the building, and lost all track of time and thus, they fail to find anything of value.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000782_445a9194e7dd84d7c1731373d86a6d2e979823988aef7143ba34a5240dd1a42b.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000783_c9b908ac7a056a759ae3d866f23c1a7b46271985e6617f387873b8a9ceaf3204.png)

## Consulting the Magical Library

The  magical  library  on  the  first  floor  (18)  is  better  suited to  researching  ghosts  and  poltergeists.  The  GM  should ask  investigators  searching  the  magical  library  to  make  a Research roll:

- A Regular success reveals that ghosts don't normally try to hurt people, as well as how long it takes for a poltergeist to form (around 100 years). It takes around two hours to locate this information.
- A failure reveals the same information, but it takes four hours to discover it.
- A Hard success or better uncovers the relevant information after just an hour of searching.
- A fumble suggests the investigator was distracted by something they found while researching ghosts, and lost all track of time and thus, they fail to find anything of value.

If  the  investigators  prefer  to  use  the  internet  for  their research,  they'll  need  to  do  so  in  the  coach  house  (15).  If using the Research skill there to find information, refer to the  results  detailed  in Using the Internet (page  295);  the investigators also find some general information about ghosts not usually hurting people.

## Formulating a Plan

By  now,  either  through  what  they  learned  on-site  at  the bookshop or with the addition of some time spent conducting research, the investigators should have learned the following:

- The attack on Warwick Anderson seems to have originated in the bookshop's art section.
- Anderson believes he was attacked by a poltergeist, but this is not normal behaviour for that type of ghost.
- It would have been almost impossible for a person to have carried out the attack without them being noticed.
- It's unlikely a spell was involved, as the till and the CCTV camera were both still working.
- There is an unusual vestigia in the bookshop that stands out from the central London background. (The investigators may or may not know that it is present both under the dome and in the basement, depending on how successful their IVAs were.)

While  all  of  this  is  useful  information,  it  still  doesn't answer the question: what attacked Warwick Anderson and why? Hence the need now for the investigators to formulate a plan for their next steps.

## Strategies

Sir  Isaac  Newton  may  not  have  recognised  the  term  'the scientific method,' but he would certainly have understood its principles: observation, research, hypothesis, testing, analysis, and conclusion.

- So far, the investigators have carried out the first two steps (observation and research) and may even have developed a hypothesis about what's happening, particularly if they cast Werelight and saw the colour change. What they need to do now is return to the bookshop to test their theories.
- If asked for his opinion, Nightingale suggests a return to the bookshop is the right thing to do to confirm that this is a genuine Falcon incident. He suggests they do so at night, around the time the original assault happened (12:30 am), thus maximising their chances of seeing something.
- If the investigators don't have a hypothesis to test, they need to carry out further research in order to develop one-again, the bookshop is the most logical place to do this. Apart from anything else, it could allow them to observe the phenomenon for themselves!

## They Didn't Cast Werelight?

If  the  investigators  didn't  cast  Werelight  while  in  the basement and have yet to see any direct evidence of spooky goings on, the GM should ask investigators with the Magic or Occult skills to make a dice roll for those skills. If they achieve  a  Regular  success  or  better,  they  realise  that  what they need is a ghost-hunting dog. Toby isn't available as Peter Grant is off dealing with another case, but there's always Mr Fitzroy's Yorkshire Terrier, Ernie ( Dramatis Personae , page 287). Thus, the investigators recall that Mr Fitzroy has a dog that might be just the thing to help them out. When they approach Mr Fitzroy, he is mildly surprised at the request to take Ernie out so late but is nevertheless quite happy for the investigators to borrow him for the evening.

- Besides the attack, the only recent event of any note in the bookshop was the swapping over of the art and children's sections.
- This vestigia is probably derived from the building's use as a cockfighting ring in the 18 th century.

Arranging  an  overnight  vigil  in  the  bookshop  requires a  phone  call  to  Ms  Jackson,  if  the  investigators  haven't already set up a return visit. She asks them to meet her at the bookshop a little after closing time (8:00 pm).

- If an investigator cast a basic Werelight while in the basement ( Cooking Up a Spell , page 296), they also know that whatever is down there probably isn't a poltergeist.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000784_636851448c4794f6fc12bae626eb41230657dfea184f530b52ab6dfa9bc916a1.png)

## Next Steps

All that is left for the investigators to do is gather anything they wish to take with them  for their ghost-hunting expedition, such as camping chairs and something to read or listen to, as well as provisions.

- If asked, Molly provides sandwiches-the GM should ask the players for a group Luck roll to see what the contents are (ask the player whose character has the lowest Luck score to make the roll, per Luck Rolls , page 113). If the roll is successful, some are cheese while the rest are ham; if the roll fails, they are all brawn and piccalilli.
- If the investigators accidentally destroyed the bookshop's CCTV camera ( Cooking Up a Spell , page 296), they may wish to purchase some surveillance equipment to set up a replacement. There's no need for the investigators to make a dice roll for this-they're in London, so someone somewhere will have what they need.

As 8:00 pm approaches, it's time for the investigators to head back to Covent Garden.

## GM ADVICE

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000785_209bee7fdc8438a937d98dc09d225e54357a8d3eac1d6b7d751df98b45f9595f.png)

Remember,  with  a  group  roll,  the  player  with  the lowest  characteristic  or  skill  value  is  the  one  who makes the skill roll, and its results affect everyone present. So, for a group Luck roll, the player with the lowest current Luck value is the one who makes the roll. And don't forget, you can't spend Luck points on a Luck roll!

## Return to Stonewaters

When the investigators return to New Row, they find Ms Jackson hovering by the boarded-up front door. If Ernie the dog is with them, she gives him a very suspicious look, but as long as the investigators assure her he's house-trained (he is, although he needs frequent bathroom breaks), she lets them in. After showing them how to activate the alarm system and lock up-in case they finish up before she comes back at 8:00 am the following morning-she leaves.

The GM should ask the investigators where they wish to set  up  watch,  and  if  they're  going  to  do  anything  specific while they wait for a manifestation. You might like to use the Player  Version  Map  of  the  Bookshop (page  369)  to help get everyone orientated in terms of where the various investigators position themselves.

- If the investigators make camp in the basement, go to Down Among the Books (page 301).
- If they decide to set up in the staffroom or in Ms Jackson's office, refer to Action at a Distance (following).
- If the investigators carry out a search of the building before settling in to rule out anyone besides themselves being on the premises, they should make an Observation roll. A success of any kind confirms that they are completely alone.

## GM ADVICE

It is possible the investigators might decide to split up, with  some  stationing  themselves  downstairs  while the rest hang around in the staff area watching the CCTV of the whole store. If they do, follow the advice in Splitting the Party (page 226), swapping between the two groups at an appropriate moment so that everyone remains engaged in the adventure.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000786_ffc1e9b77a2f23a456a79d4bac987c3a0aede591cff06fc4e7705504152af63c.png)

## Action at a Distance

What  the  investigators  see  if  they  decide  not  to  position themselves in the basement depends on where they are: The Manager's Office (following) or The Staffroom (page 301).

## The Manager's Office

If the investigators await the poltergeist's appearance in Ms Jackson's  office,  they  can  watch  the  CCTV  camera's  live feed-assuming the camera is still working, of course.

- Enterprising investigators who make a visit to the basement before settling in can alter the camera's angle to get a better view with a successful Tech skill roll.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000787_2fb71f0acc2fe4b4bd22000110be52b1c3c0f048142d9ceab6f9015ad2c401a4.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000788_766770044822671855c65362a89d932631bb170f3ad02e8f46ee5eb72c9a0df9.png)

- Similarly, investigators who know their way around a computer can reprogramme the camera's angle from Ms Jackson's computer with a successful Computer Use roll.
- If the investigators bought new surveillance equipment after destroying the bookshop's CCTV camera ( Cooking Up a Spell , page 296), a successful Tech roll is needed to install it correctly. If they lack the skill, they could try their Luck using either their DEX or INT, whichever is highest ( Trying Your Luck , page 114). A failure indicates they haven't located the equipment in the best position but will still see something, while a fumble indicates an intermittent signal that fails just before things get interesting.

All  is  quiet  until  around  11:30  pm.  Ernie,  if  present, suddenly starts barking furiously, having spent most of the evening  fast  asleep,  apart  from  his  occasional  bathroom breaks. Those watching the camera feed see dozens of books float  serenely  into  view  and  arrange themselves neatly into four equal piles on the floor.

- A successful Occult or History roll recalls a similar book stacking event that took place in the British Library in 1896, which was linked to poltergeist activity.

If  the  investigators  head  down to the basement, with or without Ernie, go to Kaboom!?! (page 302). If they decide to stay in the manager's office, 30 minutes later, another four piles of books appear in exactly the same manner. If that still doesn't tempt them to go to the basement area, after another 30 minutes-at around the same time Warwick Anderson was  attacked  the  night  before-all  the  remaining  books in  the  basement  leap  from  their  shelves  and  collide  in  the middle of the room-go to An Unholy Mess (page 304).

## The Staffroom

If  the  investigators  set  up  in  the  staffroom,  they  have  easy access to the tea and coffee making facilities-i.e., the kettle, tea  bags,  instant  coffee,  and  mugs,  plus  milk  in  the  staff fridge-as well as the microwave. There are also more chairs in here, though none quite so swish as Ms Jackson's personal office chair.

- If the investigators brought their own surveillance equipment, they could set things up so they can watch it from here with a successful Tech roll. See The Manager's Office (previous) for the consequences of a failed or fumbled roll.

History skill roll reminds the investigator of the 1896 British Library poltergeist/book stacking incident.

If Ernie is not with the investigators, and if they have not set up their own surveillance camera, then at 12:30 am, the GM should ask the players here to make an Observation roll. Those who achieve a Regular success or better hear a crash from the basement-the infant genius loci has lost its temper and started flinging books around to get their attention-go to An Unholy Mess (page 304).

## Down Among the Books

The basement is eerily quiet when the investigators re-enter it. There is no trace of the attack on Warwick Anderson, as all the undamaged books have been replaced on their shelves. If  the  investigators  undertake  another  IVA,  they  receive the  same information they did previously ( Conducting an Initial Vestigium Assessment (IVA) , page 294).

Unless  the  investigators  decide  to  cast  a  spell  ( Feeding Time ,  page  302),  the  hours  drag  on  with  no  sign  of  any poltergeist activity.  If  they  have  Ernie  with  them,  he needs  taking  out  for  a  bathroom  break  shortly  after  11:00 pm-once he is removed from the basement, things start to happen-go to The Yap Factor (page 302). Alternatively, if, at any point, everyone leaves the basement, the 'poltergeist' becomes active-go to Flight of the Folios , following.

## Flight of the Folios

The moment the last investigator leaves the basement, either by stepping off the staircase onto the ground floor or by heading through the door into the staff area, the GM should ask the investigators to make an Observation or  a Sense Vestigia skill roll. Which skill they use is up to the player, but they might want to compare their skill values to see which one is highest-the higher the value,  the  more  likely  they  are  to succeed. If both skills have the same value, the GM can ask what sort of information the investigator would like to learn: something  magic-related  (Sense  Vestigia)  or  something about what's happening around them (Observation)?

## Observation Roll

- If a Regular success, the investigator hears strange rustling noises coming from the basement.
- If a Hard success, the noises sound uncomfortably like books moving about under their own steam.

## Sense Vestigia Roll

At  11:30  pm,  Ernie,  if  present,  starts  barking  furiously, indicating that something is happening. If the investigators are watching their own camera feed, they see four piles of books gently form before their eyes. A successful Occult or

- If a Regular success, the investigator detects a sudden surge in vestigia -not the type they detected earlier, but rather the smell of blood, the taste of plasticine, and the sensation of crossed legs and chicken feathers. It must

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000789_ec03f5d78942465d4b6de61e38c7dacfa96aba4e939b470a7e8d5bfd6687ffc5.png)

8

be strong for the investigator to have detected it over the dampening effect of all the books.

- If a Hard success, the investigator not only detects the surge, but also realises that there is something powerful in the basement. It doesn't feel like a ghost, though-it feels like something… else …

How  rapidly  the  investigators  return  to  the  basement determines what they see next. If they're quick, they see books floating gently across the basement from the art section and adding  themselves  to  four  neat  piles  in  the  middle  of  the cookery section. If they take their time-for example, because they go to fetch their colleagues-they find the stacked books already in the cookery section upon their return.

As  described  in The  Manager's  Office (page  300),  a successful Occult or History roll  calls  to  mind  a  similar incident at the British Library in 1896. While it's not as if no human being would stack books like this, if the investigators have  searched  the  building,  they  know  there's  no  one  else here-never mind that they may have also watched the books float themselves into position!-go to Kaboom!?! (nearby).

## The Yap Factor

If Ernie was downstairs with the investigators, the moment he's  taken  out  for  his  11:00  pm  bathroom  break,  the  GM should ask everyone still in the basement for an Observation or Sense Vestigia skill roll:

- A successful Observation roll spots the books the moment they start floating out of the art section, although it doesn't take long for everyone else present to notice what's happening.
- Results for the Sense Vestigia roll are as previously described in Flight of the Folios (previous).

The  books  gently  wing  their  way  past  the  investigators and  form  themselves  into  four  neat  piles.  If  they  haven't already  made the connection, allow an Occult or History roll to recall what happened at the British Library in 1896. Interestingly,  the  books  show  no  signs  of  violence  towards any onlookers-go to Kaboom!?! (following).

## Kaboom!?!

What happens next depends on the investigators:

- If, having examined the four piles of books, the investigators decide to leave the basement again, the genius loci tries to attract their attention with another pile of books at midnight, and then explodes everything at 12:30 am-go to An Unholy Mess (page 304).
- If the investigators have Ernie with them, the Yorkshire Terrier begins yapping and snarling, as if his life depended on it, the moment he enters the basement. Startled by the angry furball, the genius loci panics and throws everything at the investigators-go to Ouch! (page 303).

## Feeding Time

If the investigators haven't yet cast a spell in the basement, now is the time to do so. If Anderson's attacker is a genuine ghost of some sort, then those who know the Werelight spell also know they should be able to use it to coax the ghost out.

As described in Cooking Up a Spell (page 296), a successful Magic roll produces a gently glowing, milky white werelight if the spell is unboosted (i.e., the investigator only spends 1 magic point to cast the spell). The colour rapidly changes to a dark crimson and the light begins to fade, suggesting something is feeding on the magical energy-but nothing appears. There's obviously something down here, but it isn't a ghost.

If the investigator has mastered Werelight, they can spend an  additional  magic  point  to  keep  their  existing  werelight going.  Alternatively,  to  immediately  boost  the  spell,  they could spend 2 magic points when making their Magic roll. A  boosted  werelight  flares  bright  white  and  then  rapidly shrinks down to a tiny, sapphire-blue star before winking out completely. As it does so, the caster has the distinct impression of blood, plasticine, crossed legs, and chicken feathers.

- As before ( Cooking Up a Spell , page 296), if an investigator fumbles or fails a pushed roll when casting Werelight, or if more than one investigator successfully casts Werelight in the basement, the effect is the same as a successfully boosted werelight.
- Casting any other spell besides Werelight fails to produce such an explosive effect-as long as the investigator doesn't fumble their Magic roll or fail a pushed casting roll. At night, not only does this sand the electronics in the basement, but it also provokes the genius loci into action.

Any  other  investigators  present  should  make  a Sense Vestigia or Observation skill roll:

- A successful Sense Vestigia roll identifies a strange presence-a powerful one, but not a ghost; even with the books' dampening effect, the investigator senses its vestigia very clearly: the smell of blood, the taste of plasticine, and the sensation of crossed legs and chicken feathers.
- A successful Observation roll spots that books all over the basement are rattling ominously on their shelves…
- If the investigators decide to cast Werelight to tempt out the 'poltergeist,' go to Feeding Time (following).

If everything has exploded, or the investigators attempt to leave the basement, go to Ouch! (following).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000790_8476eddf7492eb2cccbb0552bc73255ea450505fdf381cf7ede826251bc868ef.png)

## Ouch!

Suddenly, all the books in the basement take flight. The GM should ask everyone present to make an Athletics or DEX roll-again,  the  player  should  choose  whichever  has  the highest value to improve their chances of success. Those who succeeded  with  their  Observation  roll  (per Feeding Time , previous) gain a bonus die to this roll. As always, players can spend Luck points to turn a failure into a success; however, they cannot buy off a fumble ( Spending Luck , page 113).

If the roll is successful, the investigator manages to dive behind some form of cover, e.g., the till point desk, shielding them from the influx of airborne  publications.  They  suffer no  damage.  If  the  roll  is  a  failure,  the  investigator  is  hit by  a  barrage  of  books.  The  GM  should  ask  the  player  to make a Luck roll:

- If the Luck roll is a success , the investigator only gets hit with light paperbacks and suffers 1 damage; as long as they've taken no other damage during this case file, they are now Hurt (page 144), and the player should tick the relevant box on their character sheet.
- If the result is a failure , the investigator is hit by the notorious Taschen hardbacks for 2 damage. As long as they've taken no other damage during this case file, they
- If the result is a fumble , the investigator not only gets hit by Taschen hardbacks, they also bang their head when they fall, taking 3 damage and temporarily knocking themselves out. They are now Down (page 144); the player should tick the Hurt, Bloodied, and Down boxes on their character sheet to remind them; they are also impaired (see previous bullet point).
- are now Bloodied (page 144), and the player should tick both the Hurt and Bloodied boxes on their character sheet. The investigator is now also impaired, so their player should tick the Impaired box, too. Being impaired means that, until they recover, the investigator now fumbles if they roll 90 or more on their percentile dice.
- Any investigator who has the Natural Toughness advantage (page 51) deducts 1 damage from any suffered due to their inherent hardiness. This means they take either no damage (with a Luck roll success), 1 damage (Luck roll failure), or 2 damage (Luck roll fumble). The same is true if someone is wearing a Met stab vest ( Armour , page 135).

If Ernie is present, he automatically runs for cover behind the  till  point  desk,  thus  nimbly  avoiding  getting  hit  by anything. Go to An Unholy Mess (following).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000791_251804eff94c49e37f4fda293e9359a0c40632e8e2deeed53289437c0b03fde5.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000792_9561a48350dd92e7edd918761793dfa17bf11cbec97929725d5463c1a726b707.png)

## An Unholy Mess

After  the  dust  has  settled,  the  investigators  can  extricate themselves from the scene of literary devastation.

- If an investigator was hurt (incurred a total of 1 damage), they immediately recover from their injuries and can erase the mark in the Hurt box on their character sheet ( After the Fight: Hurt , page 145).
- If an investigator was down (incurred a total of 3 damage), they come to, feeling distinctly the worse for wear. Their player should erase the marks in the Down and Bloodied boxes on their character sheet, but the investigator remains Hurt until the end of the following day, unless they spend 10 Luck points to affect a rapid recovery. They are also impaired until the end of the following day, unless they spend a further 10 Luck points ( After the Fight: Down , page 145).
- If an investigator was bloodied (incurred a total of 2 damage), they also immediately recover from their injuries. They can erase the marks in the Hurt, Bloodied, and Impaired boxes on their character sheet ( After the Fight: Bloodied , page 145).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000793_1110fc815ff1032ccfdd86042589054f7ef5ede07e6f899c60e0f8d9120973fb.png)

## GM ADVICE

Why have the players mark the various boxes on their character sheets if they're going to erase them so soon after their investigators were injured? Because it gets the players into the habit of recording damage, so they know what to do the next time it happens.

The  basement  isn't  the  only  site  of  de-shelving.  Any investigator  who  heads  up  onto  the  ground  floor  to  check for damage elsewhere discovers a trail of books leading from the children's department to the stairwell. All of them are brightly coloured story books for toddlers and early readers. Nothing else on the ground floor has moved a centimetre.

## Enlightenment

If  the  players  haven't  already  figured  out  what's  happening, the GM should ask them to make an INT roll. If any of the investigators have children, they gain a bonus die to the roll, as they subconsciously recognise the genius loci's attention-seeking behaviour. As always, players can spend Luck or push the roll.

- If the roll is a failure , the investigator realises that the triggering event was the children's section being moved, but is none the wiser about what's in the basement.
- child and appears to be confined to the basement-or else it would have relocated upstairs along with the children's section. The best it could manage was an attempt to drag the children's books down to it.
- If no one succeeds at the INT roll, it's time to call DCI Nightingale for some advice. He asks them to think about what other members of the demi-monde might be powerful enough to cause such an effect. If they're still unsure, he tells them his suspicions-that they have a newly-formed genius loci on their hands.

## Conclusion

The case file is now almost over. The investigators have solved the mystery of what's in the basement one way or another, but they must still find a solution to the problem. After all, they can't leave the genius loci to attack anyone else, even if it is only doing so because it's lonely. They need to come up with a plan.

## A Book at Bedtime

The GM should encourage the players to discuss how they're going to deal with the genius loci .  If  they're stuck, the GM should suggest the investigators ring DCI Nightingale, who prompts  them-via  the  GM-to  think  about  what  might make the genius loci feel less neglected. Alternatively, if they're really stuck, the GM can ask for another INT roll  to  help them come up with a solution. Here are some suggestions for how the investigators can solve the problem:

- Perhaps someone could read stories to the genius loci once a week after the shop closes-see Reading Stories Once a Week , page 305.
- In addition, that's one heck of a mess that needs clearing up in the basement. Perhaps the investigators could get the genius loci to put the books back in return for a story? See Quid Pro Quo , following.
- If the roll is a Regular success, the investigator realises that the vestigia from the cockfighting ring has transformed into a genius loci , not a ghost. That transformation seems to be linked to the children's section, and its relocation was the trigger for the attack on Warwick Anderson. It's as if the genius loci is missing its Saturday Story Time sessions.
- Or, the investigators could devise some way of communicating with the genius loci themselves-e.g., using letter flashcards (on sale in the children's section), and so on. Communicating in this manner reveals that the little spirit is lonely and just wants someone to read to it, like they did before the children stopped coming to the basement and the Saturday Story Time sessions held there.
- Perhaps the investigators could ask Ms Jackson to play a children's audiobook in the basement at least once a week after the shop closes, especially on nights when there are staff working down there-see Audiobooks , page 305.
- Alternatively, if the investigators know any genii locorum or lower fae, they may wish to ask them to talk to the spirit in the basement to see what it wants.
- If the roll is a Hard success, the investigator also realises that the genius loci is behaving like a small, abandoned

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000794_3288fab7e0393ca4d2a56f9eea2e3d061f1fa35020d04b5dc1bc7942381dda54.png)

## GM ADVICE

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000795_4a923d3b752efd15754dc4f637811ab75d6ba33b38b7f8510ac6a48d823d6af8.png)

These  solutions  are  based  on  the  ones  in  'The Cockpit'  and  what  happened  in  various  playtest sessions. Of course, your players may come up with a completely different solution, and that's fine! Once they've described their solution to you, you'll need to judge whether it will work-or make the problem worse!  This  might  involve  the  investigators  making a  dice  roll  or  you  can  simply  judge  that  their  plan succeeds automatically, depending on what it is. The key  thing  is  not  to  force  the  ending  to  be  like  the short story on which this adventure is based-reward the  players  for  coming  up  with  their  own  solution, especially if it's really clever and inventive ( Player

Agency , page 221).

## Quid Pro Quo

The genius  loci can,  indeed,  be  encouraged  to  tidy  up  after itself in return for a story. It takes a couple of hours to put everything back where it was, so it expects a nice long story afterwards as a reward. The GM should ask for a Luck roll from whoever reads to the genius loci :

- If the roll is a success , the story is exactly the right length, and the contented genius loci causes no more trouble for a week. But, if no other arrangement is put in place to make sure it is told a story once each week, it starts throwing books at staff members again until it gets another bedtime story.
- long as the investigator complies, all is well until next week (see previous bullet point).
- If the roll is a fumble , the genius loci didn't like that story and starts throwing things around again. The investigators need to choose another book, and quickly!

The GM might also wish to call for an Observation roll to identify any books that are too badly damaged to be reshelved. The investigators can either put those to one side themselves or make a note of what needs replacing and let Ms Jackson know in the morning.

## Reading Stories Once a Week

Someone reading the genius  loci a  bedtime  story  in  person once a week-preferably on Saturdays-prevents any further attention-seeking behaviour. It doesn't matter what's read to it, as long as it's a children's book. It also doesn't matter who reads to it. If the investigators don't think of it themselves, DCI  Nightingale  adds  the  duty  to  the  Folly's  action  list so  they  can  keep  an  eye  on  the genius  loci's development; meaning that, at least once a fortnight, a junior Folly member is expected to pop in to read the bedtime story.

## Audiobooks

Playing audiobooks to the genius loci also works but needs to be done more frequently than an in-person reading. By trial and error, the investigators can determine that twice a week is enough to keep the young spirit content. They may also want to recommend that a story should be played when someone is working in the basement alone, just to be on the safe side!

- If the roll is a failure , the story wasn't long enough, and the genius loci turns the pages of the story book back to the beginning, hinting that it wants to hear it again. As

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000796_752c5a99dee274fc69a0599bfafd8ad252c89424daaa28947c32fe18dcf7492d.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000797_f21cf2e3d9bcfef2bcd2f676a994015899ba9a9fbec8921a44ee8cdc9bb8b8b2.png)

## Loose Ends

Hopefully, the only thing the investigators have left to do is  let  Ms  Jackson  know  what's  needed  to  keep  the  peace! Although she is somewhat bemused by the discovery that she has a 'spirit of books and reading' in her basement, she is content to take whatever advice the investigators give her, if it means no one else gets hurt.

As  for  Warwick  Anderson,  he  gets  a  job  in  another independent bookshop on the other side of the river. Nothing the investigators or Ms Jackson can say or do convinces him to set foot in Stonewaters ever again!

## Rewards

Once all the loose ends have been tied up, it's time for an Investigator Development Phase (page 120).

- First, award development points to the investigators. If you are using the standard system ( Gaining Development Points , page 120), that's 1 development point per session of play.
- Next, the investigators can spend their development point(s) to improve skills, learn a new spell, or master an existing spell. Alternatively, they may decide to save their development point(s) until they have enough for a new advantage ( Spending Development Points , page 120).
- Finally, each player reviews their investigator's employment status and affluence rating. Unless they somehow provoked the genius loci into totally destroying the bookshop and were suspended as a result, there should be no need to change either of these ( Reviewing Employment and Affluence Rating , page 122).
- Now, the players review their investigator's backstory to see if there are any elements that can be altered or added, with the GM's permission ( Altering an Investigator's Backstory , page 122).
- Optional: if the GM intends for there to be an in-game break between the end of this case file and the beginning of the next, they may wish to award 1 additional development point for training.

Congratulations! Your first RoL:RPG case file is now complete!

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000798_7fa6d923f793fc7d45ebe7ac96b09d62b7ad6aed9ed94148c2afe5175bf21dbb.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000799_8e1463dd273cffe4ceaf057d8cb400d55ce44227a77030d612e99d021af94ff9.png)

## Cast

The  following  section  contains  the  profiles  for  the  various NPCs the investigators are likely to run across during this case file. Common skills are listed first, followed by expert, and then combat skills. None of the NPCs in this case file besides DCI Nightingale are practitioners, so they don't have any spells listed. DCI Nightingale's profile can be found in A Rogues' Gallery (page 233).

SCIENTIA POTESTAS EST

## Additional Rules

ALL THE STANDARD rules you need to play Rivers of London: the Roleplaying Game are contained in Chapters 1, 2, 3, 4, and 6. This chapter builds on those rules to provide some different or expanded options regarding, among other things, character creation, including creating your own investigator organisations  and  playing  non-human  investigators.  There are optional tweaks to the basic rules for combat and damage, as  well  as  some  more  information  on  magic  for  the  Game Moderator (GM).

If you're new to roleplaying games or you're happy to stick with the standard rules you've read so far, that's absolutely fine. If, on the other hand, you want to add some extra nuts and bolts to your game, feel free to pick and choose from the following sections or adopt them wholesale-it's entirely up to you!

## Advanced Character Creation

Chapter 1 took you through standard character creation. This section has new tweaks and suggestions for trying something a  little  different,  starting  with  an  alternative  method  for determining an investigator's characteristics.

## Assigning Characteristics Revisited

In Step  Two:  Assign  Characteristics (page  46),  a  player shares 280 points among their investigator's characteristics and,  for  simplicity,  we  advised  allocating  the  points  in blocks of 10. The advantage being that it keeps the maths simple,  and  when  it  comes  to Half Characteristic Values (page  49),  there's  no  rounding  required.  If  you  want  more granularity  when  assigning  characteristics,  simply  let  the players allocate those 280 points however they wish between their  investigator's  five  characteristics.  If  any  rounding  is required  when  calculating  half  characteristic  values,  the player should round down, e.g., STR 57 rounds down to a half characteristic value of 28.

## Age Revisited

In  the  standard  rules,  a  player  chooses  how  old  their investigator is, with any implications arising from their age simply factored in through storytelling. For example, an old character may be described as looking or behaving older than their younger companions-grey hair, wrinkles, making outof-date cultural references, etc.-but aside from that, older characters are treated in the rules exactly the same as younger ones. If you wish to make age a more significant factor in play, you can incorporate the following changes.

## Age and Movement Rate (MOV)

Age tends to slow people down, so as characters reach their 40s and beyond, their movement rate is reduced as followsthis can be applied to non-player characters (NPCs) as well:

- A character in their 40s: deduct -1 from MOV.
- A character in their 60s: deduct -3 from MOV.
- A character in their 50s: deduct -2 from MOV.
- A character in their 70s: deduct -4 from MOV.
- A character in their 80s: deduct -5 from MOV.

The  average  competitor  at  the  Olympics  is  in  their  20s. Of  course,  there  are  some  individuals  in  their  70s  that can  outrun  some  20-year-olds,  but,  as  a  rule,  most  people slow down with age. The modifiers above are intended for mundane  human  characters.  Those  who  are  touched  by magic may not be subject to the same weathering effects of old age. Certainly Nightingale and Varvara are pretty spritely given how old they both are!

## Investigator Experience Packages

The standard characteristic value range is 30 to 80, so you could also extend this to 20 to 90, if you wish to have more exceptional characters in the game.

Players wishing to start the game with a more experienced investigator can do so, with the GM's permission. However,  in  the  interest  of  game  balance,  the  additional benefits gained come at the cost of a disadvantage ( Disadvantages , page 311).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000802_ac2c84ea0d0b26420ad17898c2fe136b0a12b8ee6bca4c869c21fdd1b857d8b8.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000803_1460d4b59cb80d3f7450fec1074ef1a9a557fa97ca4947e9f4dbe784afcf2f29.png)

## Academic Experience Package

The investigator has worked in academia for many years and has published multiple papers in reputable journals, as well as contributing chapters to reference books on their subjectperhaps they've even written a book of their own!

- Create the investigator using the Lecturer occupation (page 69).
- May not raise any skill above 80% by this method.
- Gain an additional 60 points to spend as desired on required and recommended skills.
- Must choose one disadvantage ( Disadvantages , page 311).

## Criminal Experience Package

The  investigator  has  spent  most,  if  not  all,  of  their  life involved in crime of one sort or another. They served time in prison but have turned their life around and gone straight. The  investigator  could  now  give  talks  to  young  people  to share their experience with the aim of helping others to avoid following in their footsteps.

- Create the investigator using the Criminal occupation (page 65).
- May not raise any skill above 80% by this method.
- Gain an additional 60 points to spend as desired on required and recommended skills.
- Must choose one disadvantage ( Disadvantages , page 311).

## GM ADVICE

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000804_cb6362de45d3bbf4ecc86274229b67fabb398021758ffb64e74cc9284551848f.png)

As with the standard Criminal occupation (page 65), the  GM  should  think  carefully  about  permitting  a player to choose this package, especially if the rest of the investigator group are police officers or law enforcement agents.

## Ex-Military Experience Package

The investigator served in one of the Armed Forces during a full-scale military conflict; exactly which conflict depends on the character's age. While benefitting from their experience, they also bear its scars.

- Create the investigator using the Service Member occupation (page 73).
- May not raise any skill above 80% by this method.
- Gain an additional 60 points to spend as desired on required and recommended skills.
- Must choose one disadvantage ( Disadvantages , page 311).

## Experienced Investigator Package

This  investigator's  been  around  the  block  a  few  times,  and while they may not have seen it all, they've certainly seen a lot over the years. As a result, they start the game with more skills than their less-experienced colleagues.

- Create the investigator using any of the occupations listed on page 45.
- May also choose an additional new skill at 60% or distribute the 60 points among any existing skills.
- Gain an additional 60 points to spend on skills.
- May not raise any skill above 80% by this method.
- Must choose one disadvantage ( Disadvantages , page 311).

## Paranormal Experience Package

The  investigator  has  knowledge  of  the  paranormal-more specifically, the demi-monde-either in an academic sense or through tangible experience. The player should discuss with the GM how their investigator is aware of the demi-monde, e.g.,  through  reading  old  books  or  through  actual  contact, and write this into their investigator's backstory.

- Create the investigator using the Parapsychologist occupation (page 70; or another occupation with the GM's consent).
- May not raise any skill above 80% by this method.
- Gain an additional 60 points to spend as desired on required and recommended skills.
- Must choose one disadvantage ( Disadvantages , page 311).

## Police Experience Package

The  investigator  has  served  for  a  number  of  years  on  a police force. Alternatively, they may have retired from this profession, perhaps quite recently.

- Create the investigator using the Police Officer/ Detective occupation (page 71).
- May not raise any skill above 80% by this method.
- Gain an additional 60 points to spend as desired on required and recommended skills.
- Must choose one disadvantage ( Disadvantages , page 311).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000805_d6b792aaf02ae26c6d4070f6a35da7beae17a7f661081d4e3c817c76e671128d.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000806_4c732678536179517dfb7115c1513dd36ab22741741016a0ee7e59a29977bfac.png)

## Spellcaster Experience Package

The investigator just has the knack when it comes to learning spells, or they've been using magic (quite possibly in secret) for some time now. As a result, your investigator begins the game with a higher Magic skill  and  additional  spells.  An investigator taking this package must first have chosen the Magical advantage (page 51).

- Create the practitioner investigator using any of the occupations listed on page 45.
- Have three first order spells (one mastered, two unmastered).
- Magic skill starts at 70%.
- Have two second order spells (one mastered, one unmastered).
- Must choose one disadvantage ( Disadvantages , following).

## Disadvantages

A disadvantage is something notable about an investigator that  may  cause  them  problems.  Players  who  create  their investigators using one of the investigator experience packages described in the previous section must choose one of the following disadvantages.

Some  disadvantages  have  limits  associated  with  them, which  are  akin  to  an  advantage's  prerequisites.  They  may relate to a characteristic or skill value, but may also specify which advantages they cannot be taken with, i.e., a player may  not  choose  a  disadvantage  for  their  investigator  that cancels out the effects of any previously selected advantages.

## GM ADVICE

You  may  also  wish  to  use  disadvantages  with  your NPCs.  Like  investigators,  they  should  incur  only one and are subject to the same limits.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000807_f96d98e150905bdb7774ea8b998c0f2ee0381fc5a7d442dabeb307278d6c4b04.png)

## Blunt

Limits: none.

This character wouldn't know tact and subtlety if it walked up and slapped them in the face with a wet fish. As a result, they incur a penalty die on all Social skill rolls.

## Crossed the Wrong People

Limits: none.

## Easily Distracted

Limits: none.

This character's mind is always on other things. They could daydream  or  have  a  preoccupation  with  some  theory  or other-either way, they often fail to see what's in  front  of their face. They incur a penalty die on Observation and Read Person rolls.

## Damaged

Limits: may not be taken with the Steadfast advantage (page 52).

This character has been deeply affected by the things they've witnessed in the course of their life and/or career. As a result, they are more likely to fumble a roll, meaning that any roll of 95% or above is always a fumble (rather than 100%, see Fumble ,  page  107).  When  they  suffer  impairment  from mental trauma ( Suffering Impairment ,  page 118) or when in combat ( Wounds and Healing ,  page 144), their fumble range increases to 90%, but recovers back to 95% rather than 100% ( Impairment and Recovery , page 119).

## Disorganised

Limits: none.

This  character  can  never  find  what  they're  looking  for,  or remember  where  they've  put  things.  They  incur  a  penalty die on Luck rolls to find useful items, i.e., to see if they've remembered to pack the item in question or to find what they need at their current location.

## Fingers and Thumbs

Limits: none.

This character lacks fine motor skills. As a result, they incur a penalty die on any DEX rolls involving manual dexterity, e.g.,  cutting  things  in  the  right  place.  They  also  incur  a penalty die to damage rolls when using firearms and ranged weapons, such as bows. DEX rolls involving speed, such as when determining who initiates combat ( Who Goes First? , page 124) or when putting on a Burst of Speed (page 151), are not affected.

## Frail

Limits: may  not  be  taken  with  the Natural  Toughness advantage (page 51).

This character has done something to get themselves in some powerful people's bad books. The GM should pick who those powerful people are, and ensure that they feature in play. If the  adversary  is  eliminated,  the  GM  should  contrive  their replacement-perhaps a relative looking for vengeance.

This character lacks physical resilience. They do not possess the Hurt damage  level  (page  144).  If  an  investigator  with this disadvantage incurs 1 damage, their player ignores the Hurt box on their  character  sheet  and  immediately  marks

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000808_dbe3cc99d5f836ca6e56a356d6f18e74440faf9e4194e9faea1b1c918a0e696b.png)

9

the  investigator  as  Bloodied;  if  they  incur  2  damage,  the player marks both the Bloodied and Down boxes ( Wounds and  Healing ,  page  144).  For  ease,  we  recommend  the player puts a permanent line through the Hurt box on their character sheet.

## Hot-Headed

Limits: none.

This  character  never  knows  when  to  back  down  in  a  fight. They  cannot  choose  the Flee option  (page  130)  when engaged in combat, and their Involuntary Action (page 108) in response to a fumble or failed impairment POW roll (page 118) always involves violence.
