## Iron-Blooded

Limits: cannot be taken by fae investigators.

There  is  something  undefinable  about  this  character  that certain  fae  beingsgenii  locorum ,  unicorns,  and  the  High Fae-detest  ( The  Effects  of  Cold  Iron ,  page  245).  This character cannot forge friendships with the aforementioned fae and has real difficulty striking a deal with them. They also incur a penalty die on all Social skill rolls when dealing with anyone who is vulnerable to cold iron.

## Magic is Exhausting

Limits: none.

This character has to put extra effort into casting spells. The base cost of every spell they know is increased by 1 magic point. For example, as a second order spell, the base cost for Fireball is 2 magic points; for someone with this disadvantage, the base cost would be 3 magic points. The cost for boosting a mastered spell is unaltered.

## Not a Magical Bone

Limits: may not be taken with the Magical advantage (page 51) or by fae investigators (who are inherently magical; No Newtonian Magic , page 318).

No matter how hard this character tries, they cannot learn or use magic. The forms and wisdoms-or whatever method a particular magical tradition uses-are just too slippery to take  root  in  the  character's  mind.  They  are,  however,  still susceptible to the effects of spells and spell-like effects used on or against them.

## Slow-Footed

Limits: may  not  be  taken  with  the Speedy advantage  (page  52).

This character is slow on their feet and starts with a movement rate of 5 (MOV 5) before making any adjustments for age.

## Slow Healer

Limits: none.

This character recovers from illness and injury more slowly than the average person. They may not spend Luck points to  avoid  the  after-effects  of  being  down  ( After the Fight: Down , page 145) or when recovering from a Mortal Wound (page 146). However, they may still spend Luck to turn a fatal blow into a mortal wound ( Wounds and Healing , page 144).

## Weak

Limits: maximum STR 40.

This character lacks physical strength. As a result, they incur a  penalty  die  on  all  STR  rolls,  including  when  making  a damage roll in melee combat ( The Damage Roll , page 132).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000809_20e5eb6777914b7cbc0ab1da2e51f4f41ae7977ba314c5c552895376c389a43d.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000810_24256286e0070038fcc8b808190213574d26b48e7bd10f9d70cea4ff74620ff9.png)

## Creating New Occupations

There are a huge variety of occupations in the world, and the list in the Creating Characters chapter (page 45) is designed to cover some of the most likely options. If a player wishes to choose an occupation for their investigator that isn't listed, take a look to see if there's an existing one that shares enough similarities that it can be used pretty much as is. Ideally, this should  only  involve  a  minimum  amount  of  tweaking,  e.g., changing  the  required  skill  or  altering  the  recommended skill list by one or two skills.

If  an  occupation  requires  more  alteration  than  this,  it's better  to  create  a  new  occupation,  which  is  reasonably straightforward to do. Consider the skills someone with this occupation would know: which ones are essential (required skills) for that occupation, and which are preferred but not must-haves (recommended skills)? As you can see from the list  of Occupations starting  on  page  62,  most  professions have  only  one  or  two  required  skills;  three  at  the  most. Next,  list  a  few  suggested  contacts-people  that  someone in this occupation is likely to know or be able to influence. Then,  think  about  the  equipment  that  occupation  might have as standard.

That's  it-any  further  elements  of  what  they  do  is  storybased  rather  than  rules-based.  Story  is  the  important  bit, though, so don't forget to give some thought to how someone with  this  occupation  is  going  to  integrate  with  the  other investigators and the game in general.

Example: David wants to play an antiques dealer. He and the GM decide that none of the existing occupations really work for that.  They  think  about  the  skills  someone  dealing  in  antiques would absolutely need to be able to carry out their job, and decide on  Appraise  and  an  Art/Craft  specialisation  of  some  sort.  They then think about what other skills would be useful, and come up with  a  short  list,  containing  History  (useful  for  dating  items), Languages  (for  reading  old  books  and  dealing  with  overseas clients),  Observation  (for  spotting  damage  and  fakes),  Research (for checking the provenance of objects), and Social (for negotiating good  prices  when  buying  and  selling).  All  they  need  now  are  a short list of potential contacts and some starting equipment, and the occupation is done! See the Antiques Dealer box (nearby) for the finished occupation.

## Troupe Play

One  option  that  may  appeal  to  some  groups  is  for  each player to control more than one investigator at any one time. The main advantage being that it allows for a wider range of  occupations  and  skills  for  tackling  case  files  (scenarios), especially if playing the game with only one or two players.

## ANTIQUES DEALER

Antiques dealers usually have their own shop, retail items out of their homes, or go  on  extended  buying  trips,  making  a profit on reselling to urban stores.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000811_fcd3cdcf26186af6cbd3bbdd405c0b4cec23448fe11d950ab146371f50534ee3.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000812_202de850856329033b7f7f845e102d158f1f43054f06c9ad674dda75103776cc.png)

Suggested contacts: booksellers, antique collectors, historians and historical societies, possibly criminal fences. Required skills: Appraise, Art/Craft (any). Recommended skills: History, Languages, Observation, Research, Social. Equipment: auction catalogues, jeweller's loupe, notebook, business cards.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000813_3006115c7b808f263b55025aa4fd2f22aef9abcae890d59ea4948df117012400.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000814_366751c0f51636ddef714b0b3bff6a86270c89db12b479beca9aacb25e05cb42.png)

Ordinarily,  two  investigators  might  not  have  all  the necessary skills between them to uncover all the clues in a case file, and there's a limit to how many new ones they can acquire per game session through Trying Your Luck (page 114).  This  might  mean  the  investigators  have  to  rely  more heavily on NPCs to provide them with the information or expertise they need. However, whenever possible, it's always more  fun  for  the  players  to  find  out  those  sorts  of  details themselves, so having multiple investigators in play means that  the  players-and  their  investigators-are  always  at the heart of the action. One way you can achieve this is by having Primary Investigators (following)  and Secondary Investigators (page 314).

## Primary Investigators

Each  player  creates  a  primary  investigator  that  they  play for the majority of the game, using as many-or as few-of the advanced character creation rules as they wish. Primary investigators have the opportunity to appear in most scenes and are the main focus of the game for their player.

If  it  helps,  think  of  a  primary  investigator  as  one  of  the main  characters  in  a  TV  show-they'll  appear  in  most  of an episode's scenes and almost every episode of a series, but may occasionally be off elsewhere for some reason. There are only a handful of TV shows where the main characters are in absolutely every scene of every episode, and every episode of  every  series,  so  there's  no  reason  the  group's  primary investigators have to be.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000815_1fb54fccb3291d483fa16b067d1adc3746c3f62d708974dd286fbb9a6961fda3.png)

9

## Secondary Investigators

Once  the  players  have  created  their  primary  investigators, it's  time  to  think  about  their  secondary  ones.  If  we  stick with the previous analogy of a TV show, think of them as supporting members of the cast or as recurring guest stars. Both pop in from time to time to help in solving the mystery or to develop the story, but they're not the show's-or this game's-main focus.

There  are  a  number  of  different  options  for  who  the secondary investigators are, and the GM  should  use whichever  type  they  think  is  most  appropriate  for  their current game. Of course, the type used may change after a long running case file is completed, and that's all good.

Secondary investigators can be created by the players, or the  GM  can  create  them  for  the  players  based  on  what's needed for the case file in which they'll be used-they can even be a group effort! Consider which one of the following options appeals to your group (each type is described in the following sections):

- Alternates: a second investigator for occasional use.
- Burners: for those who want to live dangerously!
- Significant others: playing friends and relatives.
- Pool: a stack of characters from which anyone can pick.

Two  of  these  types-alternate  and  pool  investigatorswork well when a player's primary investigator is absent from the scene, perhaps because the group has split up and some people have gone off to investigate a separate plot thread in a different location. It means that all the players are present for a particular scene, even if their primary investigators aren't. The only real difference between them is that pool investigators are shared by the whole group rather than each one belonging to a particular player, which can give all the players more freedom to pick the most appropriate person for the job or scene.

## Secondary Investigators: Alternates

Each player  creates  their  additional  character  in  the  usual way,  but  the  alternate  character  should  contrast  with  the player's main investigator in some way. For example, if the

## GM ADVICE

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000816_20136f04b5161dc9bed710f531cb48c29e1b8f307f53db3a9cc0f106f773ec88.png)

Secondary  investigators  come  in  handy  when  a particular  area  of  expertise  is  required  that  the primary investigators don't possess (and don't wish to try their Luck to obtain). For this reason, sometimes a secondary investigator is called in while a player's primary  investigator  is  still  present.  However,  it's preferable that each player only portrays one character in any given scene, so if a player's primary investigator is there, they should stay in the background until their secondary investigator leaves to avoid confusing matters.

primary investigator is a police officer and Folly apprentice, the  player's  alternate  might  be  a  paramedic  or  a  lecturer with  no  magical  ability  whatsoever.  The  player  plays  their primary  investigator  most  of  the  time,  but  occasionally switches to playing their secondary investigator when it feels appropriate, such as when their primary investigator is busy elsewhere-or in hospital recovering from their injuries-or when the GM frames a scene that strongly lends itself to the alternate  investigator's  inclusion,  i.e.,  when  their  specialist expertise is required.

## Secondary Investigator: Significant Others

Each player creates a secondary character who is important in  the  life  of  another  player's  primary  investigator.  For example, if someone was playing Peter Grant, another player could  create  Peter's  mum  as  their  secondary  investigator, while  someone  else  could  create  his  dad.  This  enables  the story to spread to domestic scenes outside of the main story. Such scenes can add a lot to the game and can help to bring the  primary  investigators  to  life.  As  we  see  in  the  novels, the  relationship  between  Peter  and  his  mother  shows  us another side to his character. As always, a player's secondary investigator should contrast with their primary one.

It's not expected or necessary for these secondary characters to become involved in the case file being played. The interaction with these significant others could be entirely secondary  to  your  game's  main  story,  but  it's  also  quite possible the two may overlap in some way. That's certainly something we see with Peter's father as we learn more of his history, and he certainly sets Peter on the right track on at least one occasion, thanks to his expert knowledge 1 .

You may be surprised how easily players get into the role of being one of their fellow investigator's significant others, and take  delight  in  creating  all  manner  of  interesting  dilemmas and  situations.  However,  it's  important  to  remember  that  as entertaining as developing the investigators' personal backstories may be, it's not the main focus of a Rivers of London game.

Another  important  point  to  keep  in  mind  is  that  not everyone may be comfortable with this type of in-character relationship,  i.e.,  some  players  may  be  keen  to  explore them,  whereas  other  players  may  not  be  interested  (for whatever  reason).  Before  choosing  the  option  to  create secondary investigators who are significant others, everyone's willingness to participate should be discussed and boundaries set  to  ensure  everyone's  continued  safety  and  enjoyment of the game. Players should always be able to change their minds about having someone else in the group play one of their  primary investigator's significant others, for whatever reason,  no  questions  asked.  The  same  goes  for  anyone playing  a  significant  other  as  their  secondary  investigator ( Considerate Gaming , page 44).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000817_ecdeeae8baf41c2308bd496e12c82c8a16d8b04d7f9287c26db01d55c4c60cfa.png)

Footnote:

1 Moon Over Soho

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000818_984571d25c3ab6853bb596c36dbaba19b837288870e52342af31105dfc85ba7e.png)

## Out - of - Character vs In - Character Knowledge

Sometimes,  an  issue  in  roleplaying  games is  how  to  handle  knowledge  that  you-the player-knows,  but  which  your  investigator doesn't. This can happen when playing more than  one  investigator  during  a  case  file.  For example, your primary investigator might learn some vital clue in one scene. Then, you switch to  playing  your  alternate  investigator  in  the next scene. As your primary character is not present in the new scene-you, as the player, know the vital clue, but the character you are now playing does not. In-character knowledge can be handled in a number of ways.

- You don't let what your primary investigator knows influence how your secondary investigator behaves (and vice versa). This means the secondary investigator has no idea about the important clue discovered by the primary character, and so makes no attempt to act on the information. It isn't always easy to do, but playing through your investigator's ignorance can be good fun, like when watching a film-we as viewers sometimes know things the characters do not, and this creates a tension between us (the viewer) and the film, so we sit there yelling at the screen, 'No, don't turn your back on her, she's the murderer!' In short, each character you play only knows what they know-they don't share a collective knowledge.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000819_32bd03f30322dc5a2ab77d36e75e7337f005fdfeb008f56bf2953602af25a2ae.png)

## Secondary Investigators: Burners

This is a very different type of secondary investigator. We're talking  'burners'  as  in  burner  phones  here-something that's expendable and used for only a short time. The primary investigators are meant to be like Peter Grant-intended to live through multiple stories. There's always the risk of death, but on the whole, they should be relatively durable and able to experience a number of case files.

To provide a contrast, allow the players to each create a secondary investigator designed with the intention of pushing them into the frontline of danger. The GM should provide guidance on what types of characters would be a good fit in the particular case file in which they'll be taking part.

- You can have your investigator act on the secret knowledge, but only in a manner that subtly changes their actions, as though they were acting on a hunch or as if fate was steering them in the right direction. Thus, they sort of know information they shouldn't, but only use it in a minor way.
- You can have the primary investigator share their information with the secondary investigator 'off-screen' between scenes. You might even like to have your secondary investigator say something like, 'I spoke to such-and-such on the way over, and you'll never guess what they told me!' or, 'Hey, I just got this text from X about the case.' This can often be the easiest way of dealing with the potential issue, but it's only possible if there's a feasible way the two characters could have communicated in the time available.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000820_c847c6fe651d3db787d16a4a138bd221214e0c9ba69fd8846a9e6aa167b14db7.png)

Keeping things straight in your own head as to what your different investigators know can be tricky, and there's no single right way to do it. If people do occasionally get themselves in a  muddle, try to be forgiving-the last thing anyone wants is to get into an argument that leads to bad feelings. If that happens, the best solution is always to take a short break from the  story  and  for  the  whole  group  to  have a  chat and agree on how you want to play it. After all, it's all about having fun together.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000821_a08934bb72d9b499d2702ea30d3770d060b6102b7d163d538744579859d79b90.png)

If the case file features a vampire house, for example, the burner  investigators  could  be  a  group  of  amateur  vampire hunters who lack any specialist equipment or training. As a side story, the players could enjoy a scene where they try to rid their neighbourhood of the vampire scourge, only to get themselves into terrible trouble. Such a scene could even act as a 'pre-credits' introduction to the case file-like the ones you get on police dramas and crime shows-and allows the players to see just how deadly the vampires are before their primary investigators come face to face with them. In a later scene, those primary investigators may find evidence of the burner characters (who may now be vampires themselves) or even interview any surviving ones regarding the case file.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000822_60ca2ce15481403786dcab2492ecb04355c12fa7b0cdd21dd74c3de7626e1819.png)

9

## Secondary Investigators: Pool

The  final  kind  of  secondary  investigators  are  what  we  call 'pool' investigators, because everyone in the group has some involvement  in  creating  them  and  anyone  can  play  them, i.e., they are part of a shared pool from which every player can  draw  as  and  when  the  need  arises-none  of  them  are assigned to a particular player.

To  create  a  set  of  pool  investigators,  the  group  should list  the  other  characters  within  their  primary  investigators' close circle of colleagues. For example, let's say the group's primary investigators  are  Folly  regulars  Nightingale,  Peter, and Abigail; their close circle might include Drs Walid and Vaughan,  Frank  Caffrey,  and  Harold  Postmartin-even Molly, if the GM is allowing fae investigators. Some or all of these people could then be created as secondary investigators, ideally with everyone helping to determine their attributes and skills. Then, when one of those secondary investigators features in a scene, any player can volunteer to take on the role of that character (especially if that player's character is not in the current scene).

## Investigator Organisations

The Folly is an integral part of the Rivers of London novels. Not only is it the headquarters of the Society of the Wise (page 260) and the Special Assessment Unit (SAU), it also acts  as  what  we  would  call  an  'investigator  organisation.' In  the  game,  such  organisations  provide  both  a  reason  for the players to work together and a base of operations. But not every game has to be centred around the Folly, and the GM and their players might want to create an organisation of their own.

The  following  sections  look  at  some  of  the  key  points the  GM might want to bear in mind if they decide to set their  game  outside  the  Folly  but  still  want  to  have  a  focal organisation/base of operations for their investigators.

## Creating Your Organisation

So, you've decided to design your own investigator organisation,  but  what  sort  of  components  does  it  need to  make  it  work?  Here  are  some  key  questions  for  you to think about.

- What is their base of operations? Is it a public building, like a library or a church hall? Is it a private building, e.g., one of the investigator's homes? Is it mobile, like a van? Or are they still looking for the right place?
- What resources can they access? Is one of the members wealthy and able to get the organisation whatever they need, whenever they need it? Is that benefactor a shadowy figure who only communicates with them by phone? Do they have a lot of useful contacts in the community where they're based? Or are they very much just getting started and don't have much in the way of gear or contacts?

## Organisation Structure

One  of  the  first  things  to  consider  is  your  organisation's structure,  including  who  its  members  are,  who's  in  charge, how it's structured internally, and, potentially, its relation to a wider parent organisation.

Every organisation usually has a leader-sometimes even more than one. In the Folly, for example, that's Nightingale. In  game,  it's  preferable  if  the  leader  is  not  an  investigator, which avoids having one player ordering the others around. Having the leader as an NPC also keeps them at arm's length and  gives  the  players  a  greater  degree  of  autonomy-the leader can step in to advise and direct when necessary, but isn't constantly keeping watch on the investigators' every action.

When it comes to how your organisation is set up, you may wish to use the classic cell structure. This means your group is  a  self-contained,  semi-independent  unit  within  a  larger whole-sort  of  like  the  Folly  is  within  the  Metropolitan Police Service, only more so. While you might communicate with other cells within the overarching organisation, perhaps even  sharing  relevant  and  useful  information  to  a  limited extent,  you  are  still  partitioned  from  them,  such  that  if your group is compromised, it doesn't expose the rest of the organisation.

Alternatively,  you  might  just  want  to  stick  with  the hierarchical, top-down organisation many businesses utilise-it all depends on what your organisation is (and what their aims are), who the investigators are, and what they're going to be getting up to in the course of their adventures.

- Who are its members? Do they all go to the same school? Have they formed a self-help group after experiencing 'weird bollocks'? Do they all shop in the same local comic shop, or are they all regulars at the same pub or cafe?
- Where are they based? Are they in London, or are they in another large town or city? Are they more isolated than that; out in the countryside somewhere? Are they even in the UK?

An organisation's structure may also affect what resources are  available  to  the  investigators.  For  example,  those  who are  part  of  an  independently  operating  cell-especially  if it's clandestine in nature-may have very different levels of access to resources compared to a group that is part of a large, well-funded hierarchical organisation.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000823_d7b48bd502893fc2c467db3f1a66bdbbd7b6a544c80c9c22ef730b06a95ac84f.png)

## Your Local Folly?

London is in the title of this game, but you can set your game anywhere you wish. You can easily shift it to a different city or even a different country-perhaps one that's local to you and your group. But relocating to somewhere further afield doesn't necessarily mean you can't be a part of wider Falcon operations.  For  example,  perhaps  officers  and  police  staff (civilian  workers)  have  been  sent  to  re-open  a  mothballed branch of the Folly in another part of the UK.

## GM ADVICE

While opening a new branch of the Folly within Great Britain  shouldn't  be  an  issue,  moving  to  another country  would  bring  up  all  sorts  of  jurisdictional issues-picture the Director's face if any of Nightingale's new fledglings suddenly set up shop in Germany 2 , for example!

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000824_3a7a3b622ce702834a63c6d8c3c18139ceff65fb3b012db11e5555c9659e6c9c.png)

If you decide to follow this path, your first couple of case files  could  be  pretty  minor,  serving  more  to  introduce  the investigators to their new home than dealing with anything really serious. Perhaps some of them were born and raised in this city, which is why they've been sent here by Nightingale. But slowly,  the  investigators  begin  to  realise  that  there's  a scarlet  thread  running  through  their  cases  that  hints  at something much more sinister, and that there's some unifying force behind it. With the folks at the Folly in London tied up in their own inquiries, it's up to the investigators to deal with the threat themselves before it escalates any further!

When considering the kind of stories you can tell outside London, have a think about your favourite TV shows. There are  lots  of  police  shows  and  murder  mysteries  set  in  cities, but there are also some that take place in more rural areas, too. Let's face it, according to several popular crime fiction and TV series, the Cotswolds (or somewhere very much like it)  is  a  nightmare  haven  of  murder,  thievery,  and  intrigue! Basically, what we're saying is that your branch of the Folly doesn't  have  to  be  in  a  big  city-it  could  even  be  in  your local town or village, perhaps even with some local rusticated practitioners on hand, like Hugh Oswald 3 , to offer guidance when needed.

It can be a lot of fun telling stories about places you and your players know well. It may not quite feel like the Peter Grant stories, but it can feel more like your story, and that can add a lot to your play experience.

## Non-Police Organisations

Another key element that stands out from the novels-besides London-is the presence of the police. Just as the previous entry  suggests  options  for  games  set  outside  London,  you can have games in which the investigators are not members of law enforcement. Again, consider fiction and shows that

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000825_673106927695fa34cdbfbaead829ec68b860b904abc4ad2a37759cb36d2ef7d3.png)

Footnotes:

2 The October Man , 3 Foxglove Summer

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000826_053d95394db394a67642c76beba82f7c82fc6e4ecd65c5e9e57f9bc167b72684.png)

9

feature  groups  that  investigate  and  deal  with  supernatural forces. For example, your investigators could be a group of high school pupils who battle occult forces, or members of a family who are part of a wider organisation that works to protect humanity from supernatural threats.

## Base of Operations

If  you  consider  most  films  or  TV  shows  featuring  a  group of characters, they usually have somewhere to hang out and discuss their cases. This may be as straightforward as a place of work-like the Folly-though it could equally be somewhere far  more  informal,  but  likely  just  as  important  to  those particular characters. For example, if your investigators are all high schoolers, their base of operations might be the school library. If, on the other hand, your investigators travel from town to town, their base of operations might be their vehicle.

The  organisation's base of operations may  be  pretty mundane with little in the way of resources-four walls, a roof, some furniture, and maybe a kettle-but it might also have things like magical protections, a library, an armoury, and  other  resources,  depending  on  how  fancy  it  is.  What your investigators' base of operations contains is up to your players to decide as they build their organisation, as long as it fits the story you are all building and is realistic in terms of their access to funds. For instance, an organisation financed by a wealthy dilettante is likely to have a very different base of operations to a group of older women who use the local village hall.

## Playing Fae Investigators

Experienced groups, along with those who've got the hang of  the  standard  game,  may  wish  to  try  playing  something other than human  characters. The following sections deal  with  creating  lower  fae  investigators-someone  like Zachary Palmer (page 256), for example. A separate section deals  with  Quiet  People  as  investigators  ( Playing a Quiet Person , page 319).

## Fae Character Creation

The  process  is  much  the  same  as  creating  a  standard investigator, at least to begin with. The GM may permit the players to use some or all of the advanced character creation rules  described  at  the  beginning  of  this  chapter.  Players should begin by deciding their investigators' occupations and assigning  values  to  their  characteristics  as  usual.  However, there are a few exceptions to standard character creation that have to be considered when building a fae investigator.

## GM ADVICE

## Luck

A lower fae's Luck score is equal to their POW and refreshes every day at a set time ( The Luck of the Demi-monde , page 243).  The  player  should  decide  what  that  set  time  is-for example, midnight or bang on the stroke of noon. It could also be some other regular daily event, such as the changing of the guard at Buckingham Palace (i.e., 11:00 am), or even one that isn't exactly the same time every day, such as sunrise or sunset. So long as the refresh time is clearly defined, it can be whatever you wish.

## No Newtonian Magic

As fae  characters  have  their  own  inherent  magical  powers through their  spheres  of  influence  ( Thematic Power ,  page 244),  they  cannot  use  Newtonian  magic.  This  means  they cannot take the Magical advantage or learn or use the spells listed in Chapter 4: Newtonian Magic -although they may wish to look at them for inspiration for what their thematic powers may or may not be able to do. This also means that fae investigators do not have magic points, unlike their human colleagues. As lower fae, they also do not have access to The Glamour (page 243).

## Sense Vestigia

All fae investigators begin the game with Sense Vestigia skill at 60% due to their inherent nature ( Sense Vestigia , page 85).

## Relationships and Backstory

Fae investigators  have  relatives,  friends,  and  adversaries  within the demi-monde. These should be noted in the investigator's backstory.  The  GM  can  incorporate  characters  from  the investigator's background into their cast of NPCs, as well as suggest NPC characters that the investigator might know.

## Affinities

Each fae has a special knack or sphere of influence tied to some element of the mundane world ( Thematic Power , page 244). In Zach's case, this is locks-he just seems to be able to open any lock he comes across 4 . In RoL:RPG , we call this a  'demi-monde  affinity.'  When  creating  a  fae  investigator, you should choose a demi-monde affinity that fits with Your Character Concept (page 46).

Using their affinity, a fae investigator can achieve magical effects  through  the  spending  of  Luck  points.  The  amount of  Luck  a  fae  investigator  spends  determines  whether  this is an everyday or significant manifestation of their thematic power.  The  GM  can  also  choose  to  handwave  anything  a fae  investigator  uses  their  affinity  for  if  it  simply  serves  to embellish the story, such as when Beverley glares frozen food into defrosting 5 .

Rules for creating player character genii locorum are not covered in this book.

The GM should call for the following Luck spends when a fae investigator uses their powers:

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000827_6bd039f1774409d0e9f547fb4e52b848f04d8b1a91bfb373f4017ec254d24b3e.png)

Footnotes: 4 B roken Homes, 5 Lies Sleeping

## GM ADVICE

The  following  guidelines  are  intended  for  player character use, although you may wish to implement them for lower fae NPCs as well, to keep them on a  par  with  the  investigators.  Otherwise,  use  the guidelines presented in Chapter 6 when handling an NPC's Thematic Power (page 244); always use those rules  if  the  NPC  is  a  genius  loci,  for  whom  the following Luck spends do not apply.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000828_5a333c60e9d411a07e51507cb9eda0b2777eef21866394e82f99b657dba5b806.png)

- Everyday manifestation (costs 5 Luck points) : something that adds colour to the story-such as breathing life into a wilting bouquet of flowers-or performs a useful minor task, such as closing an open door. In game terms, it could mean the investigator automatically achieves something that would otherwise require a successful Regular skill roll to achieve, such as opening a standard door lock.
- Significant manifestation (costs 20 Luck points) : something that has an impact on another character or the story in a more impressive or meaningful fashion. In game terms, it could mean the investigator automatically achieves something that would otherwise require a successful Hard skill roll, such as getting an unfamiliar animal to follow your commands. The default cost is 20 Luck points, but the GM is at liberty to increase the cost if the situation is exceedingly challenging, or the outcome is particularly spectacular.

Note: Fae investigators cannot use their affinity to directly inflict damage, but they may be able to do so indirectly, e.g., by convincing an animal to bite someone or by blowing them off balance at the top of some stairs, causing them to fall.

Some sample affinities and examples of what they might achieve with everyday and significant manifestations of their thematic power can be found in Table 13: Fae Investigator Affinities (nearby).

## GM ADVICE

Remember, we're not talking genii locorum levels of magical  ability  for  fae  investigators-even  at  their strongest,  they  aren't  going  to  be  able  to  pull  off something as impressive as flooding the entirety of a major London landmark, for instance 6 . Having said that, with the GM's permission, they may be able to pull off something really impressive once per case file for a suitably large investment of Luck points.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000829_c08b7e687fe918d2d5bcf7023fd77ef2d589ae2943f99c40ae588504953898f2.png)

## Playing a Quiet Person

The Quiet People live beneath London's streets. Zach Palmer says they are not fae as such-not like him, anyway-but they are born different 7 ,  so  definitely part of the lower demi-monde and therefore available to players as investigators. The process is  much the same as creating a standard investigator, with a  few  exceptions  ( Fae Character Creation ,  page  318,  and following).  As  with  other  fae  investigators,  Quiet  People investigators  cannot  use  Newtonian  magic,  do  not  have magic points, and do not have The Glamour (page 243).

## Occupations

Quiet People are more limited in their choice of occupation, given their previously secluded lifestyle; there are no Quiet People police officers  or  paramedics-yet!  They  tend  to  be craftspeople  (with  a  focus  on  ceramics),  farmers  (someone has to look after those albino pigs), teachers, or engineers (to maintain their amazing machinery). This means Quiet People investigators mostly have the Tradesperson occupation (with variations for the potters and engineers; page 74), the Farmer occupation (page 67), or the Lecturer occupation (page 69).

Having said that, young Quiet People who are emerging into the surface world could have a much broader range of occupations, with the GM's permission. As long as the player can  come  up  with  a  good  reason  why  their  Quiet  Person could have a particular  job,  then  they  can  take  it  and  add those details to their backstory.

## Sense Vestigia

All  Quiet  Person  investigators  begin  the  game  with  Sense Vestigia  skill  at  60%  due  to  their  inherent  nature  ( Sense Vestigia , page 85).

## Demi-Monde Affinity

Quiet  People  are  very  adept  at  shaping  and  manipulating earth and, more specifically, clay-it's just something they can do innately. In game terms, it's all part of their affinity. Use the Earth affinity in Table 13: Fae Investigator Affinities as inspiration for the everyday and significant manifestations a  Quiet  Person  investigator  can  achieve.  For  example,  by paying a Luck point cost rather than magic points, the Quiet People can use their innate powers to emulate the following spells: Terra (cost: 5 Luck, page 182), Rock Drill (cost: 10 Luck, page 180), Impello Vibrato (cost: 20 Luck, page 184), and The Coffin Bell (cost: 20 Luck, page 188).

Unlike  most  other  members  of  the  lower  demi-monde, though, the Quiet People do seem to emit a vestigia when they  use  their  thematic  powers,  so  the  player  can  either stick with the one mentioned in the Quiet Person's profile on page 253, or they can use the Creating an Investigator's Signare rules on page 164. If they choose the latter option, they should roll three times on Table 10: Signare as  usual, discard one, and then add either a momentary blast of heat or the smell of the pigsty as a third element, if they haven't already rolled either of those two.

Footnotes: 6 Rivers of London , 7 Whispers Under Ground

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000830_e55e939b842553339376872f07e7f73f42f93a67d2a71d2ed2a91fc26f1d1bc8.png)

9

## Table 13: Fae Investigator Affinities

| Affinity   | Sample Manifestations                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
|------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Air        | The investigator can bend the very air to their will. Everyday manifestation: summon a draft powerful enough to close a door (5 Luck points). Significant manifestation: create a gust of wind to move a person, requiring the target to make a successful STR roll or be pushed back 2 metres/yards (20 Luck points).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
| Animals    | Definitely at one with members of the animal kingdom. Everyday manifestation: make any animal docile (5 Luck points). Significant manifestation: get any animal to follow a one-sentence instruction; the instruction must be framed in terms the creature could reasonably understand (20 Luck points).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| Earth      | The investigator can bend the very earth to their will. Everyday manifestation: dig a 1 metre/yard square hole, 1 metre/yard deep in compacted earth in just a few minutes (5 Luck points). Significant manifestation: create an earth tremor powerful enough to unbalance someone within 10 metres/yards of the investigator, requiring the target to make a DEX roll or be shaken around and drop what they are holding (20 Luck points).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
| Fire       | The investigator can bend fire to their will. Everyday manifestation: ignite flammable material (non-living) by touching it, as if holding a match to it (5 Luck points). Significant manifestation: ignite flammable material (non-living), but as if they are holding a burning torch to it (i.e., the object ignites far quicker and suffers greater damage (20 Luck points).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| Mechanical | The investigator has a special aptitude with all things mechanical. Everyday manifestation: fix a mechanical watch and make it tick (5 Luck points). Significant manifestation: fix any car problems (mechanical in nature) in under 10 minutes, and make it run like new (20 Luck points). I f the investigator wishes, they can specialise rather than having a general mechanical aptitude. Example specialisms include clockwork or locks. For example: Everyday manifestation: open a lock that normally requires a Regular success with the Locksmith skill (5 Luck points). Significant manifestation: open a lock that requires a Hard success with the Locksmith skill (20 Luck points). |
| Plants     | The investigator can make vegetation do all sorts of interesting things. Everyday manifestation: encourage a small bouquet's worth of flowers to bloom on demand (5 Luck points). Significant manifestation: cause an obstruction or create a means of escape by making tree branches move, or swell the wood in a door so it jams, etc. (20 Luck points).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| Water      | The investigator can influence and control water, wherever it may be found, just not on quite the same scale as the rivers. Everyday manifestation: breathe underwater for several minutes or form a small volume of water into a particular shape (5 Luck points). Significant manifestation: breathe underwater for an hour or direct a jet of water (e.g., from a hosepipe or tap) to wherever they want it (20 Luck points).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000831_e7278ae0d614e6536ffd33c2c6825cda54c3187b3feb59e66ab64c1760dd2f48.png)

## Relationships and Backstory

Any  Quiet  Person  investigator  has  relatives  and  friendsand  perhaps  even  adversaries-within  the  Quiet  Person community,  and  possibly  the  wider  demi-monde.  These should be noted in the investigator's backstory. The GM can incorporate  characters  from  the  investigator's  background into their cast of NPCs, as well as suggest NPC characters that the investigator might know.

## Sensitivity

Quiet People are raised in underground communities and may never have been exposed to the bright lights and noise of the upper world. Their senses are attuned to low light and quiet sounds, so they  gain  a  bonus  die  to  Observation  rolls  in  quiet,  dimly-lit environments. They cannot see in total darkness, though.

Conversely,  they  incur  a  penalty  die  to  Observation  rolls in  noisy  or  bright  environments.  Remember when you had a migraine or a bad hangover? Remember how bright lights hurt your eyes and loud noises made your head throb? Well, now ask yourself if the light or noise would be strong enough to make you wince-if so, that's a penalty die. This would include the noise level in a busy supermarket or the light level on any day where the sun is shining brightly enough to cast strong shadows.

## Skills Revisited

The  following  section  presents  some  additional  ideas  and options regarding skills and skill rolls.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000832_8c2e8e66327e17db843fae8badba2f270fb288d9b257c4bb6588524bed245a72.png)

## Creating New Skills

As mentioned in Chapter 2 ,  it  may  be  that  a  player  wants an expert skill that isn't currently covered in the Skill List (page  81).  Creating  a  new  expert  skill  isn't  so  different  to creating a new occupation: if there's an existing skill that can be adjusted slightly to cover what the investigator needs, then go for it, with the GM's approval.

If,  on  the  other  hand,  there's  nothing  there  that  really works, look at the existing skills to determine what's needed. For example, besides the brief description of what the skill does  overall,  it  needs  details  on  whether  or  not  there  is  a skill  that  opposes  it,  what  constitutes  a  Regular  and  Hard difficulty level, how you might push the roll, and what the consequences for failing a pushed roll might be.

Example: Jo wants her investigator's occupation to be Archaeologist, but she wants them to have studied anthropology as well. As there currently isn't an Anthropology skill-and the GM isn't convinced that any of the existing expert skills quite cover it-they sit down with Jo to figure out what the new skill encompasses. They agree on a brief description of what the skill does, as well as the fact that it doesn't have an opposing skill. They then determine what might be achieved at the different difficulty levels and have fun working out what the consequences of a failed pushed roll could be, along with ways to put in the extra effort that a pushed roll requires. When they're both happy with the new skill, Jo takes it as one of her investigator's six starting skills and writes it on their character sheet. For what the finished skill looks like, see the Anthropology box, nearby.

## Anthropology

Enables the user to identify and understand an  individual's  way  of  life  through  observation. If the skill-user observes another culture from within for a time, or works from accurate records  concerning  an  extinct  culture,  then simple  predictions  can  be  made  about  that culture's  ways  and  morals,  even  though  the evidence may be incomplete. Studying the culture for a month or more, the anthropologist begins  to  understand  how  the  culture  functions  and,  in  combination  with  Read  Person ( Combined Skill Rolls , page 322), may predict the actions and beliefs of those being studied.

## Opposing skill/Difficulty level:

The difficulty for this skill is set according to the exposure of the skill-user to their subject.

- Regular difficulty: determining how someone might act after spending a month or more studying the person's culture.
- Hard difficulty level: determining how someone might act after spending only a week or less studying the person's culture.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000833_37e10658fe39c6d744fface7c211f2af887f4802335b8d59e7f1e3d0a5c888b3.png)

## Pushing Examples

Taking  more  time  to  study  the  culture;  completely immersing themselves in the culture for a period, e.g., living with the people they're studying; taking an active role in a ceremony or rite, etc.

## Sample consequences of failing a pushed roll:

being  attacked  by  the  people  studied  due  to some perceived transgression of their laws or social mores; suffering severe side-effects as the result of being involved in a ceremony that involved ingesting psychotropic plants.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000834_41f6219b8eb395943f6876d6cddef688873062f02dae55f7798c5126a53d9bab.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000835_4c37a02d88dd04d6f9927dd199906b0f102d0ffdba5b3bb173780070ea84d2db.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000836_a65a3578a7604948f06c7b1eb737c6b72327f177f10ba13b1e1e220c377109cd.png)

9

## Combined Skill Rolls

Some  situations allow-sometimes, even  demand-the simultaneous use of multiple skills. Other situations may be resolved by any one of a number of skills. In either situation, only  one  dice  roll  is  made,  with  the  result  compared  with each of the specified skills. The GM states whether a success is required for both skills-i.e., to achieve the task, the roll has to be a success for each skill involved-or if the roll only has to be a success for one of the skills involved.

Example: a street criminal moves to pull a knife from inside their jacket. A successful Observation roll would allow Nafeesa to see the  ruffian's  hand  moving  towards  the  knife,  while  a  successful Read Person would allow her to anticipate the action through the ruffian's  demeanour. The GM asks for a combined Observation and Read Person roll for Nafeesa. If the roll's result is a success for either skill, it allows Nafeesa to anticipate the man's action and perhaps give her a chance to act first.

Later, Nafeesa attends a party and wants to keep an eye open for  anyone  acting  suspiciously.  Once  again,  this  requires  both Observation and Read Person, so the GM asks for a combined Observation and Read Person roll. One roll is made and the result is compared to both skills-but in this case, the GM states that the dice roll must be a success for both Observation and Read Person for Nafeesa to achieve the task.

Note the importance of using a single dice roll in the latter example. Both Nafeesa's Read Person and Observation skills have a value of 60%. The chance of success when making one dice roll and comparing it to both skills simultaneously is 60%. If  two  separate  rolls  were  made,  first  against  Observation and then against Read Person, the chance of succeeding in both would be reduced to 36%. That's statistics for you…

## Social Skill: Difficulty Levels

A character  may  have  a  Social  skill  of  60%,  but  this  doesn't mean they can charm anyone and everyone 60% of the time. The difficulty level for a skill roll is based on the opposing factor; in this case, the Read Person skill. So, if the opposing character has a Read Person skill of 70% or higher, the person rolling

## GM ADVICE

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000837_edfcd6720f5994a8013c5cf9284cacddd8c15c99c670489a6c87b13d79663653.png)

needs  to  make  a  Hard  success  (a  roll  equal  to  or  lower  than half the investigator's Social skill; Determining the Difficulty Level , page 105). The vast majority of NPCs have a Read Person skill below 70%, though, so the difficulty level will most often be Regular and a player only needs a Regular success to achieve their goal. When an investigator is interacting with an NPC, it's up to the GM when to call for a Social skill roll.

- If the NPC is positively inclined towards the investigator's goal, don't bother rolling dice-simply have them agree.
- If the NPC is opposed to the investigator's goal, any Social roll the player makes incurs a penalty die.
- If the NPC lacks strong feelings about the investigator's goal but is being asked to do something that requires them to put themselves out, the GM could ask for a roll at the appropriate difficulty level.
- If the player comes up with something to strengthen their investigator's interaction with the NPC, the Social roll may gain a bonus die, at the GM's discretion. For example, if the investigator is trying to persuade an NPC to do something, and the player puts forward a really good argument as to why the NPC would help them, then the GM could award a bonus die. Or if the investigator hands over a significant bribe, this could also be sufficient motivation to award a bonus die to their Social roll.
- At the extremes of what's possible with a Social roll, an NPC might refuse to give up information because they fear reprisals against their family. In a case like this, the NPC would be strongly motivated not to talk. Even if the player comes up with a very persuasive case, the best they might be allowed is a Hard Social roll with a penalty die.

Example: Nafeesa is trying to get an NPC to reveal the name of their contact in the police force. The NPC is a doctor; as they're not a major or recurring NPC, the GM hasn't used the full character creation rules for them, but decides that their Social and Read Person skills are below 70%. The difficulty  level  therefore  begins  as  Regular.  However,  the doctor has no desire to reveal her contact, so the GM adds a penalty die.

In the combined skill rolls example, as Nafeesa has the same skill value for both skills, if she succeeds at one, she also succeeds at the other. If we'd used Eli Venturini in the example instead, his Observation skill is 30% and his Read Person skill is 60%. In the first part of the example, Eli still has a good chance of getting some information from the combined roll as the dice result only has to be a success for one of the two skills involved. So, as long as he rolls 60 or lower, he learns something. In the second half of the example, it's going to be much harder for him to achieve his goal as the dice result must be a success for both Read Person and Observation, i.e., he needs to roll 30 or lower to learn anything of use.

Nafeesa's player fails the skill roll, and so seeks to push the roll by threatening to expose some of the doctor's underhanded dealings to the press. Nafeesa pulls out her mobile and dials a number, asking for the news desk, and then looks the doctor straight in the eye to make sure she understands the implied threat. The GM awards Nafeesa's player a bonus die on the pushed roll, which cancels out the penalty die incurred by the doctor's reluctance to talk. Unfortunately, Nafeesa's player fails the pushed roll and it is now entirely up to the GM whether the doctor is forthcoming, regardless of any further action the investigators may take against her. Bearing in mind, of course, what would be the most interesting fallout from that failed pushed roll. Perhaps the doctor lies, sending Nafeesa off on a wild goose chase… If, on the other hand, the player had succeeded at their Social roll, the GM would have to give up the name of the contact as this was the player's stated goal.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000838_200e3e2a149035a3f772c7515590a7ff0d090ae08f03a7e3deb36ed5ef4d2818.png)

## Magic Revisited

We've  already  spoken  about vestigia , signare ,  spells,  and spellcasting  in Chapter  4:  Newtonian  Magic .  Here  we take a look at a few other aspects of magic from the Rivers of London setting, as well as some guidelines on how to create your own Newtonian spells.

## Higher-Order Spells

There are, of course, spells of a higher-order than the ones in Chapter 4-ones that take years of training and practise to master. Many are mentioned in the novels only in passing, such as the body control spell used by Nightingale against Patrick Gale 8 , which Peter guesses is at least 20 th order.

Such higher-order spells are, like Demon Traps (page 324), something  the  investigators  are  more  likely  to  experience than use themselves. Following is an example of one such spell from Rivers of London ; it has no prerequisite as it is not intended for player use.

## Dissimulo (Eighth Order)

A spell  that  alters  the  position  of  the  muscles  and  skin  in a person's face, completely changing their appearance. The spell lasts  for  10  minutes.  When  cast,  the  caster  must overcome the target with an opposed POW roll.

- If the caster loses the opposed roll, the spell fails.
- If the caster wins the opposed roll, the target's face rearranges itself. Bones break and move, and flesh remoulds to the caster's design. The target feels intense pain.

When the spell ends, the forces holding the target's face together collapse, with terrible results. Death is likely, and the  target  suffers  immediate  Terminal  damage  ( Table  7: Other Forms of Damage , page 149).

In  theory,  a  practitioner  could  cast  this  spell  upon  their own face, but quite what dire need would drive a person to do that is hard to fathom.

Boost: each  additional  magic  point  spent  increases  the duration  by  10  minutes.  However,  the  spell  automatically ends if the caster falls asleep.

I saw the dissimulo as  it  happened that time: the courier's chin seemed to bulge, I heard the distinct cracking of bone and teeth as it jutted forward into a sharp point. The lips twisted into a snarl as the nose stretched until it was almost as long. It wasn't a real face,  it  was  a  caricature  man-in-the-moon face that no human could have in real life. The mouth opened and I could see inside to the red ruin of his jaw.

- -Rivers of London

Footnote:

8 Lies Sleeping

## Creating New Spells

Aside from the higher-order spells discussed in the previous section,  we  also  haven't  included  every  single  lower-order spell  that  Ben  mentions  in  the Rivers  of  London novels, either. If there's a particular favourite spell belonging to the Newtonian tradition  that  you  or  the  players  would  like  to include in your game, you can always create it.

Look at the spells in Chapter 4 . They each have an order, which  determines  how  many  magic  points  it  costs  to  cast them. Some have prerequisites, followed by a brief description of what the spell does, and possibly, how long it lasts. If it can be boosted, the boost part of the description details what happens for each additional  magic  point  spent.  There  may also be an additional effect if the spell is mastered. These are all elements that you'll need to think about if you decide to create a spell of your own based on what's in the books.

Figuring out a spell's order, and hence its base cost, may be quite straightforward if the source novel lists the formae needed to cast it. Be careful, though, as some of the Latin terms listedlike aestus and iactus -are adjectivia or inflectentes ,  so  aren't counted when working out a spell's order. If in doubt, think about  when  it  first  appears  in  the  novels-the  earlier  in  the series Peter Grant learns it, the lower its level is likely to be-or what existing spell has a similar or equivalent effect.

The next thing to think about is the spell's prerequisites. Does it  have any, and, if yes, what are they? You should be able to figure this out from the formae used to construct it. If you're creating a second order spell that includes lux as one of its formae , then Werelight or Flash Bulb would be its prerequisite, and so on.

After that, you need to figure out what the spell does in game terms. If it's a ranged attack spell, like Fireball, you'll need to include details of its effective range and how much base spell damage it deals. If it's a spell that affects how a person  behaves,  you  should  always  include  a  way  for  the target to resist its effects. Remember, if the investigators can learn your new spell, then so can NPCs, and while players are often content to surrender a certain degree of investigator autonomy if the dice will it, no one enjoys arbitrarily losing control  of  their  character  as  a  result  of  GM  fiat.  See  also Important: Consent (page 190).

Finally, if the spell can be boosted, what does that boost do? For example, does it increase the time the spell lasts or how much damage it does? Or does it have some other effect? Does mastery give you some extra degree of fine control over the spell's effect? Not all  spells  have  to  be  boostable,  and  not  all  spells  have  a mastery effect, but these are both points you need to think about when adding the finishing touches to your creation.

Once you have everything in place, we suggest you test the spell through an NPC in one or two case files before you let the investigators get their hands on it. This way, you'll know if you've got the balance of what it does for how much it costs about right, or whether you need to go away and refine it a little further.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000839_189ec16dd2ff4d0228ae81ab14abab46520813125ddf0222445c33caa2c17f25.png)

9

## Enchantments

In the Rivers of London novels, objects can be imbued with magical power through the process known as enchantment. This  can  result  in  handy  items  such  as  Nightingale's  silvertopped cane, which serves as his Wizard's Staff (following), or at the other end of the spectrum, an enchantment is used to create the IED of magic, the terribly dangerous Demon Trap (nearby). And then there are the Rose Jars (page 326): an 18 th century means of trapping and preserving ghosts.

## The Wizard's Staff

We learn of several wizard's staffs during the course of the novels, all of them either constructed by-or with knowledge taught  by-the  legendary  British  practitioner  blacksmiths, the Sons of Weyland 9 . The wizard's staff is a source of power, and can be thought of as a rechargeable 'magic point battery.'

At the heart of the staff or cane lies a core of gunmetal blue metal, the thickness of a pencil. This core has ripples of shading along its length where the metal has been repeatedly folded, like the blade of a samurai sword. In Japanese swordsmithing, this process of folding metal (pattern welding) produced exceptional blades from crude ores. In the hands of magical blacksmiths, it creates a matrix capable of retaining magical power.

A third order spell is used to raise the temperature of the forge and keep it hot while the metal is hammered-basically, a variation of the lux forma that both heats the forge and stops you from burning your hands while you work the hot metal. It takes well over 100 hours of work to create a single staff.

A  practitioner  who  handles  a  wizard's  staff  should  feel a  buzz  of  power  running  through  it.  A  staff  made  by  one practitioner  can  be  used  by  another,  but  there  can  be complications, if you wish. Who knows what it would be like to use the staff of a dark wizard? Or perhaps a staff might have had an angry spirit trapped inside it, making it a danger akin to a Demon Trap (following).

We're not going to provide rules for investigators to create their own wizard's staff just yet, but if you, the GM, want them to have one, they can either be gifted it as a reward for having performed some exceptional service, or you can work it  into  the  story  as  something  they  craft  for  themselvesalthough it's going to take a heck of a long time for them to get it finished. Like demon traps, consider a wizard's staff as GM fiat-it's entirely up to you if your players can get their hands on one.

There  are  some  rules  of  thumb  you  may  wish  to  use  for quickly creating a wizard's staff for an NPC.

- If they forged it themselves, the maximum number of magic points it can store is the same as their maximum number of magic points at the time they created it. They can choose to store fewer magic points in it, if they wish.
- To recharge their staff, the practitioner must spend two hours of quiet concentration, focusing their energy into its core. A staff only fails to recharge if its core is damaged or broken.
- If it was forged by someone else, assume it has a store of 10 magic points for a beginner's staff and 20 magic points if created for a more experienced practitioner.
- A practitioner wielding a staff decides on the source of the magic points they're using: their own supply or the staff's charge. If one source runs out, the spell automatically draws from the other until it, too, is drained.

Footnote: 9 Broken Homes

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000840_1f1bed9ae9d224d4f38ca0c76de5f3e4f8a572fd6db9a580307894994352fb7c.png)

## Demon Trap

Given their vile nature, a demon trap is not something the investigators should be making, but it is something they may encounter. The easiest way to explain the nature of a demon trap is to compare it to a magical anti-personnel landmine. If  it's  triggered,  it  releases  a  sudden  crescendo  of vestigia followed  by  an  explosion.  The  explosion  can  be  a  physical blast  (akin  to  a  regular  anti-personnel  mine  or  IED)  or  a psychic wave (which effectively fries people's brains).

## Creating a Demon Trap

The creation of a demon trap is not especially difficult, but it is very nasty. The process originated in Scandinavia with the Vikings back in the 7 th century. It requires only a second order spell-oh,  and  torture.  Traditionally,  a  captive  individual would be tortured to death and their spirit trapped in the device-basically, a demon trap is a weaponised angry ghost.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000841_3338a2c99ee8822492dd32eeaf77249bfdd3afb4c3ccef86cf388132cf2afef4.png)

A demon trap can take many forms. Traditionally made of metal, they can also be made from plastic, some forms of which are just as good as metal for retaining magic-if not better ( Vestigia in the Environment , page 163). One demon trap described in the novels is a metal disk about 60 cm (2 feet) wide and 5 mm (0.2 inches) thick, but there are other designs on record. It's not so much the look as the feel of the trap that identifies one for what it is.

## Setting Off a Demon Trap

Two definite contacts are required to set off the trap: stepping onto the trap primes the demon, while stepping off the trap releases it, causing the explosion.

- If a person is unaware of the demon trap's presence, and is moving around in close proximity to it, the GM should ask for a Luck roll. A failure means they have stepped on it and have set off the first of the two triggers. The second trigger is activated when they step off the trap.
- If the person stepping onto the demon trap is a practitioner, they may sense the trap and have a chance of deactivating it ( Locating a Demon Trap , following).

When a demon trap goes off, the GM must determine two factors: the effect and the power level.

- First, the effect: choose whether the demon trap creates a physical blast or a psychic wave. Published case files will state which effect the demon trap uses.
- Second, the power level: choose whether it is a standard demon trap or a greater demon trap. Published case files will state what the demon trap's power level is.

For a physical blast effect:

- A standard demon trap creating a physical explosion inflicts Terminal* damage to those within 2 metres/yards, and Deadly* damage to those up to 6 metres/yards away.
- A greater demon trap creating a physical explosion inflicts Splat* damage to those within 2 metres/yards, and Terminal* damage to those up to a distance of 6 metres/yards away.

*Refer to Table 7: Other Forms of Damage (page 149) for definitions of Splat, Terminal, and Deadly damage.

For a psychic wave effect:

- A standard demon trap creating a psychic wave: those within 6 metres/yards must make a POW roll for Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation (page 177). Any
- victims within 2 metres/yards of the trap incur a penalty die to the roll.
- A greater demon trap creating a psychic wave: those within 12 metres/yards must make a POW roll for Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation (page 177). Any victims within 4 metres/yards of the trap incur a penalty die to the roll.

## Locating a Demon Trap

If the person stepping on the demon trap is a practitioner, the GM should ask for a Magic roll:

- Success: the practitioner realises they have inadvertently set off the first of the two triggers. Once the first trigger is activated, the practitioner cannot move without the trap going off. They now have an opportunity to disarm it.
- Fail: the practitioner is unaware of triggering the demon trap and it goes off, inflicting the degree of harm determined by the GM ( Setting Off a Demon Trap , nearby).

If the practitioner is aware of the presence of the demon trap, but doesn't know its precise location, they can search for it.  To do this, the practitioner takes a fresh-cut branch in  their  hand  and  feels  over  the  ground  to  find  the  edges of  the  trap.  This  requires  a  Sense  Vestigia  or  Magic  roll (whichever is higher):

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000842_bac65076aff7bc74e9be5f106e74a9922e776d071c65d7f465a1265f549e2728.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000843_e50639cfeb8cffbc098d8919987db73df93216f5c511e0e9dbff2c470df11c02.png)

9

- Success: the practitioner safely locates the trap without activating it.
- Fail: the practitioner locates the trap, but accidentally activates the first trigger. Once the first trigger is activated, the practitioner cannot move without the trap going off. They now have an opportunity to disarm it.

## Disarming a Demon Trap

Once  the  trap  has  been  located-one  way  or  another-a practitioner must use the Sīphōnem spell (page 191) to disarm it. Failing the casting roll activates a trigger on the demon trap:

- If neither of the trap's triggers have been activated, failing the casting roll activates the first trigger. Once the first trigger is activated, the practitioner cannot move away without the trap going off. They now have a second opportunity to disarm it.
- If the trap's first triggers has already been activated, failing the casting roll activates the second trigger and sets the demon trap off.

Providing the  spell  is  cast  successfully,  the  trap  is  deactivated and the noxious energy drained out of it, funnelling safely upwards into  a  swirling  vortex.  The  release  of  energy  may cause a few unfortunate, but otherwise harmless, side effects, such as every house and/or car alarm within a street or two of the demon trap's location being triggered.

If the first trigger has been activated and no one present knows  the  Sīphōnem spell,  then  there  are  limited  options available. Either call for someone who does know the spell, or evacuate the area and attempt to reduce the degree of damage suffered when the trap detonates. Whoever triggered the trap may attempt to jump to safety:

- Jumping away from a standard demon trap requires a DEX roll. On a Hard success, reduce the damage from Terminal to Deadly in the case of physical blast, or remove the penalty die in the case of psychic wave.
- Jumping away from a greater demon trap requires a DEX roll. On a Hard success, reduce the damage from Splat to Terminal in the case of physical blast. Sadly, if it's a greater demon trap, even this won't help in the case of the psychic wave because of its increased range of effect.

Any practitioner or member of the demi-monde within an 800 metre (0.5 mile) radius will also be aware that a demon trap  has  been  triggered  or  defused;  the  release  of  a  really powerful demon trap may be felt even further afield.

## Rose Jars

Demijohn-sized, glass containers filled with a cloudy liquid of  various  colours,  used  for  trapping  ghosts.  Peter  Grant first  encountered  them  when  looking  for  missing  social worker Brené McClaren 10 .  According to Abigail's research, they were developed by a Creole woman known as Rose of New Orleans, who taught the technique to her husband, the notorious  George  Buckland  ( County  Practitioners ,  page 260). According to New York Librarian Mrs Chin, she was actually  Rosemarie  Louise  Moreno,  an  18 th century  Black practitioner from New Orleans who trained at the Académie Royale de Philosophie Occulte in  Paris 11 .  Rosemarie allegedly brought  knowledge  of  how  to  produce  the  jars  to  London when she fled the French Revolution.

Although  the actual production process is currently unknown-to the Folly, at least-new variations have been created accidentally. Occupied Rose jars give off a soft glow because of the ghost or personality trapped within. Although an effective means of storage, any ghost captured in this way cannot be released without destroying it. If the jar is smashed, the  entity  within  is  immediately  destroyed.  If  released  by gentler methods, such as removing the sealed stopper, the jar cracks with the loss of one-quarter to one-half of the liquid within; the ghost appears and behaves in the same way as others of its kind ( Ghost , page 249), before gradually falling apart in a most disturbing manner, then vanishing altogether.

If  the  ghost  inside  is  amenable,  touching  a  Rose  jar transports the viewer's consciousness into the ghost's miniature realm, which appears as a single location of the ghost's choosing, surrounded, as Peter Grant describes it, by 'shifting planes of crystal.' There, the viewer can talk to the ghost and ask them questions for as long as the jar's resident wishes  them  to  remain.  A  ghost  is  under  no  obligation  to permit entry to their jar if they do not wish it.

While  it  is  unlikely  the  investigators  will  uncover  the secret of making Rose jars, the objects could be used by the GM as most unusual witnesses to a crime, or as assets to be recovered to prevent their misuse by unscrupulous collectors and unlicenced practitioners.

## Creating Non-Newtonian Special Abilities

As  you  saw  in  Chapter  6, Sahra  Guleed (page  234)  and Michael Cheung (page 238) use a form of magic that is nonNewtonian in nature. Because this book doesn't define how non-Newtonian  traditions  work,  we've  given  them  special abilities that mimic the stunts they get up to in the novels.

It's  common  for  a  demon  trap  to  also  have  a  secondary charge to catch incautious rescuers.

It may be that you want to create an NPC of your own who uses magic from a tradition very different to that practised by Peter Grant and DCI Nightingale. The simplest way to do this is to adapt the spells in Chapter 4 to better reflect that tradition but essentially use the same underlying system: a set magic point cost for a particular level of spell coupled with a

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000844_a0cf93b89ea159e17f4e98c80f5600502ffd18e5e0f604e05cbf19a175349d00.png)

Footnotes: 10 The Furthest Station , 11 False Value

Magic skill roll to see if the spell is used successfully. After all, it may be that the only difference between the traditions is  the  gestures  and  terminology  that  each  associates  with casting a particular type  of spell-i.e.,  the  underlying principles of magic remain the same no matter how you dress it up. That's certainly what we've done with the Snapdragon spell  (page  182),  which  Peter  learned  from  Lady  Caroline Linden-Limmer 12 .

Alternatively, you may wish to create a particular special ability to give your NPC their powers. Let's look at Michael Cheung as an example of how you might go about that. From the novels, we know that Michael's business card states he is a 'Legendary Swordsman 13 ,'  so  he's  going to have a sword. It's  not  going  to  be  an  ordinary  sword-he  is  using  the word legendary after all-so let's make it the equivalent of a Wizard's Staff (page 324). Michael's a master of his art, so let's say it has a store of 20 magic points. By contrast, Sahra's sword (page 235) only has a store of 10 magic points as she's still an apprentice.

Michael  has  magical  abilities,  so  he  needs  the  Magical advantage. But we need to tweak that to better reflect his tradition, which is based on movement. So, let's adapt it so it  becomes  the Mystic Art of Fighting (page  234).  When Michael expends a magic point in combat, not only can he pull off all sorts of gravity-defying manoeuvres, but he also gains a bonus die on his Fighting roll, which all adds to his legendary status.

Let's go back to Michael's sword. It may, effectively, be a wizard's staff, but it should also probably function something like  the Signature  Weapon advantage  (page  52)-every legendary  swordsman  worth  his  salt  has  one  of  those.  To make sure it matches with his fighting skills being magical in nature, let's also say the bonus die he gains on damage rolls must be paid for with a magic point spend. And there you have  him:  Michael  Cheung,  legendary  swordsman,  whose magic  skills  were  created  from  a  combination  of  tweaked advantages and an enchanted item.

## Combat Revisited

The Combat section (page 123) contains all the rules needed to run fight scenes in your game. We advise you to stick with those  until  you  become  accustomed  to  using  them.  Once you are comfortable and confident with the standard rules, you may want to add a little more crunch to combat, so what follows is a collection of optional rules from which you can pick and choose as you please.

You are not under any obligation to use any of the following suggestions  just  because  they're  here.  Everyone  has  their own preference when it comes to rules. Some people prefer to make spontaneous judgements about each new situation based on its unique merits, either narratively or with the aid of dice rolls, while others prefer to have a strongly defined set of guidelines to play by.
