You  and  your  group  know  what  suits  you  best,  but,  if you're not sure, sit down and talk about it, or try adding and removing one rules tweak at a time to see what you do and don't  like.  As  always,  take  what  you  want,  leave  what  you don't! Keep the rules that you feel enrich your game and drop the ones that don't. After all, the idea is to have fun telling stories in the Rivers of London world, and you only need to use the rules that help support you to do that.

## Surprise

Sometimes,  opponents  square  up  to  each  other  like  two boxers in the ring-they know they're engaged in a fight even before the first punch is thrown. If this is the case, the GM should start a combat round as soon as someone states they are  throwing the first  punch.  This  is  effectively  how  we've treated all combat up until now.

So,  for  your  NPC,  think  about  what  their  magical abilities  are  and  what  advantages  or  other  skills/items  you could adapt to replicate the in-game effect you're after. You may  even  want  to  look  at  the  demi-monde  affinities  used by  fae  investigators  ( Affinities ,  page  318)  for  some  ideas, particularly  if  you  think  they  come  from  a  tradition  that specialises  in  manipulating  a  specific  type  of  element.  Be careful not to make your NPC a magical jack-of-all-trades, though. Try to let each tradition have its own peculiar foibles so that the investigators recognise it the next time they see someone else using it.

However, not all fights start like that. In some cases, the attack may be a complete surprise-a sudden knee to the groin or  a  knife  in  the  back,  for  example.  When  someone  states that they're making a sudden, unexpected attack, they should have the opportunity to surprise their opponent. Of course, if the target has their wits about them, they may see the attack coming. Also, a character entering an ongoing combat could attempt to do so by stealth and gain the advantage of surprise. Once a character has been seen, or has participated in the combat, there is no further opportunity for a surprise attack as their opponent now knows they're there.

The following rules for surprise attacks assume the target has some chance of anticipating what's about to happen. If the  target  is  drugged  or  otherwise  unconscious,  they're effectively defenceless and the GM should use their discretion to judge the outcome.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000845_ee2de1354b07a75e3722261f6f749060f185f8e1ef38497b17b058ed81f9034f.png)

9

## Can I Make a Surprise Attack on My Opponent?

If you're sneaking up on a target, make a Stealth roll. The GM should use the NPC's Observation skill to set the difficulty level  for  your  Stealth  roll.  With  a  success,  you  attack  with surprise, while a failure means you attack normally. When a  character  attacks  with  surprise,  the  attack  automatically succeeds. The attacker should then make their damage roll to see how badly they've injured their opponent.

- If your target knows you're there, and you're simply making a sudden attack and hope to gain the element of surprise, make a Stealth roll. The GM should use either the NPC's Observation or Read Person skill (whichever is higher) to set the difficulty level for your Stealth roll. With a success, you attack with surprise, while a failure means you attack normally.
- In both cases, if the NPC's skill is 70 or above, the difficulty level is Hard, meaning you'll have to roll equal to or lower than half your skill value on percentile dice.

## What if Someone Tries to Make a Surprise Attack on Me?

If  someone  is  sneaking  up  on  you,  make  an  Observation roll. The GM should use the NPC's Stealth skill to set the difficulty level for your Observation roll. With a success, you anticipate the attack and resolve it normally, while a failure means you are surprised.

- If you are aware of the presence of your attacker, and they're simply making a sudden attack and hope to gain the element of surprise, make an Observation or Read Person roll (whichever is higher). The GM should use the NPC's Stealth skill to set the difficulty level for your roll. With a success, you anticipate the attack and resolve it normally, while a failure means you are surprised.
- In both cases, if the NPC's skill is 70 or above, the difficulty level is Hard, meaning you'll have to roll equal to or lower than half your skill value on percentile dice.

When the GM  attacks with surprise the attack automatically succeeds, and they should make a damage roll to see how badly they've injured their target.

## Prone Revisited

- Lying down provides a stable position from which to shoot. A prone character gains one bonus die when making a Firearms roll.
- Normally, a prone character may stand up on their next turn in the round as a free action ( Combat Manoeuvres , page 134). If another character is holding their action, they can interrupt ( Interrupting , page 328) and attempt to keep the prone character down. If they successfully interrupt, the prone character must engage them in an opposed Fighting roll to stand up. This counts as both individuals' turns for the round.
- By lying flat on the floor, a prone character also presents a smaller target, and those targeting a prone character with a gun or spell incur one penalty die on their Firearms or Magic roll-but ignore this if at point-blank range.

## Holding Your Action

Normally,  a  character  waits  until  their  turn  in  a  combat round and then initiates their combat action, whether that's fighting,  fleeing,  or  performing  some  other  action  that requires  a  dice  roll  ( Actions  in  a  Combat  Round ,  page 126).  However, a character may instead elect to hold their turn in the combat round, allowing them to take their turn following someone else's turn in the same round or remain ready to interrupt someone else's action-i.e., to try and stop that person from completing their intended action. The GM should keep careful track of the turn order in the round if they're going to allow the use of these options.

- If a player wishes to hold their investigator's action in a particular round, they can declare when they're going to act, saying something along the lines of, ' I'm going to hold my action and go after Sam. ' If they declare when they are going to act in this manner, it doesn't count as Interrupting (following).
- If a character fails to use their held action before it gets back to their regular turn in the following round, the held action is lost, but they may initiate the current round's action as normal.

## Interrupting

A  player  may  hold  their  investigator's  action  so  they  can interrupt a character's future turn. For example:

In Chapter  3 , we talked about outcomes other than damage  during  a  fight,  such  as  using  combat  manoeuvres to  knock  a  person  prone.  A  prone  character  is  assumed  to be  lying  flat  on  the  ground  and  Fighting  attacks  against someone when they are prone gain one bonus die ( Combat Manoeuvres , page 134).

- If another character breaks cover, even briefly (to fire shots for example), they may be targeted by a character who is holding their action.
- If, as an investigator, an enemy declares an attack on one of your allies, you may use your held action to intervene in some way, e.g., diving heroically in front of a colleague who is about to get shot.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000846_31d4bbd90c2af26e0f59855d4651b7ec1d66c5bac0a7efc3b5b12768686684c4.png)

Regardless of what action they are taking (melee, magic, firearms, etc.), the GM should call for an opposed DEX roll to determine who takes their action first-in the case of a tie, the person holding their action wins.

Example: Izzy has her gun trained on Nafeesa and her colleagues. Nafeesa's player says they are holding her action and will only cast a spell at Izzy if Izzy moves to shoot someone. The GM declares that Izzy is going to shoot Nafeesa, so it comes down to a DEX roll to see whether Nafeesa or Izzy gets their 'shot' off first.

## GM ADVICE

If a player really wants their investigator to dive in front of someone to save them from a spell or a bullet, and they haven't opted to hold their action, you could allow them to use their fight back response ( Response 3: Fight Back ,  page 129), on someone else's behalf. Remember that each character can only make the fight back response once per round unless they have the Fast Reactions advantage (page 51).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000847_b59fbd4788d82e8bee2676e32db4dc103e069a55965aa1220b3080b01e90648e.png)

## Automatic Fire

Some firearms can be set to automatic fire ( Table 3: Weapons , page 61), indicating that the gun keeps on firing until the trigger is released. This usually means that, in the space of a few seconds, the gun has fired all of its ammunition. When we  use  the  term  'full  auto'  in RoL:RPG ,  we're  talking about a person emptying a whole clip on their turn in the combat round.

Firing  on  full  auto  is  not  the  same  as  firing Multiple Shots (page 139), which we treat as three shots fired in rapid succession  against  a single target-either  from  three  quick pulls of the trigger or from 'burst fire' (a single trigger pull that fires three shots). The multiple shots rules allow the user to choose between increasing their chance to hit or increasing the damage they do if they hit.

With full auto, the user gets a different choice. Either they can pull the trigger and fire in an arc at a selection of targets, known as 'spray and pray,' or they can pull the trigger and hold the gun steady in order to fire on a single target or small area,  which we term 'concentrated fire.' To do either, you need to have over half the ammunition left in the clip. Using full auto almost always empties the clip (for exceptions, see following). It doesn't necessarily do that in real life, but this is a game, and for ease of play, there must be some compromise with how things work in the real world.

Given  how  quickly  automatic  weapons  fire,  some  are fitted  with  a  larger  ammunition  capacity,  which  may  be  a belt  or  drum  of  some  kind.  Such  weapons  can  be  used  to make up to 3 auto fire attacks on multiple rounds without the need to reload.

329

## GM ADVICE

As  gun  play  is  not  central  to  RoL:RPG,  we  haven't included things like the number of bullets a weapon has  per  clip  or  magazine  in Table  3:  Weapons . Instead, that's something that should be determined narratively  by  you  during  combat  based  on  the weapons in use. If it seems unreasonable for someone with an automatic weapon to use full auto because they've been shooting everything in sight with single shots up to that point, then feel free to insist they'll need to change their clip first. If necessary, the GM could ask a player to make a Luck roll to determine whether their firearm holds less than half its magazine.

## Spray and Pray

Also  known  as  'suppressing  fire,'  the  aim  is  to  make everyone in the arc of fire think again about the life choices that brought them to this moment. It's more about getting people out of the way than it is about harming them. Imagine the sound as the hail of bullets comes flying in your general direction-most people are going to throw themselves to the floor and get their heads down.

In  the  standard  firearms  rules,  we  have  the Dive  for Cover rule (page 128). However, versus full auto, diving for cover is a little different. Everyone in the arc of fire has the option to dive for cover, and anyone who takes that option automatically avoids taking damage; however, they do have to sacrifice their next turn. If they've already taken their turn in  the  present  round,  they  must  sacrifice  their  turn  in  the following round.

Holding the firearm and moving it in a sweeping motion from left to right (or vice versa) produces an arc of fire up to 90 degrees at most. As RoL:RPG doesn't use tactical combat maps as standard, the GM must decide which targets would be within the arc of fire and which would not. If the group has decided to play with visualisation aids, or if using a battle map on a tabletop gaming platform ( Distances in Combat , page 127; Who's Where? ,  page 221), then who is and isn't within the arc of fire should be obvious.

The shooter only rolls to hit if someone decided not to dive for cover, i.e., if every target dived for cover, there's no need to roll anything-the shooter fires wildly over everyone's heads. If the shooter does need to roll their Firearms skill, they do so with a penalty die due to the difficulty of controlling a gun used on full auto. A difficulty level is set according to range, as is the usual case for firearms attacks. The chance of hitting targets  with  spray  and  pray  diminishes  with  range,  so  any targets beyond 50 metres/yards are ignored. If the majority of  the  targets  are  within  point-blank  range,  the  GM  can award a bonus die (which would cancel out the penalty die; Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111).

9

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000848_e94bc8d59c8ed5aca219d8b05c0ad99d9b2fe04252285fe3452b22ff54b57eb4.png)

- Failure: no targets are hit.
- Hard success: 3 targets are hit.
- Regular success: 1 target is hit.
- Critical success: 6 targets are hit.

To determine which targets are hit, go around the table and ask each person to nominate a target. The GM goes first. Each target can only be hit once with spray and pray-if you run out of targets, the rest of the shots miss.

- Each NPC target that is hit suffers 1 damage + base weapon damage for that firearm.
- Each investigator that is hit should make a Luck roll: a success means they are lucky and suffer only 1 damage; whereas with a fail, they suffer 1 damage + base weapon damage for that firearm.

## Concentrated Fire

As the name suggests, concentrated fire focuses the gunfire on a tighter target area. The shooter has a choice: either target just one individual (central target) or target that individual and one target on either side of them (group target)-these two  additional  targets  must  be  within  arm's  reach  of  the central target, i.e., the whole group must be within a 2 metre/ yard-wide zone.

The difficulty level is set as for a normal firearms attack and any  relevant  modifiers  are  applied  (except  for  the  multiple shots rule). Using concentrated fire gains a bonus die to the shooter's  Firearms  roll,  which  cancels  out  the  penalty  die imposed  for  auto  fire.  This  means  the  shooter  rolls  their Firearms skill with only the modifiers for point-blank range, target  speed,  etc.,  affecting  the  roll,  if  applicable.  If  the shooter  ends  up  with  more  than  one  bonus  or  penalty  die, refer to Multiple Bonus or Penalty Dice? (page 143).

If  the  attack  is  successful,  the  shooter  makes  a  separate damage roll for each target. If the attack was concentrated on a single target, double the result of the damage roll before adding base weapon damage, as per the increased damage option  for Multiple  Shots (page  139).  Otherwise,  roll damage for each target hit.

If a larger group of targets is confined into a narrow space, then the number of individuals included in the group target may be increased to incorporate everyone within the 2 metre/ yard  zone.  So,  for  example,  if  the  shooter  is  firing  on  full auto  down  a  packed  Underground  train  carriage,  or  firing through the open back doors of a van filled with passengers, or  shooting into a crowded lift just as the doors open, the GM should increase the number of targets in the group. Also, targeting just one individual in a close group is impossible with automatic fire.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000849_c1656ac20c55c8c855305cb370a7bc9a197ff0b00f2a0d30896aff6db3c83236.png)

## Miscellaneous Firearms Rules

There are three other sets of additional rules the GM may wish to consider for firearms in RoL:RPG .

## Sights and Bracing

A combination of telescopic sights and some form or bracing (such  as  using  a  mounted  weapon,  bipod,  tripod,  or  some other  effective  support)  effectively  doubles  the  standard range when using firearms. If using sights with something like a .303 Lee-Enfield rifle (standard range of 100 metres/ yards) with the weapon stabilised on sandbags, for example, the  difficulty  level  would  not  be  increased  until  the  range exceeded  200  metres/yards.  The  maximum  effective  range is  also  doubled  with  sights.  Sights  are  of  no  benefit  at point-blank range.

## Complete Concealment

If a target remains completely concealed behind an obstacle for the whole round, an attacker may still take shots through the  cover  in  the  hope  of  hitting  their  target  ( Cover  and Concealment , page 138). You don't just have to hide behind something  during  combat,  though-you  can  also  attempt to conceal yourself within an area. The chance of a shooter hitting  a  hiding  target  depends  on  the  size  of  the  area  in which they are taking cover. For example, there is a great deal  of  difference  between  hiding  in  a  small  garden  shed and  hiding  inside  an  aircraft  hangar:  the  former  provides much less cover as there are only so many places you could be located within it, while the latter probably has many locations suitable  for  concealing  oneself,  meaning  the  attacker  has little to use except perhaps their intuition (and a successful Observation roll) to work out where their target is so they can shoot at them.

If the target is concealed within a larger location, the GM has several options, depending on the situation:

- Raise the difficulty level.
- Raise the difficulty level and apply a penalty die.
- Apply a penalty die.
- Only allow a hit with a Critical success-if at all.

The  GM  should  also  remember  to  reduce  any  damage inflicted  with  a  successful  hit  according  to  the  protection afforded by the materials used to construct the hiding spot ( Table 6: Cover Protection , page 138).

331

## Armour Revisited

Some forms of armour provide protection to specific body parts; for example, a helmet protects the head. The standard combat rules do not use hit locations (but see the Optional Hit Locations rules,  following).  In  the  general  rough-andtumble  of  a  fistfight  or  a  fall  from  a  window,  it's  credible that armouring specific parts of the body, such as the head, could reduce the overall damage received, and in the interest of  simplicity  and  the  flow  of  the  game,  no  further  rolls need be made.

However, in the case of a single specific attack, such as being shot, the question arises of whether the shot hit an area protected by armour. In this case, the GM should ask for a Luck roll. If the Luck roll is successful, the armour was hit, and the damage is modified by the armour value. If the Luck roll is failed, the attack hit an unarmoured area. A character wearing armour that protects both head and torso gains a bonus die to their Luck roll for armour protection.

A character wearing all-over body armour or possessing natural armour, e.g., an elephant's skin, does not require a Luck roll-the armour's protection applies in all cases. If a weakness is spotted in an enemy's armour-a vulnerable spot, such as an open mouth or eye-that area may be targeted, and  the  GM  should  set  an  increased  level  of  difficulty  or apply a penalty die for the attempt.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000850_fccdd5718030b3c3ab9b4d8132c6028b6436b2b7473b8b0f8492fe3a101e597c.png)

9

## Optional Hit Locations

Rivers of London: the Roleplaying Game doesn't use hit locations in  the  way  that  you  might  find  in  some  other  roleplaying games, as where someone is hit during combat is dealt with narratively  by  the  GM  and  the  players  in  accordance  with the  characters'  actions  during  the  fight.  However,  if  you would like to bring hit locations into your game, you can use either the Luck roll described previously to determine if an armoured part of the body has been hit, or you can take the result  of  the  damage  roll  and  consult Table 14: Optional Hit Locations .

## Table 14: Optional Hit Locations

Ignore or adjust any results that do not make sense within the context of the combat taking place.

| Damage Roll   | Location   |
|---------------|------------|
| 1-6           | Head       |
| 7-8           | Neck       |
| 9-25          | Chest      |
| 26-27         | Groin      |
| 28-46         | Abdomen    |
| 47-63         | Right leg  |
| 64-80         | Left leg   |
| 81-88         | Right arm  |
| 89-96         | Left arm   |
| 97-98         | Right hand |
| 99-100        | Left hand  |

## Damage Rolls: Alternative Method

Rather than using a damage roll to determine the amount of damage inflicted by a successful attack, the relative levels of success can be used instead. This cuts down on the number of dice being rolled and can speed up combat.

## In the case of an opposed roll:

- When both sides achieve the same level of success (i.e., there's a tie) and the attacker wins the opposed roll because it's their turn in the round, the attack inflicts only 1 damage (no base weapon damage added).

Regular success vs a Weak success, a Hard success vs a Regular success, or a Critical success vs a Hard success.

- When the attacker beats their opponent by 3 levels of success , the attack inflicts 3 damage plus base weapon damage. This could be a Hard success vs a fumble, or a Critical success vs a Weak success.
- When the attacker beats their opponent by 2 levels of success , the attack inflicts 2 damage plus base weapon damage. This could be a Regular success vs a fumble, a Hard success vs a Weak success, or a Critical success vs a Regular success.
- When the attacker beats their opponent by 4 levels of success , the attack inflicts 4 damage plus base weapon damage. This could only be a Critical success vs a fumble.

## In the case of a successful attack made  with  an unopposed roll:

- If the attacker's level of success equals the level of success required to hit the target, the attack inflicts just 1 damage (no base weapon damage added). This could be the attacker rolling a Regular success vs a Regular difficulty level, or a Hard success vs a Hard difficulty level.
- If the attacker achieves 2 levels of success above what was required to hit the target (a Critical success vs a Regular difficulty level), the attack inflicts 2 damage plus base weapon damage.
- If the attacker achieves 1 level of success above what was required to hit the target, the attack inflicts 1 damage plus base weapon damage. This could be the attacker rolling a Hard success vs a Regular difficulty level, or a Critical success vs a Hard difficulty level.

As previously described, 4 damage inflicted in one blow is a mortal wound, while 5 damage inflicted in a single attack is a fatal blow and liable to kill the character ( Wounds and Healing , page 144).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000851_29cae8b44bd85661c6402b325d48b30e227dc4f18c412e619ef993c6667d6ce6.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000852_01c3ae658c17ab90e0c8648635b8e5500b2f5eb1c33c8e0142fed767d6b200fb.png)

- When the attacker beats their opponent by 1 level of success , the attack inflicts 1 damage plus base weapon damage. This could be a Weak success vs a fumble, a

## Wounds and Healing Revisited

The standard rules for dealing with wounds and healing in Chapter 3 detail how damage inflicted during combat affects a  character  and  how  characters  recover  from  their  injuries. The Wounds and Healing section (page 144) also includes a table dealing with damage inflicted by sources other than weapons  ( Table  7:  Other  Forms  of  Damage ,  page  149). This  section  looks  at  two  aspects  of  wounds  and  healing in  greater  detail:  resisting  incapacitation  (following)  and Poisons (page 333).

## Spending Luck to Resist Incapacitation

Normally, a character that suffers 3 or more damage during combat  is Down (page  144)-they  are,  to  all  intents  and purposes, incapacitated and can take no further part in the fight. However, there is a way for them to get up and back in the action, should they really want to do so. Any character who wants to resist the effects of being down can spend Luck to remain active for a few extra rounds.

- Remaining active until the end of the current round costs 5 points of Luck.
- A character can continue to act in each subsequent round by spending an additional 5 points of Luck at the start of each round.

Getting  back  up  again  is  a  mixed  blessing,  as  an  active character  may  present  a  more  attractive  target  for  further attacks  due  to  their  obviously  weakened  state.  If  they  do suffer  further  damage,  they  are  immediately  down  on  the ground again and can no longer resist it. They'll just have to wait until the fight is over.

A  character  who  has  suffered  a Mortal  Wound (page 146) can also spend Luck points to resist their injuries in the same way, although it is up to the GM whether they can do anything other than call for help.

Example: Nafeesa  has  been  shot,  taking  4  damage  (a  mortal wound), and collapses to the ground, where her attacker leaves her for dead. She automatically falls prone, and her player marks the Down box on the character sheet; they then spend 5 points of Luck so Nafeesa can remain active until the end of the present round, giving Nafeesa enough time to call for help on her mobile phone.

333

## Poisons

Table 7: Other Forms of Damage (page  149)  covers  mild, strong, and lethal poisons in a simplified manner. While this is likely to be all you need in the game, if you wish to focus on a specific type of poison and its effects, you may wish to use this more detailed system instead.

All poisons are divided into four bands: very mild, mild, strong, and lethal. The bands indicate the deadliness of the poison and assume a single dose-application, draft, bite, or sting, depending on how the poison is administered.

- If multiple doses are received, the victim adds a penalty die to their CON roll to resist its effects.

Individual poisons cause a range of symptoms, including stomach pains, vomiting, diarrhoea, chills, sweats, cramps, jaundice,  altered  heart  rate,  impaired  vision,  convulsions, unconsciousness, and paralysis. It's up to the GM whether the symptoms permit the victim to act or not, or to act with a penalty die or an increased level of difficulty.

Alongside  the  various symptoms,  poisons  also  cause physical  damage.  If  an  investigator  is  poisoned,  the  GM should ask their player to make a CON roll.

- If the CON roll is successful, the severity of the damage is reduced by moving the strength of the poison down one level. For example, with a successful CON roll, a lethal poison is now treated as a strong poison in terms of the damage it inflicts. If the victim achieves a Hard success with their CON roll, the poison moves down two levels, e.g., from lethal to mild. The GM may allow a poison's effects to be shaken off altogether if the CON roll is a Critical success.

If the CON roll is failed, they incur they following damage:

- Very mild poisons: no damage, only temporary unconsciousness (1D6 minutes or rounds, as appropriate).
- Strong poisons: 4 damage ( Mortal Wound , page 146).
- Mild poisons: 2 damage; recovery takes around 24 hours (akin to food poisoning).
- Lethal poisons: make another CON roll. If failed, death is immediate, whereas with a success the victim suffers a Mortal Wound (page 146).

The speed at which the poison acts is determined by the administration  method  and  GM  discretion.  Fast-acting poisons  begin  to  work  within  a  single  round-death  from cyanide  poisoning  could  be  expected  within  a  minute,  for example.  Slower  poisons  may  take  hours  or  even  days  to register their effects.

9

335

9

## Writing a Case File

Now that we've addressed the additional rules for expanding the  game,  let's  turn  our  attention  to  tips  and  advice  for designing your own case files.

## Using the Source Material

Finding plots for your own case files may be easier than you think.  If  your  players  have  not  read  the Rivers  of  London novels  and  comics,  and  you  can  trust  them  not  to  at  your request, you can simply transpose some of those plots into your game setting, as the Peter Grant stories are, of course, the primary source of inspiration for this game.

However, trying to run the ongoing plot from the novels as  a  campaign  is  not  recommended.  Narrative  fiction  and interactive  games  have  different  plot  rules  and  provide different sorts of satisfaction, so what works in one may not necessarily work in the other. In the novels, Peter Grant is prone to making huge intuitive leaps that your players are highly unlikely to be able to replicate. He often finds himself randomly  in  the  right  place  at  the  right  time.  And  he  is backed up by an ever-increasing number of powerful allies who can provide information out of the blue (often without explaining how it was obtained) and rescue him when he gets into trouble.

If you're going to use a plot from one or more of the novels in your games, you'll need to adapt it. Read through the story and then strip it down to its bare essentials in terms of how the plot progresses (what are the key locations, people, and events). Then decide what are the clues and actions needed to thread the investigation together (what can the investigators learn  in  each  scene  to  move  them  to  the  next).  Work  out which  skill  rolls  are  needed  to  provide  these  clues  to  the players.  Make  your  players work for  the  revelations  they uncover-just not so hard that their investigators can't figure out what's going on. Make sure there are at least two or three ways to learn a clue (and such clues don't necessarily have to be found in the same place). And be very wary of moments where the novel handwaves over awkward metaphysical twists, bends its own magical rules, or pulls previously unforeseen information, characters, or powers out of the bag to save the day. A novelist can get away with this because they are in full control of the narrative, but it's much more difficult for a GM who is facing a group of analytically inclined players across the  table.  Thus,  your  version  of  a  story  needs  to  progress logically, with the investigators centre stage working out the clues and using them to decide their next actions.

top of that to introduce people to RoL:RPG by adding new story elements and rules explanations. Another writer might take it in a completely different direction, so this is where you get to make your own creative mark.

Case files adapted from the following stories would all run for one or two sessions each, could be played in any order, and involve little politics:

- 'A Rare Book of Cunning Device' (short story)something scuttling about in the British Library 14 .
- The Furthest Station (novella)-hunting ghosts on the Metropolitan line of the London Underground.
- 'The Loneliness of the Long-Distance Granny' (short story)-a car breakdown at night 14 .
- Body Work (graphic novel)-a malevolent cursed car, physical danger.
- Detective Stories (graphic novel)-four short cases with a helpful emphasis on police procedure.
- Black Mould (graphic novel)-a vengeful fungal haunting, physical danger.

Note  that  'A  Rare  Book  of  Cunning  Device'  and  the short  story  'King  of  the  Rats' 14 are  very  open-ended  as written, with much of the mystery left unsolved or the wider implications unexplored. They might make excellent starting points  for  you  to  write  your  own  solutions  and  introduce your own revelations, or as a springboard into a larger plot line.  And  don't  forget,  taking  the  book  for  walks  is  part of  the  duties  of  those  who  work  for  the  Folly  ( Chapter 7: Basement , page 264).

## GM ADVICE

Back when the rules system for  RoL:RPG was still being developed, both 'A Rare Book of Cunning Device' and 'The Cockpit' were used as demonstration scenarios, so we know they adapt well and that players can have fun  with  them,  even  if  they  know  the  story.  'The Cockpit' 14   was  later  developed  and  transformed into The  Bookshop (page  285),  the  introductory scenario in this book.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000854_9bec57b3fa84c6caeea803138d86d68271d77162170622c08a1d2944e78b8c04.png)

## Devising Your Own Case Files

The short stories are much easier to convert than the full novels.  The  solo  adventure  included  in  this  volume , The Domestic (page 17), is an adaption of a short story of the same name 14 . Comparison of the two shows that they are not identical-the short story provides the inspiration  and  the structure for the game, but the scenario writer has built on

Footnote: 14 Tales from the Folly

If your player group are huge fans who have read every Rivers of London story already, you will have to create your own case files, but don't worry, you don't have to be Agatha Christie! In fact, go ahead and make use of a Christie plot-she's not accoladed as the 'Queen of Crime' for nothing! If you are writing a case file for a private group of player friends, there are no copyright restrictions upon you, so feel free to borrow whatever inspiration you want from TV shows, movies, crime novels, or urban fantasy fiction. There are a ton of 'Nordic Noir' crime dramas available on various television channels,

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000855_5531b2a9a3e779127d4e79b3d30f5c39fb0bcf8081ebebaf0902837ae865f555.png)

or you could go further afield and discover the great range of  subtitled  international  crime  fiction  from  India,  Korea, China, South America, and so on. Change the names and the scenery (and some incidents if there is a danger of your players  recognising  the  plot;  you  don't  want  to  spoil  the surprises for them). And, of course, bring on the magic.

## The Antagonist

He  was  standing  at  the  far  end  of  the  roof  garden,  leaning nonchalantly against the railing. He was dressed in a beautifully tailored  dark  suit,  a  pale  silk  cravat,  and  was  carrying  a  cane topped with a mother-of-pearl handle.

-Moon Over Soho

When  writing  a  case file, start with your antagonist. Somebody (or Something) has committed a crime such as murder-or at the very least done something disruptive and unusual  enough  to  get  the  Folly's  attention.  Who  or  what is  that  antagonist?  What  are  they  trying  to  achieve?  How has this resulted in the hook or inciting incident, e.g., the discovery of a body?

Not all antagonists are villains. Some are just doing their own  supernatural  thing  when  the  investigators  decide  to stick  their  noses  in-the  Quiet  People  in Whispers  Under Ground are an example. Some aren't aware that they are doing anything wrong or have so little sentience (or a sentience so far  beyond  comprehension)  that  it's  impossible  to  ascribe them moral culpability. Ghosts, for example, might be scary and  disruptive,  but  many  are  only  the  bare  remnants  of human personalities, and our heroes' motive in finding their bones and laying them to rest is not to bring them to justice, but  simply  to  restore  peace.  The  'poltergeist'  in  the  short story 'The Cockpit ' is the magical equivalent of a confused animal and needs soothing rather than defeating.

NPC motivation matters. Remember the adage: ' Everyone is the hero of their own story .' Whatever a sentient antagonist has  done,  they  have  a  reason  in  their  own  mind  for doing it-such as:

- They were hungry or had some other compelling appetite that needed sating.
- They were trying to protect something, someone, or some community, such as their family.
- It was self-defence.
- They were forced by a higher power.
- They were trying to teach someone a lesson.
- They were trying to protect their financial interests or place in the world.
- They were out for what they see as justifiable revenge on an individual or a group.

Unless the excuse is, ' I  was drunk '  or  ' I  was high ,'  their crime  will  not  be  random,  and  random  crimes  make  for poor  investigative  plots  anyway.  The  whole  discipline  of criminal psychology is built (at least in fiction) around the investigator's ability to empathise with the perpetrator to solve the crime. And antagonists who do bad things for reasons that investigators can understand, however grudgingly, are antagonists that are memorable and have depth. You don't have to agree with or exonerate a perpetrator to see how they came to commit their crime.

## The Hook Scene

At  twenty-three  minutes  past  eleven  Robert  Weil  drove  his  53 registered  Volvo  V70  across  the  bridge  that  links  Pease  Pottage, the  improbably named English village, with Pease Pottage, the motorway  service  station.  We  know  the  exact  time  because  the Highway Agency cameras picked him up at this point.

-Broken Homes

The hook scene is your next most important touchstone after you  have  decided  upon  your  antagonist.  This  is  where  the investigators are introduced to the case. There are different types of hook:

- An investigator may run into something supernatural, such as a ghost, and decide to investigate on their own initiative.
- Someone known personally to an investigator-such as a friend or relative-who is aware of their interest in weird stuff may ask for help.
- An NPC stranger may ask for help from either an investigator, DCI Nightingale, or the Folly as a whole.
- Most commonly, the Folly is brought in on an official basis by some other branch of the Met because a crime has been committed.

One option is to bring the players in gently:

Example: ' You  are  all  present  at  the  Folly  when  a  call  comes through  that  a  body  has  been  found  in  the  British  Museum.  It looks like a Falcon case, and DCI Nightingale asks that you attend the crime scene straight away…'

This  gives  less  confident  players  time  to  collect  their thoughts  and  their  equipment,  ask  questions  like  ' Which borough does this fall under? ', ' Who is the Senior Investigating Officer  on  the  case? '  etc.,  and  generally  get  into  character. Alternatively,  you  can  follow  the  grand  tradition  of  crime drama and just drop them into the opening scene, leaving them  to  establish  the  context  later.  This  is  more  dramatic and atmospheric, but might be too far outside your players' comfort zone:

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000856_3d34650fd0165c6bd812b129a4e0db7cfe92d12bcdf0413767510f96f67b5a83.png)

- They were trying to effect much needed (as they see it) change upon society.

9

Example: 'Room 41 of the British Museum, 'The Sir Paul and Lady  Ruddock  Gallery,'  has  been  closed  off  to  the  public  with blue and white police tape. As soon as you step past the museum guards into the room, you can see the problem: there's a glass case in the centre of the room that has been smashed, and the white plinth within stands empty. Broken glass is strewn on the wooden floor, but it doesn't obscure the large circular daub on the polished floorboards that appears to have been drawn in blood. Scene of Crime Officers are shuffling around in their white protective suits. DCI Hargreaves turns and looks at you, and you can tell from his  expression  that  he's  none  too  pleased  to  have  a  Falcon  case on his patch.'

Either  way,  your  opening  or  'hook'  is  important.  It establishes atmosphere. It introduces the parameters of the case, relationships with NPCs, the perceived modus operandi and threat level of their antagonist, and the initial cache of clues the investigators need to sort through. It can take some time to play out as the players ask questions, so you need to be familiar with every aspect of it.

If the regular police have called in the Folly, what makes them think that this is a Falcon-type case? What clues has the  inciting  incident  provided?  These  could  be  mundane or  magical-or  both!  Research  on  several  fronts  will  be necessary,  and  specialist  NPCs,  such  as  pathologists,  may need to be contacted. For more information on crime scenes, types  of  evidence,  and  available  specialists,  see Criminal Investigations (page 202).

As the GM, it's a good idea to write yourself a flowchart of clues-where they can be found and how they lead to the next clue or piece of information. Your notes become your memory aide when running the scenario. Alternatively, or in addition, you might want to create a relationship map or plot progression diagram, such as the ones in Chapters 8 ,  that shows who the main NPCs are and how they are all related to each other and the events of the case.

## Further Scenes

The bulk of the case file is likely to be a series of investigative scenes  of  one  sort  of  another.  NPCs,  both  helpful  and hindering, will be encountered. New places will be explored. As  you  will  be  painting  a  verbal  picture  for  your  players, make  sure  you  have  included  enough  detail  in  your  notes when you write these scenes to see you through. Exactly how much detail that is differs from GM to GM; some manage quite  happily  with  simple  bullet  points  of  key  descriptive words, while others prefer detailed notes. Experiment to find

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000857_975d1bbe1009e1e9c77c805fa31d6134962b20c8a8dc8772bd0c79f3754bdfea.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000858_069ebef6a11a77eb9b290798b9bba684725fcc8a7218a229f11ad1891cb1af89.png)

out which works best for you, and don't be afraid to make something up on the spot if a player asks a question you didn't anticipate-just  remember  to  write  it  down  somewhere  in case it becomes important again later.

Bear in mind that investigators may encounter the scenes in  a  different  order  to  the  way  you  imagined,  particularly when  interviewing  witnesses  and  suspects,  so  make  sure that  the  plot  is  flexible  enough  to  cope  with  that,  as  an investigative game is rarely completely linear. Don't be afraid to move clues about to make sure the investigators find them, so that the plot and story you are creating with them doesn't stall or find itself at a dead end with none of the players sure of what to do or where to go next.

Your investigator group is likely to split up at some point to investigate different leads. Make sure that your clues can be relayed back to the rest of the team-it's no good having a  really  important  clue  written  in  Latin  graffiti  on  a  wall if  none  of  the  investigators  there  have  Language  (Latin), so make sure that you make its presence stand out enough that they'll either try their Luck ( Trying Your Luck ,  page 114) or take a photo and show it to someone (like another investigator or NPC) who can read Latin. In these days of mobile  communication,  maintaining  contact  and  sharing clues even when the investigators are in different locations shouldn't  be  an  issue-as  long  as  they  have  signal  ( Luck Rolls ,  page  113)  or  the  investigators'  phones  haven't  been sanded ( Spells and Technology , page 176)!

## What Will Your Antagonist Do Next?

Once  they  have  created  the  hook  scene  by,  for  example, dumping a body somewhere, what is the antagonist's  next move? Are they aware that the police are on their case  or oblivious? Will they try to hide their tracks, flee, or go on the offensive? Are they watching the investigators? Will they change their plans based on the investigators' actions?

Remember,  the  best  antagonists  don't  just  sit  around twiddling their thumbs waiting for the investigators to catch them. They have their own agenda and they're going to follow it. Having said that, be flexible when it comes to your NPCs' plans,  so  if  the  investigators  go  off-script,  their  antagonist can also improvise in an attempt to get around the obstacles the investigators have created for them.

## Clues and Notes

One  of the most  important pieces of advice we  can give you is this:

## Keep clear notes on everything

This applies both when writing a case file and when playing the game, as mentioned previously . Every time you introduce a character or location, and every time you hand out a clue, you need to be able to retrieve that information for your own use  and  for  the  players.  As  you  go  through  your  scenario notes, tick off those clues that have passed into the players' hands,  so  you  can  be  sure  they  have  all  the  pieces  of  the jigsaw puzzle. Even if you make up incidental details on the fly (' Her name is, er, Jane McLibrarian '), write it down .

- Clues should be flagged up more obviously in an RoL:RPG case file than in crime fiction, where the author is frequently trying to mislead the reader with red herrings, delay a revelation for dramatic effect, or establish their protagonist as incredibly clever or perceptive.
- If a clue is absolutely vital to your ongoing plot, don't hide it behind a firewall of dice rolls that the players could fail. For important and essential clues (i.e., ones the investigators absolutely must get) it is enough for them to say ' I search the room ,' without them having to succeed at an Observation roll-though you could use a roll to establish which investigator finds it or how long it takes them to do so.
- Clues should also be far more obvious than in real lifewhich is why you can wrap a case file up in two to three hours rather than two to three months.

## Concluding the Case

So, your players have worked out whodunnit, why they dunnit, and  how  they  dunnit.  Does  the  antagonist  need  chasing down  or  luring  out?  Will  the  antagonist  resist?  Are  the players going to take the decision to have their investigators walk away? The concluding scene is a good time to bring in a dramatic setting or dramatic emotions. It can also be a good point at which to introduce some physical or magical threat to the investigators, but at a level they can handle. While not realistic-most real-life arrests are non-violent and routineit is very much part of the crime fiction genre to end a case with a big perilous 'bang,' and this should leave your players feeling more satisfied.

## Research Your Folklore

You don't have to stick to the paranormal creatures detailed in this book. RoL:RPG takes place in a world where magic is  universal  but  manifests  differently  in  different  countries, so you are at liberty to integrate other mythologies into your version  of  London-or  wherever  you  choose  to  set  your game. The diverse multicultural nature of the UK's capital city makes for great supernatural possibilities but try to avoid the pitfall of treating ethnic minorities-even implicitly-as 'spooky  foreigners'  or  their  cultural  entities  as  'dangerous immigrant monster of the week.' The book series deliberately eschews this trope in favour of invented monsters such as jazz vampires and ancient local spirits like Mr Punch. If you are

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000859_fbc375a32eb4ba2542defa87126af4293b6ba8ede8977b96de7d0e1d0db699a2.png)

9

going to use overseas folklore as an element in your storiesif you want yokai haunting a sushi bar or a bruja operating out of The London School of Hygiene &amp; Tropical Medicine-as always, do so with respect and care.

## Use the London Setting

It  was  your  typical  Notting  Hill  mews,  a  cobbled  cul-de-sac lined with what used to be the coach-houses of the wealthy, now converted into houses and flats. It was the sort of place that gay cabinet ministers used to stash their boyfriends back when that sort of thing would have caused a scandal. These days it was probably full of bankers and the children of bankers. -Whispers Under Ground

The Rivers  of  London novels  are  deeply  tied  to  place.  The history,  mythology,  and  geography  of  a  particular  setting don't just provide flavour text to the game, they inform and help to shape the plot. There can be no RoL:RPG case file without at least some reference to locale. It's assumed that most GMs will choose to use London as a setting just because of  the  wealth  of  material  available  in  Ben's  novels,  but,  if you set your game elsewhere-Liverpool, say, or some other country such as the USA, or simply outside London in the British  countryside  (as  in Foxglove Summer )-we urge you to invoke the same depth of background and use appropriate descriptions (sights, sounds, and smells) to conjure the setting in the players' minds.

Research your setting. Use it . In any long-settled area, real life can probably provide you with more plot inspiration than you can squeeze into any single case file. It's a funny old world, and you might be surprised at the strangeness you find with just a little digging through the internet or local guidebooks, and how little you need to do to adapt it into a good plot.

But,  just  as  a  counterbalance,  don't  be  unnecessarily restricted  by  reality-this  is  an  urban  fantasy  game,  after all!  An  entirely  fictional  housing  complex,  the  Skygarden Estate, was inserted into London for the novel Broken Homes , because it had to have a specific form to meet its plot function. There are a number of important resources available to the 21 st -century GM, most of them online and completely free.

- Local government websites: every local authority in the UK (borough/town/city) has an official website. These largely consist of dry bullet points about who to contact for help with various problems, but a poke around might provide useful local information. Online reviews also exist for each council and can give an unexpurgated snapshot of what local people think of their council.
- Interior plans: many public or historical buildings have plans available online, and many galleries and museums have video tours or 3D virtual tours. If you are setting a scene in the Anglo-Saxon Gallery of the British Museum, for example, it's easy to access a detailed map of the building and get the location right-it adds immeasurably to atmosphere for your players if you can describe their walk through and allows them to search for clues in logical places if they have a map to work from.

stations-are the Metro (a morning tabloid, broadly irreverent to the establishment) and the Evening Standard (a much more conservative and pro-establishment evening paper). There are many smaller local newspapers. All have online versions which can be accessed for free.

- Street maps: as the GM, online map platforms are your best friend, especially the ones that include a 'street view' option. If you are setting a scene in, say, a big house that backs onto a park in London, don't just describe it as 'a big house that backs onto a park.' Find yourself an actual, suitable location using the online satellite map, and then look at the street view. Check out the area in glorious 3D. Are the gardens large or small, or just the last resting sites of decaying mattresses? Is your chosen house old or modern? Look at the type of cars parked on the road. Is the corner shop a genteel wine shop-delicatessen, a Polish mini-mart, or an off-licence (liquor store) clad in metal security shutters? Is there a river or stream nearby that comes under the purview of one of the genii locorum ? Does that park have historical provenance as a highwayman-haunted heath? Are there any historical ruins nearby? Once you have a street name, you can find the postcode online (formatted as, for example, SW1A 2WH)-and that is your key to various websites for house-buyers that provide demographic information, house prices, crime levels, and example house plans for the street (very useful as play aids for raids and searches!). You can also search the history of the locality (borough/ parish/village) for more inspiration.
- Local newspapers: the two newspapers that every Londoner can pick up for free at stands on the street-but especially at public transport hubs like Underground
- The London Underground: if you are using the London setting, you almost certainly need a diagram of the 'Tube' system-the London Underground. Peter Grant uses a car because he's a police officer-his obsession with routes and traffic conditions is a British peculiarity. But civilians are much more likely to use public transport or hail a ride using an online app, because it's cheaper, faster, and they won't be struggling to find somewhere to park once they arrive. Only a little over 50 percent of London households have their own car-far fewer than in Britain as a whole-and younger, more central Londoners are even less likely to keep a private vehicle larger than a bicycle.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000860_3fc04e8f9de063b8e2bc0cde304caa23823adfb12916b4a7b4e6338803b1954b.png)

- Online review sites: official websites showcase beautiful professional photographs of places as the curators want you to see them. Independent online review sites, on the other hand, collects tourist photos and comments by the general public, which may give you a different perspective.
- Weird stuff: there's a bunch of websites about weird stuff-you know, ghosts, Fortean events, folklore, and more-that can provide you with inspiration for plots and spooky encounters. Most regions and cities have a rich history of hauntings and so on, so get your search engine working by looking up things like 'Thames+ghosts' or 'Baltimore+folklore' to dig up interesting details about places where you'd like to set your case files.
- Travel guides: the one exception to using the internet for your research is that, if you are not personally familiar with a setting such as London, you will get a more focused and clearer idea of what it's like to be there by reading a professionally written and detailed travel guide from a specialist publisher-particularly the kind that emphasises a 'warts and all' approach. A good travel guide should give you an idea of what it's like using public transport, how people behave, what sort of food is available in different areas, how the streets change at different times of the day, and what sort of trouble/crime a stranger is likely to encounter. They also provide historical summaries, highlight interesting oddities of local history, and maps of buildings such as museums. And you don't necessarily need the most up-to-date version, either: older editions are just as useful, and a lot cheaper to buy.

## Writing a Campaign

When we use the word 'campaign,' what we mean is a series of case files with a linking theme, and, for full satisfaction, a plot arc. To frame it in terms of the novels, each book, for example, constitutes its own 'mini' campaign of interlinked mysteries and challenges, while the first seven novels ( Rivers of London to Lies Sleeping ) form a much longer, more intricate, overarching campaign.

Your RoL:RPG campaign  might  start  off  as  one  or  two self-contained cases, but soon alert players should notice that there  are  connections.  Perhaps  some  apparently  harmless or  random  NPC  starts  cropping  up  repeatedly.  Perhaps clues are dropped about some criminal mastermind lurking in  the  background.  Perhaps  NPC  allies  start  pulling  the investigators into their larger political machinations.

One  useful  tool for writing a campaign  is to plot backwards , i.e., know what your ideal, climactic end scene looks like and then write backwards from that. What do you need to set up that end scene? What pieces of information

341

must the investigators know? What  resources?  What magical objects must they have found? What NPCs must be present-or absent?

## GM ADVICE

In practice, campaigns are usually written from both ends  towards  the  middle,  leaving  enough  room  in that  middle  for  side-plots,  diversions,  and  inspired last-minute additions. But, even if you plot forward for much of the campaign, as soon as you know where and how the final showdown is likely to take place, or  as  soon  as  you  know  the  final  revelation,  check that  plot  backwards  just  to  make  sure  it  all  works logically.  There's  nothing  more  embarrassing  than your players saying, 'But that makes no sense,' and you suddenly realising that they are right…

When you've got that figured out, you can work out which plot clues need dropping into earlier cases and which NPCs you need to introduce and keep alive-or have alternatives to, should the worst happen. It makes the chain of logic that pulls the investigators in so much clearer if you build it link by  link  backwards  from  the  end-and  chains  of  logic  are vital in investigative plots.

It's  a  good  idea  for  your  campaign  to  include  different types of case files to vary the pace and keep the proceedings feeling fresh. Some, for instance, should involve dire physical peril, while others should be more research based. On top of that, some could be spooky, while some could be emotional.

There are plenty of laugh-out-loud moments in the Rivers of  London novels  and  comics,  so  don't  feel  you  have  to  be po-faced or overly dramatic all the time. Some NPCs can be played for humour-think of the lovelorn scrap man and motor mechanic, Thomas Debden, and his attempts to woo Molly 15 . Make others unsettling; for example, meeting genii locorum and other immensely powerful beings should never be a comfortable experience.

Send  the  investigators  into  a  variety  of  different  and memorable settings. If your game is based in London, how about  heading  down  the  sewers  and  up  Big  Ben's  clock tower? Attending a Buckingham Palace garden party or on a  chase  through the middle of the Notting Hill Carnival? Lurking in the vaults of the Bank of England or carrying out surveillance in Smithfield meat market at midnight? Use London's colour and variety, its extremes of bleak deprivation and conspicuous wealth, futuristic ambition, and historical darkness,  to  help  sell  your  story  and  create  a  memorable experience for both you and your players. And that goes for anywhere else you choose to set your game.

Good luck!

EXIT

## Appendix A: Ready - to - Play Investigators

If  you  would  prefer  to  start  playing Rivers of London: the Roleplaying  Game straight  away,  simply  use  these  readymade (pre-generated) investigators. Alternatively, you could use these investigators as examples for your players to help them become familiar with the game's rules before they go on  to  create  characters  of  their  own,  or  simply  use  them as inspiration. Note that two of the investigators have the Magical advantage while the others are proficient in other

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000861_ffe77421c9b734c8b407804e0ed6c52ae7b9cd311e0778d8a9580e9d0ae256bb.png)

areas-a combination of skills and advantages ensures the players' group has a good balance of expertise and helps to ensure everyone gets to have their moment in the spotlight during each game.

Skills  in  bold  text  are  the  investigator's  starting  skills ( Determine Skills ,  page  52).  Feel  free  to  customise  these profiles  as  you  wish,  altering  the  name,  gender,  backstory, and so on, to best suit your game and your players' preferences.

## Backstory

Your  life  was  never  straightforward.  Your  mother  is  a preacher at an evangelical church in Hammersmith, London, and you always seem to take second place. You're not sure about your father; you've heard a few things about him, but why should you get in touch when he doesn't bother? You left school at 17 and got a job in IT. Then, you married at 19 and were about to enrol for training as a special police constable when you unexpectedly got pregnant. None of that stopped you though, and now you're a full-time police officer and a mum-and if that's not enough, you ended up meeting Peter Grant  and  becoming  a  Newtonian  apprentice!  Of  course, with a husband and young child, you don't live at the Folly, but you do spend a lot of time there.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000869_8de1959f029484392fa77a51db3f9a086a1bc6bbe12ee99ff1bbf11234bc7d8e.png)

## Affluence

Average.

## Spells

Impello , Werelight (mastered) ; Fireball.

## Signare

Blackbird song, the taste of apples, and a tick-tock sound.

## Equipment

Warrant card, mechanical watch, mobile phone, and a grab bag containing police notebook, pens, spare phone battery, Airwave  radio,  torch  (flashlight)  and  spare  batteries,  Folly and  Met  forms,  crime  scene  barrier  tape,  PPE  pouch, waterproof  clothes,  snacks,  bottled  water,  and Blackstone's Crime Investigators' Handbook .

## Equipment

You grew up in Dundee and went to a private boarding school. University wasn't for you, and so you dropped out and got a job in a betting shop. Joey, another staff member, introduced you to a sword-fighting class and you loved it. After a year, the instructor invited you to join a higher level, invite-only group. This group was something else, and it quickly became apparent that they weren't only using swords, but magic as well. One of the senior pupils is also a cop, and that was the spark  that  led  to  your  present  life.  So  now  you're  a  police officer and apprentice living in the Folly.

Warrant card, mechanical watch, mobile phone, and a grab bag containing police notebook, pens, spare phone battery, Airwave  radio,  torch  (flashlight)  and  spare  batteries,  Folly and  Met  forms,  crime  scene  barrier  tape,  PPE  pouch, waterproof  clothes,  snacks,  bottled  water,  and Blackstone's Crime Investigators' Handbook .

## Equipment

Folly Consultant's Card, mobile phone and spare batteries, high  visibility  jacket,  and  a  doctor's  bag  containing  photo ID,  a  prescription  pad,  safety  alarm,  alcohol  wipes,  PPE, alcohol  gel,  sharps  bin,  small  torch  (flashlight),  specimen bottles,  syringes,  dressings  and  surgical  tape,  stethoscope, blood pressure monitor, thermometer, paracetamol (acetaminophen), ibuprofen, penicillin, adrenaline (epinephrine)  auto-injectors,  and  asthma  inhalers  (among other things).

Rugby  was  always  your  game,  but  then  you're  built  for  it. You were never just brawn though, you had brains too. You studied  medicine  in  Manchester,  and  then  followed  your boyfriend to London. You've been together for over ten years. The idea of  being  a  GP  leaves  you  cold;  quietly  talking  to old ladies about their lumbago-no, thank you. You prefer the excitement and chaos of Accident and Emergency. Staff from the Folly questioned you about some curious individuals you treated, and they seemed to warm to you-so much so, that they've called on your help a few times, and the rest, as they say, is history. So now you spend half your time at the hospital and the other half with the team from the Folly.

## Backstory

You grew up in a small town in Kent, the so-called Garden of England, where you attended the local grammar school. After  university,  you  put  down  roots  in  London  and  have stayed there ever since. You found out at a young age that you and your sister were adopted. You've always wondered about contacting your birth mother, but have never quite plucked up the courage to do so. It's funny that you became a history lecturer-it's almost as if you prefer to delve into everyone's history but your own. The 17 th century is your period, with an especial interest in the life of Sir Isaac Newton. Indeed, it was your chosen specialist subject on a well-known TV quiz show, and following that appearance, you were contacted by someone from the Folly. Turns out you have a mutual interest!

## Backstory

The main thing you remember from your childhood is a lot of  shouting.  Your  mother  moved  from  the  Philippines  to London, England, in the 1980s, and married your well-off architect father. They must have been happy once. Your father died  a  few  years  back,  and  his  new  wife  took  most  of  his estate. You still see your mother occasionally, usually when she wants something from you. The rock in your life was your stepsister. She was a few years younger than you, but she had problems no one knew about. You were the one who found her body. You don't have a fixed abode-you get by day-today-and  plans  are  what  other  people  make.  Sometimes, those people from the Folly give you jobs to do-they seem like a good lot, on the whole. At the very least, they're usually good for a hot meal.

## Backstory

You grew up near Leicester in the English Midlands. You did well at school and excelled in music. Then, you moved down to  London  to  study,  and  lodged  with  one  of  your  aunties. Following university, you took a job doing data entry for the Metropolitan Police Service. Yet, music has always been your passion, and you spend your evenings practising and writing songs in your bedroom. So far, you've made some money on the side playing gigs, but you're biding your time until you get your big break. Your auntie doesn't approve of you spending so much time alone in your room-she doesn't think it's healthy for a young woman! Your friend, Morgan Omans, asked for your advice on a music-related case they were working, and that's how you became involved with the Folly.
