## Appendix B: Rules Summaries

A  collection  of  rules  summaries  (cheat  sheets)  for  quick reference while gaming. You may find it useful to copy/print out these summaries so that they are to hand during a game.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000882_a92cdc3a708ee9080bbc1e791f631ff823de6dc233d6912341b3ca6bb88eb9f2.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000883_f9cf465ad962f82fcc7882ad7ca960d6fcd7751c15555ecb54f4eb19032f2d54.png)

## Character Creation

Summary of rules for creating an investigator (full details of character creation can be found in Chapter 1 ).

## Step One: Determine Occupation

Pick an occupation from the Investigator Occupations list on  page  45.  Further  details  about  each  occupation  can  be found in the Occupations section (page 62).

## Step Two: Assign Characteristics

- Distribute 280 characteristic points among: STR, CON, DEX, INT, and POW.
- An average human characteristic value is 50.
- Each characteristic should be assigned a value from 30 to 80. The higher the value, the stronger the characteristic.
- Allocate characteristic values in blocks of 10 (30, 40, etc.) and write them on the character sheet.
- Roll 2D10, add the results together, and then add this total to 50 to generate your Luck value.
- Calculate the half values for each characteristic and write these down, too (e.g., the half value of STR 40 is 20). The 'autocalc' character sheet calculates these half values for you.
- Your default MOV rating is 8 (unless you choose the Speedy advantage in Step Three).
- Choose your investigator's age (page 49) and write it on the character sheet.

## Step Three: Advantages

Choose  two minor or one major advantage for your investigator (page 50).

- Make sure that your investigator has the necessary prerequisites to take that advantage.

- If you choose the Speedy advantage, remember to adjust your MOV rating to 9.

Note: investigators who do not choose the Magical advantage cannot choose Magic as a skill in Step Four.

## Step Four: Determine Skills

- All common and combat skills begin at 30%. All expert skills begin at 00% (zero).
- All the skills required by your occupation.
- Choose a total of six skills; these should include:
- Any skills you wish from your occupation's recommended skills.
- Boost any chosen common or combat skills from their starting value of 30% to 60%.
- Any other skill you wish, whether common, expert, or combat (as long as you have no more than six skills).
- Boost any chosen expert skills from their starting value of 00% to 60%.
- Write the skill values for chosen common and combat skills (60%) in the space provided, then write down their half values (30%). The 'autocalc' character sheet calculates these half values for you.
- If you chose the Magical advantage, boost your Sense Vestigia skill to 60%.
- Write the chosen expert skills in the space provided, along with their full value (60%) and their half value (30%).
- An investigator's own language is a free skill (the same value as their INT or 60%, whichever is higher). If from a bilingual family, they also receive their second language at the same value (page 52). Write language skills in the space provided for expert skills, and add their full and half values.

## Magic

- If you chose the Magical advantage and the Magic skill, and your investigator is a Newtonian practitioner, you start the game with Werelight as one of your first order spells.
- Check the box next to Werelight to show it is a mastered spell ( Mastering a Spell , page 171).
- Pick one other first order spell and one second order spell from the Spell List (page 179).
- If you chose the Magical advantage and the Magic skill, and your investigator is a hedge wizard, choose two first order spells and one second order spell from the Spell List (page 179).
- Mark one of your first order spells as mastered ( Mastering a Spell , page 171).
- Divide your POW characteristic by 5 to determine your investigator's base number of magic points, then add 1 for your mastered spell.
- Choose two signare to keep and discard the third. Or, just create your own signare .
- Look at Table 10: Signare (page 165) and choose one element from each column. Alternatively, you can roll 1D100 three times (one for each column).
- If your investigator is being trained by a character with a known signare , you should incorporate one aspect from that into your own as a third element.

## Step Five: Create a Backstory

- Answer the questions about your Childhood (page 56) and The Present (page 57), then write these answers in the spaces provided.
- Next, determine their Affluence Rating (page 57) and write it in the space provided.
- Determine if your investigator has had a supernatural encounter ( Brush with the Supernatural (Optional) , page 57). If so, write down the details.
- Finally, write down the names of one or more friends or family members on whom your investigator can rely. This is the final step of creating your backstory.
- If you took the Connected advantage (page 50) during Step Three: Advantages , you can add names here, too-although you can wait until later if you haven't worked out who they are yet.

## Step Six: Equip the Character

- Work out what equipment your investigator would be able to access as part of their occupation (page 59) and write it on the character sheet. Any weapons the investigator has should be written down in the Weapons box.
- The investigator may purchase any other equipment they need with the GM's permission, as long as it fits within their Living Standard ( Table 2: Living Standards , page 58).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000884_3b168d62c34b112384d67872a52b3109c9f1b2716853126584c1a9860ebfb695.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000885_92b165b2f0e4ef8d935153651d735c158c078d586d4b6c96bf577b13bddc74c7.png)

B

## Core Rules

Rules  summaries  for  skill  rolls,  pushing  rolls,  spending Luck, opposed rolls, bonus/penalty dice, and the investigator development phase.

## Skill Rolls

Only call for a skill roll when the outcome is dramatic. The player  should  state  a  clear  goal  as  to  what  they  want  their investigator to achieve before they roll any dice. If the goal isn't acceptable, the GM should help to rephrase it.

- Critical success: a dice roll of 01 (the best possible result).
- Regular success: the dice roll is equal to or below the character's full skill or characteristic value.
- Hard success: the dice roll is equal to or below half of the character's skill or characteristic value.
- Failure (called a Weak success in opposed rolls) : the dice roll is above the character's skill or characteristic value.
- Fumble: a dice roll of 100 (the worst possible result).

## Levels of Difficulty

- Regular difficulty: a task that would challenge a competent person. The player needs to roll equal to or below their investigator's full skill or characteristic value in order to succeed. If the opponent's skill or characteristic is below 70, the difficulty level is Regular.
- Hard difficulty: a task that would challenge a professional. The player needs to roll equal to or below one-half of their investigator's skill or characteristic value in order to succeed. If the opponent's skill or characteristic is equal to or above 70, the difficulty level is Hard.

## Pushing Skill Rolls

If  an  investigator  fails  a  skill  roll,  they  can  describe  what additional  action  their  investigator  takes  to  roll  the  dice again  for  a  second  more  concerted  attempt.  If  they  fail  a second time, the consequences will be more serious. The GM may wish to foreshadow what those consequences might be before the player rolls their dice again.

## Spending Luck

Luck points can be spent:

- On a one-for-one basis to alter a dice result; e.g., to 01 to achieve a Critical success, to change a Regular success to a Hard success, and so on.
- On combat damage rolls to increase the amount of damage inflicted on a target by improving the level of success achieved on the dice roll-it does not mean
- To prevent an investigator from dying if they suffer a fatal blow in combat ( Damage Conditions , page 133).

that you can spend 1 Luck point to add 1 damage ( The Damage Roll , page 132).

- To reduce the effect of wounds after a fight ( Wounds and Healing , page 144).

Any spent Luck points are deducted from the investigator's current  Luck  value,  which  reduces  the  chance  of  passing future Luck rolls. A player may spend any number of Luck points, up to their current Luck value, on a given roll.

Luck points cannot be spent:

- On Luck rolls.
- To 'buy off' a fumble, i.e., to change a fumble to any other result.
- To alter the result of a pushed roll.
- To alter another player's dice rolls.

## Opposed Rolls

Use  when  two  characters  are  opposing  one  another,  e.g., an  investigator  and  a  non-player  character  (NPC).  Both sides roll against the appropriate skill or characteristic and compare their levels of success to determine the winner:

- A Critical success beats a Hard success.
- A Regular success beats a Weak success.
- A Hard success beats a Regular success.
- A Weak success beats a fumble.

In  the  case  of  a  tie,  the  player  can  spend  Luck  points ( Spending Luck , page 113) to improve their level of success to  a  better  one  than  their  opponent's.  If  the  player  doesn't spend  Luck,  the  character  with  the  highest  skill  value wins. If both sides have the same skill value, use a random tiebreaker, e.g., tossing a coin, or let the tie stand. Opposed rolls cannot be pushed.

## Bonus/Penalty Dice

Each skill  roll  may  have  a  maximum  of  one  bonus  or  one penalty die applied to it, at the GM's discretion.

- Bonus die: roll an additional tens die when making a skill roll, i.e., roll two tens dice and one units die. Use the tens die that gives the lowest (best) result to determine if the character succeeds or fails.
- To attempt a skill roll if no one present has the necessary expert skill ( Trying Your Luck , page 114).
- One bonus die cancels out one penalty die, and vice versa.
- Penalty die: roll an additional tens die when making a skill roll, i.e., roll two tens dice and one units die. Use the tens die the gives the highest (worst) result to determine if the character succeeds or fails.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000886_5f041273d7070cb580d63166c2a51dc7f2c3ac429fb508367e2cf2ee3adc24a0.png)

## Investigator Development Phase

Once a case file is complete, the players should work through the following steps to advance their investigators.

- Spend Development Points (page 120) to improve the investigator's skills, buy a new skill, buy a new spell, master a spell, or buy an advantage ( Spending Development Points , page 120).
- Review the investigator's employment status and affluence rating ( Reviewing Employment and Affluence Rating , page 122).
- Review the investigator's backstory to see if there are any elements that can be altered or added with the GM's permission ( Altering an Investigator's Backstory , page 122).
- Recover if an investigator is impaired ( Impairment and Recovery , page 119).
- Undertake Training (page 122) if there is to be an in-game break between case files.

## Combat

Rules summaries for combat, including actions in a round, combat  responses,  ranged  attack  modifiers,  damage  rolls, and vehicle combat.

## Steps in Combat

All fights, whether involving fists, swords, firearms, or spells, are resolved using the same six steps.

- Step 1: Set Your Goal (page 127) : describe what the character is doing (what action are you initiating?).
- Step 3: Dice Rolls (page 130) : usually, both sides roll percentile dice (an opposed roll) to determine who wins. Sometimes, especially for ranged attacks, only the attacker rolls dice (an unopposed roll).
- Step 2: Target Responds (page 127) : the target describes how they intend to respond (how is the target reacting?).
- Step 4: Damage Roll (page 132) : figure out any damage or other effects.
- Step 6: Next Player's Turn: play moves on to the next character (an investigator or NPC), who now starts their turn with Step 1.
- Step 5: Narration (page 133) : the winner describes what happens.

## Actions in a Combat Round

Combat  is  structured  into  rounds.  During  each  combat round, each investigator takes one action (a turn). They may use their action to:

- Attempt to avoid their blow and run away ( Flee , page 130).
- Initiate a melee attack using the Fighting skill.
- Initiate a firearms attack using the Firearms skill.
- Initiate a spell attack using the Magic skill, if the investigator possesses the skill.
- Perform some other action requiring time and perhaps a dice roll, such as picking a lock while others fight. Note that simple actions-like speaking-don't 'use up' a character's turn, while moving anything more than a short distance may ( Distances in Combat , page 127).
- Flee-this may require an opposed roll to break free of the fight, if already engaged in combat.
- Reload a weapon.
- Drawing an easily accessible weapon counts as a 'free' action.

## Which Skill to Roll in Combat?

- If it's a hand-to-hand melee attack, use the Fighting skill.
- If shooting a firearm or a bow, use the Firearms skill.
- If throwing something, use the Athletics skill.
- If casting a spell, use the Magic skill.

## Combat Responses

- Response 1: Do Nothing (unlimited reaction; page 128).
- Response 3: Fight Back (limited to once per combat round; page 129).
- Response 2: Dodge (unlimited reaction; page 128).
- Response 4: Flee (page 130).

## My Options: When Someone Tries to Hit Me?

If someone tries to hit you with a punch or a weapon, such as a knife, you can:

- Take no direct action and hope they don't inflict too much damage when they hit you ( Do Nothing , page 128).
- Attempt to avoid their blow and hit them back ( Fight Back , page 129). You can only fight back once per combat round unless you have the Fast Reactions advantage (page 51).
- Attempt to avoid their blow ( Dodge , page 128).
- Attempt to hit them with a spell before they hit you ( Fight Back , page 129). You can only fight back once per combat round unless you have the Fast Reactions advantage (page 51).
- If you have a firearm in your hand, you can attempt to shoot them before they hit you ( Fight Back , page 129). You can only fight back once per combat round unless you have the Fast Reactions advantage (page 51).
- If the attacker is using a weapon, you could attempt to disarm them using a Combat Manoeuvre (page 134).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000887_b054e2319e8b13f3fb3d7241416c50d87311bbb33c0eb3cac8cb1550cbeb4bf8.png)

This could allow you to knock the weapon to the floor or perhaps wrest possession of it from your opponent.

- Attempt to avoid their blow and move your attacker using a Combat Manoeuvre (page 134). This could be to knock them to the floor or push them away.
- Attempt to avoid their blow and restrain your attacker using a Combat Manoeuvre (page 134).

## My Options: When Someone Shoots at Me?

If being shot at from beyond arm's reach, you can:

- Take no direct action and hope that the shot misses ( Do Nothing , page 128).
- Dive for Cover (page 128).
- Turn and flee, in which case the shooter incurs a penalty die if you have MOV 8 or higher ( Fast-Moving Targets , page 138).
- Attempt to hit them with a spell before they hit you ( Fight Back , page 129). You can only fight back once per combat round unless you have the Fast Reactions advantage (page 51).
- If you have a firearm in your hand, you can attempt to shoot them before they hit you ( Fight Back , page 129). You can only fight back once per combat round unless you have the Fast Reactions advantage (page 51).

If  being  shot  at  from within arm's  reach,  you  can  fight back  using  your  Fighting  skill  to  perform  one  of  the following  options  (remember-you  can  only  fight  back once per combat round unless you have the Fast Reactions advantage; page 51):

- Attempt to inflict harm on the attacker. Use your Fighting skill to oppose your attacker's Firearms roll. If
- the shooter loses the opposed roll, they take damage and have fired and missed.
- Attempt to restrain the attacker. Use your Fighting skill to oppose your attacker's Firearms roll ( Combat Manoeuvre , page 134). This could be to restrain the hand and arm holding the gun (pointing it away from you as it fires) or restrain/knock the shooter and somehow foil their shot.
- Attempt to disarm the attacker. Use your Fighting skill to oppose your attacker's Firearms roll ( Combat Manoeuvre , page 134). This could allow you to knock the firearm to the floor or perhaps wrest possession of it from your opponent.
- Attempt to avoid their shot and move your attacker using a Combat Manoeuvre (page 134). This could be to knock them to the floor, or push them away.

| Modifier              | Effect                              |   Page Reference |
|-----------------------|-------------------------------------|------------------|
| Aiming                | Bonus die                           |              138 |
| Cover and concealment | Penalty die                         |              138 |
| Fast-moving target    | Penalty die                         |              138 |
| Multiple shots        | Bonus die or increased damage       |              139 |
| Point-blank range     | Bonus Die                           |              138 |
| Target size           | Large: bonus die Small: penalty die |              139 |

Table 16: Summary of Ranged Attack Modifiers

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000888_dd1539c193e3498385d77d368ec9632433d5b0e0908e86c665e931d66201ce3a.png)

## My Options: When Someone Casts a Spell at Me?

If being targeted with a spell from beyond arm's reach, you can:

- Take no direct action and hope that the spell fails ( Do Nothing , page 128).
- Dive for Cover (page 128).
- Turn and flee, in which case the caster incurs a penalty die if you have MOV 8 or higher ( Fast-Moving Targets , page 138).
- Attempt to hit them with a spell before they hit you ( Fight Back , page 129). You can only fight back once per combat round unless you have the Fast Reactions advantage (page 51).
- If you have a firearm in your hand, you can attempt to shoot them before they hit you ( Fight Back , page 129). You can only fight back once per combat round unless you have the Fast Reactions advantage (page 51).

If being targeted with a spell from within arm's reach, you can fight back using your Fighting skill to perform one of the following options (remember-you can only fight back once per combat round unless you have the Fast Reactions advantage; page 51):

- Attempt to inflict harm on your attacker. Use your Fighting skill to oppose your attacker's Magic roll. If the attacker loses the opposed roll, they take damage and their spell fails.
- Attempt to restrain the attacker. Use your Fighting skill to oppose your attacker's Magic roll ( Combat Manoeuvre , page 134). This could allow you to restrain the attacker and cause their spell to fail.

## Bonus and Penalty Dice in Combat

Each skill  roll  may  have  a  maximum  of  one  bonus  or  one penalty die applied to it.

- If, overall, an attack gains two or more bonus dice, make the attack roll with one bonus die. If the attack is successful, you use the second bonus die on the damage roll.
- If, overall, an attack incurs two or more penalty dice, make the attack roll with one penalty die. If the attack is successful, you incur the second penalty die on the damage roll.

## The Damage Roll

If an attack is successful, roll percentile dice and compare the result  with  the  attacking  character's  STR  (melee)  or  DEX (firearms/ranged/spells):

- Failed or fumbled: 1 damage (no weapon/spell damage is added).
- Hard success: 2 damage + base weapon/spell damage.
- Regular success: 1 damage + base weapon/spell damage.
- Critical success: 3 damage + base weapon/spell damage.

## Factors Affecting Damage

- If a player rolled a Critical success on their attack roll (or bought one with Luck points), they gain a bonus die to their damage roll.
- The damage roll may incur a penalty die if the successful attack roll originally incurred two or more penalty dice ( Multiple Bonus or Penalty Dice (page 143).
- The damage roll may gain a bonus die if the successful attack roll originally gained two or more bonus dice ( Multiple Bonus or Penalty Dice , page 143).
- If a character has the advantage Damage Bonus (page 51), they add +1 damage to unarmed and melee attack damage, but not to ranged, firearm, or spell attack damage.
- A character wearing regular armour, e.g., a Met stab vest, suffers -1 damage from melee attacks ( Armour , page 135). Damage from firearms and some spells is unaffected.
- If a character has the advantage Natural Toughness (page 51), they suffer -1 damage from unarmed and blunt melee weapon attacks. The damage from knives, bullets, or damage-causing spells, e.g., Fireball (page 183) is not affected.
- A character wearing ballistic armour suffers -2 damage from melee and firearms attacks ( Armour , page 135). Damage from certain types of spell attack is unaffected.
- The final amount of damage inflicted may also be affected by Cover and Concealment (page 138) and/or the Shield spell (page 185).

## Vehicle Combat

Vehicle combat uses the same six steps as regular combat, but drivers  use  their  Drive  skill  in  the  opposed  roll  in Step 3: Dice Rolls (page 130).

## Vehicle Damage

Damage  to  vehicles  is  calculated  based  on  relative  Build ( Table 8: Vehicles , page 159).

- If the vehicle's Build is greater than double the other's, the one with the higher Build is dealt no damage, while the one with the lower Build suffers 3 damage.
- If the vehicles have equal Build values, they both suffer 2 damage.
- If the vehicle's Build is greater than the other's-up to double its Build-the one with the higher Build suffers 1 damage, and the one with the lower Build suffers 2 damage.

The driver of any vehicle suffering damage must make a Control Roll (page 158) or risk crashing the vehicle.

## Vehicle Damage Conditions

Like the damage  suffered by people, vehicle  damage is cumulative.

- When a vehicle takes 1 damage, it is Pranged (still drivable).
- Successful attacks against certain fae characters with an iron weapon gain a bonus die to the damage roll ( The Effects of Cold Iron , page 245).
- If a vehicle takes 2 damage, it is Damaged (still drivable-just!).
- If a vehicle takes 3 damage, it is Wrecked (no longer drivable).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000889_47bd002a317fdb59a8cd9400fe7f0147307f5a82b06eee303b610b7f1b25ec4c.png)

## Table 7: Other Forms of Damage

| Level                                                                                   | Suggested Damage                                                                                                                                                                              | Examples                                                                                                                                                                                         |
|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Minor: a person could survive numerous occurrences of this level.                       | Make a CON roll: if failed, take 1 damage; otherwise, no damage is suffered.                                                                                                                  | Mild acid, breathing smoky atmosphere*, a thrown fist-size rock, falling (per 3 metres/ yards) onto soft swamp.                                                                                  |
| Moderate: it would take a number of occurrences at this level to kill a person.         | 1 damage                                                                                                                                                                                      | Falling (per 3 metres/yards) onto grass, strong acid, breathing water*, exposure to vacuum*, fire (e.g., a burning torch).                                                                       |
| Severe: one or two occurrences at this level would render a person unconscious or dead. | 2 damage                                                                                                                                                                                      | Falling (per 3 metres/yards) onto concrete, fire (flamethrower, running through a burning room), being 6 to 9 metres/yards from an exploding hand grenade or stick of dynamite, a mild poison**. |
| Deadly: the average person has a 50% chance of dying at this level.                     | 3 damage                                                                                                                                                                                      | Hit by a car at 48kph (30mph), being 3 to 6 metres/yards from an exploding hand grenade or stick of dynamite, a strong poison**.                                                                 |
| Terminal: outright death is likely at this level.                                       | 4 damage (probably a Mortal Wound , page 146).                                                                                                                                                | Hit by a speeding car (65+ kph/40+ mph), being within 3 metres/yards of an exploding hand grenade or stick of dynamite, a lethal poison**.                                                       |
| Splat: outright death almost certain at this level.                                     | Make a CON roll: if a success, it's a Mortal Wound (page 146); if a failure, immediate death (but can reduce to a mortal wound if 30 Luck points are spent-see Damage Conditions , page 133). | Being involved in a high-speed head-on collision, being hit by a train.                                                                                                                          |

## Notes

* Asphyxiation  and  Drowning: make  a  CON roll  for  the  character  at  the  start  of  their turn in each round (or each minute if not in combat).  Each  time  the  CON  roll  is  failed, the character suffers 1 damage as long as the threat remains. For Moderate damage (water and vacuum), apply a penalty die to the CON roll after the third round (or after 3 minutes; Bonus Dice and Penalty Dice , page 111).
- ** Poison: always call for a CON roll. If failed, apply the damage as stated. With a successful CON roll, move up one level on Table 7: Other

Forms  of  Damage .  For  example:  with  a  successful CON  roll,  a  lethal  poison  is  treated  as  a  strong poison  (Deadly  rather  than  Terminal);  with  a  Hard success,  move  up  two  levels  on Table  7: Other Forms  of  Damage .  Poisons  may  cause  a  variety  of additional symptoms including: stomach pains, vomiting, diarrhoea, chills, sweats, cramps, jaundice, altered  heart  rate,  impaired  vision,  convulsions, unconsciousness,  and  paralysis-it's  up  to  the  GM whether the symptoms permit the victim to act or not, act with a penalty die, or with an increased level of difficulty. At the GM's discretion, they may allow a poison's effects to be shaken off completely if the CON roll is a Critical success.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000890_3bef5dd962f9868d1b873eb60d48d3efef7fd5373e810cc073e97b97809c479f.png)

## Injuries and Healing

Rules  summaries  covering  impairment,  wounds,  damage conditions, and healing.

## Impairment

Impairment can be caused by mental trauma or by physical injury.

- Suffering from impairment increases an investigator's fumble range.
- While an investigator is impaired, a dice roll of 90 or above for any skill or characteristic roll counts as a fumble-rather than the usual result of 100 ( Fumble , page 107).

## Suffering Impairment

- Mental trauma: if an investigator is in a stressful situation, the GM should call for a Regular POW roll. If the player passes this roll, the investigator is fine, but if the roll is failed, the investigator is impaired and performs an Involuntary Action (page 108).
- Physical injury: if a character is Bloodied (page 144) or Down (page 144) in combat-or if they survive a mortal wound or a fatal blow-they become impaired.

## Recovering from Impairment

If  the  impairment  was  caused  by  mental  trauma , the investigator recovers either:

- At the end of a case file.
- If the player spends 10 Luck points to remove the impairment check mark from their character sheet.
- If they take a break from the case file of three or more days and succeeds at a Regular POW roll. They gain a bonus die to the POW roll if they attend therapy or counselling.

## If the impairment was caused by physical injury:

- Bloodied: the investigator recovers from impairment once the fight is over.
- Mortal wound: if the character survives (a successful Medicine roll), they remain impaired until they fully recover from being Injured (page 147).
- Down: the investigator recovers from impairment at the end of the following day unless the player spends 10 Luck points.

## Damage Conditions

- A character suffering a total of 1 damage is Hurt (page 144).
- A character suffering a total of 3 or more damage is Down (page 144).
- A character suffering a total of 2 damage is Bloodied (page 144).
- A character suffering 4 damage in a single blow (from a spell, a weapon, or a firearm) has taken a Mortal Wound (page 146).
- An investigator suffering 5 or more damage in a single attack (a fatal blow) must spend 30 Luck points immediately, otherwise they are killed outright. If they can spend 30 Luck points, the damage is reduced to 4 (a mortal wound).

## Damage Condition Effects During the Fight

- Hurt: the character is in pain but suffers no mechanical effect.
- Down: the character is incapacitated, i.e., they cannot take any further actions during this fight; they are also impaired.
- Bloodied: the character is impaired ( Impairment , page 118).
- Mortal wound: as for down , but the character needs medical attention within an hour if they are to survive. A successful INT roll extends this to two hours ( First Aid , page 146).

## Healing

The  time  taken  for  wounds  to  recover  depends  on  the investigator's damage condition at the end of the fight.

- Hurt: the investigator recovers once the fight is over.
- Down: the investigator remains hurt until the end of the following day unless they spend 10 Luck points. The investigator also remains impaired until the end of the following day unless they spend a further 10 Luck points.
- Bloodied: the investigator recovers once the fight is over, although they still show physical signs of their encounter, e.g., bruised knuckles, a black eye, a split lip, etc.
- Mortal wound: a successful Medicine roll saves the investigator's life ( Medical Aid , page 147). The skill roll may be subject to a bonus or penalty die, depending on the conditions. If the investigator survives, they must choose two of the following: Injured (page 147), Luck Loss (page 147), Development Loss (page 148), or Disfigurement (page 148).

Damage is cumulative, i.e.,  every  time  a  character  is  dealt damage  in  combat,  it  is  added  to  any  damage  they  have already suffered in the fight so far.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000891_445aae4c4305342e1e023cf65f8dabda54b8967e23d77bdbfc9cd628cc061c09.png)

B

## Chases

Rules summaries for chases.

## Investigators Fleeing

The players use the following four steps when their investigators are running away:

- Step 1: Establish the Location (page 151) : describe where your investigator is headed based on the GM's description of the location.
- Step 3: Track Successes (page 152) : each player keeps a running tally of how many chase successes and failures they have accumulated so far.
- Step 2: Chase Roll (page 151) : the investigator's actions determine whether they're using a burst of speed (DEX) or negotiating obstacles (Athletics) to avoid their pursuers.
- Step 4: Combat or Next Player's Turn: if the investigator decides not to make a ranged attack against their pursuer ( Ranged Attacks Mid-Chase , page 156), play moves on to the next character's turn.

If  a  player  gains  two  chase  successes  before  incurring two  chase  failures ,  their  investigator  wins  the  chase,  and has  outpaced  and  lost  their  pursuer.  The  chase  is  over  and they got away.

If a player incurs two chase failures before gaining two chase  successes ,  their  investigator  loses  the  chase.  Their pursuer  catches  up  with  them  and  can  now,  on  the  GM's next turn, try to grab or attack them. The investigator can continue to flee if the NPC fails to incapacitate or otherwise restrain them. On each subsequent chase round:

- Each time the investigator incurs a chase failure, the NPC stays in range and can attack them again on the GM's turn.
- If the investigator now gains a chase success on two consecutive rounds, they outpace and lose their pursuer. The chase is over and they got away.
- Each time the investigator gains a chase success, they move ahead, out of melee range of their pursuer.

Note: NPCs move automatically during a chase, so the GM does not need to roll dice for them unless they are attempting to attack the investigators.

## Investigators Chasing

Players  use  the  following  four  steps  for  chases  when  their investigators are pursuing someone:

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000892_97ed26a5aaef60b18d84b4da7842b3cb2c8ce447c1f087d035efef96d8df7bce.png)

- Step 1: Establish the Location (page 154) : describe what your investigator is doing based on the GM's description of the location.
- Step 3: Track Successes (page 154) : the player keeps a running tally of how many chase successes and failures they have accumulated so far.
- Step 2: Chase Roll (page 154) : the investigator's actions determine whether they're using a burst of speed (DEX) or negotiating obstacles (Athletics) to catch up with their target(s).
- Step 4: Combat or Next Player's Turn: if the investigator decides not to make a ranged attack ( Ranged Attacks Mid-Chase , page 156), play moves on to the next character.

If  an  investigator  incurs  two  chase  failures  before gaining two chase successes , they lose the chase and are left out of breath, while the NPC runs off out of sight.

If  an  investigator  gains  two  chase  successes  before incurring two chase failures , they catch up with the NPC and can now try to grab or hit them. If the investigator fails to incapacitate or otherwise restrain their target, the NPC can continue to flee. On each subsequent round:

- Each time the investigator incurs a chase failure, the NPC moves out of range.
- If the investigator incurs a chase failure on two consecutive rounds, the investigator loses their quarry and is left out of breath, while the NPC runs off out of sight.
- Each time the investigator gains a chase success, they catch up and can make another attack on the NPC on their next turn.

Note: NPCs move automatically during a chase, so the GM does not need to roll dice for them unless they are attempting to  attack  the  investigators  using  a  ranged  attack  ( Ranged Attacks Mid-Chase , page 156).

## Vehicle Chases

The steps used for vehicle chases are the same as for those on foot, except the investigators use their Drive skill in place of DEX and Athletics during Step 2: Chase Roll (page 151).

## Newtonian Magic

Rules  summaries  for vestigia , signare ,  magic  points,  and spellcasting.

## Detecting Vestigia

The  Sense  Vestigia  skill  can  be  used  to  detect vestigia , depending on its strength.

- Extremely strong: usually no Sense Vestigia skill roll required.
- Weak: a successful Regular Sense Vestigia roll detects the vestigium's main element; a successful Hard Sense Vestigia roll detects all the vestigium's elements.
- Strong: a successful Regular Sense Vestigia roll detects all a vestigium's elements.

## Detecting Signare

The  unique  magical  signature  that  each  practitioner  leaves behind whenever they cast a spell can also be detected using the Sense Vestigia skill.

## Casting Spells

- Each spell requires a certain number of magic points to be spent before it can be cast ( Magic Points and Spells , page 172).
- Various factors affect whether or not the spellcasting roll gains a bonus die or incurs a penalty die ( Table 11: Mastered vs Unmastered Spells , page 174; Table 16: Summary of Ranged Attack Modifiers , page 354).
- It takes one combat round (or the narrative equivalent) to cast a spell-the same amount of time it takes to throw a punch or fire a gun in combat, i.e., it takes up the caster's turn in a combat round ( The Combat Round , page 124).
- The standard rules for Multiple Bonus or Penalty Dice apply to spellcasting rolls (page 143).

## Magic Points

- A character's magic point total is equal to one-fifth of their POW + 1 for each spell they have mastered.
- The base cost of attempting to cast a spell is a number of magic points equal to the spell's order.
- Once a practitioner runs out of magic points, they may continue to cast spells, but such attempts may cause them harm ( Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation , page 177).
- There is no need to track 'negative' magic points, but a practitioner must roll for Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation every time they attempt to cast a spell when they have zero magic points available.

## Spellcasting Rolls

A Magic skill roll must be made when attempting to cast a spell, unless the caster is not pressed for time.

- Regular success or better: the spell works just as it should.
- A fumble: the caster is drained of all magic points and must roll for Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation (page 177).
- A failure: the result is in the hands of the GM; something happens, just not what the caster originally intended ( Failing Spellcasting Rolls , page 174).

As always, Luck can be spent to alter the result of a Magic skill  roll.  Spellcasting  rolls  outside  of  combat  can  also  be pushed, but a second failure means the caster is drained of all  magic  points  and  must  roll  for Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation (page 177).

## Spellcasting in Combat

In combat, spells work in the same manner as firearms. The casting (Magic) roll is used instead of a Firearms roll.

- The damage roll for spells usually uses the caster's DEX to reflect that they are attempting to hit a target. Thus, use DEX rather than POW or STR when consulting the Damage Roll section (page 132).
- The target(s) of a spell during combat have the same response options as if they are being shot at (e.g., Dive for Cover , page 128, or Fight Back , page 129).
- As with a firearms attack, spellcasting rolls in combat are subject to the same bonuses and penalties as firearms ( Ranged Attack Modifiers , page 138).
- Spellcasting rolls in combat cannot be pushed.

## Sensing Someone Casting a Spell

In a scene, when a character declares they are casting a spell, any nearby practitioners should be given the opportunity to make a detection (Magic) roll before the caster makes their spellcasting roll:

- Regular success: the practitioner senses a spell is being prepared, but has no idea which spell is being cast.
- Spent magic points regenerate naturally, returning to their normal maximum value for The Next Scene (page 173).
- The cost of a mastered spell can sometimes be boosted (raised) if the caster wishes to increase or alter its effects ( Boosting a Spell , page 176). Not all spells can be boosted.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000893_ca98f97eb17d8fb4eb1553ecd44884471efad353c1ccaace30904fd3d81cbe1e.png)

B

They may attempt to rapidly cast a spell of their own in response ( Fight Back Using Magic , page 129).

- Hard success: the practitioner senses a spell is being prepared, and, if it's a spell they are familiar with, the GM should tell them its name. If they are not familiar with the spell, some hint of the spell's effects should be given. They may attempt to rapidly cast a spell of their own in response ( Fight Back Using Magic , page 129).

## Hyperthaumaturgical Degradation (HTD)

If a character fumbles a Magic skill roll when casting a spell, fails a pushed spellcasting roll, or continues to cast spells once they have run out of magic points, they must make a POW roll. If they fumble a pushed spellcasting roll, the POW roll incurs a penalty die.

- Critical success: the character's magic points are reduced to zero, but they suffer no damage.
- Regular success: the character's magic points are reduced to zero, and they suffer 2 damage.
- Hard success: the character's magic points are reduced to zero, and they suffer 1 damage.
- Fail: the character's magic points are reduced to zero, and they immediately suffer 4 damage-a Mortal Wound (page 146).
- Fumble: the character's magic points are reduced to zero, and they suffer severe physical trauma (such as a stroke, heart attack, or aneurism). They immediately suffer 5 damage-a fatal blow. If they do not (or cannot) spend 30 points of Luck straight away to reduce the damage to a mortal wound, they die ( Damage Conditions , page 133).

## Demi-Monde Magic

Demi-monde  magic  does  not work the same way as Newtonian magic. It consists of three abilities:

- The Glamour (page 243) : the power to sway someone's mind.
- Thematic power (page 244) : a demi-monde's sphere of influence or affinity grants them certain magical powers.

## The Glamour

When the glamour is used on a regular person who knows nothing of its existence , they are permitted a POW roll:

- Fumble or failure: the target does as they are bid without question, and they don't ever think to question why they acted as they did.
- Critical success: the target is unaffected by the glamour and reacts to the request as if asked by anyone else. They're not aware of anything magical having taken place.
- Regular or Hard success: the target does as they are bid without question, but later wonders what possessed them to act that way. Of course, they soon console themselves with whatever excuse feels the most rational to them.

When the glamour is used on a person who's been  informed  about  it  and  how  to  resist  it ,  they,  too, make a POW roll:

- Fumble: the target behaves exactly as bidden, just as someone who is ignorant of the glamour would.
- Regular, Hard, or Critical success: the target is aware of the use of glamour, but they are not compelled to act upon it.
- Failure: the target begins to act as requested-perhaps taking a few steps or speaking a few words-but then recovers their self-control. They are aware of the use of the glamour.

## Thematic Power

A demi-monde's thematic powers break down into two categories:

- Minor: something which simply illustrates the character's affinity with that element or power. Routine acts don't help to resolve a case file (scenario) or further anyone's agenda, they just add some colour and character to the story.
- Major: an act that somehow furthers the plot, helps to resolve a case file, or provides an in-game benefit.

Any demi-monde NPC can perform minor manifestations of their power whenever they wish, but it's best not to overdo it or they lose their impact.

Any demi-monde NPC can perform one major manifestation of their power once per case file. Performing such acts is demanding, emotionally and energetically, and may also cost them socially.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000894_d562d7ddf15c81012ec368b0c2fdb598a619d53ec97cbd7b38f3cdd98c7dc8bc.png)

- The Luck of the Demi-monde (page 243) : a demimonde's Luck value is equal to their POW and refreshes at the same time every day.

## Handout: Bookshop 1

## Warwick Anderson's Statement

I was working in the basement. It was around midnight, give or take, moving the celebrity chef books to the otherend of the cookery section,when I washit in theback by something. I turned around to find Banksy's Wall and Piece at my feet—the paperback, not the hardback, or else that would have really hurt.

Thing is, I was supposed to be the only one in the shop. There was no sign of anyone who could have thrown it, so I'll admit, I was spooked. I went upstairs to take a look around and make sure no one had broken in, but all the doors were locked, and I couldn't see anyone. I was feeling a bit annoyed by this point, so I gave up looking and wentback toreshelving the books.

Next thing I know,I get hit on thehead by a soft toy,like the oneswekeep at the till points. I was just about to turn around and catch whoever threw it red-handed when a ton of books smashed intome from the art section and flattened me.A couple of the really big Taschen art books caught me in the back of the head, but I managed to stagger to the phone at the downstairs till and dialled999.

It''s all a bit hazy after that,but someone helped me up to the ambulance, where they told me I had a mild concussion.I went home,then I got a call askingme to come back in so I could be interviewed by some special crimes unityou lot, I'm assuming? I told them there was no way I was going within 500 metres of that place, but eventually, they convincedme to meetyou here instead.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000899_8daf5ee229be479ea143c31ef1c913b8da278db418f672eca9d0179031553613.png)

D

Handout: Bookshop 2

## Saffron Jackson's Statement

I got a phone call in the early hours from a police officer asking me to come to the bookshop to take care of the door.They said they'd had to break the glass to get inside to seewhat had happened to poor Warwick. They left shortly after that because it was all kicking off in the piazza; a fight outside a bar, or something-I don't know.

Next,I called the maintenance company to send someone over to board up the door and clean up the glass. Once the maintenance guy got here, I checked around to see if anything was missing. It didn't look like it,but it's a bit hard to tell in all that mess downstairs. I've not touched anything down there because one of the officers told me not to until you'd been to see it.

So,ifyou'd like to followme,I'll takeyou to thebasement.I don'twant totell you how to do your job,but I'd really appreciate it if you could hurry it upI'd like to get everything all squared away againbefore opening time.

Handout: Bookshop 3

## COCKFIGHTING

Cockfighting was once a highly popular—and very lucrative-spectator sport in London and elsewhere in the UK. Historically speaking, its roots go back over 6,ooo years, though it first became really popular in England during the Tudor era. By the 18th century, the blood sport was at its height; with all levels of society indulging in the heavy betting that accompanied each match. Upper class venues, like the Royal Cockpit (whose last remaining traces are Cockpit Steps by Birdcage Walk), charged a hefty admission fee to keep the riffraff out.

It took around two years to fully train a fighting bird. Due to the amount s days, this blood sport was tightly regulated—more so than any other sport at the time. It was finally banned in England and Wales in 1835 under the Cruelty to Animals Act.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000900_3e4f6801119e4719a3673ac903e5fad0ee795f8e10c3eaa116fa0f18e9eee716.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000934_7775da34e7286d108a5b93da0462c8e8a81e0fdefb2432ae2f912661b84d8125.png)

## Ben Aaronovitch

Born  and  raised  in  London,  Ben  had  the  sort  of  unrelentingly uninteresting  childhood  that  drives  a  person  to  drink  or  Science Fiction. The latter proved useful in his early career when he wrote for Doctor Who (before it was fashionable), Casualty , and the cheapest soap opera ever madeJupiter Moon . Alas, his career foundered in the late 1990s and he was forced to go out and work for a living. It was while running the Crime and Science Fiction sections at  the Covent Garden branch of Waterstones that he conceived the notion of writing novels instead. Thus was the Rivers of London series born, and when the first bookRivers of London -proved to be a runaway success,  he  waited  all  of  five  minutes  to  give  up  the  day  job  and return to the bliss that is a full-time writing career. He still lives in the city that he modestly calls 'the capital of the world,' and says he will leave when they prise London from his cold dead fingers. He promises that he is already hard at work on the next Peter Grant novel and not playing computer games-honest.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000935_39a0ed4d08e20c909435cbd945afb4bcda35d80eee8461168c749bc6dab5fa10.png)

## Gary Bedell

Gary has worked as a professional illustrator and designer for over 20  years.  From  the  age  of  three,  he  always  had  a  pencil,  pen,  or anything that he could create with in his hand. By the age of six, he knew that being an artist was his calling.

His  inspiration  comes  from  many  sources.  From  the  look  and eerie feel of Herbie Hancock's 'Rockit' video and visual artists like Katsuhiro  Otomo,  Arthur  Adams,  Koji  Morimoto,  Adam  Duff, Rodney Matthews, and Craig Mullins, to musicians like MF Doom, Thundercat,  DJP,  and  Karriem  Riggins.  But  his  main  inspiration came  from  his  grandparents  and  other  members  of  his  family. They taught him to push, work hard, and never give up, while also nurturing his creativity.

In  his  career,  Gary  has  worked  with  such  companies  as  Crypt TV, Activision, Aftershock Entertainment, Fantasy Flight Games, Disney, and Nickelodeon. He is currently the lead artist for Attack Wagon, creating conceptual art for their project Scrap Guilds .

When he's not working, Gary loves to spend time with his wife and three  kitties,  travelling,  spending  time  on  personal  projects,  DJing, and collecting vintage toys. He also runs an artist organisation called Clever Kaiju: a community where professional and aspiring artists can network, learn from each other, attend their classes and demos, and, in some cases, find employment.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000936_49476d7d295df2f1f83c6938dec9f950d5c0cb5c1f1f8ec925a5827be7ce226e.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000937_eed95885eae692357a8255c6124c5922295fc23358d5a1c1252820a2eb57f1d8.png)

## Irene Caro

Irene, under the pseudonym Araire, is an illustrator who primarily  works  on  scientific  historical  recreations  for  museums. More recently,  she  has  begun  to  produce  art  and  illustrations  for roleplaying  games  and  book  covers,  often  with  a  military  theme. Although,  like  everyone  else,  she  started  off  using  a  traditional pencil, nowadays, she uses exclusively digital painting for her work, incorporating 3D bases and photobashing. You can find her work at artstation.com/araire.

Like any lover of art, she was attracted to the world of illustration as a child but it was not until much later that she devoted herself to learning the profession. Now, she is lucky enough to dedicate herself to  it  entirely,  and  especially  enjoys  being  commissioned  to  create illustrations for fantasy literature, which is one of the main reasons she became interested in art in the first place!

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000938_6b16d2853bd33fbdbae48143ad0699fa5ce03efa6956d445ee60fbbb8dc85cde.png)

## JB Casacop

JB  is  a  professional  freelance  illustrator  who  creates  fantasy  and science fiction art for games. He holds a bachelor's degree in Fine Arts  from  the  University  of  the  Philippines.  After  college,  he explored different career paths, such as advertising and traditional animation,  before  eventually  deciding  to  specialise  in  game  art production.  He  attended  numerous  game  art  workshops,  both  in the Philippines and Singapore, to advance his skills. For the better part  of  the  past  decade,  he's  worked  for  well-known  publishers such  as  Fantasy  Flight  Games,  Atomic  Mass  Games,  Ubisoft Entertainment, Green Ronin, Alderac Entertainment Group, and, most recently, Wizards of the Coast and Chaosium. Popular titles he's illustrated cards for include Warhammer 40K Conquest , A Game of  Thrones Living  Card  Game (1 st and  2 nd edition), The Lord of the Rings LCG , and multiple Star Wars games, as well as several yet-tobe-revealed Magic: the Gathering cards.

JB is a bona fide geek whose hobbies include assembling custom lightsabres,  3D  printing  miniatures  and  cosplay  props,  playing open-world video games, and reading comic books. When he's not busy with these, he spends time learning martial arts such as Arnis, Muay Thai, Tae Kwon Do, and HEMA.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000939_d57fb6fae410465b5312c802afc779f89f686d502c17da4458d8f6d899a6152a.png)

I

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000940_1306318593c877b04f8c9d2d0c271e5dcff1455daba90dca10e65ef21f064d9f.png)

## Caleb Cleveland

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000941_5466f7cddf3c72eb586a86fdaed9ef4f6056cfd65a720287e7013086773a991c.png)

## Simeon Cogswell

Caleb is a freelance illustrator and concept artist based in San Diego, California,  specialising  in  art  direction  and  story  development. Heavily influenced by illustrators like Gustaf Tenggren, Alex Toth, and Frank Frazetta, he's been drawing and painting fantastic monsters and characters since he was old enough to pick up a copy of The Fiend Folio (TSR, Wizards of the Coast). His schooling in theatre design, where he tried to marry illustration and performance, wholly turned to illustration upon graduation. He has since explored his love for costume design, conceptual art, and dramatic storytelling in games, comics, and children books. Currently, he is Art Director at Hunters Entertainment, lending his hand to titles like Kids on Bikes and Alice is Missing , and can often be found instructing artist workshops.

Simeon is a nonbinary geek who started gaming in 1993, when they were given a copy of the Dungeons &amp; Dragons Rules Cyclopedia by their grandfather. Little did he know the kind of effect that would have on a dorky third grader, because almost 30 years later, Simeon now makes these books for a living. Sim's areas of geekery include: RPGs, anime, board games, retro video games, and typography.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000942_a455f54808fafc728fa84093efbf7be7aa5e2c809b13437df5e67b204ff161ae.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000943_6f11190c5df20465e248edd4e3a7cd89398f598719109fb5e8146183eb0c6207.png)

## Alyssa Faden

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000944_36c177c2d37f8b7a4d7c76942cfc8576ca5817e59f413b9bf5172cd9a62584b0.png)

## Paul Fricker

Alyssa has been a professional cartographer since 2012. She is best known  for  her  incredible  cityscapes  and  stunning  details,  often inviting  viewers  of  her  creations  to  see  stories-within-stories  for years  after.  Her  clientele  includes  Monte  Cook  Games,  Kobold Press,  Profantasy  Software,  Gygax  Magazine,  Golden  Goblin Press, and Troll Lord Games. Her style is unique and self-taught, with influences including the lovely ink work of such greats as Jeff Laubenstein and Jeff Easley. She is a long-time tabletop RPG gamer with a passion for game mastering Call of Cthulhu , so when asked to create some maps for Chaosium she did, of course, fall over herself in utter fan girl enthusiasm.

Paul Fricker is a freelance writer and games designer, and lead system designer for Rivers of London: the Roleplaying Game . In partnership with Mike Mason, Paul is best known for writing and revising the latest edition of Chaosium's award-winning RPG, Call of Cthulhu . Over the years, he has participated in the creation and development of numerous scenarios and campaigns, beginning with Gatsby and the Great Race , back in 2005.

Paul is also one of the hosts of the horror and gaming podcast, The Good Friends of Jackson Elias . He often can be found on Twitter (@ PaulFricker), where he endeavors to focus on the important topics in life: gaming, music, and food.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000945_353c154b67555021d7f2f893b0d29cf610a69f391b3c6049a3811185b425d59f.png)

I

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000946_3c39bc0861a0495afe15a3defb4a460f459bfb505468dc0753cd82d50ad27903.png)

## Doruk Golcu

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000947_30ba64e85d2a8868016f6a875836022a6452879668e460a1dab68e9d716fbc4d.png)

## Lloyd Gyan

From a very young age, Doruk wanted to create monsters. To that end, he studied until his eyes bled out of his skull and got degrees in  genetics  and  neurobiology,  only  to  find  that  the  creation  of monsters  was  considered  'unethical'  and  'a  crime  against  nature and humanity' in those fields. In the end, he decided to just draw the monsters instead, and became a fantasy and horror illustrator. He studied at the Art Students League of New York, as well as being mentored by several legends of the fantasy art genre through Smart School. He works mainly in the field of tabletop roleplaying games, with  companies  such  as  Chaosium,  Monte  Cook  Games,  Paizo, Draco Gaming, Epiphany Games, Penny Dragon Games, and many others. When he is not busy drawing and painting in front of his computer,  he  teaches  Argentine  tango,  practices  martial  arts  and fencing, and eats unhealthy amounts of chocolate. He tends to move to  an  entirely  new  location  every  five  years  or  so,  but  is  currently hiding in a small sleepy town in the US Midwest with his lovely wife,  a  small  pack  of  cats,  lots  of  trees,  squirrels,  bunnies,  and probably some cryptids.

From working with Modiphius Entertainment, to being the cultural liaison on RPGs such as Bastion and Liminal , Lloyd Gyan has come to  see  all  that  the  RPG  community has to offer. His unique take and perspective on the world at large brings a fine nuance to setting books-his  specialty,  coming  from  his  many  years  as  a  secondgeneration Ghanaian living in London.

'Peter Grant's magical life has always vibrated with me, being a West African boy living his best life in London. To be given a chance to work on a project as personal as this is a dream come true. Also, I actually know what Star Beer tastes like!'

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000948_5af0577108ef874d51d9f2f9338d216ff8dff6544864c337bb0dc5095c671c93.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000949_9d4045dcaddc8976a29fde66847b8781a429c12de0d4cdf5e8c60efdaa39a67e.png)

## Lynne Hardy

A multi-award-winning writer and editor, Lynne has been working on  and  off  in  the  roleplaying  industry  for  almost  30  years,  most recently  in  her  capacity  as  Associate  Editor  for  Chaosium's Call of  Cthulhu RPG.  Her  past  credits  include  work  for  Modiphius Entertainment, Cubicle 7, Green Ronin, and Pelgrane Press. She also created her own steampunk pulp adventure game: Cogs, Cakes &amp; Swordsticks . Bizarrely, for someone who works predominantly in the horror field, Lynne hasn't watched a lot of horror films as they're far too scary, but she is rather fond of tea, books, and fountain pens, along  with  the  occasional  G&amp;T.  When  she's  not  beavering  away at  the  day  job,  she  gives  talks  and  workshops  on  traditional  hand embroidery techniques.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000950_1d0bc7be1128a0aea522978a0db992885725c6a101a3252c711bdafea6c77d6d.png)

## Gavin Inglis

Gavin  Inglis  is  a  writer  and  teacher  based  in  Edinburgh,  with a  background in tech and music. He writes adventures for Call of Cthulhu ,  including the introductory solo, Alone Against the Flames . Other work includes interactive novels published through Choice of Games, and  credits  on Zombies , Run! and Fallen  London .  His  fiction  has appeared in a magazine for actual conjurers, and his story collection, Crap  Ghosts is  the  definitive  reference  to  ineffective  apparitions. Gavin was Language and Cognition Fellow at the Department for Clinical Neuroscience in Edinburgh, during which he produced a graphic novel about Functional Neurological Disorder, and an AI version of H.P. Lovecraft. His interactive web story about mental health,  Hana Feels,  was  nominated for an XYZZY award. www. gavininglis.com.

Follow her on Twitter (@CogsandCakes).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000951_222ac4ef1dbecafd9d80efeaea155739e79b5ca63132b290abc01054aa7334ec.png)

I

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000952_9669c5bf355e843c026825a90ef263880c8bc57b105306eaf06aba87f9cb5418.png)

## Kurt Komoda

Kurt  was  born  in  New  Jersey  on  May  15,  1970,  interrupting  his brother Paul's 4th birthday. The third son of two working artists, the Komoda household was never short of art supplies and had a near constant feed of sci-fi, kaiju, and horror to inspire and traumatise.

After graduating high school in 1988, Kurt joined the US Navy and served as an Aviation Anti-Submarine Warfare Operator and was a  combat aircrewman onboard Lockheed P-3Cs based out of NAS Moffett Field. When the Cold War ended in 1991, the US apparently didn't need quite so many submarine hunters, so he opted for an earlyout offered by the force reductions and returned to civilian life in 1992.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000953_6a491e80591481ad2272990357bf69885cfe4fc5ee1fb290b2af32bf1c77613a.png)

## Mike Mason

Mike  Mason  is  an  award-winning  game  writer  and  the  creative director  at  Chaosium Inc. for the Call of Cthulhu roleplaying game. Mike is the co-writer of Call of Cthulhu 7 th edition, Pulp Cthulhu , and the lead designer of the Call of Cthulhu Starter Set , Malleus Monstrorum (2 nd edition), and the revised Masks of Nyarlathotep . Traces of Mike's tentacles can be found in every book in the line.

While  working  as  a  flight  coordinator  for  Princeton  Airport, Kurt took various artist gigs, the highlight of which was painting in  the  blood  of  his  friend,  the  legendary  jewellery  designer,  Axel Stocks.  In  2001,  Kurt  started  work  as  a  commercial  artist  at  The Studio NYC, illustrating storyboards, ad comps, book illustration, character design, and anything else that was thrown across his desk. He was laid off from the art studio in 2014 but this turned out to be a blessing, as the transition to 100% freelance was surprisingly smooth and has continued to support him to this day.

Around the millennium, Mike published The Whisperer, a  fanzine devoted  to Call  of  Cthulhu ,  and  ran  the  Kult  of  Keepers,  a  group of Call of Cthulhu scenario writers organizing convention games. Mike previously worked for Games Workshop as the Line Manager and cowriter of the ENnie Award winning Warhammer 40,000 RPG : Dark Heresy , as well as working on Warhammer Fantasy Roleplay . Mike lives in  the  Midlands  of  England,  betwixt  the  rolling  Derbyshire  Peaks and the Satanic Mills of Nottinghamshire, with a grumpy cat called Stripey.

When he's not working, Kurt's hobbies include macro photography, kayaking, origami, fossil hunting, and video games. Kurt has been an avid martial artist since high school and continues to train and teach. He also takes care of Jowday, a feral that won his way inside and is the only good cat in the world.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000954_d0867ab6b6809190b1cef562456b8bb95941cd2b4723bce0aaf0fab03d71bca2.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000955_5aed8f91358184cb7633786067d3bf69e30b5b5e75a7339c229105fd7189302e.png)

## Lucas Marques

Lucas is a Brazilian illustrator with more than 10 years' experience in professional illustration, games, and animation. His passion for art was awoken by Japanese entertainment and pop culture of the 1990s. The anime and tokusatsu creations  of  that  time  still  have  a large influence on his creative process.

From  these  foundations,  Lucas  developed  his  artistic  skills  by studying the fundamentals of his craft and experimenting with new materials and techniques, mixing traditional and digital media to create  fantastical  characters  and  worlds  that  reflect  contemporary elements of pop culture and media.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000956_e6f9318a92282ac0052ad8a2bafe190e0c6414fef47813b66a6384f9201a8e69.png)

## Keris McDonald

Keris McDonald lives in the north of England, discovered Dungeons &amp; Dragons before it was even considered a threat to one's immortal soul,  and  has  been  playing  and  GMing  scenarios  for  Chaosium's Call of Cthulhu for  35  happy years. Her RPG scenarios have been published by Worlds of Cthulhu and The Cthulhu Hack .  With many years  involvement  in  writing  live-action  roleplaying  events,  she  is currently helping run a Victorian Gothic LARP system.

As  an  artist, Lucas  is driven by all the possibilities  and opportunities  his  field  presents  to  him  every  day,  and  he  is always looking for new ways to make his ideas-and those of his clients-come alive!

A writer  of  supernatural  fiction  across  the  horror,  fantasy,  and erotica genres, Keris has nine novels in print and has seen her short stories  published  in  many  magazines  and  anthologies.  Her  short story,  'The  Coat  Off  His  Back,'  was  chosen  for  inclusion  in Best Horror of the Year Vol. 7 (ed. E Datlow).

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000957_4e06cf4bbd82ad736f2cfdf99542d30be42c0f75fc08dcb0b712281889a9daa2.png)

I

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000958_6c8d98d3dd260a3fe8be23dd0ad3295104194122a407a1b601a2192bc5742e5c.png)

## Maurice Mosqua

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000959_af08c4304104e3c08cfad7c445aa8102c0eaa1e791b613918dc933d0467c9d78.png)

## Nicholas Nacario

Maurice  is  a  freelance  artist  and  illustrator  from  Germany.  He started  his  artistic  journey  in  video  games,  creating  landscapes, characters, and assets as a 2D artist. His true passion, though, always lay in creating larger scale artworks and concepts, so he decided to become a full-time freelance illustrator. Now, he mainly works on pen &amp; paper roleplaying games and board games, along with book and album covers. He loves creating atmospheric and moody worlds, places, and their different inhabitants, mainly in medieval fantasy settings. But when the occasion arises, he likes to challenge himself with new genres and themes. So, when the offer came to work on the Rivers of London RPG, he was immediately hooked and ready to go. 'Like all the other artists featured in this book, I put in a lot of work to make Ben's ideas come alive. I hope I've done Ben's vision justice, and I am proud to be a part of this project.'

Nicholas is  an  award-winning  graphic  designer,  art  director, and  part  of  the  creative  team  behind  the Call  of  Cthulhu roleplaying game. His short stories, essays, and Call of Cthulhu scenarios  have  appeared  in  various  fiction  and  roleplaying publications  such  as Corporate  Cthulhu , Destroy  All  Robots , Down Darker Trails , Ill-Considered Expeditions , and Legends of New Orleans . He is originally from the San Francisco Bay Area and now resides in Portland, OR.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000960_531fcaa75e32e7e8edec0c7d390376205163c93c794df2094bfa949aac99604a.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000961_35b151560058299394b430293b55ab40c1437a89570ecdcef3ed1f3fb84f8b28.png)

## Matt Ryan

Matt Ryan got his start in the tabletop roleplaying games industry in  2004,  writing  for  Atlas  Games' Ars  Magica .  In  2013,  he  began drawing maps, and while he still writes occasionally, he now focuses his energy on cartography and graphic design. He has drawn multiple maps for Chaosium games, including Call of Cthulhu , RuneQuest , 7th Sea , and Pendragon .

Drawing the Folly plans was a treat for Matt, as it allowed him to  incorporate  previous  versions  with  ideas  from  Ben  Aaronovitch, Lynne Hardy, and Mike Mason in order to produce the finished items. Matt also created a complete 3D digital model, which the gang had fun running through!

Matt lives in the Finger Lakes region of New York with his wife, KC. The  pair  can  often  be  found  eating  dinner  in  their  canoe  on Cayuga Lake.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000962_9815809b346760ba0fefaea72234c2963ecea361d373ee7be91fbbfe56cb33ba.png)

## Lucya Szachnowski

Lucya Szachnowski has lived in London all her life and loves her city,  roleplaying,  and  all  things  magical.  For  Chaosium's Call  of Cthulhu RPG, she co-wrote The London Guidebook , 'The Garden of Earthly  Delights'  for Strange  Aeons ,  'Suffer  Little  Children'  and 'London  Calling'  for Day of  the  Beast ,  'The  Occulted  Light'  for Before the Fall, and contributed to The Investigator's Companion and Keeper's Compendium .  She  wrote  the  introductory  scenario  for  the Sigil &amp; Sign RPG  by Cubicle 7 and Make Believe Games. After years  working  on  local  London  newspapers,  she  did  an  MA  in English literature, specialising in literary London. Under the pen name  Lucya  Starza,  she  has  written  books  on  practical  magic  in Moon Books' Pagan Portals series, and writes a London-based blog: A Bad Witch's Blog. She is very excited to be involved in the Rivers of London RPG.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000963_af0e43effc67e1c3c9b8bcb9669f4bfe9b354e70a3f24339dac98149963293c0.png)

I

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000964_6e3ee1d0f8318ecea2af3c3dca364f0ded1fd6503843edf78fc665cfb21a6fe2.png)

## Anna Urbanek

Anna is a graphic designer by trade, a librarian by education, and a folklorist by heart's demand. As a part of the Double Proficiency team, she has been fortunate to provide layout and graphic design for  such  tabletop  roleplaying  and  board  game  projects  as Starport RPG , Adventure Tactics , Old-School Essentials , Gangs of the Undercity , Inspirisles , Asunder , The  Lost  Citadel , Earthdawn ,  and  many  more. Whenever she finds the time, she collaborates with her partner on writing, editing, designing, and illustrating in-house projects, like Herbalist's Primer , a collection of encounter decks known as Wayfarer's Decks , and the upcoming strategy tabletop RPG, Aphelion Toolkit .

Outside of work, Anna is a reclusive witch hiding in the middle of a Finnish forest with a partner, a wolf-like dog, two cat familiars, and a couple of mini humans. She busies herself with researching obscure and arcane topics, embroidering  8 th -century clothing replicas,  playing Shadowrun ,  and  studying  world  mythologies and folk  stories.  Powered  by  the  glories  of  coffee  and  pistachios,  she wages an eternal (and losing) battle with the cats to keep at least one houseplant alive. Her current life goal is to befriend the local murder of ravens.

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000965_84039a438150689fb2d45848ab0512d14ed10d19bfd538608456cbf6accc80de.png)

POLICE

C

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000966_5fcdc1f796bd8c5eaa14a1ee750798ec7b2fc7940dc1cd169cca940e1a4837c0.png)

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000967_ffc1b5534d20d44e3cf3b494c3a5a732b7d753d1338b5ac5e56781a25643a265.png)

## WELCOME TO THE UNITED STATES OF AMERICA!

![Image](/home/docling/out/cha3200_-_rivers_of_london_1.4_artifacts/image_000968_be581ab12e9d0dbb3813c02cd5387226dc87fb7e87776d345449b4e3aae687ce.png)

"I ThiNK BECOMING A WiZArd Is ABOuT discovering what's real And what isn't."

-BEN AaRONOVITCh, RIvErS OF LONDON

Magic has returned to our world...

In London, officers of "the Folly"—-the Metropolitan Police Service's special magic branch—take to the streets to solve cases, catch criminals, and come ahy oym asoua-,apuow-jwap, eun yh!m sdua on been irreversibly changed by magic.

Rivers of London: the Roleplaying Game is based on the hugely successful series of novels by Ben Aaronovitch. You play newly recruited members of the Folly and, together, you solve mysteries and explore "Newtonian magic" against the backdrop of a vibrant and exciting modern London.

- An exclusive piece of short fiction by Ben Aaronovitch, "An Introduction to Collaborative Storytelling."
- Setting information and history, including details of how Newtonian magic works in Rivers of London, plus spells derived from those in the novels.
- All the rules you need to play Rivers of London, including character creation, character advancement, and advice for the Game Moderator.
- A solo adventure, "The Domestic," designed to introduce players to the world of the Rivers of London and the mechanics of the game.
- Details on policing in London and how criminal investigations are carried out.
- . "The Bookshop," a standalone adventure that further introduces players to the game and magical world of Rivers of London.
